1. Ten newborns who were cared for in the same nursery develop a pustular rash within a week of discharge from the hospital. Culture of skin lesions grows Staphylococcus aureus. Which of the following is the most likely source of the infection?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. A bassinet used in the nursery</br>b. A health professional in the nursery</br>c. Mothers of the newborns</br>d. Siblings of the newborns</br>e. Soap used in the nursery</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>B. A health professional in the nursery  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis &amp; Cognitive Approach<br /></br>1. Pustular rash in multiple neonates → think bacterial skin infection; Staphylococcus aureus is classic.<br /></br>2. All infants shared only one common environment: the newborn nursery.<br /></br>3. When the same pathogen appears in several patients cared for by the same staff, always ask, “Who touches everybody?” → usually health-care personnel (HCP).  </p></br><p>Test-taking framework for similar questions:<br /></br>• Define the common link (time, place, personnel).<br /></br>• Remember that S aureus is carried in the anterior nares of 20-30 % of adults, especially HCP.<br /></br>• Humans = primary reservoir; environmental fomites are far less common sources for S aureus outbreaks.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (Why answer B is correct)<br /></br>• Neonatal S aureus outbreaks are almost always traced to an asymptomatic nasal carrier on the nursing/medical staff.<br /></br>• HCP handle every newborn repeatedly (bathing, diaper changes, feeding, exams) → efficient horizontal spread via hands.<br /></br>• Once neonates go home, the rash manifests within days, consistent with colonization acquired just before discharge.  </p></br><p>Take-home message: In clustered nosocomial S aureus infections, suspect an asymptomatic carrier among health-care workers.  </p></br><p>Documentation pearl: If this were a real event, you would document infection-control notification, culture/screening of nursery staff, and institution of contact precautions.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• S aureus colonizes moist skin and especially the anterior nares.<br /></br>• Transmission is primarily direct contact; survives poorly on dry surfaces, making inanimate objects a less efficient reservoir.<br /></br>• Newborns have immature immune barriers, so even transient carriage can lead to pustular skin lesions.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Management &amp; Infection-Control Steps<br /></br>1. Screen nursery staff with nasal cultures.<br /></br>2. Decolonize carriers: intranasal mupirocin + chlorhexidine baths.<br /></br>3. Reinforce hand hygiene, gown/glove use.<br /></br>4. Treat infected infants with anti-staphylococcal antibiotics (e.g., dicloxacillin, cephalexin; vancomycin if MRSA suspected).  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>High-Yield Memory Aids<br /></br>• “Staff = Staph”  → Health-care staff are the reservoir for Staph aureus.<br /></br>• Picture a nurse with a glowing golden (coagulase-positive) halo around the nose to remember nasal carriage.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong<br /></br>A. Bassinet used in the nursery<br /></br>   – S aureus survives poorly on dry surfaces; environmental contamination is rarely the primary source of widespread outbreaks.  </p></br><p>C. Mothers of the newborns<br /></br>   – Each mother primarily contacts only her own infant; would not explain infection across all babies.  </p></br><p>D. Siblings of the newborns<br /></br>   – Siblings are not present in the nursery; no consistent contact with every newborn.  </p></br><p>E. Soap used in the nursery<br /></br>   – Antiseptic soaps inhibit S aureus; contaminated soap more commonly contains Gram-negatives (e.g., Pseudomonas), not coagulase-positive Staph.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Core Concept<br /></br>Clustered neonatal S aureus infections → think asymptomatic nasal carrier among health-care personnel; institute screening and decolonization.</p>
2. A 62-year-old woman comes to the physician because of a 3-day history of low-grade fever, cough productive of green sputum, and progressive shortness of breath. Initially, her shortness of breath occurred only with exertion, but last night she had to sleep in a chair to breathe. She has type 2 diabetes mellitus treated with diet and glipizide and mild congestive heart failure treated with lisinopril. Her temperature is 37.5°C (99.5°F), pulse is 110/min, respirations are 24/min, and blood pressure is 140/60 mm Hg. Breath sounds are decreased over the left lower lung field, and there is dullness to percussion. A grade 2/6 midsystolic murmur is heard. An X-ray of the chest shows a large left pleural effusion. A thoracentesis is performed. Laboratory studies show:</br></br>Serum  </br>Total protein        7 g/dL  </br>Lactate dehydrogenase (LDH)        230 U/L  </br></br>Pleural fluid  </br>pH        7.1  </br>Glucose        20 mg/dL  </br>Protein        6 g/dL  </br>LDH        200 U/L  </br></br>Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. Chylothorax  </br>b. Congestive heart failure  </br>c. Empyema  </br>d. Pulmonary embolism  </br>e. Tuberculosis</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>c. Empyema  </p></br><p>───────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Acute respiratory infection picture: 3-day fever, productive green sputum, worsening dyspnea, orthopnea.<br /></br>• Physical exam &amp; CXR: large unilateral pleural effusion (↓ breath sounds, dullness).<br /></br>• Thoracentesis chemistry is the key:<br /></br>  – pH 7.1 (very acidic)<br /></br>  – Glucose 20 mg/dL (markedly low)<br /></br>  – Pleural/serum protein ratio 6 ÷ 7 = 0.86 (&gt;0.5)<br /></br>  – Pleural/serum LDH ratio 200 ÷ 230 = 0.87 (&gt;0.6)<br /></br>  → Satisfies Light’s criteria for an exudate.<br /></br>• The combination “exudate + pH &lt; 7.2 + glucose &lt; 40” is virtually diagnostic of an infected pleural space (complicated parapneumonic effusion / empyema).  </p></br><p>Metacognitive tip: When you see pleural fluid results, first apply Light’s criteria to decide transudate vs exudate, then look for red-flag numbers: pH &lt; 7.2 or glucose &lt; 40 → think infection or rheumatoid; if both are present with recent pneumonia, answer = empyema.  </p></br><p>───────────────<br /></br>Clinical-Critical Reasoning (Step-wise)<br /></br>1. Define the effusion: Exudate by Light’s criteria.<br /></br>2. Identify “complicated” features: very low pH &amp; glucose.<br /></br>3. Link to clinical context: febrile patient with productive cough → pneumonia.<br /></br>4. Infection within the pleural space = empyema (pus or bacterial invasion).<br /></br>Take-home: Empyema = acidic, hypoglycemic, protein-rich fluid in a patient with pneumonia.  </p></br><p>Documentation points (real life): appearance of fluid (often turbid/purulent), gram stain/culture ordered, chest tube drainage plan, antibiotics started.  </p></br><p>───────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Empyema = accumulation of purulent material in pleural cavity, usually from contiguous spread of bacterial pneumonia.<br /></br>• Bacteria metabolize glucose → low glucose; neutrophil &amp; bacterial lysis release lactic acid → low pH.<br /></br>• Inflammation ↑ vascular permeability → high protein &amp; LDH (cellular enzyme).  </p></br><p>───────────────<br /></br>Management Snapshot<br /></br>1. Chest tube drainage (tube thoracostomy) ± intrapleural fibrinolytics.<br /></br>2. Broad-spectrum IV antibiotics covering S. pneumoniae, S. aureus, anaerobes (eg, ceftriaxone + vancomycin + clindamycin, adjusted by culture).<br /></br>3. Surgical decortication if loculated and non-draining.  </p></br><p>───────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>• “Empyema = Empty glucose &amp; pH” (both very low).<br /></br>• Think “Pus is Poisonously acidic.”<br /></br>• Light’s criteria mnemonic for exudate: “Pleura is PROUD” (Protein Ratio &gt;0.5, LDH Ratio &gt;0.6, LDH &gt;2/3 ULN).  </p></br><p>───────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong  </p></br><p>a. Chylothorax<br /></br>   – Milky fluid, triglycerides &gt;110 mg/dL, neutral pH, normal glucose. Not seen here.  </p></br><p>b. Congestive heart failure<br /></br>   – Produces bilateral or right &gt; left transudates with low protein (&lt;0.5 ratio) and normal pH/glucose. This fluid is exudative and acidotic.  </p></br><p>d. Pulmonary embolism<br /></br>   – Can cause a small exudate or transudate; pH and glucose are usually normal; clinical onset often sudden with pleuritic pain and hemoptysis, not subacute pneumonia.  </p></br><p>e. Tuberculosis<br /></br>   – Exudate, but pH usually ≥7.3, glucose only mildly low, lymphocyte-predominant. TB effusions are rarely as acidic or hypoglycemic as this.  </p></br><p>───────────────<br /></br>Core Concept:<br /></br>In any pleural fluid analysis, markedly low pH (&lt;7.2) and glucose (&lt;40 mg/dL) in the setting of pneumonia → think empyema that must be drained plus antibiotics.</p>
3. A 37-year-old man is brought to the emergency department 12 hours after ingesting drain cleaner in a suicide attempt. On arrival, he is sitting up and drooling. He is unable to swallow because of intense throat pain. His pulse is 110/min, and blood pressure is 150/90 mm Hg. Examination shows erythema and edema of the oropharynx with some erosions of the soft palate. There is pain on palpation of the neck. The lungs are clear to auscultation. Cardiac examination shows a normal S1 and S2 without rubs, murmurs, or gallops. There is minimal epigastric tenderness, and bowel sounds are normal. An anteroposterior x-ray of the chest shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial step in management?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Oral administration of activated charcoal in water</br>b. Oral administration of ipecac</br>c. Oral administration of magnesium citrate</br>d. Upper endoscopy</br>e. Gastric lavage</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>d. Upper endoscopy (esophagogastroduodenoscopy, EGD)</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Intentional ingestion of “drain cleaner” → strong alkali (lye, sodium / potassium hydroxide).<br /></br>• Classical caustic‐ingestion clues: severe odynophagia, drooling, refusal to swallow, sitting upright, oropharyngeal burns, neck pain.<br /></br>• Vital signs: tachycardia but hemodynamically stable; no stridor or respiratory distress → airway presently adequate.<br /></br>• CXR shows no free air → no obvious perforation of esophagus or stomach.</p></br><p>Test-taking framework<br /></br>1. ABCs first. Airway is currently intact (no stridor, normal voice), so proceed to next step.<br /></br>2. In any caustic ingestion, the next priority—within the first 12-24 h—is risk stratification with early endoscopy unless there is frank perforation or severe airway compromise.<br /></br>3. Neutralization, emesis induction, lavage, or activated charcoal do NOT improve outcome and may worsen injury.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>• Why endoscopy now?<br /></br>  – Determines depth and length of mucosal injury → guides prognosis, decisions about NPO duration, need for surgery, and long-term stricture surveillance.<br /></br>  – Safest when performed within 12-24 h before edema and friability peak (after 48 h risk of iatrogenic perforation increases).<br /></br>• Documentation pearls: time and substance ingested, airway assessment, CXR result, decision for urgent EGD, NPO status, IV access, pain control.</p></br><p>Core takeaway<br /></br>Early EGD (≤24 h) is the cornerstone of management for hemodynamically stable patients after caustic ingestion to grade injury and direct therapy. Supportive care + airway protection precede or accompany it.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review (high-yield)<br /></br>• Alkali (pH &gt; 12) causes liquefaction necrosis → deep, penetrating injury, especially to oropharynx and esophagus.<br /></br>• Complications: perforation (early), mediastinitis, peritonitis, strictures (weeks), esophageal cancer (years).</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Diagnostic &amp; Treatment Algorithm<br /></br>1. ABCs; intubate if airway threatened (stridor, hoarseness, respiratory distress).<br /></br>2. CXR/abd radiograph → look for free air.<br /></br>3. EGD within 12-24 h if no signs of perforation.<br /></br>4. NPO, IV fluids, IV PPIs; avoid steroids unless airway edema is severe (controversial).<br /></br>5. Surgery for perforation or full-thickness necrosis.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“Draino = EGD pronto” – If the patient is stable, scope within a day.<br /></br>Avoid the “4 C’s” after caustic: Charcoal, Cathartics, (i)PeCac, neutralizing Chemicals.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>a. Activated charcoal – Ineffective; charcoal does not adsorb strong acids/alkali well and obscures endoscopic view. Risk of aspiration.<br /></br>b. Ipecac – Induces emesis; vomiting re-exposes esophagus/oropharynx to caustic → worsens burns.<br /></br>c. Magnesium citrate (cathartic) – No benefit; catharsis does not limit mucosal injury and may precipitate perforation.<br /></br>e. Gastric lavage – Contraindicated; mechanical trauma plus risk of esophageal or gastric perforation in caustic burns. Also delays definitive evaluation.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Bottom Line<br /></br>After ensuring the airway is secure and ruling out perforation radiographically, the most appropriate initial definitive step in a stable patient with caustic ingestion is urgent upper endoscopy within the first 24 hours.</p>
4. A 27-year-old man comes for a routine health maintenance examination. He has had six episodes of acute pancreatitis over the past 8 years; his most recent episode occurred 3 months ago and resolved spontaneously. He has had recurrent episodes of abdominal pain since childhood. There is no history of abdominal trauma. He takes no medications and does not drink alcohol. Examination shows no hepatosplenomegaly or abdominal masses. There is no tenderness to palpation. Studies of the gallbladder and biliary tract show normal findings. Which of the following is most likely to confirm the diagnosis?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Measurement of serum ionized calcium concentration</br>b. Measurement of serum triglyceride concentration</br>c. Assay for serum mumps antibody</br>d. CT scan of the abdomen</br>e. Examination of duodenal aspirate</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>B. Measurement of serum triglyceride concentration  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li>Recurrent pancreatitis since childhood (6 attacks in 8 years, latest only 3 mo ago).  </li></br><li>Age 27, no alcohol, no gallstones/biliary disease, no drugs, no trauma.  </li></br><li>Completely normal physical examination between attacks.</li></br></ol></br><p>Pertinent positives: recurrent bouts, early onset, spontaneous resolution, absence of typical secondary causes (alcohol, gallstones).<br /></br>Pertinent negatives: no biliary tract abnormality, no hyperalimentation, no abdominal trauma, no systemic illness.</p></br><p>Pattern recognition: In a young adult with multiple unexplained pancreatitis episodes, the “rule-of-twos” differential is<br /></br>• Two metabolic: hypertriglyceridemia, hypercalcemia<br /></br>• Two hereditary: CFTR mutations, hereditary pancreatitis (PRSS1, SPINK1)<br /></br>• Two anatomic: pancreas divisum, annular pancreas<br /></br>Among these, the single most common correctable metabolic cause is severe hypertriglyceridemia (&gt;1000 mg/dL). First-line test to confirm is a fasting serum triglyceride level.</p></br><p>Metacognitive tip: When recurrent or idiopathic pancreatitis is described, immediately ask “Could it be stones, booze, calcium, TGs, structural, genetic?” Then pick the lowest-cost, highest-yield test that directly proves the leading suspect.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (Why answer B is correct)</p></br><p>Step-wise logic:<br /></br>1. Gallstones and alcohol, the two leading etiologies, have essentially been excluded.<br /></br>2. A metabolic cause is most probable because symptoms began in childhood.<br /></br>3. Hypertriglyceridemia (&gt;1000 mg/dL) is not only common in this age group (familial chylomicronemia, familial combined hyperlipidemia) but also completely silent between attacks—exactly like this case.<br /></br>4. A single fasting serum triglyceride measurement will reveal the diagnosis (often appears “lipemic” even before lab processing). No imaging or antibody assay is needed to establish that cause.</p></br><p>Take-home message: In a young patient with “idiopathic” or recurrent pancreatitis, always check fasting triglycerides.  </p></br><p>Documentation pearls: record number of previous attacks, exclude alcohol use, document gallbladder imaging, order fasting lipid profile with specific note to triglyceride value.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review</p></br><p>• When serum TG &gt; ~1000 mg/dL, excess chylomicrons ↑ blood viscosity and deliver large quantities of triglyceride to the pancreas.<br /></br>• Pancreatic lipase hydrolyzes TG into free fatty acids → toxic to acinar cells → inflammation and ischemia.<br /></br>• Triggers: high-fat meal, alcohol binge, estrogen therapy, pregnancy, uncontrolled diabetes.  </p></br><p>Genetics: Type I (LPL deficiency, ApoC-II defect), Type IV &amp; V familial hyperlipidemia, or multifactorial hypertriglyceridemia.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Work-up</p></br><p>Diagnostic:<br /></br>• Fasting lipid panel (focus on TG).<br /></br>• Consider secondary causes: HbA1c, TSH, kidney &amp; liver function.</p></br><p>Acute attack management:<br /></br>• NPO, IV fluids, analgesia (same as any pancreatitis).<br /></br>• If TG &gt; 1000–2000 mg/dL consider insulin drip, heparin (releases LPL transiently), or urgent plasmapheresis in severe cases.</p></br><p>Long-term:<br /></br>• Very-low-fat diet (&lt;15% kcal), weight control.<br /></br>• Fibrate (e.g., fenofibrate), high-dose omega-3, ± statin/niacin if indicated.<br /></br>• Tight glycemic control in diabetes, avoid estrogen or retinoids.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips</p></br><p>CHAP mnemonic for metabolic pancreatitis:<br /></br>C – Calcium high<br /></br>H – Hypertriglyceridemia<br /></br>A – Alcohol<br /></br>P – (Gall)stone Passage  </p></br><p>Or think “&gt;1000 mg/dL TG can turn your pancreas into OIL.”  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Incorrect</p></br><p>A. Serum ionized calcium – Hypercalcemia (e.g., primary hyperparathyroidism) can cause pancreatitis, but it is a far less common etiology. The vignette gives no history of kidney stones, bones, groans, psychiatric overtones, or lab clues. TG testing is simpler and has higher pre-test probability.</p></br><p>C. Serum mumps antibody – Mumps pancreatitis is acute, occurs in children with recent parotitis, and is not recurrent over years.</p></br><p>D. CT scan of abdomen – Helpful for current severity and complications (necrosis, pseudocyst) or to identify structural anomalies, but it does not “confirm” the underlying etiology.</p></br><p>E. Examination of duodenal aspirate – Used to evaluate exocrine pancreatic insufficiency (e.g., cystic fibrosis, chronic pancreatitis), not to identify the cause of acute attacks.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line</p></br><p>For recurrent “idiopathic” pancreatitis in a young patient, the single most informative next step is a fasting serum triglyceride level; severe hypertriglyceridemia (&gt;1000 mg/dL) is a classic, correctable cause.</p>
5. A 4-year-old boy is diagnosed with acute lymphocytic leukemia. Although his parents are told that chemotherapy could increase his chance of survival by 80 percent, they state that they wish to treat the boy with herbal therapy. Which of the following is the most appropriate course of action?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Allow the boy to be treated with herbal therapy</br>b. Obtain consent from the patient’s grandparents</br>c. Transfer the patient to another hospital</br>d. Obtain a court order to treat the patient with chemotherapy</br>e. Begin treatment with chemotherapy without the parents’ consent while obtaining a court order</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>d. Obtain a court order to treat the patient with chemotherapy  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Patient: 4-year-old (a minor → cannot provide legal consent).<br /></br>• Condition: Newly diagnosed acute lymphoblastic leukemia (ALL) – highly curable with modern multi-agent chemotherapy (overall 5-year survival ≈ 90%).<br /></br>• Key detail: Parents refuse proven, potentially curative therapy and request unproven herbal treatment.<br /></br>• Ethical/Legal pivot: Parents normally direct a child’s care, but the state has a parens patriae duty to protect minors when parental decisions place the child at substantial risk of serious harm or death.  </p></br><p>Metacognitive approach for exam questions:<br /></br>1. Identify the patient’s decision-making capacity (adult, minor, impaired?).<br /></br>2. Ask: “Will the refusal place the patient at significant risk of serious, preventable harm?”<br /></br>3. If YES and time allows, seek legal intervention (court order / child protective services).<br /></br>4. Only in a true emergency with no time for legal steps can you proceed without consent.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>• ALL without chemotherapy is almost uniformly fatal.<br /></br>• The proposed therapy offers an 80-90 % cure rate with acceptable risk; thus, refusal exposes the child to a high likelihood of death.<br /></br>• Physicians must first attempt to counsel and negotiate with parents; if refusal persists, they must protect the child by seeking a court order.<br /></br>Core takeaway: Parental autonomy is not absolute—life-saving care with a high likelihood of success must be provided, using judicial intervention when necessary.  </p></br><p>In a real encounter you would:<br /></br>1. Document detailed discussion with parents (diagnosis, prognosis, risks/benefits).<br /></br>2. Consult hospital ethics and legal counsel.<br /></br>3. Petition family court for temporary custody for medical decisions specific to chemotherapy.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (brief)<br /></br>• ALL = malignant proliferation of lymphoid precursors in bone marrow → anemia, thrombocytopenia, infections, bone pain.<br /></br>• Without treatment: rapidly progressive and fatal.<br /></br>• Modern chemotherapy (e.g., vincristine, glucocorticoids, anthracyclines, asparaginase) induces remission in &gt;90 % of children.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Highlights<br /></br>First-line therapy: Multi-agent chemotherapy protocols (e.g., Children’s Oncology Group protocols) ± CNS prophylaxis (intrathecal methotrexate) and maintenance therapy.<br /></br>Diagnostics already confirmed; urgency is initiating treatment, not further testing.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Mnemonic / Memory Aid<br /></br>“Parents direct, unless child’s LIFE is at stake → Call the court for the child’s sake.”<br /></br>Think “LIFE or LIMB → LEGAL intervention.”  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>a. Allow herbal therapy<br /></br> – Violates physician’s duty to protect the child from preventable death; considered medical neglect.  </p></br><p>b. Obtain consent from the patient’s grandparents<br /></br> – Grandparents have no legal authority while parents retain custody; bypassing parents without court involvement is improper.  </p></br><p>c. Transfer the patient to another hospital<br /></br> – “Dumping” the patient does not solve the ethical/legal problem and may be construed as abandonment.  </p></br><p>e. Begin chemotherapy without consent while obtaining a court order<br /></br> – Permissible only in a true emergency where delay is life-threatening and no time exists for legal remedy (e.g., airway obstruction, exsanguination). ALL treatment can generally await rapid court intervention (hours-days), so obtain the order first.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Key Pearl<br /></br>On USMLE/NBME:<br /></br>• If parents refuse life-saving, standard therapy for a minor and there is time → seek a court order.<br /></br>• If the situation is immediately life-threatening and no time for court → treat emergently.</p>
6. A previously healthy 52-year-old man comes to the physician because of a 6-month history of weakness in his right hand and legs and a 2-month history of mild neck pain. He drinks six beers daily. Examination shows marked atrophy of the hands, worse on the right than on the left, and lower extremities. There are fasciculations of the right upper extremity. Deep tendon reflexes are 4+ in the upper and lower extremities. Babinski sign is present bilaterally. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Alcoholic polyneuropathy</br>b. Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis</br>c. Cervical spondylosis</br>d. Myasthenia gravis</br>e. Vitamin B12 (cobalamin) deficiency</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>b. Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS)</p></br><p>───────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Age/sex: 52-year-old man—typical age of onset for sporadic ALS (40-70 yrs).<br /></br>• Time course: Progressive over 6 months → chronic neurodegenerative process rather than acute or relapsing.<br /></br>• Motor findings ONLY:<br /></br>  – Lower motor neuron (LMN) signs → marked atrophy, fasciculations.<br /></br>  – Upper motor neuron (UMN) signs → hyperreflexia (4+), bilateral Babinski.<br /></br>• No sensory loss, bowel/bladder symptoms, or cranial nerve involvement given.<br /></br>• Mild neck pain is nonspecific and common with spasticity or posture changes; don’t let it anchor you on a cervical spine lesion.  </p></br><p>Key diagnostic pattern: UMN + LMN signs in multiple body regions without sensory deficits = classic for ALS.</p></br><p>Metacognitive tip: When a stem gives both atrophy/fasciculations (LMN) and pathologic reflexes/Babinski (UMN) with intact sensation, immediately prioritize ALS.</p></br><p>───────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Step-wise approach:<br /></br>1. Localize lesion(s): Motor system only, anterior horn + corticospinal tracts.<br /></br>2. Look for mixed UMN/LMN features spanning more than one limb region → rules out single radiculopathy or focal cervical lesion.<br /></br>3. Absence of sensory deficits rules out polyneuropathies, B12 deficiency, dorsal column disease.<br /></br>Core takeaway: ALS is the prototypical condition that simultaneously involves the upper and lower motor neurons without sensory involvement.</p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real patient): Document pattern of weakness, reflexes, fasciculations, absence of sensory loss, and counsel on need for electromyography (EMG) and neurology referral.</p></br><p>───────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Degeneration of:<br /></br>  – UMNs in the lateral corticospinal tracts.<br /></br>  – LMNs in the anterior horn of the spinal cord, cranial nerve motor nuclei.<br /></br>• Etiology: Mostly sporadic; ~10% familial (SOD1, C9orf72).<br /></br>• Clinical course: Progressive asymmetric limb weakness, then bulbar and respiratory involvement; cognition usually preserved.</p></br><p>───────────────────────<br /></br>Diagnostics &amp; Management<br /></br>Diagnostics<br /></br>• EMG/NCS: Denervation (fibrillation potentials) + reinnervation.<br /></br>• MRI brain/spine to exclude structural lesions.<br /></br>• Labs to rule out mimics (B12, thyroid, Lyme, HIV, heavy metals).</p></br><p>Management<br /></br>• Disease-modifying: Riluzole (glutamate inhibitor) prolongs survival; edaravone (radical scavenger) slows functional decline.<br /></br>• Symptomatic: Baclofen or tizanidine for spasticity, non-invasive ventilation, PEG for nutrition, advance-care planning.</p></br><p>───────────────────────<br /></br>High-Yield Memory Aids<br /></br>“ALS = A mix of LMN + UMN Signs.”<br /></br>OR<br /></br>“Lou Gehrig’s disease: Lou swings UP (UMN), bats DOWN (LMN). No ‘feel’ for the ball (no sensory).”</p></br><p>───────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>a. Alcoholic polyneuropathy<br /></br>• Symmetric distal sensory &gt; motor loss, areflexia, stocking-glove distribution.<br /></br>• No UMN signs; would not produce Babinski or hyperreflexia.</p></br><p>c. Cervical spondylosis (cervical myelopathy)<br /></br>• Can give LMN signs at the level (hand atrophy) + UMN signs below.<br /></br>• Usually accompanied by neck pain with radicular arm pain, sensory changes, Lhermitte sign. MRI spine would reveal osteophytes/disc degeneration. Sensation often impaired—absent here.</p></br><p>d. Myasthenia gravis<br /></br>• Fatigable ocular, bulbar, and proximal limb weakness; normal reflexes, no atrophy or fasciculations.<br /></br>• Worsens with use, improves with rest—opposite pattern and no UMN signs.</p></br><p>e. Vitamin B12 deficiency<br /></br>• Causes subacute combined degeneration (dorsal columns + corticospinal tracts) → UMN signs with paresthesias, loss of proprioception/vibration, ataxia, anemia.<br /></br>• No LMN findings like fasciculations or marked atrophy.</p></br><p>───────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line<br /></br>The coexistence of progressive LMN (atrophy, fasciculations) and UMN (hyperreflexia, Babinski) signs without sensory involvement in a middle-aged patient is pathognomonic for amyotrophic lateral sclerosis.</p>
7. A healthy 27-year-old woman comes to the physician requesting a PPD skin test. She works as a clerk at a physician’s office and is concerned about tuberculosis even though she has no known exposure. Examination shows no abnormalities. A PPD skin test produces 19 mm of erythema and 8 mm of induration at 48 hours. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Reassurance and annual PPD skin test</br>b. Reassurance and second PPD skin test only if exposure occurs</br>c. Second PPD skin test in 1 week</br>d. X-ray of the chest only</br>e. X-ray of the chest and isoniazid therapy</br>f. X-ray of the chest and rifampin therapy</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>A. Reassurance and annual PPD skin test  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Key data points<br /></br>   • Healthy 27-year-old, no symptoms, no known TB exposure<br /></br>   • Works as a clerk (administrative—not direct patient care) in a physician’s office<br /></br>   • PPD read at 48 h shows 19 mm of erythema but, critically, only 8 mm of INDURATION  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Why these clues matter<br /></br>   • Only the diameter of induration is interpreted; erythema is ignored.<br /></br>   • Risk category dictates the induration cut-off that defines a “positive” result.<br /></br>        – ≥5 mm: highest risk (HIV+, recent TB contact, nodular CXR, transplant, immunosuppressed)<br /></br>        – ≥10 mm: moderate risk (health-care workers with patient contact, recent immigrants, IV-drug users, high-risk comorbidities, children &lt;4 yr, etc.)<br /></br>        – ≥15 mm: everyone else (no risk factors)<br /></br>   • An office clerk with no direct patient care is considered “low-risk”; therefore the threshold is ≥15 mm.<br /></br>   • Her 8 mm induration is therefore NEGATIVE.  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Metacognitive approach for future questions<br /></br>   • Step 1: Ask “What number do I interpret—induration or erythema?” → induration only.<br /></br>   • Step 2: Assign risk category → set the correct threshold (5, 10, or 15 mm).<br /></br>   • Step 3: Compare actual induration to the threshold → decide positive vs negative.<br /></br>   • Step 4: If negative, reassure; if positive, proceed to CXR ± latent TB treatment.  </p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Because the induration does not meet even the 10-mm cut-off, the test is negative. A negative screen in a low-risk individual needs no further evaluation. CDC guidelines for low-risk health-care settings recommend repeat screening only on an annual basis (or after a documented exposure). Therefore reassurance plus routine annual testing is the appropriate management.  </p></br><p>Take-home message: Use induration size + risk category to decide next steps; do not order CXR or give isoniazid unless the screening test is actually positive.  </p></br><p>Documentation points if this were a real patient encounter:<br /></br>• Size of induration (8 mm), date/time of reading<br /></br>• Risk assessment (no known exposure, administrative role)<br /></br>• Interpretation (negative)<br /></br>• Plan: routine annual PPD, education provided  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (high-yield)  </p></br><p>• PPD (Mantoux) test is a type IV delayed hypersensitivity reaction to purified protein derivative of Mycobacterium tuberculosis.<br /></br>• A positive test indicates prior sensitization (infection or BCG vaccination) but not necessarily active disease.<br /></br>• Induration results from T-cell–mediated inflammation; erythema alone is nonspecific.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment and Diagnostic Tests  </p></br><p>Negative screen → no treatment or imaging.<br /></br>Positive screen (≥15 mm for low-risk) → CXR to rule out active disease.<br /></br>    • If CXR normal → latent TB → 6–9 mo isoniazid (+ pyridoxine) OR rifampin-based regimens.<br /></br>    • If CXR abnormal → evaluate for active TB (AFB sputum, etc.) and treat with RIPE therapy.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>Mnemonic “5-10-15 RULE”:<br /></br>5 mm = FIVE groups at highest risk (HIV, recent contact, fibrotic CXR, organ transplant, immunosuppressed/steroid).<br /></br>10 mm = TEN everyday exposures (health-care, IVDU, immigrants &lt;5 y, kids &lt;4 y, comorbid lung dz).<br /></br>15 mm = FIFTEEN for everyone else.  </p></br><p>Think “E for Erythema = Easy to Ignore.” Only measure the firm induration.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>B. Reassurance and second PPD only if exposure occurs<br /></br>   • Misses the routine annual screening recommendation for health-care settings.  </p></br><p>C. Second PPD in 1 week (two-step testing)<br /></br>   • Two-step is done when establishing a baseline for employees with ongoing patient contact (nurses, medical techs). An office clerk does not require it under most protocols, and the stem does not state this is baseline employment screening.  </p></br><p>D. Chest X-ray only<br /></br>E. Chest X-ray + isoniazid<br /></br>F. Chest X-ray + rifampin<br /></br>   • All assume a positive screening test. With only 8 mm induration in a low-risk individual, the PPD is negative; imaging or chemoprophylaxis is unnecessary and exposes the patient to cost, radiation, and potential medication toxicity.  </p></br><p>Common Pitfall: Confusing erythema for induration and over-treating false-positive PPD results. Always palpate for firmness.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line: An 8-mm induration in a low-risk person is a negative PPD. Provide reassurance and repeat the test on a routine (annual) basis.</p>
8. A 47-year-old man comes to the physician 24 hours after the onset of fever and nausea. He has a 4-week history of diarrhea that has increased in volume and frequency. The diarrhea has not responded to use of over-the-counter medications, and he now passes watery brown stools 8 to 10 times daily. During the past 5 days, he has had mild abdominal cramps that are relieved by bowel movements. Prior to the onset of his symptoms, he went on a 2-week vacation to Mexico and took ciprofloxacin daily. He has not had fecal incontinence or any other symptoms. His temperature is 38.6°C (101.5°F), pulse is 86/min, and blood pressure is 130/80 mm Hg. The abdomen is soft. There is right upper quadrant tenderness with no rebound tenderness. Bowel sounds are increased in all quadrants. The liver is tender and enlarged. Rectal examination shows no abnormalities. Test of the stool for occult blood is positive. Laboratory studies show:</br>Hemoglobin              9.8 g/dL</br>Leukocyte count         17,200/mm3</br>Serum</br>  Total bilirubin       3.8 mg/dL</br>  Alkaline phosphatase  279 U/L</br>  AST                   96 U/L</br>  ALT                   84 U/L</br>Examination of the stool shows leukocytes. Test of the stool for Clostridium difficile toxin is negative. A CT scan of the abdomen shows an 8-cm cystic mass in the right lobe of the liver. Which of the following is the most likely explanation for this patient's CT scan findings?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Echinococcal infection</br>b. Entamoeba histolytica infection</br>c. Giardia lamblia infection</br>d. Liver fluke infection</br>e. Streptococcus milleri infection</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>B. Entamoeba histolytica infection  </p></br><p>───────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Timeline &amp; geography: 2-week trip to Mexico → classic endemic area for amebiasis.<br /></br>• Intestinal phase: 4 weeks of profuse watery/bloody diarrhea with stool leukocytes and occult blood.<br /></br>• Extra-intestinal phase: New fever + right-upper-quadrant (RUQ) pain, tender enlarged liver, ↑WBC, cholestatic pattern (↑bilirubin, ↑ALP), CT = solitary 8-cm cystic lesion in right lobe.<br /></br>• No history of biliary disease, malignancy, or animal exposure that would suggest alternate etiologies.<br /></br>Pattern: Traveler’s diarrhea that progresses, within weeks, to a solitary hepatic abscess = textbook amebic liver abscess.</p></br><p>Exam technique for similar questions<br /></br>1. Identify “traveler’s dysentery.”<br /></br>2. Look for RUQ pain + fever within weeks → think portal spread.<br /></br>3. Solitary cystic liver lesion ⇒ Entamoeba. Multiple micro-abscesses or “complex cyst with air” ⇒ pyogenic; calcified daughter cysts ⇒ echinococcus.</p></br><p>───────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Step-wise link to choice B:<br /></br>1. Enteric acquisition of E. histolytica cysts in Mexico.<br /></br>2. Colonic invasion → dysentery (blood, WBCs in stool).<br /></br>3. Trophozoites travel via portal venous system → necrotic liquefaction of hepatic tissue (“anchovy-paste” abscess) most often in right lobe.<br /></br>4. Cystic lesion on CT, systemic inflammatory response, and RUQ tenderness complete the picture.</p></br><p>Core takeaway: Post-dysentery RUQ pain + solitary liver abscess in a traveler = amebic liver abscess until proven otherwise.</p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real patient)<br /></br>• Travel history, onset of diarrhea, RUQ pain.<br /></br>• Fever pattern, stool characteristics.<br /></br>• Imaging findings.<br /></br>• Plan: serology for E. histolytica IgG, start metronidazole + luminal agent, avoid percutaneous drainage unless complicated.</p></br><p>───────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Etiology: Protozoan E. histolytica (fecal–oral cyst ingestion).<br /></br>• Risk factors: endemic travel, contaminated food/water, men who have sex with men.<br /></br>• Mechanism: Cysts → trophozoites invade colonic mucosa → bloodstream → liver → cytotoxic lectin-mediated hepatocyte necrosis → liquefied abscess.<br /></br>• Clinical manifestations:<br /></br>  – Intestinal: bloody diarrhea, abdominal cramps.<br /></br>  – Extra-intestinal (weeks–months later): fever, RUQ pain, hepatomegaly; rarely pleuro-pulmonary or brain abscesses.</p></br><p>───────────────────────<br /></br>Diagnostics &amp; Treatment<br /></br>Diagnosis<br /></br>• Imaging: solitary, round, low-attenuation lesion (often right lobe).<br /></br>• Serology (IgG) is most sensitive; stool O&amp;P often negative once liver disease develops.  </p></br><p>Management<br /></br>1. Metronidazole 750 mg PO/IV q8h × 7–10 days (kills tissue trophozoites).<br /></br>2. Follow with luminal amebicide (paromomycin 25–35 mg/kg/day divided TID × 7 days or iodoquinol) to eradicate intraluminal cysts.<br /></br>3. Drainage reserved for rupture risk, failure of therapy, or uncertain diagnosis.</p></br><p>───────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“Mexico + Bloody bowels → RUQ aching, Solitary cyst = Amebic.”<br /></br>Mnemonic: LIVER<br /></br>• L – Latency (weeks-after-dysentery)<br /></br>• I – Intestinal bloody diarrhea<br /></br>• V – Vacation to endemic area<br /></br>• E – Entamoeba<br /></br>• R – Right-lobe solitary abscess</p></br><p>───────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong<br /></br>A. Echinococcal infection (E. granulosus)<br /></br>• Hydatid cysts; exposure to sheep/dogs.<br /></br>• Usually asymptomatic for years; CT shows well-defined cyst with daughter cysts and calcification, not acute febrile illness post-dysentery.  </p></br><p>C. Giardia lamblia infection<br /></br>• Causes foul-smelling, fatty, NON-bloody diarrhea; no leukocytes, no liver invasion.  </p></br><p>D. Liver fluke infection (Clonorchis/Opisthorchis/Fasciola)<br /></br>• Acquired from undercooked fish or watercress.<br /></br>• Presents with biliary colic, cholangitis, eosinophilia, risk of cholangiocarcinoma—not solitary liver abscess.  </p></br><p>E. Streptococcus milleri (anginosus group) infection<br /></br>• Common bacterial cause of pyogenic liver abscess, usually polymicrobial, multiple abscesses, associated with biliary obstruction or intra-abdominal infection, not traveler’s diarrhea. CT often shows gas-forming lesions or multiple abscesses.  </p></br><p>───────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line<br /></br>Traveler to an endemic area who develops bloody diarrhea followed weeks later by fever and RUQ pain with a solitary hepatic abscess = Entamoeba histolytica amebic liver abscess. Treat with metronidazole plus a luminal agent, not drainage unless complicated.</p>
9. A 16-year-old girl is brought to the emergency department 6 hours after the onset of moderate lower abdominal cramps and intermittent nausea. She has not vomited during this time. She says that her last menstrual period was 2 months ago, but she has had intermittent bleeding since then, including spotting for the past 2 days. Menarche was at the age of 15 years. Menses occur at irregular 25- to 45-day intervals. She is sexually active and uses condoms inconsistently. Her temperature is 38.1°C (100.6°F), pulse is 94/min, respirations are 22/min, and blood pressure is 120/80 mm Hg. Examination shows a soft abdomen with lower quadrant tenderness, especially on the right. Bowel sounds are normal. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Complete blood count</br>b. Urinalysis</br>c. Abdominal x-ray</br>d. Pelvic examination</br>e. Exploratory laparoscopy</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>d. Pelvic examination  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues<br /></br>• 16-year-old, sexually active, inconsistent condom use → high risk for pregnancy and sexually-transmitted infections (STIs).<br /></br>• Last “normal” menses 2 months ago with irregular spotting since → possible early pregnancy, threatened/ectopic pregnancy, or anovulatory bleeding.<br /></br>• Low-grade fever (38.1 °C) + lower-quadrant abdominal pain, worse on the right → differential includes pelvic inflammatory disease (PID), tubo-ovarian abscess, ectopic pregnancy, ovarian torsion, appendicitis, UTI, etc.<br /></br>• Pain has been present only 6 h, no vomiting, normal bowel sounds → gastrointestinal causes less likely.<br /></br>Take-home: The most immediately informative, lowest-risk, bedside maneuver that distinguishes gynecologic from nongynecologic causes is a focused pelvic (speculum + bimanual) examination.  </p></br><p>Cognitive approach for similar questions<br /></br>1. Stabilize (ABCs) → she is hemodynamically stable.<br /></br>2. Complete history &amp; physical targeted to the pain location.<br /></br>   – For lower abdominal / pelvic complaints in a patient with any possibility of pregnancy or STI, a pelvic exam is an essential extension of the physical.<br /></br>3. Use exam findings to guide laboratory tests or imaging, not the other way around.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Why pelvic examination first?<br /></br>• Determines uterine size (helps gauge gestational age), adnexal or cervical motion tenderness (PID/ectopic), presence of adnexal mass (torsion, ectopic, cyst).<br /></br>• Allows immediate point-of-care pregnancy testing with a urine sample obtained in the room.<br /></br>• Permits cervical swabs for nucleic-acid amplification tests (NAATs) for Chlamydia/Gonorrhea, wet mount, KOH prep, and assessment of cervical/vaginal bleeding.<br /></br>• Noninvasive, quick, guides whether emergent imaging (transvaginal ultrasound) or surgical consult is needed.  </p></br><p>Core takeaway: In a hemodynamically stable female of reproductive age with lower abdominal pain ± vaginal bleeding, a pelvic examination is a mandatory first diagnostic step before labs or imaging.  </p></br><p>Documentation (real world)<br /></br>• Abdominal &amp; pelvic exam findings (uterine size, CMT, adnexal mass/tenderness, quality/quantity of vaginal bleeding/discharge).<br /></br>• Pregnancy test ordered/obtained.<br /></br>• STI screening discussed and swabs collected.<br /></br>• Pain scale, vitals, counseling, and plan.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (high-yield)  </p></br><p>Possible etiologies this exam helps sort:<br /></br>1. Ectopic pregnancy – Implantation outside uterus; bimanual: adnexal mass, CMT; positive β-hCG, empty uterus on US.<br /></br>2. PID – Ascending infection (N. gonorrhoeae, C. trachomatis); CMT, uterine/adnexal tenderness, mucopurulent discharge, fever.<br /></br>3. Ovarian torsion – Sudden unilateral pain; adnexal mass fixed &amp; tender; Doppler US if suspected.<br /></br>4. Threatened miscarriage – Closed cervix, intrauterine pregnancy, vaginal spotting.<br /></br>5. Appendicitis – RLQ pain, but pelvic exam often normal; rectal tenderness possible.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment / Diagnostic Algorithm (after exam)  </p></br><p>If pelvic exam suggests:<br /></br>• Pregnancy → bedside urine β-hCG → transvaginal US if positive to rule out ectopic vs intrauterine gestation.<br /></br>• PID → empiric ceftriaxone + doxycycline ± metronidazole per CDC guidelines.<br /></br>• Adnexal mass with torsion concern → prompt pelvic US with Doppler → gynecologic surgery.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>Rule of “PELVIC”:<br /></br>P – Pregnancy test (needs pelvic exam)<br /></br>E – Evaluate cervix (CMT, discharge)<br /></br>L – Look for blood source (vaginal vs cervical)<br /></br>V – Vaginal swabs (STI tests)<br /></br>I – Inspect uterus/adnexa size &amp; tenderness<br /></br>C – Consider need for further tests (US, labs) based on findings  </p></br><p>Visualize the flow: teenager → lower abdominal pain → arrow goes to “pelvic exam box” before splitting to labs/imaging.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>a. Complete blood count – May show leukocytosis or anemia but is nonspecific, doesn’t localize pathology, and should follow, not precede, a directed exam.  </p></br><p>b. Urinalysis – Helpful for pregnancy test (β-hCG) and UTI evaluation, but collection is often done in conjunction with the pelvic exam; UA without exam misses critical gynecologic information.  </p></br><p>c. Abdominal x-ray – Poor utility for pelvic gynecologic pathology, unnecessary radiation in potential pregnancy.  </p></br><p>e. Exploratory laparoscopy – Invasive, reserved for cases with high suspicion of surgical emergency (e.g., ruptured ectopic) after noninvasive evaluation.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line:<br /></br>Always perform a pelvic examination first in a reproductive-age female with pelvic pain or abnormal bleeding who is hemodynamically stable. It immediately narrows the differential and directs the need for labs or imaging.</p>
10. A 52-year-old woman, gravida 2, para 2, comes to the physician because of a 10-month history of severe hot flashes and difficulty sleeping. Menses occur at 3- to 4-month intervals. Menses previously occurred at regular 28-day intervals. Her only medication is lorazepam for generalized anxiety disorder. Examination, including pelvic examination, shows no abnormalities. The uterus is normal. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Oral estrogen and progesterone therapy</br>b. Oral estrogen therapy only</br>c. Oral progesterone therapy only</br>d. Use of an estrogen ring</br>e. Intrauterine progesterone therapy</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>A. Oral estrogen + progesterone therapy (combined systemic menopausal hormone therapy, MHT)</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• 52-year-old woman with 10 months of severe hot flashes and sleep disturbance = classic moderate-to-severe vasomotor symptoms of the menopausal transition.<br /></br>• Irregular menses at 3- to 4-month intervals → late perimenopause; ovarian estrogen production is falling but a functional endometrium is still present.<br /></br>• Pelvic exam, uterus, and Pap smear are normal → no contraindication to systemic estrogen.<br /></br>Key test-taking cue: “severe vasomotor symptoms in a woman who still has an intact uterus.”</p></br><p>Cognitive framework for similar questions:<br /></br>1. Are vasomotor symptoms bothersome enough to warrant pharmacologic therapy? (Yes)<br /></br>2. Does she have any contraindication to estrogen (e.g., breast cancer, thromboembolism, CAD, stroke)? (No)<br /></br>3. Does she have a uterus? (Yes → must add endometrial protection with a progestogen)</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Step-wise algorithm endorsed by ACOG &amp; Endocrine Society:<br /></br>• 1st-line for bothersome vasomotor symptoms in a healthy, &lt;60-year (&lt;10 y from menopause) woman = systemic estrogen.<br /></br>• Add continuous progesterone (oral, transdermal, or LNG-IUD) or a SERM (bazedoxifene) if the uterus is intact to prevent estrogen-induced endometrial hyperplasia/cancer.<br /></br>Core takeaway: Unopposed systemic estrogen is never given to a woman with an intact uterus.</p></br><p>Documentation points (if in clinic): severity/frequency of hot flashes, sleep impact, uterus present, review of estrogen contraindications, counseling on risks/benefits of MHT, and planned follow-up.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Ovarian follicular depletion → ↓ estrogen → loss of negative feedback → ↑ FSH.<br /></br>• Estrogen withdrawal destabilizes hypothalamic thermoregulation, producing episodic vasodilation (hot flashes) and sleep fragmentation.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Pearls<br /></br>Combined systemic MHT<br /></br>• Estrogen component (e.g., 0.625 mg conjugated equine estrogen or 1 mg micronized 17-β-estradiol) alleviates vasomotor symptoms.<br /></br>• Progestogen component (e.g., 200 mg micronized progesterone nightly or 2.5 mg medroxyprogesterone daily) protects endometrium.<br /></br>• Typical duration: use the lowest effective dose for the shortest period (&lt;5 y ideal) while reassessing annually.  </p></br><p>Baseline tests: BP, breast exam/mammography per guidelines; no labs needed solely for MHT unless clinically indicated.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tip<br /></br>“E-P for a U-P”<br /></br>Estrogen-Progesterone if Uterus Present; Estrogen only if uterus is Absent (post-hysterectomy).</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Why the Other Options Are Incorrect  </p></br><p>B. Oral estrogen only<br /></br>• Would relieve symptoms but delivers unopposed estrogen to the intact endometrium → risk of hyperplasia and adenocarcinoma. Appropriate only in women post-hysterectomy.</p></br><p>C. Oral progesterone only<br /></br>• Systemic progesterone alone does not meaningfully improve vasomotor symptoms; it is used solely for endometrial protection or to treat abnormal uterine bleeding.</p></br><p>D. Vaginal estrogen ring (eg, Estring)<br /></br>• Provides low-dose local estrogen for genitourinary syndrome of menopause (atrophic vaginitis, dyspareunia); inadequate systemic absorption to treat hot flashes.</p></br><p>E. Intrauterine progesterone (levonorgestrel IUD)<br /></br>• Delivers local endometrial protection and treats heavy bleeding but provides negligible systemic estrogen; therefore won’t relieve vasomotor symptoms. Could be combined with systemic estrogen, but estrogen must still be prescribed—thus not the best standalone next step.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>High-Yield Take-Home<br /></br>Combined systemic estrogen + progestogen is first-line therapy for significant vasomotor symptoms in women who still have a uterus. Never give unopposed systemic estrogen to a woman with an intact endometrium.</p>
11. Two days after operative repair of a right hip fracture, an 82-year-old woman is somnolent. Her initial postoperative course was uncomplicated. She has a 10-year history of osteoporosis. Her current medications are enoxaparin, morphine, and temazepam. She has never smoked cigarettes. Prior to the hip fracture, she was able to walk short distances with a walker. She is 157 cm (5 ft 2 in) tall and weighs 54 kg (120 lb); BMI is 22 kg/m². She responds to painful stimuli only. Her temperature is 37.2 °C (99 °F), pulse is 88/min and regular, respirations are 10/min, and blood pressure is 132/82 mm Hg. There are jugular venous pulsations 4 cm above the sternal angle. Examination shows severe kyphoscoliosis. The lungs are clear to auscultation. Examination of the right lower extremity shows ecchymoses and edema of the proximal thigh; there is trace ankle edema and no palpable cord. Examination of the left lower extremity shows no edema or palpable cord. Arterial blood gas analysis on room air shows:</br>pH 7.27</br>PCO₂ 58 mm Hg</br>PO₂ 60 mm Hg</br>HCO₃⁻ 26 mEq/L</br>O₂ saturation 88 percent</br></br>An x-ray of the chest shows a normal cardiac silhouette, no pleural effusions, and a small area of atelectasis at the right lung base. An ECG shows a normal sinus rhythm and no ischemic changes. Which of the following is the most appropriate pharmacotherapy?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. Intravenous furosemide</br>b. Intravenous heparin</br>c. Intravenous naloxone</br>d. Intravenous piperacillin-tazobactam</br>e. Intravenous tissue plasminogen activator</br>f. Nebulized albuterol</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>c. Intravenous naloxone  </p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Key clues<br /></br>   • Post-operative day 2, now “somnolent” and arousable only to painful stimuli<br /></br>   • Respiratory rate 10/min (bradypnea)<br /></br>   • Current drugs: morphine (opioid) + temazepam (benzodiazepine) + enoxaparin<br /></br>   • ABG: pH 7.27, PCO₂ 58 mm Hg, HCO₃⁻ 26  → acute respiratory acidosis with hypoxemia (O₂ sat 88 %)<br /></br>   • Lungs clear; CXR essentially normal except small basilar atelectasis<br /></br>   • No hemodynamic instability (BP 132/82), no fever, no focal infection signs  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Pertinent positives and negatives<br /></br>   • Positive: CNS depression + hypoventilation + opioid use<br /></br>   • Negative: No tachycardia, no tachypnea, no pleuritic pain (argues against PE); clear lungs &amp; normal CXR (argues against CHF or pneumonia); afebrile  </p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>Metacognitive Approach<br /></br>Whenever a post-op patient acutely becomes somnolent, check: “O-H-I-O” (Oxygenation, Hemodynamics, Infection, Opioids). A low respiratory rate plus elevated CO₂ almost always points to hypoventilation—most often medication-induced—until proven otherwise.<br /></br>––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Step-wise logic:<br /></br>1. Respiratory acidosis = alveolar hypoventilation.<br /></br>2. Hypoventilation post-op is commonly caused by opioid or benzodiazepine oversedation.<br /></br>3. The patient is receiving scheduled morphine; benzodiazepines alone rarely drive PaCO₂ this high, but opioids do.<br /></br>4. Immediate treatment for opioid-induced respiratory depression is an opioid antagonist ⇒ naloxone.<br /></br>Core takeaway: In the setting of acute hypercapnic respiratory failure and CNS depression after opioid administration, give naloxone. Document mental status, respiratory rate, pulse oximetry, and response to antidote.<br /></br>––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• Opioids bind μ-receptors in the medullary respiratory center ⇒ ↓ sensitivity to CO₂ ⇒ ↓ respiratory rate &amp; tidal volume ⇒ hypercapnia, hypoxemia, respiratory acidosis.<br /></br>• Acute rise in PaCO₂ (18 mm Hg above 40) should raise serum HCO₃⁻ by ~2 mEq/L (ΔHCO₃⁻ = 1 mEq/L per 10 mm Hg) ⇒ expected HCO₃⁻ ≈ 26, matching the ABG → confirms an acute (not chronic) process.<br /></br>––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>Management &amp; Diagnostics  </p></br><p>Immediate: 0.04–0.4 mg IV naloxone; may repeat or start infusion (half-life of naloxone &lt; morphine). Provide supplemental O₂ and monitor. If inadequate, support airway/ventilation.<br /></br>No additional labs/imaging needed before reversing a suspected opioid effect—time-critical.<br /></br>––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“MORPHINE makes you MORPH into a statue—Naloxone gets you moving.”<br /></br>Or think “CO₂ = O₂pIOID”: If CO₂ is high post-op, suspect opioid.<br /></br>––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>a. Intravenous furosemide (loop diuretic)<br /></br>   • CHF would show crackles, S₃, pulmonary edema on CXR, ↑JVP &gt; 8 cm; ABG usually shows hypoxemia with or without alkalosis—not isolated hypercapnia.  </p></br><p>b. Intravenous heparin<br /></br>   • Pulmonary embolism causes V/Q mismatch → hypoxemia + low/normal PaCO₂ (hyperventilation → respiratory alkalosis). Patient is already on prophylactic enoxaparin and shows hypercapnia, making PE unlikely.  </p></br><p>d. Intravenous piperacillin-tazobactam<br /></br>   • For hospital-acquired pneumonia: expect fever, leukocytosis, infiltrate on CXR, productive cough. None present.  </p></br><p>e. Intravenous tissue plasminogen activator (tPA)<br /></br>   • Reserved for massive PE with hemodynamic instability (hypotension) or stroke management—absent here.  </p></br><p>f. Nebulized albuterol<br /></br>   • Used for bronchospasm/asthma or COPD exacerbation, which present with wheezing and typically low PaCO₂ initially (unless severe). Lungs are clear, no wheeze, no obstructive history.  </p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>Key Take-Home Message  </p></br><p>Post-operative somnolence plus bradypnea and an acute hypercapnic respiratory acidosis in a patient receiving opioids = opioid-induced respiratory depression. Immediate reversal with intravenous naloxone is life-saving.</p>
12. A 30-month-old boy is brought to the emergency department by his parents because of progressive difficulty bearing weight on his left leg. He now refuses to walk. His parents say that he used to walk with no problems. During the past week, he had two nosebleeds and required direct pressure for 20 minutes before they stopped. He was born at 38 weeks’ gestation after an uncomplicated pregnancy and vaginal delivery. He was 51 cm (20 in) long at birth and weighed 2495 g (5 lb 8 oz). There is no personal or family history of serious illness. Immunizations are up-to-date. On arrival, he appears listless. His temperature is 39.5°C (103.1°F). Examination shows several large blue ecchymoses over the back and abdomen. The patient cries vigorously when the physician attempts to move the patient's left lower extremity. The neck is supple. The lungs are clear to auscultation. The spleen tip is palpated. The remainder of the examination shows no abnormalities. Laboratory studies show:</br>Hemoglobin                      5.5 g/dL</br>Leukocyte count                 45,000/mm³</br>  Segmented neutrophils         6 percent</br>  Lymphocytes                   92 percent</br>  Blast cells                   2 percent</br>Platelet count                  40,000/mm³</br>Erythrocyte sedimentation rate  150 mm/h</br>AP and lateral x-rays of the pelvis and left lower extremity show a permeative osteolytic lesion at the diaphysis of the proximal femur. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Radiolabeled leukocyte scan</br>b. Technetium Tc 99m bone scan</br>c. MRI of the pelvis and lower extremities</br>d. Bone marrow biopsy</br>e. Open surgical drainage of the left hip joint</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer</br>D. Bone marrow biopsy</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis (How to read this stem on test day)</p></br><ol></br><li>Constitutional &amp; bleeding clues<br /></br>   • Fever 39.5 °C, listless, large ecchymoses, recurrent epistaxis → suggests bone-marrow failure (low platelets) rather than isolated orthopedic problem.  </li></br><li>CBC abnormalities<br /></br>   • Hb 5.5 g/dL (severe anemia)<br /></br>   • WBC 45 000/µL with 92 % lymphocytes, 6 % neutrophils, 2 % blasts → leukocytosis driven by lymphoid precursors; relative neutropenia.<br /></br>   • Platelets 40 000/µL → thrombocytopenia.<br /></br>   Pancytopenia + leukocytosis = marrow packed with malignant cells.  </li></br><li>Physical exam<br /></br>   • Splenic tip palpable → extramedullary hematopoiesis/infiltration.<br /></br>   • Focal bone pain over femur.  </li></br><li>Imaging<br /></br>   • “Permeative osteolytic lesion at the diaphysis” is classic for malignant marrow infiltration (leukemia &gt; Ewing).  </li></br><li>Age 30 mo (2½ yr) → peak incidence for acute lymphoblastic leukemia (ALL).</li></br></ol></br><p>Putting it together: child with fever, pancytopenia, bruising, bone pain/lytic lesion → ALL until proven otherwise.</p></br><p>Metacognitive framework:<br /></br>A. Any child 2-10 y with bone pain + cytopenias → think leukemia.<br /></br>B. Definitive diagnosis of any leukemia requires bone-marrow evaluation, not imaging.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning</p></br><p>Step-wise approach:<br /></br>1. Recognize red-flag triad: bone pain + unexplained cytopenias + systemic symptoms.<br /></br>2. Generate differential: leukemia &gt; Langerhans cell histiocytosis, metastatic neuroblastoma, osteomyelitis.<br /></br>3. Choose the single study that will confirm leukemia and guide therapy → bone marrow biopsy with flow cytometry.</p></br><p>Key takeaway: The NBME loves to remind you that imaging can suggest malignancy, but you MUST get tissue—marrow biopsy—for diagnosis and risk stratification of ALL.</p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real life):<br /></br>• Indication for bone-marrow aspirate/biopsy (suspected ALL).<br /></br>• Full blood count, peripheral smear description.<br /></br>• Physical findings (ecchymoses, splenomegaly, bone tenderness).<br /></br>• Informed consent for procedure.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Refresher</p></br><p>Acute lymphoblastic leukemia<br /></br>• Malignant proliferation of lymphoid precursors (B-cell lineage &gt; T-cell).<br /></br>• Crowds out normal hematopoiesis → anemia, thrombocytopenia, neutropenia.<br /></br>• Bone pain from periosteal stretching; lytic “permeative” lesions on x-ray.<br /></br>• Extramedullary spread: hepatosplenomegaly, lymphadenopathy, CNS.<br /></br>• Peak age 2-5 y; associated with Down syndrome after age 5.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────</br>Diagnostics &amp; Management Snapshot</p></br><p>Diagnostics after biopsy:<br /></br>• Flow cytometry (CD10, CD19, TdT for B-cell ALL).<br /></br>• Cytogenetics → prognosis (e.g., t(12;21) good, Philadelphia chromosome poor).</p></br><p>First-line therapy: multi-agent chemotherapy (vincristine + steroids + asparaginase ± anthracycline) with intrathecal methotrexate for CNS prophylaxis. Allogeneic stem-cell transplant reserved for high-risk or relapsed disease.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────</br>High-Yield Memory Aid</p></br><p>“ALL little kids have ALL”:<br /></br>A = Anemia &amp; bruising<br /></br>L = Leukocytosis (lymphoblasts)<br /></br>L = Limp / bone pain<br /></br>→ Confirm with bone Marrow Biopsy (“M” in “ALL” upside-down).</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────</br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong</p></br><p>A. Radiolabeled leukocyte scan<br /></br>   • Detects areas of neutrophil-mediated infection (e.g., osteomyelitis). Neutrophils are low here; presentation is not infectious.</p></br><p>B. Technetium-99m bone scan<br /></br>   • Sensitive for multifocal skeletal lesions but nonspecific; still requires biopsy. Adds radiation without changing management now.</p></br><p>C. MRI of the pelvis and lower extremities<br /></br>   • Excellent anatomic detail for osteomyelitis or solid tumors, but cannot diagnose leukemia; bone marrow biopsy is faster, cheaper, definitive.</p></br><p>E. Open surgical drainage of the left hip<br /></br>   • Indicated for septic arthritis (acute severe joint pain, hip held in flexion, elevated neutrophils). Pain here is in femoral shaft, no joint findings, neutrophils low.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────</br>Bottom Line</p></br><p>In a young child with bone pain, bruising, fever, and pancytopenia, think ALL. The immediate next step is bone marrow biopsy to confirm the diagnosis and begin life-saving therapy.</p>
13. A previously healthy 47-year-old man comes to the physician because of progressive difficulty swallowing during the past 3 months. He reports that both solid foods and liquids seem to "get hung up" behind the lower part of his breast bone. He has had to take smaller bites, chew better, and eat more slowly than he has in the past. He has not had any change in weight. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. A barium swallow is shown. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Esophageal diverticulum</br>b. Esophageal sphincter dysfunction</br>c. Foreign body ingestion</br>d. Gastric bezoar</br>e. Paraesophageal hernia</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>B. Esophageal sphincter dysfunction (classically achalasia)</p></br><p>───────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Key time course: 3-month history that is progressive but NOT acute → rules out most foreign-body events.<br /></br>• Dysphagia to both solids and liquids from the outset → points toward a motility problem rather than an anatomic obstruction (obstructions usually begin with solids only, then advance to liquids).<br /></br>• “Get hung up behind the lower part of his breast bone” = distal (lower) esophagus.<br /></br>• No weight loss, no regurgitation of undigested food, no chronic heartburn → weighs against malignancy or chronic GERD with stricture.<br /></br>• Barium image (not shown) on NBME displays the classic “bird’s-beak” tapering with proximal esophageal dilation → signature for achalasia/LES (lower esophageal sphincter) dysfunction.</p></br><p>Cognitive framework for similar questions<br /></br>1. What is the pattern of dysphagia?<br /></br>   • Solids only → think structural obstruction.<br /></br>   • Solids + liquids simultaneously → think motility (achalasia, diffuse esophageal spasm, scleroderma).<br /></br>2. Acute vs progressive vs intermittent.<br /></br>3. Weight loss or alarm symptoms = malignancy until proven otherwise.<br /></br>4. Imaging clue: bird’s beak = achalasia.</p></br><p>───────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Achalasia results from degeneration of inhibitory ganglion cells in Auerbach’s (myenteric) plexus. Loss of nitric oxide (NO)– and VIP-mediated relaxation keeps the LES tonically contracted and abolishes normal peristalsis. Consequently:<br /></br>• Food cannot pass easily into the stomach → dysphagia to solids and liquids.<br /></br>• Proximal esophagus dilates and eventually forms the classic tapering on barium study.<br /></br>Take-home: “Simultaneous dysphagia + bird’s beak = LES dysfunction/achalasia.”</p></br><p>Documentation pearls if this were real:<br /></br>• Characterize dysphagia (solids vs liquids), onset, progression.<br /></br>• Weight change, aspiration, chest pain, regurgitation.<br /></br>• Plan: order esophageal manometry (gold standard), consider EGD to rule out pseudoachalasia (cancer), discuss pneumatic dilation vs Heller myotomy vs POEM.</p></br><p>───────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Etiology: idiopathic neuronal degeneration; can be secondary to Chagas disease (Trypanosoma cruzi) or malignancy (pseudoachalasia).<br /></br>• LES fails to relax, resting pressure high; aperistalsis of distal two-thirds of esophagus.<br /></br>• Symptoms: progressive dysphagia to solids &amp; liquids, chest pain, regurgitation, risk of aspiration and esophageal SCC.</p></br><p>───────────────────</br>Diagnostic &amp; Treatment Snapshot<br /></br>1. Initial test: barium swallow (bird’s-beak).<br /></br>2. Confirmatory: high-resolution esophageal manometry (absent peristalsis, elevated LES pressure).<br /></br>3. Work-up for pseudoachalasia: upper endoscopy, especially &gt;60 y or rapid weight loss.<br /></br>4. Treatment options<br /></br>   • Pneumatic balloon dilation of LES.<br /></br>   • Surgical Heller myotomy ± partial fundoplication (definitive).<br /></br>   • Per-oral endoscopic myotomy (POEM).<br /></br>   • Temporary/poor-surgical-candidate: botulinum toxin injection, nitrates, calcium-channel blockers.</p></br><p>───────────────────</br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“AAA” mnemonic for Achalasia<br /></br>A = Aperistalsis<br /></br>A = Absent relaxation of LES<br /></br>A = (bird’s) Apex beak on barium</p></br><p>Or picture a parrot (bird’s beak) perched on the LES that refuses to let food pass.</p></br><p>───────────────────</br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong  </p></br><p>a. Esophageal diverticulum (e.g., Zenker): Presents with regurgitation of undigested food, halitosis, possible cervical mass; barium shows out-pouching, not symmetric distal tapering. Dysphagia often for solids first, liquids later.</p></br><p>c. Foreign body ingestion: Acute onset in hours to days, usually solids, often with odynophagia or a clear history (bones, coins). Barium would reveal a localized filling defect, not bird’s-beak.</p></br><p>d. Gastric bezoar: Mechanical obstruction in the stomach, leads to early satiety, nausea/vomiting, not classic esophageal dysphagia; imaging would show an intragastric mass.</p></br><p>e. Paraesophageal hernia (type II hiatal hernia): Causes post-prandial fullness, chest pain, or rarely obstruction; barium shows the gastric fundus alongside the esophagus above the diaphragm—not a smooth distal esophageal taper.</p></br><p>───────────────────</br>Core Takeaway<br /></br>Progressive dysphagia to both solids and liquids + bird’s-beak barium swallow = achalasia due to lower esophageal sphincter dysfunction.</p>
14. A 42-year-old woman comes to the physician because of a 2-week history of nonradiating low back pain that she first noticed after doing some heavy lifting at work. She has not had numbness or weakness in her lower extremities or any bowel or bladder dysfunction. At the age of 12 years, she received the diagnosis of scoliosis; her condition was evaluated with periodic x-rays but did not require the use of a brace or operative repair. On examination, straight-leg raising is negative. Muscle strength is normal, and sensation is intact. Pedal pulses are 2+. Plain x-rays of the spine show a 15-degree curvature of the thoracic spine and mild degenerative changes of the lumbosacral spine. Which of the following is the most likely outcome of this patient's scoliosis?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Continued progression of the curvature</br>b. Development of thoracic back pain</br>c. Development of dyspnea on exertion</br>d. Development of lower extremity paresthesias</br>e. No symptoms are likely to develop</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>E. No symptoms are likely to develop  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li>Key clues<br /></br>   • “42-year-old woman” – Skeletal maturity reached long ago.<br /></br>   • Scoliosis diagnosed at age 12, never braced or operated.<br /></br>   • Current spinal films: 15-degree thoracic curve (mild) + mild lumbar DJD.<br /></br>   • Current complaint is recent mechanical low-back strain; neurologic exam normal.  </li></br><li>Pertinent positives/negatives<br /></br>   • Curve &lt;20° ➜ classified as mild.<br /></br>   • No neurologic deficits, no pulmonary complaints.<br /></br>   • Straight-leg raise negative, normal strength/sensation ➜ pain is muscular.  </li></br><li>Pattern to recognize<br /></br>   • Adult with a mild idiopathic curve (&lt;30° at skeletal maturity) rarely develops progression or systemic complications after growth plates close.  </li></br></ol></br><p>Metacognitive tip: In any scoliosis stem, immediately note the Cobb angle, patient age (before or after skeletal maturity), and presence/absence of symptoms (pulmonary, neurologic, cosmetic). That triad dictates prognosis and management.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Stepwise thought process:<br /></br>1. Angle at skeletal maturity? &lt;30°<br /></br>2. Current age? Adult → curve behavior now dominated by degenerative changes, not idiopathic progression.<br /></br>3. Expected natural history? Stable; most remain asymptomatic.<br /></br>4. Therefore the scoliosis itself is very unlikely to cause future pain, dyspnea, neurologic compromise, or continued progression.  </p></br><p>Take-home message: Mild idiopathic scoliosis that is &lt;30° at the end of growth almost never progresses and rarely causes symptoms later in life.  </p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real patient): “Thoracic Cobb angle 15°, stable since adolescence; no pulmonary or neurologic sequelae; reassure, advise standard back-strain care.”  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• Idiopathic scoliosis progression risk is strongly angle-dependent:<br /></br>  – &lt;30° at skeletal maturity: &lt;10% will increase ≥5° over lifetime.<br /></br>  – 30–50°: slow progression (≈0.5–1°/yr) possible.<br /></br>  – &gt;50°: high likelihood of progression, potential restrictive lung disease if thoracic.<br /></br>• Pulmonary compromise occurs when thoracic curve &gt;70–80° because of rib cage deformity → ↓FVC/FEV1.<br /></br>• Neurologic deficits (radiculopathy, spinal stenosis) relate to large curves or concomitant degenerative canal narrowing, not mild idiopathic curves.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Notes  </p></br><p>• No treatment indicated for a stable 15° curve in an adult.<br /></br>• Manage current low-back strain conservatively: NSAIDs, activity modification, core-strengthening exercise—unrelated to scoliosis.<br /></br>• Repeat imaging not routinely required unless new concerning symptoms arise.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“30-50-80 rule” for idiopathic scoliosis:<br /></br>• &lt;30° after growth = Benign (B for ‘below 30, benign’).<br /></br>• &gt;50° = Progress (P for ‘past 50, progresses’).<br /></br>• &gt;80° = Pulmonary (P for ‘past 80, pulmonary problems’).  </p></br><p>Visual: Picture a speedometer—green zone up to 30 (safe), yellow 30–50 (watch), red above 80 (lungs compressed).  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>a. Continued progression of the curvature<br /></br>   – Adult curves &lt;30° are essentially stable; progression risk minimal unless initial curve ≥50°.  </p></br><p>b. Development of thoracic back pain<br /></br>   – Chronic pain correlates with large thoracic or lumbar curves and degenerative rotation &gt;30°. A 15° curve is unlikely to be the pain source.  </p></br><p>c. Development of dyspnea on exertion<br /></br>   – Restrictive lung disease occurs when thoracic curve &gt;70–80°; far greater than 15°.  </p></br><p>d. Development of lower extremity paresthesias<br /></br>   – Neurologic symptoms arise from severe curves, congenital deformities, or coexisting spinal stenosis, none of which are present here.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line  </p></br><p>A mild (&lt;30°) idiopathic scoliosis that reached skeletal maturity without progression virtually always remains asymptomatic in adulthood; reassure the patient.</p>
15. An otherwise healthy 21-year-old woman is admitted to the hospital because of a ruptured ectopic pregnancy. In the emergency department, her pulse was 120/min, respirations were 20/min, and blood pressure was 90/70 mm Hg. After intravenous administration of 2 L of 0.9 percent saline, her blood pressure increases to 110/75 mm Hg. In the operating room, her hematocrit is 25 percent. Transfusion with packed red blood cells is begun, and general anesthesia is administered. As the skin is prepped for the operation, her blood pressure decreases to 65/30 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on an FIO2 of 0.40 shows an oxygen saturation of 75 percent. There is acute increased airway resistance to mechanical ventilation.
