The patient's blood pressure does not respond to 100 percent oxygen and 0.9 percent saline boluses. Two minutes later, her systolic blood pressure is 50 mm Hg. Her temperature is 38.2°C (100.8°F). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Amniotic fluid embolism</br>b. Anaphylactic transfusion reaction</br>c. Malignant hyperthermia</br>d. Pelvic hemorrhage</br>e. Pulmonary aspiration</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>B. Anaphylactic transfusion reaction  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Timeline: Patient is hemodynamically acceptable after resuscitation, then acutely crashes within minutes of starting packed red blood cells (PRBCs).<br /></br>• Key findings:<br /></br>  – Sudden, profound hypotension (110/75 → 65/30 → 50 mm Hg).<br /></br>  – Abrupt hypoxemia (SpO₂ 75% on FiO₂ 0.40) and “acute increased airway resistance” (bronchospasm).<br /></br>  – Modest fever 38.2 °C that begins almost immediately.<br /></br>• Pertinent negatives: No uterine manipulation/labor (rules against amniotic fluid embolism); no generalized muscle rigidity or rapidly rising end-tidal CO₂ (argues against malignant hyperthermia); no evidence of new bleeding or large blood loss during prep (pelvic hemorrhage); no gastric contents in endotracheal tube (aspiration).<br /></br>Metacognitive tip: Whenever a patient decompensates within seconds-minutes of beginning a blood product, think of an acute transfusion reaction first. Look for the dyad of hypotension + respiratory compromise.</p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Stepwise thought process:<br /></br>1. Temporal relationship: Reaction occurs immediately after transfusion started.<br /></br>2. Cardiopulmonary collapse: Severe bronchospasm → ↑airway resistance → hypoxia; systemic vasodilation → hypotension.<br /></br>3. Fits classic IgA-mediated anaphylaxis to donor plasma proteins in PRBCs.  </p></br><p>Core takeaway: Acute anaphylactic transfusion reactions present within minutes of exposure and are dominated by hypotension, bronchospasm, and hypoxemia. They differ from hemolytic reactions (fever, flank pain, hemoglobinuria) and febrile non-hemolytic reactions (fever/chills, no shock).  </p></br><p>Documentation must include: timing of reaction, type/lot of blood product, vital signs, airway findings, treatments given (epinephrine, fluids), and notification of the blood bank.</p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Etiology: IgE- or IgG-mediated reaction to donor plasma proteins—classically patients with selective IgA deficiency who possess anti-IgA antibodies.<br /></br>• Mechanism: Massive mast-cell/basophil degranulation → histamine, leukotrienes, prostaglandins → systemic vasodilation, capillary leak, bronchoconstriction.<br /></br>• Clinical picture: Seconds-minutes after transfusion start: hypotension, respiratory distress/bronchospasm, urticaria/angioedema (often occult under drapes), wheezing, possible shock. Fever may be low-grade or absent initially.</p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostics<br /></br>Immediate management (often exam-tested):<br /></br>1. STOP the transfusion; keep IV line open with normal saline.<br /></br>2. IM epinephrine 0.3–0.5 mg (1:1,000) or IV push if arrest.<br /></br>3. Airway support with 100% O₂, possible intubation/vent adjustments.<br /></br>4. Crystalloid boluses; adjuncts: antihistamines (diphenhydramine), corticosteroids.<br /></br>5. Send blood product and patient blood for work-up; future transfusions require washed RBCs or IgA-deficient products.</p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“ABCs of Acute Anaphylaxis after Blood”:<br /></br>A – Airway tight (bronchospasm)<br /></br>B – Blood pressure crashes<br /></br>C – Count seconds (immediate onset)  </p></br><p>Mnemonic for transfusion reactions by onset:<br /></br>• A – Anaphylactic (Immediately)<br /></br>• B – Bacterial contamination (Minutes-hours)<br /></br>• C – Complement/ABO-mediated hemolysis (Within 1 hr)<br /></br>• D – Delayed hemolytic (Days)<br /></br>• E – EBV/CMV transfusion-associated infection (Weeks)</p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>a. Amniotic fluid embolism – Occurs during labor or immediately postpartum; features DIC and seizures, not linked to transfusion timing.<br /></br>c. Malignant hyperthermia – Develops shortly after succinylcholine/volatile anesthetic; marked hypercarbia, muscle rigidity, rapidly rising temperature (&gt;40 °C). Bronchospasm is not dominant feature.<br /></br>d. Pelvic hemorrhage – Would cause hypotension but NOT sudden airway resistance or hypoxemia; vital signs would improve with aggressive fluids/blood, not worsen with them.<br /></br>e. Pulmonary aspiration – Causes hypoxemia and wheezing but usually during induction/intubation; hypotension is secondary (usually mild) and fever develops hours later, not within minutes.</p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line<br /></br>A dramatic drop in blood pressure plus bronchospasm and hypoxemia occurring within minutes of starting a transfusion = anaphylactic transfusion reaction until proven otherwise.</p>
16. A 72-year-old man comes to the emergency department because of a 36-hour history of severe pain in his right thigh. He rates his pain as an 8 on a 10-point scale and describes it as deep, dull, and achy. The pain increases when he moves or bears weight on the right leg. Over-the-counter ibuprofen decreased the pain to a 6 on a 10-point scale. The patient has stage IV adenocarcinoma of the lung, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, hypertension, peptic ulcer disease, and coronary artery disease. His medications are metoprolol and inhaled albuterol-ipratropium. On arrival, an intravenous bolus of morphine (2 mg) is administered. Thirty minutes later, the patient rates the pain as a 4. His temperature is 36.9 °C (98.4 °F), pulse is 72/min, respirations are 22/min, and blood pressure is 162/68 mm Hg. Bilateral end-expiratory wheezes are heard. There is tenderness to palpation over the anterior aspect of the right midthigh. His leukocyte count is 6800/mm3 with a normal differential. An x-ray of the right femur shows a midshaft lytic lesion. In addition to oral morphine, which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Physical therapy  </br>b. Acupuncture  </br>c. Gabapentin therapy  </br>d. Tramadol therapy  </br>e. Radiation therapy</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>e. Radiation therapy  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues<br /></br>• Known stage IV lung adenocarcinoma → high likelihood of bone metastasis<br /></br>• Severe, focal, “deep, dull, achy” pain in anterior midthigh that worsens with weight-bearing → classic description of metastatic bone pain<br /></br>• Plain film: solitary, mid-shaft lytic lesion of the femur → confirms metastatic deposit and impending pathologic fracture risk<br /></br>• Only partial relief with NSAID and IV morphine → analgesics alone are insufficient<br /></br>Take-home pattern: “Cancer + focal bone metastasis → think external-beam radiation for pain control (+ fracture prophylaxis).”  </p></br><p>Metacognitive approach<br /></br>1. Identify if pain is nociceptive, neuropathic, or visceral. (“Deep, dull, worsens with movement” = nociceptive bone pain.)<br /></br>2. Ask: Is the cause potentially reversible or modifiable (e.g., a mass that can be radiated, debulked, or stabilized)?<br /></br>3. When focal bone metastasis is present → external-beam radiation is first-line adjunct to opioids for durable pain relief.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Why radiation now?<br /></br>• Provides rapid (within days) and durable pain relief in 60-80 % of patients with localized bone mets.<br /></br>• Decreases tumor burden → less mechanical stress on periosteum, fewer micro-fractures.<br /></br>• Lowers risk of catastrophic pathologic fracture in weight-bearing long bone.<br /></br>• Non-invasive, outpatient, minimal systemic side-effects—ideal for frail, comorbid patient.<br /></br>Documentation (real life): “Discussed options for palliation of metastatic femoral lesion. Patient elects external-beam radiotherapy for pain control and fracture prophylaxis. Radiation oncology consult placed.”  </p></br><p>Core takeaway<br /></br>Localized painful bone metastasis warrants palliative external-beam radiation in addition to systemic and oral analgesics.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (high-yield)  </p></br><p>Lung cancer → hematogenous spread to bone → tumor secretes PTH-rP, IL-1, TNF-α → osteoclast activation → osteolytic lesions.<br /></br>Pain mechanism: periosteal stretch, inflammatory cytokines, small-fiber nerve irritation, micro-fractures.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Management Algorithm for Bone Mets Pain (Step 2 level)  </p></br><ol></br><li>Localized lesion<br /></br>   • External-beam radiation (± surgical stabilization if impending fracture).  </li></br><li>Multifocal/ diffuse lesions<br /></br>   • Systemic therapy: bisphosphonates (zoledronic acid, pamidronate) or denosumab.<br /></br>   • Continue opioid ladder (NSAID → weak opioid → strong opioid ± adjuvants).  </li></br></ol></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tip  </p></br><p>“Bone Mets? ZAP!”<br /></br>Z = Zoledronic acid (for diffuse)<br /></br>A = Analgesics<br /></br>P = Photon (external-beam) radiation for focal pain  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong  </p></br><p>a. Physical therapy<br /></br>   • Mobilization without first stabilizing/ shrinking lesion risks pathologic fracture; won’t provide rapid pain relief.  </p></br><p>b. Acupuncture<br /></br>   • Limited evidence for severe metastatic pain; not a first-line evidence-based intervention.  </p></br><p>c. Gabapentin therapy<br /></br>   • Useful for neuropathic pain (e.g., radiculopathy, post-herpetic neuralgia), not nociceptive bone pain.  </p></br><p>d. Tramadol therapy<br /></br>   • Weak μ-agonist/ SNRI. Patient already requires morphine (strong opioid); tramadol offers less analgesic potency and does not address underlying lesion.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line<br /></br>In a patient with cancer-related focal bone pain and a radiographic lytic lesion, the most appropriate next step—beyond ongoing opioid therapy—is palliative external-beam radiation therapy to control pain and prevent fracture.</p>
17. A 57-year-old nulligravid woman comes to the physician for a routine health maintenance examination. Menses occur at irregular 28- to 42-day intervals. She has not had hot flashes. She has no history of serious illness or abnormal Pap smears, and she takes no medications. She is 157 cm (5 ft 2 in) tall and weighs 102 kg (225 lb); BMI is 41 kg/m2. Physical examination, including pelvic examination, shows no abnormalities. Her serum fasting glucose concentration is 140 mg/dL. During the next 10 years, this patient is at increased risk for which of the following conditions?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Cervical cancer</br>b. Endometrial cancer</br>c. Hyperthyroidism</br>d. Osteoporosis</br>e. Ovarian cancer</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>b. Endometrial cancer  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Key clues<br /></br>  – 57-year-old, perimenopausal (irregular 28- to 42-day cycles)<br /></br>  – Obesity (BMI 41 kg/m²)<br /></br>  – Nulligravid<br /></br>  – Fasting glucose 140 mg/dL → likely type 2 diabetes mellitus  </p></br><p>• Why they matter<br /></br>  1. Obesity → ↑ peripheral aromatization of androgens to estrone in adipose tissue → chronic, unopposed estrogen stimulation of the endometrium.<br /></br>  2. Anovulatory, irregular menses (common in obesity &amp; perimenopause) → progesterone is not produced, further increasing “unopposed” estrogen exposure.<br /></br>  3. Nulliparity &amp; diabetes are additional, independent risk factors for endometrial carcinoma.  </p></br><p>Cognitive framework for future questions<br /></br>“Any perimenopausal or postmenopausal woman with obesity ± diabetes ± chronic anovulation = think unopposed estrogen → endometrial hyperplasia → carcinoma.”</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Step-wise linkage to the answer:<br /></br>1. Identify hormonal milieu: Estrogen excess without progesterone.<br /></br>2. Recognize risk factors that create that milieu (obesity, anovulation, diabetes, nulliparity).<br /></br>3. Recall the malignancy classically driven by chronic estrogen: endometrial adenocarcinoma.<br /></br>Core takeaway: Unopposed estrogen is the single most important modifiable risk factor for type I (endometrioid) endometrial cancer.</p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real clinic)<br /></br>• Counsel patient on weight loss, glucose control, and report any abnormal uterine bleeding.<br /></br>• Consider endometrial sampling for post-menopausal bleeding; discuss chemoprophylaxis (e.g., combined OCP or progestin IUD) if still premenopausal and bleeding irregularly.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>Estrogen → stimulates endometrial proliferation. Progesterone → counterbalances by promoting secretory transformation and scheduled shedding.<br /></br>Chronic exposure to estrogen without progesterone (obesity, PCOS, anovulation, exogenous estrogen, early menarche/late menopause, nulliparity) → endometrial hyperplasia → atypia → carcinoma.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Considerations<br /></br>• Screening: No routine population screening; evaluation is prompted by post-menopausal or abnormal bleeding.<br /></br>• Diagnostic: Transvaginal US (endometrial stripe ≥4 mm post-menopause) → endometrial biopsy is definitive.<br /></br>• Prevention: Weight reduction, diabetic control, cyclic or continuous progestin (e.g., levonorgestrel IUD) in high-risk anovulatory women.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>High-Yield Memory Aids<br /></br>“Obese, Old, Ovulation-less, Nulliparous, and Diabetic”<br /></br>→ O-O-O-N-D = risk factors for Endometrial cancer.</p></br><p>Mnemonic “Estrogen EXCESS Endometrium EXpands (hyperplasia → cancer).”</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Why the Other Options Are Wrong  </p></br><p>a. Cervical cancer<br /></br>• Driven by persistent high-risk HPV infection (16,18); risk ↑ with multiple sexual partners, smoking, immunosuppression—not obesity/diabetes. Regular Pap smears normal in this patient.</p></br><p>c. Hyperthyroidism<br /></br>• Obesity and type 2 DM are not risk factors; hyperthyroid patients usually present with weight loss.</p></br><p>d. Osteoporosis<br /></br>• Low BMI, smoking, premature menopause, glucocorticoids → risk. High BMI is actually protective due to extra estrogen from adipose tissue.</p></br><p>e. Ovarian cancer<br /></br>• Nulliparity is a risk, but the strongest factors are family history (BRCA, Lynch), infertility drugs, endometriosis. Obesity and diabetes have weaker/inconsistent associations. Endometrial cancer risk is far higher for this patient’s profile.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Key Take-home Point<br /></br>In perimenopausal or postmenopausal women, obesity + anovulation + diabetes + nulliparity = classic constellation for unopposed estrogen and markedly ↑ risk of endometrial carcinoma.</p>
18. During the past 2 weeks, a 17-year-old boy with a 5-year history of type 1 diabetes mellitus has had episodes of severe hypoglycemia, confusion, and disorientation after playing basketball for 30 minutes after school. He has been advised to consume additional carbohydrates before playing but refuses because of his concern about high blood sugar. He is being treated with 20 U of NPH insulin and 10 U of insulin lispro before breakfast and 15 U of NPH insulin and 8 U of insulin lispro before dinner. Until recently, his blood glucose concentrations have been in the range of 100 mg/dL to 160 mg/dL before each meal. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: F</br></br>a. Decrease physical activity  </br>b. Administer glucagon  </br>c. Administer glyburide  </br>d. Administer metformin  </br>e. Decrease dose of NPH insulin in the evening  </br>f. Decrease dose of NPH insulin in the morning  </br>g. Decrease dose of insulin lispro in the evening  </br>h. Decrease dose of insulin lispro in the morning</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>f. Decrease dose of NPH insulin in the morning  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Patient profile: 17-year-old with type 1 diabetes mellitus (T1DM) on a conventional twice-daily split-mix regimen (rapid + NPH at breakfast and dinner).<br /></br>• Timing of hypoglycemia: “after playing basketball for 30 minutes after school” → mid- to late-afternoon.<br /></br>• Pertinent negatives: Glucose 100–160 mg/dL before each meal (good overall control), so the problem is not chronic over-insulinization.<br /></br>• Key pharmacology clue: Which insulin given at breakfast is still active and PEAKING in the mid-afternoon? → NPH (intermediate-acting).  </p></br><p>Metacognitive framework:<br /></br>1. Pinpoint WHEN hypoglycemia occurs.<br /></br>2. Match that clock time to the PEAK of any exogenous insulin dose.<br /></br>3. Adjust (decrease or shift) that particular insulin rather than restrict exercise or add carbs if the patient refuses.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>• Morning lispro: peaks in 30–90 min, gone by lunchtime → not responsible.<br /></br>• Morning NPH: onset 1–2 h, PEAK 4–8 h, duration 12–16 h → peak around 2–4 pm, exactly when he plays basketball → culprit.<br /></br>• Exercise promotes non-insulin-dependent glucose uptake into skeletal muscle, further lowering glucose.<br /></br>• Therefore, lowering the morning NPH dose reduces the peak insulin level during afternoon activity, preventing exercise-related hypoglycemia without compromising fasting or pre-dinner control.  </p></br><p>Take-home: Adjust the insulin dose whose PEAK coincides with the hypoglycemic window.  </p></br><p>Documentation (real-life): Plan to reduce AM NPH by ~10–20%, educate patient on monitoring, re-check SMBG (self-monitoring of blood glucose) before and after activity.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• NPH = Neutral Protamine Hagedorn, an intermediate-acting insulin.<br /></br>  – Onset 1-2 h, peak 4–8 h, duration 12–16 h (“Noon Peak Hours” mnemonic).<br /></br>• Exercise → ↑ GLUT-4 translocation in muscle independent of insulin → ↑ glucose uptake → additive with circulating insulin → hypoglycemia.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>a. Decrease physical activity: Unnecessary; exercise is beneficial, especially in teens. Better to adjust insulin.<br /></br>b. Administer glucagon: Emergency treatment for <em>acute</em> severe hypoglycemia; does not prevent recurrent episodes.<br /></br>c. Glyburide (sulfonylurea) &amp; d. Metformin: Oral agents for type 2 DM; ineffective and inappropriate in absolute insulin deficiency (T1DM).<br /></br>e. Decrease evening NPH: Would lower early-morning or nocturnal insulin levels, not afternoon peaks.<br /></br>g. Decrease evening lispro: Peaks around dinner/early evening, unrelated to afternoon sports.<br /></br>h. Decrease morning lispro: Would lower late-morning insulin effect; hypoglycemia occurs hours later.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“INPH = Intermediate, Noon Peak Hours.”<br /></br>Visualize a clock: NPH dose at 8 am → peak hand sweeps to ~2 pm. If the patient exercises then, think “dial down the NPH.”  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Core Concept<br /></br>Match insulin pharmacokinetics to the patient’s daily schedule; prevent exercise-induced hypoglycemia by lowering the insulin that peaks during the activity—in this case, the morning NPH.</p>
19. An 8-month-old boy is brought to the physician because his parents are concerned about his development. They report that he had learned to stand with support at the age of 4 months but has been unable to stand with or without support during the past 2 weeks. During this period, he has cried when held around his chest. His parents add that he has not grown much during the past 2 months. He was born at term following an uncomplicated pregnancy and delivery. He receives no medications. His diet consists of breast milk, rice cereal, and pureed fruits. His family lives in a cold climate. At his last examination 2 months ago, he was at the 25th percentile for length and weight. Today, he is at the 3rd percentile for length and 10th percentile for weight. He is alert and mildly irritable. Vital signs are within normal limits. Examination shows a dark complexion. Cardiopulmonary examination shows no abnormalities. The abdomen is soft. The wrists are widened and tender. He screams when picked up and when placed in a standing position. X-rays of the right wrist and left knee are shown. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Child abuse</br>b. Metaphyseal dysplasia</br>c. Osteogenesis imperfecta</br>d. Osteopetrosis</br>e. Rickets</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>E. Rickets (nutritional vitamin D deficiency)</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis</br>1. Age/Setting<br /></br>   • 8-month-old, exclusively breast-fed + limited solid foods<br /></br>   • Lives in a cold climate (↓UVB exposure)<br /></br>   • Dark complexion (↑melanin blocks UVB)<br /></br>   → Three classic risk factors for vitamin D deficiency.</p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Presenting complaints<br /></br>   • Regression of motor milestone: no longer able to stand, cries when lifted → bone pain.<br /></br>   • Growth deceleration: fell from 25th %ile to 3rd %ile length in 2 mo.</p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Physical exam clues<br /></br>   • Widened, tender wrists (active physes).<br /></br>   • Pain with weight-bearing.</p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Radiographs (not shown here, but described)<br /></br>   • Characteristic wrist &amp; knee findings in rickets: metaphyseal cupping, fraying, and widening of the growth plate.</p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>Pertinent negatives: normal cardiopulmonary exam, no facial bruises or other signs of abuse, no blue sclerae, no dense “marble” bones, all arguing against other choices.</p></br><p>Metacognitive tip: When an infant with exclusive breast-feeding (or minimal vit D–fortified formula) + dark skin + little sun shows bone pain and growth failure, think “nutritional rickets” first; look for wrist/knee metaphyseal changes.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (Why answer E is correct)</p></br><p>Step-wise logic<br /></br>1. Risk factors → high pre-test probability of vit D deficiency.<br /></br>2. Vit D deficiency → ↓1,25-(OH)₂-vit D → ↓Ca²⁺/PO₄⁻ absorption → defective mineralization at growth plate.<br /></br>3. Unmineralized osteoid at physes → widened, cupped metaphyses, bone pain, growth delay, rachitic rosary, bowed legs.<br /></br>4. All findings present in vignette/X-ray → confirms rickets.</p></br><p>Core takeaway: Nutritional vitamin D deficiency in infants causes rickets; hallmark is metaphyseal cupping/fraying on X-ray plus clinical bone pain &amp; growth impairment.</p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real patient): record dietary history, sun exposure, growth chart, exam of wrists/knees/costochondral junctions, radiographic findings, plan for vit D + Ca supplementation and nutritional counseling.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review</p></br><p>Etiology: ↓vit D intake/synthesis → ↓Ca²⁺/PO₄⁻ → secondary hyperparathyroidism → ↑bone resorption yet inadequate mineralization of new osteoid.</p></br><p>Classic findings<br /></br>• Skeletal pain/irritability<br /></br>• Frontal bossing, craniotabes<br /></br>• Wrist/ankle widening, genu varum (after ambulation)<br /></br>• Rachitic rosary (costochondral swelling)<br /></br>Labs: ↓25-OH-vit D, ↓/nl Ca²⁺, ↓PO₄⁻, ↑PTH, ↑ALP.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Work-up</p></br><ol></br><li>Confirmatory labs (for completeness): serum 25-OH-vit D (&lt;20 ng/mL), Ca²⁺, PO₄⁻, ALP, PTH.  </li></br><li>Management<br /></br>   • Vitamin D (cholecalciferol – D₃) 2000 IU daily or 50 000 IU weekly for 6–12 weeks, then maintenance 400–600 IU/day.<br /></br>   • Ensure adequate calcium (500–1000 mg/day).<br /></br>   • Encourage safe sun exposure; continue breastfeeding but add vitamin-D–fortified formula/foods.  </li></br><li>Prevention: all breast-fed infants ≥400 IU/day vit D from birth.</li></br></ol></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips</p></br><p>“Dark, Dairy-less, and Daylight-less → D-deficient → Rickets.”<br /></br>Wrist mnemonic “CUP” for X-ray: Cupping, fraying, and widened Physes.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Why the Other Answers Are Wrong</p></br><p>A. Child abuse<br /></br>• Would see fractures of varying ages, corner/bucket-handle fractures, bruises; X-ray usually shows <em>complete</em> cortical breaks, not smooth metaphyseal cupping. Growth failure &amp; bilateral symmetric wrist widening are atypical.</p></br><p>B. Metaphyseal dysplasia (Schmid type)<br /></br>• Rare genetic cartilage disorder; short-limbed dwarfism, coxa vara; X-ray shows irregular sclerotic metaphyses without classic cupping/fraying. No nutritional risk factors.</p></br><p>C. Osteogenesis imperfecta<br /></br>• Defective type I collagen → recurrent fractures with minimal trauma, blue sclerae, dentinogenesis imperfecta; X-ray shows generalized osteopenia, not widened physes.</p></br><p>D. Osteopetrosis<br /></br>• Defective osteoclast resorption → <em>increased</em> bone density (“marble bone”), pathologic fractures, pancytopenia from marrow failure. X-ray opposite of rickets (bones appear too dense, not under-mineralized).</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>High-Yield Take-Home Message</p></br><p>In an exclusively breast-fed infant with dark skin and limited sun, bone pain + wrist metaphyseal cupping equals nutritional rickets—treat and prevent with vitamin D supplementation.</p>
20. A 47-year-old man comes to the emergency department because of fever during the past 8 hours. Two days ago, he sustained a small puncture wound to his lower abdomen while repairing a fence in a barnyard. He did not seek treatment at that time. He has a 10-year history of type 2 diabetes mellitus treated with glargine insulin. He appears lethargic. His temperature is 40°C (104°F), pulse is 130/min, and blood pressure is 90/60 mm Hg. Abdominal examination shows an exquisitely tender puncture wound over the right lower quadrant and surrounding erythema and warmth. During the next hour, the physician notices a 3-cm extension of the erythema. In addition to administration of intravenous fluids and broad-spectrum antibiotics, which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Hyperbaric oxygen therapy</br>b. Tetanus immune globulin therapy</br>c. Negative pressure wound therapy</br>d. Incision and drainage with local anesthesia</br>e. Surgical debridement under general anesthesia</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>E. Surgical debridement under general anesthesia  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis (How to Recognize the Tested Diagnosis)<br /></br>• “Puncture wound … barnyard” → highly contaminated injury (mixed aerobes &amp; anaerobes).<br /></br>• Rapid onset of severe pain, “exquisitely tender,” spreading erythema that enlarges 3 cm within 1 hour → pain out of proportion + rapid extension = NECROTIZING SOFT-TISSUE INFECTION (necrotizing fasciitis).<br /></br>• Systemic toxicity: temp 40 °C, HR 130, BP 90/60 → septic shock picture.<br /></br>• Major risk factor: diabetes mellitus (impaired neutrophil function &amp; vascular compromise).  </p></br><p>Metacognitive approach<br /></br>1. Any soft-tissue infection + severe pain + systemic toxicity + very rapid progression → think nec fas.<br /></br>2. Nec fas is a “don’t wait” diagnosis: treatment is surgical exploration/debridement now; imaging/labs/adjuncts may help but must not delay the OR call.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (Step-by-Step)<br /></br>1. Suspect necrotizing fasciitis clinically.<br /></br>2. Start aggressive IV fluids + broad-spectrum antibiotics (already done).<br /></br>3. Definitive, time-critical management = early, wide surgical debridement under general anesthesia.<br /></br>   • Aim: excise all necrotic fascia/subcutaneous tissue, obtain deep cultures, and decompress compartments.<br /></br>   • Multiple return trips to the OR are often needed.  </p></br><p>Key takeaway: “When you see nec fas, cut fast.”  </p></br><p>Documentation pearls for the real world<br /></br>• Rapidly progressive soft-tissue infection with systemic signs.<br /></br>• Informed consent for emergent exploration &amp; possible serial debridements.<br /></br>• Hemodynamic status, antibiotic regimen, blood cultures sent.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (High Yield)<br /></br>• Polymicrobial (type I) or group A strep/staph (type II); clostridial myonecrosis variant = gas gangrene.<br /></br>• Bacterial toxins → vascular occlusion → ischemia → fulminant tissue necrosis &amp; systemic inflammatory response → shock.<br /></br>• Risk factors: diabetes, peripheral vascular disease, obesity, chronic kidney disease, trauma, surgery.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment Algorithm<br /></br>1. Hemodynamic resuscitation (crystalloid ± vasopressors).<br /></br>2. Empiric IV antibiotics:<br /></br>   – Piperacillin-tazobactam + vancomycin + clindamycin (to inhibit toxin production).<br /></br>3. Emergent surgical debridement (choice E).<br /></br>4. Repeat debridements q24–48 h until healthy viable tissue.<br /></br>5. Adjuncts (HBOT, negative-pressure dressings) only after source control is achieved.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>• “FORCE” mnemonic for nec fas red flags:<br /></br>  F – Fever/high temp<br /></br>  O – Out of proportion pain<br /></br>  R – Rapid progression<br /></br>  C – Crepitus/skin discoloration<br /></br>  E – Erythema extending (FAST)<br /></br>Treatment = “FAST” → Fasciotomy And Surgical debridement Today.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>A. Hyperbaric oxygen therapy<br /></br>   • Experimental adjunct to inhibit anaerobes &amp; improve wound oxygenation.<br /></br>   • Never first-line and should never delay debridement; limited evidence.  </p></br><p>B. Tetanus immune globulin therapy<br /></br>   • Given for dirty wounds with uncertain immunization but does NOT treat nec fas or sepsis. Does nothing for the rapidly progressive infection described.  </p></br><p>C. Negative pressure wound therapy (vacuum-assisted closure)<br /></br>   • Used for chronic, clean wounds after adequate debridement; inappropriate before infected/necrotic tissue is excised.  </p></br><p>D. Incision and drainage with local anesthesia<br /></br>   • Appropriate for a localized abscess. Nec fas requires wide, deep debridement; local anesthesia is inadequate and drainage alone will not remove necrotic fascia.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────<br /></br>Core Concept<br /></br>Necrotizing fasciitis is a surgical emergency. After stabilizing the patient and starting broad-spectrum antibiotics, immediate surgical exploration and aggressive debridement under general anesthesia are mandatory to save life and limb.</p>
21. A 32-year-old man comes to the emergency department because of feelings of sadness and despair since his wife left him for another man 5 days ago. He also has had decreased appetite, difficulty sleeping, poor concentration, decreased energy, and no interest in the activities he previously enjoyed. He does not drink alcohol or use illicit drugs. Vital signs are within normal limits. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. He has a sad mood and dysphoric affect. He has had thoughts of ending his life but has no plan to kill himself.</br>Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Acute stress disorder</br>b. Adjustment disorder</br>c. Dependent personality disorder</br>d. Dysthymic disorder</br>e. Major depressive disorder</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>B. Adjustment disorder  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>1. Time course – “since his wife left him … 5 days ago.”<br /></br>   • &lt; 2 weeks → immediately excludes major depressive disorder (MDD ≥ 2 weeks) and dysthymia (≥ 2 years).<br /></br>2. Precipitating stressor – clear, identifiable, non-traumatic life event (marital separation).<br /></br>   • Fits the definition of adjustment disorder: emotional/behavioral symptoms within 3 months of a stressor.<br /></br>3. Symptom profile – sadness, anhedonia, appetite/sleep disturbance, low energy, poor concentration.<br /></br>   • These are “depressed-mood” symptoms, so the specifier is “adjustment disorder with depressed mood.”<br /></br>4. Safety – passive suicidal ideation without plan is common; still requires assessment but does not change diagnosis.<br /></br>5. No PTSD-type intrusions, hyperarousal, or dissociation → rules out acute stress disorder.  </p></br><p>Cognitive &amp; Metacognitive Approach<br /></br>Step 1: Ask “Is there a clear stressor?” → yes.<br /></br>Step 2: Ask “Does it meet full criteria for a primary mood/anxiety disorder?” → duration/criteria not met.<br /></br>Step 3: If answers are “yes” + “no,” think adjustment disorder.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Adjustment disorder = Emotional response that is<br /></br>• Triggered by an identifiable stressor (non-life-threatening)<br /></br>• Onset within 3 months of the stressor<br /></br>• Distress/out-of-proportion impairment<br /></br>• Does NOT meet criteria for another psychiatric disorder<br /></br>• Resolves within 6 months after stressor/its consequences end  </p></br><p>Document in chart: precipitating event, symptom onset, functional impairment, SI assessment, and absence of criteria for MDD/PTSD, etc.  </p></br><p>Take-home message: Always check timing and precipitating event before labeling with MDD.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (Brief)<br /></br>Believed to reflect maladaptive stress-response: dysregulation in HPA axis and coping mechanisms, but no enduring neurochemical changes seen in primary mood disorders. Risk factors: poor social support, prior mood illness, high stress load.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Tests<br /></br>1. First-line: Psychotherapy (supportive, CBT, problem-focused therapy).<br /></br>2. Pharmacotherapy: Usually not required; short-term sleep aid or anxiolytic can be considered but psychotherapy is preferred.<br /></br>3. Safety: Evaluate suicidality at every visit; hospitalize if active plan or intent.<br /></br>No labs/imaging are needed unless ruling out medical mimics.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“3-3-6 Rule” for Adjustment Disorder<br /></br>• Onset within 3 months of stressor<br /></br>• Does not persist &gt; 6 months after stressor ends<br /></br>• Stressor is NOT life-threatening (unlike PTSD/ASD)  </p></br><p>“ADD-on depression” → Adjustment Disorder with Depressed mood (ADD).  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>A. Acute stress disorder – Requires exposure to actual/threatened death, serious injury, or sexual violence + intrusive memories, dissociation, avoidance, arousal lasting 3 days–1 month. Break-up ≠ traumatic event by DSM-V definition.<br /></br>C. Dependent personality disorder – Lifelong pattern of submissive/clinging behavior, fear of separation beginning by early adulthood; not a sudden onset after single event.<br /></br>D. Dysthymic disorder (persistent depressive disorder) – Chronic depressed mood ≥ 2 years (≥ 1 year in children); not applicable here.<br /></br>E. Major depressive disorder – ≥ 5 SIGECAPS symptoms for ≥ 2 weeks with functional impairment; patient meets duration criterion only 5 days.  </p></br><p>Common pitfall: prematurely diagnose MDD before checking duration or overlooking the stressor.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>End of explanation.</p>
22. A 57-year-old woman with cirrhosis secondary to chronic hepatitis C is hospitalized because of tense ascites. Her medications are propranolol, furosemide, and spironolactone. On admission, her serum creatinine concentration is 1 mg/dL. Five liters of ascitic fluid is removed on therapeutic paracentesis. Intravenous furosemide and oral spironolactone therapy are begun. During the next 2 days, the patient has a 3.6-kg (8-lb) weight loss. Her pulse is 85/min and regular, and blood pressure is 100/65 mm Hg. Examination shows scleral icterus. Breath sounds are decreased at both lung bases. Abdominal examination shows moderate ascites. Laboratory studies show:</br>Prothrombin time    30 sec (INR=2.1)</br>Serum</br>Na+                  115 mEq/L</br>K+                   3.8 mEq/L</br>Cl–                  79 mEq/L</br>HCO3–                28 mEq/L</br>Urea nitrogen        30 mg/dL</br>Creatinine           2.1 mg/dL</br>Albumin              2.3 g/dL</br>Urine volume         <500 mL</br>In addition to discontinuing spironolactone and furosemide, which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. lactulose therapy</br>b. midodrine and octreotide therapy</br>c. infusion of 2 L of 0.9 percent saline during the next 24 hours</br>d. repeat large-volume paracentesis</br>e. placement of transjugular intrahepatic portosystemic shunt</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>c. Infusion of 2 L of 0.9 percent saline during the next 24 hours  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Key vignette clues<br /></br>   • Cirrhosis with tense ascites → already chronically “third-spaced.”<br /></br>   • Therapeutic paracentesis (5 L) + high-dose diuretics → large, rapid volume removal.<br /></br>   • 3.6-kg (≈3.6 L) weight loss in 48 h + urine output &lt; 500 mL → intravascular volume contraction.<br /></br>   • New labs: Na⁺ 115 mEq/L (severe hyponatremia), Cr 2.1 mg/dL (up from 1 mg/dL), BUN 30 mg/dL → prerenal azotemia.<br /></br>   • Vital signs: borderline BP (100/65) but not shock; pulse only 85 bpm → classic for cirrhotic “low effective arterial volume” despite total-body fluid overload.</p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Pertinent positives/negatives<br /></br>   • No fever, leukocytosis, abdominal pain → spontaneous bacterial peritonitis less likely.<br /></br>   • No confusion, asterixis → not overt hepatic encephalopathy.<br /></br>   • Ascites still present → she is not truly “dry” in total body water, only intravascularly depleted.</p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>Metacognitive approach:<br /></br>Whenever a cirrhotic patient’s creatinine rises after aggressive paracentesis/diuresis, first ask, “Could this simply be volume depletion?” If so, stop diuretics and give isotonic fluid (or albumin) before considering hepatorenal syndrome–specific vasoconstrictors or procedures.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (Why answer C is correct)</p></br><p>Step-wise logic<br /></br>1. Paracentesis + loop + K-sparing diuretics → ↓ effective arterial blood volume.<br /></br>2. ↓ Renal perfusion → prerenal acute kidney injury (Cr from 1 → 2.1, oliguria).<br /></br>3. Hypovolemia stimulates ADH → free-water retention → dilutional hyponatremia (Na⁺ 115).<br /></br>4. Immediate priority: restore intravascular volume with isotonic fluid.  </p></br><p>Core takeaway<br /></br>In cirrhotic patients, a sudden rise in creatinine and severe hyponatremia after volume removal is most commonly diuretic-induced prerenal azotemia. Treatment = stop diuretics and give isotonic saline (or albumin if concern for large oncotic deficit).</p></br><p>Documentation points (real encounter)<br /></br>• Assessment: Diuretic-induced hypovolemia with prerenal AKI and symptomatic hyponatremia.<br /></br>• Plan: Hold diuretics; infuse 0.9 % saline 2 L over 24 h; monitor urine output, Cr, Na⁺; daily weights; consider IV albumin if no improvement.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>Cirrhosis → portal HTN &amp; splanchnic vasodilation → low effective arterial blood volume → chronic RAAS &amp; ADH activation. When we over-diurese or remove fluid rapidly, this precarious circulatory state tips into frank hypovolemia, worsening renal perfusion. ADH surge causes hypotonic water reabsorption → severe dilutional hyponatremia.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatments &amp; Diagnostics  </p></br><p>First-line for diuretic-induced prerenal AKI:<br /></br>• Isotonic saline 1–2 L (or 1 g/kg IV albumin) while holding diuretics.<br /></br>• Monitor renal indices, Na⁺ every 4–6 h.<br /></br>If renal function fails to improve after adequate fluid challenge, THEN consider:<br /></br>• Albumin + vasoconstrictors (midodrine, octreotide, or terlipressin) for suspected hepatorenal syndrome.<br /></br>• RRT or TIPS for refractory scenarios.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“PARA for PRe-renal”:<br /></br>P – Paracentesis<br /></br>A – Aggressive diuresis<br /></br>R – Rising creatinine<br /></br>A – Answer = Add isotonic sAline  </p></br><p>Visualize an hourglass: top bulb (intravascular) empties quickly after paracentesis; refill with normal saline before kidneys “dry-heave.”</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>a. Lactulose therapy<br /></br>   • Used for hepatic encephalopathy, not volume-depletion–induced AKI/hyponatremia. Patient has no encephalopathy signs.</p></br><p>b. Midodrine and octreotide<br /></br>   • Vasoconstrictors for hepatorenal syndrome (HRS). HRS is a diagnosis of exclusion requiring failure to respond to volume resuscitation. Fluid first.</p></br><p>d. Repeat large-volume paracentesis<br /></br>   • Would worsen intravascular depletion and renal failure.</p></br><p>e. Transjugular intrahepatic portosystemic shunt (TIPS)<br /></br>   • Reserved for refractory ascites or variceal bleeding, and contraindicated in uncontrolled encephalopathy or significant renal failure. Not acute treatment for diuretic-induced AKI.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line  </p></br><p>In cirrhosis, a sudden rise in creatinine plus severe hyponatremia soon after large fluid removal is almost always prerenal from intravascular depletion. Stop diuretics and replace volume with isotonic saline (or albumin).</p>
23. A 77-year-old woman with dementia, Alzheimer type, is transferred to the emergency department from a skilled nursing care facility because of a 1-day history of abdominal distention, vomiting, and a right groin mass. She has a 15-year history of type 2 diabetes mellitus controlled with diet. She is not oriented to person, place, or time. Her temperature is 37.6°C (99.7°F), pulse is 95/min, respirations are 15/min, and blood pressure is 120/75 mm Hg. Examination shows decreased skin turgor. The abdomen is soft, distended, and mildly tender. Bowel sounds are high pitched. There is a 3-cm, tender, nonreducible mass below the right inguinal ligament. X-rays of the chest and abdomen show dilated loops of small bowel with no gas in the colon; there is a gas-filled loop near the right inguinal ligament. An emergency operation is planned. Which of the following is the most appropriate antibiotic therapy prior to this patient's operation?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Cefoxitin</br>b. Gentamicin</br>c. Oxacillin</br>d. Vancomycin</br>e. No preoperative antibiotic therapy is indicated</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>a. Cefoxitin  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Key clues </br>  – “3-cm, tender, non-reducible mass below the right inguinal ligament” → incarcerated (likely strangulated) femoral hernia.<br /></br>  – “Dilated loops of small bowel with no gas in the colon” → small-bowel obstruction.<br /></br>  – Emergency operation planned → high likelihood that ischemic/necrotic bowel or at least open bowel lumen will be encountered.<br /></br>• Take-home pattern </br>  GI tract will be entered (or may perforate) during an emergent hernia repair → need broad prophylaxis against enteric gram-negatives AND anaerobes.  </p></br><p>Metacognitive tip: Any emergent intra-abdominal procedure in which bowel contents may spill asks you to choose an antibiotic with mixed aerobic gram-negative + anaerobic coverage (second-generation “cephamycins” such as cefoxitin or cefotetan, or a third-generation cephalosporin + metronidazole).  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>1. Identify procedure type: emergent infra-inguinal hernia repair with probable small-bowel resection = “clean-contaminated” or “contaminated” case.<br /></br>2. Decide coverage: E. coli, Klebsiella, Proteus, Bacteroides fragilis.<br /></br>3. Pick single agent that reliably covers both groups and can be given as a one-time pre-incision dose.<br /></br>→ Cefoxitin (second-generation cephamycin) is standard prophylaxis for colorectal &amp; small-bowel surgery.  </p></br><p>Core takeaway: In emergent abdominal surgery where bowel will be handled or opened, give a cephamycin (cefoxitin/cefotetan) 30–60 min before incision.  </p></br><p>Documentation (real life): “Given 2 g IV cefoxitin within 30 min of incision for surgical prophylaxis per SCIP guidelines.”  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Incarcerated femoral hernias commonly strangulate owing to narrow neck → closed-loop obstruction → bowel ischemia → bacterial overgrowth &amp; translocation.<br /></br>• Translocation + surgical entry into bowel = risk of peritonitis/SSI (surgical site infection) with mixed flora.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Pearls<br /></br>• Dosing: Cefoxitin 1–2 g IV, repeat intra-op if case &gt;2–3 h or major blood loss.<br /></br>• Alternatives if β-lactam allergy:<br /></br>  – Ciprofloxacin + metronidazole<br /></br>  – Aztreonam + metronidazole<br /></br>• Coverage checklist: Gram-negatives (Enterobacteriaceae) ✓; Anaerobes (B. fragilis) ✓; Gram-positives (Strep, MSSA) ✓.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“FOX burrows in the gut.”<br /></br>CeFOXitin / ceFOteTAN = FOX/TAN burrow underground → anaerobes + gut gram-negatives; pick them for belly surgery.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>b. Gentamicin – Great for aerobic gram-negatives, NO anaerobic coverage; nephro-/ototoxic. Inadequate alone for GI surgery.<br /></br>c. Oxacillin – Narrow spectrum against MSSA only. No Gram-negative or anaerobic activity.<br /></br>d. Vancomycin – Covers MRSA and C. difficile (oral) but lacks Gram-negative/anaerobic coverage; reserved for MRSA prophylaxis in high-risk institutions.<br /></br>e. No antibiotics – Violates established guidelines; contaminated field → 3- to 5-fold higher SSI rate without prophylaxis.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line: For emergent small-bowel or colorectal surgery, choose a cephamycin (cefoxitin) to cover both Gram-negative enterics and anaerobes and administer it within an hour before incision.</p>
24. A 77-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department 2 hours after the sudden onset of shortness of breath. She has hyperlipidemia treated with atorvastatin and hypertension treated with lisinopril, hydrochlorothiazide, and amlodipine. She is 163 cm (5 ft 4 in) tall and weighs 55 kg (120 lb); BMI is 21 kg/m2. Her temperature is 37.2°C (98.9°F), pulse is 104/min, respirations are 24/min, and blood pressure is 194/102 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on room air shows an oxygen saturation of 83 percent. Crackles are heard at both lung bases. There is an S4. Her serum creatinine concentrations 1 year ago and today are 1.5 mg/dL and 2 mg/dL, respectively. Intravenous furosemide is administered, and her symptoms improve. Echocardiography shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Measurement of plasma renin activity</br>b. Measurement of serum aldosterone concentration</br>c. Measurement of serum metanephrine concentration</br>d. Renal duplex ultrasonography</br>e. Renal biopsy</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>D. Renal duplex ultrasonography  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues that point away from primary cardiac disease and toward renovascular hypertension/renal artery stenosis (RAS):<br /></br>• Sudden “flash” pulmonary edema with very high blood pressure (194/102 mm Hg)<br /></br>• Age &gt;55 yr with previously “controlled” hypertension on THREE agents (ACE-I, thiazide, CCB) → resistant or malignant HTN red-flag for secondary causes<br /></br>• Serum creatinine ↑ from 1.5 → 2.0 mg/dL after chronic ACE-I use → suggests decreased renal perfusion (ACE-I unmasks bilateral RAS)<br /></br>• Normal echocardiogram (rules out severe systolic dysfunction or valvular disease as primary cause of pulmonary edema)<br /></br>• S4 (long-standing HTN) but no other cardiac structural abnormalities  </p></br><p>Pattern to recognize:<br /></br>“Older patient + resistant HTN ± ACE/ARB-induced rise in Cr ± recurrent flash pulmonary edema” → think atherosclerotic renal artery stenosis.  </p></br><p>Metacognitive approach:<br /></br>1. Ask: Is there a secondary cause of HTN?<br /></br>2. Screen for RAS when ≥1 of the following is present: onset after age 55, malignant/resistant HTN, unexplained rise in Cr after ACE/ARB, recurrent pulmonary edema.<br /></br>3. Choose the least invasive, kidney-safe imaging test to look at the renal arteries.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Step-wise thought process:<br /></br>1. Patient meets multiple screening criteria for RAS.<br /></br>2. Next step is a non-contrast, non-invasive modality that directly visualizes renal artery flow: renal duplex Doppler US.<br /></br>3. If ultrasound is positive or equivocal, proceed to definitive imaging (CT- or MR-angiography) or interventional angiography.  </p></br><p>Take-home message:<br /></br>Renal artery stenosis should be suspected in older patients with resistant HTN, ACE-I–induced creatinine rise, or recurrent flash pulmonary edema, and the first-line screening test is renal duplex ultrasonography.  </p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real patient):<br /></br>• Rationale for secondary HTN work-up<br /></br>• Worsening renal function on ACE-I<br /></br>• Plan for renal duplex US and potential vascular referral  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>Atherosclerotic plaque narrows the renal artery → ↓ perfusion pressure sensed by juxtaglomerular apparatus → ↑ renin → ↑ angiotensin II → severe vasoconstriction + aldosterone-mediated volume expansion → severe, sometimes episodic HTN. ACE-I removes efferent arteriolar constriction, dropping GFR in stenotic kidneys, raising creatinine. Sudden volume overload precipitates pulmonary edema despite normal LV systolic function (“flash” edema).  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Diagnostics &amp; Management Snapshot  </p></br><p>Screening/confirmation:<br /></br>1. Renal duplex ultrasound (flow velocity, renal size) – best initial, no contrast.<br /></br>2. CT angiography or MR angiography – higher sensitivity/specificity, require contrast.<br /></br>3. Digital subtraction angiography – gold standard; used when planning endovascular therapy.  </p></br><p>Treatment:<br /></br>• Medical: Optimal BP control (ACE/ARB cautiously if unilateral RAS, avoid if bilateral/functionally solitary kidney unless closely monitored), statins, antiplatelets.<br /></br>• Revascularization (angioplasty ± stent) for recurrent flash pulmonary edema, refractory HTN, or progressive CKD.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>A. Plasma renin activity – May be elevated in RAS but is neither specific nor sensitive; imaging is required for diagnosis.  </p></br><p>B. Serum aldosterone – Screens for primary hyperaldosteronism (Conn syndrome). Expect HTN with hypokalemia and metabolic alkalosis; creatinine usually normal unless long-standing.  </p></br><p>C. Serum metanephrines – Tests for pheochromocytoma (paroxysmal HTN, headaches, diaphoresis, palpitations). No sympathetic surge symptoms here.  </p></br><p>E. Renal biopsy – Used for intrinsic parenchymal disease (e.g., glomerulonephritis, nephrotic syndrome). Normal urine sediment and classic RAS presentation make biopsy unnecessary and risky.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>High-Yield Memory Tips  </p></br><p>“RAPID” mnemonic for when to suspect Renal Artery Stenosis:<br /></br>R – Resistant HTN on ≥3 drugs<br /></br>A – ACE/ARB ↑ creatinine (&gt;30%)<br /></br>P – Pulmonary edema (flash)<br /></br>I – Inequality in kidney size or bruit<br /></br>D – Declining GFR without another cause  </p></br><p>Screen with duplex “D”oppler first → Duplex, then CT/MR-angiography if needed.</p>
25. A 67-year-old woman comes to the physician because of a 2-week history of persistent cough productive of white sputum. The cough awakens her from sleep and is not relieved by an over-the-counter cough suppressant. Last night, she had an episode of sweating and flushing that occurred immediately following a coughing episode. During the past 3 days, she has had three episodes of vomiting after coughing. One month ago, she had nasal congestion, low-grade fever, and sneezing that resolved spontaneously. She did not have postnasal drainage. She has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. She smoked one-half pack of cigarettes daily for 5 years but quit 40 years ago. She volunteers at a soup kitchen once weekly. The patient appears healthy and is not coughing. Her temperature is 37.8°C (100°F), pulse is 91/min, respirations are 14/min, and blood pressure is 120/78 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on room air shows an oxygen saturation of 95 percent. Cardiopulmonary examination shows no abnormalities. Her leukocyte count is 22,000/mm³ (39 percent segmented neutrophils, 1 percent bands, and 60 percent lymphocytes). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Anaerobic pneumonia</br>b. Chronic bronchitis</br>c. Miliary tuberculosis</br>d. Mycoplasmal pneumonia</br>e. Pertussis</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>e. Pertussis  </p></br><p>───────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Time course &amp; prodrome<br /></br>   • 1-month-ago “cold” (nasal congestion, low-grade fever, sneezing) = catarrhal phase of pertussis<br /></br>   • Followed by 2 weeks of relentless cough = paroxysmal phase  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Character of cough<br /></br>   • Recurrent PAROXYSMS strong enough to:<br /></br>     – wake her from sleep<br /></br>     – produce post-tussive emesis<br /></br>     – cause autonomic symptoms (sweating, flushing)<br /></br>   • OTC antitussive ineffective<br /></br>   These are classic buzzwords for pertussis in adults; the inspiratory “whoop” is often absent in older pts, so don’t rely on it.  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Laboratory clue<br /></br>   • WBC 22 000/mm³ with 60 % lymphocytes → striking lymphocytosis is almost pathognomonic; it is created by pertussis toxin blocking lymphocyte migration out of the bloodstream.  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Epidemiology/exposure<br /></br>   • Age &gt; 60 with waning immunity; volunteers in a soup kitchen (close contact with children/undervaccinated adults).  </p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>Metacognitive pearl:  On NBME/Step 2 any persistent, paroxysmal cough + post-tussive vomiting ± lymphocytosis → think Bordetella pertussis, regardless of age.  </p></br><p>───────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (Step-wise)  </p></br><ol></br><li>Identify paroxysmal cough with post-tussive emesis → narrow list to pertussis or severe asthma.  </li></br><li>Prior URI “catarrhal” phase + lymphocytic leukocytosis → points to pertussis.  </li></br><li>Lack of wheeze, normal exam, no chronic lung disease → rules out asthma/COPD.  </li></br><li>No focal infiltrate, minimal fever → makes bacterial pneumonias unlikely.<br /></br>Key takeaway: Adult pertussis is common, under-recognized, and presents WITHOUT classic whoop but WITH post-tussive vomiting &amp; lymphocytosis.  </li></br></ol></br><p>Documentation you’d make: “Suspect Bordetella pertussis—paroxysmal cough x 2 wks with post-tussive emesis, WBC 22k (lymphocytic). Will obtain PCR, start azithromycin, offer Tdap booster, notify public health, prophylax close contacts.”  </p></br><p>───────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• Bordetella pertussis releases:<br /></br>  – Pertussis toxin → disables Gi proteins → ↑cAMP, prevents lymphocyte extravasation → marked lymphocytosis.<br /></br>  – Tracheal cytotoxin → damages ciliated epithelium → persistent cough.  </p></br><p>• Phases<br /></br>  1. Catarrhal (1–2 wks): nonspecific URI; most contagious.<br /></br>  2. Paroxysmal (2–6 wks): severe coughing fits, post-tussive emesis, possible “whoop,” syncope, rib fracture.<br /></br>  3. Convalescent (&gt;6 wks): gradual resolution, “100-day cough.”  </p></br><p>───────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostics  </p></br><p>Diagnostics (if asked)<br /></br>• PCR or culture from nasopharyngeal swab (culture only in first 2 wks).<br /></br>• CBC often shows lymphocytosis as clue.  </p></br><p>Treatment<br /></br>1. Macrolide (azithromycin 5 days; clarithromycin 7 days; or erythromycin 14 days).<br /></br>   – Shortens illness if &lt;21 days; always used to ↓ contagiousness.<br /></br>2. TMP-SMX if macrolide-intolerant.<br /></br>3. Post-exposure prophylaxis: macrolide for ALL household &amp; close contacts, regardless of vaccination status.<br /></br>4. Vaccination: give Tdap booster if not received in adulthood.  </p></br><p>───────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“Barking cough that makes you Barf &amp; raises your Blood (lymphocyte) count = Bordetella.”<br /></br>OR<br /></br>3 B’s:<br /></br>• Bouts of cough<br /></br>• Barf afterward<br /></br>• Big lymphocyte count<br /></br>= Bordetella pertussis.  </p></br><p>───────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong  </p></br><p>a. Anaerobic pneumonia (lung abscess)<br /></br>   • Needs aspiration risk (alcohol, seizures, dysphagia) + foul-smelling sputum + radiographic cavitation. Not present.  </p></br><p>b. Chronic bronchitis<br /></br>   • Defined by productive cough ≥3 months/yr for 2 consecutive years, usually in heavy smoker. This pt has only 2 weeks of cough and minimal smoking history.  </p></br><p>c. Miliary tuberculosis<br /></br>   • Presents with fevers, weight loss, night sweats, diffuse millet-seed CXR, anemia. Paroxysmal cough/lymphocytosis uncommon.  </p></br><p>d. Mycoplasmal pneumonia<br /></br>   • “Walking pneumonia” → low-grade fever, dry (not productive) cough, scattered rales, often young adults, CXR&gt;exam findings, no lymphocytosis. Post-tussive emesis uncommon.  </p></br><p>───────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line  </p></br><p>A paroxysmal cough causing post-tussive vomiting plus marked lymphocytosis in an adult with recent URI symptoms is classic for pertussis. Recognize it, treat with a macrolide, and prophylax contacts.</p>
26. A 50-year-old woman with chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) comes to the office to discuss smoking cessation. The patient says she has tried to quit smoking five times in the past but has been discouraged because each time she resumed smoking. Medical history is also significant for hypertension. Medications include ipratropium, albuterol, and amlodipine. The patient has smoked two packs of cigarettes daily for the past 35 years. BMI is 18 kg/m2. Vital signs are temperature 36.7°C (98.0°F), pulse 70/min, respirations 16/min, and blood pressure 120/70 mm Hg. Physical examination discloses clubbing of the fingers. Auscultation of the lungs discloses scattered wheezes. The patient says she is willing to make another attempt to quit smoking and to set a quit date. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Advise her that there are no treatment options since she has failed quit attempts in the past  </br>b. Advise her to use an over-the-counter nicotine replacement  </br>c. Give her a telephone number for a smoking cessation hotline and advise her to chew gum  </br>d. Prescribe varenicline and refer her to a smoking cessation class</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer</br>d. Prescribe varenicline and refer her to a smoking cessation class</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis</br>• Key clues</br>  – 35-year, 2-pack/day smoker with COPD and finger clubbing → strongly nicotine-dependent, high morbidity risk<br /></br>  – Has made 5 prior quit attempts but is “willing to make another attempt and to set a quit date” → patient is in the Preparation stage of change (transtheoretical model).<br /></br>  – No contraindications to first-line pharmacotherapy (eg, no current depression with suicidality, no seizure disorder, no end-stage renal disease).  </p></br><p>• What the exam is really testing<br /></br>  – Proper execution of the 5 A’s (Ask, Advise, Assess, Assist, Arrange).<br /></br>  – “Assist” for a prepared quitter = evidence-based pharmacotherapy PLUS intensive behavioral support.<br /></br>  – Varenicline is the single most effective monotherapy; combining it with counseling yields the highest long-term quit rates.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning</br>Step 1: Identify readiness → She has chosen a quit date (Preparation stage).<br /></br>Step 2: Decide on assistive therapy → Offer FDA-approved pharmacologic aid (varenicline, nicotine replacement therapy [NRT], or bupropion SR).<br /></br>Step 3: Choose the agent with the best efficacy and no contraindication → Varenicline has the highest abstinence rates (~3× placebo, superior to single-agent NRT) and is safe in hypertensive/COPD patients.<br /></br>Step 4: Couple drug therapy with structured counseling → A smoking-cessation class (or hotline, individual counseling, etc.) doubles success compared with medication alone.</p></br><p>Take-home: For motivated patients without contraindications, prescribe varenicline (or combination NRT) and arrange formal behavioral support.</p></br><p>What would be documented</br>• Stage of change, quit date, risks/benefits of varenicline discussed, prescription sent, referral to cessation class made, follow-up arranged.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review (high-yield)</br>• Nicotine stimulates α4β2 nicotinic acetylcholine receptors in the ventral tegmental area → dopamine release → reinforcement.</br>• Varenicline = partial agonist at α4β2 → Prevents withdrawal (agonist) yet blocks nicotine binding (antagonist) → reduced craving and diminished reward if patient smokes.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────</br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Pearls</br>First-line pharmacologic options</br>1. Varenicline: most effective single agent; begin 1 week before quit date; dose adjust in CKD.<br /></br>2. Combination NRT (patch + gum/lozenge) ≈ varenicline efficacy; patch alone less effective.<br /></br>3. Bupropion SR: useful if concomitant depression, concern about post-quit weight gain; avoid in seizure or eating disorders.</p></br><p>Additive benefit: Any pharmacotherapy + ≥4 sessions of counseling (group, individual, phone, text).</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips</br>“Vareni-CLEAN” your lungs: Varenicline helps CLEAN out cigarettes.<br /></br>5 A’s mnemonic: Ask, Advise, Assess, Assist, Arrange.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────</br>Incorrect Answer Analysis</br>a. “No treatment options”<br /></br>   • Fatalistic, violates standard care. Repeated failures do NOT preclude further evidence-based attempts.</p></br><p>b. “Over-the-counter NRT alone”<br /></br>   • OTC patch or gum is acceptable but less effective than varenicline or combination therapy for a heavy smoker. Guideline-preferred to escalate to the most efficacious agent.</p></br><p>c. “Hotline + chew gum”<br /></br>   • Counseling is good, but gum alone (short-acting NRT) is inferior. Optimal care pairs comprehensive counseling with a proven first-line pharmacologic aid.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────</br>Core Takeaway</br>A motivated smoker should be “Assisted” with the most effective pharmacotherapy (varenicline or combination NRT) plus structured behavioral support—an approach that maximizes quit success and aligns with USPSTF and AHRQ guidelines.</p>
27. A 22-year-old man comes to the physician for a health maintenance examination. He has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. He has no known allergies. At the age of 3 years, he underwent orchiopexy for cryptorchidism. The patient does not smoke cigarettes or drink alcohol. His temperature is 36.8°C (98.2°F), pulse is 58/min, respirations are 12/min, and blood pressure is 104/68 mm Hg. Examination shows a well-healed scar in the right inguinal region. No other abnormalities are noted. This patient is at increased risk for which of the following disorders?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Direct inguinal hernia</br>b. Hydrocele</br>c. Spermatocele</br>d. Testicular cancer</br>e. Testicular torsion</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>d. Testicular cancer  </p></br><p>══════════════════════════════════════════<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Key clue: “undergone orchiopexy for cryptorchidism at age 3.”<br /></br>• Cryptorchid (undescended) testis = classic NBME signal for future malignancy risk.<br /></br>• Orchiopexy performed after 6-12 months of age still lowers—but does NOT normalize—the risk of germ-cell tumors.<br /></br>• No other physical findings → question is asking specifically about long-term sequelae, not an acute complication.  </p></br><p>How to attack similar questions<br /></br>1. Whenever you see “history of cryptorchidism,” immediately put testicular cancer (especially seminoma) high on the differential.<br /></br>2. Ask yourself whether orchiopexy changes the long-term risk (↓ infertility &amp; torsion, ↓ but ≠ 0 cancer risk).<br /></br>3. Eliminate distractors that are procedure-related but not supported by data.  </p></br><p>══════════════════════════════════════════<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Step-wise thought process:<br /></br>1. Cryptorchidism → germ cells exposed to higher intra-abdominal temperature → DNA damage + impaired maturation → intratubular germ‐cell neoplasia → seminoma or non-seminomatous germ-cell tumor.<br /></br>2. Orchiopexy before puberty improves fertility and allows easier palpation, but malignant transformation risk remains ~4-10× general population.<br /></br>3. Therefore, lifelong increased risk of testicular cancer is the core “take-home” point.  </p></br><p>Documentation (real patient): would counsel on monthly testicular self-examination and need for prompt evaluation of any testicular mass.  </p></br><p>══════════════════════════════════════════<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>Undescended testis<br /></br>• Etiology: failure to complete transabdominal → inguinoscrotal descent.<br /></br>• Complications<br /></br>  – Testicular cancer (seminoma &gt; embryonal carcinoma) – risk persists post-orchiopexy.<br /></br>  – Subfertility/infertility (partially mitigated if orchiopexy done &lt;1 yr).<br /></br>  – Testicular torsion &amp; inguinal hernia (risk largely eliminated after fixation).  </p></br><p>Why cancer risk persists: malignant transformation likely begins in utero/early infancy; relocating the testis does not reverse premalignant changes.  </p></br><p>══════════════════════════════════════════<br /></br>Management &amp; Screening<br /></br>• Early orchiopexy (6–12 mo) – improves spermatogenesis &amp; decreases torsion but only decreases, not abolishes, cancer risk.<br /></br>• No routine imaging; teach monthly self-exam.<br /></br>• Any solid, painless, intratesticular mass → scrotal ultrasound and urology referral.  </p></br><p>Mnemonic<br /></br>“UP you go, risk stays low; DOWN you stay, cancer on the way.”<br /></br>(Up = into scrotum early; even then, risk never zero.)  </p></br><p>══════════════════════════════════════════<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>a. Direct inguinal hernia – Due to weakness of transversalis fascia in Hesselbach triangle; not linked to prior cryptorchidism once the processus vaginalis has been ligated during orchiopexy.<br /></br>b. Hydrocele – Persistence of processus vaginalis producing fluid collection; usually presents in infancy. Orchiopexy closes the processus; adult hydrocele risk not increased.<br /></br>c. Spermatocele – Epididymal cyst unrelated to testicular descent history.<br /></br>e. Testicular torsion – Risk is high while testis is undescended, but orchiopexy (bilateral fixation) markedly lowers torsion incidence; after successful surgery, risk approximates general population.  </p></br><p>══════════════════════════════════════════<br /></br>Bottom Line<br /></br>History of cryptorchidism—even after surgical correction—confers a lifelong, markedly elevated risk of testicular germ-cell cancer, making option D the only correct choice.</p>
28. A previously healthy 20-year-old man is brought to the emergency department because of a 12-hour history of increasing fever and confusion. He has never seen a physician. He takes no medications. His temperature is 40°C (104°F), pulse is 110/min, respirations are 18/min, and blood pressure is 100/70 mm Hg. On arrival, he is delirious. Examination shows a diffuse rash over the body. A photograph of the rash is shown. There is moderate nuchal rigidity. The remainder of the examination shows no abnormalities. Immunization against which of the following is most likely to have prevented this patient's condition?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. Haemophilus influenzae</br>b. Measles virus</br>c. Neisseria meningitidis</br>d. Streptococcus pneumoniae</br>e. Varicella-zoster virus</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>c. Neisseria meningitidis  </p></br><p>──────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• “Previously healthy 20-year-old” → classic age group for meningococcal disease (adolescents, college students, military recruits).<br /></br>• “12-hour history of increasing fever and confusion” + “moderate nuchal rigidity” → acute bacterial meningitis.<br /></br>• “Diffuse rash over the body” (photo shows petechiae/purpura) → strongly points to meningococcemia; other bacterial meningitides rarely cause a rapidly progressive petechial rash.<br /></br>• Very high fever (40 °C) and delirium → fulminant course typical of N. meningitidis endotoxemia.<br /></br>Pertinent negatives: no focal neurologic deficits, no vesicles (rules out VZV), no upper respiratory prodrome with cough/coryza (rules out measles).  </p></br><p>Cognitive framework:<br /></br>1. Identify meningitis (fever + neck stiffness + altered mental status).<br /></br>2. Use age to tailor the bug list.<br /></br>3. Presence of petechiae/purpura ⇒ think meningococcus until proven otherwise.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Step-wise thought process:<br /></br>1. Acute bacterial meningitis in a young adult.<br /></br>2. Differential: N. meningitidis, S. pneumoniae, viral causes.<br /></br>3. Rash makes meningococcus overwhelmingly likely.<br /></br>4. Ask: “Which vaccine could have prevented meningococcal disease in this age group?” → quadrivalent conjugate N. meningitidis vaccine (A,C,W,Y ± serogroup B).  </p></br><p>Core takeaway:<br /></br>Petechial or purpuric rash + meningitis in adolescents/young adults = N. meningitidis; quadrivalent meningococcal vaccine prevents it.  </p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real-life): mental status, vital-sign trends, presence of petechiae/purpura, nuchal rigidity, need for emergent empiric antibiotics and droplet isolation.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• N. meningitidis = encapsulated gram-negative diplococcus.<br /></br>• Nasopharyngeal colonization → hematogenous spread → cross BBB → meningitis.<br /></br>• Endotoxin (lipooligosaccharide) triggers cytokine storm → septic shock, DIC, petechial rash, Waterhouse-Friderichsen syndrome (bilateral adrenal hemorrhage).<br /></br>• Antiphagocytic polysaccharide capsule is the main vaccine target.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────<br /></br>Treatments &amp; Diagnostics (high-yield bullets)<br /></br>Diagnosis:<br /></br>• Lumbar puncture: neutrophilic pleocytosis, low CSF glucose, ↑ protein; Gram stain shows gram-negative diplococci.<br /></br>• Blood cultures often positive.<br /></br>Management:<br /></br>• Empiric IV ceftriaxone + vancomycin; add ampicillin if &gt;50 y or immunocompromised (Listeria coverage).<br /></br>• Dexamethasone to reduce neurologic sequelae (mainly for S. pneumoniae but often started empirically).<br /></br>• Droplet precautions.<br /></br>• Chemoprophylaxis for close contacts: rifampin, ciprofloxacin, or ceftriaxone.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“Teen with fever, petechiae, and pain in the neck? Think Me-NIN-go-coccus!”<br /></br>Mnemonic:<br /></br>P – Petechiae<br /></br>E – Endotoxin shock<br /></br>N – Nuchal rigidity<br /></br>I – In dorms (college)<br /></br>N – Neisseria meningitidis vaccine prevents  </p></br><p>──────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>a. Haemophilus influenzae<br /></br>• Typically causes meningitis in unvaccinated children &lt;5 y, not healthy adults.<br /></br>• Rash is uncommon. Hib vaccine given at 2,4,6,12–15 mo.  </p></br><p>b. Measles virus<br /></br>• Maculopapular rash starts on face after cough, coryza, conjunctivitis, Koplik spots.<br /></br>• Not associated with meningitis, nuchal rigidity, or petechiae.  </p></br><p>d. Streptococcus pneumoniae<br /></br>• Most common adult meningitis pathogen, especially in elderly or asplenic patients.<br /></br>• Rash is rare; disease is less fulminant than meningococcemia. Vaccines (PCV13, PPSV23) target infants, elderly, high-risk groups.  </p></br><p>e. Varicella-zoster virus<br /></br>• Vesicular “dew-drop on a rose petal” rash, not petechial.<br /></br>• Can cause encephalitis but not acute purpuric meningitis. Varicella vaccine given in childhood; shingles vaccine for ≥50 y.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line<br /></br>A petechial rash plus meningitis in a young adult = Neisseria meningitidis. Routine adolescent meningococcal vaccination (age 11-12 with booster at 16 and separate MenB series) is the preventive measure.</p>
29. A 62-year-old woman comes to the physician because of a 2-day history of moderate left-sided lower abdominal pain. She also has had nausea and loss of appetite during this time. She has not had vomiting. She has hypertension well controlled with hydrochlorothiazide. Her temperature is 38.4°C (101.1°F), pulse is 98/min, respirations are 14/min, and blood pressure is 120/78 mm Hg. Abdominal examination shows tenderness to palpation of the left lower quadrant; there is no guarding or rebound. Bowel sounds are decreased. Rectal examination shows no abnormalities. Test of the stool for occult blood is negative. Laboratory studies show:</br>Hemoglobin 13.2 g/dL</br>Hematocrit 36 percent</br>Leukocyte count 14,000/mm³</br>Platelet count 350,000/mm³</br>Results of other laboratory studies are within the reference ranges. Chest and abdominal x-rays show no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Amoxicillin-clavulanate therapy  </br>b. Barium enema  </br>c. Flexible sigmoidoscopy  </br>d. Mesalamine therapy  </br>e. Small-bowel follow-through</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>A.  Amoxicillin-clavulanate therapy  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Key clues<br /></br>  – Age ≥60 yr with sigmoid-side (left-lower-quadrant) pain<br /></br>  – Low-grade fever (38.4 °C) and leukocytosis (14 000/µL)<br /></br>  – No peritoneal signs (no guarding/rebound) → “uncomplicated” process<br /></br>  – Nausea, ↓ bowel sounds, but no vomiting or GI bleeding<br /></br>  – Plain films normal → no free air, obstruction, or ileus<br /></br>These findings are the classic bedside pattern for acute uncomplicated diverticulitis. In a hemodynamically stable patient with an otherwise typical presentation, empiric outpatient antibiotics that cover gram-negative rods and anaerobes are recommended.</p></br><p>Test‐taking framework:<br /></br>1. Pattern recognition (elderly + LLQ pain + fever + leukocytosis) → diverticulitis.<br /></br>2. Decide if complicated (abscess, peritonitis, fistula, obstruction) → not present.<br /></br>3. Next step: treat, do NOT instrument the colon in the acute setting.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>• Core concept: Initial management of uncomplicated diverticulitis = bowel rest, fluids, and broad oral antibiotics (e.g., amoxicillin-clavulanate or ciprofloxacin + metronidazole).<br /></br>• Why no imaging?
