CT is useful if the diagnosis is unclear or to look for abscess, but a classic textbook presentation on the exam allows empiric therapy; moreover, colon contrast studies and endoscopy are contraindicated acutely because they raise intraluminal pressure and can perforate an inflamed segment.<br /></br>• Documentation essentials if this were a real encounter: Assessment “Acute uncomplicated sigmoid diverticulitis,” Plan “NPO → clear liquids, oral amox-clav 875/125 mg BID × 7–10 d, return precautions (worsening pain, fever, inability to tolerate PO). Follow-up colonoscopy in 6–8 wk after resolution.”</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Diverticulosis: saclike herniations at weak points where vasa recta penetrate muscularis.<br /></br>• Diverticulitis: microperforation → focal inflammation, may progress to macroperforation, abscess, fistula, or peritonitis.<br /></br>• Risk factors: low-fiber diet, constipation, age, obesity, NSAID use.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment Algorithm (uncomplicated)<br /></br>1. Oral broad-spectrum antibiotics<br /></br>   – Amoxicillin-clavulanate OR<br /></br>   – Ciprofloxacin + metronidazole<br /></br>2. Clear-liquid diet → advance as symptoms improve.<br /></br>3. Outpatient follow-up; colonoscopy in ~6 wk to rule out malignancy.</p></br><p>Complicated disease (peritonitis, abscess &gt;3 cm, obstruction) → hospitalize, IV piperacillin-tazobactam or cefepime + metronidazole; percutaneous drainage or surgery as needed.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“LLQ in an OLD QUEEN”<br /></br>Left-Lower-Quadrant pain in an Older patient with Leukocytosis &amp; Queasy (nausea) = Diverticulitis → give “Aug-Ment” (Augmentin = amox-clav).<br /></br>“Don’t BE FLEXible” – avoid Barium Enema and FLEXible sigmoidoscopy in acute diverticulitis.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong  </p></br><p>B. Barium enema<br /></br>• Increases intraluminal pressure; risk of perforation during acute inflammation. Imaging of diverticulosis/strictures postponed until 6–8 wk after recovery.</p></br><p>C. Flexible sigmoidoscopy<br /></br>• Endoscopic instrumentation is absolutely contraindicated during the acute episode for the same perforation risk.</p></br><p>D. Mesalamine therapy<br /></br>• Used for inflammatory bowel disease (ulcerative colitis, Crohn disease). Those disorders present with chronic diarrhea, blood, weight loss, not isolated acute LLQ pain and fever.</p></br><p>E. Small-bowel follow-through<br /></br>• Evaluates small-bowel Crohn disease or malabsorption. Not useful in colonic diverticulitis and likewise entails contrast risk if colon is inflamed.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Take-Home Message<br /></br>Classic uncomplicated diverticulitis in a stable patient → treat empirically with oral broad-spectrum antibiotics (e.g., amoxicillin-clavulanate) and avoid any contrast or endoscopic study until the acute process resolves.</p>
30. A 47-year-old man comes to the physician for a follow-up examination. He has hypertension treated with ramipril and gout treated with allopurinol. He has not had a gout flare during the past 6 months. His only other medication is aspirin. He is 180 cm (5 ft 11 in) tall and weighs 109 kg (240 lb); BMI is 34 kg/m2. His blood pressure is 152/95 mm Hg. Examination shows truncal obesity. Six months ago, his serum creatinine concentration was 1.4 mg/dL. Serum studies today show:</br>Na+               134 mEq/L</br>K+                4.8 mEq/L</br>Cl–               104 mEq/L</br>HCO3–             24 mEq/L</br>Urea nitrogen     43 mg/dL</br>Glucose           107 mg/dL</br>Creatinine        1.5 mg/dL</br>Uric acid         6.4 mg/dL</br>Which of the following is the most appropriate additional pharmacotherapy?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Amlodipine</br>b. Colchicine</br>c. Hydrochlorothiazide</br>d. Probenecid</br>e. No additional pharmacotherapy is indicated</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer</br>A. Amlodipine</p></br><p>──────────────────────── Vignette Analysis</br>1. Uncontrolled hypertension<br /></br>   • Current BP = 152/95 mm Hg despite adequate dose of the ACE-inhibitor ramipril.<br /></br>   • ≥130/80 (or ≥140/90 by older JNC-8) on medication → add a 2nd agent.</p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Comorbid conditions that influence drug choice<br /></br>   • Gout (well-controlled on allopurinol; uric acid 6.4 mg/dL).<br /></br>   • Mild chronic kidney disease (Cr 1.5 mg/dL, BUN 43 mg/dL).<br /></br>   • Obesity (BMI 34 kg/m²).</p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Medication-related clues<br /></br>   • Aspirin must be continued (cardioprotection).<br /></br>   • Avoid agents that raise uric acid or worsen renal function.</p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>Cognitive framework: Step 1 – determine if another drug is needed (yes, BP still high).<br /></br>Step 2 – choose from guideline-approved combinations (ACE-I + either thiazide diuretic or dihydropyridine calcium-channel blocker).<br /></br>Step 3 – screen for contraindications (gout → avoid thiazide).<br /></br>Result → pick amlodipine.</p></br><p>──────────────────────── Clinical Critical Reasoning</br>• First-line antihypertensives (non-black patient without specific comorbidities):<br /></br>  – ACE-I/ARB, thiazide, or dihydropyridine CCB.<br /></br>  – Combination therapy when one drug insufficient.</p></br><p>• In a patient with gout, thiazides and loop diuretics reduce urate excretion → ↑ serum uric acid → precipitate flare; therefore they are relatively contraindicated if suitable alternatives exist.</p></br><p>• Dihydropyridine CCBs (e.g., amlodipine):<br /></br>  – Potent BP-lowering effect.<br /></br>  – Neutral on uric acid.<br /></br>  – Safe in mild CKD.<br /></br>  – Well supported as add-on to ACE-I in major outcome trials (e.g., ACCOMPLISH).</p></br><p>Take-home message: When an ACE-I alone fails to control BP and gout precludes thiazides, add a dihydropyridine calcium-channel blocker.</p></br><p>Documentation (real patient): “BP remains above goal on ramipril 10 mg daily; plan to initiate amlodipine 5 mg daily, titrate to target &lt;130/80 mm Hg. Educated patient on ankle edema risk, advised leg elevation and follow-up in 4 weeks.”</p></br><p>──────────────────────── Pathophysiology Review</br>• Thiazide diuretics compete with urate for renal tubular secretion → ↓ urate clearance → hyperuricemia → gout flare.<br /></br>• Dihydropyridine CCBs act on L-type Ca²⁺ channels in vascular smooth muscle → vasodilation → ↓ systemic vascular resistance → ↓ BP; they do not affect urate handling.</p></br><p>──────────────────────── Treatments &amp; Diagnostic Tests</br>• BP goal in CKD or ASCVD risk: &lt;130/80 mm Hg (ACC/AHA 2017).<br /></br>• Labs to follow after adding amlodipine: basic metabolic panel (ACE-I may still change electrolytes) and watch for peripheral edema (no lab test needed).<br /></br>• No additional urate-lowering therapy indicated (uric acid 6.4 mg/dL; goal &lt;6–8 depending on severity; patient asymptomatic).</p></br><p>──────────────────────── Memorization Tips</br>“ACE + CCB keeps the gout at bay; ACE + thaZIDE brings the flare inside.”<br /></br>Visual: picture an ACE knight offering two shields: one labeled ‘ZIDE’ covered in gouty crystal spikes, the other labeled ‘CCB’ smooth and safe.</p></br><p>──────────────────────── Incorrect Answer Analysis</br>B. Colchicine<br /></br>• Used for acute gout flare prophylaxis only if frequent attacks despite urate-lowering therapy or when starting allopurinol. Patient has had no flare in 6 months and is stable on allopurinol.</p></br><p>C. Hydrochlorothiazide<br /></br>• Acceptable add-on for many hypertensive patients but raises uric acid and often triggers gout. Contraindicated here when an equally effective alternative exists.</p></br><p>D. Probenecid<br /></br>• Uricosuric; ineffective when serum urate is near goal and less effective with elevated creatinine. Does not address the uncontrolled BP.</p></br><p>E. No additional pharmacotherapy<br /></br>• Inappropriate because BP remains above target; guidelines recommend intensifying therapy.</p></br><p>──────────────────────── Summary</br>The patient needs a second antihypertensive. Because thiazides aggravate gout, the best evidence-based, urate-neutral add-on to an ACE-inhibitor is a dihydropyridine calcium-channel blocker—amlodipine.</p>
31. A 25-year-old woman, gravida 2, para 1, at 20 weeks’ gestation comes to the physician for a routine prenatal visit. She has hypertension well controlled with labetalol. Her first pregnancy was uncomplicated and ended in spontaneous vaginal delivery of a newborn at term. Her only other medication is a prenatal vitamin. Her blood pressure is 120/80 mm Hg. Examination shows no peripheral edema. Urinalysis shows no glucose or protein. This patient is at greatest risk for which of the following pregnancy complications?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Fetal demise</br>b. Gestational diabetes</br>c. Polyhydramnios</br>d. Preeclampsia</br>e. Preterm labor</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>D. Preeclampsia  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues:<br /></br>• “Hypertension well-controlled with labetalol” → chronic hypertension (HTN diagnosed or present before 20 wks).<br /></br>• 20 weeks’ gestation, normal BP today (120/80) and no proteinuria → currently normotensive on medication, but still a chronic-HTN pregnancy.<br /></br>• No edema, glucose, or protein → no current complications noted.  </p></br><p>Algorithmic thought process:<br /></br>1. Identify the maternal condition in the stem (chronic HTN).<br /></br>2. Recall the major maternal/fetal complications associated with chronic HTN in pregnancy.<br /></br>3. Match those complications to the answer choices.  </p></br><p>High-yield list for chronic HTN:<br /></br>• Superimposed preeclampsia/eclampsia/HELLP<br /></br>• Placental abruption<br /></br>• Fetal growth restriction / oligohydramnios<br /></br>• Preterm delivery<br /></br>• Perinatal mortality  </p></br><p>Among the options provided, preeclampsia is the most directly linked and the most common complication.  </p></br><p>Metacognitive tip: Any NBME/Step question that introduces a pregnant woman already on an antihypertensive before 20 weeks is cueing you to think “chronic HTN → risk of superimposed preeclampsia or placental problems.”  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (Why choice D?)  </p></br><p>Step-wise reasoning:<br /></br>1. Chronic HTN causes chronic uteroplacental vasoconstriction and endothelial dysfunction.<br /></br>2. This predisposes to abnormal remodeling of spiral arteries, the initiating step in preeclampsia pathogenesis.<br /></br>3. Incidence of superimposed preeclampsia in women with chronic HTN is ~25% (far higher than the 3–5% baseline risk).<br /></br>4. Therefore, compared with other listed complications, preeclampsia has the greatest relative risk increase for this specific patient.  </p></br><p>Core takeaway: Chronic HTN is the strongest risk factor among common maternal conditions for developing preeclampsia.  </p></br><p>Documentation note: In real practice you would document chronic HTN, current BP control, absence of proteinuria today, and plan for closer surveillance (baseline CMP, 24-h urine protein, growth scans, low-dose aspirin).  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>Preeclampsia = new-onset HTN (≥140/90 after 20 wks) + proteinuria or end-organ dysfunction.<br /></br>Mechanism: Abnormal trophoblastic invasion → high-resistance uteroplacental flow → placental hypoxia → systemic endothelial dysfunction → vasospasm, capillary leak, platelet activation.<br /></br>Chronic HTN women already have vascular disease, so the spiral arteries fail to remodel even more profoundly, magnifying risk.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Diagnostic &amp; Management Pearls  </p></br><p>Surveillance in chronic HTN pregnancy:<br /></br>• Baseline labs (CMP, CBC, urine protein) at first prenatal visit.<br /></br>• Low-dose aspirin (81 mg nightly) starting 12–28 weeks to ↓ preeclampsia risk.<br /></br>• Serial growth ultrasounds q3–4 wks from 28–32 wks for IUGR.<br /></br>• Tight BP control: labetalol, nifedipine, or methyldopa.  </p></br><p>If preeclampsia develops → magnesium sulfate for seizure prophylaxis and deliver at 37 wks (or earlier if severe).  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“CHaP” for Chronic Hypertension Pregnancy complications:<br /></br>C – Cesarean/preterm delivery<br /></br>H – Hypertension becomes preeclampsia (superimposed)<br /></br>a – abruption (placental)<br /></br>P – Poor growth (IUGR)  </p></br><p>Another: “CHTN moms SPaWN trouble”<br /></br>S – Superimposed Preeclampsia<br /></br>P – Placental abruption<br /></br>W – growth restriction (Weight &lt;10th percentile)<br /></br>N – Neonatal prematurity/mortality  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong  </p></br><p>a. Fetal demise – Chronic HTN does raise stillbirth risk, but the absolute risk increase is smaller than for superimposed preeclampsia. The exam wants the single greatest risk.  </p></br><p>b. Gestational diabetes – Risk linked to maternal obesity, age, previous GDM, but chronic HTN alone is not a major risk factor.  </p></br><p>c. Polyhydramnios – Typically due to uncontrolled diabetes, fetal anomalies, or isoimmunization; chronic HTN more often causes oligohydramnios via placental insufficiency.  </p></br><p>e. Preterm labor – Chronic HTN indirectly increases medically-indicated (iatrogenic) preterm delivery, but spontaneous preterm labor is less strongly tied than preeclampsia.  </p></br><p>Common pitfall: confusing medically indicated preterm birth due to preeclampsia with spontaneous preterm labor; the latter is not the primary risk emphasized here.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line  </p></br><p>A pregnant woman with chronic hypertension is most likely to develop superimposed preeclampsia; start low-dose aspirin and monitor closely.</p>
32. A 10-year-old boy with attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder is brought to the physician by his parents for a follow-up examination. He has a 5-year history of hyperactivity, impulsivity, and distractability at home and school. Cognitive behavioral therapy for the patient and his parents has yielded mild improvement in the patient’s symptoms. The patient has no other history of serious illness and takes no medications. A maternal cousin has epilepsy, and a maternal uncle died at the age of 25 years while playing basketball. The patient’s vital signs are within normal limits. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. The patient fidgets throughout the examination. Mental status examination shows easy distractibility. The parents say they are considering pharmacotherapy for the patient but are concerned about the potential adverse effects of psychostimulants. In addition to discussing the risks and benefits of pharmacotherapy for this patient’s condition, which of the following is the most appropriate next step before initiating psychostimulant therapy?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. ECG</br>b. EEG</br>c. Increased frequency of cognitive behavioral therapy sessions</br>d. Neuropsychological testing</br>e. Omega-3 acid ethyl ester supplementation</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>A. Obtain an electrocardiogram (ECG)</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues that steer the decision toward baseline cardiac screening:<br /></br>• Stimulants are being considered (methylphenidate, amphetamines) → sympathomimetic effects can precipitate tachyarrhythmias or sudden death in susceptible hearts.<br /></br>• Family history red flag: “maternal uncle died at the age of 25 years while playing basketball.” Sudden collapse during exertion suggests an inherited cardiac condition (e.g., hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, long-QT syndrome, catecholaminergic polymorphic VT).<br /></br>• No current cardiac symptoms, but any positive personal or family history of premature sudden cardiac death mandates further evaluation before exposing the child to stimulants.  </p></br><p>Metacognitive framework:<br /></br>Step 1 – Ask: “Will the proposed drug unmask or worsen a silent disease?”<br /></br>Step 2 – Screen for risk factors (FH, syncope, murmur).<br /></br>Step 3 – Order the simplest, lowest-risk test that can identify the hidden disease (ECG).  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><ol></br><li>ADHD first-line pharmacotherapy = psychostimulants.  </li></br><li>Stimulants raise HR and BP, and may trigger arrhythmia in occult structural/electrical heart disease.  </li></br><li>Current AHA/AAP guidance:<br /></br>   • Routine ECG NOT required for every child.<br /></br>   • BUT do obtain ECG (± cardiology referral) if there is:<br /></br>        – Personal history of syncope, palpitations, chest pain with exertion.<br /></br>        – Family history of sudden death &lt;35 y, cardiomyopathy, channelopathy.  </li></br><li>This patient meets the “family history” criterion → screening ECG is warranted before prescribing.  </li></br></ol></br><p>Take-home: Always screen with an ECG when stimulant therapy is contemplated in any child with a concerning cardiac history.  </p></br><p>Documentation (real life): “Reviewed family history of sudden cardiac death; plan ECG prior to initiating stimulant. Discussed risks/benefits with parents.”  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• Stimulants block NET/DAT → ↑ catecholamines → sympathetic surge.<br /></br>• In hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (HCM) or long-QT, increased sympathetic tone can provoke ventricular tachycardia/fibrillation → sudden death.<br /></br>• ECG can reveal: HCM (LVH, deep Q in I, aVL, V5-6), long QT, Brugada pattern, WPW, pre-excitation, conduction delays.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Diagnostic / Treatment Algorithm  </p></br><ol></br><li>H&amp;P → look for red flags.  </li></br><li>Red flag present → ECG ± echocardiogram, cardiology consult.  </li></br><li>If ECG normal → proceed with stimulant; monitor HR/BP at each visit.  </li></br><li>If abnormal → cardiology management before considering stimulants.  </li></br></ol></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memory Aids  </p></br><p>“FAST HEART” before stimulants:<br /></br>Family history, Arrhythmia symptoms, Syncope, Tachycardia → HEART test = ECG.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Options Are Wrong  </p></br><p>B. EEG – Screens for seizure focus; stimulants do not precipitate epilepsy, and the patient has no personal seizure history. Family epilepsy alone is NOT a contraindication.  </p></br><p>C. Increase CBT frequency – Reasonable adjunct, but the question asks for the step specifically “before initiating psychostimulant therapy.” CBT dose-escalation does not address cardiac risk.  </p></br><p>D. Neuropsychological testing – Helpful for diagnostic clarification or learning disorders, but ADHD diagnosis is already made; it does not mitigate safety concerns of stimulant initiation.  </p></br><p>E. Omega-3 acid ethyl ester – Some parents try dietary supplements, but there is no robust evidence these substitute for or improve safety of stimulant therapy, nor do they address sudden-death risk.  </p></br><p>Key contrast: Only an ECG directly screens for the lethal cardiac conditions implicated by the family history and therefore is the necessary safety step prior to prescribing stimulants.</p>
33. A 21-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department because of a 3-day history of vomiting and malaise and a 6-day history of abdominal cramping. One week ago, she had a medically induced abortion at 44 days’ gestation, with use of oral mifepristone and vaginal misoprostol. She has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. She follows a vegetarian diet. She appears ill. Temperature is 39.0 °C (102.0 °F), pulse is 151/min, respirations are 32/min, and blood pressure is 89/56 mm Hg. Physical examination discloses coarse breath sounds, a soft, nondistended abdomen, and 1+ pitting edema. Results of laboratory studies are shown:</br></br>Hemoglobin         16 g/dL  </br>Hematocrit         49 percent  </br>Leukocyte count    39,500/mm³  </br>Platelet count     498,810/mm³  </br></br>Serum  </br>Na⁺                135 mEq/L  </br>K⁺                 4.9 mEq/L  </br>Cl⁻                104 mEq/L  </br>HCO₃⁻              17 mEq/L  </br>Urea nitrogen      25 mg/dL  </br>Glucose [2-h postprandial]   99 mg/dL  </br>Creatinine         1.8 mg/dL  </br></br>Blood cultures grow gram-positive rods. Which of the following infectious agents is the most likely cause of these findings?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. Actinomyces meyeri  </br>b. Bacillus cereus  </br>c. Clostridium sordellii  </br>d. Erysipelothrix rhusiopathiae  </br>e. Nocardia brasiliensis</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>c. Clostridium sordellii  </p></br><p>─────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Setting &amp; timing: 6 days after a medically induced abortion using mifepristone + vaginal misoprostol. Post-abortal period is a classic set-up for Clostridial toxic shock.<br /></br>• Vital signs: High fever (39 °C), extreme tachycardia (151/min) and tachypnea (32/min), hypotension (89/56 mm Hg) → septic/toxic shock picture.<br /></br>• Labs:<br /></br>  – WBC 39,500/µL and Plt 498,000/µL = massive leukemoid reaction with thrombocytosis (hallmark for C. sordellii).<br /></br>  – Hct 49 % with hypotension and 1+ pitting edema → hemoconcentration from profound capillary leak (another trademark).<br /></br>  – Mild acute kidney injury (Cr 1.8) from hypoperfusion; anion-gap metabolic acidosis (HCO₃⁻ 17).<br /></br>• Microbiology: Blood cultures grow gram-positive rods. Only a few gram-positive rods routinely cause fulminant post-abortal sepsis; C. sordellii is the one that uniquely produces the above triad (leukemoid reaction + hemoconcentration + refractory hypotension).  </p></br><p>Metacognitive Pearls<br /></br>Step-2/ Shelf algorithm for post-abortal or postpartum shock:<br /></br>1. If rapid onset (≤1 wk) with massive leukocytosis and capillary leak, think Clostridium sordellii.<br /></br>2. If gas gangrene or foul uterine discharge, think C. perfringens.<br /></br>3. If toxic shock but associated with retained tampon or foreign body, think S. aureus or Group A strep.<br /></br>Remember “Clostridial toxic shock = Counts sky-high (WBC), Concentrated Hct, Collapsing BP”.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Step-wise link to the correct answer:<br /></br>1. Vaginal instrumentation (misoprostol insertion) → potential introduction of anaerobic spores.<br /></br>2. C. sordellii exotoxins (lethal toxin &amp; hemorrhagic toxin) → massive cytokine release, endothelial injury → capillary leak (edema, hemoconcentration), profound vasodilation (hypotension), and marrow stimulation (extreme leukocytosis, thrombocytosis).<br /></br>3. Gram-positive rod seen in blood culture clinches it.  </p></br><p>Take-home message: Clostridium sordellii causes rapidly fatal toxic shock after medically induced abortions or postpartum, characterized by marked leukocytosis, hemoconcentration, and refractory hypotension, often with minimal abdominal findings.  </p></br><p>Documentation items if this were real: hemodynamic status, timing in relation to abortion, WBC/Hct trend, blood culture organism, immediate broad-spectrum coverage including clindamycin + carbapenem, ICU transfer, consider hysterectomy.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Gram-positive, anaerobic, spore-forming rod closely related to C. difficile.<br /></br>• Virulence: Lethal toxin (LT) &amp; hemorrhagic toxin (HT) are glucosylating cytotoxins → endothelial &amp; myocardial damage → vascular leak and cardiac depression.<br /></br>• Incubation: 2–7 days after obstetric event.<br /></br>• Mortality ≈50–100 % despite therapy.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment / Diagnostic Work-up<br /></br>Immediate management (do NOT delay for cultures):<br /></br>1. Broad-spectrum IV antibiotics covering anaerobes &amp; resistant gram-positives. Typical empiric: IV piperacillin-tazobactam or a carbapenem PLUS clindamycin (inhibits toxin production).<br /></br>2. Aggressive fluid resuscitation, vasopressors, ICU.<br /></br>3. Early surgical evaluation—source control (uterine evacuation, hysterectomy) if uterine infection suspected.<br /></br>4. Supportive care for organ failure (renal replacement, mechanical ventilation).  </p></br><p>Key diagnostic tools: blood cultures (already positive), pelvic ultrasound to look for retained tissue, CBC (to monitor leukemoid reaction), serum lactate.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────<br /></br>High-Yield Memory Aids<br /></br>• “Sordid Sordellii” → Sordellii causes Sordid shock after Self-induced abortion.<br /></br>• 3 H’s of C. sordellii: Hematocrit ↑, High WBC, Hypotension.<br /></br>• Think “CLS” = C. sordellii, Leukemoid reaction, Shock.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>a. Actinomyces meyeri<br /></br>   – Branching filamentous gram-positive rod; causes chronic indolent pelvic infections with IUDs, not fulminant septic shock. Cultures show “molar tooth” colonies, not massive leukemoid reaction.  </p></br><p>b. Bacillus cereus<br /></br>   – Aerobic spore-former linked to reheated rice food poisoning or post-traumatic eye infections. Toxin-mediated vomiting/diarrhea occurs within hours, not 1 week later; shock and leukemoid reaction are not typical.  </p></br><p>d. Erysipelothrix rhusiopathiae<br /></br>   – Gram-positive rod from handling fish/meat; causes erysipeloid skin infection or endocarditis in farmers/butchers. Not associated with abortions or leukemoid reaction.  </p></br><p>e. Nocardia brasiliensis<br /></br>   – Weakly acid-fast, branching aerobic filamentous rod; causes chronic cutaneous or pulmonary infections in immunocompromised hosts. Presents over weeks–months, not acute toxic shock, and cultures show branching beaded rods rather than smooth gram-positive rods.  </p></br><p>Key discriminator: none of these organisms produce the triad of toxic shock, leukemoid reaction, and hemoconcentration after obstetric manipulation—only Clostridium sordellii does.</p>
34. A 65-year-old woman is admitted to the hospital because of a 1-day history of fever, and edema and erythema of the right arm. She says that overnight her right arm began throbbing and the redness worsened. She also had chills and increasingly severe arm pain throughout the night. Her temperature reached 38.9°C (102°F) at home. One year ago, she had stage II breast cancer treated with right partial mastectomy, lymphadenectomy, and radiation therapy. She was treated with a compression sleeve for lymphedema of the right upper extremity postoperatively. She has osteoarthritis of the shoulders and hands, which limits the use of her upper extremities. She says her symptoms have always been present since the operation, but the swelling has worsened since yesterday. Her temperature is 38.3°C (101°F), pulse is 88/min, respirations are 14/min, and blood pressure is 150/80 mm Hg. Examination shows a bright red, well-demarcated area from 3 cm below the elbow to 3 cm above the ulnar prominence; it is exquisitely tender to light touch. The patient has moderate tenderness to palpation of the right upper extremity. There are no open areas over the skin or fingers. Laboratory studies show:</br>Hemoglobin 11.4 g/dL</br>Hematocrit 35 percent</br>Mean corpuscular volume 82 µm3</br>Leukocyte count 19,200/mm3</br>Platelet count 260,000/mm3</br></br>Which of the following is the most likely infectious agent?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Candida albicans</br>b. Escherichia coli</br>c. Methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus</br>d. Pseudomonas aeruginosa</br>e. Streptococcus pyogenes (group A)</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>e. Streptococcus pyogenes (group A)</p></br><p>─────────</br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>1. Key clues<br /></br>   • Acute onset (overnight) of fever, chills, rapidly progressive pain and erythema<br /></br>   • Bright-red, well-demarcated plaque (“sharply raised edge”) → classic for erysipelas<br /></br>   • History of ipsilateral axillary lymph-node dissection &amp; radiation → chronic lymphedema, a well-known predisposing factor for streptococcal skin infection<br /></br>   • No open wounds or fluctuance → favors non-purulent cellulitis/erysipelas (GAS) over staphylococcal abscess-forming disease<br /></br>   • Leukocytosis (19 K) and fever reinforce acute invasive soft-tissue infection.</p></br><ol></br><li>Pertinent positives/negatives<br /></br>   • Positives: lymphedema, well-demarcated erythema, intense pain, systemic toxicity.<br /></br>   • Negatives: no pustules, abscess, drainage, or penetrating trauma—findings that would steer toward Staphylococcus aureus or gram-negative organisms.</li></br></ol></br><p>Cognitive framework: On NBME/Step 2, think “bright red with sharp borders + lymphedema/breast surgery = erysipelas → group A Strep.”</p></br><p>─────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Step-wise approach<br /></br>1. Identify pattern: Well-demarcated erythema = erysipelas.<br /></br>2. Ask “Which bugs cause erysipelas?” → almost always group A β-hemolytic Streptococcus (rarely groups C/G).<br /></br>3. Check predisposing factors: surgical lymphatic interruption → impaired local immune clearance → streptococcal entry via microscopic skin breaks.<br /></br>4. Conclude causative agent: Streptococcus pyogenes.</p></br><p>Take-home message: Non-purulent cellulitis or erysipelas in lymphedematous limbs is classically due to GAS; treat with IV β-lactam active against Streptococcus (e.g., cefazolin, penicillin G, or ceftriaxone ± clindamycin for toxin suppression).</p></br><p>Documentation in real life: record size/location of erythema, presence/absence of bullae or necrosis, baseline limb circumference, vitals, sepsis screen, empiric antibiotic choice.</p></br><p>─────────</br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Erysipelas = infection of superficial dermis + lymphatics.<br /></br>• GAS produces hyaluronidase, streptokinase, DNases → rapid spread along lymphatic channels.<br /></br>• Lymphatic removal (surgery/radiation) → stagnant protein-rich fluid → poor immune cell trafficking → high infection risk.<br /></br>• GAS exotoxins can trigger fever, severe pain, and systemic inflammatory response.</p></br><p>─────────</br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostics<br /></br>• No imaging needed unless concern for necrotizing fasciitis.<br /></br>• Labs: CBC (already shows leukocytosis); blood cultures for hospitalized patients.<br /></br>• Empiric IV therapy: cefazolin or nafcillin/oxacillin; add clindamycin if toxin-mediated disease suspected.<br /></br>• Switch to oral penicillin VK or amoxicillin once afebrile.<br /></br>• Elevation, analgesia, consider prophylactic penicillin for recurrent episodes in lymphedema patients.</p></br><p>─────────</br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“Red, Raised, Rapid, and Rectangular borders = stRep (Group A) Erysipelas.”<br /></br>“BREAST surgery → BRIGHT BORDER → B-hemolytic Strep.”</p></br><p>─────────</br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong<br /></br>a. Candida albicans – Causes intertrigo, thrush, chronic paronychia; not an acute febrile cellulitis with well-demarcated erythema.<br /></br>b. Escherichia coli – Gram-negative cellulitis occurs in postoperative wounds, perineal infections, or neutropenia; uncommon in upper-extremity lymphedema.<br /></br>c. Methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus – More likely with purulent drainage, abscess, penetrating trauma, IV drug use. Erysipelas’ sharply raised margin points away from staphylococcal infection.<br /></br>d. Pseudomonas aeruginosa – Think burns, hot-tub folliculitis, ecthyma gangrenosum in neutropenia; not typical here.</p></br><p>─────────</br>Core Takeaway<br /></br>Erysipelas presenting on a limb with chronic lymphedema after breast cancer surgery is almost always caused by Streptococcus pyogenes (group A).</p>
35. A 22-year-old woman comes to the clinic because her sexual partner told her that he was treated for Chlamydia trachomatis infection 1 week ago. They have not had sexual intercourse since he was treated. She has been sexually active and monogamous with this partner for 2 months and uses a vaginal contraceptive ring correctly and condoms inconsistently. The patient was treated for C. trachomatis infection 4 months ago, but she did not return for follow-up testing for reinfection. She has no other history of serious illness and currently takes no medications. Physical examination, including pelvic examination, shows no abnormalities. Testing for C. trachomatis is done. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Azithromycin therapy now</br>b. Cefixime therapy now</br>c. Clindamycin therapy now</br>d. Contact the partner’s physician to confirm treatment</br>e. Withhold treatment until patient’s test results are available</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer</br>A. Azithromycin therapy now (single-dose 1 g orally)</p></br><p>================================================================</br>Vignette Analysis</br>• “Sexual partner … treated for Chlamydia trachomatis infection 1 week ago” = recent, confirmed exposure.<br /></br>• “They have not had intercourse since he was treated” rules out NEW exposure, but she very likely acquired infection during the 2-month relationship before his therapy.<br /></br>• “Previously treated for C trachomatis 4 months ago … did not return for follow-up” = prior infection → higher reinfection risk; CDC classifies any exposure within the past 60 days as warranting empiric treatment.<br /></br>• “Asymptomatic” and normal pelvic exam do NOT exclude infection (70–80 % of cases are silent).  </p></br><p>Metacognitive approach:<br /></br>1. Identify whether the patient is a recent sexual contact of a diagnosed case.<br /></br>2. Recall CDC rule: Treat all partners within the preceding 60 days immediately, regardless of symptoms or pending test results.<br /></br>3. Select first-line drug: azithromycin 1 g PO × 1 (or doxycycline 100 mg BID × 7 d).</p></br><p>================================================================</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning</br>Step-wise thought process:<br /></br>1. Confirm exposure window &lt; 60 days → treat empirically.<br /></br>2. Choose regimen: Azithromycin single dose preferred for compliance; safe in pregnancy.<br /></br>3. Counsel: abstain from sex for 7 days after both partners treated; repeat screening in 3 months.</p></br><p>Core takeaway: In chlamydia control, PUBLIC-HEALTH urgency &gt; laboratory confirmation. Immediate empiric treatment of contacts prevents PID, infertility, and ongoing community spread.</p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real life): document counseling on abstinence, partner notification, medication given (drug, dose, route), plan for rescreening at 3 months.</p></br><p>================================================================</br>Pathophysiology Review (high-yield)</br>• Organism: Chlamydia trachomatis serovars D–K; obligate intracellular, lacks peptidoglycan.<br /></br>• Transmission: genital secretions; women 15–24 y highest incidence.<br /></br>• Natural history: cervicitis → ascending spread → PID → infertility, ectopic pregnancy, chronic pelvic pain.<br /></br>• Asymptomatic carriage common; therefore screen ALL sexually active women ≤ 25 y annually.</p></br><p>================================================================</br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Test Review</br>First-line:<br /></br>• Azithromycin 1 g PO once (directly observed if possible)<br /></br>OR<br /></br>• Doxycycline 100 mg PO BID × 7 days (avoid in pregnancy, adherence issues).</p></br><p>Gonorrhea co-treatment: If index case had concurrent Neisseria or if coinfection status unknown, give ceftriaxone 500 mg IM once. In this vignette, partner was treated only for chlamydia; no requirement to give ceftriaxone unless her test later returns positive for N gonorrhoeae.</p></br><p>Follow-up:<br /></br>• Test of cure 3–4 weeks ONLY if pregnant, symptoms persist, or adherence in doubt.<br /></br>• Routine rescreening at 3 months for all cases because of high reinfection rate.</p></br><p>================================================================</br>Mnemonic / Memory Aid</br>“60-DAY RULE for CHLAMYDIA”</br>If partner ± patient diagnosed, then:</br>• ≤ 60 days exposure → Z-pack TODAY<br /></br>“ONE (1) gram, ONE (1) day, stops the fun (sex) for ONE (1) week.”</p></br><p>================================================================</br>Incorrect Answer Analysis</br>B. Cefixime therapy now: Cefixime (oral 800 mg) is used for gonorrhea when ceftriaxone unavailable. It is NOT adequate for chlamydia and is no longer preferred even for gonorrhea in the U.S.</p></br><p>C. Clindamycin therapy now: Clindamycin (oral or intravaginal) treats bacterial vaginosis, not chlamydia.</p></br><p>D. Contact the partner’s physician to confirm treatment: Not required and violates patient autonomy/possible privacy issues. Management hinges on THIS patient’s exposure, not documentation of the partner’s therapy.</p></br><p>E. Withhold treatment until test results are available: Delays care, fosters continued transmission, and risks PID. CDC specifically recommends empiric treatment of exposed partners without waiting for NAAT results.</p></br><p>================================================================</br>Key Exam Pearl</br>Whenever you see “recent sexual contact of a patient treated for chlamydia” → treat immediately with azithromycin (or doxycycline) regardless of symptoms or pending labs.</p>
36. A 57-year-old man comes to the office because of a 3-week history of generalized itchiness and yellow eyes. He has not had abdominal pain, fever, or night sweats. He has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. He smoked two packs of cigarettes daily for 20 years but quit 17 years ago. Vital signs are within normal limits. Examination shows conjunctival icterus. The abdomen is soft and nontender; there are no masses. A CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis shows a 3-cm mass at the head of the pancreas and dilated common bile and intrahepatic bile ducts. Endoscopic retrograde cholangiopancreatography (ERCP) shows no mucosal abnormality. A stent is placed in the common bile duct. Results of bile culture and cytology are negative. Results of a fine-needle biopsy of the mass show no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Endoscopy-guided biopsy</br>b. HIDA scan</br>c. Repeat abdominal and pelvic CT scan in 3 months</br>d. Repeat ERCP in 3 months</br>e. Serum carcinoembryonic antigen assay</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>a. Endoscopy-guided biopsy (endoscopic ultrasound-guided fine-needle/core biopsy of the pancreatic mass)</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Key “red-flag” words: 3-week pruritus + conjunctival icterus (painless obstructive jaundice), CT: 3-cm head-of-pancreas mass + dilated common bile &amp; intra-hepatic ducts (“double-duct sign”).<br /></br>• Pertinent negatives: no abdominal pain, fever, weight loss noted, but absence of pain favors malignancy over gallstone disease.<br /></br>• Previous work-up:<br /></br>  – ERCP with stent: no cholangiographic mucosal lesion → cholangiocarcinoma less likely.<br /></br>  – Bile cytology &amp; CT-guided FNA of the mass: both negative (known false-negative rate 15-25% for pancreatic adenocarcinoma).<br /></br>• Cognitive approach: When imaging shows a discrete pancreatic mass causing biliary obstruction, malignancy probability remains high (&gt;85%) even with one negative biopsy. The algorithm:<br /></br>  1) High-quality imaging →<br /></br>  2) If tissue diagnosis needed, obtain highest-yield modality →<br /></br>  3) EUS-guided biopsy is next, because it samples the center of the lesion under direct visualization with the highest sensitivity (≈90-95%).<br /></br>• Metacognitive tip: any time radiologic and clinical suspicion of cancer is high but first biopsy is negative, think of “sampling error → repeat biopsy with the most sensitive modality.”</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Step-wise logic to correct choice:<br /></br>1. Obstructive jaundice + pancreatic head mass = presumed pancreatic adenocarcinoma until proven otherwise.<br /></br>2. Tissue confirmation is required before planning definitive therapy (surgery, chemotherapy).<br /></br>3. The first FNA was image-guided through the abdominal wall → lower yield because of necrotic tumor centers and sampling angle.<br /></br>4. Endoscopic ultrasound (EUS) allows real-time visualization of the pancreas through the gastric/duodenal wall and targets viable peripheral tumor → highest diagnostic accuracy.<br /></br>Core takeaway: In suspected pancreatic cancer with nondiagnostic initial FNA, proceed to EUS-guided biopsy for definitive histology.</p></br><p>Documentation “must write”: “Patient with obstructive jaundice and pancreatic head mass; prior percutaneous FNA nondiagnostic. Plan: EUS-guided fine-needle/core biopsy for tissue confirmation prior to staging and surgical evaluation.”</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review (high-yield)<br /></br>• Pancreatic adenocarcinoma arises from ductal epithelium; 60–70% in the head → compresses common bile duct → painless jaundice, dark urine, pale stools, pruritus.<br /></br>• Risk factors: smoking (present), chronic pancreatitis, diabetes, hereditary pancreatitis, BRCA2, Peutz-Jeghers.<br /></br>• Double-duct sign (dilated CBD + pancreatic duct) on imaging is almost pathognomonic.<br /></br>• False-negative biopsy rate: Percutaneous FNA ≈20%; EUS-guided ≈5–10%.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Treatments and Diagnostic Tests Review<br /></br>Diagnostic algorithm:<br /></br>1. Contrast CT/MRI → identify mass &amp; assess vascular invasion.<br /></br>2. Tissue diagnosis: EUS-guided FNA is preferred first in many centers; if unavailable or nondiagnostic, repeat with EUS.<br /></br>3. Staging (CT chest, PET/CT, laparoscopy) once histology confirmed.<br /></br>4. Management:<br /></br>   • Resectable (no SMA/SMV, celiac, or hepatic artery invasion) → Whipple (pancreaticoduodenectomy) + adjuvant chemo.<br /></br>   • Borderline/locally advanced → neoadjuvant FOLFIRINOX or gemcitabine/nab-paclitaxel.<br /></br>   • Metastatic → systemic chemotherapy ± palliative biliary stent (already placed here).</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“Head hurts the ducts” → Head of pancreas tumor obstructs BOTH bile &amp; pancreatic ducts → “double-duct sign.”<br /></br>“EUS is the ace when FNA strikes out.” → If first FNA (strike) misses, the “ace” (EUS) gets the tissue.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>b. HIDA scan – used to diagnose acute cholecystitis or biliary dyskinesia, not a pancreatic mass. No RUQ pain, fever, leukocytosis.<br /></br>c. Repeat CT in 3 months – delays diagnosis and treatment of probable cancer; current imaging is already diagnostic for mass.<br /></br>d. Repeat ERCP in 3 months – ERCP is mainly therapeutic/palliative (stenting) or for cholangiocarcinoma brushings; offers no better pancreatic sampling than EUS and would again delay care.<br /></br>e. Serum carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) – nonspecific; CA 19-9 is the more relevant marker but is neither sensitive nor specific enough to replace biopsy. Tumor markers never delay acquisition of tissue.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Core Takeaway<br /></br>High radiographic suspicion of pancreatic cancer + nondiagnostic first biopsy → proceed immediately to the most sensitive tissue-acquisition method: endoscopic ultrasound-guided biopsy.</p>
37. An 18-year-old man is brought to the emergency department by paramedics 20 minutes after he sustained a gunshot wound to his head. En route to the hospital, he was intubated, mechanically ventilated, and administered 2 L of intravenous 0.9 percent saline. On arrival, he is unresponsive. His Glasgow Coma Scale score is 3. Examination shows fixed and dilated pupils. Findings of brain death are confirmed by a neurosurgeon and trauma surgeon. The most appropriate person to approach this patient’s family about organ donation is which of the following?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. The neurosurgeon</br>b. A pastoral care representative</br>c. A representative from the organ procurement organization</br>d. A representative from the transplant team</br>e. The trauma surgeon</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>C. A representative from the organ procurement organization (OPO)</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis &amp; Test-Taking Framework  </p></br><p>Key clues<br /></br>• “Findings of brain death are confirmed” → patient is legally dead.<br /></br>• Next step is the donation discussion.<br /></br>• Question asks “Who is the most appropriate person to approach the family?” – i.e., who should make the request, not whether donation should occur.  </p></br><p>NBME/USMLE pattern<br /></br>After death (or imminent death) is declared, federal law (Uniform Anatomical Gift Act; CMS “Conditions of Participation”) requires that the hospital notify its regional OPO, and that a trained “designated requestor” from that OPO— not the treating team— speaks with the family. This eliminates conflict of interest and maximizes consent rates.</p></br><p>Metacognitive tip<br /></br>When a question mentions brain death + organ donation, immediately think “OPO representative” unless the stem explicitly states that the OPO has already been notified.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Step-wise thought process<br /></br>1. Brain death = legal death → donation discussion is appropriate.<br /></br>2. Conflict-of-interest rule: the person who provided care OR who will receive the organs should NOT request consent.<br /></br>3. Federal regulation: only a trained OPO representative (or a hospital staff member specifically trained and certified by the OPO) should approach the family.<br /></br>→ Choose answer C.</p></br><p>Take-home message<br /></br>The treating physicians pronounce death; the OPO makes the donation request.</p></br><p>Documentation pearl<br /></br>Chart should record time of brain-death declaration, names of physicians, notification time to OPO, and whether family was approached by OPO.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review (brief)  </p></br><p>• Brain death = irreversible cessation of all brain and brain-stem function (fixed dilated pupils, absent reflexes, apnea test).<br /></br>• Once declared, patient is legally dead under all U.S. jurisdictions.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Management &amp; Legal Algorithms  </p></br><ol></br><li>Pronounce brain death (2 physicians in many states).  </li></br><li>Notify OPO immediately.  </li></br><li>OPO representative (designated requestor) meets with family → obtains consent.  </li></br><li>Transplant team becomes involved only AFTER consent.  </li></br><li>Supportive care continues to preserve organ perfusion.</li></br></ol></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tip  </p></br><p>“ORGANS → OPO Gets Request And Notifies Survivors”</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong  </p></br><p>A. The neurosurgeon – Treated the patient; potential conflict of interest. Federal rules discourage treating physicians from requesting donation.  </p></br><p>B. Pastoral care representative – Provides emotional/spiritual support but is not the trained “designated requestor.” May accompany the OPO representative but should not lead the consent discussion.  </p></br><p>D. A representative from the transplant team – Direct recipient interest (strong conflict), explicitly barred from initiating consent.  </p></br><p>E. The trauma surgeon – Also a treating physician; same conflict-of-interest issue as with the neurosurgeon.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Bottom Line  </p></br><p>After brain death, the family should be approached by a trained representative of the regional organ procurement organization—not by the treating physicians or future transplant team.</p>
38. A 42-year-old woman who underwent an endoscopic sinus operation 30 minutes ago has the sudden onset of tachycardia and hypotension. The procedure was completed under general anesthesia without complications, and estimated blood loss was 25 mL. She has a 10-year history of asthma treated with oral prednisone. She is awake and responsive. Her temperature is 38.1°C (100.5°F), pulse is 120/min and regular, respirations are 18/min, and blood pressure is 75/50 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on 2 L/min of oxygen by nasal cannula shows an oxygen saturation of 90 percent. Examination shows no abnormalities. An ECG shows sinus tachycardia with no acute ST-segment changes. In addition to administration of intravenous fluids, which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Arterial blood gas analysis</br>b. Chest x-ray</br>c. Determination of hematocrit</br>d. Intravenous administration of ephedrine</br>e. Intravenous administration of hydrocortisone</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>e. Intravenous administration of hydrocortisone  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues that immediately narrow the differential:<br /></br>• Timing – 30 min after completion of surgery (post-anesthesia care unit).<br /></br>• Hemodynamics – sudden hypotension (75/50 mm Hg) and sinus tachycardia (120/min).<br /></br>• Negatives – Estimated blood loss only 25 mL, normal ECG, clear lungs, no surgical bleeding on exam.<br /></br>• Past history – 10-year history of asthma treated with oral prednisone (chronic exogenous glucocorticoids).<br /></br>• Low-grade fever and relative hypoxia (SpO₂ 90 % on 2 L).  </p></br><p>Pertinent positives: chronic steroid use + physiologic stress of surgery.<br /></br>Pertinent negatives: no large blood loss, no arrhythmia/MI, no bronchospasm, no obvious anaphylaxis or tension pneumothorax.  </p></br><p>Test-taking framework for sudden PACU hypotension:<br /></br>“BASIC PACT”<br /></br>Bleeding<br /></br>Anaphylaxis<br /></br>Sepsis<br /></br>Infarction (MI/PE)<br /></br>Cardiogenic (arrhythmia)<br /></br>Pneumothorax<br /></br>Adrenal crisis<br /></br>Cardiac tamponade<br /></br>Tox/meds  </p></br><p>The vignette systematically rules out most causes, leaving adrenal crisis as the best fit.  </p></br><p>Metacognitive tip: Whenever you see peri-operative hypotension in a patient who has been on ≥5 mg/day prednisone (or equivalent) for ≥3 weeks in the last year, immediately think “stress-dose steroids.”  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><ol></br><li>Chronic oral prednisone suppresses the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis.  </li></br><li>Surgical stress requires 5-10× the basal cortisol level. These patients cannot mount that response.  </li></br><li>Result = acute adrenal (Addisonian) crisis → vasodilatory shock ( ↓SVR, hypotension), tachycardia, low-grade fever, possible hyponatremia, hypoglycemia.  </li></br><li>Initial step: aggressive isotonic fluids (already ordered in stem).  </li></br><li>Definitive next step: give IV stress-dose glucocorticoid—hydrocortisone 100 mg IV bolus (then q8 h or continuous infusion). Hydrocortisone also provides mineralocorticoid activity.  </li></br></ol></br><p>Take-home message: Always give peri-operative stress-dose steroids to chronic steroid users; if missed, treat adrenal crisis emergently with IV hydrocortisone.  </p></br><p>Documentation (real patient): chronic glucocorticoid history, suspected adrenal insufficiency, vitals, fluids given, hydrocortisone dose/time, plan for ICU monitoring and electrolyte/glucose checks.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• Exogenous steroids → negative feedback on CRH &amp; ACTH → adrenal atrophy within weeks.<br /></br>• Under stress (infection, surgery, trauma) cortisol demand rises, but atrophic adrenals cannot respond.<br /></br>• Cortisol deficiency → impaired catecholamine sensitivity of arterioles → vasodilation, hypotension; impaired gluconeogenesis → hypoglycemia; cytokine surge → fever.  </p></br><p>Risk factors: &gt;20 mg/day prednisone for &gt;3 weeks, Cushingoid features, evening dosing, recent cessation (&lt;1 year).  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Management Algorithm  </p></br><ol></br><li>Prevention: 50-100 mg IV hydrocortisone at induction, then 50 mg IV q8 h for 24 h (major surgery).  </li></br><li>If crisis occurs:<br /></br>   • Bolus 1-2 L isotonic saline ± dextrose.<br /></br>   • Hydrocortisone 100 mg IV immediately, repeat q6-8 h.<br /></br>   • Check electrolytes, glucose, ACTH stim later (do not delay treatment).  </li></br><li>Taper back to home dose once stable.  </li></br></ol></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“Steroid SWEAT” – Stress-dose With Every major Anesthesia or Trauma for chronic steroid patients.  </p></br><p>Or think “No cortisol? Give SOLU-CORTEF (hydrocortisone) STAT.”  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>a. Arterial blood gas analysis – Helpful if suspect PE, aspiration, or respiratory failure. Her oxygenation is only mildly low and ABG will not treat the life-threatening shock. Does not address root cause.  </p></br><p>b. Chest x-ray – Would detect pneumothorax, pneumonia, or atelectasis, none of which explain profound hypotension with tiny blood loss.  </p></br><p>c. Determination of hematocrit – Useful if bleeding is suspected. With EBL 25 mL and no clinical bleeding, anemia is not the culprit.  </p></br><p>d. Intravenous ephedrine – A vasopressor that may transiently raise BP but wears off quickly and ignores the cortisol deficit. Without steroids, patient may remain refractory to pressors.  </p></br><p>e. Intravenous hydrocortisone – Correct; replaces deficient cortisol, restores vascular tone, and treats the underlying adrenal crisis.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line:<br /></br>Post-op patient + chronic steroids + sudden hypotension = think adrenal crisis. After fluids, immediately administer stress-dose IV hydrocortisone.</p>
39. A 32-year-old woman comes to the office because of a painless mass in the right side of her neck that she first noticed 2 months ago. The mass has not increased in size since she first saw it, and she reports no other masses. Medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no medications. She is 170 cm (5 ft 7 in) tall and weighs 57 kg (125 lb); BMI is 20 kg/m2. Vital signs are within normal limits. Examination shows a 2-cm mass in the right upper lobe of the thyroid gland. There is no lymphadenopathy. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in evaluation?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. CT scan of the chest</br>b. CT scan of the neck</br>c. Fine-needle aspiration biopsy of the mass</br>d. Ultrasonography of the thyroid gland</br>e. Whole-body PET scan</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>d. Ultrasonography of the thyroid gland  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Key clues<br /></br>  – “2-cm mass in the right upper lobe of the thyroid” → isolated thyroid nodule.<br /></br>  – Age 32, female, no compressive symptoms, euthyroid on exam → typical presentation of an incidental thyroid nodule.<br /></br>  – No lymphadenopathy or local invasion signs → low-intermediate pretest probability for malignancy.  </p></br><p>• How to approach any thyroid nodule on exams (“3-Step Rule”):<br /></br>  1. Confirm the nodule and look for sonographic risk features → Thyroid ultrasound (US).<br /></br>  2. Check thyroid function → Serum TSH (often ordered simultaneously with US).<br /></br>  3. Decide on FNA or radioiodine scan based on US appearance and TSH results.  </p></br><p>Metacognitive tip: Shelf/Step questions love the algorithm. If the stem gives you a palpable nodule but no prior US, the next step is almost always “Ultrasound ± TSH,” never jump straight to FNA unless the stem explicitly says the US already shows a solid hypoechoic nodule.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>• Goal of the first test is to risk-stratify:<br /></br>  – US tells you size, solid vs cystic, microcalcifications, vascularity, margins, extrathyroidal extension, cervical nodes.<br /></br>  – Findings then dictate whether the 2-cm nodule needs FNA (solid, hypoechoic, irregular margins, microcalcifications) or surveillance (purely cystic &lt;4 cm).  </p></br><p>Core takeaway: Ultrasound is the universal entry point for evaluating a newly discovered thyroid nodule.  </p></br><p>Documentation bullets (real life):<br /></br>– Location, size (three dimensions), sonographic characteristics, cervical node survey, plan for FNA vs follow-up.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Thyroid nodules are common (≈5% palpable, 50% incidental on imaging).<br /></br>• Malignancy risk ≈5-10%; higher with male sex, childhood radiation, rapid growth, hoarseness, cervical nodes, microcalcifications, hypoechoic solid lesions on US.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Management Algorithm (simplified)<br /></br>1. Palpable nodule → Thyroid US + TSH.<br /></br>2. If TSH low → add radionuclide (I-123) scan. “Hot” → treat hyperthyroidism; no FNA.<br /></br>3. If TSH normal/high OR nodule cold on scan → use US size/appearance to decide:<br /></br>   • Solid &amp; ≥1 cm (or any suspicious feature) → FNA.<br /></br>   • Purely cystic or spongiform &lt;4 cm → observe.<br /></br>4. Malignant or indeterminate cytology → surgery or molecular testing.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“U-S-T” = Ultrasound, Serum TSH, Then (maybe) FNA. Visualize the nodule riding an elevator: Ground floor = palpation, 1st stop US, 2nd stop TSH, top floor FNA. It can’t skip floors!  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>a. CT scan of the chest<br /></br>   • Used to stage thyroid cancer with suspected mediastinal spread or lung mets; not for initial nodule work-up. No suspicious LNs or cancer diagnosis yet.  </p></br><p>b. CT scan of the neck<br /></br>   • Helpful if invasive/anaplastic cancer, large goiters causing airway compression, or pre-op planning. Not first line for a small, uncomplicated nodule; exposes patient to contrast and radiation.  </p></br><p>c. Fine-needle aspiration biopsy<br /></br>   • Correct next step only after ultrasound confirms worrisome sonographic features or size criteria. Performing FNA blindly risks sampling error and skips the triage algorithm.  </p></br><p>e. Whole-body PET scan<br /></br>   • Reserved for high-grade or metastatic cancers (e.g., recurrent thyroid cancer with elevated thyroglobulin &amp; negative radioiodine scan). Far too aggressive and not cost-effective here.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line<br /></br>First evaluate any newly discovered thyroid nodule with high-resolution thyroid ultrasonography (and serum TSH). Ultrasound findings then dictate whether FNA, radioiodine scan, or simple surveillance is appropriate.</p>
40. A 10-year-old boy is brought to the office by his mother for a well-child examination. He has no history of serious illness and receives no medications. He is in no distress. He is at the 75th percentile for height, 50th percentile for weight, and 20th percentile for BMI. Vital signs are within normal limits. On cardiac examination, a mid-systolic ejection murmur is heard best at the left sternal border; there is an S4. No other abnormalities are noted. Echocardiography is most likely to show which of the following?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Defect of the ventricular septum  </br>b. Hypertrophy of the left ventricle  </br>c. Impaired contraction of the right ventricle  </br>d. Myxoma of the tricuspid valve  </br>e. Regurgitant flow across the aortic valve  </br>f. Stenosis of the mitral valve</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>B. Hypertrophy of the left ventricle</p></br><p>─────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Mid-systolic (“ejection”) murmur best heard at the left sternal border → classic location for the dynamic outflow murmur of hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (HCM).<br /></br>• S4 gallop → produced by atrial kick against a stiff, non-compliant ventricle; strongly suggests concentric or asymmetric ventricular hypertrophy rather than a benign (Still’s) murmur.<br /></br>• Completely healthy child, normal vitals, normal growth → rules out volume-overload lesions (VSD) or chronic heart failure.<br /></br>• No diastolic component, no click, no radiation to neck/carotids → argues against aortic stenosis; no holosystolic component → argues against VSD; no diastolic blowing murmur → argues against aortic regurgitation.</p></br><p>Take-home pattern: Child/teen + crescendo–decrescendo murmur at LSB + S4 = think HCM → echo shows asymmetric LV hypertrophy (often septal predominance) with possible systolic anterior motion (SAM) of the mitral valve.</p></br><p>Test-taking framework for future questions<br /></br>1. Is the murmur systolic, diastolic, or continuous?<br /></br>2. Where is it loudest and does it radiate?<br /></br>3. Are there extra heart sounds (S3, S4), clicks, or rubs?<br /></br>4. Correlate with age, symptoms, vitals to narrow the DDx.<br /></br>5. For any suspected structural heart disease, echo is the diagnostic test of choice.</p></br><p>─────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Step 1: The presence of an S4 forces you to abandon the benign/functional murmur pathway.<br /></br>Step 2: A systolic, crescendo-decrescendo murmur at the LSB → either pulmonic stenosis or HCM.<br /></br>Step 3: Pulmonic stenosis usually has a systolic ejection click and often right-sided findings; none are described.<br /></br>Step 4: HCM fits perfectly and the hallmark imaging finding is left-ventricular (often asymmetric septal) hypertrophy → choose “Hypertrophy of the left ventricle.”</p></br><p>Core concept: HCM is an autosomal-dominant sarcomere protein disorder that produces LV hypertrophy without chamber dilation, generating a harsh systolic murmur and an S4.</p></br><p>Documentation points if this were a real patient<br /></br>• Character, location, and maneuvers that change the murmur.<br /></br>• Presence of S4.<br /></br>• Family history of sudden cardiac death.<br /></br>• Plan: order transthoracic echo; counsel on exercise restriction; consider genetic testing.</p></br><p>─────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Etiology: AD mutations (β-myosin heavy chain, myosin-binding protein C).<br /></br>• Morphology: Asymmetric septal hypertrophy &gt; free wall; can obstruct LV outflow when septum and anterior mitral leaflet approximate in systole (SAM).<br /></br>• Hemodynamics: Stiff LV → impaired diastolic filling → S4; dynamic obstruction → crescendo-decrescendo murmur that increases with maneuvers that ↓ preload (standing, Valsalva).</p></br><p>─────────────────────────</br>Diagnostics &amp; Treatment (high-yield bullets)<br /></br>Echo findings:<br /></br>– Asymmetric LV septal hypertrophy (septum:posterior wall ≥1.3:1).<br /></br>– Systolic anterior motion of mitral valve ± LVOT gradient.</p></br><p>Management:<br /></br>1. First-line: β-blocker (e.g., metoprolol) to slow HR and improve filling.<br /></br>2. Alternatives: Non-dihydropyridine CCB (verapamil) or disopyramide.<br /></br>3. Lifestyle: Avoid dehydration &amp; intense competitive sports.<br /></br>4. Procedural: Septal myectomy or alcohol septal ablation if refractory; ICD if high-risk for sudden death.</p></br><p>─────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“HCM = 4 letters → gives you an S4.”<br /></br>“Hypertrophic murmur Hates preload” (standing or Valsalva ↑ murmur).<br /></br>Echo shows “SAM &amp; Slam” (Systolic Anterior Motion causing slam-shut LVOT).</p></br><p>─────────────────────────</br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong<br /></br>A. Ventricular septal defect – Produces a harsh HOLOsystolic murmur at the LSB; S4 is not typical and murmur is not limited to mid-systole.<br /></br>C. Impaired contraction of the right ventricle – Would manifest with right-sided failure signs (hepatomegaly, peripheral edema) and often a holosystolic tricuspid regurgitant murmur.<br /></br>D. Myxoma of the tricuspid valve – Rare in children; classically causes a “tumor plop” early diastolic sound and variable obstruction, not a midsystolic ejection murmur.<br /></br>E. Regurgitant flow across the aortic valve – Early diastolic blowing decrescendo murmur best at LSB, not midsystolic; often an S3 rather than S4.<br /></br>F. Stenosis of the mitral valve – Low-pitched diastolic rumble with opening snap at apex, not a midsystolic murmur.</p></br><p>─────────────────────────</br>Key Takeaway<br /></br>In a child with a midsystolic murmur at the left sternal border plus an S4, think hypertrophic cardiomyopathy; confirm with echocardiography demonstrating left-ventricular (asymmetric septal) hypertrophy.</p>
41. A 25-year-old woman comes to the office for a follow-up examination 4 weeks after being diagnosed with HIV infection. She feels well. She used intravenous drugs for 5 years but stopped 2 years ago. She has been sexually active with one male partner during the past year; they use condoms consistently. Results of PPD skin testing 6 months ago were negative. She has no other history of serious illness and takes no medications. Her pulse is 68/min, respirations are 14/min, and blood pressure is 105/60 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on room air shows an oxygen saturation of 98 percent. Examination shows no abnormalities. Laboratory studies show:</br>Hemoglobin 14 g/dL</br>Hematocrit 42 percent</br>Leukocyte count 9000/mm^3</br>Platelet count 350,000/mm^3</br>Serum</br>  Glucose 90 mg/dL</br>  Creatinine 0.8 mg/dL</br>  Total bilirubin 0.5 mg/dL</br>  Alkaline phosphatase 50 U/L</br>  AST 35 U/L</br>  ALT 35 U/L</br>Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Chest x-ray</br>b. CT scan of the head</br>c. Determination of hemoglobin A1c</br>d. Hepatitis B surface antigen assay</br>e. Varicella-zoster virus polymerase chain reaction test</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>d. Hepatitis B surface antigen (HBsAg) assay  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis &amp; Test-Taking Framework  </p></br><ol></br><li>Patient type: Newly diagnosed, asymptomatic HIV-positive adult.  </li></br><li>Key risk factors: Former IV drug use → high prevalence of blood-borne viruses (HBV, HCV).  </li></br><li>Purpose of visit: “Follow-up examination 4 weeks after being diagnosed with HIV infection” → the classic “baseline HIV evaluation” question.  </li></br><li>Vital signs, PE, and basic labs are normal → no acute problem; think screening/ staging labs.  </li></br></ol></br><p>USMLE/ NBME pattern: Whenever you see “new HIV diagnosis” + “feels well,” immediately recall the standard baseline work-up check-list:</p></br><p>• CD4 count &amp; HIV viral load (assumed already sent)<br /></br>• Complete metabolic profile, CBC (already in stem)<br /></br>• RPR/VDRL, gonorrhea/chlamydia NAAT, toxoplasma IgG, CMV IgG<br /></br>• HAV immunity, HBV serologies, HCV antibody<br /></br>• TB screening (PPD/IGRA)<br /></br>• Vaccination status review<br /></br>The next unanswered item in that list here is HBV serologies.</p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Why HBsAg now?<br /></br>• Coinfection prevalence: 5-10 % of people with HIV in the USA have chronic HBV, higher in former IV drug users.<br /></br>• Management impact: If HBV-positive, choose dual-active antiretrovirals (e.g., tenofovir-alafenamide or -disoproxil + emtricitabine/lamivudine) to treat BOTH viruses, and avoid stopping those drugs in future (prevents fulminant hepatitis).<br /></br>• Prevention: If HBV-negative &amp; non-immune, give HBV vaccine ASAP.  </p></br><p>Take-home: HBV serologies (at minimum HBsAg ± anti-HBs, anti-HBc) are an essential component of the initial HIV work-up because they directly influence ART selection and vaccination strategy.</p></br><p>Documentation pearl: “HBsAg ordered to evaluate for chronic HBV coinfection prior to initiation of antiretroviral therapy.”  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (high-yield)  </p></br><p>• HBV + HIV coinfection accelerates progression to cirrhosis &amp; increases hepatotoxicity risk when starting ART.<br /></br>• Tenofovir (TDF or TAF) + emtricitabine (FTC) or lamivudine (3TC) inhibit both HBV reverse transcriptase (DNA polymerase) and HIV reverse transcriptase.<br /></br>• Stopping these drugs in an HBV-infected patient can lead to severe HBV flare → always know HBV status first.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Rundown  </p></br><p>a. Chest x-ray<br /></br>   • Indicated if TB symptoms or abnormal PPD/IGRA. Her recent PPD was negative &amp; she is asymptomatic. Routine CXR is not required.  </p></br><p>b. CT scan of the head<br /></br>   • Ordered for focal neurologic deficits, seizures, severe headache, or CD4 &lt;200 with neuro complaints (rule-out toxoplasmosis, CNS lymphoma). She is asymptomatic.  </p></br><p>c. Hemoglobin A1c<br /></br>   • Baseline metabolic screening (lipids, fasting glucose) is recommended but HbA1c is not specifically required before ART and is not as time-sensitive as HBV testing that guides drug choice.  </p></br><p>e. Varicella-zoster virus PCR<br /></br>   • Used to confirm active VZV infection (e.g., shingles) in lesions/CSF; not a screening test. Baseline VZV immunity is checked with IgG antibody, not PCR.  </p></br><p>Key discriminator: Only HBsAg directly alters immediate treatment selection today.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memory Aids  </p></br><p>“HALT &amp; Screen HOT-BiDs” for new HIV visit:<br /></br>H – Hepatitis (A immunity, B surface Ag, C Ab)<br /></br>A – Anal STIs (GC/CT) &amp; Syphilis (RPR)<br /></br>L – Labs (CBC, CMP, lipids)<br /></br>T – TB screen<br /></br>H – HIV RNA / CD4<br /></br>O – Opportunistic pathogen IgG (Toxo, CMV, VZV)<br /></br>T – Trichomonas (women)<br /></br>B – Baseline pregnancy test (if applicable)<br /></br>D – Drug tox screens / dosing labs  </p></br><p>If the stem still hasn’t ticked off “B,” choose HBV serology.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Core Takeaway  </p></br><p>Before starting or modifying ART, ALWAYS determine hepatitis B status (HBsAg). It is the single screening test that immediately dictates which antiretroviral backbone you will or will not use in an HIV-infected patient.</p>
42. An 82-year-old woman with hypertension comes to the office because of a 2-month history of easy fatigability and shortness of breath with mild exertion. She says she has to use two pillows to sleep and breathe comfortably. She had a myocardial infarction at the age of 75 years. Her medications are hydrochlorothiazide, fosinopril, and potassium supplementation. She has never smoked cigarettes. Her pulse is 100/min, and blood pressure is 150/100 mm Hg. Crackles are heard at the lung bases bilaterally. There is pitting edema of the lower extremities. No other abnormalities are noted. Which of the following is the most likely set of findings in this patient?</br>Sympathetic Tone    Renal Blood Flow    Urine Sodium Concentration	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. Decreased        decreased            increased</br>b. Decreased        increased            increased</br>c. Increased        decreased            decreased</br>d. Increased        decreased            increased</br>e. Increased        increased            decreased</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>C.  ↑ Sympathetic tone  ↓ Renal blood flow  ↓ Urine sodium concentration  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Key phrases: “easy fatigability,” “SOB with mild exertion,” uses “two pillows” (orthopnea), basilar crackles, bilateral pitting edema → classic congestive heart failure (CHF) with volume overload.<br /></br>• Past MI and chronic HTN are common etiologies of systolic CHF in the elderly.<br /></br>• Pertinent positives: tachycardia (100/min), HTN, pulmonary rales, peripheral edema.<br /></br>• Pertinent negatives: no smoking history (rules out primary COPD as explanation).</p></br><p>Test‐taking framework: When you see CHF symptoms, immediately think “↓ forward flow → compensatory neurohormonal activation.” Most USMLE questions about CHF physiology probe sympathetic activity, RAAS, ADH, renal perfusion, and urine sodium/osmolarity.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>1. Failing LV → ↓ cardiac output.<br /></br>2. Baroreceptors (carotid sinus, aortic arch, renal juxtaglomerular cells) sense ↓ effective arterial volume.<br /></br>3. Compensatory responses:<br /></br>   • ↑ Sympathetic tone (β₁ ↑ HR/contractility; α₁ ↑ SVR).<br /></br>   • Renal vasoconstriction → ↓ renal blood flow &amp; GFR.<br /></br>   • RAAS &amp; ADH activation → Na⁺ and water retention.<br /></br>4. Result in urine: kidneys avidly reabsorb Na⁺ → ↓ urine Na⁺ concentration.</p></br><p>Take-home: CHF mimics intravascular volume depletion to the sensors, so the body turns on all “save salt &amp; squeeze vessels” systems.</p></br><p>Documentation (real patient): record NYHA class, presence of orthopnea/PND, edema, vital signs, and plan for diuretics/afterload reduction.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>Etiology: Post-MI LV dysfunction → systolic HF.<br /></br>Key mechanisms:<br /></br>• Sympathetic nervous system surge within minutes.<br /></br>• Renal hypoperfusion → renin release → Ang II (vasoconstriction) → aldosterone (Na⁺/H₂O retention).<br /></br>• ADH secretion → free-water retention.<br /></br>Clinical manifestations: pulmonary congestion (rales, orthopnea), peripheral edema, fatigue.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostics (FYI)<br /></br>First-line chronic therapy: loop diuretic (furosemide) for congestion, ACE-I (already on fosinopril) and β-blocker (carvedilol, metoprolol succinate) for mortality benefit, +/- spironolactone if EF ≤35%.<br /></br>Diagnostics: BNP, echocardiogram to reassess EF, CXR for pulmonary edema, CMP to trend renal function and electrolytes.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“CHF = Can’t Handle Flow ➜ body thinks you’re dry.”<br /></br>SNaPS: Sympathetic ↑, Na⁺ retention ↑, Perfusion (renal) ↓, Sodium in urine ↓.<br /></br>OR<br /></br>“CHF turns ON the SRA system”<br /></br>S – Sympathetic<br /></br>R – Renin/RAAS<br /></br>A – ADH<br /></br>All cause Salt &amp; water reAbsorption.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>A. ↓ Sympathetic / ↓ Renal blood flow / ↑ Urine Na⁺<br /></br> Sympathetic tone would not be decreased; urine Na⁺ would not be high if RAAS is active.</p></br><p>B. ↓ Sympathetic / ↑ Renal blood flow / ↑ Urine Na⁺<br /></br> Opposite of physiologic response; renal flow actually falls.</p></br><p>D. ↑ Sympathetic / ↓ Renal blood flow / ↑ Urine Na⁺<br /></br> Urine Na⁺ would rise only if natriuretic peptides predominate or patient is on loop diuretic acutely; chronic untreated CHF → Na⁺ avid kidney → ↓ urine Na⁺.</p></br><p>E. ↑ Sympathetic / ↑ Renal blood flow / ↓ Urine Na⁺<br /></br> Sympathetic renal vasoconstriction lowers, not raises, renal blood flow.</p></br><p>Key difference: only choice C has the internally consistent triad of compensatory sympathetic activation, renal hypoperfusion, and sodium retention typical of untreated congestive heart failure.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Core takeaway: In CHF, low forward flow triggers sympathetic &amp; RAAS activation → ↓ renal blood flow and avid renal Na⁺ reabsorption → low urine sodium.</p>
43. A 19-year-old man comes to the office because of a 10-hour history of shortness of breath and sharp, retrosternal chest pain radiating to his neck and left shoulder. The pain is worse with movement or swallowing. He also has a 1-week history of fever, malaise, myalgia, diarrhea, cough, and a scratchy sore throat. He has been unable to work as a summer lifeguard during this period. He smokes two packs of cigarettes daily. His temperature is 38°C (100.4°F), pulse is 70/min, respirations are 16/min, and blood pressure is 110/60 mm Hg. Examination and x-rays of the chest show normal findings. An ECG is shown. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. Arterial blood gas analysis</br>b. Coronary angiography</br>c. Echocardiography</br>d. Endomyocardial biopsy</br>e. Exercise stress test</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>C.
