Echocardiography (transthoracic echo)</p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key words &amp; phrases:<br /></br>• 19-year-old man (very low coronary risk)<br /></br>• Sharp, retrosternal chest pain radiating to neck/left shoulder that is worse with movement or swallowing - classic pleuritic/pericardial pain.<br /></br>• Viral prodrome for 1 week (fever, malaise, myalgias, diarrhea, cough, sore throat) – most common trigger for acute pericarditis.<br /></br>• Normal chest exam and normal chest x-ray – makes pulmonary and pleural causes unlikely.<br /></br>• ECG provided (not shown here but in the real question it displays diffuse ST-segment elevations with PR-segment depressions) – textbook for acute pericarditis.  </p></br><p>Pertinent negatives: no exertional/pressure-like pain, no radiation to jaw/arm typical of ischemia, normal vital signs (no tachycardia, no hypotension), no unilateral absent breath sounds (rules out pneumothorax).  </p></br><p>Metacognitive approach:<br /></br>1. Recognize “pleuritic chest pain + recent viral illness” → think pericarditis.<br /></br>2. When an ECG is shown, scan for diffuse, concave (“saddle-shaped”) ST elevation &amp; PR depression.<br /></br>3. Once pericarditis is suspected, the key next step is to evaluate for pericardial effusion/tamponade → do a transthoracic echocardiogram.  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Why echo is the next step:<br /></br>• Diagnosis of uncomplicated acute pericarditis is clinical + ECG, but guidelines recommend obtaining an echo in all new cases to:<br /></br>  – Look for pericardial effusion (present in ≈60% of cases).<br /></br>  – Screen for early tamponade physiology (requires urgent pericardiocentesis).<br /></br>  – Exclude concomitant myocarditis or structural heart disease.<br /></br>• Echo is non-invasive, widely available, and immediately changes management if a large effusion is found.  </p></br><p>Core takeaway: Any patient with suspected acute pericarditis gets an urgent transthoracic echocardiogram to rule out hemodynamically significant effusion/tamponade.  </p></br><p>Documentation pearls: chest pain characteristics, viral prodrome, ECG findings, echo results (presence/absence of effusion), hemodynamic status.  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>Acute pericarditis<br /></br>• Etiology: viral (coxsackie, echovirus, adenovirus, influenza), uremia, autoimmune, post-MI (Dressler), malignancy, radiation, drugs.<br /></br>• Mechanism: inflammation of parietal &amp; visceral pericardial layers → pleuritic pain.<br /></br>• ECG: diffuse ST elevation (concave) &amp; PR depression; possible reciprocal PR elevation in aVR.<br /></br>• Complication: pericardial effusion → cardiac tamponade (Beck triad: hypotension, muffled heart sounds, JVD).  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Management &amp; Diagnostics  </p></br><p>First-line tests:<br /></br>1. ECG (already done).<br /></br>2. Transthoracic echo – evaluate for effusion/tamponade.<br /></br>3. Basic labs (CBC, ESR/CRP) to support inflammation.<br /></br>Treatment (if no tamponade):<br /></br>• High-dose NSAIDs (eg, ibuprofen 600-800 mg TID) + colchicine (0.5-0.6 mg BID) for 3 months.<br /></br>• Restrict exercise; treat underlying cause.<br /></br>If large effusion/tamponade → pericardiocentesis.  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“PEEP for Pericarditis”:<br /></br>P = Pleuritic chest pain<br /></br>E = ECG diffuse concave ST ↑ and PR ↓<br /></br>E = Echo to check for Effusion<br /></br>P = NSAID/colchicine Pharmacotherapy  </p></br><p>Picture a “saddle-shaped” ST segment sitting on a horse that is drinking “cola-chicine” to remember ST elevation &amp; colchicine therapy.  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>a. Arterial blood gas analysis – ABG would not confirm pericarditis and is unnecessary in a hemodynamically stable patient without hypoxia.  </p></br><p>b. Coronary angiography – Used for suspected acute coronary syndrome. This 19-year-old has pleuritic pain and a viral prodrome, not exertional pressure-like pain or ischemic ECG changes (localized ST elevation with reciprocal depression).  </p></br><p>d. Endomyocardial biopsy – Reserved for unexplained, severe new-onset heart failure (&lt;2 wk) or fulminant myocarditis. Not needed in straightforward viral pericarditis.  </p></br><p>e. Exercise stress test – Contraindicated during acute chest pain and would not help diagnose pericardial disease; mainly used to evaluate stable coronary disease.  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line:<br /></br>Suspect acute viral pericarditis. The immediate diagnostic step is transthoracic echocardiography to detect pericardial effusion and impending tamponade.</p>
44. A 62-year-old woman with metastatic adenocarcinoma of the breast is brought[corrected from "brough t"] to the emergency department because[corrected from "bec ause"] of a 1-week history of weakness and a tingling sensation that has progressed from her lower legs to her chest. During the past 3 weeks, she has had mild to moderate interscapular pain, which is worse at night. Her only medication is tamoxifen. She does not appear to be in distress. Vital signs are within normal limits. Muscle strength is 4/5 in the lower extremities and 5/5 in the upper extremities. Deep tendon reflexes are 3+ in the lower extremities and 2+ in the upper extremities. Babinski sign is absent. Sensation to pinprick is decreased to the level of the mid chest. An MRI of the spine confirms the diagnosis. Administration of which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Anastrozole</br>b. Dexamethasone</br>c. Indomethacin</br>d. Morphine</br>e. Pamidronate</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>B. Dexamethasone  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Key clues<br /></br>   • Known metastatic breast adenocarcinoma → high risk for vertebral body &amp; epidural metastases.<br /></br>   • 1-week, step-wise progression of weakness/tingling from legs → chest = ascending sensory level.<br /></br>   • Mid-chest sensory cutoff to pinprick = “sensory level,” classic for spinal cord involvement.<br /></br>   • Hyperreflexia (3+) in legs but normal upper-extremity reflexes → upper-motor-neuron signs below a thoracic lesion.<br /></br>   • Interscapular pain worse at night → red flag for malignancy-related vertebral disease.<br /></br>   • “MRI of the spine confirms the diagnosis” ⇒ they are telling you epidural spinal cord compression (ESCC).  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Pertinent negatives<br /></br>   • No fever/leukocytosis → argues against epidural abscess.<br /></br>   • No Babinski may simply be early; UMN signs already present.  </p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>Exam strategy tip: When you see cancer + focal back pain + rapidly evolving neurologic deficits with a sensory level, immediately think spinal cord compression and reflexively move to IV steroids before definitive decompression or radiation.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Emergency goals in ESCC:<br /></br>1. Prevent further cord ischemia/edema.<br /></br>2. Preserve neurologic function while arranging definitive treatment (radiation or surgery).  </p></br><p>High-dose IV glucocorticoids (e.g., dexamethasone 10 mg IV bolus, then 4–6 mg IV q6 h) rapidly ↓ vasogenic edema surrounding the cord, buying time. That is why dexamethasone is the very first therapeutic step once the diagnosis is suspected (and certainly once MRI confirms it).  </p></br><p>Documentation pearls (what you would write): neurologic exam with sensory level, reflexes, strength; time of steroid administration; neurosurgery/radiation oncology consults placed; bladder/bowel function assessment.  </p></br><p>Take-home mantra: “Cancer patient + back pain + neuro deficit = STAT MRI → IV dexamethasone.”  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• Tumor in vertebral body grows posteriorly into epidural space → compresses dorsal spinal arteries &amp; cord → vasogenic edema → rapid neurologic deterioration.<br /></br>• Glucocorticoids stabilize capillary membranes, decrease permeability, and shrink tumor-associated edema.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Algorithm  </p></br><ol></br><li>Suspicion: give empiric IV dexamethasone before imaging if high pre-test probability.  </li></br><li>MRI entire spine.  </li></br><li>Definitive therapy:<br /></br>   • Radiation therapy (most breast, prostate, lung mets).<br /></br>   • Surgical decompression ± stabilization if radio-resistant tumor, spinal instability, or unknown primary.  </li></br><li>Adjuvant systemic therapy (hormonal, chemo, bisphosphonates) once acute issue is controlled.  </li></br></ol></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“BACKED” for cord compression:<br /></br>B ack pain (night)<br /></br>A scending neuro deficits / Ataxia<br /></br>C ancer history<br /></br>K (K)ord sign = sensory level<br /></br>E dema ↓ by Dexamethasone → give FIRST<br /></br>D efinitive: Decompress / radiation  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Options Are Wrong  </p></br><p>A. Anastrozole – Aromatase inhibitor for chronic endocrine therapy of estrogen-positive breast cancer. No rapid effect on cord edema; not an emergency measure.  </p></br><p>C. Indomethacin – NSAID for pain/inflammation; does not relieve cord compression or edema.  </p></br><p>D. Morphine – Addresses pain only; does nothing to halt neurological decline. Symptom control is important but secondary to preventing paralysis.  </p></br><p>E. Pamidronate – IV bisphosphonate for malignant bone pain or hypercalcemia; slow onset, not useful for acute spinal cord compression.  </p></br><p>Common pitfall: choosing analgesics (morphine, NSAIDs) because pain is prominent—remember the neurologic deficits trump pain control in priority.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Core Takeaway  </p></br><p>Epidural spinal cord compression is an oncologic emergency. Immediate high-dose IV dexamethasone is the first step to prevent permanent neurologic damage, followed by urgent imaging and definitive surgical or radiation therapy.</p>
45. A case-control study is planned to determine the association between retinal detachment and occupations that require lifting heavy objects. A total of 200 patients of a large ophthalmologic clinic aged 40 to 80 years will be recruited for the study. Patients will be asked about their histories of lifting objects weighing more than 25 kg (55 lb). Which of the following is the best strategy in study design to decrease confounding by age for history of heavy lifting?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. Adjusting for age in multivariate analyses</br>b. Excluding retirees from the control group</br>c. Matching participants by age</br>d. Recruiting only patients aged 60 years and older</br>e. Stratifying results by age in tabular analyses</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>c. Matching participants by age  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues<br /></br>• Case–control design → exposures are measured retrospectively, so confounding must be addressed up-front (design stage) or in the analysis.<br /></br>• Potential confounder identified in the stem = AGE (40- to 80-year window, and older people are both more likely to retire from heavy-lifting jobs and more likely to develop retinal pathology).<br /></br>• Question asks: “best strategy in study design to decrease confounding by age.” – “Design” is the operative word.  </p></br><p>Cognitive framework<br /></br>1. Is the factor a true confounder?<br /></br>   – Associated with exposure (heavy lifting jobs ↓ with advancing age).<br /></br>   – Independent risk factor for outcome (retinal detachment ↑ with age).<br /></br>   – Not on the causal pathway.<br /></br>2. After confirming confounding, decide whether to deal with it in the design or analysis phase.<br /></br>   – Design options: randomization (for trials), restriction, or matching.<br /></br>   – Analysis options: stratification, multivariable regression.  </p></br><p>Because this is an observational, case-control study, randomization is impossible. Matching is the most efficient, preserves sample size, and occurs in the design phase.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning / Study-Design Reasoning  </p></br><p>Stepwise logic to the correct answer<br /></br>1. Confounder identified = age.<br /></br>2. Case-control studies recruit cases and controls first, then look back for exposure.<br /></br>3. To eliminate age differences before any data analysis, recruit one (or more) control(s) of the same or similar age for every case.<br /></br>4. This “frequency” or “individual” matching equalizes the age distribution between groups, removing age as a confounding variable.  </p></br><p>Core takeaway<br /></br>In case–control studies, individual or frequency matching on the potential confounder is the most direct way to eliminate confounding during the design stage.  </p></br><p>Documentation reminder (real patient encounter): record matching variables (e.g., age ± 2 years) and confirm each control meets the matching criteria.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (quick)  </p></br><p>Age influences retinal detachment via degenerative vitreous changes and increased prevalence of lattice degeneration. Age also influences employment type (less heavy lifting with retirement). Thus, age meets all three criteria for a confounder.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment / Diagnostic Tests Review – Not directly applicable (study-design question).  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“ARM”:<br /></br>• Analysis phase → Regression (multivariable), Stratification.<br /></br>• Restriction &amp; Matching occur in the design phase.<br /></br>• Matching is the M that eliminates a confounder by making groups “Mirror” each other.  </p></br><p>Visual: Picture two “mirror” age histograms for cases and controls—matching turns the curves into a mirror image, erasing age differences.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>a. Adjusting for age in multivariate analyses<br /></br>• Works, but this is an ANALYSIS-phase solution, not a design-phase strategy requested by the question.  </p></br><p>b. Excluding retirees from the control group<br /></br>• This is restriction, but applied only to controls, not cases, creating selection bias and not eliminating confounding.  </p></br><p>d. Recruiting only patients aged 60 years and older<br /></br>• Restriction (design phase) can prevent confounding, but shrinks the eligible pool, reduces generalizability, and may still leave residual age variation within the narrowed band. Matching is more efficient here.  </p></br><p>e. Stratifying results by age in tabular analyses<br /></br>• Another ANALYSIS-phase method; good for detecting effect modification, but does not fulfill the “design” requirement.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line  </p></br><p>For a case-control study where age is a known confounder, the best design-phase method is to match each case with control(s) of the same age range, thereby equalizing age distribution and eliminating confounding before data collection even begins.</p>
46. A 21-year-old man comes to the office for a follow-up visit 1 week after urine dipstick analysis, performed for a sports physical examination, showed an increased protein concentration. He is asymptomatic. There is no personal or family history of serious illness. The patient does not smoke cigarettes, drink alcohol, or use illicit drugs. He trains for college basketball most of the year. He is 191 cm (6 ft 3 in) tall and weighs 84 kg (185 lb); BMI is 23 kg/m2. His temperature is 36.7 °C (98.0 °F), pulse is 58/min, respirations are 10/min, and blood pressure is 126/76 mm Hg. Examination shows no abnormalities. Results of laboratory studies are within the reference ranges. Urinalysis shows 1+ protein. Spot urine protein:creatinine ratio was <0.2 at 8 AM and 0.7 at 4 PM. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Cystoscopy</br>b. 24-Hour urine collection for measurement of total protein concentration and creatinine clearance</br>c. Renal biopsy</br>d. Renal ultrasonography</br>e. No further studies are indicated</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>E. No further studies are indicated  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Key findings<br /></br>  – Young (21-year-old) healthy male, entirely asymptomatic<br /></br>  – Single urine dipstick during sports physical: 1+ protein<br /></br>  – Normal vitals, normal exam, no systemic disease<br /></br>  – Split-sample protein evaluation:<br /></br>      • 8 AM (supine overnight) spot protein:creatinine (P/C) &lt; 0.2 (normal)<br /></br>      • 4 PM (upright/active) spot P/C 0.7 (elevated)  </p></br><p>• Pertinent positives/negatives that narrow the differential<br /></br>  – Normal morning sample rules out persistent or pathologic protein excretion<br /></br>  – No hematuria, hypertension, edema, systemic disease ⇒ glomerular disease unlikely<br /></br>  – Transient causes (fever, infection, strenuous exercise) absent  </p></br><p>Pattern recognition/algorithm<br /></br>Isolated proteinuria → repeat evaluation to distinguish:<br /></br>1. Transient (illness, exercise, fever)<br /></br>2. Orthostatic (postural)<br /></br>3. Persistent (pathologic)  </p></br><p>Orthostatic proteinuria is confirmed when:<br /></br>• Normal protein excretion in a first-morning void (&lt; 0.2 g protein/g creatinine)<br /></br>• Elevated protein in a daytime/upright specimen  </p></br><p>Once orthostatic proteinuria is documented, no further diagnostic testing is needed; the condition is benign and typically resolves by adulthood.  </p></br><p>Metacognitive tip: On exams, any healthy adolescent/young adult with isolated proteinuria that disappears in a first-morning specimen = orthostatic proteinuria → reassure, no work-up.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (Step-wise)<br /></br>1. Detect proteinuria on screening dipstick.<br /></br>2. Repeat testing to rule out laboratory error and classify pattern: split day vs morning.<br /></br>3. Normal morning P/C &lt; 0.2 g/g + elevated upright = orthostatic proteinuria.<br /></br>4. Benign condition ⇒ patient education and routine annual follow-up only.<br /></br>Core takeaway: First-morning urine that is normal in an otherwise healthy patient rules out clinically significant renal disease.  </p></br><p>Documentation (real patient): “Diagnosis: orthostatic proteinuria. Counseling: benign, no further testing indicated; will recheck urine annually.”  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Orthostatic (postural) proteinuria<br /></br>  – Unknown exact mechanism; thought to relate to increased renal vein pressure or hyperfiltration while upright.<br /></br>  – Occurs predominantly in tall, thin adolescents/young adults.<br /></br>  – Daily protein loss usually &lt; 1 g/day; long-term renal prognosis is excellent.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Management / Diagnostic Tests<br /></br>• Required test: compare first-morning (supine) with daytime (upright) protein excretion.<br /></br>• If morning sample is normal, additional tests (24-h collection, imaging, biopsy) provide no benefit and add cost/risk.<br /></br>• Follow-up: repeat dipstick annually to ensure it resolves with age. No pharmacotherapy needed.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“Protein peaks when you’re on your feet.”<br /></br>Think “O” for Orthostatic = “OK” kidneys. First-morning void is your “baseline.”  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>a. Cystoscopy<br /></br>   – Indicated for gross hematuria or suspected bladder pathology, not isolated proteinuria.<br /></br>b. 24-Hour urine collection<br /></br>   – Used to quantify persistent proteinuria (&gt; 300 mg/day on multiple samples). Once orthostatic pattern is proven, this test is unnecessary.<br /></br>c. Renal biopsy<br /></br>   – Reserved for persistent proteinuria ≥ 1 g/day, nephrotic syndrome, abnormal renal function, hematuria, or systemic disease. None are present.<br /></br>d. Renal ultrasonography<br /></br>   – Helpful for structural abnormalities or obstructive uropathy; not indicated in benign orthostatic proteinuria with normal renal function.  </p></br><p>Each alternative would expose the patient to needless procedures, cost, and anxiety without clinical benefit.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line<br /></br>Isolated proteinuria that disappears in the first-morning specimen = benign orthostatic proteinuria. Reassure the patient; no additional studies are needed beyond routine follow-up.</p>
47. A 63-year-old man comes to the office because of chronic intermittent mild chest pain that usually occurs when he walks up two flights of stairs. He is otherwise asymptomatic. He has hypertension and coronary artery disease. He is adherent to his medication regimen of atorvastatin, clopidogrel, carvedilol, and aspirin. He has a sedentary lifestyle. He smoked two packs of cigarettes daily for 25 years but now smokes five cigarettes daily. He is 173 cm (5 ft 8 in) tall and weighs 91 kg (200 lb); BMI is 30 kg/m2. Blood pressure is 130/65 mm Hg. Examination shows no other abnormalities. The patient asks about undergoing revascularization. The physician is aware of a randomized controlled trial in which aggressive lifestyle management is comparable to percutaneous coronary intervention (PCI) in terms of meaningful cardiovascular outcomes. Which of the following statistical features of the study is most supportive of a recommendation for lifestyle management in this patient?</br>α Level    β Level ( percent)	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. 0.20    5</br>b. 0.20    10</br>c. 0.25    10</br>d. 0.25    20</br>e. 0.5     50</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>A. α = 0.20 β = 5 %</p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues<br /></br>• “Randomized controlled trial” → look at study design characteristics.<br /></br>• Trial showed “aggressive lifestyle management is comparable to PCI in terms of meaningful cardiovascular outcomes” → a NEGATIVE (no-difference/equivalence) result.<br /></br>• Physician wants to rely on that result to counsel the patient → must be confident that a real difference was not missed.</p></br><p>Cognitive approach<br /></br>When a trial finds “no difference,” the threat is a Type II error (β) — falsely accepting the null hypothesis and overlooking a true effect. Therefore, for an equivalence/non-inferiority conclusion to be trusted, the β level should be very small (high power).<br /></br>Step-wise reasoning:<br /></br>1. Focus on β (power = 1−β).<br /></br>2. Pick the lowest available β.<br /></br>3. Verify α is acceptable (it can be relatively liberal here because Type I error is less concerning when you failed to show superiority).</p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Take-home concept<br /></br>• Type I error (α): concluding there IS a difference when none exists.<br /></br>• Type II error (β): concluding there is NO difference when one actually exists.<br /></br>For counseling that two strategies are equivalent, minimize β → maximize power → larger sample size and/or stronger study design.</p></br><p>Documentation pearl<br /></br>“Informed patient that high-power RCT (β = 0.05; power = 95 %) detected no superiority of PCI over lifestyle modification; therefore lifestyle optimization recommended.”</p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (brief)  </p></br><p>Stable angina results from fixed atherosclerotic lesions. Both optimal medical therapy (OMT) with risk-factor modification and PCI relieve ischemia, but multiple trials (e.g., COURAGE) show no mortality difference. Endorsing OMT requires confidence that the trial could have detected a mortality benefit if one truly existed → high power.</p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Statistics in Plain Language  </p></br><p>Power (1−β) = probability the study will find a difference when it truly exists.<br /></br>• β = 5 % → power 95 % (excellent).<br /></br>• α = 20 % (liberal) means investigators were willing to accept up to a 20 % chance of a false positive, yet they still found no significant difference; this further reassures us the therapies are truly similar.</p></br><p>Mnemonic<br /></br>“β bites you in negative studies.”<br /></br>If result is negative, always check β; a small β keeps the bite small.</p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>B. α = 0.20, β = 10 %: Power only 90 %. Acceptable, but not as strong as 95 %. You would still worry (1 in 10 chance) that PCI is truly better and the study missed it.</p></br><p>C. α = 0.25, β = 10 %: Even higher α (25 %) and β 10 % → lower power and higher false-positive tolerance. Less convincing.</p></br><p>D. α = 0.25, β = 20 %: Power only 80 %. A fifth of the time the study could miss a real benefit of PCI.</p></br><p>E. α = 0.50, β = 50 %: Totally unreliable; coin-flip risk for both Type I and Type II errors.</p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>• “α = Accuse (false accusation of difference).”<br /></br>• “β = Blind (you’re blind to a real difference).”<br /></br>• For non-inferiority/equivalence questions, keep β tiny.<br /></br> “β for ‘Beat’ the null: if small, the study can beat the null when it should.”</p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line  </p></br><p>A study intended to support lifestyle management over PCI needs very high power (low β). Among the choices, α = 0.20 with β = 5 % (power = 95 %) best minimizes the chance of missing a true benefit of PCI, making its equivalence conclusion the most trustworthy.</p>
48. An 82-year-old man is admitted to the hospital 4 hours after he fell at home. His wife, who witnessed the fall, says his legs "gave out" when he stood up from his armchair. He was initially unresponsive but regained consciousness after 20 seconds. He did not have bowel or bladder incontinence during the episode. Medical history is remarkable for hypertension, type 2 diabetes mellitus, and benign prostatic hyperplasia. Medications are amlodipine, metformin, atorvastatin, and tamsulosin. On arrival at the hospital, pulse is 78/min, respirations are 18/min, and blood pressure is 98/72 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on room air shows an oxygen saturation of 97 percent. Cardiac examination discloses a late-peaking, barking systolic murmur heard at the upper right sternal border without an appreciable S2. Carotid upstrokes are diminished, and peripheral pulses are symmetrically weak. The remainder of the physical examination discloses no abnormalities. Laboratory studies show a serum glucose concentration of 117 mg/dL. Which of the following is the most likely underlying cause of this patient's fall?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Aortic stenosis  </br>b. Autonomic dysreflexia  </br>c. Hyperglycemia  </br>d. Orthostatic hypotension  </br>e. Vertebrobasilar insufficiency</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>a. Aortic stenosis  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis &amp; Clinical Reasoning  </p></br><ol></br><li>Key clues that immediately narrow the differential<br /></br>   • Elderly patient (82 y) with a brief loss of consciousness when he tried to stand (“legs gave out,” 20-second LOC, no incontinence or post-ictal state) → classic description of exertional syncope rather than seizure.<br /></br>   • Cardiac exam:<br /></br>     – “Late-peaking, barking systolic murmur” at the upper right sternal border<br /></br>     – Absent/soft S2<br /></br>     – Diminished, delayed carotid upstrokes + weak peripheral pulses (pulsus parvus et tardus)<br /></br>   • Hypotension (98/72 mm Hg) in the ED, reinforcing low forward flow.  </li></br></ol></br><p>These findings together scream “severe aortic stenosis (AS).”  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>How the clues rule other things in/out<br /></br>   • Orthostatic hypotension could fit the timing but would not explain the classic AS murmur or delayed carotids.<br /></br>   • Hyperglycemia is ruled out by normal glucose (117 mg/dL) and, more importantly, hyperglycemia rarely causes sudden transient LOC.<br /></br>   • Autonomic dysreflexia occurs only in patients with high spinal cord injuries—no such history here.<br /></br>   • Vertebrobasilar insufficiency usually presents with focal neuro deficits (vertigo, diplopia, ataxia), absent here.</p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Metacognitive tip<br /></br>   On NBME/Step exams, when you see “elderly + exertional/light-headed syncope + crescendo-decrescendo murmur at RUSB,” immediately think “calcific AS → fixed stroke volume → syncope.”  </p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• Etiology in elderly: degenerative (calcific) thickening of aortic valve cusps → fixed LV outflow obstruction.<br /></br>• Hemodynamics: During exertion or position change, systemic vasodilation demands ↑CO; but the stenotic valve prevents stroke-volume augmentation → transient cerebral hypoperfusion → syncope.<br /></br>• Classic symptom triad: SAD = Syncope, Angina, Dyspnea (heart failure).<br /></br>• Physical signs of severe disease:<br /></br>  – Late-peaking systolic murmur (the later the peak, the worse the stenosis)<br /></br>  – Soft/absent A2, single S2<br /></br>  – Pulsus parvus et tardus (slow, weak carotid upstroke).</p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Diagnostic &amp; Management Pearls  </p></br><p>Diagnosis confirmation<br /></br>• Echocardiography (transthoracic) → valve area ≤1.0 cm² or mean gradient ≥40 mm Hg.  </p></br><p>Definitive treatment<br /></br>• Surgical or transcatheter aortic valve replacement (SAVR/TAVR) for symptomatic severe AS.<br /></br>• No medical therapy alters disease progression; manage comorbidities and avoid preload/afterload reduction that can worsen hypotension.</p></br><p>Documentation must include: episode description, vitals, murmur characteristics, assessment of suspected AS with plan for echocardiogram and cardiology consult.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Memory Aid  </p></br><p>“Aortic Stenosis makes you ‘SAD’ and ‘poor &amp; late’:<br /></br>• Syncope<br /></br>• Angina<br /></br>• Dyspnea<br /></br>• Pulsus Parvus (poor) et Tardus (late).”  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong  </p></br><p>b. Autonomic dysreflexia<br /></br>   • Requires high spinal cord lesion (T6 or above) → not present; plus it causes severe hypertension, not hypotension.  </p></br><p>c. Hyperglycemia<br /></br>   • Blood glucose normal (117); hyperglycemia causes polyuria/polydipsia, not abrupt syncope.  </p></br><p>d. Orthostatic hypotension<br /></br>   • Could explain standing syncope, especially with amlodipine/tamsulosin, but would not give the classic AS murmur or pulsus parvus et tardus. NBME expects you to give higher priority to the pathognomonic cardiac findings.  </p></br><p>e. Vertebrobasilar insufficiency<br /></br>   • Usually presents with vertigo, diplopia, dysarthria, ataxia; murmurs and carotid findings do not fit.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Take-home Message  </p></br><p>In an elderly patient, exertional or postural syncope plus a late-peaking systolic murmur at the right upper sternal border and weak slow carotid pulses is classic for severe aortic stenosis—requiring prompt echocardiographic evaluation and valve replacement.</p>
49. A 7-year-old girl is brought to the clinic by her mother for a well-child examination. The mother is single, and the patient is the third of five children. The family comes late to the appointment because they missed the first bus on the route to the clinic. The patient’s medical record shows that she missed her last two appointments and was last seen for a physical examination 2 years ago. The mother reports her daughter has frequent illnesses. She receives no medications. The patient is clean and appropriately dressed but appears tired and sluggish. She is at the 50th percentile for height, 10th percentile for weight, and below the 5th percentile for BMI. Two years ago, she was at the 50th percentile for height, 25th percentile for weight, and 15th percentile for BMI. Vital signs are within normal limits. Examination shows dry, flaky skin and brittle hair. No other abnormalities are noted. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Ask the mother whether she is worried about feeding her family  </br>b. Call child protective services  </br>c. Counsel the patient’s mother regarding healthy nutrition  </br>d. Order a complete blood count and metabolic panel  </br>e. Recommend that the patient drink a daily protein supplement</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>A. Ask the mother whether she is worried about feeding her family</p></br><p>━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues:<br /></br>• Age 7 y/o, previously normal height-for-age (50th %ile) – linear growth maintained → rules out most chronic systemic diseases or endocrine disorders that would affect height.<br /></br>• Weight &amp; BMI have progressively fallen (25th → 10th %ile weight; 15th → &lt;5th %ile BMI). Crossing two major percentile lines = red flag for inadequate caloric intake (“failure to thrive” in an older child).<br /></br>• Physical signs of under-nutrition: dry flaky skin, brittle hair, tired‐appearing.<br /></br>• Social context: single parent, 5 children, came by bus, repeatedly missed visits → limited resources/possible poverty.<br /></br>• No exam findings of organic disease; vitals normal.  </p></br><p>Pattern: Preserved linear growth + falling weight/BMI + socioeconomic risk factors ⇒ caloric insufficiency from food insecurity is top of differential.</p></br><p>Metacognitive approach for similar questions:<br /></br>1. Differentiate weight loss patterns:<br /></br>   – Weight ↓ first, height ↓ later? Think calories.<br /></br>   – Height &amp; weight ↓ together? Think chronic illness/endocrine/genetic.<br /></br>2. Scan vignette for psychosocial vs organic clues.<br /></br>3. In possible neglect vs poverty, FIRST screen, THEN decide on CPS involvement.</p></br><p>━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Step-wise logic:<br /></br>1. Identify failure to thrive (FTT) pattern.<br /></br>2. Most common etiology in school-aged children = inadequate intake, frequently linked to food insecurity.<br /></br>3. American Academy of Pediatrics (AAP) recommends routine 2-item screen:<br /></br>   “Within the past 12 months, we worried whether our food would run out before we got money to buy more.”<br /></br>   “Within the past 12 months, the food we bought just didn’t last and we didn’t have money to get more.”<br /></br>4. Therefore, the immediate next step is to ASK about ability to obtain food.<br /></br>Take-home: Before ordering tests or notifying CPS, clinicians should screen for food insecurity and connect families to resources (WIC, SNAP, food banks).</p></br><p>What would need to be documented?<br /></br>• Growth chart percentiles &amp; downward crossing.<br /></br>• Positive/negative responses to food-insecurity screen.<br /></br>• Education provided &amp; referrals (eg, social work, WIC).  </p></br><p>━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>Caloric/protein malnutrition → negative energy balance:<br /></br>• Mobilization of fat stores → ↓ BMI.<br /></br>• ↓ skin oil production → dry flaky skin.<br /></br>• Protein deficit → ↓ keratin quality → brittle hair.<br /></br>• Adequate growth hormone &amp; thyroid levels maintain normal linear growth (height spared early).  </p></br><p>━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━<br /></br>Diagnostic Tests &amp; Management  </p></br><p>Initial management algorithm for suspected nutritional FTT:<br /></br>1. Screen for food insecurity → yes? engage social services, nutritionist, public assistance.<br /></br>2. If negative or if weight fails to rebound after support, THEN pursue labs (CBC, CMP, ESR, celiac screen, thyroid, etc).  </p></br><p>Labs now (Choice D) are lower yield until intake issue addressed.</p></br><p>━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“Weight before Height = Worry about food.”<br /></br>If Weight percentile drops while Height plateau stays → first think inadequate calories → ask about Wallet (food insecurity).</p></br><p>Mnemonic: “ASK before ACT”<br /></br>A – Ask about food<br /></br>S – Support with resources<br /></br>K – Keep monitoring growth<br /></br>(Only after ASKing do you consider CPS or extensive labs.)</p></br><p>━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>B. Call child protective services<br /></br>• CPS is appropriate for suspected abuse/neglect. Poverty alone, when caregiver is engaged and child is clean/attending clinic, is not neglect. Must first assess &amp; offer help.</p></br><p>C. Counsel mother on healthy nutrition<br /></br>• Counseling presumes food access. Problem may be affordability, not knowledge. Must screen for insecurity first.</p></br><p>D. Order CBC &amp; metabolic panel<br /></br>• Organic disease less likely with preserved height &amp; normal vitals. Labs become second-line if intake issues ruled out or no improvement after intervention.</p></br><p>E. Recommend daily protein supplement<br /></br>• Treats symptom, not cause. Supplements may be unaffordable and do not address chronic household food shortage.</p></br><p>━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━<br /></br>Core Takeaway<br /></br>In a child with falling weight but preserved height and socioeconomic risk factors, the immediate step is to screen for food insecurity by asking the caregiver whether she worries about having enough food.</p>
50. A 44-year-old woman comes to the clinic because of a 6-month history of persistent skin lesions over her scalp and neck despite topical clobetasol therapy. She has hypertension and discoid lupus erythematosus. Her only other medication is hydrochlorothiazide. Temperature is 37.2°C (99.0°F), pulse is 82/min, respirations are 14/min, and blood pressure is 126/74 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on room air shows an oxygen saturation of 99 percent. Physical examination shows several erythematous indurated plaques with overlying scales over the scalp and lateral aspects of the neck. Oropharynx is moist and pink without lesions. There is no joint swelling, tenderness, or erythema. Cardiopulmonary, abdominal, and neurologic examinations disclose no abnormalities. The physician prescribes hydroxychloroquine once daily to be increased to twice daily during the next 2 weeks. Which of the following screening studies is most appropriate at this time?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. ECG</br>b. Ophthalmologic examination</br>c. Pulmonary function testing</br>d. Serum lipid studies</br>e. Serum thyroid-stimulating hormone concentration</br></br></br><p>Correct answer<br /></br>b. Ophthalmologic examination  </p></br><p>───────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Key clues: “discoid lupus erythematosus,” lesions persistent despite “topical clobetasol,” decision to start “hydroxychloroquine.”<br /></br>• Hydroxychloroquine is an antimalarial immunomodulator frequently used for cutaneous and systemic lupus that can cause irreversible retinopathy (bull’s-eye maculopathy).<br /></br>• The question asks for “screening studies … at this time” → what test must be obtained <strong>before</strong> or <strong>at initiation</strong> of hydroxychloroquine.  </p></br><p>Metacognitive tip: When you see hydroxychloroquine (or chloroquine) on NBME/Step, immediately think “baseline dilated eye exam and periodic follow-up.”  </p></br><p>───────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>1. Identify medication about to be started.<br /></br>2. Recall its major organ-specific toxicity (retina).<br /></br>3. Apply guideline: baseline ophthalmologic exam within 1 year of starting, then annually after 5 years (earlier if risk factors: high dose &gt;5 mg/kg/day actual body weight, renal disease, tamoxifen use).<br /></br>Core takeaway: Always couple hydroxychloroquine with an eye exam.  </p></br><p>Documentation (real life): Informed consent regarding retinal toxicity, weight-based dosing (&lt;5 mg/kg/day), and that baseline ophthalmology referral was placed.  </p></br><p>───────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Mechanism: Hydroxychloroquine accumulates in retinal pigment epithelium → photoreceptor damage → paracentral scotomas → irreversible vision loss if not detected early.<br /></br>• Risk increases with dose &gt;1,000 g cumulative (≈5–7 years typical dosing) or pre-existing renal impairment (drug cleared renally).  </p></br><p>───────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Tests Review<br /></br>• First-line for discoid lupus: sun protection, potent topical steroids.<br /></br>• Refractory/widespread: oral antimalarials (hydroxychloroquine 200–400 mg/day).<br /></br>• Monitoring schedule:<br /></br>  – Baseline comprehensive dilated exam + objective retinal screening (e.g., automated visual fields ± spectral-domain OCT).<br /></br>  – Annual exams starting at year 5 (earlier if high-risk).<br /></br>• Other labs sometimes obtained (CBC, CMP) but not mandatory screening tests for specific toxicity.  </p></br><p>───────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“Hydroxy <strong>CHLORO-queen</strong> needs her <strong>eye</strong> crown checked.”<br /></br>Visual: Queen with big glasses reminding you to order an eye exam.  </p></br><p>───────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>a. ECG – Needed for drugs that prolong QT or cause cardiomyopathy (tricyclics, dofetilide, anthracyclines). Hydroxychloroquine is not started with baseline ECG in routine outpatient dosing.<br /></br>c. Pulmonary function testing – Baseline PFTs are required for drugs causing interstitial lung disease (amiodarone, bleomycin, methotrexate) or before initiating certain biologics. Hydroxychloroquine has no significant pulmonary toxicity.<br /></br>d. Serum lipid studies – Appropriate when starting drugs that raise triglycerides (isotretinoin, oral estrogens) or lower them excessively (statins monitoring). Not indicated here.<br /></br>e. Serum thyroid-stimulating hormone – Baseline TSH is ordered for drugs affecting thyroid (amiodarone, lithium) or in dyslipidemia workup. Hydroxychloroquine does not.  </p></br><p>Common pitfall: Confusing hydroxychloroquine with chloroquine or quinidine regarding cardiac side effects; at standard doses hydroxychloroquine’s retinal toxicity is the clinically limiting factor requiring formal screening.  </p></br><p>───────────<br /></br>High-Yield Take-Home Message<br /></br>Any time you start hydroxychloroquine (or chloroquine), arrange a baseline ophthalmologic examination to screen for retinopathy; continue periodic monitoring to prevent irreversible vision loss.</p>
51. A 42-year-old woman has had progressive shortness of breath with exertion over the past 2 months. Cardiac examination shows a prominent cardiac impulse along the left sternal border and a fixed split S₂. A grade 1/6, low-pitched diastolic murmur is heard along the left sternal border. Which of the following is the most likely cause of these findings?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. Aortic insufficiency</br>b. Aortic stenosis</br>c. Atrial septal defect</br>d. Coarctation of the aorta</br>e. Mitral insufficiency</br>f. Mitral stenosis</br>g. Pulmonic stenosis</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>c. Atrial septal defect (ASD)</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• “Prominent cardiac impulse along the left sternal border” → right-sided impulse = right-ventricular (RV) heave from chronic volume overload.<br /></br>• “Fixed split S₂” → classically seen in ASD; inspiratory/expiratory variation is absent because blood shunts continuously from LA → RA, keeping RV stroke volume high in both phases of respiration.<br /></br>• “Grade 1/6, low-pitched diastolic murmur along the left sternal border” → low-frequency flow rumble across the tricuspid valve (↑ flow during diastole from the left-to-right shunt).  </p></br><p>Pertinent negatives: no carotid radiation, no opening snap, no early-diastolic decrescendo, no differential BP → rules out the valve lesions &amp; coarctation.</p></br><p>Cognitive approach for future questions<br /></br>1. Hear a FIXED split S2 → think ASD first.<br /></br>2. Ask: “Which lesion causes chronic RV volume overload without pulmonary HTN?” → ASD.<br /></br>3. Corroborate with: RV heave + flow murmurs (systolic over pulmonic area, diastolic over tricuspid area).</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (Why ASD?)<br /></br>Step 1: Fixed split S2 pinpoints constant delay of P2 → equal RV filling in inspiration &amp; expiration.<br /></br>Step 2: Left-to-right shunt allows continuous RV overfilling → RV dilation → palpable RV heave.<br /></br>Step 3: Increased flow across right-sided valves creates soft midsystolic ejection murmur (pulmonic) and low-pitched diastolic rumble (tricuspid).  </p></br><p>Core takeaway: Fixed splitting + RV heave = ASD until proven otherwise.</p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real patient)<br /></br>• Cardiac auscultation: fixed split S2, RV heave, flow murmurs.<br /></br>• Evidence of RV dilation on echo.<br /></br>• Recommend transthoracic echocardiography ± bubble study.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Most ASDs are secundum type in the region of the fossa ovalis.<br /></br>• Left atrial pressure &gt; right atrial pressure → left-to-right shunt.<br /></br>• Consequence: chronic RV volume overload → dilation, increased pulmonary flow, but normal pulmonary pressures early on.<br /></br>• Long-term risk: Eisenmenger syndrome (shunt reversal &amp; cyanosis), atrial arrhythmias, paradoxical emboli.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Diagnostic Work-up<br /></br>1. Transthoracic echo w/ Doppler = initial test.<br /></br>2. Bubble contrast or transesophageal echo for small or complex defects.<br /></br>3. ECG: incomplete RBBB, RA enlargement.<br /></br>4. CXR: mild cardiomegaly, ↑ pulmonary vasculature.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Management<br /></br>• Asymptomatic small secundum ASDs may close spontaneously.<br /></br>• Indications for closure (percutaneous device or surgery):<br /></br>  – Any symptomatic patient (dyspnea, arrhythmia)<br /></br>  – Evidence of RV volume overload on echo (RA/RV dilation)<br /></br>  – Qp:Qs ≥ 1.5:1<br /></br>• Endocarditis prophylaxis generally not required after isolated ASD closure.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>High-Yield Memory Aids<br /></br>“ASD = A-S-D”<br /></br>A = Atrial impulse along LSB (RV heave)<br /></br>S = Split (fixed) S2<br /></br>D = Diastolic (tricuspid) rumble</p></br><p>Picture a “hole” in the interatrial septum acting like a whistle that keeps the doors (valve leaflets) swinging open all the time—no pause, hence a fixed split.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong<br /></br>a. Aortic insufficiency (AI): Early decrescendo blowing diastolic murmur along LSB, bounding pulses, widened pulse pressure. Split S2 not fixed; AI causes LV (not RV) volume overload.<br /></br>b. Aortic stenosis (AS): Harsh crescendo-decrescendo systolic murmur at RUSB, radiates to carotids, delayed pulses, single or paradoxically split S2—not fixed.<br /></br>d. Coarctation of the aorta: Upper vs lower extremity BP differential, rib notching, continuous murmur left infrascapular region; no fixed split S2.<br /></br>e. Mitral insufficiency (regurgitation): Holosystolic blowing murmur at apex radiating to axilla; findings of LV volume overload, S3 possible, no fixed split.<br /></br>f. Mitral stenosis: Opening snap followed by diastolic rumble at apex (left lateral decubitus), loud S1; often with pulmonary HTN → variable P2, not fixed.<br /></br>g. Pulmonic stenosis: Harsh crescendo-decrescendo systolic murmur at left upper sternal border, systolic ejection click, widely split S2 that varies with respiration (NOT fixed).</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Key Take-Home Message<br /></br>Fixed splitting of S2 + right-sided flow murmurs = atrial septal defect until proven otherwise.</p>
52. Fifteen minutes ago, a 19-year-old man sustained a gunshot wound to the right temporal region with an exit wound at the left temporal region. He was intubated at the scene and received supplemental oxygen and intravenous fluids. On arrival, his pulse is 60/min, and blood pressure is 170/100 mm Hg. His pupils are fixed and dilated, and he does not respond to stimuli. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step to determine brain death?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Measurement of intracranial pressure</br>b. Assessment of brain stem reflexes</br>c. CT scan of the head</br>d. EEG</br>e. Cerebral angiography</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>b. Assessment of brain-stem reflexes  </p></br><p>──────────────────────── Vignette Analysis ────────────────────────<br /></br>Key clues:<br /></br>• “Gunshot wound … right temporal region with an exit wound at the left temporal region” → devastating, likely bi-hemispheric injury.<br /></br>• Fixed, dilated pupils + no motor response → profound neurologic dysfunction.<br /></br>• Bradycardia (60/min) + hypertension (170/100) → Cushing reflex = markedly elevated intracranial pressure.<br /></br>• Already intubated/ventilated → airway &amp; oxygenation secured, allowing a formal neurologic exam.  </p></br><p>Metacognitive approach: 1) Stabilize airway/breathing/circulation. 2) Ask, “Is there any potentially reversible cause of coma?” 3) If not, proceed with the brain-death protocol, which starts with a bedside neurologic examination focused on the brain stem.</p></br><p>─────────────────────── Clinical Critical Reasoning ───────────────────────<br /></br>Brain death = irreversible cessation of all brain (including brain-stem) function. U.S. guidelines require:<br /></br>1. Fulfillment of pre-requisites (normothermia, normal BP, no CNS-depressant drugs, no severe metabolic or endocrine derangement).<br /></br>2. Clinical exam showing coma, absence of brain-stem reflexes, and apnea test.<br /></br>Therefore, the very next step once prerequisites are met is to perform a formal assessment of brain-stem reflexes (pupillary, corneal, oculocephalic/vestibulo-ocular, gag, cough) followed by the apnea test. Ancillary tests (EEG, cerebral blood-flow studies) are used only if the clinical exam cannot be completed or is equivocal.</p></br><p>Take-home: Determination of brain death is first and foremost a clinical diagnosis made at the bedside; you prove absence of brain-stem activity before reaching for machines.</p></br><p>Documentation: Time, temperature, BP; results of each reflex test; apnea test results; attending signatures.</p></br><p>──────────────────────── Pathophysiology Review ───────────────────────<br /></br>• Gunshot traversing temporal lobes → massive cerebral edema, herniation → brain-stem ischemia/necrosis → loss of reflexes.<br /></br>• Fixed, dilated pupils = uncal herniation compressing CN III or direct midbrain damage.<br /></br>• Cushing triad (HTN + bradycardia ± irregular respirations) = brain-stem compression from ↑ICP.</p></br><p>───────────────────── Treatments &amp; Diagnostic Tests Review ─────────────────<br /></br>• No imaging or monitoring tool can replace the bedside neurologic assessment for brain death.<br /></br>• Ancillary studies:<br /></br>  – EEG: looks for electrocerebral silence.<br /></br>  – Cerebral angiography, transcranial Doppler, or radionuclide scan: assess cerebral blood flow.<br /></br>  Used only when exam/apnea test cannot be done (e.g., severe facial trauma, high cervical SCI, sedative intoxication).<br /></br>• ICP monitoring helps manage head injury but is irrelevant to declaring brain death.</p></br><p>───────────────────────── Memorization Tips ─────────────────────────<br /></br>“Brain death = Bedside exam first.”<br /></br>Mnemonic “BRAIN DEAD”:<br /></br>B – Blood pressure adequate &amp; normothermia<br /></br>R – Reversible causes ruled out<br /></br>A – Absent brain-stem reflexes (pupil, corneal, oculocephalic, gag, cough)<br /></br>I – Impulse to breathe absent (apnea test)<br /></br>N – Neurologic coma (no motor response)<br /></br>D/E/A/D – Documentation / EEG or Angiography only if Doubt</p></br><p>────────────────────── Incorrect Answer Analysis ──────────────────────<br /></br>a. Measurement of intracranial pressure<br /></br>• Elevated ICP explains his vitals but does NOT diagnose brain death. Many patients with high ICP recover brain function after treatment.</p></br><p>c. CT scan of the head<br /></br>• Defines structural injury but cannot prove functional loss of the brain stem. CT can appear devastating yet some brain activity may persist.</p></br><p>d. EEG<br /></br>• Acceptable ancillary study if clinical exam/apnea test cannot be performed, but not the initial required step; bedside reflex assessment comes first.</p></br><p>e. Cerebral angiography<br /></br>• Gold standard for absent cerebral blood flow but invasive and reserved for inconclusive/unfinished clinical exams.</p></br><p>──────────────────────── Core Takeaway ─────────────────────────<br /></br>For brain-death determination, the first mandatory action is a meticulous bedside neurologic examination demonstrating the absence of all brain-stem reflexes; ancillary tests are secondary and only used when the clinical exam is unattainable or equivocal.</p>
53. A randomized, double-blind, controlled trial is conducted to assess the effectiveness of a new drug to treat duodenal ulcer disease. The study requires that patients are symptomatic and have evidence of duodenal ulcer disease on upper endoscopy. After informed consent is obtained, patients are randomly assigned to receive the new drug or a placebo via a random numbers table. Upper endoscopy will be performed on each patient at the end of the study. The end points are patient-reported symptoms and results of the upper endoscopy. Which of the following raises the most concern about this trial?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Double-blind design</br>b. Entrance criteria</br>c. Patient randomization techniques</br>d. Selected end points</br>e. Use of an inappropriate control</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>E. Use of an inappropriate control (placebo)</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis</br>• Key phrases: “duodenal ulcer disease,” “new drug,” “randomized, double-blind, controlled,” “placebo,” “symptomatic,” “upper endoscopy at the end of the study.”<br /></br>• Pertinent positives: 1) A well-established, highly effective, and inexpensive standard-of-care (proton-pump inhibitor [PPI] therapy, e.g., omeprazole) already exists for symptomatic duodenal ulcers. 2) Subjects are required to be symptomatic—the trial is withholding proven therapy from patients who are currently in pain and at risk for complications (bleeding, perforation).<br /></br>• Pertinent negative: No mention that existing effective treatment will be provided if symptoms worsen or ulcers bleed, nor that the placebo is being used because no standard therapy exists.  </p></br><p>Metacognitive approach: When screening any therapeutic trial vignette, always ask “Is placebo ethically permissible or must the control arm receive current best therapy?” If proven effective therapy exists and withholding it poses more than minimal risk, placebo is usually unethical unless justified by add-on design (standard therapy + new drug vs. standard therapy + placebo).</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (Why E is correct)</br>1. Ethical principle: Beneficence/non-maleficence. Investigators must not knowingly expose participants to unnecessary harm when a recognized effective therapy is available.<br /></br>2. Regulatory standards (FDA, Declaration of Helsinki): Placebo control is acceptable only when (a) no existing therapy of proven efficacy exists or (b) placebo is added to standard therapy (add-on design) and use of placebo does not pose additional risk of serious or irreversible harm.<br /></br>3. Duodenal ulcer disease has &gt;90% healing rates with once-daily PPIs in 4-8 weeks. Withholding PPIs exposes patients to pain, anemia from occult bleeding, frank upper GI hemorrhage, and perforation. Therefore, placebo alone is an “inappropriate control.”<br /></br>Take-home: The ethical soundness of the control arm is a critical validity criterion; an unethical design can invalidate an otherwise methodologically perfect trial.</p></br><p>Documentation pearls (if this were a real IRB review):<br /></br>• Justification for control arm, assessment of risk/benefit, rescue therapy plan.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review</br>• Duodenal ulcers: acid/pepsin injury, often from Helicobacter pylori or NSAID use.<br /></br>• PPIs decrease gastric acid secretion → ulcer healing, symptom relief.<br /></br>• Complications when untreated: bleeding, perforation, gastric outlet obstruction.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Tests</br>Standard of care<br /></br>• Omeprazole (or other PPI) 20–40 mg daily x 4–8 weeks ± H. pylori eradication (PPI + clarithromycin + amoxicillin).<br /></br>Diagnostic follow-up<br /></br>• Repeat endoscopy reserved for alarm features or lack of symptom resolution.<br /></br>In the study scenario, repeat endoscopy on every subject is acceptable; problem is withholding PPI.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>High-Yield Memory Tips</br>“PPI for a Peptic Problem” – anytime an exam stem says symptomatic peptic/duodenal ulcer, think PPI first.<br /></br>Ethics mnemonic “PLACEBO”<br /></br>P Proven therapy exists?<br /></br>L Life-threatening if withheld?<br /></br>A Add-on design possible?<br /></br>C Complications risk increased?<br /></br>E Ethics committee needed.<br /></br>B Beneficence must be preserved.<br /></br>O Ok only if no effective therapy.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Why Other Choices Are Wrong</br>A. Double-blind design – Gold-standard for avoiding measurement and observer bias; no ethical or methodological problem here.<br /></br>B. Entrance criteria – Appropriate: symptomatic duodenal ulcer confirmed by endoscopy.<br /></br>C. Patient randomization – Random-numbers table is standard and minimizes selection bias.<br /></br>D. Selected end points – Clinically meaningful (symptom relief, endoscopic healing).<br /></br>E. Use of an inappropriate control – Violates ethical standards given existing effective therapy → greatest concern.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Core Takeaway</br>In clinical trials, the most rigorous methodology still fails if the control arm is unethical. If proven therapy exists and withholding it poses non-trivial risk, placebo alone is an inappropriate control. Always scrutinize the control group first when evaluating study validity on NBME/Step 2 questions.</p>
54. A 27-year-old woman, gravida 2, para 1, at 34 weeks' gestation comes to the physician because she is concerned about her baby's growth. Her friends comment regularly about the small size of her abdomen. Her first child was delivered at 39 weeks' gestation and weighed 3000 g (6 lb 10 oz); he now is at the age of 16 months and has had normal growth and development. Her last menstrual period and ultrasound measurements are compatible with a current estimated gestational age of 34 weeks. Her husband is 168 cm (5 ft 6 in) tall. She is 140 cm (4 ft 7 in) tall and currently weighs 46 kg (102 lb). Abdominal examination shows a uterus consistent in size with a 32-week gestation and a well-engaged vertex. Ultrasonography shows a fetus consistent in size with a 32-week gestation. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Reassurance</br>b. Weekly nonstress test</br>c. Fetal fibronectin test</br>d. Amniocentesis for fetal lung maturity</br>e. Immediate delivery</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>A. Reassurance  </p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Key clues<br /></br>   • Dates certain and 1st-trimester US agree on 34-week GA → dating is reliable.<br /></br>   • Fundal height and sonographic EFW (estimated fetal weight) ≈ 32-week size → only 2-week lag.<br /></br>   • Both parents are short (mother 140 cm, father 168 cm).<br /></br>   • No maternal disease (no HTN, diabetes, lupus, smoking, substance use).<br /></br>   • Normal amniotic fluid reported (not oligohydramnios) and vertex head is well-engaged.  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Pertinent positives &amp; negatives<br /></br>   • Positive: Slightly small fetus.<br /></br>   • Negative: No risk factors or ultrasound evidence of pathologic growth restriction (EFW &lt;10th percentile, abnormal Doppler, oligohydramnios, structural anomalies).  </p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>Pattern/Algorithm<br /></br>Fundal height &lt; or &gt; dating by ≤3 cm → variation is normal; &gt;3 cm → further evaluation.<br /></br>Symmetric small size + short parents + no risk factors → “constitutional smallness,” not intrauterine growth restriction (IUGR).  </p></br><p>Metacognitive tip: When growth appears “off,” ask, “Is the discrepancy &gt;3 cm or &gt;2 wk AND are there pathologic risk factors?” If not, reassure.  </p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Step-wise thought process<br /></br>1. Confirm accurate dating (done).<br /></br>2. Compare fundal height/US to GA. A 2-wk lag at 34 wk is within normal biological variation.<br /></br>3. Look for maternal/fetal causes of IUGR (HTN, preeclampsia, infection, multiple gestation, substance use, placental insufficiency). None are present.<br /></br>4. Consider parental stature → likely constitutionally small fetus.<br /></br>5. Management: no intervention; schedule routine prenatal care.  </p></br><p>Take-home message: Small-appearing abdomen with minor size lag and no risk factors = reassurance.  </p></br><p>What would be documented: “Fundal height 32 cm at 34 wk; US EFW = 32 wk; no maternal/fetal risk factors; likely constitutionally small fetus; patient reassured, routine prenatal follow-up.”  </p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• IUGR (fetal growth restriction) = EFW &lt;10th percentile for GA.<br /></br>   – Symmetric (early) often genetic/infectious.<br /></br>   – Asymmetric (late) usually uteroplacental insufficiency.<br /></br>• Constitutional smallness: fetus is small but healthy, growth curve parallels normal, parents often small.<br /></br>• Fundal height rule: difference ≤3 cm from GA (in weeks) is acceptable.  </p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>Diagnostics &amp; Management  </p></br><p>No additional tests needed now. If lag had been &gt;3 cm or EFW &lt;10th percentile → detailed US with Doppler, serial growth scans, and antenatal testing (NST/biophysical profile).  </p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“3-2-1 Reassure”<br /></br>3 cm fundal height, ≤2 wk US lag, 1 (no) risk factor → reassure.  </p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>B. Weekly non-stress test<br /></br>   • Indicated when IUGR is confirmed or in high-risk pregnancies (HTN, DM, oligohydramnios). Not needed for normal constitutional smallness.  </p></br><p>C. Fetal fibronectin test<br /></br>   • Screens for risk of preterm labor in symptomatic women (contractions, cervical change) at 22–34 wk, not for growth assessment.  </p></br><p>D. Amniocentesis for fetal lung maturity<br /></br>   • Considered when elective preterm delivery (&lt;37 wk) is planned. No plan for early delivery here.  </p></br><p>E. Immediate delivery<br /></br>   • Reserved for severe IUGR with non-reassuring testing or other obstetric emergencies (preeclampsia with severe features, placental abruption). Not warranted.  </p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>Bottom Line: A 2-week size discrepancy without risk factors in short parents represents a healthy, constitutionally small fetus. Provide reassurance and continue routine prenatal care.</p>
55. A previously healthy 37-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department 2 hours after the sudden onset of severe pain and light intolerance of the right eye. She has no history of trauma, foreign bodies, or irritating substances in the right eye. She takes no medications and has no known allergies. Her temperature is 37°C (98.6°F). Examination shows mild injection of the right conjunctiva and mild clouding of the anterior chamber. Direct light into the right eye produces severe pain. The pupils are asymmetric. Visual acuity and intraocular pressures are normal bilaterally. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Corneal abrasion</br>b. Endophthalmitis</br>c. Glaucoma</br>d. Iritis</br>e. Optic neuritis</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer</br>d. Iritis (acute anterior uveitis)</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis</br>• “Sudden onset of severe pain and light intolerance (photophobia)” → classic for anterior uveitis.</br>• “Mild injection … mild clouding of the anterior chamber” → cells/flare (“smoky” or “hazy” anterior chamber) produced by inflammatory leukocytes &amp; protein.</br>• “Direct light into the right eye produces severe pain” → consensual photophobia (shine light → constriction of inflamed iris → pain) – very high-yield clue for iritis.</br>• “Pupils are asymmetric” → inflamed iris often becomes edematous → irregular or constricted pupil.</br>• “Visual acuity and intraocular pressures are normal” → rules out acute angle-closure glaucoma (↓ vision, ↑ IOP) and optic neuritis (↓ central vision).</p></br><p>Cognitive framework: When an NBME stem gives (1) photophobia, (2) cells/flare or hazy anterior chamber, (3) pain triggered by light, immediately think “iritis/uveitis.” Always check IOP and cornea—if normal, glaucoma &amp; corneal processes fall on the differential.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning</br>Step-wise thought process:</br>1. Photophobia implies either corneal irritation or inflammation of the iris/ciliary body.<br /></br>2. No trauma, foreign body, or fluorescein description → corneal abrasion less likely.<br /></br>3. Cloudy anterior chamber (cells/flare) + consensual photophobia → points straight to anterior uveitis.<br /></br>Core takeaway: Consensual photophobia + cells/flare = iritis.</p></br><p>Documentation (real life): visual acuity, pupillary shape/reactivity, IOP, slit-lamp findings (cells/flare), presence/absence of hypopyon, degree of ciliary flush, funduscopic exam, associated systemic symptoms (back pain, joint pain, skin lesions).</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review</br>• Acute anterior uveitis = inflammation of iris ± ciliary body.<br /></br>• Etiologies: idiopathic, HLA-B27 seronegative spondyloarthropathies (ankylosing spondylitis, reactive arthritis, psoriatic arthritis, IBD), sarcoidosis, infections (HSV, CMV, syphilis, TB), trauma.<br /></br>• Inflammatory cells leak into aqueous humor → “cells,” proteins cause “flare” → pain, photophobia, blurred vision.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Tests</br>1. Urgent ophthalmology referral.<br /></br>2. Topical cycloplegic/mydriatic (e.g., homatropine or cyclopentolate) to paralyze ciliary muscle &amp; prevent synechiae; provides pain relief.<br /></br>3. Topical corticosteroids (prednisolone acetate); periocular/ systemic steroids or immunomodulators for refractory or posterior disease.<br /></br>4. Work-up for systemic disorders if recurrent (HLA-B27 testing, chest X-ray for sarcoidosis, RPR/VDRL, etc.).</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>High-Yield Mnemonic</br>“Pain in the iris when light is the brightest” = IRISitis<br /></br>(“iris” for iritis, “brightest” = light → pain)</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Incorrect Answer Analysis</br>a. Corneal abrasion<br /></br>   • Severe foreign-body sensation, tearing, fluorescein uptake; pain worsens with blinking, not specifically light; anterior chamber clear. IOP and pupil usually normal.</p></br><p>b. Endophthalmitis<br /></br>   • Usually post-operative or penetrating trauma; marked vision loss, hypopyon, severe conjunctival injection, often no photophobia. Would not have normal visual acuity.</p></br><p>c. Glaucoma (acute angle-closure)<br /></br>   • Severe ocular pain, headache, nausea, halos, mid-dilated fixed pupil, markedly ↑ IOP, corneal edema → blurred vision. In this vignette IOP and vision are normal, and pupil is not mid-dilated/fixed.</p></br><p>e. Optic neuritis<br /></br>   • Pain with eye movement, decreased central vision/visual acuity, afferent pupillary defect, normal anterior chamber. Photophobia is not a feature; IOP normal. Here visual acuity is normal.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Key Memory Hook</br>Consensual photophobia + cells/flare + normal IOP = think IRITIS (Anterior Uveitis).</p>
56. A previously healthy 4-year-old girl is brought to the physician because of a 1-week history of abdominal pain and diarrhea. Her stools have been streaked with blood. She has not had fever. She has received all recommended immunizations except for hepatitis B vaccine (HBV). She is pale and irritable. Her temperature is 37.5°C (99.5°F), pulse is 160/min, respirations are 26/min, and blood pressure is 128/84 mm Hg. Examination shows generalized moderate edema. Laboratory studies show:  </br>Hematocrit            24 percent  </br>Reticulocyte count    10 percent  </br>Platelet count        40,000/mm3  </br>Serum  </br>  Urea nitrogen       90 mg/dL  </br>  Creatinine          7.8 mg/dL  </br>Which of the following is most likely to have prevented this patient's condition?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Administration of antibiotics  </br>b. Administration of gastrointestinal antimotility drugs  </br>c. Administration of HBV  </br>d. Administration of immune globulin  </br>e. Appropriate food preparation</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>E. Appropriate food preparation (thorough cooking of ground beef, pasteurization of milk/juice, hand-washing after handling raw meat or visiting petting zoos)</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues<br /></br>• 4-year-old with 1 week of abdominal pain + bloody diarrhea → think invasive bacterial colitis (Shigella, EHEC, Campylobacter).<br /></br>• Afebrile (99.5 °F) → makes EHEC more likely than Shigella (classically febrile).<br /></br>• Triad now present:<br /></br>  – Microangiopathic hemolytic anemia (Hct 24 %, ↑ retics, scleral pallor)<br /></br>  – Thrombocytopenia (Plt 40 K)<br /></br>  – Acute kidney injury (BUN 90, Cr 7.8)<br /></br>• Generalized edema from renal failure (↓ GFR, salt &amp; water retention).  </p></br><p>This triad in a child after bloody diarrhea = Hemolytic-Uremic Syndrome (HUS) most commonly triggered by Shiga-toxin–producing Escherichia coli O157:H7 acquired from undercooked beef, unpasteurized milk/juice, or contaminated produce at petting zoos.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (stepwise)  </p></br><ol></br><li>Identify hemolysis (low Hct, high retic, schistocytes would be present).  </li></br><li>Note low platelets without severe bleeding → consumptive process not marrow failure.  </li></br><li>Recognize severe renal failure (Cr 7.8) in a child.  </li></br><li>Link the above to recent bloody diarrhea = typical (diarrhea-positive) HUS.  </li></br><li>Prevention hinges on eliminating the toxin-producing E. coli source → safe food handling &amp; adequate cooking (≥160 °F for ground beef).  </li></br></ol></br><p>Core takeaway: Most pediatric HUS is precipitated by ingestion of EHEC; proper food preparation is the single most effective preventive measure.</p></br><p>Documentation pearls: recent diet (hamburgers, raw milk), exposure history, daily urine output, neuro status (seizures can occur), labs (LDH, haptoglobin, peripheral smear).</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• Shiga toxin damages endothelial cells → microvascular thrombi (platelet consumption &amp; mechanical hemolysis).<br /></br>• Renal vessels are especially susceptible → AKI.<br /></br>• Toxin release is more likely if bacterial lysis is triggered (why certain antibiotics worsen disease).  </p></br><p>Risk factors: undercooked ground beef, unpasteurized dairy/juice, contaminated leafy greens, animal petting zoos.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Notes  </p></br><p>Diagnosis: clinical + stool culture/PCR for Shiga toxin.<br /></br>Management: supportive (IV fluids, electrolyte control, dialysis if needed). Plasma exchange reserved for atypical HUS.<br /></br>Avoid:<br /></br>• Antibiotics (may ↑ toxin release and severity).<br /></br>• Antimotility agents (increase toxin contact time).  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>High-Yield Memory Aids  </p></br><p>“Hamburger → HUS”: Hamburger (undercooked beef) → Shiga toxin E. coli → HUS triad (Anemia, Thrombocytopenia, Renal failure).  </p></br><p>“STEC causes STec (Shiga-Toxin E. coli causes Shearing of RBCs, Thrombocytopenia, Elevated Creatinine).”</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Why Other Options Are Wrong  </p></br><p>A. Antibiotics<br /></br>– Many studies show certain antibiotics (fluoroquinolones, TMP-SMX) increase HUS risk by promoting toxin release. Thus not preventive; may worsen.</p></br><p>B. Gastrointestinal antimotility drugs (e.g., loperamide)<br /></br>– Slow gut transit, prolonging toxin exposure and ↑ HUS risk; contraindicated.</p></br><p>C. Administration of HBV vaccine<br /></br>– Prevents hepatitis B, unrelated to shiga-toxin colitis or HUS.</p></br><p>D. Administration of immune globulin<br /></br>– Used for exposure prophylaxis in some viral/bacterial infections (e.g., hepatitis A, varicella) but offers no protection against EHEC or HUS.</p></br><p>E. Appropriate food preparation<br /></br>– Eliminates the reservoir of Shiga-toxin producing E. coli and is therefore the correct preventive strategy.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Exam Tip  </p></br><p>When you see: child + bloody diarrhea → quickly screen for platelet count, Hct, and creatinine. If all three abnormal, think HUS. If the question asks “how could it have been prevented?” the answer is almost always food safety (cook beef, avoid unpasteurized products).</p>
57. A 47-year-old Russian man comes to the physician because of a 3-week history of chest pain after walking three blocks. He describes the pain as a tightness in the left side of his chest. He has hypertension and type 2 diabetes mellitus. Current medications include omeprazole and paroxetine. His pulse is 90/min, and blood pressure is 150/90 mm Hg. Examination shows no abnormalities. Serum studies show a total cholesterol concentration of 230 mg/dL, HDL-cholesterol concentration of 40 mg/dL, and LDL-cholesterol concentration of 170 mg/dL. An ECG shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. X-ray of the chest</br>b. Exercise stress test</br>c. Dobutamine stress echocardiography</br>d. MUGA scan</br>e. Cardiac catheterization</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>B. Exercise stress test  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Key clues<br /></br>   • “3-week history of chest pain after walking three blocks … tightness” → Exertional, pressure-like pain = classic stable angina pattern.<br /></br>   • Age 47 with multiple CAD risk factors (HTN, diabetes, hyperlipidemia, male, Eastern European).<br /></br>   • Resting ECG “shows no abnormalities” → baseline ST-segment is interpretable.<br /></br>   • Patient can walk (“after walking three blocks”) → able to exercise.  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Pertinent positives/negatives<br /></br>   • Positive: exertional chest pain, risk factors.<br /></br>   • Negative: no resting ECG changes, no active chest pain now, no physical‐exam red flags (CHF, arrhythmia).<br /></br>   • Together → intermediate pre-test probability for CAD; next step = non-invasive stress test if patient can exercise and ECG is interpretable.  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Test-taking framework<br /></br>   • Stable angina diagnostic algorithm:<br /></br>     a. Can exercise?<br /></br>     b. Is resting ECG interpretable?<br /></br>       – If both “yes” → Exercise ECG stress test.<br /></br>       – If “no” to either → Pharmacologic or imaging-based stress (dobutamine echo, adenosine/nuclear).<br /></br>     c. High-risk or positive stress → coronary angiography.  </p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (why choice B is correct)  </p></br><p>Step-by-step thought process:<br /></br>1. Suspect stable angina.<br /></br>2. Estimate pre-test probability (age/sex/symptoms) → intermediate.<br /></br>3. Patient is ambulatory and has a normal resting ECG.<br /></br>4. Guidelines (AHA/ACC/ESC/US Preventive Services Task Force) recommend a standard treadmill (or bicycle) exercise ECG stress test as FIRST-LINE for diagnosing ischemia in this exact scenario.<br /></br>5.
