Therefore, order an exercise stress test now; further imaging or invasive testing depends on the results.  </p></br><p>Core takeaway: In patients with suspected stable CAD who (1) can exercise and (2) have an interpretable baseline ECG, the first diagnostic step is a simple exercise ECG stress test.  </p></br><p>Documentation pearls for real practice: chest-pain characteristics, functional capacity, risk factors, resting ECG findings, indication for stress testing, and informed consent.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (high-yield)  </p></br><p>Stable angina = transient myocardial ischemia due to fixed atherosclerotic narrowing; oxygen demand exceeds supply during exertion or stress. Coronary perfusion is adequate at rest; therefore resting ECG and troponin are typically normal. Diagnosis rests on demonstrating demand-induced ischemia (ST depression, angina reproduction) during stress testing.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Diagnostics &amp; Management Snapshot  </p></br><p>• Initial diagnostic ladder (suspected stable angina):<br /></br>  1. Resting ECG, fasting lipids, HbA1c.<br /></br>  2. Exercise ECG stress test (if criteria met).<br /></br>  3. If positive or high-risk features → imaging stress test or coronary angiography.  </p></br><p>• Medical therapy once diagnosis confirmed:<br /></br>  – β-blocker (metoprolol) = mortality benefit.<br /></br>  – Aspirin 81 mg daily + high-intensity statin (e.g., atorvastatin 40–80 mg).<br /></br>  – Sublingual nitroglycerin PRN; consider ACE inhibitor in diabetics.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“2-E Rule” for initial angina work-up:<br /></br>  E xercise capable + E CG normal → Exercise ECG stress test.<br /></br>If either “E” is absent, add imaging or pharmacologic agent.  </p></br><p>Visualization: Picture a patient walking on a treadmill with perfectly straight ST segments on the screen beforehand; this mental image cues “treadmill stress test first.”  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Incorrect  </p></br><p>A. Chest X-ray: Useful for pulmonary/mediastinal pathology or heart failure, but has very low sensitivity/specificity for CAD; not indicated as first test in classic exertional chest pain.  </p></br><p>C. Dobutamine stress echocardiography: Reserved when patient cannot exercise (e.g., severe arthritis) or has uninterpretable baseline ECG (LBBB, paced rhythm, &gt;1 mm ST depression at rest). Far costlier than simple treadmill ECG.  </p></br><p>D. MUGA scan (radionuclide ventriculography): Assesses ventricular ejection fraction or chemotherapy cardiotoxicity, not first-line for detecting inducible ischemia.  </p></br><p>E. Cardiac catheterization (coronary angiography): Gold standard but invasive, expensive, and carries risk (bleeding, stroke, contrast nephropathy). Appropriate only for high-risk presentations (unstable angina, NSTEMI, positive high-risk stress test) or those with high pre-test probability needing revascularization.  </p></br><p>Common pitfall: jumping directly to pharmacologic stress or cath when a simpler treadmill ECG suffices. Stick to the algorithm!  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line: In a patient with suspected stable angina who can exercise and has a normal resting ECG, the most appropriate initial diagnostic step is a standard exercise stress test.</p>
58. A hospitalized 57-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes mellitus has had an increase in her serum creatinine concentration from 1.0 to 2.4 mg/dL during the past 3 days. She was admitted to the hospital 7 days ago for treatment of pneumonia. She had severe shortness of breath and hypotension on admission; she was intubated and mechanically ventilated for 2 days. Six days ago, a CT scan of the chest with contrast showed pneumonia at both lung bases. She is now breathing 3 L/min of oxygen via nasal cannula and has mild shortness of breath. Her blood pressure has been stable for 6 days. Her urine output has been 1 L/d during the past 2 days. Current medications include ceftriaxone, azithromycin, albuterol, insulin, and lisinopril. Pulse oximetry while breathing 3 L/min of oxygen via nasal cannula shows an oxygen saturation of 94 percent. Crackles are heard at both lung bases. Urinalysis shows:</br>Protein 1+</br>RBC 10/hpf</br>WBC 9/hpf</br>Casts none</br></br>A Wright stain of urine shows eosinophils. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. Acute tubular necrosis</br>b. Diabetic nephropathy</br>c. Interstitial nephritis</br>d. Lisinopril-induced renal insufficiency</br>e. Pyelonephritis</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>c. Interstitial nephritis  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis &amp; Cognitive Framework<br /></br>1. Rapid rise in creatinine (1.0 → 2.4 mg/dL in 3 days) = acute kidney injury (AKI).<br /></br>2. Urinalysis: mild proteinuria, hematuria (10 RBC/hpf), pyuria (9 WBC/hpf), BUT “casts none.”<br /></br>3. Special stain: EOSINOPHILS in the urine.<br /></br>4. Recent exposure to a β-lactam antibiotic (ceftriaxone) started during this hospitalization.  </p></br><p>Key test‐taking rule: Eosinophiluria in the setting of a new drug exposure → think acute interstitial nephritis (AIN) first unless the stem explicitly directs elsewhere.  </p></br><p>Metacognitive tip: When you see AKI + “allergic” features (rash, fever, eosinophilia/eosinophiluria) + new medication, immediately run the AIN algorithm before considering ischemic/toxic ATN.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Step 1: Identify AKI.<br /></br>Step 2: Categorize UA pattern. Muddy brown casts → ATN; RBC casts → glomerulonephritis; WBC casts or eosinophils → interstitial nephritis.<br /></br>Step 3: Look for temporal relationship to exposures (drugs, infections, autoimmune). Ceftriaxone was started 7 days ago, perfectly matching the 3–10-day window for drug-induced AIN.  </p></br><p>Core takeaway: Drug-induced hypersensitivity reaction produces interstitial inflammation → impaired tubular function → creatinine rise and eosinophiluria.  </p></br><p>Documentation pearl: “Suspect drug-induced AIN secondary to ceftriaxone; plan to discontinue offending agent, obtain renal consult, consider steroids if no improvement within 3–5 days.”  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Type IV hypersensitivity reaction: T-cell/IgE-mediated injury to renal interstitium and tubules.<br /></br>• Infiltration by lymphocytes and eosinophils → edema, impaired tubular secretion → AKI.<br /></br>• Classic triad (appears in &lt;50%): fever, rash, eosinophilia/eosinophiluria.  </p></br><p>Common offenders (“5 P’s” mnemonic):<br /></br>Pee (diuretics, esp. sulfa)<br /></br>Pain-free (NSAIDs)<br /></br>Penicillins/cephalosporins<br /></br>Proton-pump inhibitors<br /></br>RifamPin  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostics<br /></br>1. STOP the offending drug (usually sufficient).<br /></br>2. Supportive care: fluids, correct electrolytes.<br /></br>3. If renal function does not improve in 3–5 days or if severe, consider corticosteroids.<br /></br>4. Renal biopsy rarely needed unless diagnosis unclear.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Pitfalls<br /></br>a. Acute tubular necrosis (ATN)<br /></br>   • Would show muddy brown “granular” casts, not eosinophils.<br /></br>   • Often follows hypotension or IV contrast, but eosinophiluria is not a feature.  </p></br><p>b. Diabetic nephropathy<br /></br>   • Chronic process: years of hyperglycemia, heavy albuminuria (often 3-4+), not an acute jump in creatinine with eosinophils.  </p></br><p>d. Lisinopril-induced renal insufficiency<br /></br>   • ACE inhibitors raise creatinine modestly (≤30%) and usually within days of initiation or dose change, especially in renal artery stenosis.<br /></br>   • UA typically bland; no eosinophils.  </p></br><p>e. Pyelonephritis<br /></br>   • Presents with fever, flank pain, costovertebral angle tenderness, WBC casts.<br /></br>   • No eosinophiluria, and creatinine rise is uncommon unless there is obstruction or sepsis.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>High-Yield Memory Aids<br /></br>• “EoSINophils in the SINterstitium” → Acute SINterstitial nephritis.<br /></br>• 5 P’s of drug causes (see above).  </p></br><p>By distinguishing the characteristic urine findings (especially eosinophils) and linking them to recent drug exposure, you can rapidly identify interstitial nephritis on exams and in practice.</p>
59. A 6-year-old boy who has cerebral palsy is brought to the office for a routine health maintenance examination. His parents are concerned that he has never been able to walk. The parents report that the child has been healthy and has not had weight loss, developmental regression, change in appetite or activity level, or pain in his joints. The child's mental development seems normal to his parents. The mother says that her son undergoes weekly physical therapy. Today, head circumference is at the 25th percentile for age; height and weight are at the 5th percentile for age. Vital signs are temperature 36.8°C (98.4°F), pulse 90/min, respirations 22/min, and blood pressure 100/60 mm Hg. Examination of the upper extremities shows no abnormalities. Hips are adducted. There is moderate lower extremity spasticity and three beats of clonus at both ankles and knees. The parents are considering educational options for the following year for their son and his twin sister, who is not affected with cerebral palsy. The father asks, "What is the public school’s obligation to my children?" Which of the following is the most appropriate response to the parents?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Their children can go to the same public school and their son's special needs will be accommodated in a regular classroom</br>b. Their children can go to the same public school but their son will be placed in a special classroom</br>c. Their son will have to ambulate with assistive devices before he can be placed in a public school</br>d. Their son will have to be placed in a private school</br>e. Their son will have to be placed in a school for disabled children</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>A. “Their children can go to the same public school and their son’s special needs will be accommodated in a regular classroom.”</p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Key clues: 6-y-o with cerebral palsy, normal cognition, receives weekly PT, parents are only asking about school placement—no medical urgency.<br /></br>• Context phrase: “What is the public school’s obligation…?” signals a legal/educational-rights question rather than a medical management one.<br /></br>• Pertinent positives: cognitively intact, can benefit from typical curriculum if supports are provided.<br /></br>• Test-taking pattern: NBME repeatedly tests the “mainstreaming” principle/“least restrictive environment” mandated by federal law.</p></br><p>Metacognitive tip: Any U.S. child with a disability → think IDEA (Individuals with Disabilities Education Act) → “free appropriate public education (FAPE) in the least restrictive environment (LRE).”</p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning / Core Concept<br /></br>1. Federal law (IDEA, Part B, ages 3–21) requires:<br /></br>   a. Free appropriate public education.<br /></br>   b. Individualized Education Program (IEP) written jointly with parents.<br /></br>   c. Placement in the least restrictive environment—usually the regular classroom with aids, unless disability clearly prevents benefit.</p></br><ol></br><li>Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act and the Americans with Disabilities Act (ADA) add that public institutions must provide reasonable accommodations and may not discriminate on the basis of disability.</li></br></ol></br><p>Take-home: A child with a physical disability but adequate cognition is mainstreamed; the school must supply PT/OT, adaptive seating, aides, etc., inside the standard classroom whenever feasible.</p></br><p>Documentation in real life: Note physical findings, cognitive assessment, parents’ concerns, and that an IEP referral was initiated or continued.</p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (concise)<br /></br>Cerebral palsy = non-progressive motor impairment from an early brain insult. Spastic diplegia (leg &gt; arm involvement) common in premature infants due to periventricular leukomalacia. Physical disability does not equal intellectual disability; many children have normal intellect and benefit from typical schooling.</p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Diagnostic &amp; Management Pearls<br /></br>• Educational management: IEP meeting with school psychologist, PT/OT, teacher, and parents.<br /></br>• Medical management (for completeness): PT, spasticity control (baclofen, botulinum toxin, selective dorsal rhizotomy). None of these change school placement rights.</p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“IDEA gives every kid an IDEA-l setting: FAPE + LRE.”<br /></br>FAPE (Free Appropriate Public Education) → think “FREE to attend PUBLIC school.”<br /></br>LRE (Least Restrictive Environment) → “Let ’em join Regular Education first.”</p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>B. Same school but automatic special classroom<br /></br>• Violates LRE. Special classes are used only when regular placement with supports is inadequate, determined by IEP team—not imposed up front.</p></br><p>C. Must ambulate before attending public school<br /></br>• No legal basis. ADA/504 ensure access regardless of mobility status; schools must supply ramps, adaptive desks, etc.</p></br><p>D. Must be placed in a private school<br /></br>• Public system cannot force private placement. Rarely, if public school truly cannot provide FAPE, district pays for private schooling, but that is an exception reached after IEP review, not the default.</p></br><p>E. Must go to a “school for disabled children”<br /></br>• Same error as D; contradicts FAPE/LRE principles and anti-segregation mandates.</p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line<br /></br>Under IDEA, a 6-year-old with cerebral palsy but normal cognition has the right to attend the neighborhood public school in a regular classroom with appropriate accommodations spelled out in an IEP.</p>
60. A previously healthy 22-year-old man comes to the physician because he has had watery, nonbloody diarrhea five times daily for 3 days. He also has had nausea, decreased appetite, and abdominal cramps during this period. He returned yesterday from a trip to Mexico. His temperature is 37.5°C (99.5°F), pulse is 80/min, respirations are 14/min, and blood pressure is 128/80 mm Hg. There are no orthostatic blood pressure or pulse changes. The mucous membranes are moist. Abdominal examination shows diffuse mild tenderness to palpation without masses or rebound. Which of the following is the most likely causal organism?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Entamoeba histolytica</br>b. Enterotoxigenic Escherichia coli</br>c. Rotavirus</br>d. Salmonella typhi</br>e. Yersinia enterocolitica</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>b. Enterotoxigenic Escherichia coli (ETEC)</p></br><p>───────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• “Returned yesterday from a trip to Mexico” → classic set-up for traveler’s diarrhea.<br /></br>• “Watery, non-bloody diarrhea five times daily for 3 days” → toxin-mediated secretory diarrhea, not invasive (no blood, fever, or leukocytosis).<br /></br>• Mild illness: afebrile, normal vitals, well hydrated → suggests self-limited enterotoxin illness rather than invasive/bacteremic infection.  </p></br><p>High-yield cognitive framework for diarrhea after travel:<br /></br>1. Timing: 1–3 days after ingestion → pre-formed or quickly produced toxin (ETEC, Staph aureus, B. cereus).<br /></br>2. Stool character: watery vs bloody → watery = non-invasive.<br /></br>3. Systemic findings: high fever / sepsis suggests typhoid, Campylobacter, Shigella, etc.<br /></br>4. Geography: Latin America → ETEC is #1 cause (“Montezuma’s revenge”).  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>ETEC produces heat-labile (↑cAMP) and heat-stable (↑cGMP) enterotoxins → chloride and water secretion → profuse watery diarrhea without mucosal invasion. On NBME/Step exams, “young traveler + watery diarrhea ± nausea/abd cramps + Latin America/Asia/Africa” = ETEC until proven otherwise. Mainstay of management is oral rehydration; short course of fluoroquinolone, azithromycin, or rifaximin considered if severe or patient must travel.  </p></br><p>Core takeaway: Traveler’s watery diarrhea in an otherwise healthy adult is most commonly due to ETEC.  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (ETEC)<br /></br>• Reservoir: human GI tract; transmitted by fecally contaminated food/water.<br /></br>• Virulence factors:<br /></br>  – Heat-labile toxin (LT): ADP-ribosylates Gs → ↑cAMP.<br /></br>  – Heat-stable toxin (ST): activates guanylate cyclase → ↑cGMP.<br /></br>• Both toxins inhibit NaCl reabsorption and stimulate Cl-/H₂O secretion → secretory diarrhea.<br /></br>• Incubation: 1–3 days; duration: 3–5 days.<br /></br>• No invasion → no leukocytes or RBCs in stool, minimal fever.  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Pearls<br /></br>• Diagnosis usually clinical; stool cultures rarely done.<br /></br>• Oral rehydration = first line.<br /></br>• Antibiotics (e.g., azithromycin 1 g PO x1, ciprofloxacin 500 mg PO BID x3 d, or rifaximin 200 mg TID x3 d) reserved for severe disease or to shorten course.<br /></br>• Prevention: “boil it, cook it, peel it, or forget it.” Travelers may use prophylactic rifaximin in high-risk situations.  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“ETEC = Enterotoxin-Travel-El Montezuma’s Curse”<br /></br>Heat-Labile = Labile in the Air (↑cAMP); Heat-Stable = Stable on the Ground (↑cGMP).  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong  </p></br><p>a. Entamoeba histolytica<br /></br>• Causes invasive colitis → bloody diarrhea, abdominal pain, liver abscess (“anchovy-paste”). Incubation weeks, not 3 days; would have hematochezia, possible fever.  </p></br><p>c. Rotavirus<br /></br>• Watery diarrhea in unvaccinated infants/young children; rare in healthy immunized adults. Seasonality (winter), daycare outbreaks.  </p></br><p>d. Salmonella typhi (typhoid fever)<br /></br>• Gradual onset high fever, relative bradycardia, abdominal pain, rose spots, constipation → pea-soup diarrhea later. Much sicker patient; possible leukopenia, hepatosplenomegaly.  </p></br><p>e. Yersinia enterocolitica<br /></br>• Often from pork or unpasteurized milk; causes bloody diarrhea with RLQ pain mimicking appendicitis or terminal ileitis. Can trigger reactive arthritis or erythema nodosum.  </p></br><p>Key distinguishing feature: all of the above are invasive/bloody or systemic, unlike the benign watery illness of ETEC.  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────<br /></br>Take-Home Message<br /></br>In a traveler with abrupt-onset watery, afebrile diarrhea after visiting developing regions, think Enterotoxigenic E. coli—remember its dual toxins (LT, ST) and supportive care as primary therapy.</p>
61. A 27-year-old man who recently received the diagnosis of HIV infection comes to the physician for a routine follow-up examination. He feels well and has had no HIV-related complications. Highly active antiretroviral therapy has not yet been initiated. His childhood immunizations are up-to-date, and he has had no vaccinations during the past 10 years. He has no history of chickenpox during childhood and tested negative for varicella-zoster virus antibodies at his last visit. Examination today shows no abnormalities. His CD4+ T-lymphocyte count is 180/mm^3 (Normal ≥ 500), and plasma HIV viral load is 50,000 copies/mL. In addition to avoiding exposure to chickenpox or shingles, which of the following is the most appropriate next step regarding prevention of varicella-zoster infection in this patient?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Varicella-zoster immune globulin if exposed to chickenpox or shingles  </br>b. Varicella vaccine if exposed to chickenpox or shingles  </br>c. Varicella vaccine at this visit  </br>d. Varicella-zoster immune globulin every 3 months when CD4+ T-lymphocyte count is less than 200/mm^3  </br>e. Varicella-zoster immune globulin every 3 months beginning at this visit</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>A. Varicella-zoster immune globulin (VariZIG) if exposed to chickenpox or shingles  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Key clues<br /></br>   • Newly diagnosed HIV, HAART not yet started<br /></br>   • CD4 count = 180 cells/mm³ (immunocompromised; live vaccines generally contraindicated when &lt;200)<br /></br>   • Seronegative for VZV (never had chickenpox, no antibodies)<br /></br>   • Question stem explicitly asks for “most appropriate next step regarding prevention of varicella-zoster infection”</p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Pertinent positives/negatives<br /></br>   (+) CD4 &lt;200 → severe immunosuppression<br /></br>   (+) No immunity to VZV<br /></br>   (–) No current exposure or symptoms</p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Recognizable algorithm<br /></br>   a. Assess immunity → seronegative.<br /></br>   b. Is live-attenuated varicella vaccine safe? Only if CD4 ≥200 (or ≥15% in kids). Here CD4 &lt;200 → contraindicated.<br /></br>   c. Post-exposure prophylaxis in immunocompromised → VariZIG within 10 days of exposure.<br /></br>   d. No role for scheduled, repetitive immune-globulin doses.</p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>Cognitive approach for similar questions:<br /></br>   • Always check CD4 threshold before giving live vaccines (200 for adults).<br /></br>   • If live vaccine is unsafe and the patient is seronegative, your only option is passive immunization (VariZIG) after a bona-fide exposure.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Step-by-step link to correct choice:<br /></br>1. Need to prevent severe VZV in an immunocompromised, non-immune adult.<br /></br>2. Active immunization (varicella vaccine) is live attenuated → contraindicated CD4 &lt; 200.<br /></br>3. Baseline, there is no routine scheduled passive prophylaxis; rather, administer passive antibodies only if an exposure actually occurs.<br /></br>4. Therefore the correct prevention plan: tell patient to avoid exposure and, if exposure occurs, administer VariZIG ASAP (ideally ≤96 h, up to 10 days).</p></br><p>Take-home message: In HIV patients with CD4 &lt;200 who lack VZV immunity, give VZV immune globulin for post-exposure prophylaxis; do not give the live vaccine.</p></br><p>Documentation points if real patient:<br /></br>• CD4 count, VZV serology, vaccine contraindication noted.<br /></br>• Counseling: avoid exposure; return immediately for VariZIG if exposed.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• Varicella-zoster virus is a live-attenuated vaccine virus. Cellular immunity (CD4+) required to contain replication.<br /></br>• HIV patients with CD4 &lt;200 are at high risk for disseminated, severe varicella if given live vaccine.<br /></br>• VariZIG provides high-titer anti-VZV IgG, supplying immediate, short-term protection until risk period passes.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Pearls  </p></br><p>• Live vaccines (MMR, varicella, intranasal influenza, yellow fever) are contraindicated in adults with CD4 &lt;200 cells/mm³.<br /></br>• VariZIG: 125 IU/10 kg (max 625 IU) IM within 96 h of exposure; useful up to 10 days.<br /></br>• If no VariZIG available, IVIG is alternative.<br /></br>• Begin ART promptly; when CD4 recovers ≥200 for ≥3 mo, two-dose varicella vaccine can be given.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“Live-2-200 rule”: Live vaccines need CD4 ≥200.<br /></br>“Expose-Give-IG”: If an immunocompromised patient is exposed to a live-virus disease for which they lack immunity, Give Immune Globulin.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>B. Varicella vaccine if exposed: Post-exposure live vaccine is used in healthy people (best within 3–5 days) but is unsafe in CD4 &lt;200.  </p></br><p>C. Varicella vaccine now: Same contraindication; live vaccine could cause disseminated disease.  </p></br><p>D. Varicella-zoster immune globulin every 3 months when CD4 &lt;200: Not evidence-based; passive Ig gives short-lived antibodies (½-life ≈3 weeks); chronic prophylaxis is impractical and not recommended.  </p></br><p>E. Varicella-zoster immune globulin every 3 months beginning now: Same problem—no guideline recommends routine periodic Ig without a documented exposure.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Summary: With CD4 180 and no VZV immunity, defer live varicella vaccine and give VariZIG only if the patient is exposed.</p>
62. A 32-year-old woman is scheduled for elective surgical correction of bladder prolapse. A recent echocardiogram showed mitral valve prolapse but no mitral valve insufficiency. She has no history of serious illness, and she takes no medications on a regular basis. Her temperature is 36.8°C (98.2°F), pulse is 84/min, respirations are 12/min, and blood pressure is 90/58 mm Hg. The lungs are clear to auscultation. A grade 2/6, systolic ejection murmur is heard at the cardiac base, and two early systolic clicks are heard at the left sternal border. Which of the following is the most appropriate antibiotic prophylaxis for this patient?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Oral administration of amoxicillin 1 hour preoperatively followed by one-half dose 6 hours postoperatively</br>b. Intramuscular administration of ceftriaxone 1 hour preoperatively</br>c. Intravenous administration of amoxicillin and gentamicin 30 minutes preoperatively</br>d. Intravenous administration of vancomycin and gentamicin 1 hour preoperatively</br>e. No antibiotic prophylaxis is needed</br></br></br><p>Correct answer: e. No antibiotic prophylaxis is needed  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Procedure: “Elective surgical correction of bladder prolapse” → a clean genitourinary (GU) operation without active infection.<br /></br>• Cardiac history: “Mitral valve prolapse but no mitral valve insufficiency” → a benign, low-risk valvular lesion.<br /></br>• Cardinal clue: Question explicitly asks for “antibiotic prophylaxis,” signaling a test of current infective endocarditis (IE) prophylaxis guidelines.<br /></br>• No other high-risk features (no prosthetic valve, no prior IE, no cyanotic CHD, no transplanted valve).  </p></br><p>Approach framework for similar questions<br /></br>1. Ask: “Is this one of the four AHA ‘high-risk’ cardiac lesions?”<br /></br>   – Prosthetic valve or repair with prosthetic material<br /></br>   – Previous infective endocarditis<br /></br>   – Certain congenital heart disease (unrepaired cyanotic, repaired with prosthetic material &lt;6 mo, or residual defects)<br /></br>   – Cardiac transplant with valvulopathy<br /></br>   ➜ If NO, skip prophylaxis.<br /></br>2. If YES, ask: “Is the procedure on the list that causes significant bacteremia (dental, respiratory incision, infected skin/MSK)?”<br /></br>   ➜ If NO, skip prophylaxis.  </p></br><p>In this patient both answers are “NO,” therefore no IE prophylaxis.</p></br><p>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>• Current (2007, reaffirmed 2021) AHA/ACC guidelines eliminated routine prophylaxis for mitral valve prolapse, rheumatic/degenerative valvular disease, and most GU/GI procedures.<br /></br>• GU procedures need prophylaxis only if (a) patient has a high-risk cardiac lesion AND (b) the urinary tract is infected. Neither applies here.<br /></br>• Therefore the safest, guideline-concordant choice is to give no antibiotics.</p></br><p>Core takeaway: Endocarditis prophylaxis is now reserved for a very small subset of cardiac lesions AND only for procedures proven to cause significant bacteremia. Mitral valve prolapse without regurgitation is not on that list, and clean GU surgery is not a qualifying procedure.</p></br><p>Pathophysiology refresher<br /></br>• Endocardial injury + transient bacteremia → vegetation formation.<br /></br>• Benign MVP (no MR) produces minimal turbulent jet and rarely seeds bacteria, so risk ≈ general population.<br /></br>• Unnecessary antibiotics increase resistance, cost, and adverse events.</p></br><p>Memorization tip<br /></br>“Prophylax the PROSthetic, PREVious, cyanoTIC, TRANSplant valve” (PROS-PREV-TIC-TRANS) only for Dirty Dental or Respiratory/skin cuts.</p></br><p>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>a. Oral amoxicillin pre-/post-op: Classic dental prophylaxis regimen; not indicated for MVP or GU surgery.<br /></br>b. IM ceftriaxone: Used for some GI/GU surgical site infection prophylaxis, not IE prevention; question stem focuses on cardiac prophylaxis.<br /></br>c. IV amoxicillin + gentamicin: Former recommendation for high-risk cardiac lesions undergoing GU/GI with infection; patient has neither criterion.<br /></br>d. IV vancomycin + gentamicin: Reserved for high-risk patients with severe β-lactam allergy or resistant organisms; again, no indication.  </p></br><p>Bottom line: In a patient with isolated, uncomplicated mitral valve prolapse undergoing a clean GU procedure, give no endocarditis prophylaxis.</p>
63. A 27-year-old woman, gravida 1, para 1, comes to the physician 1 day after an episode of light-headedness while she was jogging. She has a long-standing history of heavy menses treated with a prescription iron supplement until 8 months ago. She has had hemorrhoids since the birth of her son 2½ years ago. She is otherwise healthy. Vital signs are within normal limits with no orthostatic changes. Examination shows no abnormalities except a mild flow murmur. Laboratory studies show:  </br>Hemoglobin 8.2 g/dL  </br>Hematocrit 24 percent  </br>Mean corpuscular volume 76/µm³  </br>Leukocyte count 5300/mm³  </br>Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Oral ferrous sulfate therapy  </br>b. Oral multivitamin supplementation  </br>c. Transfusion of 2 units of packed red blood cells  </br>d. Parenteral ferrous sulfate therapy  </br>e. Colonoscopy</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>a. Oral ferrous sulfate therapy  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Pertinent positives<br /></br>   • 27-year-old premenopausal woman (low a priori risk for colorectal cancer).<br /></br>   • Long-standing heavy menses that stopped iron pills 8 mo ago → chronic blood loss source already identified.<br /></br>   • Microcytic indices: Hb 8.2 g/dL, Hct 24 %, MCV 76 fL → classic iron-deficiency pattern.<br /></br>   • Vital signs normal, no orthostasis, only “light-headed once” and benign exam (flow murmur is common with anemia).  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Pertinent negatives<br /></br>   • No constitutional or alarm GI symptoms (weight loss, change in stool, family history of colon CA, melena).<br /></br>   • No evidence of malabsorption or intolerance to oral meds.<br /></br>   • Not hemodynamically unstable, Hb &gt; 7 g/dL.</p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Metacognitive approach<br /></br>   Step 1: Classify anemia (MCV ↓ → microcytic).<br /></br>   Step 2: Identify the likely etiology (menstrual blood loss + stopped iron).<br /></br>   Step 3: Ask “Do I need to transfuse, give iron, or pursue an invasive work-up?”<br /></br>   • Transfuse only if Hb &lt; 7 g/dL or active symptoms/instability.<br /></br>   • Treat the deficiency first; investigate further only if no improvement or red-flag signs.</p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>• Chronic iron deficiency from menorrhagia is extremely common in reproductive-age women.<br /></br>• First-line treatment is oral ferrous sulfate (325 mg elemental iron TID) for 3–6 mo after Hb normalizes to replete stores.<br /></br>• Transfusion is unnecessary because she is stable and mildly symptomatic.<br /></br>• Parenteral iron is reserved for malabsorption, intolerance, chronic kidney disease on dialysis, or need for very rapid repletion.<br /></br>• Colonoscopy is not the initial step in a 27-year-old without alarm features; if Hb fails to rise after adequate iron therapy, then further GI evaluation is warranted.</p></br><p>Take-home message: In stable premenopausal women with iron-deficiency anemia and an obvious gynecologic source, treat first with oral iron; reserve transfusion, parenteral iron, or invasive evaluation for specific indications.</p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real world)<br /></br>• Severity and symptoms of anemia, vitals/orthostatics, suspected source (menstrual), treatment plan (oral iron dose, duration, GI side-effect counseling), and follow-up CBC in 4–6 weeks.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (high-yield)  </p></br><p>• Iron deficiency → ↓ storage iron (ferritin), ↑ transferrin/TIBC, ↓ serum iron, ↓ % saturation → impaired heme synthesis → microcytosis (extra divisions) and hypochromia (pale cells).<br /></br>• Early symptoms: fatigue, light-headedness, exertional dyspnea; signs: pallor, flow murmur, koilonychia.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Algorithm  </p></br><ol></br><li>Confirm deficiency (CBC + low ferritin/TIBC, though board vignettes often skip labs).  </li></br><li>Start oral ferrous sulfate 325 mg TID with vitamin C (enhances absorption) on an empty stomach if tolerated.  </li></br><li>Re-check Hb/ferritin at 4–6 wk; expect Hb ↑ ~1 g/dL per week initially.  </li></br><li>If poor response or intolerance → switch to parenteral iron or investigate for occult bleeding/malabsorption.</li></br></ol></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“Fe-male anemia”:<br /></br>   • Fe = iron.<br /></br>   • male (even though it’s a woman) → think heavy fe-male periods = give Fe.  </p></br><p>IRON mnemonic for treatment indications<br /></br>   I – Intolerance to oral iron<br /></br>   R – Refractory (no rise in Hb)<br /></br>   O – Ongoing large blood loss needing rapid repletion<br /></br>   N – Non-absorption (malabsorption, gastric bypass)<br /></br>If none apply → stick with oral.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>b. Oral multivitamin supplementation<br /></br>   • Multivitamins contain minimal elemental iron (~18 mg) – inadequate to correct 8 g/dL Hb.  </p></br><p>c. Transfusion of 2 units PRBCs<br /></br>   • Indicated when Hb &lt; 7 g/dL (stable) or symptomatic/unstable. She is hemodynamically stable, Hb 8.2 g/dL → unnecessary risk.  </p></br><p>d. Parenteral ferrous sulfate therapy<br /></br>   • More expensive, risk of anaphylaxis, used only when oral iron is impossible or ineffective. She can absorb/tolerate oral iron.  </p></br><p>e. Colonoscopy<br /></br>   • First-line evaluation for occult lower GI bleed in patients ≥50 y or with red-flag features. In a 27-year-old with obvious menstrual source, invasive work-up is deferred unless anemia persists despite adequate iron.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line: Stable premenopausal woman + heavy menses + microcytic anemia → start oral ferrous sulfate.</p>
64. A 14-year-old girl is brought to the physician for a well-child examination. She appears well nourished. Menarche has not occurred. She is at the 35th percentile for height and weight. She has grown 8 cm during the past year. On examination, breast and pubic hair development are sexual maturity rating stage 3. The patient is concerned because all of her friends have begun menstruating and appear more developed than she does. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Reassurance that the patient is developing normally</br>b. Karyotype analysis</br>c. Measurement of serum luteinizing hormone and follicle-stimulating hormone concentrations</br>d. X-rays of the wrist and hand to determine bone age</br>e. Pelyic examination</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>A. Reassurance that the patient is developing normally  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues<br /></br>• Age 14 y (normal window for menarche 10-15 y)<br /></br>• Breast &amp; pubic hair Tanner (sexual-maturity) stage 3 → estrogen is being produced, HPO axis is functioning<br /></br>• Recent growth spurt (8 cm last year) → physiologic pubertal acceleration that usually occurs just before menarche<br /></br>• Normal height/weight percentiles; no chronic illness signs  </p></br><p>Pertinent positives: secondary sexual characteristics, growth velocity.<br /></br>Pertinent negatives: No virilization, no galactorrhea, no chronic disease, no cyclic pelvic pain → nothing suggesting an endocrine, structural, or genetic disorder.  </p></br><p>Algorithmic approach (cognitive framework)<br /></br>1. Define “primary amenorrhea.”<br /></br>   • No menses by age 13 y WITH absent secondary sexual characteristics OR<br /></br>   • No menses by age 15 y WITH secondary sexual characteristics.<br /></br>2. Ask: Does the patient meet either criterion?<br /></br>   • She is 14 y and HAS secondary sexual characteristics ⇒ criteria NOT met.<br /></br>3. Management: reassurance &amp; routine follow-up; no work-up indicated.  </p></br><p>Metacognitive tip: On test day, first decide if the patient satisfies the definition of primary amenorrhea. Only if the answer is “yes” do you proceed to labs, imaging, or karyotype.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Step-wise thought process<br /></br>1. Puberty is progressing (Tanner 3 breasts/pubic hair).<br /></br>2. Average interval from thelarche (breast buds, Tanner 2) to menarche is ±2½ y; menarche usually happens around Tanner 4.<br /></br>3. Therefore, a 14-year-old at Tanner 3 who has not bled yet is within the normal physiologic range.<br /></br>Core takeaway: Do NOT label as primary amenorrhea until ≥15 y (with secondary sex characteristics) or ≥13 y (without).  </p></br><p>Documentation (real clinical encounter)<br /></br>• “Pubertal development Tanner stage 3, growth spurt noted. Discussed normal timing of menarche; provided anticipatory guidance and reassurance.”  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• Menarche depends on a functional hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian (HPO) axis and adequate estrogen levels.<br /></br>• Secondary sexual characteristics prove estrogen is present.<br /></br>• The pubertal growth spurt peaks just before menarche; her 8-cm growth confirms appropriate timing.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatments and Diagnostic Tests Review  </p></br><p>Not indicated yet. Routine anticipatory guidance: nutrition, iron intake, menstrual hygiene education.  </p></br><p>When WOULD you investigate?<br /></br>• No menses by 15 y with breast development or by 13 y without.<br /></br>• Abnormal findings (no secondary characteristics, short stature, galactorrhea, virilization, pelvic pain suggesting outflow obstruction, chronic disease).  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“1-3-5 rule” for primary amenorrhea:<br /></br>1 = Age 13 without boobs → investigate<br /></br>3 = Menarche occurs ~3 Tanner stages after thelarche<br /></br>5 = Age 15 with boobs → investigate if still no menses  </p></br><p>Visual: Picture a traffic light:<br /></br>Green (&lt;13 no breasts, &lt;15 with breasts) = Go (reassure)<br /></br>Red (≥13 no breasts, ≥15 with breasts) = Stop &amp; investigate  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>B. Karyotype analysis<br /></br>• Indicated when there is primary amenorrhea WITH absent secondary sexual characteristics or signs of Turner (streak gonads, short stature). This patient has normal breast development and average height.  </p></br><p>C. Measure LH &amp; FSH<br /></br>• Used to differentiate central (low/normal FSH, LH) vs. primary ovarian failure (high FSH, LH) in true primary amenorrhea. Unnecessary until criteria met.  </p></br><p>D. Wrist/hand X-ray for bone age<br /></br>• Helpful when growth delay or constitutional delay suspected (short stature, delayed puberty). Her growth spurt proves puberty is on time.  </p></br><p>E. Pelvic examination<br /></br>• Would evaluate obstructive outflow lesions (imperforate hymen, transverse vaginal septum) — classically present with cyclic pelvic pain, bulging hymen, or hematocolpos, none of which are described.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line:<br /></br>A 14-year-old girl with Tanner stage 3 development and a recent growth spurt is still within the normal window for menarche. Provide reassurance and routine follow-up; no diagnostic work-up is warranted until age 15 if menses has not begun.</p>
65. An 18-year-old woman comes to the physician because of a 5-year history of recurrent, painful sores in her mouth and vagina. Multiple over-the-counter topical preparations have provided only temporary resolution of the sores. She is unable to identify any predisposing factors. One year ago, she had an episode of "walking pneumonia" treated with azithromycin. She has been otherwise healthy. Her mother has type 2 diabetes mellitus but is otherwise well. The patient has never been sexually active. Her temperature is 37°C (98.6°F), pulse is 84/min, respirations are 16/min, and blood pressure is 132/72 mm Hg. Examination shows scattered white plaques over the buccal mucosa and palate that are friable and bleed when scraped with a cotton swab. The toenails are yellow and thickened. Pelvic examination shows thick, white vaginal discharge and erythematous vaginal mucosa. There is mild cervical motion tenderness and no adnexal tenderness or masses. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's condition?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Combined hypogammaglobulinemia</br>b. Defect in cell-mediated immunity</br>c. Hyperglycemia</br>d. IgA deficiency</br>e. Recent antibiotic use</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer</br>B. Defect in cell-mediated immunity (impaired T-cell/Th17–IL-17 response)</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis</br>• Key findings:</br>  – 5-year history of “recurrent, painful sores” in mouth &amp; vagina → chronic mucosal infection, not episodic aphthous ulcers<br /></br>  – White plaques on buccal mucosa/palate that bleed when scraped → classic description of oral thrush (Candida)<br /></br>  – Thick, white vaginal discharge &amp; erythematous mucosa → vulvovaginal candidiasis<br /></br>  – Yellow, thick toenails → onychomycosis (often Candida or dermatophyte)<br /></br>• Pattern: Repeated mucocutaneous Candida infections in an otherwise healthy teenager = Chronic mucocutaneous candidiasis (CMC).<br /></br>• CMC is strongly associated with isolated defects in T-cell–mediated immunity, especially impaired Th17 differentiation or IL-17 signaling required to clear Candida from skin &amp; mucosa.</p></br><p>Metacognitive tip: Any NBME question that bundles recurrent thrush ± skin/nail Candida in a young patient → think “T-cell problem,” not “antibiotic use” or “diabetes.” Ask, “Is this one-time or chronic? Local or multi-site?”</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning</br>1. Identify organism: scrapable white oral plaques + vaginal cottage-cheese discharge = Candida albicans.<br /></br>2. Single vs chronic? 5-year history, nails involved ⇒ chronic.<br /></br>3. What immune arm defends against mucosal Candida?  • Neutrophils protect against systemic candidemia, but mucosal surfaces rely on Th17/IL-17-driven cell-mediated immunity.<br /></br>4. Therefore, the root cause is a defect in cell-mediated immunity.</p></br><p>Take-home: Recurrent mucocutaneous candidiasis = think T-cell/IL-17 pathway defect.</p></br><p>Documentation pearl: Include history of chronic oral/vaginal thrush, nail changes, lack of predisposing meds or diabetes, and plan for immunologic evaluation.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review</br>• Chronic mucocutaneous candidiasis</br>  – Genetic defects (e.g., STAT1 gain-of-function, AIRE mutations) → impaired Th17 cell development or IL-17 receptor signaling.<br /></br>  – Th17 cells recruit neutrophils and stimulate epithelial defenses; without them, Candida persists on mucosa, skin, nails.<br /></br>  – Serum immunoglobulins are usually normal.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Management &amp; Diagnostics</br>• Confirm Candida by KOH prep or fungal culture if needed.<br /></br>• Screen for endocrinopathies (CMC can coexist with autoimmune polyendocrine syndrome type I).<br /></br>• Treat acute episodes with topical/oral azoles (clotrimazole troches, fluconazole).<br /></br>• Long-term: Intermittent or chronic azole suppression; investigate immunologic defect in specialty clinic.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips</br>“Candid-T-a” → Candida mucosal disease = T-cell problem.<br /></br>“Neutro-blood, Th17-mucosa” → Neutrophils = bloodstream Candida, Th17 = mouth/vagina/skin.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Incorrect Answer Analysis</br>a. Combined hypogammaglobulinemia<br /></br>   – B-cell problem → recurrent sinopulmonary &amp; GI bacterial infections (Strep, H. flu), not isolated Candida. Thrush uncommon.</p></br><p>c. Hyperglycemia (diabetes)<br /></br>   – Can cause occasional vaginal candidiasis, but chronic widespread thrush &amp; nail involvement in an 18-year-old with normal vitals/labs is unlikely.</p></br><p>d. IgA deficiency<br /></br>   – Recurrent respiratory &amp; GI infections, anaphylaxis to blood products; candidiasis is not a hallmark.</p></br><p>e. Recent antibiotic use<br /></br>   – Explains a single episode of vaginal candidiasis after antibiotics, not 5-years of multi-site disease plus nail involvement.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Core Takeaway</br>Persistent or recurrent Candida limited to skin, nails, and mucous membranes in an otherwise healthy patient = defect in cell-mediated (Th17/IL-17) immunity.</p>
66. A previously healthy 23-year-old woman comes to the physician because of a 3-week history of seeing images after she looks at the television. She occasionally sees colored streaks out of the corners of her eyes. She experimented with a variety of illicit drugs from the ages of 15 to 22 years but has not used any since then. Her temperature is 37°C (98.6°F), pulse is 74/min, respirations are 16/min, and blood pressure is 110/70 mm Hg. On mental status examination, there is no evidence of auditory hallucinations, delusions, or paranoid thinking. Her serum thyroid-stimulating hormone concentration is 3.4 µU/mL. Urine toxicology screening is negative. Which of the following substances is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. Ecstasy (3,4-methylenedioxymethamphetamine)</br>b. γ-Hydroxybutyrate</br>c. LSD</br>d. PCP (phencyclidine)</br>e. Peyote (Lophophora williamsii)</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>c. LSD  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li>Time course: Visual phenomena are occurring 3 weeks after last drug use and in the absence of acute intoxication.  </li></br><li>Symptom quality: “Colored streaks” and after-images triggered by watching television; strictly visual with preserved insight, no psychosis.  </li></br><li>Exam/labs: Normal vitals, normal TSH, negative urine tox (rules out current intoxication or thyrotoxicosis).  </li></br></ol></br><p>Key clue: Recurrent, benign visual disturbances long after the drug was taken → classic for Hallucinogen Persisting Perception Disorder (HPPD), informally called “flashbacks,” most strongly linked to LSD.  </p></br><p>Metacognitive approach:<br /></br>• When you see delayed visual-only hallucinations with negative tox screen, think HPPD.<br /></br>• NBME likes the association “LSD → flashbacks,” so lock it in when the vignette shows persistent visual distortions without other psychotic features.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (Why LSD?)  </p></br><p>Step 1: Identify pattern – recurrent visual distortions (after-images, halos, trails).<br /></br>Step 2: Exclude mimics – no acute drug in system, no metabolic disturbance, no psychosis → not schizophrenia, delirium, thyroid disease, or intoxication.<br /></br>Step 3: Recognize hallmark association – HPPD occurs in up to 4–5 % of prior LSD users and far less frequently with other hallucinogens.  </p></br><p>Take-home message: LSD is the prototypical cause of flashback visual phenomena weeks to years after use.  </p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real patient):<br /></br>• Prior hallucinogen use (agent, dose, frequency, last use)<br /></br>• Nature and frequency of perceptual disturbances<br /></br>• Impact on functioning, insight preserved<br /></br>• Screen for comorbid mood, anxiety, substance use disorders  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>LSD (lysergic acid diethylamide): Potent 5-HT2A receptor agonist → cortical sensory disinhibition.<br /></br>HPPD: Thought to stem from persistent alterations in serotonergic cortical circuits leading to episodic release of stored visual memories when triggered by complex visual stimuli (e.g., TV).  </p></br><p>Risk factors: High cumulative LSD dose, polysubstance use, underlying anxiety disorders.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Management  </p></br><ol></br><li>Education/reassurance: Symptoms are usually benign and self-limited.  </li></br><li>Reduce triggers: Avoid psychedelics, stimulants, cannabis, sleep deprivation.  </li></br><li>Pharmacotherapy (if distressing): Trials of benzodiazepines or low-dose atypical antipsychotics (evidence limited).  </li></br><li>Document and monitor for progression to anxiety or mood disorder.  </li></br></ol></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memory Aids  </p></br><p>“L SD = L ong-term S ensory D istortions” → think flashbacks.<br /></br>Visual “trails” follow the person like an “L-S-D ribbon.”  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong  </p></br><p>a. Ecstasy (MDMA): Primarily causes acute empathogenic euphoria, bruxism, hyponatremia, hyperthermia. Flashbacks are not a typical delayed complication.  </p></br><p>b. γ-Hydroxybutyrate (GHB): CNS depressant used as a “date-rape” drug. Produces sedation, amnesia, respiratory depression—not delayed visual streaks.  </p></br><p>d. PCP (phencyclidine): Produces violent behavior, vertical/horizontal nystagmus, analgesia, hypertension during intoxication. No classic long-term flashbacks; urine tox would often be positive for days.  </p></br><p>e. Peyote (mescaline): Can cause acute hallucinations similar to LSD, but persistent perception disorder is far less common and far less classically tested.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line  </p></br><p>Recurrent visual “flashbacks” weeks after drug use with negative tox screen = Hallucinogen Persisting Perception Disorder. The board-favorite and most likely culprit is LSD.</p>
67. A 42-year-old man comes to the physician because of intermittent lower abdominal pain, diarrhea, and a 6.8-kg (15-lb) weight loss during the past year.  </br>He says that he tires easily.  </br>During the past 2 years, he has had a recurrent perirectal abscess treated with antibiotic therapy.  </br>Currently, his only medication is a daily multivitamin.  </br>He does not appear to be in acute distress.  </br>He is 180 cm (5 ft 11 in) tall and weighs 79 kg (175 lb); BMI is 24 kg/m2.  </br>His temperature is 37.2°C (99°F), pulse is 84/min, respirations are 16/min, and blood pressure is 145/75 mm Hg.  </br>Cardiopulmonary examination shows no abnormalities.  </br>A mildly tender abdominal mass is palpated in the right lower quadrant.  </br>Results of a barium enema are shown.  </br>Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. Antibiotic-associated colitis  </br>b. Cecal cancer  </br>c. Crohn disease  </br>d. Gastroenteritis  </br>e. Ulcerative colitis</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>c. Crohn disease  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>1. Chronic course: 1-year history of intermittent lower abdominal pain, diarrhea, and 15-lb weight loss → points toward a chronic inflammatory bowel disorder rather than an acute infection.<br /></br>2. Perianal disease: Recurrent perirectal abscess for 2 years is a classic extra-intestinal/perianal manifestation that strongly favors Crohn disease (occurs in ≈35% of patients) and is very uncommon in ulcerative colitis (UC) or other options.<br /></br>3. Right-lower-quadrant (RLQ) tender mass: Suggests thickened, inflamed terminal ileum or an inflammatory phlegmon—another hallmark of Crohn (ileocecal involvement is most common).<br /></br>4. Imaging clue: Although the actual image isn’t shown here, NBME loves the “string sign” (narrow, irregular terminal ileum) or “skip areas/cobblestoning” on barium studies—all textbook for Crohn.<br /></br>5. Pertinent negatives: No recent antibiotic use (rules out C difficile colitis); not an acute, self-limited gastroenteritis; no continuous rectum-to-colon involvement (would suggest UC).</p></br><p>Metacognitive tip: When you see chronic abdominal pain + weight loss + diarrhea + perianal fistula/abscess, jump to Crohn. Then confirm with location (RLQ/ileum) and imaging (string sign/skip lesions).</p></br><p>───────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (step-wise)<br /></br>• Chronic inflammatory bowel disease on differential → Look for key distinguishing features.<br /></br>• Presence of perianal abscess/fistula? → If yes, think Crohn.<br /></br>• Segmental involvement (skip lesions) and RLQ mass → Crohn over UC.<br /></br>Core takeaway: Perianal disease + segmental ileocecal inflammation = Crohn.</p></br><p>Documentation pearl: Note duration, weight loss, bowel habit changes, perianal complications, abdominal mass, and planned colonoscopy ± biopsy for confirmation.</p></br><p>───────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Crohn disease = transmural granulomatous inflammation anywhere mouth-to-anus; terminal ileum most common.<br /></br>• Transmural nature → fistulas, strictures, abscesses (e.g., perirectal).<br /></br>• Skip lesions with normal mucosa in between; creeping fat; cobblestone mucosa.<br /></br>• Etiology: immune dysregulation in genetically predisposed (NOD2), microbiome factors, and environmental triggers (smoking worsens).  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────<br /></br>Diagnostics &amp; Management (high yield)<br /></br>Initial work-up: CBC (microcytic anemia, leukocytosis), CRP/ESR, stool calprotectin, rule-out infection (stool cultures, C diff toxin), colonoscopy with ileoscopy &amp; biopsy (non-caseating granulomas).<br /></br>Imaging: CT/MR enterography for strictures/fistulas; barium studies show “string sign.”  </p></br><p>Treatment algorithm:<br /></br>1. Induction of remission<br /></br>   • Mild-moderate ileocecal disease: oral budesonide.<br /></br>   • Moderate-severe: systemic corticosteroids (prednisone).<br /></br>2. Maintenance<br /></br>   • Immunomodulators: azathioprine, 6-MP, methotrexate.<br /></br>   • Biologics: anti-TNF (infliximab, adalimumab), anti-integrin (vedolizumab) for steroid-dependent or fistulizing disease.<br /></br>3. Complications<br /></br>   • Abscess: drainage + IV antibiotics (e.g., piperacillin-tazobactam).<br /></br>   • Stricture/obstruction: possible surgical resection.<br /></br>   • Surveillance colonoscopy ≥8 years after diagnosis, then q1-3 years (↑CRC risk).</p></br><p>───────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“C-rowns” mnemonic for Crohn:<br /></br>C – Cobblestone mucosa<br /></br>R – RLQ pain (terminal ileum)<br /></br>O – Obstruction/strictures (“string sign”)<br /></br>W – Weight loss<br /></br>N – Noncaseating granulomas<br /></br>S – Skip lesions &amp; perianal fistula/abscess  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong<br /></br>a. Antibiotic-associated colitis (C difficile) – Presents days-weeks after antibiotic use with acute watery diarrhea ± fever; colonoscopy shows pseudomembranes, not chronic RLQ mass or perianal abscess.<br /></br>b. Cecal cancer – Can cause weight loss and RLQ mass, but usually presents with occult GI bleeding/iron-deficiency anemia; does NOT cause perianal abscesses or chronic inflammatory changes; barium enema shows “apple-core” lesion.<br /></br>d. Gastroenteritis – Acute, self-limited vomiting/diarrhea, no chronic weight loss, perianal disease, or RLQ mass.<br /></br>e. Ulcerative colitis – Continuous mucosal inflammation starting at rectum; presents with left-sided cramps, bloody diarrhea; complications include toxic megacolon, PSC, but perianal fistulas/abscesses and skip lesions are rare; barium enema shows “lead-pipe” colon.</p></br><p>───────────────────────────<br /></br>Key Take-Home Message<br /></br>Chronic intermittent diarrhea, weight loss, RLQ pain, and especially recurrent perianal abscesses point overwhelmingly to Crohn disease—further confirmed by “string sign” or skip lesions on imaging.</p>
68. A 72-year-old man is brought to the physician by his wife because of a 5-year history of progressive, waxy, dry, scaly lesions over his back. He has no history of serious illness or known allergies. His only medication is 81-mg aspirin. Examination shows approximately 20 nonpruritic lesions scattered over the back; the lesions range from 1 to 4 cm in diameter. A photograph of one of the lesions is shown. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Repeat examination in 12 months</br>b. Topical imiquimod therapy</br>c. Topical triamcinolone therapy</br>d. Punch biopsy</br>e. Wide excisional biopsy</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer</br>a. Repeat examination in 12 months</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis</br>• Age &amp; chronicity: Elderly patient (72 y) with a slow 5-year evolution of lesions → favors benign, age-related disorders.<br /></br>• Morphology: “Waxy, dry, scaly,” classically described as having a “stuck-on” appearance → buzz-phrase for seborrheic keratoses (SKs).<br /></br>• Number &amp; distribution: ~20 lesions, 1–4 cm, scattered over the back → SKs are commonly multiple on the trunk and face.<br /></br>• Symptoms: Non-pruritic, non-tender → inflammatory or malignant lesions (e.g., eczema, SCC) are often symptomatic.<br /></br>• Photograph (not shown here) on NBME typically depicts the raised, verrucous, well-circumscribed, brown/tan “stuck-on” plaque.</p></br><p>Key takeaway: Classic, benign SKs with no alarming features (rapid growth, bleeding, ulceration, color variegation, irregular border) → observation is sufficient.</p></br><p>Metacognitive approach:<br /></br>Step 1. Identify classic descriptive buzz-words (“waxy,” “stuck-on”).<br /></br>Step 2. Check for red-flag melanoma/SCC criteria (ABCDE, ulceration, bleeding, tenderness).<br /></br>Step 3. If none → reassurance ± routine skin surveillance.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning</br>• SK is benign and requires treatment only if symptomatic (e.g., pruritus) or for cosmetic reasons.<br /></br>• No suspicious features → biopsy or destructive therapy adds cost and morbidity without benefit.<br /></br>• Annual full-skin exams are recommended for older adults; thus, “Repeat examination in 12 months” is appropriate.</p></br><p>What you would document:</br>“Multiple classic seborrheic keratoses on back. No concerning morphologic changes. Patient educated; routine annual skin check planned.”</p></br><p>Core concept: Recognition of benign SK versus premalignant/malignant lesions prevents unnecessary biopsies.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review</br>• Etiology: Benign clonal proliferation of immature keratinocytes; accumulates keratin-filled cysts (horn cysts).<br /></br>• Risk factors: Age &gt;50 y, genetic predisposition, sun exposure.<br /></br>• Clinical variants: Flat, velvety macules early (“seborrheic melanosis”) → progress to raised, verrucous, waxy plaques.<br /></br>• Leser-Trélat sign: Sudden eruption of numerous SKs → consider internal malignancy (GI adenocarcinoma most common).</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────</br>Management Algorithm</br>1. Typical appearance, asymptomatic → reassurance, routine follow-up.<br /></br>2. Symptomatic or cosmetic concern → options include cryotherapy, curettage, electrodessication, laser therapy.<br /></br>3. Atypical or changing lesion → biopsy (usually shave or punch).</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips</br>“Stuck-on barnacles sit on old ships.”<br /></br>• Barnacle = SK (waxy plaque “stuck” to skin).<br /></br>• Old ship = elderly patient.<br /></br>Remember: If it looks like a barnacle and doesn’t bleed or itch, leave it be.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────</br>Incorrect Answer Analysis</br>b. Topical imiquimod therapy<br /></br>   • Used for actinic keratoses, superficial basal cell carcinoma, genital warts. Actinic keratoses are rough, sand-papery, sun-exposed and premalignant; not waxy or stuck-on.</p></br><p>c. Topical triamcinolone therapy<br /></br>   • Corticosteroid for inflammatory dermatoses (eczema, psoriasis). SKs are not inflammatory and do not respond to steroids.</p></br><p>d. Punch biopsy<br /></br>   • Indicated when diagnosis is uncertain or lesion shows malignant features. Classic SK appearance with long, stable history removes need for biopsy.</p></br><p>e. Wide excisional biopsy<br /></br>   • Reserved for pigmented lesions suspicious for melanoma or high-risk non-melanoma skin cancers. Over-treatment here.</p></br><p>Common pitfall: Biopsying every pigmented lesion. Learn to recognize benign SK to avoid unnecessary procedures.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────</br>Bottom Line</br>Classic, asymptomatic seborrheic keratoses in an elderly patient require no therapy—just routine annual skin examination.</p>
69. A 6-week-old boy is brought to the emergency department by paramedics after his father found the patient with his eyes rolled back and gasping for air 15 minutes after being put down for a nap. When paramedics arrived, the patient was limp and not breathing. They performed cardiopulmonary resuscitation and intubated him at the scene. Ten minutes later, his pulse was 166/min. The father reports that earlier the child had been fussy and had nasal congestion. The patient was born at term following a forceps-assisted vaginal delivery. On arrival, the patient remains intubated and ventilated by hand-valve mask; there is occasional spontaneous respiratory effort. His temperature is 35.4°C (95.7°F), pulse is 184/min, respirations are 30/min, and blood pressure is 56/34 mm Hg. Examination of the head shows a tense anterior fontanel that is slightly bulging with sutures separated by 3 mm. The pupils are 5 mm and react sluggishly to light. Funduscopic examination shows retinal hemorrhages bilaterally. There is no gag reflex. Deep tendon reflexes are absent throughout; there are no spontaneous movements. The patient does not open his eyes in response to verbal or noxious stimuli; there is minimal movement of the hands and feet in response to noxious stimuli. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. Aspiration with respiratory arrest</br>b. Meningitis</br>c. Nonaccidental trauma</br>d. Seizure</br>e. Sepsis</br>f. Subdural hematoma from forceps</br>g. Sudden infant death syndrome</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>c. Non-accidental trauma (shaken-baby syndrome)</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>1. Acute, unexplained cardiopulmonary collapse in a 6-week-old.<br /></br>2. Neurologic “red flags”:<br /></br>   • Tense, bulging anterior fontanel &amp; suture separation → ↑ intracranial pressure (ICP).<br /></br>   • No gag reflex, absent DTRs, minimal response to pain → severe global brain injury.<br /></br>3. Classic ocular finding: bilateral retinal hemorrhages.<br /></br>4. No external bruising, but infant was “fussy” beforehand (common precipitant for shaking).<br /></br>5. Birth history includes forceps, but injury is now (6 weeks later) and more severe than typical obstetric trauma.  </p></br><p>Pattern recognition: Retinal hemorrhage + bulging fontanel/Subdural bleed in a young infant = think shaken-baby (non-accidental head trauma).  </p></br><p>Metacognitive tip: When an afebrile infant presents with neurologic collapse, always scan the stem for retinal hemorrhages or inconsistent trauma history—those two clues virtually clinch the diagnosis of abuse on NBME/Step questions.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (Why C is correct)<br /></br>• Shaking causes rapid acceleration–deceleration ⇒ tearing of bridging veins ⇒ subdural hematoma and ↑ICP (bulging fontanel).<br /></br>• Traction on retinal vessels during the oscillatory motion ⇒ multilayered retinal hemorrhages.<br /></br>• Cerebral edema &amp; herniation account for absent brain-stem reflexes (gag, pupillary sluggishness), hypotension, and respiratory arrest.<br /></br>Core takeaway: In any infant with retinal hemorrhage ± subdural bleed, presume non-accidental trauma until proven otherwise; it is a medical emergency and a mandatory reportable condition.</p></br><p>Documentation (real life): neurologic exam, ophthalmology and neuroimaging findings, social history, chain-of-events, and notification of child-protective services.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Mechanism: Violent shaking → rotational brain movement → shear injury of veins &amp; axons → subdural blood + diffuse axonal injury → massive cerebral edema.<br /></br>• Risk factors: Caregiver stress, inconsolable crying, unrealistic expectations.<br /></br>• Clinical triad (classic boards): subdural hematoma, retinal hemorrhages, and encephalopathy.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Diagnostics &amp; Management<br /></br>Immediate:<br /></br>1. Airway/breathing/circulation (already intubated).<br /></br>2. Neuroimaging: CT head or MRI to confirm subdural/SAH, cerebral edema.<br /></br>3. Skeletal survey for occult fractures (posterior ribs, metaphyseal lesions).<br /></br>4. Ophthalmology exam documentation.<br /></br>5. Mandatory report to child protective services; admit for safety.<br /></br>Medical Tx: control ICP (mannitol, hypertonic saline, elevate head), neurosurgical consult for possible evacuation.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Memory Aids<br /></br>“SHAKE”<br /></br>S – Subdural<br /></br>H – Hemorrhagic retina<br /></br>A – Absent reflexes / Apnea<br /></br>K – Kids &lt;1 year<br /></br>E – Emergency, must report</p></br><p>Visual: picture a snow globe (brain) being shaken—blood flakes (retinal hemorrhages) and water level rising (↑ICP).</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong  </p></br><p>a. Aspiration with respiratory arrest<br /></br>   • Would explain apnea but not retinal hemorrhages or bulging fontanel; neuro exam should improve after oxygenation.</p></br><p>b. Meningitis<br /></br>   • Usually fever (or hypothermia) plus irritability and maybe bulging fontanel, but retinal hemorrhages are not typical; CSF signs needed.  </p></br><p>d. Seizure<br /></br>   • Post-ictal unresponsiveness common, but retinal hemorrhages and severe brain-stem depression are not explained; fontanel bulge suggests mass-effect, not isolated seizure.</p></br><p>e. Sepsis<br /></br>   • Could cause shock and neuro depression, but retinal hemorrhage + focal intracranial signs are not typical; no mention of infectious focus, WBC, or temp instability pattern.</p></br><p>f. Subdural hematoma from forceps<br /></br>   • Instrumental delivery hematomas present in the neonatal period (first days), not 6 weeks later, and rarely cause extensive retinal hemorrhage.</p></br><p>g. Sudden infant death syndrome (SIDS)<br /></br>   • Diagnosis of exclusion; infants are found dead, not resuscitated with clear neurologic injury and retinal hemorrhages.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line<br /></br>In an afebrile, collapsed infant with retinal hemorrhages &amp; signs of intracranial bleeding, non-accidental trauma (shaken-baby syndrome) is overwhelmingly likely and must be reported and managed emergently.</p>
70. A 72-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department by paramedics 40 minutes after she had a generalized tonic-clonic seizure. On arrival, she is mildly lethargic but able to answer questions. She says she had been feeling well prior to the seizure. She has hypertension and type 2 diabetes mellitus. She had breast cancer treated with lumpectomy and radiation therapy 12 years ago. Medications are lisinopril, atenolol, and glyburide. Her temperature is 36.3°C (97.4°F), pulse is 72/min, respirations are 22/min, and blood pressure is 130/70 mm Hg. Pupils are equal and reactive to light. The lungs are clear to auscultation. Cardiac examination shows no abnormalities. Examination of the left upper and lower extremities shows clonus. On the left, muscle strength is 4/5, deep tendon reflexes are 3+, and Babinski sign is present. Examination of the right upper and lower extremities shows no abnormalities. A CT scan of the head is shown. Intravenous phenytoin is administered. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Administration of dexamethasone</br>b. Administration of mannitol</br>c. Administration of metronidazole, ampicillin, and ceftriaxone</br>d. Intubation and mechanical ventilation</br>e. Radiation therapy of the right parietal lobe</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>a. Administration of dexamethasone  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Key clues<br /></br>   • New-onset generalized tonic-clonic seizure in an elderly patient<br /></br>   • Focal neurologic deficits: left-sided clonus, 4/5 strength, hyperreflexia, +Babinski → right cortical (parietal) lesion<br /></br>   • Past history of breast cancer (most common solid-organ tumor to metastasize to brain along with lung &amp; melanoma)<br /></br>   • CT image (not shown in text) in this context is almost certainly a ring-enhancing or heterogeneously enhancing mass with surrounding hypodense area (vasogenic edema).<br /></br>   • Vital signs and exam: no fever, no systemic infection signs, no airway compromise → stable patient.</p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Cognitive framework<br /></br>   Stepwise approach to a patient with first seizure and focal findings:<br /></br>   • Stabilize airway/breathing/circulation; give anticonvulsant (already done with phenytoin)<br /></br>   • Identify precipitating structural lesion on neuro-imaging<br /></br>   • If mass with surrounding edema → give corticosteroid (dexamethasone) to rapidly ↓vasogenic edema and ICP before definitive therapy (resection/radiation/chemo).  </p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Why dexamethasone now?<br /></br>• Brain metastases provoke vasogenic edema by disrupting the blood-brain barrier → extracellular fluid accumulation mainly in white matter.<br /></br>• Steroids (high-dose dexamethasone 10 mg IV bolus then 4 mg IV q6h) stabilize the BBB, ↓ capillary permeability, and dramatically improve focal deficits and lower risk of herniation within hours.<br /></br>• Guideline: Any patient with symptomatic brain tumor/metastasis and mass effect receives corticosteroid immediately after imaging confirmation.</p></br><p>What would be documented: “CT head shows right parietal mass with surrounding edema consistent with metastasis. Plan: Begin dexamethasone 10 mg IV now, continue q6h, neurosurgery &amp; radiation oncology consult, obtain MRI brain with gadolinium.”</p></br><p>Core takeaway: Symptomatic cerebral mass = treat swelling first with high-dose steroids.</p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (high-yield)  </p></br><p>• Vasogenic edema: plasma filtrate leaks through disrupted tight junctions → ↑extracellular fluid.<br /></br>• Glucocorticoids up-regulate tight junction proteins and inhibit inflammatory mediators → ↓edema and ICP.<br /></br>• Breast cancer metastases typically seed the gray-white junction and can present years after primary therapy.  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Treatments &amp; Diagnostic Tests  </p></br><p>Immediate<br /></br>1. IV dexamethasone (10 mg load; 4 mg q6h).<br /></br>2. Antiepileptic prophylaxis (already started).<br /></br>Subsequent/definitive<br /></br>3. MRI with contrast to define number/size of lesions.<br /></br>4. If solitary and surgically accessible → resection + whole-brain or stereotactic radiosurgery.<br /></br>5. If multiple lesions → whole-brain radiation or stereotactic radiosurgery ± systemic therapy.  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“Brain MaSS = Steroid STAT”<br /></br>M – Metastasis<br /></br>aS – Administer Steroid</p></br><p>Think “DeX presses edema” (DeXamethasone depresses swelling).  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>b. Mannitol<br /></br>• Osmotic diuretic for acute elevations in ICP (trauma, intracerebral hemorrhage, impending herniation). Effect short-lived; does not treat vasogenic edema long-term. Steroids are first-line for tumor-related edema.  </p></br><p>c. Metronidazole, ampicillin, ceftriaxone (triple antibiotics for brain abscess)<br /></br>• Abscess more likely with fever, leukocytosis, contiguous infection (otitis, sinusitis) or immunosuppression; CT often shows rim-enhancing lesion but history here strongly favors metastatic tumor and no infection signs.  </p></br><p>d. Intubation &amp; mechanical ventilation<br /></br>• Indicated if airway compromise, GCS &lt; 8, refractory seizures, or severe ICP with herniation signs (Cushing triad). Patient is awake, answering questions, stable respirations.  </p></br><p>e. Radiation therapy of the right parietal lobe<br /></br>• Definitive therapy but not the FIRST step. Radiation takes days-weeks to relieve mass effect; neurologic deterioration can occur in the interim without steroids.  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Take-Home Message  </p></br><p>In any patient with symptomatic brain tumor/metastasis, administer high-dose dexamethasone immediately to reduce vasogenic cerebral edema before pursuing definitive surgical or radiation therapy.</p>
71. A 32-year-old woman comes to the physician because of severely chapped skin and bleeding of her hands. Since she and her husband divorced 6 months ago, she has had an increasing preoccupation with cleanliness and hygiene. She repeatedly washes her hands after touching anything she believes may be contaminated. She acknowledges that the hand washing is excessive, but when she tries to stop she becomes anxious and feels compelled to wash even more to make up for the omission. Vital signs are within normal limits. Physical examination shows chapped and erythematous hands. No other abnormalities are noted. Mental status examination shows an anxious and depressed mood and dysphoric affect. On further questioning, the patient says she prefers not to go to counseling but is willing to try a medication. Which of the following is the most appropriate pharmacotherapy?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Alprazolam</br>b. Buspirone</br>c. Clonazepam</br>d. Risperidone</br>e. Sertraline</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>E. Sertraline (selective serotonin-reuptake inhibitor, SSRI)</p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis – How to recognize OCD in 20 seconds<br /></br>• “Repeatedly washes her hands … believes may be contaminated” → classic contamination obsession with a washing compulsion.<br /></br>• “Acknowledges that the hand washing is excessive” → ego-dystonic (pt knows behavior is unreasonable), a key feature of obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD).<br /></br>• “Feels anxious if she tries to stop” → compulsions performed to relieve anxiety from obsessions.<br /></br>• Functional impairment (bleeding/chapped skin) and clinically significant distress.<br /></br>Differential quickly narrows to OCD; no psychosis, no purely generalized worry, no panic attacks.  </p></br><p>Test-taking framework<br /></br>1. Diagnose. 2. Ask: therapy, meds, or both?<br /></br>   • First-line = exposure &amp; response-prevention CBT ± SSRI.<br /></br>3. Patient “prefers not to go to counseling but is willing to try a medication” → pick an SSRI.</p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning – Why sertraline?<br /></br>1. Evidence: Robust RCT data show SSRIs reduce both obsessions and compulsions.<br /></br>2. Dosing: often higher and longer trial (10-12 weeks) than for depression.<br /></br>3. Sertraline, fluoxetine, fluvoxamine, paroxetine = FDA-approved; clomipramine (TCA) is second-line due to side-effects.<br /></br>Take-home: SSRIs are the drug class of choice for OCD when patient declines psychotherapy.</p></br><p>What you would document<br /></br>• DSM-5 criteria for OCD met.<br /></br>• Discussed treatment options; patient declines CBT.<br /></br>• Risks/benefits of SSRI reviewed; sertraline 50 mg daily initiated, plan to titrate.</p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Brief<br /></br>• Dysfunction in cortico-striato-thalamo-cortical circuits with serotonergic dysregulation.<br /></br>• SSRIs increase synaptic serotonin, modulating this loop and reducing intrusive thoughts/rituals.</p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────</br>Treatment Algorithm Snapshot<br /></br>1.
