Pancreatic insufficiency – Think cystic fibrosis (CFTR mutation), not AAT deficiency. Panacinar emphysema in AAT differs from the bronchiectasis/pancreatic disease triad of CF.  </p></br><p>E. Thymoma – Classically associated with myasthenia gravis, pure red cell aplasia; no relation to AAT deficiency.  </p></br><p>The only option that represents a well-established, high-yield lifetime complication of AAT deficiency is cirrhosis.</p>
86. A 73-year-old woman, who resides in a skilled nursing care facility, is brought to the emergency department by staff because of a 6-hour history of confusion. She has schizoaffective disorder treated with risperidone. The staff members say that she usually is alert and fully oriented. Her temperature is 39.4°C (103°F), pulse is 84/min, respirations are 16/min, and blood pressure is 116/78 mm Hg. Physical examination shows muscle rigidity in all the extremities. On mental status examination, she is minimally cooperative and moans incoherently. She is oriented to person but not to place or time. Serum studies show a creatine kinase concentration of 2000 U/L. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Benztropine therapy</br>b. Discontinuation of risperidone</br>c. Electroconvulsive therapy</br>d. Hemodialysis</br>e. Vitamin E supplementation</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer</br>Discontinuation of risperidone (choice B)</p></br><p>───────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis</br>• “6-hour history of confusion” in a patient who was previously alert → acute encephalopathy.<br /></br>• Temp 39.4 °C (103 °F) + “muscle rigidity in all the extremities” → hyperthermia + lead-pipe rigidity.<br /></br>• Elevated creatine kinase 2000 U/L → rhabdomyolysis from sustained muscle contraction.<br /></br>• Current medication: risperidone (a dopamine-blocking antipsychotic).  </p></br><p>Classic tetrad for neuroleptic malignant syndrome (NMS):<br /></br>1. Hyperthermia<br /></br>2. Severe “lead-pipe” rigidity<br /></br>3. Autonomic instability (here: fever; BP OK but can fluctuate)<br /></br>4. Altered mental status  </p></br><p>Algorithmic approach for any suspected medication adverse effect:<br /></br>Step 1 – Stop the offending drug.<br /></br>Step 2 – Supportive care (IV fluids, cooling, ICU).<br /></br>Step 3 – Consider pharmacologic reversal (dantrolene, bromocriptine, amantadine) if symptoms are severe or not improving.</p></br><p>───────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning</br>Why discontinuation is the FIRST, most appropriate step:</br>• NMS is triggered by dopamine blockade; removing the dopamine antagonist halts further pathophysiology.<br /></br>• All other interventions (cooling, dantrolene, bromocriptine) are adjunctive and follow drug discontinuation.<br /></br>• Early withdrawal of the antipsychotic improves survival and decreases complications (renal failure from rhabdomyolysis).</p></br><p>Documentation points if this were a real patient:</br>• Time and dose of last risperidone administration.<br /></br>• Vital sign trends, neuro exam, CK level.<br /></br>• Orders to hold risperidone and all dopamine antagonists.<br /></br>• Plans for ICU transfer, cooling measures, IV fluids, and consideration of dantrolene.</p></br><p>───────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review</br>• Dopamine D₂ receptor blockade (particularly in hypothalamus and nigrostriatal pathways) → impaired thermoregulation and extreme muscle rigidity.<br /></br>• Sustained contraction → hypermetabolic state, ↑CK, myoglobinuria, potential acute kidney injury.<br /></br>Risk factors: high-potency typicals (haloperidol), but ANY antipsychotic (including atypicals like risperidone), rapid dose escalation, parenteral administration, dehydration.</p></br><p>───────────────────────────────</br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Tests</br>1. Immediate: Discontinue antipsychotic.<br /></br>2. Supportive: Aggressive IV fluids, external cooling, vital-sign monitoring, treat complications (renal failure, arrhythmias).<br /></br>3. Medications (if needed):<br /></br>   • Dantrolene – skeletal muscle relaxant.<br /></br>   • Bromocriptine or amantadine – dopamine agonists.<br /></br>Labs to trend: CK, electrolytes (K⁺, Ca²⁺), renal function, urinalysis for myoglobin.</p></br><p>───────────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips</br>“FEVER” for NMS:<br /></br>F – Fever<br /></br>E – Encephalopathy<br /></br>V – Vitals unstable (autonomic)<br /></br>E – Elevated Enzymes (CK)<br /></br>R – Rigidity (lead-pipe)  </p></br><p>“Stop the DOPE” to remember first step:<br /></br>D – Discontinue<br /></br>O – Offending<br /></br>P – Psychotic<br /></br>E – E-blocker (dopamine blocker)</p></br><p>───────────────────────────────</br>Why the Other Answers Are Wrong</br>A. Benztropine therapy<br /></br>• Used for extrapyramidal symptoms such as acute dystonia or parkinsonism, not for NMS. Rigidity in NMS is due to hypermetabolic state, not cholinergic excess.</p></br><p>C. Electroconvulsive therapy<br /></br>• Reserved for severe mood disorders or refractory catatonia; has no role in the acute management of NMS.</p></br><p>D. Hemodialysis<br /></br>• Not helpful—risperidone is highly protein bound and not dialyzable. Dialysis is indicated for certain drug ingestions (e.g., lithium), severe renal failure, or myoglobin-induced AKI that is refractory to medical therapy (far later in the course).</p></br><p>E. Vitamin E supplementation<br /></br>• Occasionally used for tardive dyskinesia prophylaxis or treatment, but tardive dyskinesia is a chronic choreiform movement disorder without fever or rigidity; unrelated to this acute presentation.</p></br><p>Take-Home Message</br>Neuroleptic malignant syndrome = hyperthermia + “lead-pipe” rigidity + AMS + ↑CK in a patient on dopamine antagonists. The very first and most important action is to stop the offending antipsychotic.</p>
87. A 33-year-old primigravid woman at 19 weeks’ gestation comes to the office for a routine prenatal visit. She feels well, and pregnancy has been uncomplicated. Ultrasonography 1 week ago showed a dichorionic-diamniotic intrauterine twin gestation. She has smoked one to two cigarettes weekly during pregnancy. Her pulse is 90/min, and blood pressure is 105/65 mm Hg. Fundal height is 22 cm. Twin A has a heart rate of 130/min, and twin B has a heart rate of 140/min. There is no lower extremity edema. Urine dipstick shows trace protein. This patient is at greatest risk for which of the following pregnancy complications?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Abruptio placentae  </br>b. Fetal demise  </br>c. Fetal growth restriction  </br>d. Preterm delivery  </br>e. Twin–twin transfusion syndrome</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>d. Preterm delivery  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li>Key clues<br /></br>   • “Dichorionic-diamniotic intrauterine twin gestation” → multiple gestation<br /></br>   • No other maternal comorbidities (BP normal, no edema, only trace protein, minimal smoking)<br /></br>   • GA 19 wk, fundal height slightly large (22 cm) → consistent with twins  </li></br><li>Pertinent positives/negatives<br /></br>   • Multiple gestation is the dominant risk factor in this healthy woman.<br /></br>   • Dichorionic status rules OUT twin–twin transfusion (occurs only in monochorionic twins).<br /></br>   • No chronic HTN, no cocaine/trauma to push abruptio to the top.<br /></br>   • Normal fetal heart tones &amp; growth → no current FGR or demise.  </li></br><li>Test-taking framework<br /></br>   • On NBME/Step questions, ask: “What is the SINGLE most common or most likely complication given the info?” For twins, the answer is almost always preterm labor/delivery unless another, stronger risk factor is supplied.  </li></br></ol></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (Why option D is correct)  </p></br><p>• Multiple gestation stretches the uterus → triggers myometrial contractility and cervical shortening → markedly higher rate of preterm labor.<br /></br>• Epidemiology:<br /></br>  – Twins: ~60% deliver &lt;37 wk, ~10% &lt;32 wk.<br /></br>  – Singletons: ~10% &lt;37 wk.<br /></br>• No other risk in the stem exceeds that probability. Therefore, preterm delivery is the greatest risk.<br /></br>Take-home: The NUMBER ONE complication of twin pregnancy (any chorionicity) is spontaneous or indicated preterm delivery.  </p></br><p>Documentation pearl: In the chart you'd flag this pregnancy as “high-risk twin gestation—counsel regarding signs of preterm labor; arrange serial cervical-length monitoring and possible progesterone/cerclage depending on findings.”  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• Uterine over-distention increases prostaglandin production → stimulates contractions.<br /></br>• Placental crowding → higher rates of preeclampsia &amp; growth issues, but those percentages remain lower than the incidence of preterm birth.<br /></br>• Chorionicity matters for TTTS but not for preterm-birth risk (both mono- and dichorionic twins deliver early).  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Management &amp; Diagnostic Points  </p></br><p>• Prevention/monitoring:<br /></br>  – Serial transvaginal cervical-length ultrasound (CL &lt;25 mm before 24 wk predicts preterm birth).<br /></br>  – Vaginal progesterone if short cervix; cerclage in select cases.<br /></br>  – Educate about contractions, pelvic pressure, vaginal bleeding/fluid leak.<br /></br>• Delivery timing:<br /></br>  – Uncomplicated dichorionic twins: 38 wk elective delivery.<br /></br>  – Mono-di: 34-37 wk; Mono-mono: 32-34 wk (C-section).  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“Twins come TWO soon.”<br /></br>Think “2 babies, delivery 2-3 weeks early (or more).”  </p></br><p>“Mono = TTTS risk, Di = Different plugs (2 placentas) so NO TTTS.”  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong  </p></br><p>a. Abruptio placentae<br /></br>   • Risk ↑ with hypertension, cocaine, trauma, prior abruption. Multiple gestation is a mild risk but incidence (~1–2%) is still far below that of preterm delivery (≈60%).  </p></br><p>b. Fetal demise<br /></br>   • Single-fetal or co-twin demise occurs, but overall risk remains low and far below preterm birth rates.  </p></br><p>c. Fetal growth restriction (FGR)<br /></br>   • Twins have ~15–25% risk of FGR, but again this is less than the 60% risk of preterm delivery.  </p></br><p>e. Twin–twin transfusion syndrome<br /></br>   • Requires a shared monochorionic placenta. This pregnancy is dichorionic-diamniotic, eliminating the possibility.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Core Takeaway  </p></br><p>In any twin pregnancy, the single most likely complication is preterm labor and delivery. Always think “prematurity” first when you see multiples on the exam.</p>
88. A 56-year-old man comes to the office because of a 3-month history of an enlarging painful ulcer on his left ankle.  </br>The ulcer occasionally exudes a clear, yellow fluid.  </br>He has cleaned the ulcer daily with soap and lukewarm water and applied an over-the-counter topical antibiotic to the area, with minimal relief.  </br>He has not had fever or chills or sustained any recent trauma.  </br>He has type 2 diabetes mellitus, coronary artery disease, hypertension, and hyperlipidemia.  </br>His medications are metformin, glipizide, atorvastatin, enalapril, hydrochlorothiazide, metoprolol, isosorbide mononitrate, and aspirin.  </br>He has no known drug allergies.  </br>He smoked one and one-half packs of cigarettes daily for 33 years but quit 5 years ago.  </br>He is 180 cm (5 ft 11 in) tall and weighs 90 kg (198 lb); BMI is 28 kg/m2.  </br>Vital signs are within normal limits.  </br>He does not appear to be in acute distress.  </br>A photograph of the left lower extremity is shown.  </br>Dorsalis pedis pulses are 2+ bilaterally; posterior tibial pulses cannot be palpated.  </br>Monofilament testing shows decreased sensation over the feet; proprioception is intact.  </br>Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Hypersensitivity vasculitis  </br>b. Ischemic ulcer  </br>c. Neuropathic ulcer  </br>d. Pyoderma gangrenosum  </br>e. Stasis ulcer  </br>f. Ulcerative lymphangitis</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>e. Stasis (venous) ulcer  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li>Location: “Left ankle” – specifically the medial malleolar region on the photo (classic site for venous ulcers).  </li></br><li>Appearance/Drainage: “Enlarging painful ulcer … occasionally exudes a clear, yellow fluid.” Venous ulcers are shallow, exudative, and have irregular borders.  </li></br><li>Vascular exam: Dorsalis pedis 2+; posterior tibial non-palpable but still at least one palpable pedal pulse. Intact pulses argue against severe arterial insufficiency.  </li></br><li>Neurologic exam: Decreased sensation (diabetic peripheral neuropathy), but ulcer is painful and not on a pressure point → argues against classic painless neuropathic (diabetic) ulcer on the plantar surface.  </li></br><li>Chronic comorbidities: Hypertension, obesity (BMI 28), prior smoking – all predispose to chronic venous hypertension and valvular incompetence.  </li></br><li>No systemic infection symptoms, no trauma history.  </li></br></ol></br><p>Approach/Framework:<br /></br>• Step 1 – “Where is the ulcer?” (Medial ankle → think venous; distal toes/heel → arterial; plantar metatarsal heads → neuropathic).<br /></br>• Step 2 – “What do the pulses say?” (Present → venous/neuropathic; absent → arterial).<br /></br>• Step 3 – “Pain?” (Venous = mild-moderate aching; arterial = severe ischemic pain; neuropathic = minimal).<br /></br>• Step 4 – “Skin changes around it?” (Hyperpigmentation, edema, lipodermatosclerosis → venous).  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Venous stasis ulcers develop from chronic venous insufficiency → venous hypertension → capillary leakage → tissue edema &amp; hypoxia → ulceration, usually above medial malleolus. They weep serous fluid and improve with compression and elevation. The presence of reasonably palpable pulses rules out critical limb ischemia, and the pain plus non-plantar location rules out a pure neuropathic diabetic ulcer. Core takeaway: medial ankle + intact pulses + shallow exudative ulcer = venous (stasis) ulcer.  </p></br><p>Documentation points in real practice: ulcer size/depth, appearance of granulation tissue, surrounding skin changes (hyperpigmentation, edema), pedal pulses, sensation, and plan for compression therapy ± wound care referral.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• Primary problem: Venous valve incompetence (post-thrombotic or idiopathic) → ↑ venous pressure.<br /></br>• Result: Capillary dilation &amp; leakage of fibrin + RBCs → hemosiderin deposition (brown discoloration), inflammation, dermal fibrosis (lipodermatosclerosis), edema.<br /></br>• Ulcer formation: Minor trauma + chronic hypoxia &amp; protein extravasation → poor healing → shallow ulcer.  </p></br><p>Risk factors: Age &gt;50, obesity, prolonged standing, prior DVT, CHF, pregnancy, HTN.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Tests  </p></br><ol></br><li>First-line:<br /></br>   • Leg elevation.<br /></br>   • Graduated compression therapy (30–40 mm Hg stockings or multilayer wraps) – cornerstone.<br /></br>   • Moist wound dressings (hydrocolloid/foam) ± debridement of necrotic tissue.  </li></br><li>Address underlying causes: Weight loss, treat varicose veins, manage CHF if present.  </li></br><li>Imaging: Duplex venous ultrasound if diagnosis uncertain or to plan surgery.  </li></br><li>Second-line / refractory: Endovenous ablation, surgical vein ligation/stripping, skin grafting.  </li></br><li>Antibiotics only if there is clinical cellulitis (erythema, warmth, purulent drainage).  </li></br></ol></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“VENOUS” mnemonic for venous ulcer:<br /></br>V – Velcro stockings (compression)<br /></br>E – Edema &amp; Exudative ulcer<br /></br>N – Near medial malleolus<br /></br>O – Ok pulses<br /></br>U – Uneven borders, shallow<br /></br>S – Skin changes: Staining (brown), Stasis dermatitis  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>a. Hypersensitivity vasculitis<br /></br>   • Presents with palpable purpura or small multiple ulcers, often on lower legs, often follows new drug or infection. Single, chronic ankle ulcer is atypical.  </p></br><p>b. Ischemic (arterial) ulcer<br /></br>   • Located on tips of toes, lateral malleolus, heels. “Punched-out,” very painful, minimal exudate, hair loss, cool shiny skin, absent or markedly diminished pulses. This patient has intact dorsalis pedis pulses and medial ankle location.  </p></br><p>c. Neuropathic ulcer<br /></br>   • Classically painless due to loss of protective sensation, found on pressure points (plantar metatarsal heads, heel). This ulcer is painful and on the medial ankle.  </p></br><p>d. Pyoderma gangrenosum<br /></br>   • Rapidly enlarging, undermined violaceous borders, often pathergic, associated with inflammatory bowel disease or rheumatoid arthritis. Histology shows neutrophilic dermatosis. The evolution here is slower and location typical for venous disease.  </p></br><p>f. Ulcerative lymphangitis<br /></br>   • Typically follows cutaneous inoculation with organisms (eg, Sporothrix schenckii); presents with nodular lesions tracking along lymphatics rather than a solitary ankle ulcer.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Key Takeaway  </p></br><p>A chronic, shallow, exudative ulcer just above the medial malleolus with intact distal pulses in a patient with risk factors for venous insufficiency = venous (stasis) ulcer. Recognize location + pulses + surrounding stasis changes to distinguish from arterial and neuropathic ulcers on exam questions.</p>
89. A 15-year-old girl is brought to the office for a well-child examination. Menarche has not occurred. She has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. Immunizations are up-to-date. Vital signs are within normal limits. She is below the 3rd percentile for height and at the 25th percentile for weight. Breast development is sexual maturity rating stage 2; there is no axillary or pubic hair. The remainder of the examination shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is most appropriate to determine if this patient is a candidate for growth hormone therapy?	Correct answer: F</br></br>a. Chromosome analysis  </br>b. Genetic testing for Russell-Silver syndrome  </br>c. Growth hormone stimulation test  </br>d. Measurement of serum luteinizing hormone and follicle-stimulating hormone concentrations  </br>e. Measurement of serum thyroxine and thyroid-stimulating hormone concentrations  </br>f. Skeletal survey to assess epiphyses</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>f. Skeletal survey to assess epiphyses (bone-age radiograph)</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues<br /></br>• 15-year-old girl, height &lt;3rd percentile ➜ pathological short stature possible.<br /></br>• Pubertal delay (Tanner stage 2 breasts, no pubic/axillary hair, primary amenorrhea).<br /></br>• Question stem: “Which of the following is most appropriate to determine if this patient is a candidate for growth-hormone (GH) therapy?”</p></br><p>How the clues guide our thinking<br /></br>1. A patient must have <em>open epiphyseal (growth) plates</em> for exogenous GH to cause additional linear growth.<br /></br>2. The most practical way to confirm open plates is a left-hand/wrist bone-age film (often referred to as a skeletal survey of the hand and wrist).<br /></br>3. Therefore the single test that answers the “candidate for GH?” question directly is assessment of epiphyseal status.</p></br><p>Metacognitive approach<br /></br>Step 1: Identify what the question is truly asking (GH candidacy, not etiology of short stature).<br /></br>Step 2: Recall prerequisite for GH benefit (open growth plates).<br /></br>Step 3: Pick the one option that gives that information (bone-age radiograph).</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Why a bone-age film?<br /></br>• GH works by stimulating chondrocyte proliferation at growth plates.<br /></br>• Once the growth plates fuse (usually soon after menarche in girls), additional GH will not increase height.<br /></br>• A bone-age film compares maturation of hand/wrist epiphyses to chronologic age and directly tells you if plates are still open.</p></br><p>What would be documented in the chart<br /></br>• “Bone-age radiograph obtained to determine epiphyseal status as prerequisite for GH therapy consideration.”</p></br><p>Core takeaway<br /></br>Always check bone age before starting GH therapy; open plates = potential benefit, closed plates = no benefit.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review in 3 Sentences  </p></br><p>• GH acts via IGF-1 to stimulate longitudinal growth at open epiphyseal plates.<br /></br>• Estrogen (from puberty or exogenous sources) triggers plate fusion; after fusion, no further height gain is possible.<br /></br>• Therefore, GH therapy is only indicated in children/adolescents whose bone age shows un-fused plates.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Treatment / Diagnostic Algorithm Snapshot  </p></br><p>Short stature work-up:<br /></br>1. Plot height velocity and growth curve.<br /></br>2. Obtain bone age film.<br /></br>   • Delayed bone age ➜ constitutional delay vs endocrine cause → further labs (TSH, IGF-1, etc.).<br /></br>   • Normal bone age ➜ familial short stature.<br /></br>3. If GH deficiency suspected → GH stimulation test <strong>after</strong> confirming open plates.<br /></br>4. GH therapy considered when: open plates, pathologic GH deficiency, Turner syndrome, chronic renal failure, etc.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Mnemonics / Memory Aids  </p></br><p>“GH = Grow Here”<br /></br>G – Growth plates must be<br /></br>H – Hand X-ray first to prove they’re Here (open).</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>a. Chromosome analysis – Useful to diagnose Turner syndrome (45,X), a cause of short stature; but Turner patients also need open plates for GH. It does not answer candidacy for GH directly.<br /></br>b. Genetic testing for Russell-Silver – Identifies a growth-restriction syndrome but, again, does not determine plate status.<br /></br>c. GH stimulation test – Determines endogenous GH secretion; you would not perform (or interpret) this test until you know the plates are open.<br /></br>d. Serum LH/FSH – Assesses pubertal axis (e.g., gonadal failure) but tells nothing about epiphyseal fusion.<br /></br>e. Serum T4/TSH – Screens for hypothyroidism (a reversible cause of poor growth) but does not speak to plate closure.  </p></br><p>Only option f directly establishes whether additional linear growth is physically possible, making it the best first step to judge GH therapy eligibility.</p>
90. A 31-year-old woman, gravida 2, para 2, comes to the office for a health maintenance examination. She says she has not eaten or drunk anything during the past 8 hours. Two years ago, she underwent bilateral tubal ligation. She has no known drug allergies. Her only medication is a daily multivitamin. She has smoked one pack of cigarettes daily for 16 years. She drinks two glasses of wine monthly. Her children are aged 5 and 11 years. She works at a doughnut shop. She is 157 cm (5 ft 2 in) tall and weighs 91 kg (200 lb); BMI is 37 kg/m2. Her pulse is 76/min, and blood pressure is 165/100 mm Hg. Examination shows facial hair and truncal obesity. Her fingerstick blood glucose concentration is 130 mg/dL. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Adrenal adenoma</br>b. Metabolic syndrome</br>c. Pituitary adenoma</br>d. Small-cell lung cancer</br>e. Uncontrolled type 2 diabetes mellitus</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>B. Metabolic syndrome  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis &amp; Test-Taking Framework  </p></br><ol></br><li>Abdominal/central obesity<br /></br>   • BMI 37 kg/m², “truncal obesity.”  </li></br><li>Hypertension<br /></br>   • BP 165/100 mm Hg (≥130/85 meets criterion).  </li></br><li>Impaired fasting glucose<br /></br>   • 8-hour fast finger-stick 130 mg/dL (≥100 mg/dL meets criterion).  </li></br><li>Hirsutism<br /></br>   • Common in insulin resistance; hyperinsulinemia ↑ovarian/thecal androgen production.  </li></br><li>Smoking and sedentary job are additional cardiometabolic risk factors.  </li></br></ol></br><p>Metabolic syndrome is diagnosed when ≥3 of the following are present:<br /></br>• Abdominal obesity (waist &gt;102 cm men, &gt;88 cm women; substitute BMI if waist not given)<br /></br>• Fasting glucose ≥100 mg/dL or drug treatment for hyperglycemia<br /></br>• Triglycerides ≥150 mg/dL or drug treatment<br /></br>• HDL &lt;40 mg/dL (men) or &lt;50 mg/dL (women) or drug treatment<br /></br>• Blood pressure ≥130/85 mm Hg or antihypertensive therapy  </p></br><p>The patient clearly meets three criteria (obesity, hypertension, hyperglycemia); lipid values were not provided but are unnecessary once the threshold of three is met.  </p></br><p>Cognitive road map for similar questions<br /></br>• Scan quickly for the “metabolic 5”: waist/BMI, BP, fasting glucose, TGs, HDL.<br /></br>• If you can tick off 3, think metabolic syndrome before jumping to rare endocrine tumors.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning &amp; Core Takeaway  </p></br><p>Metabolic syndrome = constellation of insulin resistance + cardiometabolic risk factors that markedly elevates risk for type 2 diabetes mellitus (T2DM), myocardial infarction, and stroke. Recognition is essential because aggressive lifestyle modification and medical therapy (statins, ACE inhibitors/ARBs, metformin) alter long-term morbidity and mortality.  </p></br><p>Document in chart: “Meets ≥3 ATP III criteria for metabolic syndrome (central obesity, fasting glucose 130 mg/dL, BP 165/100 mm Hg). Counsel on diet, exercise, weight loss; order fasting lipid panel &amp; HbA1c; initiate antihypertensive therapy; discuss metformin if HbA1c ≥5.7%.”  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Snapshot  </p></br><p>• Central adiposity → excess free fatty acids → hepatic steatosis, ↑VLDL, ↓HDL<br /></br>• Hyperinsulinemia to maintain euglycemia → insulin resistance in muscle/adipose<br /></br>• Insulin &amp; IGF-1 stimulate ovarian theca cells → ↑androgens → hirsutism (also seen in PCOS)<br /></br>• Endothelial dysfunction + low-grade inflammation (↑CRP, IL-6) → hypertension, atherosclerosis  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Management Pearls  </p></br><p>First line (cornerstone)<br /></br>• Weight loss ≥7% body weight &amp; ≥150 min/week moderate exercise<br /></br>• Dietary pattern: Mediterranean or DASH<br /></br>Adjunct pharmacotherapy (as indicated)<br /></br>• Metformin for impaired fasting glucose / pre-diabetes (A1c 5.7-6.4%)<br /></br>• Statin for elevated LDL or 10-year ASCVD risk ≥7.5%<br /></br>• ACE inhibitor/ARB ± thiazide for BP ≥130/80 mm Hg<br /></br>• Consider ezetimibe/PCSK9 inhibitor, GLP-1 agonists, or SGLT2 inhibitors in select patients  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>High-Yield Memory Aid  </p></br><p>“BATH-G” for Metabolic Syndrome<br /></br>B – Blood pressure ≥130/85<br /></br>A – Abdominal fat (large waist or BMI &gt;30)<br /></br>T – Triglycerides ≥150<br /></br>H – HDL low (&lt;40 M / &lt;50 W)<br /></br>G – Glucose ≥100 (fasting)  </p></br><p>Need 3 of the 5 to draw the metabolic BATH.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong  </p></br><p>A. Adrenal adenoma (ACTH-independent Cushing syndrome)<br /></br>• Would share central obesity, HTN, glucose intolerance, hirsutism.<br /></br>• However usually shows additional Cushing stigmata (purple striae, muscle wasting, easy bruising, osteopenia, facial plethora) and is far less common. Nothing in the vignette specifically points to cortisol excess beyond the more prevalent metabolic syndrome.  </p></br><p>C. Pituitary adenoma (Cushing disease)<br /></br>• Same Cushing stigmata missing here; plus many patients have headaches or galactorrhea if prolactinoma. Again, prevalence and lack of classic signs make this unlikely.  </p></br><p>D. Small-cell lung cancer (ectopic ACTH)<br /></br>• Occurs in older heavy smokers with systemic symptoms (weight loss, SIADH, paraneoplastic syndromes). A 31-year-old woman with weight gain, not loss, does not fit.  </p></br><p>E. Uncontrolled type 2 diabetes mellitus<br /></br>• A single fasting glucose of 130 mg/dL meets ADA criteria only if confirmed on repeat testing or if HbA1c ≥6.5%. Even if confirmed, diabetes alone does not explain hypertension and central obesity; metabolic syndrome encompasses all three abnormalities.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line  </p></br><p>This young, centrally obese woman with fasting hyperglycemia and hypertension fulfills ≥3 ATP III criteria—diagnostic of metabolic syndrome—an important precursor to overt diabetes and cardiovascular disease requiring comprehensive lifestyle and pharmacologic intervention.</p>
91. A 32-year-old woman comes to the office because of a 3-day history of vaginal itching and white vaginal discharge. During this time, she also has had a burning sensation with urination. She has type 2 diabetes mellitus treated with insulin. She is sexually active with one male partner; they use condoms inconsistently. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. Pelvic examination shows an erythematous vagina with thick, white discharge. The uterus is nontender and normal in size and shape. There is no adnexal tenderness. Microscopy of the vaginal discharge is shown. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. Bacterial culture of the vagina</br>b. Metronidazole therapy</br>c. Terconazole therapy</br>d. Viral culture of the vagina</br>e. Yeast culture of the vagina</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>c. Terconazole therapy (topical azole antifungal)</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues that immediately narrow the differential for vaginitis:<br /></br>• “Vaginal itching” + “burning with urination” (external irritation)<br /></br>• Thick, white, adherent/cottage-cheese–like discharge<br /></br>• Erythematous vaginal mucosa; cervix/uterus/adnexa nontender (rules out PID)<br /></br>• Major risk factor: diabetes mellitus (hyperglycemia favors Candida overgrowth)<br /></br>• Wet-mount microscopy image (not shown here) in this setting almost certainly demonstrates budding yeast ± pseudohyphae.</p></br><p>Pattern recognition: Pruritus + thick white discharge + normal vaginal pH (not stated, but exam usually implies) = Vulvovaginal candidiasis (VVC).  </p></br><p>Metacognitive approach: For vaginitis questions, first classify the discharge.<br /></br>1. Thin, gray, fishy odor → Bacterial vaginosis (BV) → metronidazole.<br /></br>2. Frothy, yellow-green, strawberry cervix → Trichomoniasis → metronidazole + treat partner.<br /></br>3. Thick, white, cottage cheese, pruritic → Candida → topical or oral azole.<br /></br>If classic presentation + microscopy confirmation, treat empirically; cultures are reserved for atypical, recurrent, or refractory cases.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Step-wise thought process:<br /></br>1. Identify that symptoms and exam fit Candida.<br /></br>2. Recognize that uncomplicated VVC in a non-pregnant, immunocompetent adult is treated first-line with intravaginal azole (e.g., terconazole 0.4% or 0.8% cream x 3–7 days) or a single 150-mg oral fluconazole.<br /></br>3. Because the diagnosis is straightforward, no additional laboratory testing is required.</p></br><p>Core takeaway: Classic VVC = treat, don’t test.</p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real life): “External genitalia erythematous, thick adherent white discharge; wet mount positive for budding yeast; counseling on topical azole therapy provided.”</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review (High-Yield)  </p></br><p>• Candida albicans is part of normal vaginal flora in ≤20% of women.<br /></br>• Risk factors: diabetes, recent antibiotics, high estrogen states (OCPs, pregnancy), immunosuppression, corticosteroids, tight clothing.<br /></br>• Vaginal pH remains 3.8-4.5 (unlike BV or trichomoniasis).<br /></br>• Yeast forms pseudohyphae under microscopy with KOH prep.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Tests Review  </p></br><p>First-line options for uncomplicated VVC:<br /></br>• Intravaginal azoles: terconazole, clotrimazole, miconazole, butoconazole (3–7-day regimens).<br /></br>• Single-dose oral fluconazole 150 mg (contraindicated in pregnancy—use topical).<br /></br>No testing beyond wet mount unless:<br /></br>• Recurrent (≥4 episodes/yr)<br /></br>• Severe or non-albicans species<br /></br>• Immunocompromised or pregnant.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“CANDIDA”:<br /></br>C – Cottage-cheese discharge<br /></br>A – Acidic pH (normal 3.8-4.5)<br /></br>N – No odor<br /></br>D – Diabetes, Drugs (antibiotics) predispose<br /></br>I – Itching<br /></br>D – Dyspareunia/Dysuria external<br /></br>A – Azole creams or fluconazole treat</p></br><p>Think “Three A’s”: Acidic pH, Adherent discharge, Azole therapy.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>a. Bacterial culture of the vagina<br /></br>• BV is diagnosed clinically (Amsel criteria) or with clue cells on wet mount; routine bacterial cultures are not recommended.</p></br><p>b. Metronidazole therapy<br /></br>• Targets anaerobes (BV) and protozoa (Trichomonas). Ineffective against Candida; would leave patient symptomatic.</p></br><p>d. Viral culture of the vagina<br /></br>• Indicated for suspected HSV lesions, not pruritic discharge with yeast on microscopy.</p></br><p>e. Yeast culture of the vagina<br /></br>• Done only for recurrent or refractory VVC to identify non-albicans species. Unnecessary in a first, classic presentation.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Bottom Line  </p></br><p>Classic “cottage-cheese” vaginitis + budding yeast on microscopy = vulvovaginal candidiasis → treat empirically with an intravaginal azole such as terconazole. No further cultures or metronidazole are indicated.</p>
92. A 16-year-old girl is brought to the emergency department immediately after she collapsed on her way to the bathroom at home. She has a history of anorexia nervosa and has had an 18-kg (40-lb) weight loss during the past 16 months. Menarche was at the age of 11 years, but her last menstrual period was 14 months ago. She does not drink alcohol or use illicit drugs. On arrival, she appears cachectic. She is 165 cm (5 ft 5 in) tall and weighs 36 kg (80 lb); BMI is 13 kg/m2. Her pulse is 65/min, respirations are 12/min, and blood pressure is 85/55 mm Hg. Physical examination shows fine hair over the skin. An ECG is shown. This patient is most likely to have which of the following sets of serum electrolyte findings?</br>Na+ (mEq/L) K+ (mEq/L) Cl⁻ (mEq/L) HCO₃⁻ (mEq/L)	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. 125 3.2 102 25</br>b. 125 6.8 102 27</br>c. 140 2.3 94 28</br>d. 140 6.8 94 28</br>e. 155 3.2 102 26</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>C.  Na⁺ 140 mEq/L | K⁺ 2.3 mEq/L | Cl⁻ 94 mEq/L | HCO₃⁻ 28 mEq/L  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Key clues<br /></br>   • 16-year-old girl with severe anorexia nervosa (BMI 13 kg/m², lanugo).<br /></br>   • Syncope + BP 85/55 → volume depletion.<br /></br>   • 14-month secondary amenorrhea → prolonged malnutrition.<br /></br>   • ECG (not shown, but classic test set-up) → most NBME stems show U-waves/flattened T-waves → hypokalemia.  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Diagnostic reasoning<br /></br>   • Purging behaviors (self-induced vomiting, diuretic/laxative abuse) are common in anorexia nervosa and cause:<br /></br>     – Loss of gastric H⁺ &amp; Cl⁻ → metabolic alkalosis (↑ HCO₃⁻, ↓ Cl⁻).<br /></br>     – Volume contraction → activates RAAS → aldosterone reabsorbs Na⁺ in exchange for renal K⁺ and H⁺ loss → hypokalemia, further alkalosis.<br /></br>   • Sodium can be low, normal, or mildly high; RAAS and free-water retention often normalize Na⁺.  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Pattern to memorize:<br /></br>   Volume contraction alkalosis (“contraction alkalosis”) =<br /></br>     Normal/↑ Na⁺, ↓ K⁺, ↓ Cl⁻, ↑ HCO₃⁻.  </p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (Step-wise)  </p></br><p>Step 1 – Identify likely purging → think metabolic alkalosis.<br /></br>Step 2 – Ask: “What does metabolic alkalosis look like in the basic panel?”<br /></br>          ↑ HCO₃⁻  (&gt;26–28 mEq/L) + ↓ Cl⁻.<br /></br>Step 3 – Remember RAAS effect → hypokalemia (often profound).<br /></br>Step 4 – Check sodium: typically normal because aldosterone reabsorbs Na⁺.<br /></br>Choice C is the only option with all four features.  </p></br><p>Core takeaway: Vomiting/diuretic abuse → metabolic alkalosis with hypochloremia and hypokalemia.  </p></br><p>Documentation pearl: For a real patient you would document orthostatic vitals, ECG findings (e.g., U-waves), and CMP results supporting contraction alkalosis; note concern for arrhythmia from severe hypokalemia.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><ol></br><li>Loss of gastric fluid (HCl) → direct loss of H⁺ and Cl⁻.  </li></br><li>Volume depletion → ↓ renal perfusion → ↑ renin → ↑ angiotensin II → ↑ aldosterone.  </li></br><li>Aldosterone → Na⁺ reabsorption / K⁺ and H⁺ secretion in collecting duct → worsens hypokalemia &amp; alkalosis.  </li></br><li>Low Cl⁻ limits kidney’s ability to excrete HCO₃⁻ (Cl⁻/HCO₃⁻ exchanger), sustaining alkalosis.  </li></br></ol></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostics Review (high-yield quick hits)  </p></br><p>• Immediate: cardiac monitor, IV isotonic saline ± IV KCl replacement.<br /></br>• Long-term: multidisciplinary eating-disorder management, psychotherapy (CBT), nutrition rehabilitation, treat osteoporosis, screen for depression.<br /></br>• Labs: CMP, Mg²⁺ (often low), ECG.<br /></br>• Avoid overly rapid refeeding → risk of refeeding syndrome (hypophosphatemia).  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“Vomiting CHiPs away at you”<br /></br>C = ↓ Chloride<br /></br>H = ↑ HCO₃⁻<br /></br>P = ↓ Potassium<br /></br>(Sodium tends to stay Put)  </p></br><p>or<br /></br>“Lose acid, lose Cl⁻, keep the base, dump K⁺”  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>A. 125 / 3.2 / 102 / 25<br /></br>   – Hyponatremia present, but Cl⁻ and HCO₃⁻ are normal: no alkalosis → inconsistent with vomiting.  </p></br><p>B. 125 / 6.8 / 102 / 27<br /></br>   – Severe hyperkalemia (6.8) would show peaked T-waves, not U-waves. Vomiting causes hypo-, not hyper-kalemia.  </p></br><p>D. 140 / 6.8 / 94 / 28<br /></br>   – Has alkalosis &amp; hypochloremia, but hyperkalemia contradicts aldosterone effect and the ECG findings typical of anorexia-related collapse.  </p></br><p>E. 155 / 3.2 / 102 / 26<br /></br>   – Hypernatremia with normal Cl⁻ &amp; HCO₃⁻ (no alkalosis). Also Cl⁻ not low.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom line: In purging-type anorexia nervosa, expect normal/↑ Na⁺, ↓ K⁺, ↓ Cl⁻, and ↑ HCO₃⁻ → exactly the profile in choice C.</p>
93. An 8-year-old boy with osteosarcoma of the left proximal tibia that is now metastatic to the lungs is transferred to the care of home hospice services for management of his symptoms. At the time of diagnosis 2 years ago, the patient was treated with surgical resection of the tumor with an above-knee amputation and chemotherapy. However, during the past 2 months, he has developed multiple metastatic pulmonary lesions that have not responded to chemotherapy. Today, he reports chest pain and difficulty breathing. He appears weak and cachectic. Vital signs are temperature 36.9°C (98.4°F), pulse 112/min, and respirations 28/min and shallow. Pulse oximetry on room air shows an oxygen saturation of 95 percent. Lungs are clear to auscultation. Cardiac examination discloses a grade 2/6 systolic murmur with a normal S1 and S2. Abdominal examination discloses no abnormalities. The parents say they want to focus care on management of their son's pain and symptoms. Which of the following is the most appropriate statement to the family regarding their wishes for the patient?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Intramuscular opioids will allow rapid resolution of pain symptoms</br>b. Intravenous opioids can only be given in the hospital</br>c. Opioids can be initiated but must be discontinued if he develops hypoventilation</br>d. Opioids should not be initiated since he already has signs of respiratory involvement</br>e. Opioids will control both his dyspnea and pain</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>E. “Opioids will control both his dyspnea and pain.”</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis</br>• Key words &amp; phrases: “metastatic osteosarcoma … multiple pulmonary lesions,” “home hospice,” “focus care on management of pain and symptoms,” “chest pain and difficulty breathing,” tachypnea (28/min) but normal oxygen saturation and clear lungs.<br /></br>• Pertinent positives: terminal cancer, chest pain, air-hunger/dyspnea despite 95 % SpO₂ ⇒ classic palliative scenario.<br /></br>• Pertinent negatives: no rales/wheezes, no hypoxia, so dyspnea is not due to reversible bronchospasm or pneumonia; it is a subjective sensation likely from tumor burden/pleural irritation—high yield clue that centrally acting meds (opioids) are indicated.<br /></br>• Cognitive framework: In hospice, priority = comfort. Morphine (or another opioid) is first-line for 1) cancer pain and 2) the subjective sensation of dyspnea independent of oxygen saturation.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning</br>1. Goals-of-care discussion confirms symptom control only.<br /></br>2. Determine which therapy simultaneously addresses pain + “air hunger.”<br /></br>3. Opioids (oral, subcutaneous, IV) ↓ central perception of dyspnea, ↓ ventilatory drive (calms rapid, shallow breathing), and relieve nociceptive pain.<br /></br>4. Therefore, the most appropriate counseling statement: “Opioids will control both his dyspnea and pain.”</p></br><p>Take-home message: In end-of-life care, low-dose systemic opioids are evidence-based first-line agents for subjective dyspnea even when oxygen saturation is adequate.</p></br><p>Documentation pearl: Record that parents understand the palliative intent of opioid therapy and consent to titration for comfort even if respiratory rate decreases.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review</br>• Dyspnea in advanced cancer is multifactorial—mechanical restriction from tumors, pleural irritation, anxiety, and increased ventilatory drive.<br /></br>• Opioids act on μ-receptors in brainstem respiratory centers → raise CO₂ threshold &amp; reduce ventilatory response → patient feels less “air hunger.”<br /></br>• They also modulate nociceptive pathways at the dorsal horn and supraspinal sites → analgesia.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Pearls</br>• Preferred routes in home hospice: oral liquid/sublingual morphine, immediate-release tablets, or continuous subcutaneous infusion.<br /></br>• Titrate to comfort; respiratory depression is monitored but not a reason to withhold therapy when comfort is the goal.<br /></br>• Supplemental oxygen is useful only if hypoxic; here SpO₂ 95 %, so opioids are superior for symptomatic relief.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>High-Yield Memory Aids</br>“Morphine Manages More than pain:<br /></br>Pain, Panic (air hunger), and Pulmonary discomfort.”</p></br><p>Think “MORPH” for hospice dyspnea:<br /></br>M – Morphine<br /></br>O – Oxygen only if hypoxic<br /></br>R – Reassure family about intent<br /></br>P – PRN escalation<br /></br>H – Home route (oral/SC) acceptable</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Incorrect Answer Analysis</br>A. “Intramuscular opioids will allow rapid resolution of pain symptoms.”<br /></br>• IM injections are painful, have erratic absorption, and are not standard in hospice; oral or SC preferred. Does not address dyspnea.</p></br><p>B. “Intravenous opioids can only be given in the hospital.”<br /></br>• False. Home hospice can use continuous SC/IV infusions via portable pumps.</p></br><p>C. “Opioids can be initiated but must be discontinued if he develops hypoventilation.”<br /></br>• In palliative care, mild ↓RR is expected; goal is comfort, not strict respiratory parameters. Titrate rather than reflexively discontinue.</p></br><p>D. “Opioids should not be initiated since he already has signs of respiratory involvement.”<br /></br>• Precisely in respiratory involvement/dyspnea opioids are indicated. Avoiding them would withhold the most effective therapy.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Core Concept</br>Low-dose systemic opioids are the cornerstone of symptomatic treatment for BOTH cancer pain and refractory dyspnea in hospice patients, irrespective of oxygen saturation.</p>
94. A 17-year-old girl is brought to the office by her parents because of a 3-week history of changes in her behavior. She previously received grades of A’s in school; during the past month, she has received grades of C’s. She runs on the cross-country team at school. The patient’s mother also is concerned about her daughter’s diet, noting that she follows a high-carbohydrate diet before races and otherwise eats only vegetables. When interviewed alone, the patient says that she has had difficulty concentrating in school during the past 2 weeks because she “is tired all of the time” and is not interested in the classes like she used to be. During this time, she also has had difficulty sleeping. She says she sometimes eats a large amount of junk food and then feels guilty about it. She refuses to answer questions about purging. She has no other history of serious illness. Her only medication is a daily multivitamin. She is 170 cm (5 ft 7 in) tall and weighs 54 kg (120 lb); BMI is 19 kg/m². Vital signs are within normal limits. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. On mental status examination, she has a dysphoric affect and describes her mood as “irritable.” She has thought about death but has no suicidal intent. Which of the following medications is contraindicated in this patient?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Bupropion</br>b. Desipramine</br>c. Fluoxetine</br>d. Mirtazapine</br>e. Venlafaxine</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>A. Bupropion  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Key clues pointing to an eating disorder<br /></br>   • Competitive cross-country runner (high-risk cohort).<br /></br>   • Restrictive “vegetables-only” diet except for pre-race carbohydrate loading.<br /></br>   • Recurrent episodes of eating “a large amount of junk food” followed by guilt → binge-eating behavior.<br /></br>   • Refusal to answer questions about purging (common in bulimia).<br /></br>   • Normal BMI (19 kg/m²) → argues against anorexia but is typical for bulimia nervosa.  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Mood symptoms<br /></br>   • 3-week history of ↓ concentration, fatigue, insomnia, irritability, anhedonia → meets criteria for a current major depressive episode.<br /></br>   • Suicidal ideation denied, no intent.  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Exam &amp; labs<br /></br>   • Vitals and PE unremarkable; no signs of malnutrition → again fits bulimia picture.  </p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>Metacognitive approach for similar questions<br /></br>• When you see binge eating + guilt in a normal-weight adolescent or young adult, immediately place “bulimia nervosa” high on the differential.<br /></br>• In any patient with AN or BN, screen pharmacologic options for seizure-threshold-lowering drugs (bupropion, clozapine, tramadol) and agents causing QT prolongation/electrolyte issues.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Why bupropion is contraindicated:<br /></br>• Mechanism: Norepinephrine–dopamine reuptake inhibitor with stimulant properties.<br /></br>• Adverse effect: Significantly lowers seizure threshold.<br /></br>• BN and AN patients have frequent vomiting/laxative use → hypokalemia, metabolic alkalosis, and fluid shifts, all of which further predispose to seizures.<br /></br>• Therefore, FDA boxed warning: bupropion is contraindicated in patients with current or past bulimia nervosa or anorexia nervosa.  </p></br><p>Take-home message:<br /></br>Never use bupropion in eating-disorder patients; choose an SSRI (fluoxetine is FDA-approved) for comorbid depression or bulimic behaviors.  </p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real world)<br /></br>• Record DSM-5 criteria met for bulimia nervosa.<br /></br>• Document discussion of seizure risk and medication selection.<br /></br>• Note suicide assessment and safety planning.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (concise)  </p></br><p>Bulimia nervosa<br /></br>• Core pathology: recurrent binge eating + compensatory behaviors (vomiting, laxatives, fasting, excessive exercise) at least weekly for 3 months.<br /></br>• Electrolyte abnormalities (↓K⁺, ↓Cl⁻) and metabolic alkalosis from vomiting → neuronal irritability → seizures.<br /></br>• Comorbidities: mood disorders, anxiety, substance use.  </p></br><p>Bupropion<br /></br>• Inhibits neuronal reuptake of norepinephrine and dopamine.<br /></br>• Stimulant effect → weight-neutral or slight loss, makes it attractive to weight-conscious patients but dangerous in eating disorders.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Algorithm  </p></br><ol></br><li>First-line for bulimia:<br /></br>   • Psychotherapy: cognitive-behavioral therapy (CBT).<br /></br>   • Pharmacotherapy: fluoxetine 60 mg/day (only FDA-approved drug).  </li></br><li>Avoid: bupropion, tramadol, MAOIs with tyramine risk (vomiting → labile BP).  </li></br><li>Obtain: baseline electrolytes, ECG if concern for prolonged QT, evaluate for suicidality.  </li></br></ol></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memory Tip  </p></br><p>“Bupi-BOOM” – Bupropion goes BOOM (seizure) in Bu-limia.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>B. Desipramine (TCA)<br /></br>• Lowers seizure threshold slightly and causes cardiotoxicity in overdose, but not an absolute contraindication; TCAs are generally second-/third-line due to side-effect burden, not a specific prohibition in BN.  </p></br><p>C. Fluoxetine (SSRI)<br /></br>• Preferred agent; decreases binge-purge cycles and treats depressive symptoms.  </p></br><p>D. Mirtazapine<br /></br>• Increases appetite/weight; can be useful in underweight depressed patients. No seizure contraindication.  </p></br><p>E. Venlafaxine (SNRI)<br /></br>• Can treat depression/anxiety; no specific ban in eating disorders, though monitor BP and withdrawal symptoms.  </p></br><p>Key discriminator: only bupropion carries a formal contraindication because of a markedly increased seizure risk in bulimia or anorexia.</p>
95. A previously healthy 77-year-old woman comes to the office because of a 4-month history of hoarseness. She has not had difficulty breathing or swallowing. She has had a chronic cough and gastroesophageal reflux disease for 5 years. Her only medication is omeprazole. She has smoked two packs of cigarettes daily for 60 years. Her temperature is 37.2°C (99.0°F), pulse is 76/min, respirations are 20/min, and blood pressure is 102/66 mm Hg. On examination, there is no cervical or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy. Oropharyngeal examination shows no abnormalities. The thyroid gland is not enlarged and moves with swallowing; no masses are palpated. Cardiopulmonary examination shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. Barium swallow</br>b. CT scan of the neck</br>c. Flexible laryngoscopy</br>d. Ultrasonography of the thyroid gland</br>e. Observation and follow-up examination in 2 months</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>C. Flexible laryngoscopy  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Key clues<br /></br>  – “Hoarseness” persisting 4 months (chronic = &gt;3–4 weeks).<br /></br>  – Very high-risk profile: 60-pack-year smoker, longstanding GERD, chronic cough.<br /></br>  – No red-flag airway symptoms (no stridor, dysphagia, respiratory distress), but malignancy risk is substantial.<br /></br>  – Physical exam of the oral cavity, thyroid, neck, and lymph nodes is unremarkable → the pathology is most likely located in the larynx itself and cannot be visualized externally.  </p></br><p>• Pattern/algorithm<br /></br>  1. Hoarseness &lt;2 weeks and obvious benign trigger (URI, voice overuse) → conservative care.<br /></br>  2. Hoarseness ≥3–4 weeks OR any red-flag (smoking, alcohol, neck mass, hemoptysis, weight loss, occupational exposure) → visualize the larynx with flexible (or fiber-optic) laryngoscopy.<br /></br>  3. Imaging (CT/MRI) is performed only after a lesion is seen to stage/plan treatment.  </p></br><p>Metacognitive tip: Any time hoarseness persists beyond the duration of a typical viral laryngitis (≈2 weeks) OR the stem mentions smoking, immediately think “look at the cords” with laryngoscopy.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (Why C is correct)<br /></br>Step-wise thought process:<br /></br>1. Chronic hoarseness = need to directly inspect vocal cords and laryngeal structures.<br /></br>2. Flexible laryngoscopy is quick, office-based, and visualizes mucosal lesions, nodules, polyps, paralysis, or early carcinoma.<br /></br>3. Finding dictates next steps (biopsy, CT for staging, etc.).  </p></br><p>Take-home: Direct visualization is the diagnostic cornerstone for chronic hoarseness.  </p></br><p>Documentation (real patient): duration, associated symptoms, risk factors (smoking, reflux), prior treatments, laryngoscopy findings, counseling about smoking cessation, and plan for biopsy/imaging if lesion found.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Laryngeal squamous cell carcinoma: Most common in smokers/alcohol users; presents with progressive hoarseness because tumor involves true vocal cords early.<br /></br>• Other causes of chronic hoarseness:<br /></br>  – Vocal cord nodules/polyps (voice overuse)<br /></br>  – Laryngopharyngeal reflux (acid irritation)<br /></br>  – Vocal cord paralysis (recurrent laryngeal nerve injury, malignancy, iatrogenic, viral)<br /></br>  – Neurologic disorders (Parkinson disease, myasthenia, ALS)<br /></br>Regardless of etiology, visualization is required to differentiate these entities.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Diagnostics &amp; Management Pearls<br /></br>First-line diagnostic test: Flexible fiber-optic laryngoscopy.<br /></br>If a mass is seen → biopsy (often during direct laryngoscopy under anesthesia) ± CT/MRI neck/chest for staging.<br /></br>If vocal cord paralysis without obvious mass → evaluate with CT from skull base to mediastinum to find nerve-compressing lesions.<br /></br>Conservative GERD management (PPI, lifestyle) is appropriate only after ruling out structural disease.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“Hoarse &gt; 3 weeks? Put the scope in the cheeks.”<br /></br>OR<br /></br>“SPEECH”: Smoking, Persistent (&gt;3 wk), Elderly, Alcohol, Cancer History → Scope.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>A. Barium swallow<br /></br>   • Used for dysphagia, esophageal motility, or Zenker diverticulum—not isolated hoarseness.  </p></br><p>B. CT scan of the neck<br /></br>   • Helpful for staging or when recurrent laryngeal nerve palsy is suspected with no visible lesion. Imaging before visualization risks missing subtle mucosal disease and delivers unnecessary radiation.  </p></br><p>D. Ultrasonography of the thyroid gland<br /></br>   • Evaluates thyroid nodules/goiters; thyroid compression of the recurrent laryngeal nerve would usually present with a palpable mass or tracheal deviation, neither of which is present.  </p></br><p>E. Observation and follow-up in 2 months<br /></br>   • Guideline-discordant; delays diagnosis of possible laryngeal cancer in a high-risk patient.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line<br /></br>Any patient with hoarseness persisting longer than 3–4 weeks—especially with smoking or other risk factors—requires prompt flexible laryngoscopy for direct inspection of the vocal cords and larynx.</p>
96. A 77-year-old man is brought to the clinic by his sister for a follow-up examination. The sister reports he was well until sustaining a head injury in a motor vehicle collision 7 months ago. She notes, "This changed him." During the past 3 months, he has been "slowing down" and having "urinary accidents." He has no other history of serious illness. His only medications are a multivitamin and ibuprofen as needed. He is a retired accountant. On neurologic examination, he has difficulty starting to walk from a sitting or standing position, a shuffling gait, and no arm swing bilaterally. Glabella and palmomental reflex testing is positive. On mental status examination, he has a flat affect and monotonous speech. Brain imaging is most likely to show which of the following in this patient?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Chronic subdural hematoma in the occipital lobe</br>b. Low T2 signal intensity in the globus pallidus</br>c. Multiple patches of demyelination</br>d. Prominent atrophy around the sylvian fissure</br>e. Widely dilated ventricles</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>E. Widely dilated ventricles  </p></br><p>━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key “buzz-phrases” and pertinent positives<br /></br>• 77-year-old with subacute (months) decline beginning after a head injury → known precipitant of communicating/normal-pressure hydrocephalus (NPH).<br /></br>• Gait: “difficulty starting to walk,” “shuffling,” “no arm swing” → classic magnetic, broad-based gait of NPH.<br /></br>• Urinary “accidents” → urge incontinence.<br /></br>• Cognitive/behavioral change: flat affect, monotonous speech, frontal release signs (glabellar, palmomental) → frontal-subcortical dysfunction typical of NPH.  </p></br><p>Pertinent negatives<br /></br>• No resting tremor, cogwheel rigidity, or pill-rolling (rules against idiopathic Parkinson disease).<br /></br>• No sensory level or leg spasticity (argues against cervical myelopathy).  </p></br><p>Framework to approach similar questions<br /></br>1. Elderly patient with the triad “Wet, Wobbly, Wacky.”<br /></br>2. Ask: Was there a preceding bleed, trauma, or meningitis?<br /></br>3. Imaging clue you must recall: ventricular enlargement out of proportion to cortical sulcal atrophy.  </p></br><p>━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Step 1 – Recognize the triad<br /></br>• Gait disturbance is usually earliest and most prominent.<br /></br>• Urinary urgency → incontinence.<br /></br>• Cognitive slowing/apathy rather than frank dementia at first.  </p></br><p>Step 2 – Connect to pathophysiology<br /></br>• Impaired CSF resorption at the arachnoid granulations after trauma or subarachnoid hemorrhage → chronically elevated CSF volume but NORMAL mean CSF opening pressure at lumbar puncture.<br /></br>• Ventriculomegaly stretches corona radiata white-matter pathways controlling gait and bladder.  </p></br><p>Step 3 – Expected imaging<br /></br>• CT/MRI: symmetrically enlarged lateral and third ventricles; sulci are NOT proportionally atrophic. Hence: “widely dilated ventricles.”  </p></br><p>Take-home core concept: The classic triad + ventriculomegaly out of proportion to cortical atrophy = Normal-Pressure Hydrocephalus.  </p></br><p>Documentation pearl for real practice<br /></br>• Document gait exam findings, urinary symptoms, and cognitive screen.<br /></br>• Record discussion about diagnostic large-volume lumbar tap or high-volume CSF drainage to predict shunt response.  </p></br><p>━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (high-yield)  </p></br><p>Etiology: Impaired CSF absorption (post-trauma, SAH, meningitis) → communicating hydrocephalus.<br /></br>Mechanism: Ventricular enlargement compresses periventricular white matter →<br /></br>• Frontal lobe symptoms (executive dysfunction, release reflexes)<br /></br>• Corticospinal urinary pathways → detrusor overactivity<br /></br>• Corticoreticulospinal tracts → gait initiation failure  </p></br><p>━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━<br /></br>Management Algorithm  </p></br><ol></br><li>MRI/CT to confirm ventriculomegaly.  </li></br><li>Large-volume lumbar tap (30–50 mL) or external lumbar drainage trial: transient improvement predicts benefit.  </li></br><li>Definitive therapy: Ventriculoperitoneal (VP) or lumboperitoneal shunt.  </li></br><li>Address reversible contributors (e.g., stop sedatives, PT for gait).  </li></br></ol></br><p>━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>Mnemonic: “Wet (urinary), Wobbly (gait), Wacky (cognition) after a Whack (head injury) → think hydrocephalus.”<br /></br>Visualization: Picture a water balloon (ventricle) pressing on the legs of a stick figure (gait) and bladder (wet), while dimming a light bulb over the head (cognition).  </p></br><p>━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>A. Chronic subdural hematoma in the occipital lobe<br /></br>• Would appear as crescentic extra-axial collection, usually produces focal neurologic deficits (hemiparesis, visual field loss) or fluctuating consciousness, not the classic triad.</p></br><p>B. Low T2 signal intensity in the globus pallidus<br /></br>• Seen in Wilson disease or manganese deposition (hepatic encephalopathy). Presents with dystonia, psychiatric disease, liver failure—does not match triad or age group.</p></br><p>C. Multiple patches of demyelination<br /></br>• MRI hallmark of multiple sclerosis. MS affects younger adults, produces relapsing focal neurologic deficits (optic neuritis, internuclear ophthalmoplegia), not gait-urinary-cognitive triad in the elderly.</p></br><p>D. Prominent atrophy around the sylvian fissure<br /></br>• Characteristic of frontotemporal dementia (Pick disease). Manifests with early personality/behavior change and language variants, but gait and urinary symptoms are late or absent.</p></br><p>E. Widely dilated ventricles (Correct)<br /></br>• Fits the clinical picture of NPH induced by prior head injury.</p></br><p>━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━<br /></br>Bottom Line: “Wet, Wobbly, and Wacky” in an elderly patient—especially after trauma or SAH—should immediately trigger the image of enlarged ventricles on CT/MRI and the diagnosis of normal-pressure hydrocephalus. Early recognition is crucial because surgical shunting can significantly improve gait and quality of life.</p>
97. A 25-year-old woman comes to a military outpatient clinic for an examination prior to a deployment to South Asia. She is in good health and feels well. She has had three urinary tract infections during the past year and is concerned about another one occurring during her deployment. Her only medication is an oral contraceptive. Vital signs are within normal limits. Examination shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most appropriate response to this patient's concern?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Begin prophylactic atovaquone-proguanil therapy against both malaria and urinary tract infections</br>b. Give the patient a prescription for nitrofurantoin and guidance about self-treatment of urinary tract infections</br>c. Recommend that the patient use a spermicide rather than an oral contraceptive to decrease her risk for a urinary tract infection</br>d. Tell the patient that an increased fluid intake, especially of cranberry juice, has been shown to prevent urinary tract infections during deployments</br>e. Tell the patient that women wearing underwear containing nylon or polyester rather than cotton has been associated with fewer urinary tract infections during deployment</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>b. Give the patient a prescription for nitrofurantoin and guidance about self-treatment of urinary tract infections</p></br><p>──────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues<br /></br>• 25-year-old healthy woman with a history of “three urinary tract infections during the past year” → meets the definition of recurrent uncomplicated cystitis (≥2 in 6 mo or ≥3 in 12 mo).<br /></br>• She is about to deploy to South Asia, where access to medical care will be limited.<br /></br>• She is asymptomatic today; the issue is prevention/management during travel.</p></br><p>How these clues narrow the differential<br /></br>• Recurrent cystitis in a young woman automatically raises two guideline-driven options: (1) continuous or post-coital antibiotic prophylaxis, or (2) patient-initiated (“self-start,” “self-diagnosis”) therapy with a short course of antibiotics to be taken at the first sign of cystitis.<br /></br>• In a setting where rapid evaluation may be difficult (military deployment), self-start therapy is strongly favored by both IDSA and ACOG guidelines.</p></br><p>Metacognitive framework<br /></br>When you see: “recurrent UTI + upcoming travel/remote area” → think “give patient a prescription and instructions for self-treatment.”</p></br><p>──────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Step-wise thought process<br /></br>1. Identify that the patient meets the definition of recurrent UTI.<br /></br>2. Recognize the special circumstance (deployment) that will limit access to care.<br /></br>3. Apply guideline: provide a short course of an appropriate antibiotic (nitrofurantoin 100 mg PO BID × 5 days or TMP-SMX 160/800 mg BID × 3 days) with education on how to self-diagnose and treat.<br /></br>4. No evidence suggests changing contraceptive method, clothing fabric, or drinking cranberry juice is equivalently effective.</p></br><p>Take-home message<br /></br>For women with recurrent uncomplicated cystitis traveling to areas with limited medical support, guideline-recommended best practice is to dispense a short-course antibiotic and teach self-treatment at onset of symptoms.</p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real-life)<br /></br>• Review of prior UTI history and culture data<br /></br>• Discussion of risks/benefits of prophylaxis vs self-start<br /></br>• Prescription written (nitrofurantoin 100 mg, #10)<br /></br>• Patient education provided on signs/symptoms and when to seek further care</p></br><p>──────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology &amp; Guideline Review  </p></br><p>• Recurrent cystitis usually results from periurethral colonization by uropathogenic E. coli.<br /></br>• Nitrofurantoin concentrates in urine and is bactericidal against most E. coli, with minimal collateral damage to gut flora and low resistance rates; it is Pregnancy Category B and compatible with oral contraceptives.<br /></br>• IDSA 2019: self-start therapy is appropriate for women who can reliably recognize symptoms and have a history of culture-proven infections.</p></br><p>──────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment Algorithm Snapshot  </p></br><p>Recurrent uncomplicated cystitis (non-pregnant woman)<br /></br>A. Behavioral measures (hydration, post-voiding, avoid spermicides)<br /></br>B. If episodes continue →<br /></br>   1. Patient-initiated therapy (first-line when medical access limited)<br /></br>   2. Continuous daily prophylaxis (e.g., nitrofurantoin 50–100 mg HS)<br /></br>   3. Post-coital prophylaxis (if temporally related to intercourse)</p></br><p>Diagnostic tests not needed today because patient is asymptomatic.</p></br><p>──────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“Rule of 3’s” for recurrent cystitis:<br /></br>• ≥3 UTIs/yr OR ≥2 in 6 mo → consider prophylaxis or self-start.<br /></br>Travel tip mnemonic: “Pack NITRO for the trip.” (NITROfurantoin for deployment/travel self-treatment)</p></br><p>──────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>a. Atovaquone-proguanil prophylaxis<br /></br>• Indicated for malaria prevention, has no activity against uropathogens; unnecessary polypharmacy and expense.</p></br><p>c. Switching to a spermicide<br /></br>• Spermicides (esp. nonoxynol-9) actually INCREASE UTI risk by altering vaginal flora; opposite of what patient needs.</p></br><p>d. Increased fluids/cranberry juice proven preventive<br /></br>• Hydration is benign but evidence that cranberry products prevent UTIs is inconsistent and weak; not guideline-endorsed as a sole strategy, especially in high-risk travelers.</p></br><p>e. Nylon/polyester underwear decreases UTI risk<br /></br>• Breathable cotton is preferred; synthetic fabrics can trap moisture and may increase, not decrease, colonization.</p></br><p>──────────────────────<br /></br>Core Takeaway<br /></br>For a woman with recurrent uncomplicated UTIs who is traveling or deploying, the most evidence-based, practical approach is to give her a prescription (e.g., nitrofurantoin) and clear instructions for self-diagnosis and self-treatment at symptom onset.</p>
98. A 52-year-old man with HIV infection comes to the office because of a 3-month history of increasingly severe pain of his knees. He says he has 20 minutes of stiffness and increased pain after he awakens in the morning or sits for long periods. He has not had redness, swelling, or warmth. He has no history of trauma to the knees. Acetaminophen provides moderate relief. Other medications are antiretroviral therapy and vitamin D supplementation. He says he does not want to take any more pills. He is 180 cm (5 ft 11 in) tall and weighs 100 kg (220 lb); BMI is 31 kg/m2. Vital signs are within normal limits. Cardiopulmonary examination shows no abnormalities. Examination of the knees shows crepitus and small effusions; there is no erythema, warmth, or decreased range of motion. Lachman and McMurray tests show no abnormalities. X-rays of the knees show decreased joint space and osteophyte formation. The patient begins a low-impact exercise regimen. Two months later, he returns to the office for follow-up examination. He has had no progression of his symptoms and a 4.5-kg (10-lb) weight loss. He continues to take acetaminophen and has had no adverse effects. The patient asks if he will eventually develop severe osteoarthritis and wants to discuss potential treatment options. Which of the following is the most appropriate recommendation for this patient?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Continued oral acetaminophen therapy</br>b. Intra-articular hyaluronic acid therapy</br>c. Intra-articular triamcinolone therapy</br>d. Oral glucosamine and chondroitin therapy</br>e. Total knee replacement</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>a. Continued oral acetaminophen therapy  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Key clues: 52-year-old, chronic gradually worsening knee pain; &lt;30-min morning stiffness; pain worse after inactivity; crepitus, small effusion, no warmth/erythema; x-ray shows joint-space narrowing + osteophytes. Classic pattern of osteoarthritis (OA).<br /></br>• Pertinent positives: mechanical pain pattern, obesity (BMI 31), radiographic OA changes.<br /></br>• Pertinent negatives: no inflammatory signs, normal Lachman/McMurray → rules out inflammatory arthritis or internal derangement.<br /></br>• Course: After 2 mo of guideline-directed non-pharmacologic care (low-impact exercise, 10-lb weight loss) plus acetaminophen, his pain is stable and tolerable.  </p></br><p>Metacognitive approach:<br /></br>1. Identify that the question asks for “most appropriate recommendation” NOW, not ultimate therapy.<br /></br>2. Apply OA management ladder:<br /></br>   a. Non-drug (exercise, weight loss, PT)<br /></br>   b. First-line drug for mild pain = acetaminophen (≤3 g/d unless liver dz)<br /></br>   c. Escalate only if inadequate relief or functional impairment.</p></br><p>Since he is doing well, remain on the current step.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Why answer A?<br /></br>• Symptoms are controlled (“no progression,” moderate relief).<br /></br>• No adverse effects.<br /></br>• He explicitly “does not want to take any more pills,” but is willing to keep current single agent.<br /></br>• Guidelines (AAOS, ACR): continue non-pharm + acetaminophen if adequate. Escalate only when pain limits function or acetaminophen fails.<br /></br>Take-home: Do not jump to invasive or costly treatments when first-line therapy is effective. Document continued acetaminophen use, counsel on max daily dose, reinforce exercise/weight loss.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (high-yield)<br /></br>• OA = progressive articular cartilage degeneration + subchondral bone changes.<br /></br>• Risk factors: age, obesity, prior trauma, metabolic disorders.<br /></br>• Pain originates from subchondral bone &amp; synovium, not cartilage itself. Morning stiffness &lt;30 min distinguishes OA from inflammatory arthritides.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Algorithm<br /></br>1. Non-pharm: weight loss, aerobic/strengthening exercise, PT, assistive devices.<br /></br>2. Mild pain: Acetaminophen up to 3 g/d (4 g if no liver disease).<br /></br>3. Moderate/severe or acetaminophen failure: Topical NSAIDs → oral NSAIDs (evaluate GI/CV risk).<br /></br>4. Short-term flares: intra-articular corticosteroid (e.g., triamcinolone).<br /></br>5. Refractory: viscosupplement (hyaluronic acid) – limited evidence.<br /></br>6. End-stage disease with functional limitation: total knee arthroplasty.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“OA CARE”:<br /></br>O – Obesity reduction<br /></br>A – Acetaminophen first<br /></br>C – COX inhibitors (NSAIDs) if A inadequate<br /></br>A – Acute flare → steroid injection<br /></br>R – Rooster comb (hyaluronic) questionable<br /></br>E – End-stage → arthroplasty</p></br><p>Visualize a ladder labeled OA; don’t climb to the next rung until the current rung breaks.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>b. Intra-articular hyaluronic acid: Considered after failure of conservative &amp; NSAID therapy; results mixed, costly, invasive. Not needed when acetaminophen adequate.<br /></br>c. Intra-articular triamcinolone: Good for acute flare with joint effusion or severe pain; relief lasts weeks, not indicated for stable mild symptoms.<br /></br>d. Oral glucosamine/chondroitin: Large RCTs show no meaningful benefit; most guidelines advise against routine use.<br /></br>e. Total knee replacement: Reserved for severe OA with radiographic destruction AND debilitating pain refractory to all conservative measures; premature in a patient functioning well on acetaminophen.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line<br /></br>The patient’s OA pain is currently well-controlled with non-pharmacologic measures and acetaminophen. Continue the same regimen and reassess periodically; escalate only if pain or functional status worsens.</p>
99. A 30-year-old man with a 7-year history of Crohn disease is admitted to the hospital because of a 2-month history of intermittent temperatures to 38.9°C (102.0°F), nonproductive cough, night sweats, and fatigue. He has not had diarrhea, or blood or mucus in his stools. His Crohn disease symptoms have been well controlled with subcutaneous adalimumab for the past year. Chest x-ray 2 weeks ago showed lingular and right lower lobe infiltrates, and he was treated with successive courses of levofloxacin and azithromycin for presumed community-acquired pneumonia without improvement of his symptoms. Medical history otherwise is unremarkable, and his only other routine medication is a daily multivitamin. He does not smoke cigarettes or drink alcoholic beverages. He has no pets. Three months ago, he spent 1 week racing dune buggies in the Southwestern USA, but he has not traveled outside of the country. He is 178 cm (5 ft 10 in) tall and weighs 72 kg (160 lb); BMI is 23 kg/m². Temperature is 38.5°C (101.3°F), pulse is 105/min and regular, respirations are 20/min, and blood pressure is 112/68 mm Hg. Crackles are heard in both lower lung fields. Results of fiberoptic bronchoscopy with bronchoalveolar lavage are shown. Which of the following is the most appropriate pharmacotherapy?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Doxycycline</br>b. Fluconazole</br>c. Isoniazid, pyrazinamide, ethambutol, and rifampin</br>d. Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole</br>e. Vancomycin</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>B. Fluconazole  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>1.
