Key clues<br /></br>   • Subacute “pulmonary–constitutional” picture: 2 months of fevers to 38.9 °C, night sweats, cough, fatigue.<br /></br>   • Failed two appropriate outpatient pneumonia regimens (levofloxacin, then azithromycin) → think “not routine CAP.”<br /></br>   • Immunosuppression: on adalimumab (anti-TNF-α) for Crohn disease → greatly increases risk of certain fungi and mycobacteria.<br /></br>   • Geographic exposure: 1-week trip to the Southwestern USA desert.<br /></br>   • BAL cytology/culture (image/text in stem) shows thick-walled, double-contoured spherules packed with endospores → pathognomonic for Coccidioides spp. (“cocci spherule = soccer ball full of spores”).  </p></br><ol></br><li>Pertinent positives/negatives<br /></br>   • No diarrhea → flare of Crohn unlikely.<br /></br>   • No pet exposure, no foreign travel → brucellosis, histoplasmosis (caves/birds), etc., less likely.<br /></br>   • Lung infiltrates localized to lower lobes and lingula—not upper-lobe cavitary TB pattern.<br /></br>   • Immunosuppression without HIV/AIDS makes Pneumocystis (usually diffuse bilateral interstitial pattern) less likely.  </li></br></ol></br><p>Metacognitive tip: On NBME/Step 2, combine (a) time course ≥2 weeks, (b) failed routine antibiotics, (c) travel/residence clue, and (d) immunocompromise → think endemic mycoses or TB. Then let the geographic key clinch the organism.  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Step 1: Recognize endemic mycosis risk (Southwest desert → “Valley fever” = coccidioidomycosis).<br /></br>Step 2: Confirm with classic lab finding (BAL spherules with endospores).<br /></br>Step 3: Select therapy.  For mild-to-moderate pulmonary or disseminated disease in an immunocompromised host, an oral azole (fluconazole or itraconazole) is first-line. Amphotericin B is reserved for severe, rapidly progressive, or CNS disease.  </p></br><p>Core takeaway: In patients on anti-TNF agents who develop a subacute pulmonary syndrome after travel/residence in the Southwest, think Coccidioides and treat with an oral azole.  </p></br><p>Real-life documentation: “30-y-o male on adalimumab with subacute febrile pulmonary illness; BAL positive for Coccidioides spherules. Plan: start oral fluconazole 400 mg daily, monitor LFTs, repeat chest imaging in 4–6 weeks.”  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Coccidioides immitis/posadasii is a dimorphic fungus endemic to the arid Southwest (central/southern California, Arizona, New Mexico, west Texas). Arthroconidia in soil become airborne → inhaled → transform in lungs into spherules filled with endospores.<br /></br>• TNF-α is critical for granuloma maintenance; blockade (adalimumab, infliximab, etanercept) predisposes to reactivation or new infection with intracellular pathogens and fungi.<br /></br>• Clinical spectrum: asymptomatic, self-limited “Valley fever,” chronic pneumonia, or disseminated disease (skin, bone, meninges).  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Management Snapshot<br /></br>First-line:<br /></br>• Fluconazole 400 mg PO daily (or itraconazole) for 3–12 mo; longer for immunosuppressed.<br /></br>Second-line / severe:<br /></br>• Liposomal amphotericin B IV.<br /></br>Monitor: LFTs, symptom resolution, imaging. Consider lifelong suppressive therapy if immunosuppression cannot be discontinued.  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“DESERT F.L.U.”<br /></br>D – Desert southwest exposure<br /></br>E – Endospores in SpherulEs (picture)<br /></br>S – Subacute Sweats<br /></br>E – azolE therapy (fluconazolE)<br /></br>R – Rains (dust storms) raise spores<br /></br>T – TNF-blocker risk<br /></br>FLU – treat with FLU-conazole  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>A. Doxycycline<br /></br>   • Covers atypical CAP (Mycoplasma, Chlamydia) and tick-borne infections. Would usually improve on outpatient therapy; BAL would not show spherules.  </p></br><p>C. RIPE (isoniazid, pyrazinamide, ethambutol, rifampin)<br /></br>   • For tuberculosis. Expect upper-lobe cavitation, caseating granulomas, positive AFB smear/culture, travel to endemic TB regions or high-risk contacts. Not consistent with Southwest travel + spherules.  </p></br><p>D. Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole<br /></br>   • Drug of choice for Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia (PJP). PJP presents with diffuse bilateral ground-glass infiltrates, pronounced hypoxemia, and foamy silver-stained cysts on BAL, typically in HIV (CD4 &lt; 200) or prolonged high-dose steroids. Not the finding here.  </p></br><p>E. Vancomycin<br /></br>   • Used for MRSA and other gram-positive cocci. Bacterial pneumonias improve with broad-spectrum antibiotics unless there is resistance; no gram-positive cocci on BAL, and spherules are fungal, not bacterial.  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line<br /></br>Endemic fungi must be high on the differential in any patient with subacute pneumonia that fails outpatient therapy—especially if they are immunosuppressed and have traveled to an endemic area. Southwest desert exposure + spherules on BAL = coccidioidomycosis → treat with an oral azole such as fluconazole.</p>
100. An orthopaedic surgeon has completed his first surgical procedure of the day in an operating room (OR) where he has booked five cases in sequence for the day. After the first patient is taken to the recovery room, the surgeon visits the next patient in the OR holding area at 9:25 AM in anticipation of the start of the operation before 10 AM. The patient is a 25-year-old woman with a closed comminuted fracture of the humerus that will be repaired with open reduction and internal fixation using metal plates and screws. She is 168 cm (5 ft 6 in) tall and weighs 64 kg (142 lb); BMI is 23 kg/m2. The surgeon orders prophylactic intravenous administration of 1 g of cefazolin, which is completed at 9:30 AM. The OR is suddenly commandeered for an emergency case. No other OR is open, so the humeral operation is postponed. The patient eventually enters the OR at 11:30 AM. The Surgical Care Improvement Project guidelines, which are a core measure by the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services, recommend that intravenous antibiotic therapy be administered within 1 hour prior to the first surgical incision. Which of the following is the most appropriate management of this patient prior to proceeding with the operation?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Administer another 1-g dose of cefazolin now  </br>b. Administer an equivalent dose of gentamicin now  </br>c. Delay the procedure until 3:30 PM and administer another 1-g dose of cefazolin  </br>d. Wait until the next day to proceed with administering additional antibiotic prophylaxis</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer</br>A. Administer another 1-g dose of cefazolin now</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis</br>• Time stamps are the key clues.<br /></br>  – First dose finished: 09:30.<br /></br>  – Patient rolls into OR: 11:30. Incision will be shortly thereafter (&gt; 2 h after first dose).<br /></br>• Surgical Care Improvement Project (SCIP) core measure: the pre-incision antibiotic must be completed within 60 min (within 120 min for long-infusing drugs such as vancomycin or fluoroquinolones) of the skin incision.<br /></br>• Orthopaedic hardware implantation = “clean–contaminated” procedure with a high risk of catastrophic infection → cefazolin is the recommended first-line agent.<br /></br>• Therefore the original dose is now “out of window” and must be repeated before cutting.</p></br><p>Metacognitive tip<br /></br>Always write the start/finish time of prophylactic antibiotics on the dry-erase board or your notes. If &gt; 60 min will elapse before incision, plan to redose.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (step-wise)</br>1. Identify the guideline: give IV cefazolin ≤ 60 min before incision.<br /></br>2. Compare guideline with actual timeline: 120 min have elapsed.<br /></br>3. Solution: give the same drug again immediately so the infusion finishes &lt; 1 h pre-incision.<br /></br>4. No need to delay surgery; simply restore guideline compliance.</p></br><p>Take-home message<br /></br>Pre-incision prophylaxis must be within one hour for most antibiotics; redose if schedule slips or if the operation is prolonged (&gt; 4 h) or there is massive blood loss (&gt; 1.5 L).</p></br><p>Documentation pearl<br /></br>Chart the exact clock time the repeat dose is started/finished; this is a publicly reported quality metric tied to reimbursement.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review</br>• Post-operative infections after fixation can lead to chronic osteomyelitis, non-union, hardware removal.<br /></br>• Staphylococcus aureus (MSSA) is the most common pathogen → first-generation cephalosporin (cefazolin) is preferred.<br /></br>• Cefazolin half-life ≈ 1.8 h; therapeutic tissue levels fall after ~3–4 h. Redosing keeps concentrations above the minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) during incision and early wound healing.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Test Review</br>• Standard adult dose: cefazolin 2 g if weight &lt; 120 kg, 3 g if ≥ 120 kg; some centers still use 1 g for smaller patients, as in this vignette.<br /></br>• Redosing guidelines<br /></br>  – Delay &gt; 1 h past completion of the first dose and before incision.<br /></br>  – Procedure lasts &gt; 4 h or blood loss &gt; 1.5 L.<br /></br>• Alternatives (if β-lactam allergy): clindamycin 900 mg or vancomycin 15 mg/kg (infuse over ≥ 1 h, so start 2 h before incision).</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips</br>“Time to CUT = Time to RE-DOSE”<br /></br>CUT:<br /></br>C – Clock &gt; 60 min since prophylaxis finished<br /></br>U – Unanticipated delay or prolonged surgery<br /></br>T – Transfusion/bleeding &gt; 1.5 L<br /></br>Any one → redose.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Incorrect Answer Analysis</br>B. Gentamicin now<br /></br>• Aminoglycosides are not first-line for routine orthopaedic prophylaxis; used only as an adjunct for Gram-negative coverage in selected contaminated cases. Gentamicin also requires weight-based dosing and therapeutic drug monitoring. Does not satisfy SCIP metric for a clean orthopaedic case.</p></br><p>C. Delay until 3:30 PM and give cefazolin<br /></br>• Unnecessary postponement; guideline requires antibiotic ≤ 60 min before incision, not a fixed delay. Adds 4 h of needless fasting/anxiety and disrupts OR efficiency.</p></br><p>D. Wait until the next day to proceed with additional prophylaxis<br /></br>• No clinical justification and would worsen fracture management outcomes; violates both quality metrics and patient care standards.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Bottom Line</br>If &gt; 60 min have elapsed between completion of cefazolin infusion and expected incision time, administer another full dose immediately and proceed with surgery.</p>
101. An 18-year-old man is brought to the emergency department 10 minutes after sustaining a gunshot wound to the abdomen. On arrival, his pulse is 120/min, respirations are 16/min, and blood pressure is 90/60 mm Hg. Examination shows an entrance wound in the right lower quadrant of the abdomen; no exit wound is identified. The abdomen is diffusely tender with voluntary guarding. Bowel sounds are hypoactive. After fluid resuscitation, which of the following is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Ultrasonography of the abdomen</br>b. CT scan of the abdomen</br>c. Laparoscopy</br>d. Peritoneal lavage</br>e. Laparotomy</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>E. Exploratory laparotomy  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues<br /></br>• “Gunshot wound to the abdomen” → penetrating abdominal trauma.<br /></br>• “No exit wound” → missile may still be in abdomen; high likelihood of visceral/vascular injury.<br /></br>• Vital signs: pulse 120/min, BP 90/60 mm Hg → class III hemorrhagic shock; hemodynamically unstable.<br /></br>• “Abdomen is diffusely tender with voluntary guarding, bowel sounds hypoactive” → clear peritoneal irritation ⇒ visceral perforation or intra-abdominal bleeding.  </p></br><p>Pertinent positives = penetrating mechanism + instability + peritonitis.<br /></br>Pertinent negatives = none of the stabilizing findings (no normal vitals, no benign abdominal exam).  </p></br><p>Cognitive framework<br /></br>1. Penetrating abdominal trauma algorithm:<br /></br>   a. Hemodynamic status<br /></br>   b. Signs of peritonitis/evisceration<br /></br>   c. Need for immediate operative management vs. imaging.<br /></br>2. Any ONE of the following mandates immediate laparotomy:<br /></br>   • Hemodynamic instability<br /></br>   • Peritonitis/guarding/rebound<br /></br>   • Gunshot wound traversing peritoneal cavity<br /></br>   • Impalement/evisceration  </p></br><p>All are present here, so skip imaging and go straight to the OR. Exploratory laparotomy is both diagnostic and therapeutic.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Step-wise thought process:<br /></br>1. Is the patient stable enough for CT? No (tachycardic, hypotensive).<br /></br>2. Does the abdominal exam show peritoneal signs? Yes (diffuse tenderness, guarding).<br /></br>3. Therefore, further ED diagnostics (FAST, DPL) add no value and could delay life-saving surgery.<br /></br>4. Proceed directly to exploratory laparotomy, which will identify injuries (diagnostic) and allow repair (therapeutic).  </p></br><p>Take-home point: In penetrating abdominal trauma, the presence of hemodynamic instability OR peritonitis overrides all other considerations → immediate laparotomy.  </p></br><p>Documentation pearl: record vitals, mechanism, exam confirming peritonitis, resuscitation given, decision for emergent operative exploration with surgical consent (if possible).  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (high-yield)  </p></br><p>• Gunshot wounds produce cavitation and unpredictable trajectories; small-caliber bullets can ricochet off bones or fascia causing multi-visceral injury.<br /></br>• Peritonitis from hollow-viscus perforation releases GI contents, causing chemical then bacterial peritonitis → guarding/rebound.<br /></br>• Hypotension/tachycardia reflect acute blood loss (solid-organ or mesenteric vessel injury).  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Management &amp; Diagnostic Tests  </p></br><p>Hierarchy in penetrating trauma:<br /></br>1. ABCs and fluid/blood resuscitation.<br /></br>2. If unstable or peritonitic → straight to OR (exploratory laparotomy).<br /></br>3. If stable + no peritonitis:<br /></br>   • FAST → quick screen for hemoperitoneum.<br /></br>   • CT with IV contrast for precise mapping of injury.<br /></br>   • Selective non-operative management possible in stab wounds; rarely in GSWs.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“Penetrating P.I.T” (Peritonitis, Instability, Thoracoabdominal GSW) = Go to the P.I.T (OR)!<br /></br>P – Peritonitis<br /></br>I – Instability (SBP &lt;100, HR &gt;100)<br /></br>T – Thoracoabdominal gunshot or impalement  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>A. FAST/Ultrasonography – Useful in unstable BLUNT trauma or stable penetrating injuries without peritonitis. Here, exam already tells you there’s an acute surgical abdomen; ultrasound would delay care and may miss hollow-viscus injury.  </p></br><p>B. CT abdomen – Requires hemodynamic stability and absence of peritonitis to justify transport to scanner; not safe in this patient.  </p></br><p>C. Laparoscopy – Minimally invasive option for stable patients with equivocal findings; insufflation can worsen hypotension and doesn’t allow rapid vascular control.  </p></br><p>D. Diagnostic peritoneal lavage (DPL) – Historical test for bleeding; positive result would still send patient to OR. Since the patient already meets operative criteria, DPL is redundant.  </p></br><p>E. Exploratory laparotomy – Correct: immediately identifies and controls bleeding/perforation in unstable, peritonitic penetrating abdominal trauma.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line  </p></br><p>Penetrating abdominal trauma + hemodynamic instability or peritoneal signs = emergent exploratory laparotomy—no additional ED imaging required.</p>
102. A 5-year-old boy is brought to the physician for a well-child examination. His mother says that he plays outside frequently, and she has been having trouble keeping him away from the poison ivy in their backyard. Examination shows no abnormalities. His mother asks what can be done to prevent a rash after exposure to poison ivy. Which of the following is the most appropriate recommendation after exposure?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Bathing with soap</br>b. Topical antihistamine therapy</br>c. Topical corticosteroid therapy</br>d. Oral corticosteroid therapy</br>e. No further recommendations are indicated</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>A. Bathing with soap (immediately after contact)</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis</br>• Key phrases: “trouble keeping him away from the poison ivy,” “no abnormalities” (ie, no current rash), “what can be done to prevent a rash after exposure?”<br /></br>• Task clarification: The mother is asking for post-exposure prophylaxis, not treatment of an already-present dermatitis.<br /></br>• Clinical pattern: Rhus (poison ivy/oak/sumac) allergic contact dermatitis is triggered when urushiol resin remains on the skin long enough to penetrate, bind epidermal proteins, and initiate a delayed (type IV) hypersensitivity reaction. Removing the resin before it is absorbed prevents the dermatitis.</p></br><p>Metacognitive tip: When a question stem specifies “after exposure” and the physical exam shows “no abnormalities,” think decontamination first; reserve pharmacologic therapy for established or unavoidable disease.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning</br>1. Identify the inciting agent (urushiol).<br /></br>2. Recall pathophysiology: penetration + protein binding → T-cell–mediated reaction in 12-48 h.<br /></br>3. Immediate priority: physically remove the lipid-soluble resin within minutes to hours.<br /></br>4. Best method: copious washing with soap (especially detergent or dish soap) and cool running water; ideally within 10–15 min but still worthwhile for several hours.</p></br><p>Core takeaway: Early washing with soap and water is the single most effective, evidence-based maneuver to prevent poison ivy dermatitis after contact.</p></br><p>Documentation (real patient): “Counseled parent to wash exposed skin with soap and water as soon as possible after suspected poison ivy contact; reviewed signs of dermatitis and when to seek care.”</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review</br>• Etiology: Urushiol oleoresin found on leaves/stems/roots of Toxicodendron spp.<br /></br>• Mechanism: Urushiol penetrates stratum corneum → conjugates with skin proteins → neo-antigen → sensitized memory T cells → eczematous, pruritic rash.<br /></br>• Time course: Sensitized individuals develop rash 12–48 h after exposure; naïve patients may take up to 7 days on first exposure.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Pearls</br>Pre-rash (prophylaxis):<br /></br>• Immediate washing with soap, detergent, or rubbing alcohol.<br /></br>• Removal of contaminated clothing, cleaning of tools/pets.</p></br><p>Established rash:<br /></br>• Mild/moderate, localized: high-potency topical corticosteroid (e.g., clobetasol 0.05%).<br /></br>• Severe or widespread (face/genitals/hands &gt;20% TBSA): 14–21-day taper of oral prednisone.<br /></br>• Symptomatic relief: oral antihistamines for pruritus (do NOT prevent or treat dermatitis itself).</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips</br>“LAWN” for poison ivy care after exposure:<br /></br>L – Laundry (clothes washed hot)<br /></br>A – Alcohol wipe or soap ASAP<br /></br>W – Water rinse for ≥2 min<br /></br>N – Nails scrubbed (urushiol hides under nails)</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis</br>B. Topical antihistamine therapy<br /></br>• Diphenhydramine cream may relieve itching but does NOT prevent T-cell activation and can itself cause contact dermatitis. Only useful after rash develops, not prophylaxis.</p></br><p>C. Topical corticosteroid therapy<br /></br>• Effective treatment once dermatitis appears, but applying before rash can’t remove the antigen. It does nothing if urushiol is still on the skin.</p></br><p>D. Oral corticosteroid therapy<br /></br>• Reserved for severe or extensive dermatitis. Unnecessary, potentially harmful (growth suppression, mood changes) as prophylaxis.</p></br><p>E. No further recommendations are indicated<br /></br>• Fails to address a modifiable risk. Simple washing markedly decreases incidence and severity—standard counseling point in pediatrics and family medicine.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>High-Yield Take-Home Message</br>After suspected poison ivy contact, the first—and usually only—step needed to prevent dermatitis is prompt washing with soap and water to remove urushiol resin before it penetrates the skin.</p>
103. Six hours after beginning dicloxacillin therapy for cellulitis of the right lower extremity, a 37-year-old woman comes to the emergency department because of a 3-hour history of pruritic rash, dyspnea, and mild cough. She has a history of childhood asthma but no history of adverse reactions to medication. Six months ago, she had streptococcal pharyngitis treated with penicillin. She has been otherwise healthy. Her temperature is 37.1°C (98.8°F), pulse is 110/min, respirations are 22/min, and blood pressure is 86/64 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on room air shows an oxygen saturation of 92 percent. Examination of the skin shows 1- to 2-cm areas of pink urticaria over the trunk and upper and lower extremities and a 3-cm, erythematous, mildly tender area over the distal right lower extremity. Air movement is poor, and inspiratory and expiratory wheezes are heard on auscultation. Her hemoglobin concentration is 13.1 g/dL, and leukocyte count is 9500/mm3. A chest x-ray shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is most likely to prevent recurrence of this patient’s symptoms?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Avoidance of β-lactam antibiotics</br>b. Diphenhydramine prophylaxis</br>c. Prednisone prophylaxis for 4 weeks</br>d. Self-injectable epinephrine</br>e. Stanozolol prophylaxis daily</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>a. Avoidance of β-lactam antibiotics  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Timing: Symptoms began 3 h after taking dicloxacillin and progressed rapidly—classic for an IgE-mediated (type I) immediate hypersensitivity reaction.<br /></br>• Clinical triad of anaphylaxis:<br /></br>  – Skin: diffuse, pruritic urticaria<br /></br>  – Respiratory: wheezing, dyspnea, cough, ↓air movement<br /></br>  – Cardiovascular: hypotension (BP 86/64 mm Hg)<br /></br>• Prior exposure: She received penicillin 6 mo ago → sensitization and IgE production against β-lactam determinants.<br /></br>Key takeaway: Second exposure to a β-lactam triggered mast-cell degranulation with systemic release of histamine and leukotrienes.</p></br><p>Metacognitive tip: When you see “urticaria + wheeze + hypotension” after a drug exposure, automatically think anaphylaxis → identify the antigen and eliminate future exposure.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Step-wise approach:<br /></br>1. Recognize anaphylaxis.<br /></br>2. Ask: what is the inciting antigen? → the β-lactam ring shared by penicillin and dicloxacillin.<br /></br>3. Prevention revolves around eliminating that antigen.<br /></br>Thus, the best way to PREVENT recurrence is complete avoidance of β-lactam antibiotics (penicillins, most cephalosporins, carbapenems). If a β-lactam is absolutely required, penicillin skin-testing or desensitization can be considered, but the preventive cornerstone is avoidance.</p></br><p>Core concept: IgE-mediated drug allergy = avoid the culprit drug class.</p></br><p>Documentation pearl: Clearly label the chart “Anaphylaxis to β-lactams—avoid penicillins/cephalosporins/carbapenems; consider aztreonam if gram-negative coverage needed.”</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>1. Sensitization phase: First β-lactam exposure → drug (hapten) binds host proteins → processed by APCs → TH2 response → class-switch to IgE specific to penicilloyl determinant.<br /></br>2. Re-exposure: Drug cross-links IgE on mast cells/basophils → release of histamine, tryptase, prostaglandins, leukotrienes.<br /></br>3. Clinical effects:<br /></br>   – Histamine → vasodilation, ↑vascular permeability → hypotension, urticaria<br /></br>   – Leukotrienes → bronchospasm, wheezing<br /></br>   – Overall systemic reaction = anaphylaxis.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment / Diagnostic Pearls<br /></br>Acute management (already done/implicit): IM epinephrine, airway support, IV fluids, H1/H2 blockers, corticosteroids.<br /></br>Prevention:<br /></br>• Strict avoidance of β-lactams.<br /></br>• If necessary, use non–β-lactam alternatives (e.g., macrolides, clindamycin, doxycycline, fluoroquinolones) or aztreonam (minimal cross-reactivity except with ceftazidime).<br /></br>• Penicillin skin testing can stratify risk if a β-lactam is the best/only therapy.<br /></br>• Desensitization is reserved for situations where no alternative exists (e.g., neurosyphilis in pregnancy).</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“Penicillin allergy? Be-lactam B-gone.”<br /></br>ABC of anaphylaxis:<br /></br>A – Avoid antigen for prevention<br /></br>B – Be ready with epinephrine for treatment<br /></br>C – Chart the allergy clearly</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Options Are Wrong<br /></br>b. Diphenhydramine prophylaxis – Antihistamines can blunt urticaria but do NOT prevent mast-cell degranulation or life-threatening bronchospasm/hypotension.<br /></br>c. Prednisone prophylaxis – Steroids act hours later; they do not prevent immediate IgE reactions and are not practical for lifelong use with every antibiotic course.<br /></br>d. Self-injectable epinephrine – Epinephrine is life-saving for treatment, not prevention. The allergen exposure would still provoke anaphylaxis.<br /></br>e. Stanozolol – An androgenic steroid with no role in allergic disease; included as a distractor.</p></br><p>Bottom line: The only reliable way to PREVENT another anaphylactic episode is to eliminate future exposure to the responsible β-lactam antibiotics.</p>
104. A 27-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department by her parents because of a 3-day history of auditory hallucinations. One week ago, her husband was convicted of a drug-dealing charge. At that time, she learned that she and her 16-month-old son may be at risk for violence from former associates of her husband. After leaving the courthouse, she remembers hearing a voice telling her to jump in front of a red car. Since that time, she has heard other voices; they appear more often when she is under stress. She says the voices are telling her that she is in danger and that she should hide. She has no history of psychiatric illness. She does not drink alcohol or use illicit drugs. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. On mental status examination, she is calm and cooperative but seems mildly suspicious of the physician. Her thought process is organized. She becomes tearful when questioned about her husband. She says she does not want to be hospitalized because her baby needs her. She is worried that the hospital may try to take him away. She says that she hears frightening voices but that they have not told her to harm herself again since the first day she experienced them. Her parents are also worried about the patient being separated from her son and say they can plan for someone to be with her at all times. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Cognitive behavioral therapy  </br>b. Lithium carbonate therapy  </br>c. Lorazepam therapy  </br>d. Risperidone therapy  </br>e. Sertraline therapy</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>D. Risperidone therapy  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Key findings<br /></br>  – Sudden‐onset auditory hallucinations (“voices telling her to jump in front of a red car,” “voices telling her she is in danger”)<br /></br>  – Duration 3 days (i.e., &gt; 24 h but &lt; 1 month)<br /></br>  – Clear temporal association with an overwhelming stressor (husband’s conviction, threats of violence).<br /></br>  – No prior psych history, no substance use, organized thought process, no sustained mood episode, no current suicidal or homicidal intent, no danger to baby; family can provide 24-h supervision.<br /></br>• Differential narrows to stress-related psychotic conditions:<br /></br>  – Acute stress disorder → primarily dissociative &amp; intrusive symptoms, not true psychosis.<br /></br>  – Brief psychotic disorder → ≥ 1 psychotic symptom (hallucinations, delusions, disorganized speech/behavior) lasting 1 day–1 month, often precipitated by stress, with return to baseline.<br /></br>• Pattern recognition algorithm<br /></br>  – Acute psychosis + sudden stressor + &lt; 1 month ⇒ Brief psychotic disorder ⇒ treat acutely with an antipsychotic.  </p></br><p>Cognitive &amp; Metacognitive Approach<br /></br>1. Identify psychotic symptoms (hallucinations/delusions).<br /></br>2. Time-course: &lt; 1 month? If yes, brief psychotic disorder.<br /></br>3. R/O substance or medical cause (negatives in vignette).<br /></br>4. Decide on safety: no imminent risk, family supervision adequate → outpatient possible.<br /></br>5. Initiate antipsychotic for symptom control.  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>• Core takeaway: The NBME frequently tests that new-onset psychosis triggered by a stressor and lasting &lt; 1 month = brief psychotic disorder, first-line treatment is an antipsychotic (second-generation preferred, e.g., risperidone, olanzapine, quetiapine).<br /></br>• Documentation if this were real: assessment of psychosis, stressor, duration, safety evaluation (SI/HI, ability to care for child), plan for antipsychotic Rx, follow-up, and involvement of family support.  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (Brief Psychotic Disorder)<br /></br>• Etiology: Severe psychological stress or trauma can precipitate a transient dysregulation of dopamine pathways in susceptible individuals → hallucinations/delusions.<br /></br>• Epidemiology: More common in women, peak 20–30 y.<br /></br>• Clinical course: abrupt onset, full remission within 1 month, low but present risk of progression to schizophrenia or mood disorder.  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Tests<br /></br>1. Second-generation antipsychotic (e.g., risperidone 1–2 mg PO daily, titrate)<br /></br>   – Mechanism: Dopamine-2 and 5-HT2A antagonism reduces positive symptoms.<br /></br>2. Short-term benzodiazepine only if severe agitation (not needed here).<br /></br>3. Psychotherapy (CBT, supportive) AFTER acute psychosis is controlled.<br /></br>4. Follow-up within days; reassess safety, side-effects (EPS, prolactin), and taper off if symptoms remit &lt; 1 month.<br /></br>No labs/imaging required unless ruling out metabolic/toxic causes (already done clinically).  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>• “1 stressor, 1 symptom, &lt; 1 month = Brief Psychotic Disorder → 1 pill (antipsychotic).”<br /></br>• Think “RISPeridone for RISPy (‘risky’) voices.”  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>A. Cognitive behavioral therapy<br /></br>   – Helpful once patient is stable; not adequate as sole acute treatment for active psychosis.<br /></br>B. Lithium carbonate<br /></br>   – Mood stabilizer for bipolar disorder &amp; augmentation in depression; not indicated in isolated brief psychosis without manic features.<br /></br>C. Lorazepam<br /></br>   – Useful for acute agitation, catatonia, or severe anxiety; does not treat hallucinations directly and she is calm.<br /></br>E. Sertraline<br /></br>   – SSRI for depression, anxiety, PTSD; has no primary antipsychotic effect and would not relieve hallucinations.  </p></br><p>Common Pitfall: Choosing hospitalization or benzodiazepines when there is no current suicidal/homicidal command or agitation and when family can ensure safety.  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>High-Yield Pearl<br /></br>New-onset hallucinations precipitated by stress, lasting &lt; 1 month, require an antipsychotic—usually an oral second-generation agent—unless safety mandates inpatient care.</p>
105. A 62-year-old woman with pancreatic cancer metastatic to the liver comes to the physician because of mild to moderate abdominal pain for 2 days. She currently takes no pain medications. She says that her pain has increased from 0 to 2 on a 10-point scale. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial pharmacotherapy for her pain?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Acetaminophen</br>b. Codeine</br>c. Fentanyl</br>d. Methadone</br>e. Morphine</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>A. Acetaminophen  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Key clues<br /></br>   • “Mild to moderate abdominal pain” (rated 2/10).<br /></br>   • No current analgesic therapy.<br /></br>   • Known advanced malignancy (pancreatic cancer with liver mets).  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>How they steer you<br /></br>   • The pain intensity is low (score 2), not severe.<br /></br>   • The phrase “initial pharmacotherapy” signals you should start at the bottom of the World Health Organization (WHO) cancer‐pain ladder, not jump straight to strong opioids.  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Test-taking framework<br /></br>   • WHO cancer pain ladder:<br /></br>     Step 1 – non-opioid ± adjuvant (e.g., acetaminophen, NSAID).<br /></br>     Step 2 – weak opioid ± non-opioid (e.g., codeine, hydrocodone).<br /></br>     Step 3 – strong opioid ± non-opioid (e.g., morphine, fentanyl, methadone).<br /></br>   • Escalate only when pain is uncontrolled or side-effects limit the lower step.  </p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>• Current pain severity (2/10) → Step 1 agent is sufficient.<br /></br>• Acetaminophen is the prototypical Step 1 analgesic; it has a favorable side-effect profile and no ceiling effect on hepatic dosing ≤4 g/day in normal liver function.<br /></br>• Document in chart: pain score, functional impact, chosen therapy, plan for reassessment and escalation if pain persists.  </p></br><p>Take-home message: Begin cancer‐pain management with a scheduled non-opioid for mild pain, adding or escalating to opioids only as clinically indicated.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology / Pharmacology Pearl  </p></br><p>• Pancreatic cancer causes visceral pain via capsular stretch, perineural invasion, and hepatic metastases.<br /></br>• Acetaminophen inhibits central COX and modulates descending serotonergic pathways, giving analgesia without significant peripheral anti-inflammatory action.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Notes  </p></br><ol></br><li>Dosing: acetaminophen 650 mg PO every 6 h or 1 g every 8 h (max 3–4 g/24 h).  </li></br><li>Reassess pain in 24–48 h. If ≥4/10 or worsening, add a weak opioid (Step 2).  </li></br><li>Liver function tests are not mandatory before short-term use unless pre-existing hepatic disease or heavy alcohol use.  </li></br></ol></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“ABC Ladder”<br /></br>A – Acetaminophen/NSAID (Step 1)<br /></br>B – “B-team” opioids (codeine, hydrocodone) (Step 2)<br /></br>C – “Champion” opioids (morphine, fentanyl, methadone) (Step 3)  </p></br><p>Visualize climbing a 3-rung ladder; you must stand on the first rung before stepping higher.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong  </p></br><p>B. Codeine – Weak opioid (Step 2). Appropriate only after non-opioid trial fails or pain ≥4/10. Starting here skips a step and risks nausea, constipation.  </p></br><p>C. Fentanyl – Potent Step 3 opioid (often transdermal for chronic severe pain). Over-treatment for 2/10 pain; risk of respiratory depression, requires opioid tolerance before patch use.  </p></br><p>D. Methadone – Long-acting strong opioid (Step 3) with complex kinetics and QT prolongation risk. Reserved for severe chronic pain or opioid-use disorder. Not first-line.  </p></br><p>E. Morphine – Prototype strong opioid (Step 3). Excellent for moderate-to-severe cancer pain, but unnecessary here and carries higher constipation, sedation, and dependency risks.  </p></br><p>Common Pitfall: Thinking “cancer = morphine.” Always match therapy intensity to pain severity; don’t skip steps in the ladder.</p>
106. A 32-year-old nulligravid woman comes to the physician with her husband because she has been unable to conceive for 2 years. The couple has sexual intercourse without contraception approximately three times weekly. Menses occur at irregular 25- to 35-day intervals. Her last menstrual period was 3 weeks ago. She is otherwise healthy and takes no medications. Physical examination, including pelvic examination, shows no abnormalities. Serum studies show:</br>Thyroid-stimulating hormone  1.2 µU/mL</br>Prolactin                    14 ng/mL</br>Progesterone                 0.5 ng/mL (menstrual cycle day 21: follicular N<3; luteal N>3–5)</br></br>Semen analysis shows:</br>Count     36 million/mL (N>20)</br>Motility  65 percent (N>60 percent)</br></br>Hysterosalpingography shows bilateral tubal patency. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's infertility?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Anovulation</br>b. Cervical stenosis</br>c. Endometriosis</br>d. Male factor</br>e. Unexplained infertility</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>a. Anovulation  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li>Time-based infertility definition: ≥12 months of regular, unprotected intercourse without conception.  </li></br><li>Cycles: “irregular 25- to 35-day intervals.” True ovulatory cycles vary ≤ 2–3 days month-to-month; a swing of 10 days suggests oligo-/anovulation.  </li></br><li>Day-21 (mid-luteal) progesterone: 0.5 ng/mL (&lt;3 ng/mL rules OUT recent ovulation).  </li></br><li>Normal TSH &amp; prolactin → thyroid and prolactin disorders unlikely.  </li></br><li>Semen analysis normal (count &amp; motility).  </li></br><li>Hysterosalpingography shows patent tubes → mechanical factors excluded.  </li></br></ol></br><p>Pattern: Woman with irregular menses + low mid-luteal progesterone + normal partner and anatomy → classic NBME set-up for “anovulation.”  </p></br><p>Test‐taking framework:<br /></br>• Step 1: Check male factor → semen analysis normal.<br /></br>• Step 2: Rule out tubal/uterine → HSG normal.<br /></br>• Step 3: Confirm ovulation → mid-luteal progesterone &lt;3 ng/mL = anovulation.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Mid-luteal progesterone is the single best serum marker of ovulation because only a functional corpus luteum secretes &gt;3–5 ng/mL. A value of 0.5 ng/mL on presumed cycle day 21 means no corpus luteum was formed → no ovulation → infertility.  </p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real clinic): cycle pattern, mid-luteal progesterone &lt;3 ng/mL, discussion of ovulation-induction options (letrozole, clomiphene), counseling on weight, exercise, and possible PCOS evaluation.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (high-yield)  </p></br><p>• Ovulation is triggered by an LH surge mid-cycle.<br /></br>• Without ovulation → no corpus luteum → minimal progesterone → variable cycle length.<br /></br>Common etiologies: PCOS, hypothalamic amenorrhea, hyperprolactinemia, thyroid disease, obesity, premature ovarian failure. Work-up begins with labs given here (TSH, prolactin) and an anovulatory value for progesterone.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Review  </p></br><ol></br><li>Confirm anovulation: mid-luteal progesterone or serial basal body temperatures/ovulation predictor kits.  </li></br><li>First-line ovulation induction in most anovulatory women (esp. PCOS):<br /></br>   • Letrozole (aromatase inhibitor) &gt; clomiphene citrate (SERM)  </li></br><li>Second-line: exogenous gonadotropins, IVF.  </li></br><li>Address underlying cause (weight loss for obesity‐related, metformin for PCOS, pulsatile GnRH for hypothalamic amenorrhea, etc.).  </li></br></ol></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“P for Progesterone = Proof of ovulation.”<br /></br>• Day-21 Progesterone &lt;3 = no P, no O (ovulation).  </p></br><p>“Ovulation triad”: Regular 28 ± 2 day cycles + Mittelschmerz + mid-luteal P&gt;3 → ovulation present. Absence of any = suspect anovulation.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong  </p></br><p>b. Cervical stenosis<br /></br>• Would present with scant menses, severe cramping, difficulty passing HSG catheter; progesterone could still be normal because ovulation occurs.  </p></br><p>c. Endometriosis<br /></br>• Typically causes dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, chronic pelvic pain; infertility mechanism is mechanical/ inflammatory. Tubal obstruction or peritoneal adhesions often seen; HSG usually abnormal or laparoscopy required. Nothing in vignette supports pain history or lesions. Progesterone often normal.  </p></br><p>d. Male factor<br /></br>• Normal semen count (&gt;20 million/mL) and motility (&gt;60 %) rules this out.  </p></br><p>e. Unexplained infertility<br /></br>• Diagnosis of exclusion after confirming normal ovulation, anatomy, and semen—here we have a clear abnormality (anovulation).  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Key Takeaway  </p></br><p>Low mid-luteal serum progesterone (&lt;3 ng/mL) in a woman with irregular cycles and otherwise normal infertility work-up pinpoints anovulation as the cause of infertility.</p>
107. A previously healthy 4-month-old boy is brought to the physician because of a 2-day history of fever and episodes of vomiting and crying. He is fussy but consolable. His temperature is 39.1°C (102.4°F), pulse is 142/min, respirations are 28/min, and blood pressure is 88/54 mm Hg. On examination, the left testicle is not palpable in the scrotal sac. Laboratory studies show:</br>Hematocrit 35 percent</br>Leukocyte count 19,500/mm^3</br>Platelet count 250,000/mm^3</br>Urine</br> RBC 10/hpf</br> WBC 30/hpf</br></br>A Gram stain of a catheterized urine sample shows gram-negative rods. Urine and blood cultures are pending. Which of the following is the most likely underlying cause of these abnormal laboratory findings?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. Cryptorchidism</br>b. Immunodeficiency</br>c. Posterior urethral valves</br>d. Renal calculi</br>e. Renal vein thrombosis</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>c. Posterior urethral valves (PUV)</p></br><hr /></br><p>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>1. Age/sex: 4-month-old boy → PUV occurs exclusively in boys.<br /></br>2. Presenting picture: 2 days of high fever, vomiting, irritability → suggests systemic infection.<br /></br>3. Vital signs/labs: Temp 39.1 °C, leukocytosis (19,500/µL), pyuria (30 WBC/hpf), hematuria (10 RBC/hpf), gram-negative rods on urine Gram stain → acute febrile urinary tract infection (UTI), most commonly E coli.<br /></br>4. Physical exam red-herring: “Left testicle not palpable” (cryptorchidism). Important to note but not the etiology of UTI.<br /></br>5. Key pattern: Male infant + febrile UTI ⇒ always think about an anatomic obstruction such as PUV or severe vesicoureteral reflux.  </p></br><p>Cognitive framework:<br /></br>• Step 1 – Identify infection type (febrile UTI/pyelonephritis).<br /></br>• Step 2 – In infants, search for a structural urinary tract abnormality.<br /></br>• Step 3 – Recall that the most common obstructive lesion unique to boys &lt;1 yr is PUV.</p></br><hr /></br><p>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>PUV are congenital membranous folds in the posterior urethra that act as a “flap-valve,” causing bladder-outlet obstruction. Consequences:<br /></br>• Urinary stasis → recurrent gram-negative UTIs.<br /></br>• Increased bladder pressure → reflux, hydronephrosis, renal dysplasia → hematuria, possible renal insufficiency.<br /></br>• May present as poor urinary stream, distended bladder, or nonspecific signs like fever and vomiting.</p></br><p>Take-home: Any febrile UTI in a male infant warrants renal/bladder ultrasound ± voiding cystourethrography to look for PUV or vesicoureteral reflux, because delayed diagnosis leads to permanent renal damage.</p></br><hr /></br><p>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Etiology: Abnormal Wolffian duct insertion creates obstructing mucosal leaflets in the posterior urethra.<br /></br>• Only occurs in males.<br /></br>• Pathogenesis: Obstruction → high intravesical pressure → bilateral hydroureteronephrosis, renal parenchymal damage, and predisposition to infection.<br /></br>• Long-term risks: Chronic kidney disease, bladder dysfunction.</p></br><hr /></br><p>Diagnostics &amp; Management (FYI)<br /></br>1. Initial: Renal &amp; bladder ultrasound (thick-walled bladder, hydronephrosis).<br /></br>2. Definitive: Voiding cystourethrogram shows dilated posterior urethra &amp; abrupt cutoff.<br /></br>3. Acute management: Empiric IV antibiotics for UTI (e.g., third-generation cephalosporin such as cefotaxime).<br /></br>4. Definitive therapy: Endoscopic transurethral valve ablation; in severe cases temporary urinary diversion (vesicostomy).</p></br><hr /></br><p>High-Yield Memory Aid<br /></br>“Posterior Urethral Valves Plug Up Little Boys”:<br /></br>• Posterior location<br /></br>• Urethra obstructed<br /></br>• Valves = membranous folds<br /></br>• Plug → stasis &amp; infection<br /></br>• Little Boys only</p></br><hr /></br><p>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong  </p></br><p>a. Cryptorchidism<br /></br>• Risk: infertility, malignancy, testicular torsion—not UTIs. Does not explain pyuria or gram-negative rods.</p></br><p>b. Immunodeficiency<br /></br>• Would predispose to broader spectrum (viral, fungal) and recurrent infections at multiple sites. Normal leukocyte count may be low in certain immunodeficiencies, not elevated. Does not specifically cause hematuria.</p></br><p>d. Renal calculi<br /></br>• Uncommon in infants. Can cause hematuria, pain, sterile pyuria but not typically febrile gram-negative UTI in a 4-month-old.</p></br><p>e. Renal vein thrombosis<br /></br>• Presents with gross hematuria, flank mass, thrombocytopenia, often in neonates with dehydration or maternal diabetes. Pyuria and gram-negative rods are not expected.</p></br><hr /></br><p>Key Takeaway<br /></br>In any male infant with a febrile UTI, think posterior urethral valves or other obstructive anomalies—diagnose early to prevent permanent kidney damage.</p>
108. A previously healthy 57-year-old man comes to the physician because of a 2-month history of increasing left foot pain at rest. He has smoked two packs of cigarettes daily for 30 years. Left popliteal and pedal pulses cannot be palpated. The foot is cool, and dependent rubor is noted. Arteriography shows occlusion of the left superficial femoral artery and stenosis of the left profunda femoris artery. Arterial bypass graft is scheduled. The patient is at greatest risk for development of which of the following postoperative complications?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. Cerebral infarction</br>b. Ischemic colitis</br>c. Myocardial infarction</br>d. Pneumonia</br>e. Renal failure</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>c. Myocardial infarction (MI)</p></br><p>───────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis</br>• Key clues<br /></br>  – 57-year-old man, heavy smoker → long-standing atherosclerotic disease is likely systemic, not limited to the leg.<br /></br>  – Peripheral arterial disease severe enough to require a femoropopliteal bypass → “major vascular surgery,” which is considered the highest–cardiac-risk category in peri-operative guidelines.<br /></br>  – No mention of chronic lung, kidney, or cerebrovascular symptoms that would independently raise those complication risks.<br /></br>• Big picture pattern: In patients with clinically significant peripheral arterial disease (PAD) who undergo vascular surgery, the greatest early postoperative threat is cardiac ischemia due to the same diffuse atherosclerosis that produced the limb ischemia.</p></br><p>Metacognitive tip: As soon as you read “elective (or emergent) major vascular surgery,” think “high-risk for peri-operative MI” unless another complication is screaming out from the stem.</p></br><p>───────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning</br>1. Classify the procedure’s inherent risk. Vascular operations on the aorta, femoral, or popliteal arteries = “high-risk” (&gt;5% cardiac event rate).<br /></br>2. Apply Revised Cardiac Risk Index (Lee index):<br /></br>   • High-risk surgery → 1 point.<br /></br>   • History of ischemic heart disease, heart failure, cerebrovascular disease, insulin-dependent diabetes, or creatinine &gt;2 mg/dL would add more points, but even 1 point in a high-risk operation keeps the cardiac event probability highest among the listed complications.<br /></br>3. Therefore myocardial infarction tops the list of likely complications within 48-72 h post-op.</p></br><p>Take-home message: Vascular surgery + atherosclerotic risk factors = assume diffuse coronary artery disease unless proven otherwise → plan peri-operative β-blockade, statin, and close telemetry.</p></br><p>───────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review</br>• Atherosclerosis is a systemic process. PAD is strongly associated with occult coronary artery disease (CAD).<br /></br>• Surgical stress → catecholamine surge, ↑HR, ↑afterload → ↑myocardial oxygen demand; anesthesia and blood loss ↓oxygen supply → supply–demand mismatch → subendocardial ischemia/MI.<br /></br>• Most peri-operative MIs are NSTEMIs from supply–demand mismatch, not plaque rupture.</p></br><p>───────────────────</br>Diagnostic &amp; Management Pearls</br>Pre-op:<br /></br>• β-blocker continuation (or initiation if already on one).<br /></br>• High-intensity statin.<br /></br>• Consider stress testing only if it would change management.</p></br><p>Post-op:<br /></br>• Telemetry for 48 h, serial troponins, aggressive pain control, early mobilization, avoid hypotension/hypoxia/anemia.</p></br><p>───────────────────</br>Mnemonics / Memory Aids</br>“Vascular = Very high risk for Ventricular ischemia.”<br /></br>Alternatively: The three V’s of PAD surgery → “Vessel disease, Ventricular disease, Vigilant monitoring.”</p></br><p>───────────────────</br>Incorrect Answer Analysis</br>a. Cerebral infarction: Stroke risk does rise in diffuse atherosclerosis, but peri-operative stroke rates after infra-inguinal bypass are &lt;1%, far lower than MI.</p></br><p>b. Ischemic colitis: Classically follows aortic aneurysm repair or low-flow states, not femoropopliteal bypass.</p></br><p>d. Pneumonia: More common after thoracic/abdominal surgery, prolonged intubation, underlying COPD. This patient is having a lower-extremity procedure with minimal respiratory compromise expected.</p></br><p>e. Renal failure: Most often seen with suprarenal aortic clamping, contrast-induced nephropathy, or pre-existing CKD. In an infra-inguinal bypass, kidneys are usually not jeopardized directly.</p></br><p>────────────</br>Bottom Line: Major peripheral vascular surgeries carry the highest peri-operative cardiac risk; myocardial infarction is the complication you must anticipate and prevent.</p>
109. A 17-year-old primigravid patient at 38 weeks' gestation is admitted to the hospital in labor. Contractions have occurred for 2 hours every 2 to 3 minutes. Her pregnancy has been uncomplicated. A fetal heart tracing is shown. The cervix is 4 cm dilated and 90 percent effaced; the vertex is at 0 station. In addition to continued fetal monitoring, which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Observation only</br>b. Biophysical profile</br>c. Doppler ultrasonography of the umbilical artery</br>d. Fetal scalp sampling</br>e. Cesarean delivery</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>a. Observation only  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues<br /></br>• 17-year-old G1 at term (38 wks) admitted in active labor → intrapartum fetal heart rate (FHR) interpretation is the focus.<br /></br>• Contractions q 2–3 min for 2 h → plenty of tracings available.<br /></br>• Cervix 4 cm/90 % effaced/0 station → early active phase; intervention should be undertaken only for true fetal distress.<br /></br>• Stem asks for “most appropriate next step” in addition to “continued fetal monitoring.”<br /></br>• Correct answer supplied = Observation ⇒ therefore the tracing must be a Category I (reassuring) pattern—most classically baseline 110-160 bpm, moderate variability, ± early decelerations, no late or variable decels.  </p></br><p>Metacognitive approach<br /></br>Step 1: Identify whether FHR strip is Category I, II, or III.<br /></br>Step 2: Category I → expectant management; Category III → intrauterine resuscitative maneuvers ± expedited delivery; Category II → look for correctable cause or further testing (e.g., scalp pH, vibroacoustic stimulation).<br /></br>Step 3: Match answer choice to management algorithm.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Because the tracing is normal/reassuring (Category I):<br /></br>• No sign of fetal acidemia or hypoxia.<br /></br>• Labor is progressing (4 cm and fully effaced for a primigravid woman after only 2 h of contractions is normal).<br /></br>• Therefore, simply continue routine intrapartum care and observation.  </p></br><p>Take-home message:<br /></br>A reassuring (Category I) FHR tracing in an otherwise uncomplicated labor requires no immediate intervention other than ongoing observation and routine intrapartum care. Over-intervention increases maternal risk without improving neonatal outcome.  </p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real life)<br /></br>• Category of tracing, baseline HR, variability, presence/absence of accelerations/decels.<br /></br>• Maternal vitals, contraction pattern, cervical exam.<br /></br>• Plan: “Continue routine labor management; reassess FHR every 15 min in active phase.”  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (high-yield)  </p></br><p>Fetal heart rate categories (NICHD):<br /></br>1. Category I (normal): baseline 110-160, moderate variability (6-25 bpm), no late/variable decels, ± early decels/accelerations. → ≤1 ‰ risk of acidemia.<br /></br>2. Category II (indeterminate): everything else not in I or III. → monitor, corrective measures, ancillary testing.<br /></br>3. Category III (abnormal): absent variability plus recurrent late/variable decels, bradycardia, or sinusoidal pattern. → prompt evaluation, intrauterine resuscitation, likely operative delivery.  </p></br><p>Early decelerations = fetal head compression → vagal stimulation → mirror contractions → benign.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatments &amp; Diagnostic Tests Review  </p></br><p>Management ladder for a NON-reassuring tracing (not needed here but good to memorize):<br /></br>1. Maternal reposition, O₂, IV fluids, d/c oxytocin.<br /></br>2. Amnioinfusion if recurrent variables.<br /></br>3. Scalp stimulation or scalp pH sampling if still unclear.<br /></br>4. Expedite delivery (operative vaginal or cesarean) for persistent Category III.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“VEAL CHOP” mnemonic for decelerations:<br /></br>• Variable → Cord compression<br /></br>• Early → Head compression<br /></br>• Accelerations → OK<br /></br>• Late → Placental insufficiency  </p></br><p>If you see Early = “Head”; think “happy baby,” do nothing.  </p></br><p>Quick triage mantra:<br /></br>“I → Observe, II → Evaluate, III → Deliver.”  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>b. Biophysical profile (BPP)<br /></br>• Used antenatally when the mother is NOT in labor to follow up a non-reactive NST or assess high-risk pregnancies (IUGR, oligohydramnios).<br /></br>• Intrapartum it provides no new info; continuous FHR gives real-time data.  </p></br><p>c. Doppler ultrasonography of the umbilical artery<br /></br>• Tool for chronic placental insufficiency/IUGR in outpatient setting, not for acute intrapartum monitoring of a term fetus with normal strip.  </p></br><p>d. Fetal scalp sampling (scalp pH or lactate)<br /></br>• Considered when tracing is Category II/III and you wish to confirm fetal acidemia prior to operative delivery. Not indicated with Category I pattern.  </p></br><p>e. Cesarean delivery<br /></br>• Reserved for persistent Category III tracing unresponsive to intrauterine resuscitation or for obstetric complications (malpresentation, arrest disorders). Unwarranted here and would expose mother to unnecessary surgical risk.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line<br /></br>A normal (Category I) fetal heart tracing during active labor in an otherwise healthy pregnancy requires no intervention beyond continued observation.</p>
110. A 77-year-old man comes to the physician 24 hours after the gradual onset of mild shortness of breath. He has alcohol-induced cirrhosis treated with furosemide, spironolactone, and a potassium supplement, but he says that he ran out of his medications last week. Since then, he has noticed progressive swelling of his feet and increased abdominal girth. There is no other history of serious illness. He is uncomfortable and in mild respiratory distress. He appears cachectic. His pulse is 82/min, respirations are 14/min, and blood pressure is 102/74 mm Hg. Cardiopulmonary examination shows no abnormalities. Abdominal examination shows severe distention. The liver is not palpated. There is 2+ pedal edema bilaterally. Serum studies show:</br>Na+ 126 mEq/L  </br>K+ 3.9 mEq/L  </br>Cl− 96 mEq/L  </br>HCO3− 26 mEq/L  </br>Urea nitrogen 18 mg/dL  </br>Creatinine 1.3 mg/dL  </br>Albumin 1.6 g/dL  </br>Which of the following is most likely to be decreased in this patient?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Serum ADH (vasopressin)  </br>b. Serum aldosterone  </br>c. Serum angiotensin  </br>d. Total body water  </br>e. Urine sodium</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>e.  Urine sodium</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues that drive the diagnosis/physiology:<br /></br>• Alcohol-induced cirrhosis with ascites and peripheral edema → classic “third-spacing” state.<br /></br>• Ran out of furosemide/spironolactone → no longer antagonizing renal sodium retention.<br /></br>• Hyponatremia (Na⁺ 126) with low albumin (1.6 g/dL) → dilutional hyponatremia from free-water retention on a background of low oncotic pressure.<br /></br>• Normal BP on the low side (102/74) and cachexia → kidneys interpret this as ↓ effective arterial blood volume (EABV) even though total body water is actually high.  </p></br><p>Framework to approach similar questions<br /></br>1. Ask: “Is the EABV low?” If yes (eg, cirrhosis, CHF, nephrotic, severe burns), RAAS and ADH turn on.<br /></br>2. When RAAS &amp; ADH are ON → kidneys avidly reabsorb Na⁺ and water → urine Na⁺ falls (&lt;10–20 mEq/L).<br /></br>3. All plasma volume-expanding hormones (renin → angiotensin → aldosterone, plus ADH) rise; total body water rises; only urine Na⁺ falls.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Stepwise logic linking the vignette to choice E:<br /></br>1. Cirrhosis causes portal &amp; splanchnic vasodilation → ↓ EABV sensed at the juxtaglomerular apparatus.<br /></br>2. This triggers:<br /></br>   • ↑ Renin → ↑ Angiotensin II (choice c would be ↑, not ↓).<br /></br>   • ↑ Aldosterone (choice b would be ↑, not ↓).<br /></br>   • ↑ ADH/vasopressin (choice a would be ↑, not ↓).<br /></br>3. Aldosterone stimulates Na⁺ reabsorption in the distal nephron → urine Na⁺ concentration drops.<br /></br>4. Because diuretics were stopped, nothing blocks this process → urine Na⁺ is even lower than usual.<br /></br>Therefore, the parameter most likely to be decreased is urine sodium.</p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real-life encounter)<br /></br>• “Patient with decompensated cirrhosis, tense ascites, dilutional hyponatremia. Likely low EABV with secondary RAAS/ADH activation, evidenced by expected low urine Na⁺.”  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>Cirrhosis → portal hypertension &amp; systemic vasodilation → ↓ EABV despite ↑ total body water.<br /></br>Kidney response:<br /></br>• ↑ Renin → ↑ Angiotensin II (vasoconstriction + efferent constriction)<br /></br>• ↑ Aldosterone (Na⁺/H₂O retention, K⁺ excretion)<br /></br>• ↑ ADH (free-water retention)<br /></br>Net result:<br /></br>• Expansion of total body water (ascites, edema)<br /></br>• Dilutional hyponatremia<br /></br>• Very low urine Na⁺ (&lt;10 mEq/L)</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Diagnostic &amp; Management Notes (high-yield)  </p></br><p>Laboratory confirmation:<br /></br>• Urine Na⁺ typically &lt;10 mEq/L in cirrhosis without diuretics.<br /></br>• Low FeNa consistent with avid Na⁺ retention.  </p></br><p>Management overview:<br /></br>1. Restart diuretics (spironolactone first ± furosemide in 100:40 mg ratio).<br /></br>2. Na⁺ restriction, fluid restriction for severe hyponatremia.<br /></br>3. Large-volume paracentesis with albumin replacement if tense ascites.<br /></br>4. Evaluate for TIPS, transplant eligibility.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“Third-spacing states keep salt in the body’s lake”:<br /></br>• Third-spacing (Cirrhosis, CHF, Nephrotic)<br /></br>• Keep (↑) RAAS &amp; ADH<br /></br>• Salt (↓ urine Na⁺) stays in<br /></br>• Body’s lake (↑ total body water) enlarges.</p></br><p>Visual: Picture a dam (RAAS) closing gates on a river of salt—water backs up into a swollen lake (ascites), and only a trickle (low urine Na⁺) escapes.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong  </p></br><p>a. Serum ADH (vasopressin) – rises because baroreceptors sense low EABV; would be increased, not decreased.<br /></br>b. Serum aldosterone – rises via RAAS activation; not decreased.<br /></br>c. Serum angiotensin – rises via renin release; not decreased.<br /></br>d. Total body water – increases (ascites, edema); not decreased.<br /></br>e. Urine sodium – the only parameter that falls due to aldosterone-mediated Na⁺ retention → correct answer.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Core Takeaway<br /></br>In cirrhosis (and any condition with ↓ effective arterial blood volume), RAAS and ADH are activated, leading to avid renal sodium retention and therefore a LOW urine sodium concentration.</p>
111. A 2½-year-old girl is brought to the physician for a well-child examination. She was born at term following an uncomplicated pregnancy and has no history of serious illness. She can climb steps, throw a ball overhand, and build a tower of six blocks. She is beginning to dress herself. She uses two-word sentences. She is shy around strangers. She participates in interactive play in day care. Examination shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most accurate assessment of this child's development?  </br>Fine Motor Development | Gross Motor Development | Language Development | Social Development	Correct answer: H</br></br>a. Delayed delayed delayed delayed  </br>b. Delayed delayed normal normal  </br>c. Delayed normal delayed delayed  </br>d. Delayed normal normal normal  </br>e. Normal delayed normal normal  </br>f. Normal normal delayed normal  </br>g. Normal normal normal delayed  </br>h. Normal normal normal normal</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>h. Normal | Normal | Normal | Normal  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>1. Age anchor → 2½ years (≈ 30 months). Always start by recalling the standard “must-do-by-this-age” milestones.<br /></br>2. Pertinent positives<br /></br>   • Gross motor – “climb steps” and “throw a ball overhand.” Both are expected by 24 mo.<br /></br>   • Fine motor – “build a tower of six blocks.” Six-block tower is classic 24-month milestone.<br /></br>   • Language – “uses two-word sentences.” Two-word phrases are expected by 24 mo (50-word vocabulary).<br /></br>   • Social – “participates in interactive play… shy around strangers.” Parallel/interactive play begins around 2 yr; stranger shyness is normal.<br /></br>3. Pertinent negatives – no red flags such as loss of skills, single-word speech only, inability to walk, etc.  </p></br><p>Metacognitive tip: When a milestone stem asks “most accurate assessment,” map each domain to age-based checklists (Gross motor, Fine motor, Language, Social). If every listed skill is on-time or ahead, all are normal.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Step-wise thought process<br /></br>1. Identify age.<br /></br>2. Recall milestones due by that age.<br /></br>3. Compare listed skills to the checklist.<br /></br>4. Decide normal vs delayed in each domain.  </p></br><p>Take-home: At 2 years a child should:<br /></br>• Run, kick or throw ball, walk up/down stairs (gross)<br /></br>• Stack 4-6 blocks, copy a line (fine)<br /></br>• 2-word phrases, 50-word vocabulary (language)<br /></br>• Parallel play, imitates actions (social)  </p></br><p>Documenting in real practice: “Development appropriate for age in all domains; no concerns for delay.”  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>Most developmental delays are multifactorial (genetic, prenatal insults, environmental). None are present here; therefore no pathology.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Diagnostic Tests / Treatment<br /></br>None indicated. Continue routine surveillance, reassure parents, and encourage reading &amp; play to foster language and social skills.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“2 by 2 by 2”:<br /></br>• 2 years<br /></br>• 2-word phrases<br /></br>• Tower of 2 × 3 = 6 blocks.<br /></br>“GFL-S” ladder (Gross, Fine, Language, Social): every two numbers (2 yr, 4 yr, etc.) add a new major skill.  </p></br><p>Visualization: Picture a 2-year-old on the 2nd stair throwing a ball, next to a 6-block tower, saying “more juice,” while two toddlers play side-by-side.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>a. Delayed | Delayed | Delayed | Delayed<br /></br> Would imply child cannot perform age-appropriate tasks in ANY domain (e.g., not walking, no pincer grasp, no words) – clearly not this case.  </p></br><p>b. Delayed | Delayed | Normal | Normal<br /></br> Gross &amp; fine delays would show inability to build blocks or climb stairs; absent here.  </p></br><p>c. Delayed | Normal | Delayed | Delayed<br /></br> Fine motor delay would look like poor grasp or unable to stack blocks; language/social delays would be limited speech &amp; lack of interactive play.  </p></br><p>d. Delayed | Normal | Normal | Normal<br /></br> Fine motor is normal (tower), so “delayed” incorrect. Gross, language, social are on time, so “delayed” mismatches.  </p></br><p>e. Normal | Delayed | Normal | Normal<br /></br> Gross motor delay would be inability to climb stairs or throw; she can do both.  </p></br><p>f. Normal | Normal | Delayed | Normal<br /></br> Language delay would be &lt;50 words or no 2-word phrases at 2 yr; she meets milestone.  </p></br><p>g. Normal | Normal | Normal | Delayed<br /></br> Social delay would be lack of interest in peers; she engages in interactive play.  </p></br><p>Only option h appropriately labels all four domains as normal.</p>
112. A 57-year-old woman comes to the physician because of a 3-month history of intermittent nonproductive cough and shortness of breath with exertion and at night. She has not had fever or weight loss. She immigrated to the USA from Kenya 10 years ago. She has a 5-year history of hypertension controlled with losartan and hydrochlorothiazide. The patient is 168 cm (5 ft 6 in) tall and weighs 82 kg (180 lb); BMI is 29 kg/m2. Her temperature is 37.2°C (99°F), pulse is 72/min and regular, respirations are 12/min, and blood pressure is 138/88 mm Hg. Jugular venous pulsations are present 3 cm above the sternal angle. Low-pitched expiratory wheezes are heard over the lungs posteriorly. A grade 2/6 systolic murmur is heard at the left sternal border and second intercostal space. A chest x-ray shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Adverse effect of losartan</br>b. Asthma</br>c. Cystic fibrosis</br>d. Sarcoidosis</br>e. Tuberculosis</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>b. Asthma  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Key clues<br /></br>   • “Intermittent non-productive cough” ± “shortness of breath with exertion and at night” – classic language used in NBME stems for asthma.<br /></br>   • “Low-pitched expiratory wheezes” – expiratory wheeze = lower‐airway obstruction.<br /></br>   • “Normal chest x-ray” – most patients with stable asthma (between attacks) have a normal film.<br /></br>   • No fever, weight loss, hemoptysis – points away from infectious or malignant causes.<br /></br>   • Medication list includes an ARB (losartan), not an ACE inhibitor (ACEI), so drug-induced cough is unlikely.  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Pertinent negatives<br /></br>   • No sinus congestion, no heartburn → less support for post-nasal drip or GERD.<br /></br>   • CXR lacks hilar adenopathy, reticulonodular pattern, or cavitations, arguing against sarcoid and TB.  </p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>Test-taking framework (cognitive &amp; metacognitive)<br /></br>Step 1: Chronic cough (&gt;8 wk) → obtain CXR. If normal, think of the “BIG 3”:<br /></br>  a) Upper-airway cough syndrome (post-nasal drip)<br /></br>  b) Asthma<br /></br>  c) GERD<br /></br>Step 2: Look for buzzwords. Nocturnal cough + wheeze → asthma jumps to top.<br /></br>Step 3: Check drug list for ACEI; if absent, eliminate ACE-cough.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Asthma pathophysiology: episodic, reversible bronchial hyper-responsiveness → airway narrowing → cough, dyspnea, wheeze that is worse at night/early morning. Normal CXR between attacks.<br /></br>Documentation points (real patient): Duration; triggers; prior history/allergies; PFT/spirometry plan; rule-out ACEI.  </p></br><p>Take-home message: Intermittent nocturnal cough + expiratory wheeze + normal CXR in an adult not taking an ACEI = asthma until proven otherwise.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (high-yield)  </p></br><p>• Trigger (allergen, infection, exercise) → TH2 lymphocyte activation → IL-4/5/13 → IgE + eosinophils → reversible bronchoconstriction, mucus hypersecretion, airway edema.<br /></br>• Circadian drop in endogenous cortisol &amp; catecholamines at night → ↑ airway hyper-reactivity → nocturnal symptoms.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Diagnosis &amp; Management Pearls  </p></br><ol></br><li>Confirm with spirometry: ↓ FEV1/FVC that normalizes (≥12% &amp; ≥200 mL rise in FEV1) after bronchodilator.  </li></br><li>Work-up of chronic cough: If asthma suspected, order spirometry ± methacholine challenge.  </li></br><li>Treatment ladder (Step 2CK must-know):<br /></br>   • Intermittent: SABA (e.g., albuterol) PRN<br /></br>   • Persistent: add low-dose inhaled corticosteroid (ICS) (e.g., budesonide, fluticasone)<br /></br>   • Escalate with LABA (salmeterol/formoterol) + ↑ ICS dose, leukotriene receptor antagonist (montelukast), or anti-IgE (omalizumab) as indicated.<br /></br>   • Acute exacerbation: SABA + systemic steroids; consider ipratropium, O2, MgSO4.  </li></br></ol></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“Cough that AWAKENS you” – think Asthma/ACE/Whooping cough; CXR normal + wheeze → Asthma.<br /></br>Night-time cough mnemonic: “NITE”<br /></br>N – Nocturnal<br /></br>I – Inspiratory/expiratory wheeze<br /></br>T – Totally normal x-ray<br /></br>E – Episodic → Asthma  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>a. Adverse effect of losartan<br /></br>• ARBs rarely cause cough (ACEIs do). An ACEI cough would be persistent, non-wheezy, and starts within weeks of initiation.  </p></br><p>c. Cystic fibrosis<br /></br>• Presents in childhood/young adulthood with chronic productive cough, recurrent infections, steatorrhea; abnormal CXR (bronchiectasis).  </p></br><p>d. Sarcoidosis<br /></br>• Should show bilateral hilar adenopathy, reticulonodular infiltrates, or erythema nodosum; often constitutional symptoms; CXR here is normal.  </p></br><p>e. Tuberculosis<br /></br>• History from Kenya raises possibility, but typical TB symptoms = chronic cough ± hemoptysis, fever, night sweats, weight loss + CXR cavitary or upper-lobe infiltrates.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line<br /></br>Intermittent nocturnal cough and dyspnea with expiratory wheezes and a normal chest x-ray in an adult not on an ACE inhibitor is most consistent with asthma.</p>
113. An asymptomatic 57-year-old man who is HIV positive comes to the physician as a new patient with his medical records. He currently takes no medications. He has a history of sexual activity with male and female partners but has been in a monogamous relationship with a woman for the past 2 years. His partner uses an oral contraceptive, and they do not use condoms. On questioning, he says his partner does not know about his HIV status, but that his previous physician “knew all about this and said it was not a big deal.” Examination today shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Contact the patient's previous physician for further information</br>b. Discontinue care until the patient tells his partner about his HIV status</br>c. Emphasize the importance of consistent use of condoms</br>d. Encourage the patient to tell his partner about his HIV status</br>e. Inform the partner of the patient's HIV status</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>D. Encourage the patient to tell his partner about his HIV status  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues<br /></br>• 57-year-old man with documented HIV infection, currently asymptomatic<br /></br>• Engaged in unprotected intercourse with a single partner for 2 years<br /></br>• Partner is unaware of his HIV status<br /></br>• Patient believes “his previous physician said it was not a big deal” → suggests prior counseling may have been inadequate<br /></br>Testable pattern<br /></br>1. A serious communicable disease that poses a clear, foreseeable risk of significant harm to an identifiable third party (the partner).<br /></br>2. Physician’s primary ethical duties:<br /></br>   • Respect confidentiality (autonomy)<br /></br>   • Prevent harm to the partner (beneficence / non-maleficence)<br /></br>3. Stepwise approach endorsed by CDC, AMA, and state public-health statutes:<br /></br>   a. Counsel and strongly encourage patient to disclose and/or use protection.<br /></br>   b. If patient refuses, involve public-health authorities / partner-notification programs.<br /></br>   c. Direct disclosure by the physician without the patient’s consent occurs only after patient refuses and the risk remains.  </p></br><p>Cognitive framework for similar questions<br /></br>• Ask: “Is there an imminent, serious threat to an identifiable person?”<br /></br>• If yes → First counsel the patient to disclose or take protective measures.<br /></br>• Only if that fails → Consider permissible breach through public-health channels or duty-to-warn (Tarasoff-type exceptions).  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Why D is correct<br /></br>1. Addresses the immediate ethical obligation to protect the partner while maintaining the patient’s confidentiality.<br /></br>2. Respects patient autonomy by allowing him the first opportunity to disclose.<br /></br>3. Fulfills beneficence/non-maleficence by attempting to prevent transmission.<br /></br>4. Aligns with CDC partner-notification guidelines: “Clinicians should urge HIV-infected patients to inform all sexual partners and facilitate disclosure.”  </p></br><p>Documentation if this were a real patient<br /></br>• Counseling provided on disclosure, safer sex, condoms, and antiretroviral therapy (ART).<br /></br>• Patient’s response to counseling.<br /></br>• Plan for follow-up and possible referral to public-health partner-notification services if disclosure does not occur.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (high-yield)  </p></br><p>• HIV viral load is highest when untreated, increasing transmission risk.<br /></br>• Effective ART can reduce viral load to undetectable levels (“U = U”), dramatically lowering but NOT eliminating risk → reinforces need for disclosure and possibly PrEP (pre-exposure prophylaxis) for partner.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Management &amp;
