Diagnostic Pearls  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Immediate<br /></br>   • Strongly encourage disclosure (correct answer).<br /></br>   • Initiate ART for patient per current guidelines.<br /></br>   • Counsel on condom use and offer STI screening.  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>If patient refuses disclosure<br /></br>   • Explain legal/public-health implications.<br /></br>   • Contact local health department or partner-notification program (procedures vary by state).<br /></br>   • Directly inform partner only when mandated/permitted by law and after patient refusal.  </p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“C-D-E” steps for HIV partner risk:<br /></br>C – Counsel patient to disclose (first step)<br /></br>D – Department of health if patient refuses<br /></br>E – Exception: physician may directly inform partner only as last resort (Tarasoff-type)  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>a. Contact the patient’s previous physician for further information<br /></br>   • Not necessary for immediate risk mitigation; records already provided.<br /></br>   • Does not address partner safety or disclosure.  </p></br><p>b. Discontinue care until the patient tells his partner<br /></br>   • Abandons patient, violates duty of care, and may delay treatment/disclosure.<br /></br>   • Potentially increases risk to partner.  </p></br><p>c. Emphasize the importance of consistent use of condoms<br /></br>   • Important counseling point but insufficient alone; does not fulfill duty to urge disclosure.<br /></br>   • Condom use does not completely eliminate risk.  </p></br><p>e. Inform the partner of the patient’s HIV status<br /></br>   • Breaches confidentiality prematurely.<br /></br>   • Ethically and often legally permissible only after patient refuses disclosure and public-health notification measures have been pursued.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Take-home Message  </p></br><p>For serious communicable diseases such as HIV, the physician must first counsel and encourage the patient to inform at-risk partners. Breach of confidentiality is reserved for situations in which the patient refuses and the threat persists.</p>
114. A 37-year-old woman, gravida 3, para 2, at 24 weeks' gestation comes to the physician 4 weeks after noticing a lump in her left breast during self-examination. There is no family history of cancer. She currently takes no medications. Examination shows a 1 × 2-cm, unilateral, dominant mass in the left breast. Examination of the right breast shows tissue changes of pregnancy and no evidence of a mass. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Reexamination after delivery</br>b. MRI of the left breast after delivery</br>c. Tamoxifen therapy immediately</br>d. Biopsy of the mass after delivery</br>e. Fine-needle aspiration of the mass immediately</br>f. Recommendation of pregnancy termination</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>E. Fine-needle aspiration of the mass immediately  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues<br /></br>• 24 weeks’ gestation (2nd trimester) – still many months before delivery; delaying work-up could postpone a cancer diagnosis for ≥4 mo.<br /></br>• “Unilateral, dominant mass” – unlike the diffuse, symmetric nodularity/enlargement normally seen in pregnancy; a dominant, discrete lesion must be assumed malignant until proven otherwise.<br /></br>• No systemic symptoms and no family history – but breast cancer is the most common malignancy in pregnancy; physiologic changes do NOT rule it out.  </p></br><p>Cognitive framework<br /></br>Any new dominant breast mass → Triple assessment (clinical exam + imaging + tissue).<br /></br>In pregnancy: 1) Ultrasound (no radiation), 2) tissue sampling (FNA or core). Do NOT wait until postpartum.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (why “E” is correct)  </p></br><ol></br><li>Urgency: Cancer doubles in size every 1–3 mo; waiting until delivery (≈16 wk away) risks up-staging.  </li></br><li>Safety: Fine-needle aspiration (FNA) is quick, office-based, no ionizing radiation, no anesthesia risk, and is safe at any gestational age.  </li></br><li>Diagnostic value: Provides cytology (and, if core biopsy follows, receptor status) to guide timely surgery or chemotherapy that can be administered in the 2nd/3rd trimester if needed.  </li></br></ol></br><p>Take-home message: Pregnancy never excuses delaying work-up of a suspicious breast mass; immediate tissue diagnosis with a pregnancy-safe technique is mandatory.  </p></br><p>Documentation pearl: Record size, location, mobility, skin/nipple changes, lymph nodes, patient counseling, and consent for FNA.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• Pregnancy induces lobular-alveolar hyperplasia → breasts feel nodular, but true cancer presents as a discrete, unilateral, firm mass, often without pain.<br /></br>• Pregnancy-associated breast cancer behaves like non-gestational cancer but is often diagnosed later because physiologic changes mask findings; early tissue confirmation improves prognosis.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Algorithm  </p></br><ol></br><li>Imaging: Ultrasound (first line). Mammography with abdominal shielding if microcalcifications must be assessed. MRI with gadolinium is avoided in pregnancy.  </li></br><li>Tissue: FNA (cytology) or core biopsy (histology, receptors). Both are safe.  </li></br><li>If cancer confirmed:<br /></br>   • Surgery (lumpectomy or modified radical mastectomy) is safe in all trimesters.<br /></br>   • Anthracycline-based chemotherapy is permissible in 2nd/3rd trimesters.<br /></br>   • Radiation and endocrine therapy (tamoxifen) deferred until postpartum.  </li></br></ol></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>High-Yield Memory Aids  </p></br><p>“Pregnant + Palpable mass = Probe &amp; Poke NOW.”<br /></br>Probe (ultrasound), Poke (FNA/core).  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>A. Reexamination after delivery – Delays diagnosis; violates “assume malignancy until proven otherwise.”  </p></br><p>B. MRI of the left breast after delivery – Unnecessary delay; gadolinium crosses placenta and is avoided. Imaging never replaces tissue confirmation.  </p></br><p>C. Tamoxifen therapy immediately – Teratogenic (category D); also therapy should never start before establishing a pathologic diagnosis.  </p></br><p>D. Biopsy of the mass after delivery – Core or excisional biopsy is correct technique but timing is wrong; delaying &gt;16 wk risks progression.  </p></br><p>F. Recommend pregnancy termination – Cancer treatment (surgery ± chemotherapy) can be safely administered during pregnancy; termination is rarely indicated and certainly not before a diagnosis.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line: Any dominant breast mass in pregnancy warrants immediate tissue diagnosis with a safe modality (ultrasound-guided FNA or core biopsy). Never defer evaluation until postpartum.</p>
115. A previously healthy 47-year-old man is brought to the emergency department by his wife because of a 2-day history of increasing confusion. He thinks it is 1992 and often makes statements that do not make sense. He has not had recent weight loss or other symptoms. He has smoked two packs of cigarettes daily for 30 years. He is oriented to person and place but not to time. His temperature is 37°C (98.6°F), pulse is 80/min, respirations are 20/min, and blood pressure is 110/70 mm Hg. The remainder of the examination shows no abnormalities. Laboratory studies show a serum sodium concentration of 115 mEq/L, urine sodium concentration of 80 mEq/L, and urine osmolality of 600 mOsmol/kg H₂O. Which of the following is the most likely underlying cause of this patient’s confusion?	Correct answer: F</br></br>a. Adrenal insufficiency</br>b. Cerebral salt wasting</br>c. Diabetes insipidus</br>d. Ectopic adrenocorticotropic hormone production</br>e. Hypovolemic hyponatremia</br>f. Syndrome of inappropriate secretion of ADH (vasopressin)</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>f. Syndrome of inappropriate secretion of ADH (vasopressin) ― “SIADH”</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues that jump-start the diagnosis:<br /></br>• Severe hyponatremia: serum Na⁺ 115 mEq/L<br /></br>• Inappropriately concentrated urine for the degree of hyponatremia: urine Osm 600 mOsm/kg (normal kidney response would be &lt; 100 mOsm/kg when serum Na⁺ is this low)<br /></br>• High urine Na⁺ (80 mEq/L) despite normal BP and no clinical evidence of hypovolemia → kidneys are being told to dump Na⁺ even though body water is excessive → ADH-mediated state.<br /></br>• Euvolemic on physical exam (no edema, no orthostasis, normal mucous membranes).<br /></br>• 30-pack-year smoker → classic risk factor for small-cell lung carcinoma, the prototypic tumor that secretes ectopic ADH.</p></br><p>Pattern recognition framework for hyponatremia:</br>1. Check serum Osm (not provided, but with Na⁺ 115 it is almost certainly low → true hypotonic hyponatremia).<br /></br>2. Assess volume status.<br /></br>   • Hypovolemic: tachycardia, orthostasis, dry mucous membranes → not present.<br /></br>   • Hypervolemic: edema, CHF, cirrhosis, nephrotic syndrome → not present.<br /></br>   • Euvolemic → think SIADH, hypothyroidism, adrenal insufficiency, reset osmostat, psychogenic polydipsia.<br /></br>3. Look at urine Osm and Na⁺.<br /></br>   • Urine Osm &gt; 100 mOsm/kg = ADH is ON.<br /></br>   • Urine Na⁺ &gt; 40 mEq/L = renal Na⁺ wasting because volume status is normal or expanded (RAAS off).</p></br><p>Applying this algorithm pins SIADH as the best fit.</p></br><p>Metacognitive tip: When you see “low Na⁺ + high urine Osm + clinically euvolemic,” reflexively think “SIADH until proven otherwise,” then scan for lung cancer, CNS disease, drugs (SSRIs, carbamazepine), or pain.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Why SIADH causes confusion:<br /></br>• Excess ADH → water retention → dilutional hyponatremia → cerebral edema → altered mental status (confusion, seizures, coma).</p></br><p>Documentation points if this were a real patient:<br /></br>• Mental status exam, volume status exam, precipitating medications/ malignancy history.<br /></br>• Plan: fluid restriction, hypertonic saline if neuro symptoms worsen, search for small-cell carcinoma (CXR/CT chest).</p></br><p>Core takeaway: Euvolemic hyponatremia with inappropriately concentrated urine = SIADH.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review (high-yield)  </p></br><ol></br><li>Ectopic or inappropriate ADH release → ↑ aquaporin insertion in collecting ducts → free water reabsorption.  </li></br><li>Plasma dilution → ↓ serum Na⁺ and serum Osm.  </li></br><li>Slight volume expansion suppresses RAAS → kidneys excrete sodium (high urine Na⁺).  </li></br><li>Urine remains concentrated because ADH is persistently active.</li></br></ol></br><p>Common triggers:<br /></br>• Small-cell lung carcinoma (paraneoplastic)<br /></br>• CNS disturbances (stroke, hemorrhage, trauma)<br /></br>• Pulmonary pathology (pneumonia, TB)<br /></br>• Drugs (SSRIs, carbamazepine, cyclophosphamide, vincristine)<br /></br>• Post-operative pain/nausea.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Work-up  </p></br><p>Acute symptomatic (confusion, seizures):<br /></br>1. Hypertonic (3%) saline ± IV furosemide.<br /></br>2. Correct Na⁺ by no more than 8 – 10 mEq/L in 24 h to avoid osmotic demyelination syndrome.</p></br><p>Chronic or mild:<br /></br>• Fluid restriction (1 L/day) = first-line.<br /></br>• Salt tablets ± loop diuretic, oral urea.<br /></br>• Vasopressin receptor antagonists (tolvaptan, conivaptan) or demeclocycline if refractory.</p></br><p>Look for underlying cause: CXR/CT chest, review meds, TSH, cortisol.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“SIADH = Soaked Inside, Dry Hyperosmolar urine”<br /></br>• Soaked inside (euvolemic dilution).<br /></br>• High urine Osm (&gt; 100) and Na⁺ (&gt; 40).  </p></br><p>Smoke → Small-cell → SIADH. Picture a cigarette dripping with “water” (ADH) into the bloodstream.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>a. Adrenal insufficiency<br /></br>• Can produce euvolemic hyponatremia, but patients usually have hypotension, weight loss, hyperkalemia, ↑ ACTH, and ↓ cortisol. No such findings here.</p></br><p>b. Cerebral salt wasting<br /></br>• Occurs after significant intracranial pathology; causes hypovolemia (orthostatic hypotension, tachycardia). Urine Na⁺ high, but patient would be volume-depleted—not euvolemic.</p></br><p>c. Diabetes insipidus<br /></br>• Presents with hypernatremia and dilute urine (urine Osm &lt; 300 mOsm/kg). Opposite picture.</p></br><p>d. Ectopic ACTH production<br /></br>• Small-cell tumors can secrete ACTH, but that causes Cushing syndrome (hyperglycemia, hypertension, hypokalemic alkalosis), not hyponatremia.</p></br><p>e. Hypovolemic hyponatremia<br /></br>• Patients appear dehydrated; urine Na⁺ often &lt; 20 mEq/L if losses are extrarenal (vomiting, diarrhea) or &gt; 20 mEq/L if renal (diuretics). Vignette shows no signs of volume loss.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Bottom Line  </p></br><p>A euvolemic patient with severe hyponatremia, high urine osmolality, and high urine sodium— especially a long-term smoker—fits classic SIADH due to ectopic ADH from small-cell lung carcinoma.</p>
116. Forty-eight hours after spontaneous rupture of membranes, a 425-g (1-lb) newborn is delivered at 21 weeks' gestation to a 42-year-old primigravid woman. Her pregnancy had been uncomplicated, and her estimated date of delivery was confirmed by ultrasonography at 12 weeks' gestation. Chorionic villus sampling at 12 weeks' gestation showed a karyotype of 46,XY. The newborn's heart rate is 40/min, and a gasp is noted. On examination, the eyelids are fused, and the skin is transparent. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management of the newborn?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Palliative care</br>b. Inotropic therapy</br>c. Placement of a urinary catheter</br>d. Chest compressions</br>e. Intubation and mechanical ventilation</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>• Palliative care  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues that immediately flag a non-viable/extremely preterm infant:<br /></br>1. Gestational age = 21 weeks (confirmed by early ultrasound → dating is accurate).<br /></br>2. Birth weight = 425 g (well below the 500-g threshold usually achievable only ≥23–24 weeks).<br /></br>3. Physical exam: fused eyelids, transparent skin—classic morphologic signs of ≤22-week fetus.<br /></br>4. Heart rate 40/min with only an agonal gasp → profound bradycardia, minimal respiratory effort.  </p></br><p>Pertinent negatives: no mention of advanced neonatal intensive-care capabilities that could alter outcome; no anomalies incompatible with life that would change counseling.  </p></br><p>Pattern/algorithm:<br /></br>• Neonatal Resuscitation Program (NRP) &amp; American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists (ACOG) guidelines:<br /></br>  – &lt;22 0/7 weeks → resuscitation is NOT recommended; comfort (palliative) care.<br /></br>  – 22 0/7–23 6/7 weeks → individualized, shared decision-making.<br /></br>  – ≥24 weeks or &gt;1000 g → full resuscitation unless contraindicated.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (Why palliative care?)  </p></br><p>Step-wise thought process:<br /></br>1. Confirm gestational age accuracy (12-week US is gold standard).<br /></br>2. Check weight &amp; physical signs → both confirm extreme prematurity.<br /></br>3. Consult viability guidelines: infant is previable (&lt;22 weeks, &lt;500 g).<br /></br>4. Any aggressive intervention (intubation, compressions, inotropes) has essentially 0% chance of intact survival and exposes infant to futile, painful procedures.<br /></br>5. Ethically &amp; per national guidelines, provide warmth, analgesia, parental bonding → palliative care.  </p></br><p>Take-home concept: Below the accepted threshold of viability, the standard-of-care is comfort measures only.  </p></br><p>Documentation: gestational age confirmation, discussion of prognosis with parents, decision for comfort care, interventions withheld.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• Lung development: canalicular phase (16–25 weeks) → airways present but type II pneumocytes not yet producing enough surfactant; alveolar-capillary membranes too immature for gas exchange.<br /></br>• Cardiovascular &amp; CNS systems likewise underdeveloped; survival outside uterus impossible without profound morbidity.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Tests  </p></br><p>Not applicable beyond:<br /></br>• Warm delivery room, dry infant, skin-to-skin, sucrose/oral morphine for comfort, allow family presence.<br /></br>• No invasive lines, ventilation, or chest compressions.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“2-2-5 Rule for Viability”:<br /></br>• &lt;22 weeks OR &lt;500 g → 0 chance → comfort only.<br /></br>• 22–23 weeks → 50/50 conversation.<br /></br>• ≥24 weeks &amp; ≥600 g → Full resuscitation.  </p></br><p>Visual cue: “Fused eyelids = Futile resuscitation.”  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>b. Inotropic therapy<br /></br>• Requires IV access and relative cardiovascular maturity. Profound bradycardia here is secondary to immaturity/anoxia, not treatable shock. Futile at 21 weeks.  </p></br><p>c. Placement of a urinary catheter<br /></br>• Indicated for critically ill but viable neonates to monitor urine output or bladder obstruction. Adds no benefit, causes trauma in a previable infant.  </p></br><p>d. Chest compressions<br /></br>• NRP: start compressions only if HR &lt;60 after 30 s of effective PPV. However, PPV itself is not indicated in a non-viable (&lt;22 wk) infant. Thus compressions are inappropriate.  </p></br><p>e. Intubation and mechanical ventilation<br /></br>• Intubation is first advanced step for viable extremely preterm infants. At 21 weeks, lungs lack structural maturity and surfactant; ventilation cannot sustain life.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line  </p></br><p>A 21-week, 425-g neonate with fused eyelids is below the threshold of viability. The ethically and medically appropriate course is palliative (comfort) care.</p>
117. A 37-year-old man comes to the physician for a routine examination. He has no history of serious illness, has no known allergies, and takes no medications. His mother died of a brain neoplasm at the age of 60 years. His father is 86 years old and healthy. During the past 4 years, the patient has volunteered at a homeless shelter and has donated blood once every 2 months. He appears healthy. He is 178 cm (5 ft 10 in) tall and weighs 75 kg (165 lb); BMI is 24 kg/m^2. Vital signs are within normal limits. Examination shows no abnormalities. Laboratory studies show:</br>Hemoglobin    14.5 g/dL</br>Hematocrit    44 percent</br>Mean corpuscular hemoglobin concentration    30 percent Hb/cell</br>Mean corpuscular volume    90 µm^3</br>Leukocyte count    2600/mm^3</br>  Segmented neutrophils    60 percent</br>  Eosinophils    1 percent</br>  Lymphocytes    35 percent</br>  Monocytes    4 percent</br>Platelet count    250,000/mm^3</br>Results of fasting serum lipid studies are within the reference ranges. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Repeat complete blood count in 3 months</br>b. Serum antineutrophil cytoplasmic autoantibody assay</br>c. Serum bactericidal assay</br>d. Ultrasonography of the spleen</br>e. Bone marrow biopsy</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>a. Repeat complete blood count in 3 months  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues:<br /></br>• Completely asymptomatic 37-year-old presenting only for routine care.<br /></br>• Normal vital signs, normal physical exam, normal red cell indices and platelet count.<br /></br>• Isolated abnormality = total leukocyte count 2 600/mm³ (reference ≈ 4 000-11 000).<br /></br>• Absolute neutrophil count (ANC) ≈ 0.60 × 2 600 = 1 560/mm³ (mild neutropenia = 1 000-1 500).<br /></br>• No recurrent infections, medication exposure, radiation, systemic symptoms, lymph- node or splenic enlargement.  </p></br><p>Pattern/algorithm:<br /></br>Isolated, mild leukopenia (or neutropenia) discovered incidentally in a healthy patient → 1st step is to repeat the CBC in ~6-12 weeks to confirm persistence before initiating an etiologic work-up. Transient bone-marrow suppression from a subclinical viral illness or lab variation is common.  </p></br><p>Metacognitive approach:<br /></br>1. Ask: isolated vs pancytopenia? (isolated)<br /></br>2. Assess severity &amp; symptoms? (mild, asymptomatic)<br /></br>3. Confirm abnormality with repeat test. Only if persistent/progressive do you escalate to antibody studies, imaging, or bone-marrow biopsy.  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Why choice A is correct:<br /></br>• Mild, asymptomatic neutropenia is often transient (e.g., viral infection within past 2-4 weeks, benign ethnic neutropenia).<br /></br>• Guidelines (e.g., UpToDate “Approach to the adult with unexplained neutropenia”) recommend repeat CBC in 3 months (or sooner if clinically indicated) as the initial step.<br /></br>• In the interim, no intervention is required because ANC &gt;1 000 confers minimal infection risk.<br /></br>Take-home: Always verify an isolated lab abnormality before ordering invasive or costly tests.  </p></br><p>Documentation pearls if real patient:<br /></br>– Note current ANC, absence of infection/history, plan to re-check CBC in 3 months, and return precautions for fever.  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review – Neutropenia  </p></br><p>Etiologies (big picture):<br /></br>1. Decreased production (viral suppression, drug, marrow failure, B12/folate deficiency, congenital).<br /></br>2. Redistribution/sequestration (hypersplenism).<br /></br>3. Increased destruction (autoimmune).  </p></br><p>Transient viral-related marrow suppression is the most common cause of single low WBC in a healthy adult.  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Diagnostic &amp; Management Ladder  </p></br><p>Step 1: Verify abnormality (repeat CBC).<br /></br>Step 2: If persistent or worsening, add peripheral smear, infection panel (HIV, hepatitis), vitamin B12/folate, autoimmune markers, consider medication review.<br /></br>Step 3: If ANC ≤1 000, recurrent infections, or unexplained pancytopenia → bone marrow biopsy &amp; imaging.  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tip  </p></br><p>“Low WBC? First, CBC!”<br /></br>(If the patient feels OK, repeat in 1-3 months before doing anything invasive.)  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>b. Serum ANCA assay – Ordered when you suspect ANCA-associated vasculitis (sinusitis, pulmonary-renal syndrome). No systemic symptoms or organ involvement present.  </p></br><p>c. Serum bactericidal assay – Checks complement function in patients with recurrent Neisseria infections. Our patient has no infection history.  </p></br><p>d. Ultrasonography of the spleen – Hypersplenism causes cytopenias but usually presents with palpable splenomegaly and often affects platelets as well. Exam is normal and platelets are normal.  </p></br><p>e. Bone marrow biopsy – Indicated for unexplained severe neutropenia (ANC &lt;500), pancytopenia, blasts on smear, or if repeat CBC confirms persistent abnormality. Too invasive as an initial step here.  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Core Takeaway  </p></br><p>For an incidental, mild, isolated leukopenia/neutropenia in a healthy, asymptomatic adult, the most appropriate first step is simply to repeat the complete blood count in a few months to confirm persistence before initiating an extensive work-up.</p>
118. A 62-year-old man is brought to the emergency department 30 minutes after falling while putting away groceries. The patient reports that he became dizzy before he fell. He has had increasing episodes of dizziness during the past 3 months. He has Parkinson disease. His only medication is carbidopa–levodopa. He appears frail but is not in acute distress. His pulse is 74/min, and blood pressure is 122/70 mm Hg while seated; his pulse is 88/min, and blood pressure is 96/60 mm Hg while standing. When asked to stand, the patient becomes dizzy. Examination shows no other abnormalities. Volume repletion with 0.9 percent saline is begun. Two hours later, the patient's blood pressure is 128/80 mm Hg while seated; he continues to have dizziness upon standing. Oral fludrocortisone therapy is begun. Repeat laboratory studies 3 days later are most likely to show which of the following findings?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Decreased serum potassium concentration</br>b. Decreased serum sodium concentration</br>c. Decreased urine potassium concentration</br>d. Increased plasma renin activity</br>e. Increased serum urea nitrogen concentration</br>f. Increased urine sodium concentration</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>A. Decreased serum potassium concentration  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues:<br /></br>• Elderly man with Parkinson disease develops reproducible orthostatic hypotension (seated BP 122/70 → standing 96/60 mm Hg with dizziness).<br /></br>• No evidence of acute blood loss; volume challenge with normal saline does not correct the orthostasis → points toward neurogenic (autonomic) orthostatic hypotension, common in Parkinson disease.<br /></br>• Treatment started: oral fludrocortisone, a potent synthetic mineralocorticoid used to expand intravascular volume in autonomic failure.  </p></br><p>Test-taking framework:<br /></br>1. Identify orthostatic hypotension.<br /></br>2. Decide whether it is hypovolemic vs autonomic. Normal saline fails → autonomic.<br /></br>3. Recognize that fludrocortisone’s mineralocorticoid activity will raise BP by retaining Na⁺/water and excreting K⁺.<br /></br>4. Predict lab change that best reflects that mechanism after several days: ↓serum K⁺.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Fludrocortisone → binds mineralocorticoid receptors in the distal nephron →<br /></br>• ↑ ENaC &amp; Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase expression<br /></br>• ↑ Na⁺ and water reabsorption → ↑ plasma volume<br /></br>• ↑ K⁺ and H⁺ secretion → hypokalemia &amp; metabolic alkalosis<br /></br>• Volume expansion suppresses renin &amp; aldosterone.  </p></br><p>Thus the most likely new laboratory abnormality after 3 days is hypokalemia (decreased serum potassium).  </p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real patient): orthostatic vitals, dizziness, Parkinson disease, failure of IV fluids, start fludrocortisone 0.1 mg PO daily, monitor BMP for K⁺/Na⁺, reassess BP lying &amp; standing.  </p></br><p>Core takeaway: Any mineralocorticoid (fludrocortisone, aldosterone) causes Na⁺ retention and K⁺ wasting → watch for low serum K⁺.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>Autonomic (neurogenic) orthostatic hypotension<br /></br>• Etiology: α-synuclein deposition in autonomic nuclei (Parkinson), multiple system atrophy, diabetic autonomic neuropathy, etc.<br /></br>• Mechanism: impaired baroreflex → inadequate vasoconstriction &amp; HR rise on standing.  </p></br><p>Fludrocortisone<br /></br>• Potency: very strong mineralocorticoid, minimal glucocorticoid effect in low doses.<br /></br>• Actions: mimics aldosterone at the cortical collecting duct.<br /></br>• Adverse effects: hypokalemia, hypernatremia, edema, hypertension, CHF exacerbation, metabolic alkalosis, suppressed renin/aldosterone.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatments &amp; Diagnostics Quick Review  </p></br><p>First-line for neurogenic orthostasis:<br /></br>1. Non-pharm: ↑ salt &amp; water intake, compression stockings, slow position changes.<br /></br>2. Meds:<br /></br>   • Fludrocortisone (volume expansion)<br /></br>   • Midodrine (α1-agonist vasoconstrictor)<br /></br>   • Droxidopa, pyridostigmine in some cases  </p></br><p>Monitoring while on fludrocortisone: BMP (K⁺, Na⁺), BP supine &amp; standing, signs of fluid overload.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“Fludro FLOODs you with fluid but FLUSHES out K⁺.”<br /></br>OR<br /></br>A-L-D-O:<br /></br>• A = Adds Na⁺<br /></br>• L = Loses K⁺<br /></br>• D = Drops renin<br /></br>• O = “Overfills” intravascular space  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>B. Decreased serum sodium: opposite of mineralocorticoid effect; Na⁺ increases or stays normal.<br /></br>C. Decreased urine potassium: mineralocorticoids increase K⁺ secretion → urine K⁺ rises, not falls.<br /></br>D. Increased plasma renin activity: volume expansion suppresses renin via negative feedback.<br /></br>E. Increased serum BUN: volume expansion “dilutes” BUN and improves renal perfusion → BUN tends to fall or stay normal.<br /></br>F. Increased urine sodium: Na⁺ is reabsorbed, so urinary Na⁺ falls.  </p></br><p>Remember: think through the drug’s mechanism, then predict which lab value must logically change in that direction.</p>
119. A 27-year-old woman comes to the physician because of a 2-year history of irregular menstrual periods, acne, and increased facial hair growth. Previously, menses occurred at regular 28- to 35-day intervals and lasted 4 days. Menses now occur at 45- to 90-day intervals and last up to 10 days. She is otherwise asymptomatic. She is 162 cm (5 ft 4 in) tall and weighs 55 kg (122 lb); BMI is 21 kg/m2. Physical examination shows acne vulgaris and mild hirsutism over the upper lip, chin, breasts, and upper back. Pelvic examination shows no abnormalities. Measurement of which of the following is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Serum ACTH concentration</br>b. Serum catecholamine concentrations</br>c. Serum estrogen concentration</br>d. Serum 17α-hydroxyprogesterone concentration</br>e. Urine electrolyte concentrations</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>d. Serum 17α-hydroxyprogesterone concentration</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues<br /></br>• Woman, 27 yo, gradually developing:<br /></br>  – Oligo/amenorrhea (45- to 90-day cycles)<br /></br>  – Hirsutism (upper lip, chin, breasts, back)<br /></br>  – Acne<br /></br>• Normal BMI (21 kg/m²) – not the typical obese phenotype of PCOS<br /></br>• Onset after menarche, progressive over ≈ 2 years<br /></br>• Normal pelvic exam (no virilized external genitalia, no adnexal mass)</p></br><p>Pertinent negatives<br /></br>• No rapid-onset virilization → androgen-secreting tumor unlikely<br /></br>• No Cushingoid features → Cushing syndrome less likely<br /></br>• No electrolyte disturbances or salt-wasting symptoms → classic CAH unlikely</p></br><p>Cognitive framework<br /></br>When evaluating hirsutism/oligomenorrhea:<br /></br>1. Confirm androgen excess (clinical signs present)<br /></br>2. Rule out virilizing tumors (rapid, severe, ↑DHEAS or testosterone)<br /></br>3. Consider the three common chronic causes:<br /></br>   A. Polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS)<br /></br>   B. Nonclassic congenital adrenal hyperplasia (NCAH)<br /></br>   C. Idiopathic (rare)<br /></br>4. Screen for NCAH with early-morning 17-hydroxyprogesterone (17-OHP).</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>• Lean patient with new gyne-androgenic symptoms → NCAH rises higher on the differential than PCOS.<br /></br>• The first-line screening test for NCAH (21-hydroxylase deficiency) is a baseline 17-OHP. If elevated, confirm with an ACTH stimulation test.<br /></br>Take-home message: In reproductive-age women with hirsutism and menstrual irregularity but without classic PCOS phenotype, measure serum 17-OHP to look for nonclassic 21-hydroxylase deficiency.</p></br><p>Documentation pearl: Record menstrual pattern, degree/location of hirsutism (Ferriman-Gallwey score), BMI, and plan for 17-OHP testing +/- endocrinology referral.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review – Nonclassic CAH  </p></br><p>• Partial 21-hydroxylase deficiency → impaired cortisol synthesis → ACTH up-regulation → adrenal hyperplasia → shunting of precursors to androgen pathway.<br /></br>• Cortisol and aldosterone levels are often normal; therefore, patients do not have salt-wasting or adrenal crisis.<br /></br>• Presents at puberty/early adulthood with hirsutism, acne, oligomenorrhea/infertility.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Diagnostics &amp; Management Snapshot  </p></br><p>Diagnostic sequence:<br /></br>1. Early-morning 17-OHP: ↑ (&gt;200 ng/dL).<br /></br>2. ACTH stimulation test: exaggerated rise in 17-OHP confirms.<br /></br>3. If confirmed, treat with low-dose glucocorticoids (e.g., hydrocortisone or dexamethasone) ± OCPs or anti-androgens for symptoms.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Mnemonic  </p></br><p>“17-OHP at 17 years.”<br /></br>When a teen/young adult shows androgen excess and cycle problems, think 17-OHP for late-onset CAH.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Incorrect Answer Rundown  </p></br><p>a. Serum ACTH concentration<br /></br>   – Useful for primary vs secondary adrenal insufficiency or Cushing disease, not a first step for hirsutism work-up.</p></br><p>b. Serum catecholamine concentrations<br /></br>   – Screens for pheochromocytoma (episodic hypertension, headaches, diaphoresis), unrelated to menstrual dysfunction/hirsutism.</p></br><p>c. Serum estrogen concentration<br /></br>   – Fluctuates widely across the cycle; not helpful for diagnosing the cause of androgen excess.</p></br><p>e. Urine electrolyte concentrations<br /></br>   – Ordered when suspecting mineralocorticoid problems (e.g., hyperaldosteronism) or classic salt-wasting CAH; patient lacks hypertension or electrolyte abnormalities.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Bottom Line  </p></br><p>Lean woman + gradual hirsutism + menstrual irregularity → screen for nonclassic 21-hydroxylase deficiency with a serum 17α-hydroxyprogesterone level.</p>
120. A 32-year-old woman comes to the emergency department because of a 30-minute history of chest tightness, shortness of breath, and tremulousness that began after she heard of a coworker's unexpected death. She has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. She does not drink alcohol or use illicit drugs. Her pulse is 110/min, respirations are 24/min, and blood pressure is 132/78 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on room air shows an oxygen saturation of 99 percent. Examination shows no abnormalities. An ECG shows sinus tachycardia. The physician orders intravenous administration of lorazepam, but the nurse administers intravenous diazepam. Twenty minutes later, the patient's symptoms have not improved. The physician notes that diazepam rather than lorazepam was administered. This incident should be reported to the hospital's quality assurance committee as which of the following?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. Error by the physician</br>b. Malpractice</br>c. Medication error</br>d. Sentinel event</br>e. This incident does not need to be reported</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>c. Medication error  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis &amp; Test‐Taking Framework<br /></br>1. “The physician orders intravenous lorazepam, but the nurse administers intravenous diazepam.”<br /></br>   • Wrong drug given ≙ wrong-medication event.<br /></br>2. “Twenty minutes later, the patient’s symptoms have not improved.”<br /></br>   • No mention of death, permanent disability, or other serious harm—only lack of therapeutic effect.<br /></br>3. “This incident should be reported to the hospital’s quality-assurance committee …”<br /></br>   • The stem is testing your knowledge of quality-improvement terminology (medication error vs sentinel event vs malpractice, etc.).<br /></br>Approach tip: Whenever a vignette describes ANY discrepancy between the ordered drug/dose/route/time and what was administered, immediately think “medication error,” even if the drugs are in the same class and even if no harm occurs.</p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>• Definition (Joint Commission &amp; NCC MERP): A medication error is any preventable event that may cause or lead to inappropriate medication use or patient harm while the medication is in the control of the health-care professional, patient, or consumer.<br /></br>• Wrong-drug errors are classic examples and are mandatorily reportable to the institution’s quality-improvement (QI) or risk-management system so that root-cause analysis (RCA) and process fixes can be implemented.<br /></br>Take-home message: All wrong-drug, wrong-dose, wrong-route, or wrong-time events are medication errors and must be reported, regardless of outcome.</p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (brief)<br /></br>Not directly tested here, but remember: Both lorazepam and diazepam are benzodiazepines that enhance GABA-A receptor activity. They differ in onset, duration, and active metabolites—important for acute panic vs chronic anxiety management—but giving the wrong one still constitutes a medication error.</p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>a. Error by the physician<br /></br>   • The physician ordered the correct drug (lorazepam). The error occurred in administration, not ordering.<br /></br>b. Malpractice<br /></br>   • Malpractice requires 4 D’s (Duty, Dereliction, Damage, Direct causation). No patient harm (“Damage”) is documented, so malpractice is not met.<br /></br>d. Sentinel event<br /></br>   • Defined as an unexpected event resulting in death, permanent severe injury, or severe temporary harm. Merely missing the intended therapeutic effect without serious harm is NOT sentinel.<br /></br>e. This incident does not need to be reported<br /></br>   • All medication errors must be reported for safety monitoring and process improvement, even if no harm occurred.</p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“Five Rights” of medication administration (Right patient, drug, dose, route, time). Violate any = medication error.<br /></br>Mnemonic for reportable events: “MEDS”<br /></br>M – Medication errors<br /></br>E – Equipment failures<br /></br>D – Diagnostic or procedural errors<br /></br>S – Sentinel events (death/serious harm)  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>What to Document in the Chart<br /></br>• Exact medication ordered vs given, dose, route, time.<br /></br>• Patient’s clinical status before and after.<br /></br>• Notifications: treating physician, charge nurse, pharmacy, and initiation of institutional incident report.  </p></br><p>Core concept: Wrong-drug administration without serious harm = reportable medication error, not malpractice or sentinel event.</p>
121. A 27-year-old woman comes to the emergency department because of a 1-week history of diarrhea. She has bipolar disorder treated with medication, but she cannot remember the name of the drug. Two weeks ago, she was feeling depressed and increased the dose she normally takes. She does not drink alcohol or use illicit drugs. Her temperature is 37°C (98.6°F), pulse is 72/min, respirations are 16/min, and blood pressure is 125/75 mm Hg. Physical examination shows a fine resting tremor of the hands. On mental status examination, she is oriented to person but not to place, date, or time. She makes several errors performing serial sevens and is unable to repeat seven digits forward and five in reverse sequence. Which of the following medications is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. Carbamazepine</br>b. Lamotrigine</br>c. Lithium carbonate</br>d. Topiramate</br>e. Valproic acid</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>c. Lithium carbonate  </p></br><p>───────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Key clues<br /></br>   • Known bipolar disorder → almost certainly on a mood-stabilizer.<br /></br>   • “Increased the dose” 2 weeks ago → sets up a dose-dependent toxicity.<br /></br>   • 1-week watery diarrhea + fine resting tremor + confusion/poor attention (can’t repeat digits, serial 7s, disoriented).<br /></br>   • Vitals are normal; no focal neuro deficits.  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Pertinent positives/negatives<br /></br>   ✓ GI upset (nausea/diarrhea) is the earliest manifestation of lithium toxicity.<br /></br>   ✓ Fine tremor is the classic, most common lithium side-effect; progresses to coarse tremor, ataxia, and seizures with higher levels.<br /></br>   ✓ Cognitive slowing and confusion occur with chronic or sub-acute toxicity.<br /></br>   ✗ No skin rash (lamotrigine, carbamazepine SJS), no ataxia/diplopia (carbamazepine), no weight loss/kidney stones (topiramate), no hepatotoxicity (valproate).  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Test-taking framework<br /></br>   “Bipolar + tremor ± GI or neuro Sx = think lithium first; check if dose or renal clearance recently changed.”  </p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>───────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (why lithium)  </p></br><p>Step 1: Identify the drug class possibilities (mood stabilizers).<br /></br>Step 2: Match side-effect profile:<br /></br>   – Lithium = GI upset, fine tremor, nephrogenic DI, thyroid dysfunction, neurocognitive changes.<br /></br>Step 3: Look for a precipitant: the patient self-increased dose.<br /></br>Step 4: Rule out distractors by their unique toxidromes (see below).<br /></br>Take-home: Lithium has a very narrow therapeutic window (0.6–1.2 mEq/L). Even modest dose increases can cause toxicity.  </p></br><p>Documentation (real life): record mental status changes, GI symptoms, medication history, serum lithium level, basic metabolic panel, start IV fluids, consider nephrology if level ≥4 mEq/L or severe neuro findings.  </p></br><p>───────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• Renal excretion; behaves like sodium—anything that decreases GFR or Na⁺ reabsorption (NSAIDs, ACE-I, thiazides, dehydration) raises lithium level.<br /></br>• Toxicity continuum:<br /></br>   1. Acute ingestion → GI (N/V/D).<br /></br>   2. Chronic / subacute → neurologic (tremor, confusion, myoclonus, seizures).<br /></br>• Mechanism unclear; proposed inhibition of inositol monophosphatase → altered second-messenger signaling.  </p></br><p>───────────────────────<br /></br>Management Pearls  </p></br><ol></br><li>Check serum lithium level, BMP (for Cr and Na⁺).  </li></br><li>Stop lithium; aggressive isotonic saline.  </li></br><li>Hemodialysis if level ≥4 mEq/L, or ≥2.5 with severe neuro signs/renal failure.  </li></br></ol></br><p>───────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“LiTHIUM”<br /></br>• L = Low Thyroid &amp; Tremor<br /></br>• i = Increases Urine (nephrogenic DI)<br /></br>• T = Teratogen (Ebstein anomaly)<br /></br>• H = Heart conduction changes<br /></br>• I/U = Inverted T-waves? (EKG) / Use Narrow therapeutic window<br /></br>• M = Movement (tremor), Mental fog, GI Muddle  </p></br><p>Visual: picture a “lit” battery shaking (tremor) and leaking fluid (diarrhea) after you turned up the voltage (dose increase).  </p></br><p>───────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong  </p></br><p>a. Carbamazepine<br /></br>   – Side effects: diplopia, ataxia, agranulocytosis, SIADH (hyponatremia), dermatologic rash (SJS). Does not typically cause isolated fine tremor + diarrhea.  </p></br><p>b. Lamotrigine<br /></br>   – Risk of life-threatening rash (SJS/TEN) and aseptic meningitis. GI upset and tremor are not characteristic.  </p></br><p>d. Topiramate<br /></br>   – Cognitive slowing (“Dopiramate”), weight loss, kidney stones, paresthesias, angle-closure glaucoma. Diarrhea and fine tremor uncommon.  </p></br><p>e. Valproic acid<br /></br>   – GI upset and tremor can occur, but more prominent toxicities are hepatotoxicity, pancreatitis, alopecia, weight gain, neural tube defects. Usually causes coarse rather than fine tremor and not classically associated with diarrhea + acute mental status change in the setting of a dose increase.  </p></br><p>───────────────────────<br /></br>Core Takeaway  </p></br><p>In any bipolar patient who develops GI distress, fine tremor, and new cognitive impairment—especially after a dose change or renal insult—think lithium toxicity first.</p>
122. A 19-year-old man comes to the physician because of an 8-hour history of headache, stiff neck, and fever. His brother and sister died of a "brain infection." The patient's temperature is 39.4°C (103°F). He appears lethargic. Examination shows nuchal rigidity. Cerebrospinal fluid analysis shows a leukocyte count of 5000/mm^3, a glucose concentration of 10 mg/dL, and a protein concentration of 140 mg/dL. Treatment with ceftriaxone is started, and his symptoms improve. Gram stain shows gram-negative cocci. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. Measurement of serum IgA concentration</br>b. Measurement of leukocyte myeloperoxidase activity</br>c. Serum complement assays</br>d. Neutrophil adhesion studies</br>e. Neutrophil chemotaxis studies</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer (per key):  c. Serum complement assays  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Patient profile: 19-year-old previously healthy male.<br /></br>• Presentation: acute bacterial meningitis (fever 103 °F, headache, nuchal rigidity).<br /></br>• CSF profile: very high neutrophils (5000/µL), very low glucose (10 mg/dL), high protein (140 mg/dL) → classic for purulent meningitis.<br /></br>• Gram stain: gram-negative diplococci → Neisseria meningitidis.<br /></br>• Family history: both brother and sister died of a “brain infection” → powerful clue for an inherited susceptibility to meningococcal disease.<br /></br>Key takeaway: A young patient + meningococcal meningitis + lethal sib infections strongly suggests an inherited complement (C5-C9) deficiency.  </p></br><p>Metacognitive framework for similar NBME questions:<br /></br>1. Identify organism. 2. Ask “why is THIS patient getting THIS infection?” 3. If recurrent/severe Neisseria → think terminal complement deficiency. 4. Order a total hemolytic complement level (CH50) or individual C5-C9 assays.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Neisseria is uniquely susceptible to killing by the membrane-attack complex (MAC) of complement (C5b–C9). Deficiency of any terminal component → &gt;7000-fold increased risk for recurrent, often fatal, meningococcal infections. Therefore, once meningococcal disease is diagnosed—especially with a striking family history—the next diagnostic step is to measure complement activity (CH50) or individual C5-C9 levels.  </p></br><p>Documentation points (real world):<br /></br>• Past/family history of invasive meningococcal disease.<br /></br>• Order “CH50; if low, reflex C5, C6, C7, C8, C9 levels.”<br /></br>• Counsel on vaccine prophylaxis and antibiotic prophylaxis for future exposure.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Terminal complement pathway (C5b-C9) forms MAC pores → lysis of Neisseria spp.<br /></br>• Autosomal-recessive deficiencies result in normal immunity to most pathogens but markedly impaired clearance of Neisseria.<br /></br>• Patients typically present in adolescence with first episode; siblings often similarly affected.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Tests<br /></br>1. Acute episode: high-dose IV ceftriaxone ± vancomycin, dexamethasone as indicated.<br /></br>2. Diagnostic confirmation: CH50 (screens entire classic pathway). If CH50 is absent/low, order individual C5-C9 levels.<br /></br>3. Long-term management:<br /></br>   • MenACWY and MenB vaccines (boosters q5 y).<br /></br>   • Consider chronic meningococcal prophylaxis (e.g., oral penicillin, macrolide if allergic).<br /></br>   • Education: seek immediate care for fever.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“Neisseria Needs the MAC.”<br /></br>If N. meningitidis keeps coming back → Check the MAC (complement C5-C9).<br /></br>Visual: Imagine a knight (MAC) shielding a young person from a crowd of Neisseria diplococci; broken shield in multiple siblings → recurrent meningitis.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Options Are Incorrect<br /></br>a. Serum IgA measurement<br /></br>   • IgA deficiency causes recurrent sinopulmonary/GI infections and anaphylaxis to blood products, not invasive meningococcal meningitis specifically.  </p></br><p>b. Leukocyte myeloperoxidase (MPO) activity<br /></br>   • MPO deficiency predisposes mostly to Candida and some bacterial infections, not a classic risk factor for Neisseria.  </p></br><p>d. Neutrophil adhesion studies (evaluate for Leukocyte Adhesion Deficiency)<br /></br>   • LAD → recurrent skin/umbilical infections, delayed cord separation, high neutrophil counts, NO pus; not linked to isolated meningococcal disease.  </p></br><p>e. Neutrophil chemotaxis studies (e.g., Chediak-Higashi, Job syndrome)<br /></br>   • Would present with albinism, peripheral neuropathy, “cold” abscesses, or retained primary teeth—none seen here.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Core Takeaway<br /></br>Recurrent or familial Neisseria meningitidis infections = think terminal complement (C5-C9) deficiency; confirm with serum complement assays (CH50).</p>
123. A 15-month-old girl is brought to the physician because of a 1-week history of symptoms of an upper respiratory tract infection. Her temperature is 38°C (100.4°F). On examination, she is alert and turns her head appropriately in response to sounds. The left external ear and canal appear normal. The left tympanic membrane is shown. There is minimal movement on pneumatic otoscopy. The right external ear, ear canal, and tympanic membrane appear normal. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Acute otitis media</br>b. Cholesteatoma</br>c. Mastoiditis</br>d. Retraction pocket of the tympanic membrane</br>e. Serous otitis media</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>a. Acute otitis media (AOM)  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Age &amp; setting: 15-month-old (peak age for AOM), 1-week viral URI history → classic precursor that inflames the eustachian tube and traps middle‐ear fluid.<br /></br>• Fever 38 °C → points toward an acute infectious process rather than a sterile effusion.<br /></br>• Otoscopy: photo (not shown here) in the exam displays an erythematous, opaque, bulging tympanic membrane.<br /></br>• Pneumatic otoscopy: “minimal movement” (decreased compliance) is the single most sensitive physical-exam sign of middle-ear effusion; when paired with bulging/erythema, it clinches AOM.<br /></br>Pertinent negatives: no post-auricular swelling, no mastoid tenderness, no chronic white debris, no mention of retraction—all of which argue against the distractors.</p></br><p>Metacognitive tip: For any ear question start with three quick questions—1) Is there middle-ear fluid (↓ mobility)? 2) Is there an inflammatory picture (bulging, erythema, fever, pain)? 3) Any complications (mastoid swelling, cranial nerve findings)? This “Fluid → Inflammation → Complication” ladder sorts AOM, OME (serous), cholesteatoma, and mastoiditis within seconds.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (Why AOM?)<br /></br>Step 1: URI → eustachian-tube edema.<br /></br>Step 2: Negative middle-ear pressure draws in or traps secretions → effusion.<br /></br>Step 3: Bacterial invasion (Streptococcus pneumoniae, nontypeable Haemophilus influenzae, Moraxella catarrhalis) → acute inflammation: bulging TM, fever, otalgia, ↓ hearing, and ↓ mobility.<br /></br>Take-home: Bulging ± erythema + systemic signs = treat as AOM, not merely observe effusion.</p></br><p>Documentation pearl: “Left TM erythematous, bulging, landmarks obscured, decreased mobility with insufflation—consistent with acute otitis media.”</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Eustachian-tube dysfunction (short, horizontal tube in infants) predisposes to fluid stasis.<br /></br>• Viral URI fosters bacterial superinfection.<br /></br>• Middle-ear cavity fills with purulent exudate → pressure on TM (pain) and ossicles (conductive hearing loss).</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Diagnostics &amp; Treatment<br /></br>Diagnosis is clinical; tympanocentesis rarely needed.<br /></br>First-line Rx: High-dose amoxicillin (80–90 mg/kg/day divided BID) x 5–10 d.<br /></br>If penicillin allergy (non-anaphylactic): oral cefdinir; if anaphylaxis: azithromycin.<br /></br>Recheck or give amoxicillin-clavulanate if no improvement in 48-72 h.<br /></br>Tympanostomy tubes considered for ≥3 episodes in 6 mo or ≥4 in 12 mo.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Memory Aids<br /></br>“Bulging AOM is BAD”<br /></br>B – Bulging TM<br /></br>A – Acute symptoms (fever, pain)<br /></br>D – Decreased mobility  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong  </p></br><p>b. Cholesteatoma<br /></br>• Keratinizing squamous epithelium collection; appears as pearly white mass behind TM, often with chronic otorrhea and conductive hearing loss—not acute fever/URI findings.  </p></br><p>c. Mastoiditis<br /></br>• Post-auricular swelling, erythema, ear protrusion, tenderness; usually follows untreated AOM by days-weeks. None of these signs are present.  </p></br><p>d. Retraction pocket of the tympanic membrane<br /></br>• Negative middle-ear pressure in chronic eustachian-tube dysfunction; TM appears sucked in, not bulging; no acute systemic symptoms.  </p></br><p>e. Serous otitis media (Otitis media with effusion, OME)<br /></br>• Middle-ear effusion WITHOUT acute inflammation: translucent/amber TM, possible air-fluid levels, NO fever or marked erythema; mobility decreased but membrane not bulging. Presence of fever and bulging rules this out.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Key Takeaway<br /></br>Combining decreased mobility (effusion) with bulging/erythema and systemic signs (fever, pain) distinguishes acute otitis media from a simple effusion and from chronic ear diseases.</p>
124. A 42-year-old woman comes to the physician for a follow-up examination 6 weeks after undergoing in vitro fertilization for male factor infertility. She has no history of serious illness. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. Pelvic examination shows vaginal atrophy and a small amount of discharge tinged with blood and mucus in the posterior vaginal vault. Bimanual examination shows no cervical motion tenderness. The uterus is midline, smooth, nontender, and consistent in size with a 10-week gestation. There is fullness and mild tenderness at the left adnexa. Endovaginal ultrasonography shows a twin gestation with one chorion and one amniotic sac; there are two separate embryos, each with a strong heartbeat. This patient's fetuses are at greatest risk for which of the following?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Akinesia sequence</br>b. Amniotic band syndrome</br>c. Choroid plexus cyst</br>d. Macrosomia</br>e. Umbilical cord entanglement</br></br></br><p>Correct answer: E. Umbilical cord entanglement  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• “One chorion and one amniotic sac” = monochorionic-monoamniotic (MC-MA) twins.<br /></br>• MC-MA twins share both placenta and amniotic cavity → no dividing membrane between the two fetuses.<br /></br>• Absence of a separating membrane allows the free-floating umbilical cords to wrap around each other or the fetuses themselves.<br /></br>• Key negations: no mention of hydramnios discrepancy (twin-twin transfusion is MC-diamnio­tic), no congenital anomalies or maternal diabetes.  </p></br><p>Mnemonic to ID chorionicity:<br /></br>• λ (lambda) sign on early US = dichorionic.<br /></br>• T-sign = monochorionic.<br /></br>When you see only ONE amniotic sac ➜ monoamniotic.  </p></br><p>Cognitive framework for similar questions:<br /></br>1. Read ultrasound description first; identify number of chorions (placentas) and amniotic sacs.<br /></br>2. Recall risks unique to each twin type:<br /></br>   – Dizygotic (DC-DA): prematurity, IUGR.<br /></br>   – MC-DA: twin-twin transfusion.<br /></br>   – MC-MA: cord entanglement, conjoined twins.<br /></br>3. Match answer choices accordingly.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Why E is correct:<br /></br>• MC-MA twins lack an inter-twin membrane. Up to 70% have some degree of cord entanglement; ~10–15% develop lethal occlusion (fetal demise, neurologic injury).<br /></br>• Standard management: Inpatient monitoring with daily non-stress tests from 26–28 wk GA; planned C-section at 32–34 wk before entanglement complications arise.  </p></br><p>Documentation pearls if real patient:<br /></br>– Chorionicity/amnionicity confirmed on US.<br /></br>– Counsel patient on increased monitoring, hospitalization timeline, and preterm delivery plan.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Twinning occurs at different days post-fertilization:<br /></br>   – 0–3 d: DC-DA<br /></br>   – 4–8 d: MC-DA<br /></br>   – 8–12 d: MC-MA<br /></br>   – &gt;13 d: Conjoined<br /></br>• In MC-MA twins, two cords occupy one cavity; fetal movements → twisting, knotting, compression. This compromises blood flow → hypoxia, fetal death.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>A. Akinesia sequence (Potter sequence)<br /></br>   – Caused by prolonged oligohydramnios (e.g., bilateral renal agenesis); not specifically linked to monochorionicity or IVF.  </p></br><p>B. Amniotic band syndrome<br /></br>   – Results from rupture of the amnion creating fibrous strands that constrict limbs/digits. Occurs sporadically, not due to shared sac.  </p></br><p>C. Choroid plexus cyst<br /></br>   – Benign variant or associated with trisomy 18; not increased by twin type.  </p></br><p>D. Macrosomia<br /></br>   – Seen with maternal diabetes, prolonged gestation, or Beckwith-Wiedemann; IVF twins, especially MC-MA, are typically growth-restricted rather than macrosomic.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“Two share both → They can tie a knot.”<br /></br>• Share placenta (monoCHORionic).<br /></br>• Share sac (monoAMNiotic).<br /></br>→ Risk = “Tied” umbilical cords.  </p></br><p>OR<br /></br>“MA-MA: ‘Ma, Ma, the cords are tangled!’”  </p></br><p>Keep a mental table of twin risks using the phrase:<br /></br>“DiDi–premie; MonoDi–transfuse; MonoMono–entangle; Conjoined–late split.”  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Take-home Message<br /></br>Monochorionic-monoamniotic twins have the highest risk of umbilical cord entanglement because the fetuses share both the placenta and the amniotic cavity with no separating membrane.</p>
125. A 12-year-old girl is brought to the physician because of pain in the right thigh; the pain has been present for 2 months but has become markedly worse during the past 10 days. She also has had a low-grade fever for 1 week. Her temperature is 37.2°C (99°F). Examination of the right lower extremity shows tenderness in the middle of the thigh. The remainder of the examination shows no abnormalities. Her leukocyte count is 10,000/mm3. An x-ray of the right femur is shown. Without treatment, which of the following is most consistent with the natural history of this patient's disorder?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Arterial occlusion</br>b. Lung metastases</br>c. Neurologic deficit in the extremity</br>d. Spontaneous resolution</br>e. Venous occlusion</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>B. Lung metastases  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Key clues<br /></br>   • Age 12 yr → primary malignant bone tumors of childhood/adolescence (osteosarcoma, Ewing sarcoma) are highest on the differential.<br /></br>   • Progressive deep thigh pain for weeks, now worse → neoplastic bone pain, not transient musculoskeletal strain.<br /></br>   • Low-grade fever + normal/only mildly elevated WBC (10 000/mm³) → “tumor fever” often seen with Ewing sarcoma. Infection such as osteomyelitis tends to give higher leukocytosis.<br /></br>   • Tender mid-shaft (diaphysis) of femur → classic anatomic location for Ewing sarcoma (osteosarcoma prefers metaphysis).<br /></br>   • Radiograph (not shown to you) in NBME usually demonstrates an “onion-skin” or “moth-eaten” diaphyseal lesion with layered periosteal reaction—again Ewing sarcoma.  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Pertinent negatives<br /></br>   • No constitutional B symptoms or systemic illness pointing toward leukemia/lymphoma.<br /></br>   • No acute redness, warmth, or marked leukocytosis that would push toward osteomyelitis.  </p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>Metacognitive framework<br /></br>When an NBME stem describes an adolescent with persistent bone pain ± fever, always:<br /></br>Step 1: Identify location (diaphysis → Ewing, metaphysis → osteosarcoma).<br /></br>Step 2: Recall primary route of spread (hematogenous → lung).<br /></br>Step 3: Ask what happens untreated (rapid pulmonary metastasis → death).  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>• Ewing sarcoma is a highly aggressive, small round blue-cell malignancy (t[11;22] EWS-FLI1 fusion) that spreads early via blood.<br /></br>• The lung is the most common site of distant metastasis for all high-grade primary bone sarcomas; in Ewing sarcoma it can occur within months if not resected/chemoradiated.<br /></br>• Therefore, the “natural history” without therapy = hematogenous dissemination to lungs → respiratory compromise and death.  </p></br><p>Take-home message: In pediatric bone tumors, think “Ewing = Early lung spread.”  </p></br><p>Documentation pearl (real patient): Must note suspected malignant bone tumor, order MRI of entire bone and staging CT chest ± bone scan, and expedite referral to ortho oncology.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• Origin: Primitive neuroectodermal cells.<br /></br>• Genetics: Somatic translocation t(11;22)(q24;q12) creating EWS-FLI1 fusion protein → oncogenesis.<br /></br>• Location: Diaphysis of long bones (femur, tibia, humerus) and pelvis.<br /></br>• Histology: Sheets of uniform small round cells, PAS-positive glycogen, CD99+.<br /></br>• Clinical: Bone pain, tenderness, low fever, occasional anemia/WBC elevation, palpable mass.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Tests (FYI for exams)  </p></br><ol></br><li>Imaging<br /></br>   • Plain film → onion-skin periosteal reaction.<br /></br>   • MRI → marrow &amp; soft-tissue extent.<br /></br>   • CT chest → lung mets.  </li></br><li>Tissue diagnosis via core needle or open biopsy.  </li></br><li>Management (multimodal)<br /></br>   • Neoadjuvant multi-agent chemotherapy (vincristine, doxorubicin, cyclophosphamide alternating with ifosfamide/etoposide).<br /></br>   • Limb-sparing surgical resection ± radiation.<br /></br>   • Adjuvant chemo.  </li></br></ol></br><p>Without these steps → metastatic progression, most commonly to lungs.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“E-wing takes flight to the lungs.”<br /></br>• E = Eleven; t(11;22)<br /></br>• Wing = onion layers (onion-skin)<br /></br>• Flight = hematogenous spread → lungs  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>A. Arterial occlusion – Vascular occlusion is not a recognized complication of bone sarcoma; arterial compromise would present acutely with the 6 Ps (pain, pallor, pulselessness, etc.).  </p></br><p>C. Neurologic deficit in the extremity – Nerve compression occurs with tumors in confined spaces (e.g., osteoid osteoma near spinal canal) but is not the natural course of mid-femoral Ewing sarcoma.  </p></br><p>D. Spontaneous resolution – Benign lesions (non-ossifying fibroma) may regress, but malignant Ewing sarcoma is uniformly fatal without therapy.  </p></br><p>E. Venous occlusion – Deep-vein thrombosis risk increases after surgery or immobilization, not as a direct natural sequel of the tumor itself.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Core Takeaway<br /></br>Ewing sarcoma in children/adolescents rapidly disseminates hematogenously, with the lungs being the first and most common metastatic site; thus, untreated disease leads to pulmonary metastases and death.</p>
126. A 47-year-old man comes to the physician for a follow-up examination. He has a 1-year history of asthma poorly controlled with salmeterol, tiotropium, theophylline, β-adrenergic agonists, and short courses of prednisone. His temperature is 36.9°C (98.4°F), pulse is 84/min, respirations are 18/min, and blood pressure is 132/76 mm Hg. On pulmonary examination, diffuse expiratory wheezes are heard bilaterally. Previous x-rays of the chest have shown patchy infiltrates with a suggestion of central bronchiectasis. Results of laboratory studies show eosinophilia. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Aspergillus species serologic studies</br>b. Bacterial culture of sputum</br>c. Sputum stain and culture for Mycobacterium tuberculosis</br>d. Blood cultures</br>e. Lung biopsy</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer (per key): Aspergillus species serologic studies (choice A)</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis</br>• Chronic, hard-to-control asthma despite multiple controller agents (salmeterol = LABA, tiotropium = LAMA, theophylline, intermittent prednisone). Refractory asthma is a red flag for an allergic/structural complication.</br>• Patchy infiltrates + “central bronchiectasis” on prior chest imaging. Central (proximal) bronchiectasis is classic for allergic bronchopulmonary aspergillosis (ABPA).</br>• Peripheral eosinophilia. ABPA is a hypersensitivity reaction (type I &amp; III) to Aspergillus fumigatus colonizing the airways, producing marked eosinophilia and elevated total/Aspergillus-specific IgE.</br>Pertinent Negatives: Afebrile, no hemoptysis or weight loss → argues against active TB or severe necrotizing pneumonia; no mention of immunosuppression → invasive aspergillosis less likely.</br>Pattern recognition: “Asthma + eosinophilia + fleeting/patchy infiltrates + central bronchiectasis” → think ABPA and order serologic testing for Aspergillus-specific IgE/IgG or skin-prick testing.</p></br><p>Cognitive strategy for future questions:</br>1. Ask “Is this just bad asthma, or asthma plus something else?”</br>2. Look for clues: recurrent infiltrates (“fleeting”), eosinophils, bronchiectasis (central), brown mucus plugs.</br>3. When ABPA suspected, next diagnostic step = immunologic evidence of Aspergillus sensitivity (serology or skin test). Imaging or biopsy come later only if doubt remains.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning</br>Step-wise thought process:</br>1. Recognize poor asthma control despite maximal therapy.</br>2. Identify classic radiographic and hematologic clues (central bronchiectasis, eosinophilia).</br>3. Generate focused differential: ABPA, eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangiitis (EGPA), chronic infection (TB, atypical bacteria).</br>4. Choose the least-invasive, highest-yield test that can confirm suspected diagnosis: Aspergillus serology (total IgE and Aspergillus-specific IgE/IgG). This is required for the diagnostic criteria of ABPA and guides therapy (systemic glucocorticoids ± itraconazole).</p></br><p>Take-home: Refractory asthma + radiographic bronchiectasis + eosinophilia → test for ABPA with Aspergillus serologies.</p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real life): include asthma control assessment, radiology findings, peripheral eosinophil count, rationale for ABPA work-up, plan for IgE testing.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review</br>• Etiology: Hypersensitivity (IgE-mediated &amp; immune complex) to Aspergillus fumigatus colonizing bronchial lumen.</br>• Risk group: long-standing asthma or cystic fibrosis.</br>• Pathogenesis: Repeated allergen exposure → bronchial inflammation → mucus plugging → proximal (central) bronchiectasis and “fleeting” infiltrates as plugs move/lift.</br>• Labs: marked eosinophilia, very high total IgE (&gt;1000 IU/mL), positive Aspergillus-specific IgE/IgG.</br>• Complication: Progressive bronchiectasis → chronic lung damage if untreated.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Diagnostic &amp; Treatment Snapshot</br>1. Diagnostic criteria (simplified):</br>   • Asthma/CF + immediate skin reactivity or Aspergillus-specific IgE</br>   • Elevated total IgE (&gt;417 IU/mL, often &gt;1000)</br>   • Radiographic evidence (centrilobular opacities, central bronchiectasis)</br>   • Peripheral eosinophilia</br>2. Additional testing once serology positive: High-resolution CT to define bronchiectasis; sputum culture may show Aspergillus but is not required.</br>3. Treatment:</br>   • First-line: Oral prednisone taper (e.g., 0.5 mg/kg/day × 2 weeks → slow taper).</br>   • Add-on: Oral itraconazole (or voriconazole) to reduce antigen burden and steroid dose.</br>   • Monitor: Total IgE level to detect relapse.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips</br>“ABPA ABCDE”:</br>A – Asthma / Allergy background<br /></br>B – Brown bronchial mucus plugs<br /></br>P – Proximal (central) bronchiectasis / Patchy “fleeting” infiltrates<br /></br>A – Aspergillus-specific IgE ↑<br /></br>E – Eosinophilia, Elevated total IgE  </p></br><p>Another mnemonic: “ABPA = All Bronchi Plugged with Aspergillus”</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Incorrect Answer Analysis</br>B. Bacterial culture of sputum<br /></br>   • Would be indicated for recurrent bacterial pneumonia or bronchiectasis due to infection (e.g., Pseudomonas in CF). Neither fever, productive purulence, nor acute infiltrate pattern suggesting bacterial pneumonia is present. Culture would not establish ABPA.</p></br><p>C. Sputum stain &amp; culture for Mycobacterium tuberculosis<br /></br>   • Consider with chronic cough, weight loss, cavitary lesions, apical infiltrates, night sweats. Central bronchiectasis and eosinophilia are not TB features. </p></br><p>D. Blood cultures<br /></br>   • Useful for suspected bacteremia/sepsis (e.g., febrile, hypotensive). Patient is afebrile, vitals stable.</p></br><p>E. Lung biopsy<br /></br>   • Highly invasive; reserved when non-invasive tests inconclusive or to diagnose interstitial lung disease, malignancy, EGPA, etc. ABPA diagnosis almost always achieved non-invasively.</p></br><p>Common Pitfall: Ordering invasive tests (biopsy) or empiric antibiotics before confirming hypersensitivity diagnosis → unnecessary morbidity and delays correct therapy.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Key Takeaway</br>When an asthmatic patient develops eosinophilia and imaging shows central bronchiectasis or “fleeting” infiltrates, suspect allergic bronchopulmonary aspergillosis. The first diagnostic step is non-invasive serologic testing for Aspergillus-specific IgE/IgG (or skin testing).</p>
127. A 6-year-old boy is brought to the physician for a routine well-child examination prior to a scheduled dental extraction. He has a ventricular septal defect that has not required operative repair. He has no other history of serious illness. Vital signs are within normal limits. Examination shows multiple dental caries. There are no bruits or heaves on auscultation. A grade 1/6 holosystolic murmur is heard best at the lower left sternal border. Which of the following is the most appropriate pharmacotherapy for this patient prior to his dental procedure?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Amoxicillin</br>b. Ceftriaxone</br>c. Doxycycline</br>d. Sulfisoxazole</br>e. No pharmacotherapy is indicated</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>E. No pharmacotherapy is indicated  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Key clues<br /></br>  – Ventricular septal defect (VSD) that has never been repaired → a non-cyanotic, simple congenital heart lesion.<br /></br>  – “No other history of serious illness”; “grade 1/6 holosystolic murmur” → confirms a small, hemodynamically insignificant VSD.<br /></br>  – Imminent dental extraction (a procedure that causes gingival manipulation → transient bacteremia with viridans streptococci).<br /></br>• The test is asking: “Does this cardiac lesion require infective endocarditis (IE) prophylaxis for a dental procedure?”  </p></br><p>Framework to approach similar questions<br /></br>1. Identify the procedure: dental work that manipulates gingiva = YES, prophylaxis considered—IF the heart condition is high-risk.<br /></br>2. Apply AHA/ACC 2020 IE prophylaxis criteria (memorize “PHR^3” below).<br /></br>3. If the lesion is not on the list, answer “no prophylaxis.”  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>AHA/ACC guidelines restrict antibiotic prophylaxis to patients at highest risk of catastrophic IE:<br /></br>1. Prosthetic cardiac valve or material<br /></br>2. History of previous infective endocarditis<br /></br>3. Certain congenital heart disease (CHD)<br /></br>   • Unrepaired cyanotic CHD (including palliative shunts/conduits)<br /></br>   • Repaired CHD with prosthetic material, during the first 6 months after repair<br /></br>   • Repaired CHD with residual defects adjacent to the prosthetic patch/device<br /></br>4. Cardiac transplant recipients who develop valvulopathy  </p></br><p>A small, unrepaired, acyanotic VSD is specifically listed as NOT requiring prophylaxis. Therefore, “No pharmacotherapy” is correct.  </p></br><p>Take-home message: IE prophylaxis is now limited to a narrow group. Over-prescribing offers no benefit and promotes resistance &amp; adverse drug events.  </p></br><p>Documentation pearl: In a real chart you would write “Per 2020 AHA guidelines, patient’s simple unrepaired VSD does not meet criteria for IE prophylaxis; no antibiotic given before dental extraction. Risks/benefits discussed.”  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Dental manipulation → transient bacteremia with oral viridans streptococci.<br /></br>• Bacteria may seed abnormal endocardium → IE.<br /></br>• However, absolute risk in simple VSD ≈ general population; outweighing routine antibiotic use.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Review<br /></br>Because prophylaxis is NOT indicated, no antibiotic, lab, or imaging is needed. If the patient had an indication, first-line would be a single oral dose of amoxicillin 30–60 min before the procedure (adults 2 g, children 50 mg/kg).  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tip<br /></br>Use the mnemonic “PHR³” (pronounced “far-three”):<br /></br>P = Prosthetic valve/material<br /></br>H = History of IE<br /></br>R1 = Repair &lt; 6 mo with prosthetic material<br /></br>R2 = Residual defect next to prosthetic patch/device<br /></br>R3 = Right-to-left (cyanotic) unrepaired CHD<br /></br>(+ transplant valvulopathy)<br /></br>If the heart lesion isn’t in PHR³, skip prophylaxis.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>a. Amoxicillin – Correct drug IF prophylaxis were indicated, but guidelines say not needed for simple VSD.<br /></br>b. Ceftriaxone – Parenteral; reserved for patients unable to take oral meds or with amoxicillin allergy, and only when prophylaxis is indicated.<br /></br>c. Doxycycline – Acceptable oral choice for penicillin-allergic adults, but not first-line in children (&lt; 8 y risk of tooth discoloration). Again, not indicated.<br /></br>d. Sulfisoxazole – Not part of current IE prophylaxis regimens. Choosing this suggests outdated knowledge.<br /></br>e. No pharmacotherapy – Only option that aligns with current evidence-based guidelines for this patient.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Bottom Line<br /></br>A small, unrepaired VSD is NOT a high-risk cardiac condition. Therefore, no antibiotic prophylaxis is required before dental extraction.</p>
128. A 57-year-old man is referred to a surgeon after he is diagnosed with an asymptmatic abdominal aortic aneurysm that is 5 cm in diameter. The average survival of untreated patients with aneurysms larger than 6 cm is 20 months. The mortality of patients with spontaneous out-of-hospital rupture of an aortic aneurysm is greater than 85 percent. Which of the following additional pieces of information will be most useful in assessing this patient's need for an operation?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. Family history of aneurysm</br>b. The patient's ability to pay for health care</br>c. The percentage of aneurysms smaller than 6 cm that rupture</br>d. The probability of renal failure from the procedure</br>e. Whether or not the aneurysm involves the iliac arteries</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>C.
