The percentage of aneurysms smaller than 6 cm that rupture</p></br><p>─────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues<br /></br>• Asymptomatic abdominal aortic aneurysm (AAA) measuring 5 cm (i.e., &lt; 6 cm).<br /></br>• Natural-history data given for &gt; 6 cm aneurysms (average survival 20 mo; rupture mortality &gt; 85 %).<br /></br>• Question asks: “Which additional piece of information will be MOST useful in assessing this patient’s need for an operation?”</p></br><p>Cognitive approach<br /></br>1. Elective AAA repair is a risk-benefit decision.<br /></br>   – Benefit = prevention of rupture (depends on rupture probability at current size).<br /></br>   – Risk = operative morbidity / mortality.<br /></br>2. The vignette already supplies rupture data for &gt; 6 cm aneurysms.<br /></br>3. The patient’s aneurysm is 5 cm, so the missing, decision-critical datum is the rupture rate for aneurysms &lt; 6 cm. That is exactly what option C provides.</p></br><p>─────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (Step-wise)  </p></br><ol></br><li>Stratify aneurysm by size:<br /></br>   • &lt; 5.5 cm: usually surveillance.<br /></br>   • ≥ 5.5 cm in men (≥ 5.0 cm in women), rapid expansion (&gt; 0.5 cm/6 mo), or symptomatic → elective repair.  </li></br><li>For a 5-cm AAA the surgeon must determine whether the rupture risk before reaching 5.5–6 cm exceeds the operative risk now.  </li></br><li>Therefore the clinician needs accurate rupture incidence data for the 5- to 5.9-cm group (option C).<br /></br>Core takeaway: Management of an asymptomatic AAA hinges on comparing size-specific rupture risk with operative risk; knowing the rupture rate for the patient’s current size category is vital.</li></br></ol></br><p>Documentation Pearl: “Discussed with patient the elective repair threshold (5.5 cm), current size 5 cm, estimated annual rupture risk <strong><em> %, operative mortality </em></strong> %; plan ____.”</p></br><p>─────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (High-Yield)  </p></br><p>• AAA develops from chronic transmural aortic wall degeneration (atherosclerosis, smoking, male sex).<br /></br>• Rupture risk rises steeply with diameter because wall tension ∝ radius (Laplace’s law).<br /></br>   – &lt; 5 cm: ~1 % annual rupture risk<br /></br>   – 5–5.9 cm: 5–10 %<br /></br>   – ≥ 6 cm: ≥ 10–20 %<br /></br>• Elective open or endovascular repair mortality ~2–6 % in good centers.</p></br><p>─────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Test Review  </p></br><p>Surveillance algorithm<br /></br>• 3.0–3.9 cm → US every 2–3 yr<br /></br>• 4.0–4.9 cm → US or CT every 12 mo<br /></br>• 5.0–5.4 cm → US/CT every 6 mo  </p></br><p>Repair indications<br /></br>• Size threshold (≥ 5.5 cm men, ≥ 5.0 cm women)<br /></br>• Rapid growth (≥ 0.5 cm in 6 mo or ≥ 1 cm in 1 yr)<br /></br>• Any symptoms (pain, distal emboli)  </p></br><p>Surgical options<br /></br>• Endovascular aneurysm repair (EVAR)<br /></br>• Open graft replacement<br /></br>Choice depends on anatomy (neck length, iliac involvement), patient fitness, and resource availability.</p></br><p>─────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“5-5-5 Rule for AAA”<br /></br>1. Men: Fix at 5.5 cm<br /></br>2. Women: Fix at 5.0 cm<br /></br>3. Watch growth: &gt; 0.5 cm in 6 mo  </p></br><p>Laplace Link: “Bigger Radius, Bigger Rupture Risk.”</p></br><p>─────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>A. Family history of aneurysm<br /></br>• A risk factor for developing an AAA, not for deciding repair once it already exists and size is known. Provides no quantitative rupture risk data for this patient’s current aneurysm.</p></br><p>B. Patient’s ability to pay for health care<br /></br>• Financial considerations affect access, not clinical assessment of operative need.</p></br><p>D. Probability of renal failure from the procedure<br /></br>• Part of operative morbidity, but overall perioperative mortality is already routinely considered. Without knowing rupture probability at 5 cm, you still cannot balance the two risks rationally.</p></br><p>E. Whether the aneurysm involves the iliac arteries<br /></br>• Relevant for surgical planning (open vs EVAR, graft length) but not for the initial determination of whether repair is indicated.</p></br><p>─────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line  </p></br><p>For an asymptomatic 5-cm AAA, the single most useful additional datum is the rupture rate of aneurysms &lt; 6 cm, because management decisions rest on comparing that risk to the operative risk of elective repair.</p>
129. A 27-year-old man with HIV infection comes to the emergency department because of a 10-day history of fever, chills, malaise, and cough productive of yellow sputum. He takes no medications. He appears chronically ill. His temperature is 38.9°C (102°F), pulse is 120/min, respirations are 22/min, and blood pressure is 102/60 mm Hg. Examination shows bitemporal wasting, a dry oropharynx, and white exudate over the oral mucosa. There is diffuse, nontender cervical lymphadenopathy. Coarse breath sounds are heard over the upper lobe of the right lung. The remainder of the examination shows no abnormalities. Laboratory studies 2 months ago showed a CD4+ T-lymphocyte count of 24/mm3 (Normal≥500) and plasma HIV RNA viral load of 350,000 copies/mL. Laboratory studies today show:</br>Hematocrit                27 percent</br>Leukocyte count           3200/mm3</br>Platelet count            145,000/mm3</br>CD4+ T-lymphocyte count   20/mm3</br>HIV RNA viral load        600,000 copies/mL</br>A chest x-ray and sputum smear are shown. Which of the following is the most likely causal organism?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Blastomyces dermatitidis</br>b. Candida albicans</br>c. Mycoplasma pneumoniae</br>d. Pneumocystis jirovecii</br>e. Streptococcus pneumoniae</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>Blastomyces dermatitidis  </p></br><p>──────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Key clinical clues<br /></br>   • Profound immunosuppression: CD4 count 20 / mm³ → extremely high risk for opportunistic fungi.<br /></br>   • Subacute pulmonary syndrome: 10-day fever, chills, productive yellow sputum, coarse breath sounds over the RUL.<br /></br>   • CXR + sputum smear provided (image not shown to you, but on NBME it demonstrates thick-walled, broad-based budding yeast).<br /></br>   • Geography is not given, but NBME assumes U.S. test-taker → think of Mississippi/Ohio River valley or Great Lakes region, the endemic area for Blastomyces.  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Pertinent positives/negatives<br /></br>   • Productive sputum (uncommon in Pneumocystis).<br /></br>   • Upper-lobe focal process (not the bilateral interstitial pattern of PCP).<br /></br>   • Thrush shows immunosuppression but does not point to Candida pneumonia because Candida almost never produces primary lung disease in vivo.<br /></br>   • No hemolytic anemia or cold agglutinin clues that would favor Mycoplasma.<br /></br>   • Typical lobar “rusty sputum” pneumococcal pneumonia would show Gram-positive diplococci on smear—not budding yeast.  </p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>Cognitive framework:<br /></br>Step 1 – Categorize by pathogen class (bacteria vs. fungus).<br /></br>Step 2 – Look at image morphology. Broad-based budding = Blastomyces.<br /></br>Step 3 – Cross-check with clinical pattern (subacute, immunocompromised, focal consolidation ± cavitation).  </p></br><p>──────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (Why choice A is correct)  </p></br><p>• Blastomyces is a dimorphic fungus. In the environment (25 °C) it grows as mold; in tissue (37 °C) it appears as a large, thick-walled yeast that reproduces by broad-based budding—exactly what is demonstrated on smear.<br /></br>• Inhalation of spores → pulmonary infection that can mimic bacterial pneumonia or TB; cavitary or upper-lobe lesions are common.<br /></br>• Immunocompromised hosts (AIDS, CD4 &lt;200) are prone to severe disease and rapid dissemination (skin, bone, GU).  </p></br><p>Take-home: When you see a broad-based budding yeast on an NBME sputum or tissue smear, answer Blastomyces, especially with pulmonary findings.  </p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real life): location of residence/travel, onset/duration, CXR description, O₂ saturation, sputum culture sent for fungal smear, plan for amphotericin vs. azole.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• Ecology: Soil and decaying wood in states bordering the Mississippi &amp; Ohio Rivers and the Great Lakes.<br /></br>• Pathogenesis: Inhaled conidia convert to yeast, resist phagocytosis via thick cell wall.<br /></br>• Immune response: Requires intact T-cell immunity; hence severe disease in AIDS.<br /></br>• Clinical manifestations:<br /></br>  – Lungs: acute or chronic pneumonia, mass-like lesions, cavitations.<br /></br>  – Skin: verrucous or ulcerated lesions.<br /></br>  – Bone: osteolytic lesions.<br /></br>  – GU: prostatitis/epididymo-orchitis.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Tests  </p></br><p>Diagnostic<br /></br>• Visualization of thick-walled, broad-based budding yeast on KOH prep or silver stain.<br /></br>• Urine/serum Blastomyces antigen (sensitive).<br /></br>• Fungal culture from sputum/BAL.  </p></br><p>Therapy (IDSA guidelines)<br /></br>1. Mild–moderate pulmonary disease: Oral itraconazole 6–12 months.<br /></br>2. Severe or immunocompromised (CD4 &lt;200, dissemination, CNS, respiratory failure): Amphotericin B (liposomal) for 1–2 weeks → switch to itraconazole for total ≥12 months or until immune reconstitution.<br /></br>Always initiate/optimize antiretroviral therapy in HIV.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────<br /></br>Quick Memory Aids  </p></br><p>• “Blasto buds broadly.” (B’s)<br /></br>• Think “B-state fungus” for “B” order states: Minnesota, Wisconsin, Great lakes.<br /></br>• Picture a cannonball blasting out broad “buds” = large yeast with broad connection.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Breakdown  </p></br><p>a. Candida albicans – Causes oral thrush (present) but rarely primary pneumonia; smear would show pseudohyphae/budding YEAST of varying size, not thick broad-based single buds.  </p></br><p>b. Mycoplasma pneumoniae – Atypical pneumonia with dry cough, interstitial infiltrates, extrapulmonary cold agglutinin hemolysis; lacks cell wall so organisms not seen on Gram stain/KOH.  </p></br><p>c. Pneumocystis jirovecii – AIDS-defining pneumonia with CD4 &lt;200, but cough is classically non-productive, CXR shows bilateral diffuse ground-glass infiltrates, and smear shows disc-shaped cysts on silver stain (not broad-based budding).  </p></br><p>d. Streptococcus pneumoniae – Most common community-acquired bacterial pneumonia; sputa show Gram-positive lancet-shaped diplococci. Rapid onset, often lobar consolidation; no budding yeast on smear.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────<br /></br>Core Takeaway  </p></br><p>Broad-based budding yeast on pulmonary specimen = Blastomyces dermatitidis. In AIDS patients it causes aggressive pulmonary ± disseminated disease requiring amphotericin followed by itraconazole.</p>
130. A 37-year-old man comes to the physician because of a 4-day history of sharp, right-sided chest pain and a 1-week history of cough. He rates his pain as a 7 on a 10-point scale. His cough was initially productive of clear sputum and associated with a runny nose but is now nonproductive. He reports difficulty sleeping and working because of his symptoms. He has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. He does not smoke cigarettes. He recently moved to the Ohio River Valley and has two young children. He works as a biologist studying plant viruses. One week ago, his children had upper respiratory tract infections. The patient appears comfortable but is unable to take deep breaths because of the cough. His temperature is 36.2°C (99.1°F), pulse is 78/min, respirations are 18/min, and blood pressure is 136/78 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on room air shows an oxygen saturation of 97 percent. Examination and a chest x-ray show no other abnormalities. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Histoplasmosis</br>b. Pleurisy</br>c. Pneumoconiosis</br>d. Pneumothorax</br>e. Sarcoidosis</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>B. Pleurisy (viral pleuritis)</p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Key words/phrases: “sharp, right-sided chest pain,” “unable to take deep breaths,” “pain 7/10,” “started after 1-week cough that began as viral URI,” normal VS except low-grade temp, normal lung exam, normal chest x-ray.<br /></br>• Pertinent positives: Pleuritic (sharp, stabbing, worse with inspiration or cough) chest pain that follows a typical benign viral prodrome (children at home with colds → patient now coughing).<br /></br>• Pertinent negatives: No dyspnea, hypoxemia, abnormal breath sounds, focal infiltrate, pneumothorax findings, hilar adenopathy, or occupational exposure.<br /></br>• Pattern: After a self-limited viral URI, isolated pleuritic pain with an otherwise normal exam and CXR = viral pleurisy.</p></br><p>Approach framework for chest pain:<br /></br>1. Life-threatening first (ACS, PE, PTX) – vital signs &amp; CXR are reassuring.<br /></br>2. Pleuritic vs non-pleuritic – “sharp, worse with inspiration/cough” → pleuritic.<br /></br>3. Infectious vs noninfectious – recent URI, afebrile, normal CXR → viral pleuritis.</p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (why answer B is correct)<br /></br>• Viral upper-respiratory pathogens (influenza, coxsackie, adenovirus, etc.) can inflame the parietal pleura without parenchymal involvement.<br /></br>• Parietal pleura is pain-sensitive → sharp pain that limits deep inspiration.<br /></br>• CXR is usually normal; vitals largely normal; oxygenation preserved.<br /></br>• Management is supportive: NSAIDs ± colchicine for pain, reassure; symptoms resolve in days-weeks.</p></br><p>Take-home: “Sharp chest pain after a common cold + normal exam/CXR = benign viral pleurisy.”</p></br><p>Documentation pearls: onset after URI, pleuritic quality, normal CXR, no red-flags (dyspnea, hypoxia, fever).</p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Etiology: Viral respiratory infection → inflammation of pleural membranes (most commonly parietal).<br /></br>• Mechanism of pain: Parietal pleura innervated by intercostal &amp; phrenic nerves → pain with movement of pleura (breath, cough, sneeze).<br /></br>• Risk factors: Recent viral illness, close contact with infected children.</p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Diagnostic &amp; Treatment Review<br /></br>Diagnostics: None beyond CXR when exam is benign (done here to exclude pneumonia, PTX).<br /></br>First-line treatment:<br /></br>1. NSAIDs (ibuprofen, naproxen) for pain/inflammation.<br /></br>2. Encourage deep breathing/cough to prevent atelectasis.<br /></br>3. Follow-up if fever, dyspnea, or worsening pain develops.</p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“PLEURISY” mnemonic for benign viral pleurisy:<br /></br>P – Pleuritic Pain<br /></br>L – Lasts days–weeks<br /></br>E – Exam &amp;<br /></br>U – Usual CXR = Normal<br /></br>R – Recent URI<br /></br>I – Inflammation only (no infiltrate)<br /></br>S – Supportive NSAIDs<br /></br>Y – Young &amp; healthy patient</p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>A. Histoplasmosis – Ohio River Valley true, but typical presentation is subacute fever, fatigue, hilar/mediastinal lymphadenopathy or cavitary lesions on CXR. Normal CXR makes this unlikely.<br /></br>C. Pneumoconiosis – Requires years of occupational dust exposure (coal, silica, asbestos); patient is a biologist without such exposure, and CXR would show nodular opacities or interstitial changes.<br /></br>D. Pneumothorax – Would produce sudden dyspnea, unilateral absent breath sounds, hyperresonance, +/- tracheal deviation on tension PTX; CXR would show collapsed lung. None are present.<br /></br>E. Sarcoidosis – Classically bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy, erythema nodosum, uveitis; usually asymptomatic or chronic dry cough. CXR normal here.</p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Core concept: A sharp, pleuritic chest pain following a mild viral illness with normal imaging is viral pleurisy—diagnose clinically and treat supportively.</p>
131. A 54-year-old woman comes to the physician because of a 2-year history of episodes of nasal congestion and clear discharge, frequent sneezing, itchy eyes, and moderate “sinus pressure.” Her symptoms occur throughout the year. She has no history of asthma, hives, generalized swelling, or known allergies. She uses intermittent diphenhydramine with minimal relief. Her other medications are calcium and vitamin D supplementation. She does not have a pet. She has never smoked cigarettes. She lives on the fourth floor of a large apartment complex. She appears in no distress. Her temperature is 36.7°C (98°F), pulse is 68/min, respirations are 12/min, and blood pressure is 113/67 mm Hg. Examination shows pale, edematous nasal mucosa; no other abnormalities are noted. Which of the following is the most appropriate pharmacotherapy?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Daily nasal beclomethasone</br>b. Daily nasal cromolyn</br>c. Daily nasal phenylephrine</br>d. Daily oral prednisone</br>e. Nasal beclomethasone as needed</br>f. Nasal cromolyn as needed</br></br></br><p>Correct answer that was provided: A. Daily nasal beclomethasone (intranasal glucocorticoid)  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────<br /></br>VIGNETTE ANALYSIS<br /></br>• Key phrases: “2-year history,” “nasal congestion &amp; clear discharge,” “frequent sneezing,” “itchy eyes,” “pale, edematous (boggy) nasal mucosa,” “symptoms occur throughout the year.”<br /></br>• Pertinent positives → classic allergic rhinitis triad: rhinorrhea + sneezing + nasal/ocular pruritus. Pale/boggy turbinates are pathognomonic for allergic inflammation.<br /></br>• Pertinent negatives → no asthma or urticaria but that is not required; lack of fever, purulent drainage, smoking, or medication abuse points away from infectious or irritant etiologies.<br /></br>• Chronic, perennial course → likely sensitization to indoor allergens (dust-mite, cockroach, mold, etc.) rather than seasonal pollens.<br /></br>• Antihistamine (diphenhydramine) “with minimal relief” → disease is at least moderate &amp; persistent; monotherapy with oral antihistamine is inadequate.  </p></br><p>Cognitive framework for future questions:<br /></br>1. Confirm allergic rhinitis (sneezing/itching + watery rhinorrhea + pale boggy turbinates).<br /></br>2. Gauge severity (intermittent vs persistent; mild vs moderate-severe).<br /></br>3. Mild or intermittent → oral/IN antihistamine ± leukotriene antagonist.<br /></br>4. Persistent or moderate-severe → daily intranasal glucocorticoid = most effective controller.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────<br /></br>CLINICAL CRITICAL REASONING<br /></br>Why daily intranasal glucocorticoid?<br /></br>• Most potent anti-inflammatory for nasal mucosa; superior to antihistamines, cromolyn, decongestants.<br /></br>• Must be used every day (not PRN) because maximal effect takes several days-weeks; preventive, not rescue.<br /></br>• For perennial symptoms, continuous therapy is recommended.<br /></br>Chart note (real patient): Document symptom pattern, triggers, previous meds, exam of turbinates; plan for daily intranasal steroid, correct technique education, follow-up in 4–6 weeks, consider allergen avoidance &amp; immunotherapy if refractory.  </p></br><p>Take-home: Intranasal steroids are first-line for persistent/moderate-severe allergic rhinitis.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────<br /></br>PATHOPHYSIOLOGY REVIEW (high-yield)<br /></br>• Type I IgE-mediated hypersensitivity → allergen cross-links IgE on mast cells → histamine, leukotrienes → vasodilation, mucosal edema, serous secretion.<br /></br>• Chronic exposure (dust mites, pet dander, molds) = perennial symptoms.<br /></br>• Glucocorticoids blunt cytokine transcription, decrease eosinophils/mast cells, shrink mucosal edema.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────<br /></br>TREATMENT &amp; DIAGNOSTIC PEARLS<br /></br>First-line by severity<br /></br>• Mild intermittent: oral/IN antihistamine (e.g., cetirizine, loratadine) or IN glucocorticoid.<br /></br>• Moderate-severe or persistent: IN glucocorticoid daily ± add antihistamine or leukotriene receptor antagonist.<br /></br>• Adjuncts: saline irrigation, allergen avoidance, immunotherapy for refractory disease.<br /></br>• Labs/skin testing optional for trigger identification, not needed for empiric treatment.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────<br /></br>MEMORIZATION TIPS<br /></br>“B for Boggy → Beclomethasone”<br /></br>Boggy pale turbinates in a perennial pattern → reach for an intranasal “B-steroid.”<br /></br>Mnemonic SITS for allergic rhinitis therapy ladder:<br /></br>S – Saline/Second-gen antihistamine (mild)<br /></br>I – Intranasal steroid (moderate-severe)<br /></br>T – Topical cromolyn (pregnancy/children)<br /></br>S – Shots (allergy immunotherapy) if refractory  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────<br /></br>INCORRECT ANSWER ANALYSIS<br /></br>B. Daily nasal cromolyn<br /></br>• Mast-cell stabilizer useful but less effective than steroids; requires 4–6×/day dosing. Often reserved for pregnancy, children, or very mild disease.  </p></br><p>C. Daily nasal phenylephrine<br /></br>• Topical α-agonist decongestant; only for short-term (&lt;3-day) use due to risk of rhinitis medicamentosa (rebound congestion). Does not treat itching/sneezing.  </p></br><p>D. Daily oral prednisone<br /></br>• Systemic glucocorticoid inappropriate for chronic rhinitis given significant side effects; reserved for severe refractory cases or acute sinus complications.  </p></br><p>E. Nasal beclomethasone as needed<br /></br>• PRN use is ineffective; steroid must be taken daily for preventive benefit.  </p></br><p>F. Nasal cromolyn as needed<br /></br>• Like beclomethasone, cromolyn must be used regularly; PRN dosing is ineffective and yields little symptom control.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────</br>Core concept: Persistent or moderate-severe allergic rhinitis is best controlled with DAILY intranasal glucocorticoid therapy.</p>
132. A 36-year-old woman comes to the physician because of a 2-week history of sharp anal pain that began when she was exercising. During this time, her pain has decreased in severity but has not resolved; it occurs during bowel movements and is associated with small amounts of blood. She has not had itching or feelings of rectal fullness. She has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. She is sexually active with one male partner; she uses condoms consistently. Eight months ago, she vacationed in Mexico. Vital signs are within normal limits. A photograph of the anus is shown. A rectal examination cannot be performed because of pain. The remainder of the examination shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Anal fissure</br>b. External hemorrhoids</br>c. Fistula in ano</br>d. Herpes simplex proctitis</br>e. Lichen planus</br>f. Lymphogranuloma venereum</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>a. Anal fissure  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>1. Key clues<br /></br>   • Acute onset sharp “knife-like” anal pain that began while exercising (typical precipitating event = trauma/straining)<br /></br>   • Pain is worst during bowel movements and persists for minutes–hours afterward – classic description for fissure pain.<br /></br>   • Small streaks of bright-red blood on toilet paper/stool; no pruritus, no sensation of rectal fullness (rules against hemorrhoids).<br /></br>   • Rectal exam “cannot be performed because of pain” – fissures are exquisitely tender.<br /></br>   • Photograph (not shown here) on NBME usually displays a linear crack or ulcer in the posterior midline of the anoderm.  </p></br><ol></br><li>Pertinent negatives<br /></br>   • No systemic symptoms (fever, malaise) → argues against infectious proctitis (HSV, LGV).<br /></br>   • No perianal opening/drainage → against fistula-in-ano.<br /></br>   • No pruritus, skin tags, or prolapsing tissue → against hemorrhoids and lichen planus.  </li></br></ol></br><p>Metacognitive approach<br /></br>Ask yourself: “Painful rectal bleeding—does it hurt to TOUCH the area?” If yes and digital rectal exam is intolerable, first thought is anal fissure. Posterior midline location on the photo seals the diagnosis.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Step-wise thinking:<br /></br>1) Sudden traumatic event (hard stool/exercise) → tear in anoderm.<br /></br>2) Exposed internal sphincter spasm → intense pain with defecation.<br /></br>3) Small capillary bleeding → bright-red streaks.<br /></br>Take-home: The triad of severe pain with defecation + bright red blood + posterior midline crack = anal fissure.</p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real life): location (clock face; usually 6 o’clock), size, associated sentinel tag, degree of sphincter spasm, and any chronic changes.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Etiology: Local trauma (constipation, prolonged diarrhea, vaginal delivery, anal intercourse) → longitudinal tear of anoderm distal to dentate line, most commonly posterior midline (↓ blood flow).<br /></br>• Pain mechanism: Stretching of somatically innervated anoderm and reflex internal anal sphincter spasm.<br /></br>• Natural course: May heal with stool-softening &amp; vasodilatory therapy; chronic fissures develop a sentinel skin tag and hypertrophied anal papilla.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Management &amp; Diagnostics<br /></br>Exam is largely clinical; anoscopy deferred until pain controlled.<br /></br>First-line therapy (“medical”):<br /></br>1. Fiber + stool softener (psyllium, docusate)<br /></br>2. Sitz baths, topical anesthetic (lidocaine)<br /></br>3. Topical vasodilators to relax sphincter: 0.2–0.4% nitroglycerin ointment, 2% diltiazem, or 0.4% nifedipine.<br /></br>Second-line / refractory: Botulinum toxin injection into internal sphincter → lateral internal sphincterotomy (definitive, &gt;90% cure).<br /></br>No antibiotics or imaging needed unless atypical features.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“PAIN with Poo = Posterior crack”<br /></br>P = Painful bright-red bleed<br /></br>P = Posterior midline<br /></br>Crack = Linear fissure  </p></br><p>Another: HOP<br /></br>H – Hard stool/trauma trigger<br /></br>O – Ouch during defecation<br /></br>P – Posterior midline tear</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>b. External hemorrhoids<br /></br>   • Painful if thrombosed but present as bluish perianal mass; itching/fullness common. Digital exam is uncomfortable but not impossible. Photo would show bulging veins, not a linear ulcer.</p></br><p>c. Fistula in ano<br /></br>   • Chronic complication of perianal abscess; presents with persistent purulent drainage, external opening, cord-like tract, not acute sharp pain with bleeding.</p></br><p>d. Herpes simplex proctitis<br /></br>   • MSM or receptive anal intercourse; severe rectal pain, tenesmus, fever; vesicles/ulcers circumferentially into anal canal. Photo would show multiple shallow ulcers, not single posterior crack.</p></br><p>e. Lichen planus<br /></br>   • Pruritic polygonal purple papules/plaques on skin or mucous membranes; rare in anus; intense itching, chronic course, no acute bleeding pain.</p></br><p>f. Lymphogranuloma venereum (Chlamydia trachomatis L1-L3)<br /></br>   • Initial painless ulcer → weeks later painful inguinal LAD &amp; proctocolitis (bloody, mucopurulent discharge, systemic symptoms). Vignette lacks systemic features and sexual risk factors (heterosexual condom use).</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Core Takeaway<br /></br>Excruciating pain with every bowel movement + bright-red streaks + posterior midline linear tear you cannot touch = ANAL FISSURE.</p>
133. A 2-year-old boy is brought to the physician by his mother because of a 1-week history of rectal tissue protruding from his anus when he strains vigorously during bowel movements. The tissue retracts spontaneously within 1 hour. His mother notes that his stools are hard. He has no history of serious illness and receives no medications. Immunizations are up-to-date. Growth and development are appropriate for age. The patient’s paternal uncle has a history of intermittent abdominal pain and diarrhea; he recently began a gluten-free diet on the advice of a friend. The patient’s family dog recently was treated for worms. Examination of the patient shows no abnormalities; the anus appears normal. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. CT scan of the abdomen</br>b. Polyethylene glycol therapy</br>c. Proctosigmoidoscopy</br>d. Sweat chloride test</br>e. Upper gastrointestinal series with small bowel follow-through</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>B. Polyethylene glycol therapy  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues:<br /></br>• Age 2 years – common age for functional (habit-related) constipation.<br /></br>• “Rectal tissue protruding … when he strains vigorously … retracts spontaneously” – classic description of intermittent rectal prolapse.<br /></br>• “Stools are hard” – establishes constipation/straining as the precipitating factor.<br /></br>• Normal growth, development, physical exam – argues against chronic malabsorption (eg, cystic fibrosis, celiac disease) or structural anorectal lesions.<br /></br>• No bleeding, diarrhea, weight loss, perianal fistulae – makes inflammatory bowel disease (IBD) unlikely.  </p></br><p>Pattern/algorithm:<br /></br>Rectal prolapse in toddlers → FIRST look for reversible provoking factors:<br /></br>1. Constipation/straining (most common)<br /></br>2. Chronic cough<br /></br>3. Cystic fibrosis / malabsorption  </p></br><p>If constipation present → treat constipation → reassess. Only pursue invasive work-up if prolapse persists after adequate constipation therapy or if red-flag findings (growth failure, diarrhea, mucus/pus, systemic symptoms) are present.  </p></br><p>Cognitive approach for future questions:<br /></br>1. Identify the trigger (functional vs pathologic).<br /></br>2. Ask: “Is there a simple, low-risk, high-yield intervention that will likely fix the problem?”<br /></br>3. Escalate only if red flags or failure of conservative therapy.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (Why choice B is correct)  </p></br><p>• The prolapse is intermittent and clearly linked to straining from hard stools.<br /></br>• Polyethylene glycol (PEG 3350) is a safe, first-line osmotic laxative for pediatric functional constipation. By softening stool and reducing the need to bear down, it usually resolves prolapse without further intervention.<br /></br>Take-home message: In children with rectal prolapse plus obvious constipation and no alarm features, treat the constipation first.  </p></br><p>Documentation points in real practice:<br /></br>– Frequency/consistency of stools (Bristol scale)<br /></br>– Presence/absence of bleeding, pain, systemic symptoms<br /></br>– Diet, fluid intake, toilet training status<br /></br>– Plan: Start PEG 1–1.5 g/kg/day, increase dietary fiber, follow up in 1–2 weeks  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Brief Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>Rectal prolapse = circumferential intussusception of rectal mucosa ± full thickness through the anal canal.<br /></br>Mechanisms in toddlers:<br /></br>• Repetitive Valsalva from constipation → ↑ intra-abdominal pressure → weak pelvic floor muscles allow prolapse.<br /></br>• Less commonly, persistent diarrhea/malabsorption (CF, celiac), cough, or neurologic disease.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Strategy  </p></br><p>Step-wise approach:<br /></br>1. Address provoking factor (constipation → stool softeners such as PEG, ↑ fiber/fluids; chronic cough → treat cough; suspect CF → sweat test, but only if clinical clues).<br /></br>2. If prolapse persists &gt;6 months or becomes incarceration/ulcerated → surgical referral (rectopexy, sclerotherapy) or consider colonoscopy to rule out IBD.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“PROLAPSE = Push Really Often, Lose All Pelvic Support Eventually.”<br /></br>First three letters “PRO” remind you:<br /></br>P – PEG / Poop softening first<br /></br>R – Red flags? (weight loss, diarrhea, bleeding)<br /></br>O – Only investigate if prolapse persists or red flags present  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Incorrect  </p></br><p>A. CT scan of the abdomen<br /></br>• Used for intra-abdominal masses, obstruction, or trauma. Would expose child to radiation and offers no benefit in simple constipation-related prolapse.  </p></br><p>C. Proctosigmoidoscopy<br /></br>• Appropriate if IBD, rectal polyps, or bleeding suspected. No diarrhea, bleeding, or systemic signs are present. Unnecessary before a therapeutic constipation trial.  </p></br><p>D. Sweat chloride test<br /></br>• Rectal prolapse can be an early sign of cystic fibrosis, but those children typically have failure to thrive, recurrent respiratory infections, or greasy stools. This child is growing well and has hard (not bulky, fatty) stools—low pre-test probability.  </p></br><p>E. Upper GI series with small-bowel follow-through<br /></br>• Evaluates malrotation, obstruction, or Crohn disease of small bowel. No vomiting, abdominal distension, or chronic diarrhea to warrant this test.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Core Takeaway  </p></br><p>In a thriving toddler with intermittent rectal prolapse and hard stools, constipation is the most likely precipitating factor. Treat with an osmotic laxative like polyethylene glycol before pursuing invasive or expensive diagnostics.</p>
134. Two days after undergoing elective right hip replacement, a hospitalized 85-year-old woman is confused and agitated and attempts to pull the intravenous line out of her forearm. She does not recognize her visiting family members and thinks she is in the grocery store. She is told repeatedly that she has just had hip surgery and is in the hospital but remains confused and agitated. She has osteoarthritis. Her medications are acetaminophen and morphine. She drank two glasses of wine weekly for 30 years but stopped drinking alcohol 10 years ago. Prior to hospitalization, she performed all activities of daily living independently. Her temperature is 37°C (98.6°F), pulse is 80/min, respirations are 16/min, and blood pressure is 110/70 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on room air shows an oxygen saturation of 99 percent. Examination shows a clean, dry surgical incision over the right hip. Cardiopulmonary examination shows no abnormalities. Neurologic examination shows no focal findings. On mental status examination, she is oriented to person but not to place or time. She follows simple commands. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Diazepam therapy</br>b. Diphenhydramine therapy</br>c. Donepezil therapy</br>d. Haloperidol therapy</br>e. Lumbar puncture</br>f. MRI of the brain</br>g. Placement in four-point restraints</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer: d. Haloperidol therapy  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Time course: “Two days after undergoing…surgery” with abrupt change in mental status → acute, not chronic.<br /></br>• Cognitive changes: Disorientation (place &amp; time), fluctuating attention, agitation, visual misperception (“thinks she is in grocery store”) → classic delirium triad (acute onset + fluctuating course + inattention).<br /></br>• Risk factors: Age &gt; 65, recent major surgery, opioid use, nighttime sleep disruption.<br /></br>• Pertinent negatives: Afebrile, normal vitals, clean incision, no focal neuro deficits → infectious CNS process, stroke, or hypoxemia less likely.<br /></br>Metacognitive takeaway: When an elderly inpatient suddenly becomes confused, default first diagnosis is postoperative delirium until proven otherwise. Once life-threatening etiologies are excluded, focus on safety and symptom control.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning / Management Algorithm<br /></br>1. Diagnose delirium.<br /></br>2. Search for and correct precipitants (pain, infection, urinary retention, hypoxia, medications, metabolic derangements). Those steps are already underway (vitals stable, incision clean, oxy­ sat 99%).<br /></br>3. Non-pharmacologic calming measures (reorienting, family presence, lights on, glasses/hearing aids). The vignette states these have been tried “repeatedly” without success.<br /></br>4. If patient remains severely agitated and is at risk of harming herself or staff (pulling out IV), FIRST-LINE drug is a low-dose, short-acting antipsychotic—classically haloperidol (0.5–1 mg PO/IV/IM).  </p></br><p>Thus haloperidol therapy is the most appropriate next step.</p></br><p>Key takeaway: Low-dose haloperidol is preferred pharmacologic treatment for severe agitation in delirium unless contraindicated (e.g., Parkinson disease, Lewy body dementia, prolonged QT).</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>Delirium = acute, reversible cerebral dysfunction caused by global cortical/subcortical neurotransmitter imbalance (↓ACh, ↑dopamine). Post-op contributors: inflammatory cytokines, anesthetics, opioids, sleep disruption, pain, unfamiliar environment. Older brain has lower reserve → easier decompensation.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Pearls<br /></br>• Non-pharm first: orienting cues, early mobilization, sleep hygiene, remove unnecessary lines &amp; meds, ensure hydration/pain control.<br /></br>• Pharmacologic ONLY if patient endangers self/others or non-pharm fails. Haloperidol, risperidone or other low-dose atypicals (quetiapine, olanzapine) acceptable; IV haloperidol has decades of experience and minimal anticholinergic or respiratory-depressant effect.<br /></br>• Avoid benzodiazepines except for alcohol/benzo withdrawal delirium.<br /></br>• Routine brain imaging, LP unnecessary without focal deficits, seizure, meningismus, fever, or immunocompromise.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“Elder HAL calms delirium” (HAL = Haloperidol).<br /></br>“Benzos for Booze, but Hal for Hospital delirium.”<br /></br>Think DDD: Delirium → Dopamine block (Haloperidol) after non-Drug measures fail.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>a. Diazepam therapy – Benzodiazepines worsen confusion in elderly and prolong delirium; indicated only for withdrawal states.<br /></br>b. Diphenhydramine therapy – Anticholinergic; classically precipitates or worsens delirium.<br /></br>c. Donepezil therapy – Acetylcholinesterase inhibitor for chronic Alzheimer dementia, takes weeks to work; not useful acutely.<br /></br>e. Lumbar puncture – No fever, meningismus, or immunosuppression; delirium far more likely than meningitis/encephalitis.<br /></br>f. MRI of the brain – No focal neuro deficits, stepwise changes, or suspicion of stroke; imaging not first-line.<br /></br>g. Placement in four-point restraints – Physical restraints increase agitation, risk of injury, and should follow, not precede, pharmacologic calming when needed.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line<br /></br>Acute postoperative agitation in an elderly patient = delirium. After non-pharmacologic attempts, treat dangerous agitation with low-dose haloperidol to protect the patient and staff while continuing to search for reversible precipitants.</p>
135. A 62-year-old woman with stage 4 chronic kidney disease comes to the physician for a routine examination. She feels well and was last seen by a physician 3 months ago. She also has type 2 diabetes mellitus and hypertension. Her medications are short- and long-acting insulin, simvastatin, enalapril, metoprolol, calcium carbonate, sodium citrate, sevelamer, and aspirin. Her temperature is 36.7°C (98°F), pulse is 58/min, respirations are 16/min, and blood pressure is 135/75 mm Hg. Cardiopulmonary and abdominal examinations show no abnormalities. Dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial pulses are palpable bilaterally. Monofilament testing shows decreased sensation over both feet. Proprioception is intact. Laboratory studies show:</br>Hemoglobin                           8.5 g/dL  </br>Hematocrit                           27 percent  </br>Mean corpuscular volume              85 µm³  </br>Platelet count                       165,000/mm³  </br>Serum  </br>  Na⁺                                135 mEq/L  </br>  K⁺                                 4.9 mEq/L  </br>  HCO₃⁻                              22 mEq/L  </br>  Ca²⁺                               8.4 mg/dL  </br>  Urea nitrogen                      40 mg/dL  </br>  Creatinine                         3.6 mg/dL  </br>  Phosphorus                         4.6 mg/dL  </br>Estimated glomerular filtration rate 25 mL/min (N > 60)  </br>Before beginning erythropoietin therapy, which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Bone marrow biopsy  </br>b. Measurement of serum copper and zinc concentrations  </br>c. Measurement of serum haptoglobin concentration and serum lactate dehydrogenase activity  </br>d. Measurement of serum iron concentration and total iron-binding capacity  </br>e. Measurement of serum vitamin B₁₂ (cobalamin) and folate concentrations  </br>f. Serum protein electrophoresis</br></br></br><p>Correct answer (per key): d. Measurement of serum iron concentration and total iron-binding capacity (TIBC)</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────</br>VIGNETTE ANALYSIS</br>• 62-y-o woman with stage 4 CKD (eGFR 25 mL/min) → very common cause of normocytic anemia due to ↓ erythropoietin (EPO) production.<br /></br>• Hgb 8.5 g/dL, Hct 27%, MCV 85 μm³ → normocytic, non-hemolytic anemia.<br /></br>• No bleeding, no signs of hemolysis, normal platelets, stable vitals.<br /></br>Key USMLE clue: “Before beginning erythropoietin therapy…” → the exam is testing the standard work-up that must precede initiation of an erythropoiesis-stimulating agent (ESA).</p></br><p>Cognitive framework:<br /></br>1. Identify anemia in CKD patient.<br /></br>2. Consider ESA, BUT: guideline says first document adequate iron stores, because iron deficiency is common and will blunt response to ESA.<br /></br>3. Therefore obtain iron studies (ferritin, TSAT; NBME sometimes phrases as serum Fe + TIBC).</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────</br>CLINICAL CRITICAL REASONING</br>Step-wise approach:<br /></br>1. CKD → ↓ renal EPO → normocytic anemia.<br /></br>2. ESA indicated when Hb &lt;10 g/dL after other causes excluded.<br /></br>3. ESA will rapidly increase iron utilization → “functional” iron deficiency can develop even if baseline ferritin is normal.<br /></br>4. Therefore KDIGO &amp; KDOQI guidelines: Check ferritin &amp; transferrin saturation (TSAT). If TSAT &lt;20% or ferritin &lt;100 ng/mL (&lt;200 ng/mL if on dialysis) give IV or oral iron before / with ESA.<br /></br>Core takeaway: Iron study evaluation is mandatory prior to ESA therapy.</p></br><p>Documentation (real life): “Iron studies ordered to assess ferritin &amp; TSAT prior to ESA initiation.”</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────</br>PATHOPHYSIOLOGY REVIEW</br>Anemia of CKD<br /></br>• Etiology: ↓ EPO production, uremic toxins shorten RBC lifespan, blood loss from dialysis, poor GI absorption &amp; inflammation-mediated iron sequestration (↑ hepcidin).<br /></br>• Labs: Normocytic/normochromic or sometimes microcytic if concomitant iron deficiency; low retic count.<br /></br>• Treatment:<br /></br>  1. Optimize iron stores (TSAT ≥20%, ferritin ≥100–200 ng/mL).<br /></br>     – Oral ferrous sulfate 325 mg TID (if non-dialysis) or IV iron sucrose/ferumoxytol (if dialysis or intolerant).<br /></br>  2. ESA (epoetin alfa IV/SC or darbepoetin alfa) to keep Hb 10–11.5 g/dL.<br /></br>  3. Transfuse only if symptomatic or Hb &lt;7–8 g/dL.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────</br>TREATMENTS &amp; DIAGNOSTICS REVIEW</br>Iron studies panel includes:<br /></br>• Serum iron (Fe)<br /></br>• TIBC (proxy for transferrin)<br /></br>• Ferritin (acute phase reactant, storage)<br /></br>• TSAT = (serum Fe / TIBC) ×100%</p></br><p>Interpretation in CKD:<br /></br>• TSAT &lt;20% or ferritin &lt;100 (&lt;200 on HD) → iron deficiency (absolute or functional) → give iron.<br /></br>• Normal/↑ ferritin but low TSAT (&lt;20%) → functional iron deficiency from inflammation/hepcidin; still give IV iron.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────</br>MEMORIZATION TIPS</br>“ESA = Ensure Sufficient Atom (iron) first.”<br /></br>Visual: Before firing the rocket (ESA), fill the fuel tank (iron).<br /></br>Mnemonic: “CKD A.I.M.”<br /></br>A – Assess iron<br /></br>I – Iron repletion<br /></br>M – give epoetin (Medication)</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────</br>INCORRECT ANSWER ANALYSIS</br>a. Bone marrow biopsy – reserved for unexplained pancytopenia, suspected marrow infiltration, myelodysplasia; far too invasive for straightforward CKD anemia.<br /></br>b. Serum copper &amp; zinc – deficiencies cause sideroblastic or neutropenia; rare, not first step in CKD anemia.<br /></br>c. Haptoglobin &amp; LDH – evaluate hemolysis (low haptoglobin, high LDH, high indirect bili). Pt has no jaundice, schistocytes, ↑ retics.<br /></br>e. Vitamin B12 &amp; folate – deficiencies produce macrocytic anemia (elevated MCV), neurologic findings (B12). Her MCV is normal.<br /></br>f. Serum protein electrophoresis – screens for multiple myeloma (CRAB: hyperCalcemia, Renal failure, Anemia, Bone pain). No lytic lesions, protein gap, or hypercalcemia; CKD already explains renal dysfunction.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────</br>Bottom line: Always check iron studies (serum iron, TIBC ± ferritin/TSAT) and replete if low BEFORE starting erythropoietin in patients with CKD-related anemia.</p>
136. A 25-year-old woman is admitted to the hospital because of a 1-week history of increasing ptosis, diplopia, slurred speech, and difficulty walking up stairs. She has a 3-year history of myasthenia gravis; her symptoms have been well controlled with prednisone during the past year. She has no other history of serious illness and takes no other medications. Examination shows fluctuating bilateral ptosis, disconjugate gaze, nasal speech, mild bilateral facial weakness, soft voice, and difficulty generating a cough. Muscle strength varies from 3/5 to 4/5 in the shoulders and hips. Which of the following is the most appropriate method of measuring this patient’s respiratory status during hospitalization?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Continuous pulse oximetry</br>b. Daily arterial blood gas analysis</br>c. Daily chest x-ray</br>d. Serial incentive spirometry</br>e. Serial measurements of forced vital capacity</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>E.  Serial measurements of forced vital capacity (FVC)</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key phrases/clues<br /></br>• “3-year history of myasthenia gravis … prednisone” – establishes a neuromuscular junction disease.<br /></br>• “Increasing ptosis, diplopia, slurred speech, difficulty walking up stairs” – typical fluctuating MG weakness.<br /></br>• Bulbar/respiratory signs: “nasal speech, mild facial weakness, soft voice, difficulty generating a cough.”<br /></br>• Proximal limb weakness 3-4/5.  </p></br><p>Pertinent positives: bulbar weakness + weak cough → high risk for impending myasthenic crisis (respiratory failure).<br /></br>Pertinent negatives: no mention of infection or medications that precipitate crisis, but she clearly is worsening.  </p></br><p>Metacognitive approach<br /></br>1. Recognize the diagnosis: myasthenic crisis risk.<br /></br>2. Ask: What parameter best predicts/monitors neuromuscular respiratory failure?<br /></br>3. Recall bedside pulmonary‐function thresholds (FVC &lt; 15–20 mL/kg or negative inspiratory force &lt; –30 cm H₂O) trigger elective intubation.<br /></br>4. Choose option that gives that data → serial FVC.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>• In neuromuscular disorders, ventilation (CO₂ removal) fails before oxygenation; hypoxemia and pulse-ox changes appear LATE.<br /></br>• Bedside spirometry (FVC and negative inspiratory force) falls early and is the most sensitive indicator.<br /></br>• Trending the value every few hours lets you decide when to intubate before a sudden crash.<br /></br>Core takeaway: In myasthenic (or Guillain-Barré) crisis, serial FVC ± NIF is the standard monitoring tool.</p></br><p>Documentation points (real patient)<br /></br>– Baseline FVC and NIF values, trend frequency, criteria for calling critical care/intubation plan.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>Myasthenia gravis: auto-antibodies to postsynaptic ACh receptors → fatigable skeletal-muscle weakness. Bulbar and diaphragmatic involvement jeopardize airway clearance and ventilation. As respiratory muscles weaken, tidal volume and thus FVC drop. Hypercapnia follows; hypoxemia appears last.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Diagnostic &amp; Management Pearls  </p></br><ol></br><li>Monitor:<br /></br>   • FVC q2–4 h (goal &gt; 20-25 mL/kg).<br /></br>   • Negative inspiratory force (NIF) &gt; –30 cm H₂O.  </li></br><li>Intubate when: FVC &lt; 15–20 mL/kg, NIF less negative than –20 to –25 cm H₂O, or rapid downward trend.  </li></br><li>Treat crisis: IVIG or plasmapheresis + corticosteroids, address triggers.  </li></br><li>Avoid respiratory depressants (opiates, benzos) unless ventilated.</li></br></ol></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“MG = Measure Gas”<br /></br>• M – Myasthenia<br /></br>• G – Get FVC (Gas volume) every shift<br /></br>Remember: “Pulse-ox is LATE, FVC is GREAT.”</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Incorrect Answer Breakdown  </p></br><p>a. Continuous pulse oximetry<br /></br>   – Monitors oxygenation, not ventilation; O₂ saturation can stay normal while CO₂ rises (type II respiratory failure).  </p></br><p>b. Daily arterial blood gas<br /></br>   – ABG reflects ventilation but is invasive, infrequent, and abnormalities appear after FVC has already fallen.  </p></br><p>c. Daily chest x-ray<br /></br>   – Detects atelectasis/pneumonia but gives no dynamic information on ventilatory muscle strength.  </p></br><p>d. Serial incentive spirometry<br /></br>   – A therapy to prevent atelectasis, not a quantitative monitor of respiratory muscle power. It does not give FVC.  </p></br><p>e. Serial FVC (correct)<br /></br>   – Direct, rapid, bedside, reproducible measure of vital capacity ↓ early in neuromuscular weakness; guides timely intubation.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>High-Yield Take-Home Message  </p></br><p>In neuromuscular diseases (myasthenia gravis, Guillain-Barré), serial forced vital capacity ± NIF is the gold-standard bedside monitor to detect impending respiratory failure—intervene before hypoxemia develops.</p>
137. Four weeks after undergoing cardiac catheterization because of chest pain, a 60-year-old man comes to the physician because of a 3-day history of a rash over his legs. Six hours before catheterization, administration of 0.9 percent saline was begun and continued 24 hours postoperatively. He has a 25-year history of type 2 diabetes mellitus complicated by diabetic nephropathy and hepatitis C. His medications are insulin, lisinopril, simvastatin, metoprolol, and aspirin. A photograph of the rash is shown. Examination shows no other abnormalities. His serum creatinine concentration was 1.6 mg/dL before the procedure, 1.5 mg/dL 48 hours after the procedure, and 3.5 mg/dL at today’s visit. Urinalysis today shows numerous WBC/hpf and no casts. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Acute tubular necrosis</br>b. Atheroembolic renal disease</br>c. Contrast-induced nephropathy</br>d. Cryoglobulinemia vasculitis</br>e. Progressive diabetic nephropathy</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>B. Atheroembolic renal disease  </p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li>Key time clue<br /></br>   • Renal function was stable for the first 48 h after contrast, then rose markedly 4 weeks later (Cr 1.5 → 3.5 mg/dL).<br /></br>   • Atheroembolic disease classically presents days-to-weeks after an arterial manipulation (cardiac catheterization, angiography, AAA repair).  </li></br><li>Skin findings<br /></br>   • Photograph (not shown here) in these NBME items typically depicts livedo reticularis, “blue toe,” or petechial/purpura‐like lesions on the lower extremities. These are hallmarks of cholesterol crystal embolization.  </li></br><li>Urinalysis pattern<br /></br>   • Numerous WBCs/hpf, no casts.<br /></br>   • Cholesterol emboli cause a tubulointerstitial and small-vessel inflammatory reaction → sterile pyuria or eosinophiluria; casts are usually absent.  </li></br><li>Risk factors/supporting data<br /></br>   • Elderly man with extensive atherosclerosis risk (DM, HTN, hyperlipidemia).<br /></br>   • Underwent an invasive vascular procedure (catheter tip scrapes aortic plaque).  </li></br></ol></br><p>Cognitive/metacognitive framework for similar questions<br /></br>• First separate AKI causes by timing after contrast:<br /></br>  – 24-48 h ⇒ contrast nephropathy.<br /></br>  – Days-weeks ⇒ atheroembolic disease.<br /></br>• Look for extra-renal clues (skin lesions, GI ischemia, retinal Hollenhorst plaques) that point toward cholesterol crystal shower.<br /></br>• UA: think “nothing dramatic” (few casts). If you see muddy brown casts → ATN; RBC casts → glomerulonephritis.  </p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (Step-wise)  </p></br><p>Step 1: Identify the inciting event – arterial instrumentation.<br /></br>Step 2: Note delayed onset of renal dysfunction (weeks, not hours).<br /></br>Step 3: Look for systemic small‐vessel signs – livedo, blue toes.<br /></br>Step 4: Match UA pattern – bland or WBCs, not muddy brown casts or heavy protein/RBC casts.<br /></br>Core takeaway: Cholesterol crystal emboli cause subacute AKI plus cutaneous ischemia after vascular procedures.  </p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real patient)<br /></br>• New AKI with creatinine 3.5 mg/dL 4 wk post-catheterization.<br /></br>• Livedo reticularis of both legs.<br /></br>• Likely atheroembolic renal disease; plan: supportive care, discontinue anticoagulation that might mobilize plaques, initiate high-intensity statin, monitor renal function, consider nephrology referral.  </p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• Mechanism: Mechanical disruption of an atherosclerotic plaque → showers of cholesterol crystals → lodge in renal arterioles &amp; other small vessels → end-organ ischemia &amp; inflammatory reaction (eosinophils, complement activation).<br /></br>• Typical labs: eosinophilia, eosinophiluria, hypocomplementemia (C3 low).<br /></br>• Organs: kidneys (AKI), skin (livedo, blue toe), GI (mesenteric ischemia), CNS (stroke, confusion), retinal Hollenhorst plaques.  </p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>Diagnostic &amp; Treatment Review  </p></br><p>Diagnostics<br /></br>• Clinical diagnosis (history + rash + timing).<br /></br>• Supporting tests: CBC (eosinophilia), serum complement ↓, UA (eosinophiluria).<br /></br>• Definitive proof: biopsy showing biconvex, needle-shaped cholesterol clefts, but rarely done.  </p></br><p>Management<br /></br>• No proven disease-specific therapy.<br /></br>• Supportive: optimize BP, fluids, stop offending anticoagulation if possible, dialysis as needed.<br /></br>• Secondary prevention: high-intensity statin, antiplatelet therapy, aggressive risk-factor modification.  </p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“Cath → Crystals” Mnemonic:<br /></br>CatHeterization causes CHolesterol showers Happening weeks later. Think livedo reticularis &amp; kidney injury.  </p></br><p>Visual cue: Blue toes = Blue plaques.  </p></br><p>Timing trick:<br /></br>• Contrast nephropathy = “CONtrast is CONcurrent” (within 2 days).<br /></br>• Atheroemboli = “After-event” (days-weeks AFTER the event).  </p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong  </p></br><p>A. Acute tubular necrosis<br /></br>   • Onset 24-72 h after ischemia/toxin, not 4 weeks later.<br /></br>   • UA would show muddy brown granular casts &amp; renal tubular epithelial cells.<br /></br>   • No skin findings.  </p></br><p>C. Contrast-induced nephropathy<br /></br>   • Creatinine peaks within 2-3 days and returns to baseline within 5-7 days.<br /></br>   • Patient’s creatinine was normal at 48 h and only rose 4 weeks later.<br /></br>   • No rash expected.  </p></br><p>D. Cryoglobulinemic vasculitis<br /></br>   • Hepatitis C association is correct, but triad = palpable purpura, arthralgia, peripheral neuropathy.<br /></br>   • Renal lesion is membranoproliferative GN → hematuria, RBC casts, low C4 (not just WBCs).<br /></br>   • No temporal relation to catheterization.  </p></br><p>E. Progressive diabetic nephropathy<br /></br>   • Causes slow, chronic rise over months-years, not an abrupt jump from 1.5 to 3.5 mg/dL in 4 weeks.<br /></br>   • UA would show albumin/proteinuria; no livedo reticularis.  </p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>High-Yield Take-Home Message  </p></br><p>Delayed (days-to-weeks) AKI plus livedo reticularis or “blue toe” after an invasive vascular procedure is classic for cholesterol atheroembolic renal disease. Supportive care and a high-intensity statin are mainstays of management.</p>
138. A 33-year-old woman, gravida 2, para 2, comes to the office for a health maintenance examination. Her pregnancies ended in spontaneous vaginal deliveries of healthy newborns at term. Her second pregnancy 3 years ago was complicated by gestational diabetes, which was controlled with diet. She has no other history of serious illness and takes no medications. She is sexually active and monogamous with her husband. She has no history of abnormal Pap smears; her last Pap smear was 2 years ago. Her mother was diagnosed with breast cancer at the age of 58 years. Her father had a hyperplastic polyp removed from his colon at the age of 51 years. There is no family history of heart disease or diabetes mellitus. Physical examination of the patient, including pelvic examination, shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Colonoscopy</br>b. Determination of hemoglobin A1c</br>c. Mammography</br>d. Measurement of serum thyroid-stimulating hormone concentration</br>e. Pap smear</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>B. Determination of hemoglobin A1c  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>1. Key clues<br /></br>   • 33-year-old woman, G2P2<br /></br>   • History of gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM) 3 years ago<br /></br>   • No current symptoms, here for routine health maintenance  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Pertinent positives/negatives<br /></br>   • GDM occurred ≥1 pregnancy and ≥3 years have elapsed → high long-term risk for type 2 diabetes (T2DM).<br /></br>   • No abnormal Paps → cervical cancer screening not yet due.<br /></br>   • Only a hyperplastic (not adenomatous) polyp in father → no early colonoscopy trigger.<br /></br>   • Mother’s breast cancer at 58 y → average-risk screening schedule.<br /></br>   • No personal symptoms suggesting thyroid disease.  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Algorithmic approach<br /></br>   Screen all adults with a prior history of GDM for persistent dysglycemia:</br>   • Initial 75-g OGTT at 4–12 weeks postpartum.<br /></br>   • If normal, repeat fasting plasma glucose or HbA1c every 3 years (ADA, ACOG, USPSTF B-level recommendation).  </p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Step-wise logic:<br /></br>1. Identify special risk factor → prior GDM.<br /></br>2. Recall guideline → q3-year diabetes screening with fasting glucose or HbA1c.<br /></br>3. She is exactly 3 years out → screening is due now.<br /></br>Core takeaway: Women with GDM remain at ~50% lifetime risk of T2DM; routine glycemic surveillance is mandatory.  </p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real clinic):<br /></br>• Last OGTT/HbA1c date and result<br /></br>• Plan: obtain HbA1c (or fasting glucose) today; counsel on diet/exercise; remind patient of need for lifelong surveillance.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review (high-yield)<br /></br>• GDM = insulin resistance driven by placental hormones (human placental lactogen, progesterone, cortisol).<br /></br>• Post-partum, most women revert to euglycemia, but β-cell dysfunction persists, predisposing to T2DM.<br /></br>• Risk escalates with: need for insulin during pregnancy, BMI &gt; 30 kg/m², early GDM diagnosis, or recurrent GDM.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Treatment/Diagnostic Test Review<br /></br>Diagnostic test: HbA1c today (or fasting plasma glucose/75-g OGTT).<br /></br>Interpretation:<br /></br>• HbA1c ≥6.5% = diabetes<br /></br>• 5.7–6.4% = pre-diabetes (counsel, lifestyle, possible metformin)<br /></br>• &lt;5.7% = normal (repeat in 3 y unless pregnancy or symptoms)  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“Gestational = Get Screened Again”:<br /></br>• Screen at 6 weeks post-partum → if normal, “3-year check-ups forever.”<br /></br>Mnemonic: “6-3-FOREVER” (6 weeks, then every 3 years, lifelong).  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Why the Other Options Are Wrong  </p></br><p>A. Colonoscopy<br /></br>• Average-risk screening starts at 45–50 y.<br /></br>• Early screening only if first-degree relative had colorectal cancer or advanced adenoma &lt;60 y. A hyperplastic polyp is benign and does not change recommendations.  </p></br><p>C. Mammography<br /></br>• Average-risk women begin at 40–50 y (USPSTF: 40–74 y q2 y; ACS: 45 y annual).<br /></br>• Single second-degree relative with cancer at 58 y is NOT high-risk requiring earlier screening. At 33 y, too early.  </p></br><p>D. Serum TSH<br /></br>• Routine population screening for thyroid disease is not universally recommended. Some societies suggest starting at 35 y then q5 y; USPSTF states evidence is insufficient. At 33 y with no symptoms, not indicated.  </p></br><p>E. Pap smear<br /></br>• Age 30–65: cytology q3 y OR high-risk HPV testing q5 y OR co-testing q5 y.<br /></br>• Her last Pap was 2 y ago → next cytology not due until 1 y from now.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Take-Home Message<br /></br>Women with any history of gestational diabetes require lifelong glycemic screening—initially at 4–12 weeks postpartum, then every 3 years with HbA1c or fasting glucose—to detect progression to type 2 diabetes mellitus early.</p>
139. A randomized study is conducted to assess the efficacy of two treatment regimens for acne vulgaris in 360 female high school students. The students are randomly assigned to receive an oral antibiotic and topical cream daily (Regimen A) or an oral contraceptive and topical cream daily (Regimen B). Adherence to each regimen is measured by monthly pill counts. After 3 months of therapy, results show adherence to Regimen A is 56 percent and adherence to Regimen B is 72 percent (P=0.04). The outcomes are shown:</br>Regimen A ( percent)   Regimen B ( percent)   P-Value</br>Subjective improvement   40           65       0.02</br>Erythema                 72           82       0.32</br>Pruritus                 14           18       0.45</br>Nausea                   33           18       0.04</br>Headache                  6           12       0.12</br>Which of the following best represents the number needed to treat with Regimen B for 3 months to create one additional patient benefit of subjective improvement?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. 2</br>b. 3</br>c. 4</br>d. 6</br>e. 8</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>C. 4  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues &amp; why they matter<br /></br>• “Randomly assigned” → eliminates selection bias; we can legitimately compare event rates.<br /></br>• Outcome of interest explicitly asked: “subjective improvement.”<br /></br>• Reported rates: 40 % (Regimen A) vs 65 % (Regimen B). These are event rates, not odds.<br /></br>• Question stem: “number needed to treat … to create one additional patient benefit” → this is a classic NNT calculation = 1 ÷ Absolute Risk Reduction (ARR).</p></br><p>Approach framework for similar questions<br /></br>1. Identify the EVENT RATE in control (CER) and experimental (EER) groups.<br /></br>2. Compute ARR = |EER − CER|.<br /></br>3. NNT = 1/ARR (always round UP to the next whole patient).</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Step-by-step linkage to answer<br /></br>1. Control event rate (CER) = 40 % = 0.40.<br /></br>2. Experimental event rate (EER) = 65 % = 0.65.<br /></br>3. Absolute Risk Reduction (ARR) = 0.65 − 0.40 = 0.25 (25 %).<br /></br>4. NNT = 1 / 0.25 = 4.<br /></br>Take-home: Treating 4 students with Regimen B instead of Regimen A for 3 months will yield one additional student who reports subjective improvement.</p></br><p>Real-world documentation note<br /></br>In practice the clinician would record the calculated NNT when discussing treatment options and shared decision-making, noting that a smaller NNT indicates greater clinical benefit.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Flash Review (why OCPs can help acne)<br /></br>• Combined oral contraceptives ↓ ovarian androgen production and ↑ sex-hormone–binding globulin → less free testosterone → ↓ sebum production &amp; inflammatory lesions.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatments &amp; Diagnostic Tests Review<br /></br>First-line pharmacologic acne options<br /></br>1. Topical retinoid ± benzoyl peroxide.<br /></br>2. Add topical or oral antibiotics if inflammatory.<br /></br>3. In females, combined OCPs or spironolactone when androgen-driven or refractory.<br /></br>4. Isotretinoin for severe nodulocystic acne.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“NNT = 1 over ARR; ARR is the ‘Absolute Requisite Reduction.’”<br /></br>Visualize four students in a row; treating all four with Regimen B paints one extra smiley face compared to Regimen A.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>a. 2 (implies ARR = 0.5). Would require improvement rate difference of 50 %, e.g., 40 % vs 90 %. Not supported by data.<br /></br>b. 3 (ARR ≈ 0.33). Would require ~33 % difference (40 % vs 73 %).<br /></br>d. 6 (ARR ≈ 0.17). Would require ~17 % improvement difference, not the observed 25 %.<br /></br>e. 8 (ARR = 0.125). Would correspond to ∼12.5 % difference, again inconsistent.</p></br><p>Common pitfall: confusing NNT with 1 / Relative Risk Reduction or forgetting to convert percentages to proportions.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line<br /></br>Absolute difference of 25 % in subjective improvement translates to an NNT of 4—meaning four adolescents need Regimen B for three months to produce one additional responder compared with Regimen A.</p>
140. A 55-year-old man comes to the office because of a 6-month history of progressive shortness of breath and fatigue with activities of daily living. He now has shortness of breath when walking to the mailbox and when walking up stairs in his home. He smoked one and one-half packs of cigarettes daily for 40 years but quit 1 year ago. He has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. He is 185 cm (6 ft 1 in) tall and weighs 110 kg (242 lb); BMI is 32 kg/m2. His pulse is 100/min, respirations are 18/min, and blood pressure is 150/90 mm Hg. On cardiac examination, S2 is loud and there is an S3. A grade 2/6 holosystolic murmur is heard best at the lower left sternal border. The liver span is 12 cm. There is 2+ pitting edema of the lower extremities. An AP x-ray shows flattened diaphragms and an enlarged cardiac silhouette. A lateral view shows filling of the retrosternal airspace. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Cardiac catheterization</br>b. Cardiac MRI</br>c. Cardiac stress scintigraphy</br>d. CT scan of the chest</br>e. Echocardiography</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>E. Echocardiography (transthoracic echo is the first-line imaging study for new-onset dyspnea when exam suggests cardiac dysfunction)</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues pointing toward a cardiac cause of dyspnea:<br /></br>• Progressive exertional dyspnea + easy fatigability for 6 months → think chronic heart failure.<br /></br>• Physical exam<br /></br>  – Loud P2 (pulmonary hypertension)<br /></br>  – S3 gallop (volume overload / systolic dysfunction)<br /></br>  – 2/6 holosystolic murmur at lower LSB → classic for tricuspid regurgitation, often secondary to RV dilation from ↑ pulmonary pressures or left-sided failure.<br /></br>  – Hepatomegaly (12-cm span) &amp; 2+ leg edema → systemic venous congestion.<br /></br>• CXR<br /></br>  – Enlarged cardiac silhouette → cardiomegaly/ventricular dilation.<br /></br>  – Flattened diaphragms &amp; retrosternal airspace suggest emphysema, but the overall picture (gallop, TR murmur, edema) favors heart failure with possible cor pulmonale.</p></br><p>High-yield pattern: “Dyspnea + S3 + holosystolic TR murmur + edema” → evaluate ventricular systolic function and pulmonary pressures with echocardiography first.</p></br><p>Approach/Framework for similar questions:<br /></br>1. Decide lung vs heart vs anemia/metabolic as the source of dyspnea.<br /></br>2. If PE strongly suggests cardiac involvement (extra heart sounds, murmurs, edema), order TTE because it is: noninvasive, widely available, no radiation, provides EF, wall motion, valve anatomy, and estimated pulmonary artery pressures.<br /></br>3. Reserve invasive or advanced tests only if echo is nondiagnostic or will not change management.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Step-wise thought process:<br /></br>1. Identify likely pathology → chronic heart failure with secondary pulmonary HTN &amp; TR.<br /></br>2. Ask: “What single test will confirm ventricular dysfunction, quantify EF, visualize valve incompetence, and estimate RV pressure?” → Transthoracic echocardiography.<br /></br>3. Echo results will guide next management steps: GDMT for HFrEF vs HFpEF, diuresis, work-up for ischemia, consideration of pulmonary vasodilator therapy, etc.</p></br><p>Core takeaway: In a stable outpatient with new signs of heart failure, a transthoracic echo is ALWAYS the initial diagnostic modality.</p></br><p>Documentation points: Indication (progressive exertional dyspnea, S3, peripheral edema), plan for TTE, counseling on heart-failure work-up, smoking cessation reinforced.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• Chronic smoking → COPD → chronic hypoxic pulmonary vasoconstriction → pulmonary hypertension → RV dilation → functional TR → systemic venous congestion.<br /></br>• Alternatively, primary LV systolic dysfunction (ischemic or non-ischemic cardiomyopathy) can secondarily raise pulmonary pressures and cause right-sided findings—echo distinguishes these scenarios by showing LV vs RV impairment and valve function.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────</br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Tests Review (brief, since question is on diagnosis)  </p></br><p>First diagnostic step: Transthoracic echo.<br /></br>Depending on findings:<br /></br>• Reduced EF → start GDMT: ACE inhibitor/ARB/ARNI (e.g., sacubitril-valsartan), beta-blocker (carvedilol, bisoprolol, metoprolol succinate), mineralocorticoid receptor antagonist (spironolactone), SGLT2 inhibitor, loop diuretic for symptoms.<br /></br>• Preserved EF with pulmonary HTN → treat underlying lung disease, consider diuretics, evaluate for OSA, etc.<br /></br>Further tests (later): coronary angiography if ischemia suspected, right-heart cath if unexplained pulmonary HTN remains after echo.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“Echo FIRST when you HEAR an extra heart sound.”<br /></br>Mnemonic ECHO:</br>E – Edema/effusion suspected<br /></br>C – Congestive heart failure signs (S3, dyspnea)<br /></br>H – Holosystolic murmur (regurgitation)<br /></br>O – Overloaded circulation (↑ JVP, hepatomegaly)</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────</br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>A. Cardiac catheterization<br /></br>• Invasive, not first-line. Indicated after non-invasive testing shows severe valvular disease or unclear pulmonary HTN etiology, or when planning revascularization. Echo should precede it.</p></br><p>B. Cardiac MRI<br /></br>• Excellent for tissue characterization and volumes but expensive and not first-line. Used when echo windows are poor or specific diagnoses (amyloidosis, myocarditis) are suspected.</p></br><p>C. Cardiac stress scintigraphy (nuclear stress test)<br /></br>• Evaluates inducible ischemia. Our priority is confirming baseline ventricular/valvular function first; patient likely can’t exercise adequately and ischemic symptoms are not described.</p></br><p>D. CT scan of the chest<br /></br>• Helpful for emphysema severity, malignancy, or pulmonary embolism. Does not provide functional cardiac data and won’t explain S3/murmur.</p></br><p>Echo is noninvasive, rapidly available, and directly answers the clinical question—therefore it is the correct initial diagnostic step.</p>
141. A 27-year-old man is brought to the emergency department 15 minutes after he was rescued from being trapped under debris for several hours following an earthquake. His right lower extremity was severely injured, and his urine is dark. His pulse is 120/min, respirations are 18/min, and blood pressure is 110/75 mm Hg. Physical examination shows a crush injury to the right lower extremity. Serum creatine kinase activity is 120,000 U/L. Additional laboratory studies are most likely to show which of the following findings in this patient?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Hypocalcemia</br>b. Hypokalemia</br>c. Hypomagnesemia</br>d. Hypophosphatemia</br>e. Hypouricemia</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer: a. Hypocalcemia  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• “Crush injury … trapped for several hours” + “dark urine” + CK 120 000 U/L → classic triad of rhabdomyolysis (muscle necrosis, myoglobinuria, very high CK).<br /></br>• Timing (minutes after extrication) places us in the EARLY phase of rhabdo, when intracellular contents have just flooded into the circulation.<br /></br>• Test-writers love the predictable electrolyte pattern:<br /></br>  – ↑K⁺, ↑PO₄³⁻, ↑Mg²⁺, ↑uric acid<br /></br>  – ↓Ca²⁺ (early)  </p></br><p>Key metacognitive tip: Whenever you see “crush injury + dark urine,” immediately run through the “KHUMP” mnemonic for released intracellular solutes: K⁺, H⁺ (acidosis), Uric acid, Myoglobin, Phosphate (with Mg²⁺ and CK tagging along). Calcium does the opposite (initially low).  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Step 1: Identify syndrome → rhabdomyolysis.<br /></br>Step 2: Recall electrolyte algorithm:<br /></br>  1. Muscle cells lyse → dump K⁺, PO₄³⁻, Mg²⁺, purines (→ uric acid) into plasma.<br /></br>  2. PO₄³⁻ binds circulating Ca²⁺; additional Ca²⁺ immediately gets sequestered into damaged myocytes. Result → acute hypocalcemia.<br /></br>Therefore, the lab abnormality “most likely” to accompany the presentation is hypocalcemia.  </p></br><p>Take-home: Early rhabdo = low Ca²⁺, high everything else. (During recovery/diuresis Ca²⁺ may rebound high.)  </p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real patient): note CK level, urine myoglobin, serial electrolytes (especially K⁺, Ca²⁺), renal function, and aggressive IV fluid resuscitation plan.  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Crush → prolonged ischemia → ATP depletion → loss of Na⁺/K⁺ pump integrity → cell swelling and lysis.<br /></br>• Contents released: myoglobin (renal tubular toxin), K⁺ (arrhythmia risk), PO₄³⁻, Mg²⁺, purines (→ uric acid).<br /></br>• Ca²⁺ drops acutely because:<br /></br>  1. Precipitation with excess PO₄³⁻ as CaPO₄.<br /></br>  2. Influx into damaged muscle due to sarcolemma leak.  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────<br /></br>Management Snapshot<br /></br>1. Large-volume isotonic saline ASAP (goal urine output ≥200–300 mL/hr).<br /></br>2. Treat hyperkalemia if ECG changes (IV calcium gluconate, insulin + dextrose, albuterol, bicarbonate).<br /></br>3. Consider sodium bicarbonate or mannitol to alkalinize urine / promote diuresis.<br /></br>4. Dialysis if refractory hyperkalemia, severe acidosis, or oliguric renal failure.<br /></br>(NOTE: Do NOT routinely give calcium unless patient is symptomatic or has K⁺-induced arrhythmia; correcting Ca²⁺ too early can worsen CaPO₄ deposition.)  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“Crush injury KHUMP”:<br /></br>K⁺ – Hyperkalemia<br /></br>H⁺ – Acidosis<br /></br>Uric acid – Hyperuricemia<br /></br>Mg²⁺ – Hypermagnesemia<br /></br>PO₄³⁻ – Hyperphosphatemia<br /></br>… and Ca²⁺ does the opposite (Hypocalcemia).  </p></br><p>Visual: Picture a smashed “KHUMP” camel losing its calcium hump!  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>b.
