Hypokalemia – FALSE. Cell lysis releases massive intracellular K⁺ → hyperkalemia, not hypo.<br /></br>c. Hypomagnesemia – FALSE. Mg²⁺ follows phosphate out of cells, so it is elevated.<br /></br>d. Hypophosphatemia – FALSE. Phosphate is released → hyperphosphatemia.<br /></br>e. Hypouricemia – FALSE. Purine breakdown produces uric acid → hyperuricemia.  </p></br><p>Common Pitfall: Confusing early hypocalcemia with late rebound hypercalcemia; always ask yourself “early or recovery phase?”  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────<br /></br>Core Takeaway<br /></br>Early rhabdomyolysis from crush injury causes hypocalcemia due to Ca²⁺ sequestration/precipitation, while K⁺, PO₄³⁻, Mg²⁺, and uric acid rise. Always screen and treat life-threatening hyperkalemia first while aggressively hydrating the patient.</p>
142. A 28-year-old woman, gravida 2, para 1, at 32 weeks’ gestation is brought to the emergency department because of progressive shortness of breath during the past day. She has had intermittent shortness of breath during the past 2 months that she initially attributed to pregnancy. She has not had fever. Her pregnancy has been otherwise uncomplicated. She has no history of serious illness. Her only medication is a prenatal vitamin. She does not smoke cigarettes. On arrival, she is in respiratory distress but is not using accessory muscles of respiration. She has difficulty speaking full sentences. She is 163 cm (5 ft 4 in) tall. She weighed 57 kg (125 lb) prior to her pregnancy; BMI was 22 kg/m2. She has had a 9-kg (20-lb) weight gain during her pregnancy. Her temperature is 37.5°C (99.5°F), pulse is 120/min and regular, respirations are 30/min, and blood pressure is 120/85 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on room air shows an oxygen saturation of 90 percent. Examination shows labored breathing. Diffuse inspiratory and expiratory wheezes are heard. Fundal height is 32 cm. The fetal heart rate is 140/min. In addition to administration of oxygen, which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Doppler ultrasonography of the lower extremities</br>b. Intravenous administration of heparin</br>c. Intravenous administration of methylprednisolone</br>d. Measurement of serum D-dimer concentration</br>e. Nebulized albuterol therapy</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>E. Nebulized albuterol therapy (short-acting inhaled β2-agonist)</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Key clues<br /></br>   • 32-week pregnant woman with 1-day history of “progressive shortness of breath.”<br /></br>   • Prior 2-month history of intermittent dyspnea → suggests chronic/reactive airway problem rather than an acute thromboembolic event.<br /></br>   • Diffuse inspiratory AND expiratory wheezes = classic asthma exam finding.<br /></br>   • Afebrile, no cough productive of sputum → infection less likely.<br /></br>   • Hypoxemic (SpO₂ 90% on room air) and tachypneic (RR 30), but normotensive → consistent with moderate asthma exacerbation.  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Pertinent negatives<br /></br>   • No pleuritic chest pain, hemoptysis, unilateral leg swelling → argues against pulmonary embolism (PE/DVT).<br /></br>   • No fever or crackles → argues against pneumonia.<br /></br>   • No accessory-muscle use or inability to speak full sentences completely → exacerbation is moderate, not imminent respiratory failure.  </p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>Cognitive framework:  In ANY patient (pregnant or not) with suspected asthma exacerbation you must (1) stabilize oxygenation, (2) relieve bronchospasm with a rapid-acting inhaled β2-agonist, and (3) add systemic steroids if the first two steps are insufficient.  Pregnancy does not change this algorithm—the fetus depends on maternal oxygenation.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (Why albuterol now?)  </p></br><p>Step-wise thinking:<br /></br>1. Give O₂ → already being done.<br /></br>2. Reverse bronchoconstriction quickly → inhaled SABA (nebulized albuterol) is fastest, safest, and evidence-based.<br /></br>3. Reassess; if inadequate response → add systemic corticosteroid (IV methylprednisolone) ± ipratropium, magnesium, etc.  </p></br><p>Core takeaway:  Acute asthma management in pregnancy = same as in non-pregnant patient; β2-agonists are FDA category C but extensive experience shows safety for mother and fetus. The fetal heart tracing is reassuring (140/min), so improving maternal oxygenation with albuterol is the priority.</p></br><p>Documentation pearls: respiratory rate, SpO₂, use of accessory muscles, response to SABA, fetal heart rate before/after treatment.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>Asthma = chronic airway inflammation → episodic bronchoconstriction, mucosal edema, mucus plugging → wheeze, dyspnea. Pregnancy can worsen asthma due to hormonal changes (↑ mucosal edema, altered smooth-muscle tone). Acute exacerbation causes ventilation-perfusion mismatch → hypoxemia (SpO₂ 90%). Maternal hypoxemia threatens fetal oxygen delivery, so rapid reversal is critical.</p></br><p>Albuterol = short-acting β2-agonist → activates adenylate cyclase → ↑cAMP in bronchial smooth muscle → bronchodilation within minutes.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────</br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Tests Review  </p></br><p>Acute asthma algorithm (pregnant or not):<br /></br>1. Oxygen to keep SpO₂ &gt;95%.<br /></br>2. SABA (albuterol neb 2.5 mg q20 min ×3 doses, then as needed).<br /></br>3. Add inhaled ipratropium for moderate/severe cases.<br /></br>4. Systemic corticosteroids (prednisone PO or methylpred IV) if inadequate response or prior steroid use.<br /></br>5. Adjuncts: IV magnesium sulfate for severe exacerbation, intubation if impending respiratory failure.</p></br><p>Diagnostic tests only after stabilization (peak flow, ABG if severe, chest x-ray if suspicion of pneumonia/pneumothorax).</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“OPAIR” for acute asthma (including pregnancy)<br /></br>O – Oxygen<br /></br>P – β2-agonist Puff (albuterol)<br /></br>A – Atrovent (ipratropium) if moderate/severe<br /></br>I – IV steroids if not improving<br /></br>R – Reassess / Repeat nebs</p></br><p>Visual: Picture a pregnant woman holding an “ALBert-blue” inhaler giving oxygen to her fetus balloon—reminds you albuterol + O₂ first.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────</br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>A. Doppler ultrasonography of the lower extremities<br /></br>   • Appropriate initial imaging if PE strongly suspected. However, diffuse wheezing + prior intermittent dyspnea highly favor asthma. No unilateral leg pain or swelling. Not an immediate therapeutic step for hypoxemia.</p></br><p>B. Intravenous administration of heparin<br /></br>   • Empiric anticoagulation is life-saving for massive PE but carries bleeding risk. Indicators for PE (sudden pleuritic chest pain, syncope, right-heart strain) are absent. Wheezing would be atypical for PE.</p></br><p>C. Intravenous administration of methylprednisolone<br /></br>   • Systemic steroids are part of exacerbation management but are NOT the first intervention after oxygen; inhaled β2-agonist provides faster bronchodilation. Steroids take 4–6 hours to work.</p></br><p>D. Measurement of serum D-dimer concentration<br /></br>   • D-dimer is normally elevated in pregnancy, making the test nonspecific and low-yield. Furthermore, diagnostic testing should not delay treatment of obvious asthma.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────</br>Bottom Line  </p></br><p>In a pregnant patient with signs of acute asthma exacerbation, administer supplemental oxygen and immediate inhaled short-acting β2-agonist therapy (nebulized albuterol). Pregnancy does NOT change the first-line treatment algorithm for asthma.</p>
143. A 25-year-old man, who is a welder, comes to the office because of difficulty at work since he was transferred to make emergency repairs on a bridge 1 week ago. He says he always has been very fearful of heights and cannot complete his work each day despite all the safety precautions that are in place. Until this project began, he was able to avoid projects that involved working in high places. He has called in sick for the past 2 days. He fears he will lose his job if he continues to stay home or cause an accident if he returns to work. He has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. Examination shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Buspirone therapy</br>b. Cognitive behavioral therapy</br>c. Fluoxetine therapy</br>d. Insight-oriented psychotherapy</br>e. Lorazepam therapy</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>B. Cognitive-behavioral therapy (CBT)</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis</br>• Key words: “very fearful of heights,” “always,” “avoids projects that involved working in high places,” now causing occupational impairment.<br /></br>• Pertinent positives: Long-standing (&gt;6 mo) marked fear of a specific situation (heights), immediate anxiety when exposed, avoidance, clinically significant distress/impairment.<br /></br>• Pertinent negatives: No spontaneous panic attacks in multiple settings, no worry about having a panic attack (rules out panic disorder &amp; agoraphobia); fear is not about social scrutiny (rules out social anxiety disorder); no excessive worry generalizing to multiple domains (rules out GAD).</p></br><p>Pattern: A discrete fear that is situation-specific = Specific Phobia. First-line treatment = CBT with graduated exposure (systematic desensitization).</p></br><p>Test-taking framework<br /></br>1. Identify stimulus (heights).<br /></br>2. Confirm 6-mo duration + avoidance + impairment.<br /></br>3. Recall algorithm: Specific phobia → CBT ± short-term benzo for infrequent unavoidable exposure (e.g., flight).<br /></br>4. Eliminate pharmacologic long-term options (SSRIs, buspirone) that work for other anxiety disorders, not phobias.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning</br>Specific phobia is best treated with skills-based CBT that includes:<br /></br>• Psychoeducation about conditioned fear response.<br /></br>• Cognitive restructuring of catastrophic beliefs (“I will fall even with safety gear”).<br /></br>• Graduated in-vivo or imaginal exposure to hierarchy of height-related situations until habituation.  </p></br><p>Document in chart: Diagnosis, discussion of CBT plan, informed consent for exposure therapy, referral to qualified therapist, reassurance regarding prognosis.</p></br><p>Core takeaway: Exposure-based CBT is the definitive, evidence-based therapy for specific phobia; medication is neither necessary nor as effective.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review (high-yield)</br>• Trigger → amygdala hyperactivation → sympathetic arousal.<br /></br>• Maintained by operant conditioning: avoidance = immediate anxiety reduction (negative reinforcement) → phobia persists.<br /></br>• CBT breaks this cycle through repeated safe exposure, promoting extinction learning and prefrontal inhibitory control over limbic response.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Pearls</br>First-line: CBT with exposure (most durable).<br /></br>Second-line/adjuncts:<br /></br>• Short-acting benzodiazepine (e.g., lorazepam) 30-60 min before an unavoidable single exposure (e.g., one-time MRI) but not daily—risk of sedation, dependence, reduced efficacy of exposure learning.<br /></br>• SSRIs helpful for social anxiety, panic disorder, GAD—not specific phobias.</p></br><p>No labs/imaging needed for diagnosis; it is clinical.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips</br>“FOBIAS” for Specific Phobia Tx<br /></br>F – Face the Fear (exposure)<br /></br>O – One stimulus (specific)<br /></br>B – Behavior therapy (CBT)<br /></br>I – Immediate anxiety on exposure<br /></br>A – Avoidance maintains fear<br /></br>S – Systematic desensitization</p></br><p>Visual aid: Picture a welder climbing a ladder rung-by-rung (gradual exposure) while wearing a hard hat labeled “CBT.”</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong</br>A. Buspirone – Anxiolytic for generalized anxiety disorder; ineffective for phobias.<br /></br>C. Fluoxetine – SSRI for panic, social anxiety, OCD, depression; not first-line for specific phobia and takes weeks to work—patient needs functional improvement sooner.<br /></br>D. Insight-oriented psychotherapy – Useful for personality disorders, trauma, or mood disorders where uncovering unconscious conflicts is therapeutic; no evidence it cures phobias.<br /></br>E. Lorazepam – Could transiently blunt anxiety, but daily use risks dependence, cognitive/motor impairment (dangerous for a welder at heights) and does not treat the underlying conditioned fear.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Bottom Line</br>A long-standing, situation-specific fear causing avoidance and functional impairment = Specific Phobia. The evidence-based, first-line management is exposure-based Cognitive-Behavioral Therapy.</p>
144. A 22-year-old man who is in the US Army is brought to the military base hospital because of severe groin pain since he was accidentally struck with the stock of a rifle 2 hours ago as he jumped from a personnel carrier. He has no history of major medical illness and takes no medications. Vital signs are within normal limits. Physical examination shows an ecchymotic, edematous scrotum. Urinalysis shows 3+ blood. Doppler ultrasonography of the scrotum shows decreased blood flow to the left testicle. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Application of ice and elevation of the scrotum, in addition to bed rest</br>b. Cystoscopy</br>c. Manual examination of the scrotum under anesthesia</br>d. Orchiectomy</br>e. Surgical exploration of the left scrotum</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>E. Surgical exploration of the left scrotum  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues<br /></br>1. Blunt scrotal trauma (rifle stock blow) → high‐energy mechanism.<br /></br>2. Rapid onset severe groin pain + marked ecchymosis/edema.<br /></br>3. Urinalysis: 3+ blood → suggests significant genitourinary injury.<br /></br>4. Doppler US: decreased blood flow to the left testicle → objective evidence of vascular compromise.  </p></br><p>Why these clues matter<br /></br>• Any post-traumatic testis with absent or diminished perfusion on Doppler is presumed to have either testicular torsion, testicular rupture, or spermatic cord injury until proven otherwise.<br /></br>• Testicular viability declines steeply after 4–6 h of ischemia. Time = testicle.<br /></br>• Algorithm:<br /></br>   – Scrotal trauma → Doppler US.<br /></br>   – Normal perfusion → conservative care.<br /></br>   – Abnormal/absent flow OR sonographic signs of tunica albuginea disruption/large hematocele → immediate surgical exploration.  </p></br><p>Metacognitive Pearl<br /></br>When a question pairs “scrotal trauma” with “abnormal Doppler flow,” automatically think “OR now.” Do not get distracted by supportive measures or diagnostic detours.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Step-wise thought process<br /></br>1. Determine injury severity: blunt trauma + hematuria already raises concern.<br /></br>2. Use Doppler to assess blood flow: decreased flow = threatened viability.<br /></br>3. Management goal: salvage the testis. Only surgery can untwist, debride, repair tunica albuginea, evacuate hematocele, and perform orchiopexy.<br /></br>4. Therefore, surgical exploration is the definitive next step.  </p></br><p>Core takeaway<br /></br>Compromised testicular blood flow after trauma mandates prompt scrotal exploration—delay risks irreversible infarction and need for orchiectomy.  </p></br><p>Documentation points (real patient)<br /></br>• Mechanism and timing of injury<br /></br>• Physical findings (ecchymosis, swelling, tenderness)<br /></br>• Doppler result (↓ arterial flow)<br /></br>• Decision for emergent scrotal exploration + informed consent discussion  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>Testicular rupture: tear of the tough tunica albuginea → extrusion of seminiferous tubules + hematoma that can tamponade arterial inflow.<br /></br>Traumatic torsion: sudden rotation of the testis within a swollen hemiscrotum can kink the spermatic cord.<br /></br>Either scenario obstructs arterial inflow → ischemia → infarction &gt;6 h.  </p></br><p>Risk factors: high-impact blunt trauma, athletic or combat settings, cremasteric spasm.  </p></br><p>Clinical manifestations: acute pain, swelling, ecchymosis, hematocele, often gross or microscopic hematuria.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Tests Review  </p></br><ol></br><li>Imaging: Color Doppler US is the study of choice (rapid, bedside, no ionizing radiation).<br /></br>   • Normal/↑ flow → conservative therapy.<br /></br>   • ↓/absent flow OR ruptured tunica → OR.  </li></br><li>Surgery:<br /></br>   • Hematoma evacuation.<br /></br>   • Debridement/reconstruction of tunica albuginea.<br /></br>   • Detorsion if necessary.<br /></br>   • Bilateral orchiopexy to prevent future torsion.  </li></br><li>Orchiectomy ONLY if testicular tissue is non-viable intra-op.  </li></br><li>Post-op: scrotal support, antibiotics (e.g., cefazolin) to prevent infection.  </li></br></ol></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>Mnemonic: “No Flow? Go!” (No Doppler flow → Go to OR).<br /></br>Visualization: Picture the testis as a grape inside a tough skin (tunica albuginea). If the skin tears or the stem twists, the grape’s juice (blood) can’t get in—must open the skin to save the grape.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>A. Ice, elevation, bed rest<br /></br>• Appropriate for minor contusions WITH NORMAL blood flow. Would miss a salvageable torsion/rupture → leads to infarction and later orchiectomy.  </p></br><p>B. Cystoscopy<br /></br>• Evaluates bladder/urethra, not testicular perfusion. Hematuria here is secondary to scrotal injury, not intravesical trauma needing scope.  </p></br><p>C. Manual examination under anesthesia<br /></br>• Attempted manual detorsion is blind and unreliable, especially in swollen scrotum. Even if successful, you still need operative fixation. Skips definitive repair of possible rupture.  </p></br><p>D. Orchiectomy<br /></br>• Premature. The goal is salvage. Only if exploration reveals a non-viable testis should orchiectomy be performed.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line  </p></br><p>Abnormal or absent testicular blood flow after blunt scrotal trauma mandates immediate surgical exploration to maximize testicular salvage and fertility potential.</p>
145. A 66-year-old woman with rheumatoid arthritis comes to the office because of a 1-year history of daily, slowly progressive, severe pain in her knees. She is now unable to walk more than one block. During the past 5 years, her pain has been controlled with etanercept. She has no other history of serious illness and takes no other medications. Vital signs are within normal limits. Examination of the knees shows nodularity and mild swelling but no warmth. There is mild varus deformity, which is corrected to normal valgus with gentle manipulation. There is moderate tenderness to palpation of the medial and lateral joint lines bilaterally. Range of motion of the knees is to 90 degrees of flexion and produces pain. X-rays of both knees show joint destruction, bony erosions, and 5 degrees of varus malalignment; joint spaces are not visible. Which of the following is the most appropriate recommendation to achieve a good long-term prognosis in this patient?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Adding daily oral celecoxib therapy to the medication regimen</br>b. Bilateral total knee replacement</br>c. Intra-articular corticosteroid injections</br>d. Physical therapy</br>e. Switching from etanercept to infliximab</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>b. Bilateral total knee replacement  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Chronic, progressive knee pain (1 year) now limiting ambulation to &lt;1 block = marked functional impairment.<br /></br>• Rheumatoid arthritis (RA) already treated for 5 years with a potent biologic (etanercept) → medical therapy maximized.<br /></br>• Exam: nodularity, mild swelling, no warmth → little active synovitis; correctable varus deformity; ROM limited (≤90°) and painful → mechanical/end-stage damage.<br /></br>• X-ray: “joint destruction, bony erosions, joint spaces are not visible, 5° varus malalignment” = end-stage RA arthropathy.  </p></br><p>Together these findings satisfy classic surgical indications: severe pain + major functional limitation + radiographic end-stage joint damage despite optimal medical therapy.  </p></br><p>Metacognitive approach for similar stems:<br /></br>1. Ask “Is there still active inflammation or is this mechanical end-stage disease?”<br /></br>2. Decide if additional medical therapy can realistically reverse the process.<br /></br>3. If joint space is gone or deformity fixed/advanced → think arthroplasty.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>• RA initially managed with disease-modifying antirheumatic drugs (DMARDs) &amp; biologics to prevent damage.<br /></br>• Once articular cartilage is completely destroyed (no visible joint space) and erosions cause pain/deformity, nothing short of replacing the joint restores function.<br /></br>• Total knee arthroplasty reliably improves pain, corrects alignment, and restores mobility; long-term survival of modern implants ≈ 90 % at 10–15 years.<br /></br>Documentation if real patient: objective functional limitations, failure of conservative therapy, radiographic grade, discussion of risks/benefits of surgery, referral to orthopedics.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• RA → chronic synovitis → pannus formation → cartilage destruction + marginal bone erosions.<br /></br>• Loss of cartilage = loss of joint space; varus/valgus malalignment develops as the supporting bone collapses.<br /></br>• Biologics slow but do not always halt damage; once bone-on-bone contact occurs, only mechanical replacement alleviates pain.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Pearls<br /></br>First-line RA: methotrexate ± other DMARDs → biologic (eg, etanercept, infliximab) if inadequate.<br /></br>End-stage joint (Kellgren-Lawrence grade IV or Larsen stage IV):<br /></br>1. Optimize pain control/assistive devices (short-term).<br /></br>2. Definitive: total joint arthroplasty.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“E for End-stage, E for End-prosthesis”:<br /></br>• Erosions + Extinguished joint space + Everyday activities limited → End with joint rEplacement.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong  </p></br><p>a. Daily celecoxib<br /></br>• NSAID only offers temporary analgesia; does not correct deformity or restore absent cartilage. Long-term prognosis (function) remains poor.  </p></br><p>c. Intra-articular corticosteroid injections<br /></br>• Provide short (weeks) symptom relief for synovitis or mild OA. Ineffective once bone-on-bone and gross deformity present. Repeated injections can further damage cartilage/bone.  </p></br><p>d. Physical therapy<br /></br>• Valuable early for strength &amp; ROM, but cannot overcome structural loss of joint space or correct malalignment; will not give “good long-term prognosis” here.  </p></br><p>e. Switch to infliximab<br /></br>• Another TNF-α inhibitor; unlikely to benefit when active inflammation is minimal and structural damage already complete. Changing biologics will not regenerate cartilage.  </p></br><p>Key takeaway: In RA patients with radiographic end-stage destruction and severe disability despite optimized medical therapy, total joint replacement is the definitive step for restoring long-term function.</p>
146. A 13-month-old girl is brought to the office because of a 1-month history of progressive loss of developmental skills. During this time, she has become increasingly uncoordinated and has lost her ability to pull to a standing position. She also has had difficulty grasping and reaching. Her parents note that she no longer plays with toys and crayons, has been less interactive, and has had increasingly poor eye contact. She used to babble, imitate sounds, and say “mama” and “dada,” but she has stopped using words. She has not had head trauma, fever, vomiting, or diarrhea. She has no history of serious illness and receives no medications. She was born at term following an uncomplicated pregnancy and spontaneous vaginal delivery. At her last examination 4 months ago, growth and development were appropriate for age. Today, her length and weight have decreased from the 50th to the 25th percentile, and her head circumference has decreased from the 30th to below the 5th percentile. Examination shows no dysmorphic features. The patient is minimally interactive and makes poor eye contact. She is not in acute distress. She does not reach for toys presented to her. When pulled to a standing position, she has poorly coordinated truncal movements and gait, even with support. MRI of the brain shows microcephaly; no other abnormalities are noted. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Autism spectrum disorder</br>b. Congenital cytomegalovirus infection</br>c. Hypoxic ischemic encephalopathy</br>d. Mitochondrial myopathy</br>e. Rett syndrome</br>f. Subclinical seizure disorder</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer: e. Rett syndrome  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>1. Age &amp; Sex: 13-month-old GIRL → Rett is seen almost exclusively in girls (X-linked dominant mutation in MECP2; hemizygous males usually die in utero).<br /></br>2. Normal early development followed by REGRESSION beginning 6–18 mo:<br /></br>   • Loss of previously acquired motor (pulling to stand), language (“mama,” “dada”), and social skills (poor eye contact, decreased interaction).<br /></br>3. Deceleration of head growth: head circumference fell from 30th %ile → &lt;5th %ile (“acquired microcephaly”)—nearly pathognomonic for Rett.<br /></br>4. Motor findings: truncal ataxia/poorly coordinated gait.<br /></br>5. Absence of acute inciting events (no trauma, fever, hypoxic episode).<br /></br>6. MRI: only microcephaly, no structural lesions—fits Rett (a neurodevelopmental, not destructive, process).  </p></br><p>Cognitive framework for future questions:<br /></br>• Ask: Was development ever normal? If yes, WHEN did regression start?<br /></br>   – 6–18 mo girl with regression + ↓ head growth → think Rett.<br /></br>• Look for loss of purposeful hand skills &amp; emergence of stereotyped hand-wringing (not yet described here but classic).<br /></br>• Check head-circumference percentiles in any regression question.</p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Step-by-step linkage to choice E:<br /></br>1. Epidemiology (girl, infancy).<br /></br>2. Pattern (normal development, then regression).<br /></br>3. Key distinguishing sign (acquired microcephaly).<br /></br>4. Gait/truncal ataxia, loss of hand use.<br /></br>These four features together are virtually diagnostic of Rett, making other etiologies unlikely.</p></br><p>Take-home message: In a previously normal infant girl, developmental regression between 6–18 months with decelerating head growth = Rett syndrome (MECP2 mutation).</p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real patient):<br /></br>• Detailed developmental milestones with dates.<br /></br>• Serial growth chart showing head-circumference drop.<br /></br>• Neurologic exam (tone, reflexes, gait).<br /></br>• Family history &amp; genetic counseling note.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Gene: MECP2 (methyl-CpG-binding protein 2) on Xq28.<br /></br>• Mechanism: aberrant transcriptional repression → widespread synaptic dysfunction.<br /></br>• X-linked dominant; random X-inactivation leads to variable severity.<br /></br>• Clinical course:<br /></br>  – 6–18 mo: normal → plateau → regression.<br /></br>  – Loss of speech, purposeful hand use; onset of hand-wringing, tapping, or clapping.<br /></br>  – Gait ataxia/apraxia, seizures, breathing abnormalities, scoliosis.<br /></br>• Prognosis: progressive motor impairment; most survive into adulthood with varying disability.</p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Management &amp; Diagnostic Work-up<br /></br>• Confirm with targeted MECP2 genetic testing.<br /></br>• Supportive care:<br /></br>  – Early intervention (PT/OT/speech).<br /></br>  – Manage seizures (levetiracetam, valproate, etc.).<br /></br>  – Treat scoliosis/contractures.<br /></br>  – Nutritional support; monitor QT interval (prolongation risk).<br /></br>There is no disease-modifying therapy yet, but clinical trials of gene therapy are ongoing.</p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>High-Yield Mnemonic<br /></br>“RETT”:<br /></br>R – Regression after initial normal development<br /></br>E – decelerating hEad growth (microcephaly)<br /></br>T – stereotyped hand-wringing moTions<br /></br>T – Truncal &amp; gait ataxia  </p></br><p>Visualization: Picture a toddler girl clapping (“hand-wringing”) while her growth chart head-circumference line dive-bombs off the curve.</p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong  </p></br><p>a. Autism spectrum disorder<br /></br>• Typically presents by age 2 with social/communication deficits and restricted interests, but DOES NOT show loss of head circumference percentiles or clear motor regression after previously normal motor milestones. Head growth often normal or ↑.  </p></br><p>b. Congenital cytomegalovirus infection<br /></br>• Findings present at birth: microcephaly, periventricular calcifications, hepatosplenomegaly, sensorineural hearing loss. This patient was healthy at birth and developed normally for 1 yr. MRI showed no calcifications.  </p></br><p>c. Hypoxic-ischemic encephalopathy<br /></br>• Occurs peri-natally (sentinel event during labor) → immediate neonatal neurologic abnormalities, not delayed regression months later.  </p></br><p>d. Mitochondrial myopathy<br /></br>• Often presents with exercise intolerance, lactic acidosis, myopathy, seizures; usually family history and systemic involvement (cardiomyopathy, optic atrophy). Head circumference deceleration is not characteristic, and regression begins later.  </p></br><p>f. Subclinical seizure disorder<br /></br>• Seizures can cause episodic regression, but head circumference would not plummet and examination/MRI would reveal cortical changes or epileptiform activity on EEG. Regression usually fluctuates, not steadily progressive over a month.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Key Memory Hook<br /></br>“Girl who was fine at 6 months, then stops talking, stops using her hands, and her head-circumference line falls off the chart = Rett.”</p>
147. An 18-year-old man comes to the urgent care center because of painful swelling below his right jaw that he first noticed on awakening this morning 2 hours ago. He also reports a 2-day history of malaise and mild headache, which he had attributed to jet lag after arriving in the United States from England 3 days ago to participate in a soccer tournament. He says that yesterday evening he did not feel like eating and had mild chills. Medical history is unremarkable and he takes no medications. Temperature is 37.9°C (100.2°F); other vital signs are within normal limits. Physical examination discloses an enlarged and painful right parotid gland; palpation of the left parotid gland discloses no abnormalities. There is no rash or discoloration of the skin, no pharyngeal erythema, and no axillary or inguinal lymphadenopathy. This patient is at increased risk for development of which of the following?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Focal segmental glomerulosclerosis</br>b. Hemolytic anemia</br>c. Non-Hodgkin lymphoma</br>d. Orchitis</br>e. Splenomegaly</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>d. Orchitis  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Sudden-onset “painful swelling below his right jaw” = parotid gland enlargement (parotitis).<br /></br>• Low-grade fever, malaise, headache, anorexia, chills = nonspecific viral prodrome.<br /></br>• Age 18 years (post-pubertal male) → places him in the group that develops classic mumps complications.<br /></br>• No pharyngeal erythema, rash, or generalized lymphadenopathy → argues against bacterial pharyngitis, infectious mononucleosis, or acute HIV.<br /></br>Key algorithm:<br /></br>Acute parotitis + viral prodrome + incomplete/unknown vaccination status → think mumps (paramyxovirus). In post-pubertal males, the most worrisome complication is orchitis, which can threaten future fertility.  </p></br><p>Metacognitive tip: When you see “parotid swelling” on NBME/Step questions, immediately run through the mnemonic “POM-PM”: Parotitis → Orchitis, Meningitis, Pancreatitis, (rare) deafness.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>1. Identify syndrome: unilateral (early) or bilateral parotid swelling + viral prodrome → mumps.<br /></br>2. Recall classic complications:<br /></br>   • Orchitis (testicular pain/swelling, may lead to infertility)<br /></br>   • Aseptic meningitis<br /></br>   • Pancreatitis<br /></br>   • Sensorineural deafness<br /></br>3. Prioritize complications by age/sex: In post-pubertal males, orchitis is most common and test writers love to ask about it.  </p></br><p>Documentation for a real encounter would include: vaccination history (MMR status), onset and character of swelling, systemic symptoms, GU symptoms, and counseling on contagion/isolation.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Virus: Mumps virus, an enveloped negative-sense RNA paramyxovirus that spreads via respiratory droplets.<br /></br>• Tropism for glandular tissue (parotid, testes, pancreas, CNS).<br /></br>• Testicular involvement causes lymphocytic infiltration → interstitial edema → pressure- mediated ischemia → risk of seminiferous tubule atrophy and subfertility.  </p></br><p>Risk factors<br /></br>• Lack of MMR vaccination or waning immunity (outbreaks in college/athletic settings).<br /></br>• Close-contact environments (military barracks, sports teams).  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostics<br /></br>• Diagnosis is clinical during outbreaks; can confirm with serum or buccal PCR for mumps RNA or IgM.<br /></br>• Management is supportive: NSAIDs for pain, scrotal support/ice packs if orchitis develops.<br /></br>• Notify public health, enforce 5-day isolation from onset of parotitis.<br /></br>Prevention = two-dose MMR vaccine; a third (“booster”) dose may be given during outbreaks.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“POM-PM” for Mumps complications:<br /></br>P – Parotitis<br /></br>O – Orchitis<br /></br>M – Meningitis (aseptic)<br /></br>P – Pancreatitis<br /></br>M – (sensorineural) Muffled hearing (deafness)  </p></br><p>Visual: Imagine an “orchid” flower growing from a swollen parotid gland—orchid → orchitis → testis-shaped.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>a. Focal segmental glomerulosclerosis – Linked to HIV, heroin, obesity, sickle cell disease; not a mumps complication.<br /></br>b. Hemolytic anemia – Seen with parvovirus B19 infection (aplastic crisis), Mycoplasma pneumoniae (cold agglutinins), EBV; not typical for mumps.<br /></br>c. Non-Hodgkin lymphoma – Associated with Epstein-Barr virus, HIV, immunosuppression; unrelated to mumps.<br /></br>e. Splenomegaly – Prominent in infectious mononucleosis (EBV/CMV) or hematologic malignancies. Mumps rarely affects the spleen.  </p></br><p>Take-home Message:<br /></br>Parotitis in an under-vaccinated young adult → think mumps; in males, always anticipate orchitis.</p>
148. A 50-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department because of a 6-day history of confusion, increased thirst, progressive fatigue, and constipation. She also has a 2-month history of moderate low back pain. She has hypertension treated with lisinopril. She is somnolent but easily arousable. She is oriented to person and place but not to time. Temperature is 37.2°C (99.0°F), pulse is 112/min, respirations are 18/min, and blood pressure is 100/60 mm Hg. On examination, the conjunctivae are pale. There is decreased axillary sweat. Cardiopulmonary examination shows no other abnormalities. Results of laboratory studies are shown:</br>Hemoglobin 9 g/dL</br>Leukocyte count 4000/mm³</br>Platelet count 150,000/mm³</br>Serum</br>  Na⁺ 136 mEq/L</br>  K⁺ 4 mEq/L</br>  Cl⁻ 100 mEq/L</br>  HCO₃⁻ 25 mEq/L</br>  Ca²⁺ 13.8 mg/dL</br>  Urea nitrogen 60 mg/dL</br>  Glucose 90 mg/dL</br>  Creatinine 1.4 mg/dL</br>  Protein, total 8.8 g/dL</br>  Albumin 3 g/dL</br>Intravenous administration of 0.9 percent saline is begun. Eight hours later, examination findings and laboratory study results are unchanged. In addition to continuing intravenous administration of fluids and beginning furosemide therapy, which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Bone marrow transplant</br>b. Cytarabine and daunorubicin therapy</br>c. Immune globulin therapy</br>d. Pamidronate therapy</br>e. Prednisone therapy</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer: d. Pamidronate therapy  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>VIGNETTE ANALYSIS<br /></br>• “Confusion, increased thirst, progressive fatigue, constipation” = classic neuro-GI signs of significant hypercalcemia (&gt;13 mg/dL).<br /></br>• Ca²⁺ 13.8 mg/dL despite 8 hrs of normal-saline resuscitation → refractory or severe hypercalcemia that needs a drug to lower serum calcium.<br /></br>• Normovolemic after fluids + about to start loop diuretic (furosemide) → next pharmacologic step is a bisphosphonate.<br /></br>• Anemia + leukopenia + back pain + ↑total protein with low albumin (globulin gap) → strongly suggests multiple myeloma as the underlying cause (i.e., osteolytic bone lesions → Ca release).  </p></br><p>Metacognitive framework for similar questions:</br>1. Confirm symptomatic hypercalcemia (often &gt;12 mg/dL).<br /></br>2. Start vigorous isotonic saline.<br /></br>3. Once euvolemic, add loop diuretic to enhance calciuresis.<br /></br>4. Give an anti-resorptive agent (IV bisphosphonate preferred) for long-term control if malignancy is suspected.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>CLINICAL CRITICAL REASONING<br /></br>• Mechanism: Hypercalcemia of malignancy (especially myeloma) is driven by osteoclast activation. The definitive way to lower calcium is to stop bone resorption.<br /></br>• Pamidronate (or zoledronic acid) is an IV bisphosphonate that binds hydroxyapatite and induces osteoclast apoptosis → falls in Ca begin in 24-48 h and are sustained.<br /></br>• Documentation points if this were a real patient: mental-status exam, volume status, urine output, current Ca trend, bisphosphonate consent, renal function monitoring.  </p></br><p>TAKE-HOME MESSAGE: After volume expansion ± loop diuretic, intravenous bisphosphonate is the standard next step for malignant hypercalcemia.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>PATHOPHYSIOLOGY REVIEW<br /></br>• Multiple myeloma → plasma-cell proliferation → osteolytic cytokines (IL-1β, TNF-α, RANK-L) → activated osteoclasts → Ca release + bone pain.<br /></br>• Hypercalcemia causes nephrogenic DI (thirst), decreased excitability (constipation), altered mental status.<br /></br>• Bisphosphonates mimic pyrophosphate, directly inhibit osteoclast resorption, and are renally cleared (hence monitor Cr).  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>TREATMENTS &amp; DIAGNOSTIC TESTS<br /></br>Immediate:<br /></br>1. Normal saline (200–300 mL/h) → correct volume depletion.<br /></br>2. Loop diuretic once euvolemic (furosemide) → calciuresis.<br /></br>3. IV bisphosphonate (pamidronate 60–90 mg over 2 h or zoledronic acid 4 mg over 15 min).<br /></br>Adjunct/alternative if needed:<br /></br>• Calcitonin (rapid but short-lived).<br /></br>• Hemodialysis for Ca &gt;18 or severe renal failure.<br /></br>Long-term myeloma management:<br /></br>• Chemotherapy ± autologous stem-cell transplant, but not part of emergent Ca control.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>MEMORIZATION TIPS<br /></br>“Saline, Loop, Bone-STOP” mnemonic for malignant hypercalcemia:<br /></br>Saline hydration → Loop diuretic → Bisphosphonate to STOP bone resorption.  </p></br><p>Think “Myeloma = M-spike, Medullary defects, Monoclonal plasma cells, and Massive Calcium.”  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>INCORRECT ANSWER ANALYSIS<br /></br>a. Bone marrow transplant – Autologous stem-cell transplant is definitive therapy for eligible myeloma patients, but it does not acutely lower calcium; takes weeks-months.<br /></br>b. Cytarabine and daunorubicin – Induction regimen for acute myeloid leukemia; irrelevant to hypercalcemia or myeloma.<br /></br>c. Immune globulin therapy – Used for immune thrombocytopenia, Guillain-Barré, Kawasaki, etc. No role in hypercalcemia.<br /></br>e. Prednisone therapy – Glucocorticoids lower Ca in vitamin D–mediated processes (sarcoidosis, lymphoma) but are less effective in myeloma-related osteolysis and not first-line after saline/loops.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom line: In symptomatic malignant hypercalcemia that persists after aggressive hydration, IV bisphosphonate (pamidronate) is the evidence-based next step.</p>
149. A 33-year-old woman comes to the clinic to discuss results of a recent Pap smear. She feels well. She has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. She does not smoke cigarettes or use illicit drugs. She drinks two cocktails daily. She is sexually active with three male partners; they use condoms inconsistently. The physician informs the patient that the Pap smear results show atypical squamous cells of undetermined significance, and test results are negative for high-risk human papillomavirus infection. The patient says she wants treatment to cure her infection. In addition to expressing empathy toward the patient, which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Cone biopsy</br>b. Counseling the patient on safer-sex practices</br>c. Cryotherapy</br>d. Loop electrosurgical excision of the cervical transformation zone</br>e. Repeat Pap smear in 6 months</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer: Counseling the patient on safer-sex practices (Choice B)</p></br><p>───────────────────────</br>VIGNETTE ANALYSIS<br /></br>• Screening result: “Atypical squamous cells of undetermined significance (ASC-US) AND high-risk HPV negative.”<br /></br>• Patient factors: 33 y/o, asymptomatic, no prior abnormal cytology stated.<br /></br>• Key phrase: “She wants treatment to cure her infection.” The result tells you there is no identified oncogenic HPV infection and no high-grade lesion to “treat.”<br /></br>• Guideline trigger: ASC-US + HPV (–) → NO immediate treatment; routine screening interval is acceptable.  </p></br><p>Metacognitive approach:<br /></br>1. Classify cytology (ASC-US vs LSIL vs HSIL).<br /></br>2. Check HPV status.<br /></br>3. Plug into ASCCP algorithm → decide on surveillance vs diagnostic excision.  </p></br><p>───────────────────────</br>CLINICAL CRITICAL REASONING<br /></br>• ASC-US is the most common abnormal Pap result and often reflects transient inflammatory/reactive changes.<br /></br>• Reflex high-risk HPV testing stratifies cancer risk. A negative HPV test = very low immediate risk for CIN 2/3 or cancer (&lt;1%).<br /></br>• Therefore, no ablative/excisional therapy is indicated.<br /></br>• The question tests the ability to reassure and provide preventive counseling when patients request unnecessary “treatment.”<br /></br>• In real practice you would document: “Discussed Pap/HPV results, explained low cancer risk, counseled on safer sexual practices and routine screening interval per guidelines.”</p></br><p>Core takeaway: ASC-US with negative high-risk HPV → routine age-based screening (e.g., repeat Pap or co-test in 3 y) + health-promotion counseling.</p></br><p>───────────────────────</br>PATHOPHYSIOLOGY REVIEW<br /></br>• HPV oncogenic subtypes (16, 18, etc.) drive progression from CIN → invasive carcinoma.<br /></br>• Without high-risk HPV DNA, progression probability is extremely low; hence aggressive procedures are unwarranted.  </p></br><p>───────────────────────</br>TREATMENT &amp; DIAGNOSTIC TESTS REVIEW<br /></br>Current ASCCP 2019 guidelines for ASC-US:<br /></br>1. If reflex HPV testing negative →<br /></br>   • Age 25-29: repeat cytology in 3 y.<br /></br>   • Age ≥30: repeat co-testing or HPV testing in 3 y.<br /></br>2. If HPV positive → either immediate colposcopy or repeat co-test in 1 y depending on algorithm.  </p></br><p>Diagnostic/therapeutic procedures (colposcopy, LEEP, cone biopsy, cryotherapy) are reserved for:<br /></br>• Persistent ASC-US with HPV+<br /></br>• LSIL with HPV+ (age ≥30)<br /></br>• HSIL, ASC-H, AGC, or obvious CIN 2/3 on biopsy.</p></br><p>───────────────────────</br>MEMORIZATION TIPS<br /></br>Mnemonic “A-N-R” for ASC-US, HPV Negative, Return in 3:<br /></br>A = ASC-US<br /></br>N = HPV Negative<br /></br>R = Return in 3 years  </p></br><p>───────────────────────</br>INCORRECT ANSWER ANALYSIS<br /></br>A. Cone biopsy – Excisional procedure reserved for suspicion/confirmation of CIN 2+ (e.g., HSIL cytology, CIN 2/3 on biopsy, discordant Pap/colpo). Over-treatment here would expose patient to cervical incompetence risks.<br /></br>C. Cryotherapy – Ablative therapy for biopsy-proven CIN 1-3 when transformation zone fully visualized; unnecessary without proven lesion.<br /></br>D. Loop electrosurgical excision procedure (LEEP) – Similar indications to cone; not first-line for ASC-US/HPV–.<br /></br>E. Repeat Pap smear in 6 months – Outdated; modern algorithms extend interval to 3 y when HPV negative to minimize false-positive harms.</p></br><p>───────────────────────</br>BOTTOM LINE<br /></br>ASC-US with negative high-risk HPV signifies very low oncogenic risk. The correct management is reassurance, routine rescreening in 3 years, and health-promotion counseling such as safer-sex practices.</p>
150. A 7-year-old boy is brought to the office by his mother because of a 1-week history of hair loss and a 1-month history of an enlarging, itchy lesion on his scalp. He has no history of serious illness. Vaccinations are up-to-date. Physical examination shows the findings in the photograph. Microscopic examination of a scraping of the lesion treated with KOH shows branching hyphae. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial pharmacotherapy for this patient?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Oral doxycycline</br>b. Oral terbinafine</br>c. Topical miconazole</br>d. Topical tacrolimus</br>e. Topical triamcinolone</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>• Oral terbinafine  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>1. Key phrases<br /></br>   • “1-month history of an enlarging, itchy lesion on his scalp” – chronic, pruritic plaque suggests dermatophyte infection.<br /></br>   • “1-week history of hair loss” – alopecia with broken hairs (“black dots”) is classic for tinea capitis.<br /></br>   • “KOH shows branching hyphae” – confirms dermatophyte (tinea) rather than bacterial or inflammatory process.  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Pertinent positives<br /></br>   • Child in elementary-school age group (highest incidence).<br /></br>   • Scalp involvement (hair shaft + follicle).  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Pertinent negatives<br /></br>   • No fever or lymphadenitis → less likely bacterial impetigo or kerion.<br /></br>   • No pustules/abscess → less likely folliculitis.  </p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>Cognitive &amp; metacognitive framework<br /></br>• Step 1: Identify a scaly, pruritic patch/alopecia → think dermatophyte.<br /></br>• Step 2: Location = scalp → remember that topical agents cannot penetrate the hair shaft; therefore systemic therapy is required.<br /></br>• Step 3: Choose an oral fungicidal agent with pediatric safety data → terbinafine (or griseofulvin).  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Take-home algorithm:<br /></br>Scalp lesion + scaling ± alopecia → KOH prep → branching hyphae → Tinea capitis → Oral antifungal (terbinafine 6 weeks or griseofulvin 6–8 weeks) + adjunctive selenium sulfide shampoo for household contacts.</p></br><p>Why oral terbinafine?<br /></br>• Fungicidal allylamine that accumulates in keratinized tissues and hair shafts.<br /></br>• Shorter course, higher cure rate, and better side-effect profile vs griseofulvin for Trichophyton tonsurans (most common U.S. pathogen).<br /></br>Documentation pearl: Record lesion size/appearance, KOH result, and plan for oral antifungal with baseline LFTs if therapy &gt;4–6 weeks.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Dermatophytes (Trichophyton, Microsporum, Epidermophyton) digest keratin via keratinase, invade stratum corneum, hair shafts, and nails.<br /></br>• In tinea capitis, fungi enter the hair shaft → hair breaks → “black-dot” alopecia and inflammatory patches.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Tests<br /></br>First-line pharmacotherapy<br /></br>• Oral terbinafine: 6 mg/kg/day (max 250 mg) for 4–6 weeks.<br /></br>Alternative/older first line<br /></br>• Oral griseofulvin (ultra-microsize) 20–25 mg/kg/day for 6–8 weeks; better for Microsporum spp.<br /></br>Adjuncts<br /></br>• Selenium sulfide or ketoconazole shampoo twice weekly to reduce spore carriage.<br /></br>• Treat close contacts if symptomatic; counsel on not sharing hats/combs.<br /></br>Baseline tests<br /></br>• LFTs if prolonged terbinafine; not routinely needed for short pediatric courses.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“Capitis = Can’t Cure with Creams → need Tablets.”<br /></br>“T for Tinea, T for Terbinafine (and Tablets).”  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>a. Oral doxycycline<br /></br>   • Antibacterial, not antifungal. Used for MRSA skin infection, acne, etc. No activity against dermatophytes.  </p></br><p>c. Topical miconazole<br /></br>   • Topical azole works for tinea corporis/pedis/cruris but cannot reach hair follicle; failure rates high in tinea capitis.  </p></br><p>d. Topical tacrolimus<br /></br>   • Calcineurin inhibitor for atopic dermatitis or vitiligo; no antifungal effect. Could worsen infection by local immunosuppression.  </p></br><p>e. Topical triamcinolone<br /></br>   • Potent corticosteroid; would suppress inflammation and mask infection, risking deeper invasion (Majocchi granuloma).  </p></br><p>Common Pitfall<br /></br>Seeing “antifungal” and picking a topical agent—remember that any dermatophyte infection of hair (capitis) or nails (onychomycosis) mandates oral therapy.</p>
151. A comatose 77-year-old woman with alcohol-induced cirrhosis is being treated for bacterial peritonitis and hepatic encephalopathy. After 1 week of antibiotic and lactulose therapy, she remains in a coma. She has a living will stating that she does not want resuscitation or artificial feeding if she has a terminal condition. Her son is her health care proxy. When the patient's course is reviewed with the husband, he states that he is tired of seeing his wife suffer and asks the physician to just make her comfortable and discontinue all therapy. Which of the following is the most appropriate response?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Comply with the husband's wishes</br>b. Contact the hospital ethics committee</br>c. Continue the current treatment plan</br>d. Recommend liver transplantation</br>e. Review the situation with the husband and the son together</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>e. Review the situation with the husband and the son together  </p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues<br /></br>• “Living will stating that she does not want resuscitation or artificial feeding if she has a terminal condition.”<br /></br>• “Her son is her health-care proxy.”<br /></br>• Husband (not the proxy) now asks to “discontinue all therapy.”  </p></br><p>Pertinent positives/negatives<br /></br>• The advance directive (AD) only refuses CPR and artificial nutrition/hydration in a terminal situation; it says nothing about antibiotics or other potentially reversible treatments.<br /></br>• The patient is being treated for bacterial peritonitis and hepatic encephalopathy—conditions that can be reversible even in a cirrhotic patient. The prognosis is therefore uncertain; she is not clearly dying imminently.<br /></br>• Decision-making authority in the AD legally rests with the son (proxy), not the husband.  </p></br><p>Metacognitive approach<br /></br>1. Identify whether an advance directive exists.<br /></br>2. Determine who is the legally designated surrogate.<br /></br>3. If disagreement arises, first attempt a family meeting before seeking outside help (ethics committee, courts).  </p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Why option E is correct<br /></br>• The first step in any conflict about goals of care is to bring all involved parties together, clarify the patient’s expressed preferences, and review the medical facts.<br /></br>• The son, as health-care proxy, holds the legal authority to interpret his mother’s wishes. The husband’s perspective is important but not determinative.<br /></br>• A physician-led family meeting permits:<br /></br>  – Education about prognosis (reversible vs terminal).<br /></br>  – Clarification of the limits of the living will (DNR ≠ “do nothing”).<br /></br>  – Conflict resolution before escalation.  </p></br><p>Take-home message: In surrogate decision-making conflicts, hold a family meeting that includes the legally appointed proxy before discontinuing therapy or involving the ethics committee.  </p></br><p>Documentation pearls (what you would chart)<br /></br>• Content of the AD and who holds proxy power.<br /></br>• Medical assessment of prognosis and reversibility.<br /></br>• Details of the family meeting, questions addressed, and decisions made.  </p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (brief)  </p></br><p>Spontaneous bacterial peritonitis (SBP) and hepatic encephalopathy are common complications of end-stage liver disease but are frequently reversible with antibiotics ± albumin (SBP) and lactulose/rifaximin (encephalopathy). Therefore, treating them is consistent with standard care unless explicitly refused by the patient.  </p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>Management Algorithm (ethics focus)  </p></br><ol></br><li>Advance directive present?<br /></br>   • Yes → follow it.  </li></br><li>Does AD cover the current decision?<br /></br>   • If no, move to surrogate.  </li></br><li>Identified surrogate?<br /></br>   • Yes → engage surrogate.  </li></br><li>Disagreement among family?<br /></br>   • Hold family meeting → ethics consult only if unresolved.  </li></br></ol></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>Memory Tips  </p></br><p>“F-A-M”:<br /></br>F = Family meeting first<br /></br>A = Advance directive honored<br /></br>M = Move to ethics only after meeting fails  </p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>a. Comply with the husband’s wishes<br /></br>• Husband lacks legal authority; would violate both the patient’s AD and the designated proxy’s role.  </p></br><p>b. Contact the hospital ethics committee<br /></br>• Appropriate only after an attempt at direct conflict resolution (family meeting) fails. Skips a necessary step.  </p></br><p>c. Continue the current treatment plan<br /></br>• Ignores the husband’s concerns and misses an opportunity to resolve potential conflict; may erode trust.  </p></br><p>d. Recommend liver transplantation<br /></br>• Not relevant to the ethical dilemma. Transplant is generally contraindicated in active SBP and requires detailed eligibility assessment; moreover, does not address surrogate conflict.  </p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>Bottom Line: When a conflict arises between family members about care and a legally designated proxy exists, the physician should first convene a meeting with all parties—especially the proxy—to review the patient’s wishes and medical facts before altering therapy or seeking outside intervention.</p>
152. A 57-year-old woman comes to the physician because she has had a sore throat for 3 days. She has not had any other symptoms. She has Graves disease treated with propylthiouracil and type 2 diabetes mellitus controlled with metformin and rosiglitazone. She takes daily ibuprofen and glucosamine for knee pain. There is no family history of serious illness. Her temperature is 37.3°C (99.2°F), pulse is 108/min, respirations are 14/min, and blood pressure is 123/74 mm Hg. There are multiple tender, enlarged lymph nodes in the anterior cervical chain. Examination of the oropharynx shows erythema and edema of the tonsils with no exudate. The remainder of the examination shows no abnormalities. A rapid streptococcal test is positive. Laboratory studies show:  </br>Hemoglobin               12 g/dL  </br>Hematocrit               38 percent  </br>Erythrocyte count        4 million/mm^3  </br>Mean corpuscular hemoglobin         30 pg/cell  </br>Mean corpuscular hemoglobin concentration       34 percent Hb/cell  </br>Mean corpuscular volume    90 µm^3  </br>Leukocyte count           2300/mm^3  </br>Segmented neutrophils     24 percent  </br>Eosinophils               5 percent  </br>Basophils                 2 percent  </br>Lymphocytes               62 percent  </br>Monocytes                 7 percent  </br>Platelet count            200,000/mm^3  </br></br>Which of the following medications is the most likely cause of this patient’s neutropenia?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Glucosamine  </br>b. Ibuprofen  </br>c. Metformin  </br>d. Propylthiouracil  </br>e. Rosiglitazone</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>d. Propylthiouracil  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li>Key clues<br /></br>   • Chronic Graves disease treated with propylthiouracil (PTU)<br /></br>   • Acute‐onset sore throat + tender cervical nodes → suggests infectious pharyngitis but can also be the first warning sign of drug-related agranulocytosis.<br /></br>   • Rapid strep positive (true infection present).<br /></br>   • CBC: WBC 2300/mm³ with absolute neutrophil count ≈ 0.55 ×10⁹/L (severe neutropenia/agranulocytosis). Hb, platelets normal → isolated granulocyte suppression.  </li></br><li>Pattern recognition<br /></br>   • “Patient on antithyroid drug + sore throat/fever + very low neutrophils” = classic NBME/Step pattern for PTU- or methimazole-induced agranulocytosis.  </li></br><li>Pertinent negatives<br /></br>   • No pancytopenia, no macrocytosis, no hemolysis → rules out bone-marrow failure syndromes, B12/folate issues, etc.<br /></br>   • No mention of other marrow-toxic drugs (e.g., clozapine, carbamazepine, TMP-SMX).  </li></br></ol></br><p>Metacognitive approach:<br /></br>When you see an isolated marked neutropenia in a patient taking one of the “agranulocyte culprits” (PTU, methimazole, clozapine, dapsone, carbamazepine, ticlopidine), stop and pick that drug. Memorize: “PTU = Put The Neutrophils Underground.”</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (Why PTU is the answer)  </p></br><p>Step-wise thought process<br /></br>1. Identify the hematologic abnormality → isolated neutropenia/agranulocytosis.<br /></br>2. Recall common drug causes → antithyroid drugs high on list.<br /></br>3. Match with medications patient is actually taking → only PTU fits.  </p></br><p>Core takeaway<br /></br>Antithyroid thionamides (propylthiouracil, methimazole) can cause idiosyncratic, potentially life-threatening agranulocytosis, usually within the first 3 months but possible at any time. Any patient on these drugs who develops fever or sore throat must have an immediate CBC; if neutropenia is confirmed, the drug must be discontinued permanently.</p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real life)<br /></br>• Note ANC, clinical symptoms, suspected PTU-induced agranulocytosis.<br /></br>• Discontinue PTU, start broad-spectrum antibiotics if febrile, consider G-CSF.<br /></br>• Counsel patient on alternative definitive therapy for Graves (radio-iodine or surgery).</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• Incidence: ~0.3–0.5% of patients on thionamides.<br /></br>• Mechanism: thought to be immune-mediated destruction or direct toxic effect on myeloid precursors → abrupt drop in mature neutrophils.<br /></br>• Onset: classically within 90 days but can occur late.<br /></br>• Not dose related; therefore routine CBC monitoring is not cost-effective—patient education about warning signs (fever, pharyngitis) is key.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment / Diagnostic Algorithm  </p></br><ol></br><li>Suspect agranulocytosis → obtain CBC.  </li></br><li>Discontinue offending drug immediately.  </li></br><li>If ANC &lt; 500/µL or fever → hospitalize, reverse isolation, blood cultures, empiric broad-spectrum IV antibiotics (e.g., antipseudomonal β-lactam).  </li></br><li>Consider filgrastim (G-CSF) to hasten marrow recovery.  </li></br><li>Manage hyperthyroidism with non-thionamide options:<br /></br>   • β-blocker for symptoms<br /></br>   • Iodinated contrast or potassium iodide temporarily if thyroid storm-risk<br /></br>   • Definitive: radioactive iodine ablation or thyroidectomy.</li></br></ol></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“PTU Puts The Neutrophils Under.”<br /></br>“MMM PPP CCTD” (drugs causing agranulocytosis):<br /></br>• Methimazole, Metamizole<br /></br>• PTU, Penicillin G<br /></br>• Phenothiazines<br /></br>• Clozapine, Carbamazepine, Colchicine<br /></br>• Ticlopidine<br /></br>• Dapsone</p></br><p>Visual mnemonic: Imagine a grave (Graves disease) with neutrophils buried underground by PTU.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>a. Glucosamine<br /></br>   • Dietary supplement for osteoarthritis; not associated with marrow suppression. Expected labs would be normal.  </p></br><p>b. Ibuprofen<br /></br>   • NSAIDs rarely cause mild neutropenia but usually after prolonged high-dose use; far less common and typically not severe agranulocytosis. Would expect concurrent platelet dysfunction or GI/renal side-effects, not isolated ANC &lt; 600.  </p></br><p>c. Metformin<br /></br>   • Can cause lactic acidosis, B12 deficiency (macrocytic anemia) with long-term use, but not isolated neutropenia.  </p></br><p>e. Rosiglitazone<br /></br>   • Thiazolidinedione; main adverse effects: weight gain, edema, heart failure risk, hepatotoxicity. No link to agranulocytosis.  </p></br><p>Each of these alternatives lacks the well-established, board-relevant association with sudden, severe neutropenia seen with PTU.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line  </p></br><p>In any patient on propylthiouracil (or methimazole) who develops sore throat or fever, think “agranulocytosis until proven otherwise,” get an immediate CBC, and stop the drug.</p>
153. A 25-year-old woman comes to the physician because of a 1-week history of progressive diarrhea. She initially had loose, nonbloody bowel movements six to eight times daily; during the past 2 days, she has passed small amounts of loose, bloody, stool with mucus 8 to 10 times daily associated with lower abdominal cramps. She went on a vacation in Guatemala 3 weeks ago. She has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. Her current temperature is 37.6°C (99.7°F). Abdominal examination shows mild tenderness in the lower quadrants. Rectal examination shows mucus and gross blood in the rectal vault. Examination of a wet mount preparation of the stool shows trophozoites. Which of the following is the most appropriate pharmacotherapy?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Mesalamine enemas and oral diphenoxylate and atropine</br>b. Oral ciprofloxacin and loperamide</br>c. Oral fluconazole</br>d. Oral mebendazole</br>e. Oral metronidazole and iodoquinol</br>f. Oral trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>e. Oral metronidazole and iodoquinol  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Key clues<br /></br>  – Recent travel to Guatemala → high risk for water- or food-borne parasites<br /></br>  – 1 week of diarrhea that has progressed from watery to bloody with mucus (“dysentery”)<br /></br>  – Wet-mount stool exam: trophozoites present (pathognomonic for Entamoeba histolytica)<br /></br>• Pertinent positives: invasive bloody diarrhea, abdominal cramps, trophozoites<br /></br>• Pertinent negatives: no high fever or severe systemic toxicity typical of bacterial enteritis such as Shigella; no history of inflammatory bowel disease<br /></br>Framework:<br /></br>1. Infectious vs inflammatory bowel disease → travel history + trophozoites = infectious.<br /></br>2. Identify pathogen → trophozoites + bloody diarrhea = E. histolytica.<br /></br>3. Know treatment algorithm → tissue agent (metronidazole or tinidazole) PLUS luminal agent (iodoquinol or paromomycin) to eradicate both trophozoites and intraluminal cysts.  </p></br><p>Cognitive approach for future questions<br /></br>• Any patient with BLOODY diarrhea after travel + trophozoites/RBC-laden organisms on stool O&amp;P → think Entamoeba.<br /></br>• Always pair a tissue amoebicide with a luminal amoebicide.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>1. Diagnose amoebic dysentery (Entamoeba histolytica).<br /></br>2. Tissue invasion causes symptoms; metronidazole (kills tissue trophozoites) is required.<br /></br>3. Cysts remain in the gut lumen → add iodoquinol (or paromomycin) to prevent relapse and transmission.<br /></br>Take-home message: Dual therapy (systemic + luminal) is mandatory; metronidazole alone is insufficient.  </p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real life)<br /></br>• Stool O&amp;P positive for E. histolytica trophozoites.<br /></br>• Plan: 10-day course metronidazole 750 mg PO TID, followed by 20-day course iodoquinol 650 mg PO TID.<br /></br>• Educate on safe water/food practices; arrange follow-up stool exam 1–3 months after therapy.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Etiology: Ingestion of cysts in contaminated water/food.<br /></br>• Mechanism: Excystation in ileum → trophozoites colonize colon, create flask-shaped ulcers, ingest RBCs → bloody diarrhea. Can spread via portal circulation → liver abscess.<br /></br>• Clinical manifestations: Ranges from asymptomatic colonization to colitis with dysentery, right-lobe liver abscess (RUQ pain, “anchovy-paste” pus).  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Tests Review<br /></br>1. First-line tissue agent: Metronidazole 500–750 mg PO/IV TID x 7–10 days (or tinidazole 2 g daily x 3 days).<br /></br>2. Luminal agent: Iodoquinol 650 mg PO TID x 20 days OR paromomycin 500 mg PO TID x 7 days.<br /></br>3. Imaging: RUQ ultrasound/CT if liver abscess suspected.<br /></br>4. Labs: Stool O&P; E. histolytica serology if extraintestinal disease.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>• “Metro handles the city (tissues); Iodo cleans the canal (lumen).”<br /></br>• E. histo = “Eat RBCs, cause Flask ulcers, needs Two drugs.”  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong<br /></br>a. Mesalamine enemas &amp; diphenoxylate/atropine<br /></br>   – Mesalamine treats ulcerative colitis, not an infection with trophozoites. Antimotility drugs are contraindicated in invasive diarrhea.  </p></br><p>b. Oral ciprofloxacin &amp; loperamide<br /></br>   – Used for traveler’s diarrhea due to enterotoxigenic E. coli (watery, non-bloody). Loperamide is contraindicated in bloody/invasive diarrhea.  </p></br><p>c. Oral fluconazole<br /></br>   – Antifungal (Candida, Cryptococcus). No role in protozoal infection.  </p></br><p>d. Oral mebendazole<br /></br>   – Anti-nematode (hookworm, pinworm, Ascaris). Ineffective against protozoa.  </p></br><p>f. Oral trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole<br /></br>   – Covers Cyclospora, Isospora, Salmonella, Shigella, but not E. histolytica.  </p></br><p>Common pitfall: treating with metronidazole alone—relapse occurs because luminal cysts persist.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line<br /></br>Entamoeba histolytica dysentery after travel requires BOTH metronidazole (kills invasive trophozoites) and a luminal agent such as iodoquinol (eradicates cysts) to achieve cure and prevent transmission.</p>
154. A 6-month-old boy is admitted to the hospital for treatment of his second episode of Streptococcus pneumoniae sepsis within the past 5 weeks. He was treated for acute otitis media at the ages of 3 months and 4 months. He was delivered at term following an uncomplicated pregnancy and delivery. His maternal uncle died as an infant of Haemophilus influenzae type b sepsis and meningitis. The patient's immunizations are up-to-date, and there have been no complications of immunizations. He is mildly irritable but easily consoled. His temperature is 38.5°C (101.3°F), pulse is 160/min, and respirations are 42/min. Examination shows no lymphadenopathy. Laboratory studies show:</br>Leukocyte count 17,000/mm³</br>Segmented neutrophils 64 percent</br>Bands 8 percent</br>Eosinophils 1 percent</br>Lymphocytes 25 percent</br>Monocytes 2 percent</br>Serum</br>IgG 20 mg/dL (N=172–1069)</br>IgA <5 mg/dL (N=4.4–84)</br>IgM <5 mg/dL (N=33–126)</br>His CD4+ and CD8+ T-lymphocyte counts are within the reference ranges. There are no CD19 B lymphocytes. The remainder of the examination shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. Subcutaneous granulocyte colony-stimulating factor therapy</br>b. Subcutaneous interferon gamma-1b therapy</br>c. Intravenous immune globulin therapy</br>d. Bone marrow transplantation</br>e. Thymus transplantation</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>c. Intravenous immune globulin (IVIG) therapy  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues that point to one specific primary immunodeficiency:<br /></br>• Age ≈ 6 mo – infections begin after maternally transferred IgG wanes.<br /></br>• Recurrent infections with encapsulated bacteria (Strep pneumoniae, prior otitis media).<br /></br>• Absent lymphadenopathy/tonsils (classic physical clue).<br /></br>• Quantitative pan-hypogammaglobulinemia (IgG 20, IgA &lt;5, IgM &lt;5).<br /></br>• Flow cytometry: 0% CD19⁺ B cells while CD4/CD8 counts are normal.<br /></br>• Positive family history in a maternal male relative → X-linked pattern.  </p></br><p>Taken together this is textbook X-linked agammaglobulinemia (XLA, Bruton disease).  </p></br><p>Metacognitive framework:<br /></br>1. Ask “which arm of immunity is missing—phagocytes, complement, T cells, B cells?”<br /></br>2. Look for pattern of infections (encapsulated bacteria → humoral defect).<br /></br>3. Check immunoglobulin levels and presence of B-cell markers.<br /></br>4. Correlate with age of onset and family history for inheritance clues.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning / Why IVIG is correct  </p></br><p>XLA is caused by mutations in the Bruton tyrosine kinase (BTK) gene → failure of pre-B cells to mature → absent peripheral B cells and severe deficiency in all immunoglobulin isotypes.  </p></br><p>Mainstay of therapy = passive replacement of the missing antibodies:<br /></br>• IVIG 400–600 mg/kg every 3–4 weeks (or SCIG weekly) maintains protective IgG levels and dramatically lowers risk of serious bacterial infections.<br /></br>• Prophylactic antibiotics can be added but do not replace IVIG.  </p></br><p>What would be documented: diagnosis of XLA, plan for lifelong IVIG infusions, education about infection precautions, consideration of prophylactic azithromycin, and family genetic counseling.  </p></br><p>Core takeaway: In any infant boy with near-absent Igs and no circulating B cells, immediate antibody replacement with IVIG is life-saving and first-line.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (High-Yield)  </p></br><p>• Gene: BTK on X chromosome → X-linked recessive (males affected, female carriers).<br /></br>• Block at pro-B → pre-B stage → no germinal centers, no plasma cells, no Ig.<br /></br>• Normal T-cell numbers and function, so viral/fungal infections are not prominent early.<br /></br>• Clinical onset after 3–6 mo when maternal IgG disappears.<br /></br>• Repeated sinopulmonary &amp; invasive infections with encapsulated organisms (S. pneumo, H. influenzae, Pseudomonas).  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Pearls  </p></br><ol></br><li>Confirmatory tests: very low/absent IgG, IgA, IgM + absent CD19/CD20 B cells.  </li></br><li>First-line therapy: lifelong IVIG (or SCIG) to keep trough IgG ≥500 mg/dL.  </li></br><li>Vaccines: live vaccines contraindicated (e.g., rotavirus, intranasal influenza, MMR, varicella).  </li></br><li>Monitor for chronic lung disease (bronchiectasis) and GI infections (Giardia).  </li></br></ol></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“Bruton Boys have Broken B-cells”<br /></br>• Boys (X-linked)<br /></br>• Broken Ig (all low)<br /></br>• Bacterial encapsulated bugs<br /></br>• Treat with IVIG  </p></br><p>Visual: imagine a boy wearing a “BTK” tool-belt that’s missing the “B-cell wrench,” and he receives an “IV bag shield” for protection.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong  </p></br><p>a. Granulocyte colony-stimulating factor – Used for congenital or cyclic neutropenia, chemotherapy-induced neutropenia; patient’s neutrophil count is normal.  </p></br><p>b. Interferon-γ-1b – Indicated for chronic granulomatous disease (defective NADPH oxidase → catalase-positive infections). CGD has normal Igs &amp; B cells but abnormal neutrophil oxidative burst.  </p></br><p>d. Bone marrow transplantation – Curative for combined T- and B-cell defects (SCID, Wiskott-Aldrich), severe aplastic anemia, etc. In XLA, T-cell immunity is intact and outcome with IVIG is excellent; BMT risk &gt; benefit.  </p></br><p>e. Thymus transplantation – Therapy for complete DiGeorge syndrome (athymia → T-cell absence). This patient has normal T-cell numbers/function; thymic replacement is unnecessary.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Key Exam Pearl:<br /></br>Whenever you see 1) recurrent encapsulated bacterial infections beginning at 3–6 months, 2) pan-low immunoglobulins, and 3) absent B-cell markers, think X-linked agammaglobulinemia and answer IVIG.</p>
155. A 17-year-old boy undergoes an emergency inguinal herniorrhaphy under general anesthesia for an incarcerated hernia. On induction, he aspirates vomitus while being observed by the anesthesiologist. He is intubated but is difficult to ventilate. Examination shows acrocyanosis. Breath sounds are equal. Pulse oximetry on 100 percent oxygen shows an oxygen saturation of 85 percent. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. CT angiography of the chest</br>b. Antibiotic therapy</br>c. Bronchoscopy</br>d. Needle thoracostomy</br>e. Tracheostomy</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>c. Bronchoscopy  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• “Emergency … herniorrhaphy” + “aspirates vomitus on induction”: Full-stomach patient at very high risk for aspiration.<br /></br>• “Intubated but is difficult to ventilate” + “pulse ox 85 % on 100 % O₂”: A newly intubated patient who is still profoundly hypoxemic ⇒ think acute airway/bronchial obstruction or severe pulmonary pathology.<br /></br>• “Breath sounds are equal”: Rules out right-mainstem intubation and tension pneumothorax (both produce asymmetric breath sounds).  </p></br><p>Metacognitive note: For sudden desaturation after intubation use the mnemonic DOPES (Displacement, Obstruction, Pneumothorax, Equipment failure, Stomach). Equal breath sounds + good ET-CO₂ wave form eliminate displacement; equipment is working; obstruction is now top of the list.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>1. Patient aspirated solid and liquid gastric contents.<br /></br>2. Liquid (acid) causes chemical pneumonitis; solid chunks can plug large bronchi or the ETT.<br /></br>3.
