High-dose prednisone therapy<br /></br>• Steroids can shorten ACS in children but increase rebound pain crises in adults; not first-line and never substitute for transfusion in severe cases.  </p></br><p>C. Hydroxyurea therapy<br /></br>• Disease-modifying agent that increases HbF and prevents future crises; started as OUTPATIENT for frequent episodes. Does not treat an acute, severe episode.  </p></br><p>D. Incentive spirometry<br /></br>• Absolutely indicated in ALL ACS cases to prevent atelectasis, but the stem states “in addition to … oxygen, antibiotics, etc.” and asks for the NEXT step. With severe hypoxia &amp; anemia, spirometry alone is insufficient—exchange transfusion is lifesaving and takes priority.  </p></br><p>E. Nebulized albuterol<br /></br>• Considered if bronchospasm or asthma exacerbation; no wheezing or reversible airway obstruction is described. Does not address hypoxia/anemia driving severity.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line: Severe acute chest syndrome in sickle cell disease (SpO₂ &lt; 90 %, Hb &lt; 7 g/dL, multilobar infiltrates, or rapid progression) requires urgent exchange transfusion in addition to standard supportive measures.</p>
170. A 52-year-old woman comes to the physician as a new patient because of a 5-day history of temperatures to 38.2°C (100.8°F) and cough productive of thick, gray-green sputum. She says the sputum is difficult to cough up. During the past 15 years, she has had similar symptoms twice yearly, which resolved after treatment with antibiotics. Because of lack of insurance during this time, she was treated at urgent care clinics. She has no known allergies and no history of serious illness. Her only medication is an over-the-counter cough suppressant. She does not smoke cigarettes or use illicit drugs. She drinks three glasses of wine weekly. She is 170 cm (5 ft 7 in) tall and weighs 91 kg (200 lb); BMI is 31 kg/m2. Her temperature is 38°C (100.4°F), pulse is 80/min, and blood pressure is 138/84 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on room air shows an oxygen saturation of 94 percent. On pulmonary examination, there is an increased expiratory phase; rhonchi and faint wheezes are heard bilaterally. A CT scan of the chest is shown. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Asthma</br>b. Bronchiectasis</br>c. Idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis</br>d. Primary pulmonary hypertension</br>e. Pulmonary embolism</br>f. Tuberculosis</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>B. Bronchiectasis  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li>Recurrent pattern: “similar symptoms twice yearly for 15 years” → chronic, relapsing airway disease.  </li></br><li>Sputum quality: “thick, gray-green sputum … difficult to cough up.” Copious, mucopurulent sputum is classic for bronchiectasis.  </li></br><li>Exam: prolonged expiratory phase, rhonchi, faint wheezes → obstructive physiology with retained secretions.  </li></br><li>CT imaging (not shown here, but on exams): dilated, thick-walled bronchi, often described as “tram-track” or “signet-ring” appearance.  </li></br><li>Oxygenation essentially normal and no acute respiratory distress, supporting chronic rather than fulminant process.</li></br></ol></br><p>Metacognitive tip: On test day, the triad “chronic productive cough + large-volume purulent sputum + recurrent infections” should instantly trigger bronchiectasis in the differential; the CT scan virtually clinches it.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (Why B is correct)  </p></br><p>• Bronchiectasis = irreversible dilation/destruction of bronchi due to chronic infection/inflammation → impaired mucociliary clearance → ongoing cycle of infection, inflammation, and remodeling.<br /></br>• Clinical hallmarks:<br /></br>  – Daily cough with &gt;30 mL mucopurulent sputum (“cups of sputum”)<br /></br>  – Recurrent lower respiratory infections responding to antibiotics<br /></br>  – Crackles/rhonchi/wheezes, sometimes hemoptysis<br /></br>  – Obstructive PFT pattern (↓FEV₁/FVC)  </p></br><p>Core takeaway: A middle-aged patient with years of episodic, antibiotic-responsive, purulent sputum production and CT evidence of airway dilation has bronchiectasis until proven otherwise.</p></br><p>Documentation pearls in real life: frequency of exacerbations, sputum character, prior cultures, imaging description, need for airway-clearance therapy, evaluation for underlying causes (e.g., CF, immunodeficiency).  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• Etiologies: post-infectious (childhood pneumonia, TB), cystic fibrosis, primary ciliary dyskinesia, ABPA (allergic bronchopulmonary aspergillosis), immune deficiencies, AAT deficiency, autoimmune disease.<br /></br>• Pathogenesis: Neutrophil-mediated inflammation destroys elastic/muscular bronchial wall → outward dilation. Stagnant secretions encourage bacterial colonization (Pseudomonas, H flu, Staph).<br /></br>• Complications: respiratory failure, massive hemoptysis, cor pulmonale, secondary amyloidosis.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Management &amp; Diagnostics  </p></br><ol></br><li>Baseline work-up: sputum culture, PFTs, quantitative immunoglobulins, +/- sweat chloride / CFTR testing, ciliary studies.  </li></br><li>Acute exacerbation: empiric or culture-directed antibiotics (e.g., levofloxacin, amoxicillin-clavulanate; inhaled tobramycin for chronic Pseudomonas).  </li></br><li>Airway clearance: chest physiotherapy, high-frequency chest wall oscillation, nebulized hypertonic saline.  </li></br><li>Bronchodilators (albuterol, tiotropium) if obstructive physiology.  </li></br><li>Chronic macrolide therapy (azithromycin 3 times/week) in patients with ≥2 exacerbations per year.  </li></br><li>Vaccinations (influenza, pneumococcal) and smoking-cessation if applicable.  </li></br><li>Surgery or lung transplantation for focal disease or end-stage diffuse disease.</li></br></ol></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“Bronchi-E-X-treme Sputum”:<br /></br>B – Big Bronchi on CT (“tram-tracks,” “signet ring”)<br /></br>E – Excessive sputum (mucopurulent cups)<br /></br>X – eXacerbations repeatedly needing antibiotics  </p></br><p>Visualization: Picture railroad “tram-tracks” (CT) full of green muck (purulent sputum) that a patient tries to shovel out twice a year.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong  </p></br><p>A. Asthma – Episodic wheeze/dyspnea triggered by allergens or exercise; sputum scant, not copious/foul. Imaging usually normal or shows hyperinflation, not bronchial dilation.  </p></br><p>C. Idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis – Progressive dyspnea, dry cough, basilar “velcro” crackles, clubbing; CT shows subpleural reticulation/honeycombing, not dilated airways full of secretions.  </p></br><p>D. Primary pulmonary hypertension – Presents with exertional dyspnea, syncope, loud P2, right-sided heart failure; no productive cough or recurrent infections; CT angiography shows enlarged pulmonary arteries, not bronchiectasis.  </p></br><p>E. Pulmonary embolism – Acute pleuritic chest pain, dyspnea, clear lungs or focal findings; CT angiogram shows filling defects, not airway dilation; sputum generally absent or scant blood-streaked.  </p></br><p>F. Tuberculosis – Chronic constitutional symptoms, night sweats, weight loss, hemoptysis; imaging reveals apical cavitary lesions; sputum is often blood-tinged, not large-volume purulent, and episodes are not reliably twice yearly over 15 years.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line  </p></br><p>Recurrent, antibiotic-responsive bouts of copious purulent sputum plus CT evidence of dilated bronchi = bronchiectasis. Recognizing this pattern is high-yield for NBME/Step 2 CK questions.</p>
171. A 37-year-old man comes to the physician because of a 1-month history of increased thirst, frequent urination, and fatigue. He has a 20-year history of alcohol use disorder and has had multiple episodes of pancreatitis during the past 10 years. He began pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy 5 years ago. An abdominal x-ray 1 year ago showed calcifications within the pancreas. He is 175 cm (5 ft 9 in) tall and weighs 59 kg (130 lb); BMI is 19 kg/m2. Vital signs are within normal limits. Examination shows no abnormalities. His serum glucose concentration is 288 mg/dL. Which of the following is the most appropriate pharmacotherapy?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. Acarbose</br>b. Glipizide</br>c. Insulin</br>d. Metformin</br>e. Repaglinide</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>c. Insulin  </p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues:<br /></br>• Chronic pancreatitis – 10-year history + pancreatic calcifications on imaging + pancreatic enzyme replacement (exocrine failure).<br /></br>• Alcohol use disorder – most common cause of chronic pancreatitis in adults.<br /></br>• New “polydipsia, polyuria, fatigue” with random glucose 288 mg/dL – diagnostic of diabetes mellitus.<br /></br>• Low‐normal BMI (19 kg/m²) – argues against classic type 2 diabetes.  </p></br><p>Pertinent positives: pancreatic destruction, lean habitus, hyperglycemia.<br /></br>Pertinent negatives: No obesity, no insulin resistance stigmata (acanthosis, HTN, dyslipidemia).  </p></br><p>Pattern/algorithm:<br /></br>1. Chronic pancreatitis → pancreatic fibrosis &amp; calcifications.<br /></br>2. Loss of β-cells (insulin) ± α-cells (glucagon) → “pancreatogenic diabetes” (type 3c).<br /></br>3. Management parallels absolute insulin deficiency (type 1) → start exogenous insulin.  </p></br><p>Metacognitive tip: When a question describes pancreatic pathology (CF, chronic pancreatitis, pancreatectomy) + diabetes, immediately think “insulin-dependent diabetes, oral agents won’t work.”  </p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Step-wise thought process:<br /></br>1. Identify diabetes. Random glucose ≥200 mg/dL + classic symptoms = diabetes.<br /></br>2. Classify type:<br /></br>   • Type 1 – autoimmune, younger age.<br /></br>   • Type 2 – insulin resistance, obesity.<br /></br>   • Type 3c – pancreatic destruction (chronic pancreatitis, CF, hemochromatosis, pancreatectomy).<br /></br>3. In type 3c the primary defect is lack of insulin production, not resistance.<br /></br>4. Therefore the only effective pharmacotherapy is physiologic insulin replacement.  </p></br><p>Take-home message: Any form of diabetes caused by loss of pancreatic tissue requires insulin. Oral hypoglycemics need residual β-cell function or insulin sensitivity to work.  </p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real life): note hyperglycemia, history of chronic pancreatitis, malnutrition, plan for basal-bolus insulin and hypoglycemia education (impaired glucagon response).  </p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>Chronic alcohol-induced pancreatitis → repeated inflammation → fibrosis and dystrophic calcifications. Endocrine insufficiency develops when &gt;90 % of islet mass is destroyed. Result:<br /></br>• ↓Insulin → hyperglycemia.<br /></br>• ↓Glucagon → blunted counter-regulation → higher hypoglycemia risk once insulin started.  </p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>Diagnostic &amp; Treatment Highlights  </p></br><p>Diagnostics already sufficient (symptomatic hyperglycemia). HbA1c at baseline is still ordered to gauge chronicity.  </p></br><p>Therapy:<br /></br>• Basal-bolus insulin or once-daily basal + rapid-acting with meals.<br /></br>• Educate on hypoglycemia (carry glucose tablets because glucagon response is poor).<br /></br>• Continue pancreatic enzymes, fat-soluble vitamins, nutrition optimization.  </p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“3C = Chronic pancreatitis/CF/Cut-out pancreas → Can’t make insulin → Cartridges (insulin pens).”<br /></br>OR<br /></br>“TITIS in pancreaTITIS knocks out β-cells → need InSulIN.”  </p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong  </p></br><p>a. Acarbose – α-glucosidase inhibitor that slows carbohydrate absorption; requires some endogenous insulin, often worsens GI symptoms (already malabsorbing).  </p></br><p>b. Glipizide – sulfonylurea that stimulates remaining β-cells; ineffective when β-cells are destroyed, and high risk of hypoglycemia given absent glucagon.  </p></br><p>d. Metformin – first-line for insulin-resistant type 2 diabetes; relies on hepatic insulin sensitivity. Offers no benefit when insulin is absent; plus caution with alcohol use/malnutrition (lactic acidosis risk).  </p></br><p>e. Repaglinide – meglitinide, similar to sulfonylureas; needs functional β-cells, same limitations as glipizide.  </p></br><p>Common trap: assuming every new adult-onset diabetic gets metformin. Always check for pancreatic loss or very lean phenotype – those patients need insulin.</p>
172. A 47-year-old woman is scheduled for discharge home 3 days after total abdominal hysterectomy and bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy for leiomyomata uteri. She has no other history of serious illness. Medications are ibuprofen and oxycodone. She has a family history of osteoporosis, and she is concerned about estrogen therapy because of a strong family history of breast cancer. Vital signs are within normal limits. Examination shows a clean, dry, intact incision site. In addition to exercise and calcium with vitamin D supplementation, which of the following is the most appropriate recommendation for bone health in this patient?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Transdermal estrogen patch</br>b. Glucosamine-chondroitin therapy</br>c. Thyroid hormone therapy</br>d. Calcitonin nasal spray</br>e. No additional therapy is indicated</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>e. No additional therapy is indicated  </p></br><p>──────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues<br /></br>• 47-year-old woman → pre-menopausal age range<br /></br>• Total abdominal hysterectomy with bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy (TAH-BSO) performed 3 days ago → she has just become surgically menopausal, but bone loss from estrogen deficiency is a chronic rather than immediate process.<br /></br>• Only risk factor supplied: first-degree relatives with osteoporosis; no personal history of fragility fracture, chronic glucocorticoid use, rheumatoid arthritis, smoking, alcohol abuse, malabsorption, or low BMI.<br /></br>• Vital signs and incision are normal; she is recovering well.<br /></br>• Already counseled on weight-bearing exercise + calcium/vitamin D.  </p></br><p>Test-writer’s pattern<br /></br>NBME routinely asks: “When is pharmacologic osteoporosis prevention started?” Answer: Only after documented low bone mass (T-score ≤ –2.5) or the presence of strong clinical risk factors that yield a high FRAX score. A single family history without additional risks in a woman &lt;50 does NOT trigger drug therapy.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (Step-wise)  </p></br><ol></br><li>Stratify baseline fracture risk.<br /></br>   • Age &lt;50 = low baseline risk.<br /></br>   • One risk factor (family history) is not enough for medication.  </li></br><li>Decide if bone mineral density (BMD) testing is required now.<br /></br>   • DEXA screening begins at 65 (USPSTF) or earlier only if risk factors equal a 65-year-old woman’s risk. At 47, she does not yet meet that threshold.  </li></br><li>Management algorithm:<br /></br>   • Low risk → lifestyle measures (exercise, adequate calcium 1,200 mg + vitamin D 800–1,000 IU daily, avoid smoking/alcohol).<br /></br>   • Moderate/high risk or proven osteoporosis → pharmacologic therapy (bisphosphonates first line; others second line).  </li></br></ol></br><p>Core takeaway: Do not overtreat low-risk, pre-menopausal (or newly menopausal) women; reserve drugs for those with documented low BMD or high clinical risk.  </p></br><p>Documentation (if this were a real patient)<br /></br>• Note surgical menopause and counsel on menopause symptoms.<br /></br>• Record calcium/vitamin D and weight-bearing exercise advice.<br /></br>• Plan: routine follow-up, consider BMD testing at age 65 or earlier if additional risk factors develop.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• Estrogen deficiency accelerates osteoclast-mediated bone resorption.<br /></br>• Meaningful cortical and trabecular bone loss occurs over months–years; it is not an emergency in the immediate post-operative period.<br /></br>• Pharmacologic prevention is aimed at those who will actually fracture—not every woman who loses ovarian function.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────<br /></br>Treatments &amp; Diagnostics Summary  </p></br><p>First-line prevention in low-risk women<br /></br>1. Weight-bearing &amp; muscle-strengthening exercise.<br /></br>2. Calcium 1,200 mg elemental + vitamin D 800–1,000 IU daily.<br /></br>3. Lifestyle: stop smoking, limit alcohol, maintain healthy BMI.  </p></br><p>Pharmacologic options (NOT indicated here)<br /></br>• Bisphosphonates (alendronate, risedronate) – mainstay once criteria met.<br /></br>• Estrogen/HRT – considered for severe vasomotor symptoms, not solely for bone.<br /></br>• SERMs (raloxifene), calcitonin spray, denosumab, teriparatide – second line / special cases.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────<br /></br>High-Yield Memory Aids  </p></br><p>“COACH” for baseline bone health in low-risk patients:<br /></br>C – Calcium<br /></br>O – Outdoor/weight-bearing exercise<br /></br>A – Avoid alcohol &amp; tobacco<br /></br>C – Check vitamin D<br /></br>H – Healthy BMI &amp; diet  </p></br><p>──────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong  </p></br><p>a. Transdermal estrogen patch<br /></br>   • Does preserve bone, but HRT is reserved for symptomatic menopause or high fracture risk; unnecessary here and patient is uneasy due to family breast cancer history.  </p></br><p>b. Glucosamine-chondroitin therapy<br /></br>   • Nutraceutical for osteoarthritis pain, not proven for bone density.  </p></br><p>c. Thyroid hormone therapy<br /></br>   • Not related to bone health; excess thyroid hormone can precipitate osteoporosis.  </p></br><p>d. Calcitonin nasal spray<br /></br>   • Weak anti-resorptive benefit, reserved for women with established osteoporosis who cannot tolerate other agents; no role in primary prevention in a low-risk 47-year-old.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line<br /></br>A 47-year-old newly menopausal woman with only a family history of osteoporosis does not yet meet criteria for pharmacologic prevention. Lifestyle modification with adequate calcium and vitamin D suffices; therefore, no additional therapy is required at this time.</p>
173. A 15-year-old girl with cystic fibrosis is admitted to the hospital because of a 1-month history of progressive shortness of breath and cough productive of purulent sputum. She has had three hospitalizations for pulmonary exacerbations, the most recent of which was 6 months ago. Her medications are oral montelukast, inhaled fluticasone, inhaled tobramycin, inhaled dornase alfa, and oral azithromycin. She is in moderate respiratory distress. Her temperature is 37.8°C (100°F), pulse is 80/min, and respirations are 30/min. Pulse oximetry on room air shows an oxygen saturation of 85 percent. Examination shows diffuse crackles bilaterally. Sputum culture results are pending. Which of the following is the most likely causal agent?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Bordetella bronchiseptica</br>b. Haemophilus influenzae type b</br>c. Penicillin-resistant Streptococcus pneumoniae</br>d. Pseudomonas aeruginosa</br>e. Staphylococcus epidermidis</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>d. Pseudomonas aeruginosa  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Key clues<br /></br>   • Known cystic fibrosis (CF) patient<br /></br>   • Age 15 years (adolescent)<br /></br>   • Recurrent “pulmonary exacerbations” with hospitalization<br /></br>   • Currently on inhaled tobramycin (chronically targets Pseudomonas)<br /></br>   • Progressive dyspnea, purulent sputum, diffuse crackles, hypoxemia  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>How they narrow the differential<br /></br>   • In CF, the airway flora evolves with age:<br /></br>     – Infancy/early childhood: Staphylococcus aureus, non-typeable Haemophilus influenzae<br /></br>     – Adolescence &amp; adulthood: Pseudomonas aeruginosa (often mucoid biofilm-forming strains)<br /></br>   • Chronic inhaled tobramycin is almost always prescribed once a patient is colonized with Pseudomonas → strong hint<br /></br>   • No risk factors in the stem for atypical animal pathogens (Bordetella bronchiseptica) or skin flora pneumonia (Staph epidermidis).  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Pertinent positives/negatives<br /></br>   • Positive: chronic inhaled antibiotic, repeated hospitalizations = chronic colonization organism.<br /></br>   • Negative: No lobar consolidation described, less suggestive of pneumococcal pneumonia.  </p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>Metacognitive tip: For CF lung exacerbation questions, immediately pair patient age with the “bug progression” timeline; the stem often embeds the answer in the medication list (e.g., inhaled tobramycin = Pseudomonas).  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Stepwise thought process:<br /></br>1. Identify the clinical context: CF pulmonary exacerbation.<br /></br>2. Recall age-based microbiology.<br /></br>3. Note chronic suppressive therapy (inhaled tobramycin).<br /></br>4. Select Pseudomonas aeruginosa as the most likely causal agent.  </p></br><p>Core takeaway: In CF, once patients reach adolescence, Pseudomonas aeruginosa becomes the predominant pathogen driving exacerbations and dictates both chronic suppressive therapy and acute IV antipseudomonal coverage.  </p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real patient): sputum culture results, previous culture history (mucoid Pseudomonas?), oxygen requirement, plan for broad-spectrum antipseudomonal IV antibiotics plus airway clearance.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• CFTR dysfunction → dehydrated, viscous airway secretions → impaired mucociliary clearance.<br /></br>• Repeated infections + neutrophil-rich inflammation → bronchiectasis.<br /></br>• P. aeruginosa adapts by forming mucoid biofilms (alginate capsule) that resist host defenses &amp; antibiotics, leading to chronic colonization and progressive lung damage.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Test Review (high-yield)  </p></br><p>Acute CF exacerbation management:<br /></br>1. Obtain sputum culture to guide therapy.<br /></br>2. Start empiric IV dual antipseudomonal therapy:<br /></br>   • Beta-lactam with pseudomonal coverage (piperacillin-tazobactam, cefepime, ceftazidime, or meropenem) PLUS<br /></br>   • IV aminoglycoside (tobramycin) or a fluoroquinolone (ciprofloxacin).<br /></br>3. Continue airway clearance (chest physiotherapy, dornase alfa, hypertonic saline).<br /></br>4. Supplemental O₂, consider non-invasive ventilation if needed.  </p></br><p>Inhaled chronic suppressive options: tobramycin, aztreonam, or colistin cycles.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>• “CF bug timeline”:<br /></br>  – SAHiP → Staph aureus &amp; non-typeable H influenzae (childhood) → Pseudomonas (teen)<br /></br>• Visual mnemonic: Picture a 15-year-old surfer (“teen in the pool”) riding a green wave—green pigment (pyocyanin) = Pseudomonas.<br /></br>• Drug-bug pairing: inhaled TOBramycin → Think “TOB-ramosa” (Tobramycin fights Pseudomonas).  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>a. Bordetella bronchiseptica<br /></br>   • Zoonotic pathogen from dogs/cats; extremely rare human pneumonia. Not typical CF colonizer.  </p></br><p>b. Haemophilus influenzae type b<br /></br>   • Encapsulated “type b” strain primarily causes meningitis/epiglottitis in unvaccinated children, not chronic CF lung disease. Non-typeable H influenzae can colonize young CF kids, but prevalence falls by adolescence.  </p></br><p>c. Penicillin-resistant Streptococcus pneumoniae<br /></br>   • Causes community-acquired lobar pneumonia with high fever and pleuritic pain; less likely chronic colonizer in CF and usually presents earlier with focal infiltrate rather than diffuse crackles.  </p></br><p>e. Staphylococcus epidermidis<br /></br>   • Normal skin flora; causes catheter-related bloodstream infections, prosthetic infections; rarely a lung pathogen.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line:<br /></br>For an adolescent or adult with cystic fibrosis experiencing a pulmonary exacerbation—especially one already on inhaled tobramycin—the most likely pathogen is Pseudomonas aeruginosa.</p>
174. A 22-year-old man is brought to the emergency department 50 minutes after he was involved in a motor vehicle collision. He was the unrestrained driver and struck his chest on the steering wheel. He did not strike his head or lose consciousness. On arrival, he has soreness in the midsternal area. He does not have shortness of breath. His pulse is 90/min, and blood pressure is 140/80 mm Hg. The lungs are clear to auscultation. Examination shows a large, exquisitely tender, ecchymotic area over the sternum. An ECG shows ST-segment elevation in leads V1 through V4. An x-ray of the chest shows no sternal fracture. Echocardiography shows an anterior wall motion hypokinesis. Which of the following is the most appropriate immediate step in management?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Admission to the cardiac unit</br>b. Intravenous ACE inhibitor therapy</br>c. Intravenous heparin therapy</br>d. Intravenous nitroglycerin therapy</br>e. Thrombolysis</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>a. Admission to the cardiac unit (continuous telemetry/observation)</p></br><p>================================================================</br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Mechanism: Unrestrained driver with blunt, steering-wheel impact to the chest → classic set-up for myocardial contusion.<br /></br>• Key findings that point to contusion rather than acute coronary thrombosis:<br /></br>  – Young (22 y) with no atherosclerotic risk factors.<br /></br>  – Immediately post-trauma; pain described as “soreness” at midsternum, not ischemic chest pressure.<br /></br>  – ST-segment elevation in the anterior leads (V1-V4) + echo showing anterior wall hypokinesis → both can occur with myocardial contusion because of transmural edema/hemorrhage.<br /></br>  – Normal blood pressure, clear lungs, no evidence of cardiogenic shock.<br /></br>  – Chest x-ray: no sternal fracture, but absence of fracture does NOT exclude contusion.  </p></br><p>Approach on test day<br /></br>1. Ask: “Could this be a classic STEMI from plaque rupture?” 22-year-old + trauma = low pre-test probability.<br /></br>2. If post-traumatic ST elevation → think myocardial contusion → next step is monitoring for arrhythmias, NOT reperfusion drugs.<br /></br>3. In real life and on exams: any patient with suspected blunt cardiac injury gets 24-48 h of continuous telemetry.</p></br><p>================================================================</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>• Goal: Detect lethal arrhythmias (PVCs, VT, VF, heart block) that often occur within the first 24 h after myocardial contusion.<br /></br>• Management algorithm:<br /></br>  1. Baseline ECG ± troponins.<br /></br>  2. If ECG abnormal (ST changes, arrhythmia) or biomarkers elevated → admit to monitored setting (telemetry/ICU).<br /></br>  3. Treat complications (eg, heart failure, tamponade, arrhythmia) if they appear.<br /></br>Core takeaway: Post-blunt chest trauma + ECG/echo abnormalities = observation, not lysis or anti-thrombotic therapy.</p></br><p>Documentation (real patient): mechanism of injury, hemodynamics, ECG changes, echo results, plan for continuous telemetry and serial enzymes.</p></br><p>================================================================</br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>Blunt force → intramyocardial hemorrhage &amp; edema → regional wall motion abnormality, ST-T changes, release of troponin. Injury most often affects RV and anterior LV because they lie directly behind sternum. Arrhythmias arise from irritated myocardium.</p></br><p>================================================================</br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Tests<br /></br>• No immediate reperfusion therapy because no coronary occlusion.<br /></br>• Serial troponins &amp; repeat ECGs help trend injury.<br /></br>• Treat only if complications arise (eg, amiodarone for VT, pericardiocentesis for tamponade, diuretics for HF).</p></br><p>================================================================</br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“STEERING-wheel = S-T Elevation, but first STEER the patient to Telemetry.”<br /></br>Blunt Cardiac Injury → BCI → “B” for Bedside monitor/telemetry, not “T” for Thrombolysis.</p></br><p>================================================================</br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>b. Intravenous ACE inhibitor therapy<br /></br>   • Used in true MI or systolic HF to limit remodeling; not an acute stabilization measure for contusion and offers no immediate benefit.</p></br><p>c. Intravenous heparin therapy<br /></br>   • Indicated for ACS due to intraluminal thrombus. Trauma patients also carry bleeding risk; heparin without evidence of thrombotic MI is contraindicated.</p></br><p>d. Intravenous nitroglycerin therapy<br /></br>   • Relieves ischemic chest pain and reduces preload in HF. This patient is hemodynamically stable, not congested, and pain is from contusion, not ischemia.</p></br><p>e. Thrombolysis<br /></br>   • High-risk, potentially fatal in trauma victims (risk of internal bleeding) and inappropriate without coronary occlusion.</p></br><p>================================================================</br>Bottom Line<br /></br>Blunt chest trauma with ECG/echo abnormalities = myocardial contusion → admit for continuous cardiac monitoring to detect and treat arrhythmias. No anticoagulation, antiplatelets, thrombolysis, or vasodilators unless another clear indication emerges.</p>
175. A 32-year-old man comes to the physician because of pain in his right hand 3 days after sustaining a puncture wound to the index finger while gardening. His temperature is 38.9°C (102°F). Examination of the right index finger shows erythema and swelling from the fingertip to the level of the metacarpophalangeal joint. The finger is held in a slightly flexed position. Passive extension of the right index finger increases the pain. There is no drainage from the wound. X-rays of the index finger show soft-tissue swelling. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Flexor tenosynovitis</br>b. Midpalmar space infection</br>c. Osteomyelitis</br>d. Paronychia</br>e. Septic arthritis</br></br></br><p>CORRECT ANSWER<br /></br>A. Flexor tenosynovitis  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>VIGNETTE ANALYSIS  </p></br><p>Key words &amp; clues (“Kanavel signs”):</br>1. Penetrating trauma 3 days ago – classic inciting event for inoculation of organisms into a closed tendon sheath.<br /></br>2. Fusiform erythema &amp; swelling “from fingertip to the metacarpophalangeal (MCP) joint” – entire digit looks like a sausage.<br /></br>3. Finger held in slight flexion – patient finds this position least painful.<br /></br>4. “Passive extension … increases pain” – pathognomonic for flexor‐sheath infection.<br /></br>5. Fever 38.9 °C – systemic inflammatory response.</p></br><p>When you see the four Kanavel signs (flexed posture, fusiform swelling, exquisite pain with passive extension, tenderness along the tendon sheath) after penetrating trauma, immediately think FLEXOR TENOSYNOVITIS.</p></br><p>Metacognitive approach:<br /></br>• Ask: “Is the whole finger swollen like a sausage? Does passive motion across the flexor sheath hurt more than across the joint? Was there a puncture or bite?”<br /></br>• Closed‐space infections (sheaths, compartments) are time‐sensitive emergencies → surgical consult.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>CLINICAL CRITICAL REASONING  </p></br><p>Step-wise thought process:</br>1. Identify setting (gardening puncture → bacterial seeding).<br /></br>2. Localize infection – swelling tracks the tendon sheath, not a single joint or only nail fold.<br /></br>3. Look for Kanavel signs → confirm flexor tenosynovitis.<br /></br>4. Rule out mimickers (see Incorrect Answer Analysis).  </p></br><p>Take-home message: Flexor tenosynovitis is an orthopedic emergency; delayed treatment risks tendon necrosis &amp; loss of function. Document Kanavel signs, neurovascular status, antibiotics started, and urgent hand-surgery consultation.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>PATHOPHYSIOLOGY REVIEW  </p></br><p>• Etiology: Most often Staphylococcus aureus (including MRSA), also Streptococci, Gram-negatives in water/soil injuries, or Eikenella in human bites.<br /></br>• Anatomy: The flexor tendon is surrounded by a synovial sheath that is poorly vascularized → infection spreads rapidly and pressure rises, compromising tendon blood supply.<br /></br>• Timeline: Symptoms typically develop in 24–72 h.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>TREATMENT &amp; DIAGNOSTICS  </p></br><ol></br><li>Immediate broad-spectrum IV antibiotics<br /></br>   • Example: Vancomycin + ceftriaxone (or pip-tazo if soil contamination).  </li></br><li>Emergent operative irrigation &amp; debridement of the sheath.  </li></br><li>Culture of sheath fluid to tailor antibiotics.  </li></br><li>Imaging rarely needed; plain films only to look for foreign body or osteomyelitis if &gt;2 wk.</li></br></ol></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>MEMORIZATION TIPS  </p></br><p>“KANAVEL = 4 K’s”<br /></br>1. Kurls (finger flexed)<br /></br>2. Kan’t extend (pain with passive extension)<br /></br>3. Kielbasa (fusiform swelling)<br /></br>4. Kinetic tenderness (along tendon path)  </p></br><p>Visualize a hot dog (fusiform swelling) stuck in a straw (tendon sheath) – any attempt to straighten it (extend) causes sharp pain.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>INCORRECT ANSWER ANALYSIS  </p></br><p>B. Midpalmar space infection – Presents with swelling in the central palm and dorsal hand, pain with finger abduction/adduction, not isolated sausage-digit findings or Kanavel signs.</p></br><p>C. Osteomyelitis – Requires weeks to develop; early x-ray usually normal. Would cause deep, localized bone pain rather than sheath-related movement pain.</p></br><p>D. Paronychia – Infection of the lateral nail fold. Red, fluctuant area adjacent to the nail plate; does NOT cause whole-digit swelling or pain on passive extension.</p></br><p>E. Septic arthritis – In the hand, MCP or PIP joint would be swollen, erythematous, and exquisitely tender; pain is greatest with motion limited to that joint, not along the entire flexor tendon sheath.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>BOTTOM LINE: A swollen, painful, flexed “sausage” finger after a puncture wound with pain on passive extension = flexor tenosynovitis. Treat emergently with IV antibiotics and surgical irrigation.</p>
176. A 32-year-old woman comes to the physician because of a 1-week history of blood in her urine. She does not have pain or burning with urination. She has no history of serious illness or similar symptoms and takes no medications. Two weeks ago, she fell down a flight of stairs while carrying books. Menses occur at regular 30-day intervals; her last menstrual period was 10 days ago. She is sexually active with one male partner, and they use condoms consistently. Physical examination shows ecchymoses over the right flank with tenderness to palpation. Pelvic examination shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Bladder cystoscopy</br>b. Complete blood count</br>c. Pregnancy test</br>d. Urinalysis</br>e. X-ray of the pelvis</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>d. Urinalysis  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Key findings<br /></br>   • “Blood in her urine” = hematuria (patient‐reported gross hematuria).<br /></br>   • “No pain or burning” → painless hematuria (not classic cystitis or ureteral stone).<br /></br>   • Recent trauma with “ecchymoses over the right flank” could be a source, but the time course (2-week delay) makes traumatic renal laceration less likely.<br /></br>   • Otherwise healthy 32-year-old woman with normal menstrual interval and LMP 10 days ago (rules out menstrual contamination).  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Diagnostic algorithm for hematuria (Urologic/Nephrologic standard of care, USMLE favorite):<br /></br>   Step 1 – Confirm hematuria and characterize it with a urinalysis (UA) ± urine microscopy.<br /></br>   Step 2 – If UA confirms hematuria → distinguish glomerular vs nonglomerular source (RBC morphology, protein, casts).<br /></br>   Step 3 – Based on Step 2, decide on imaging and/or cystoscopy.  </p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>Metacognitive pearl: Anytime the stem gives “blood in urine” without an already documented UA, your reflex should be “Get a UA first.” This is similar to “get a chest x-ray before diagnosing pneumonia” or “get a β-hCG before giving teratogens.”  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>• We do NOT yet know whether the patient truly has hematuria; patient-reported red/brown urine could be myoglobinuria, beet ingestion, phenazopyridine, etc.<br /></br>• UA will:<br /></br>  – Confirm RBCs.<br /></br>  – Provide RBC morphology (dysmorphic = glomerular, isomorphic = lower tract).<br /></br>  – Assess for protein, casts, leukocytes, nitrites, crystals.<br /></br>• Only after UA do we escalate (renal ultrasound, CT urography, or cystoscopy).  </p></br><p>Take-home message: Urinalysis with microscopic examination is the essential first diagnostic step for any new hematuria, painless or painful, in adults.  </p></br><p>Documentation (real life): “Obtained mid-stream clean-catch UA with microscopy to evaluate reported hematuria.”  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (concise)  </p></br><p>Hematuria differential:<br /></br>1. Glomerular (dysmorphic RBCs, protein, casts) – IgA nephropathy, post-strep GN, Alport, thin basement membrane disease.<br /></br>2. Nonglomerular<br /></br>   a. Upper tract – stones, pyelonephritis, renal tumor, papillary necrosis, trauma.<br /></br>   b. Lower tract – cystitis, bladder cancer, BPH, urethral injury.  </p></br><p>The algorithm’s first branch point is UA findings.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Tests Review  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>UA result suggests next test:<br /></br>   • Isomorphic RBCs, no protein/casts → CT urogram ± cystoscopy (search for malignancy, stone).<br /></br>   • Proteinuria/dysmorphic RBCs → nephrology work-up (serology, renal biopsy).<br /></br>   • Infection markers → urine culture, antibiotics (e.g., nitrofurantoin for uncomplicated cystitis).  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Imaging (CT, renal US) or cystoscopy is never first unless the patient is unstable (massive hematuria with clots) or hemodynamically compromised.  </p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>Mnemonic “H-E-M-A-T-U-R-I-A”:<br /></br>H – History of blood? →<br /></br>E – Examine urine (UA) FIRST →<br /></br>M – Morphology of RBCs decides … (glomerular vs nonglomerular)<br /></br>A – Advanced imaging / cystoscopy NEXT  </p></br><p>Visual: Picture a red urine sample trying to get through airport security—UA is the TSA check; nothing proceeds without that screening.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>a. Bladder cystoscopy<br /></br>   • Indicated for evaluation of persistent painless hematuria after UA confirms nonglomerular source, especially in patients &gt;35-40 yr or with risk factors for urothelial cancer. Premature without UA.  </p></br><p>b. Complete blood count<br /></br>   • May assess anemia from blood loss but does not identify source; not first-line for hematuria work-up.  </p></br><p>c. Pregnancy test<br /></br>   • Important whenever pelvic radiation, CT with contrast, or teratogenic meds are considered; however, hematuria evaluation algorithm starts with UA irrespective of pregnancy status. No pregnancy symptoms here.  </p></br><p>e. X-ray of the pelvis<br /></br>   • Low sensitivity for renal/ureteral pathology; does not evaluate hematuria causes and would miss soft-tissue lesions.  </p></br><p>Common pitfall: Jumping straight to cystoscopy or imaging because “painless hematuria = cancer” without first confirming true hematuria. Remember: UA first, always.</p>
177. A 52-year-old man with major depressive disorder comes to the physician for a follow-up examination. He began fluoxetine therapy 6 weeks ago. Two weeks later, he reported improvement in his mood and said he was feeling hopeful about the future. He has no other history of serious illness and takes no other medications. He does not drink alcohol or use illicit drugs. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. On mental status examination, he has a neutral mood and full range of affect. He says that 1 week ago, he fell out of bed after dreaming about being chased. His wife says he has been intermittently thrashing about while asleep. On one occasion, he punched at her, yelling and cursing. He was surprised and embarrassed when he realized he acted out his dream. He has no history of similar incidents. Which of the following is the most likely explanation for this patient's current nocturnal symptoms?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. REM sleep behavior disorder</br>b. Restless legs syndrome</br>c. Sleep apnea</br>d. Sleep terror disorder</br>e. Sleepwalking disorder</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer: A. REM sleep behavior disorder (RBD)</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis</br>• Key clues</br>  – 52-year-old man (RBD most common in men &gt;50 y)</br>  – “Thrashing,” “punching,” “acting out his dream,” fell out of bed</br>  – Full dream recall on awakening (“dreaming about being chased”)</br>  – Episodes began after starting an SSRI (fluoxetine), a classic precipitant</br>  – No prior parasomnia history; occurs during sleep, not when falling asleep or waking</br>  – Wife is the primary observer—typical presentation of RBD</p></br><p>• Pertinent positives: violent dream-enactment behaviors, retained dream content, adult age, SSRI exposure.<br /></br>  Pertinent negatives: no confusion or amnesia afterward (rules out sleep terrors &amp; sleepwalking); no snoring or daytime hypersomnolence (rules out sleep apnea); no uncomfortable leg sensations at sleep onset (rules out RLS).</p></br><p>Framework to approach similar questions<br /></br>1. Identify the behavior (movement vs respiratory vs sensory).<br /></br>2. Determine whether the patient recalls a vivid dream → REM phenomenon.<br /></br>3. Check age group &amp; precipitants (medications, neurodegenerative disease).<br /></br>4. Use sleep-stage timing: REM = latter half of the night with recall; N3 (slow-wave) = first third of night, no recall.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning</br>• Core concept: Loss of normal skeletal-muscle atonia during REM sleep → patient physically “acts out” REM dreams.<br /></br>• SSRI, SNRI, TCA, β-blockers, and alcohol withdrawal can unmask or worsen RBD.<br /></br>• Documentation in real practice: frequency, severity, injury risk, medication list, neurologic review (screen for Parkinson-plus symptoms), counseling about environmental safety, possible clonazepam/melatonin trial.</p></br><p>Take-home message: Middle-aged or older man + vivid dream-enactment behavior + dream recall ± SSRI = REM sleep behavior disorder.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review</br>• During normal REM sleep, pontine tegmental nuclei inhibit spinal motor neurons → atonia.<br /></br>• In RBD, this circuitry fails (degeneration or medication-induced dysregulation).<br /></br>• Strong association with α-synuclein neurodegenerative disorders (Parkinson disease, dementia with Lewy bodies, multiple system atrophy); RBD can precede them by years.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────</br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Pearls</br>1. Ensure safe sleep environment (remove sharp objects, pad floor, separate beds if necessary).<br /></br>2. Pharmacologic: low-dose clonazepam (0.25–2 mg) or melatonin 3–12 mg at bedtime if safety measures insufficient.<br /></br>3. Screen and follow for evolving parkinsonism or cognitive decline.<br /></br>4. Polysomnography shows REM sleep without atonia (confirmatory but not always required for exam questions).</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────</br>Mnemonic / Memory Aid</br>“REMEMBER your dream, REMEMBer your punches”<br /></br>– If the patient REMEMBERS the dream and REMEMBERS punching, think REM sleep behavior.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────</br>Why the Other Choices Are Incorrect</br>B. Restless legs syndrome<br /></br>• Uncomfortable urge to move legs at rest, worse in evening, relieved by movement; no violent dream enactment or dream recall.</p></br><p>C. Obstructive sleep apnea<br /></br>• Recurrent apnea episodes, loud snoring, daytime sleepiness, morning headaches. Does not cause purposeful dream-enactment movements.</p></br><p>D. Sleep terror disorder (night terrors)<br /></br>• Arise from N3 (slow-wave) sleep in children; screaming and autonomic arousal, patient is inconsolable and has no dream recall the next morning.</p></br><p>E. Sleepwalking disorder (somnambulism)<br /></br>• Complex motor behavior during N3 in children/young adults; patient typically has no memory of the event and behavior is usually non-violent wandering, not directed dream enactment. </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────</br>Key Exam Pearl</br>Dream recall + violent or complex motor activity during sleep in an older adult is virtually pathognomonic for REM sleep behavior disorder, especially if an SSRI or neurodegenerative disease is present.</p>
178. A 64-year-old woman with hypertension comes to the physician because of a 6-month history of moderate midepigastric pain that worsens with eating. During this time, she has had an 8.2-kg (18-lb) weight loss despite no change in appetite. She has four to six bowel movements daily, typically after eating. Her only medication is hydrochlorothiazide. She has smoked one-half pack of cigarettes daily for 40 years and drinks two martinis daily. She is 168 cm (5 ft 6 in) tall and weighs 59 kg (130 lb); BMI is 21 kg/m2. Her temperature is 36.9°C (98.4°F), pulse is 90/min, respirations are 14/min, and blood pressure is 136/70 mm Hg. Examination shows several telangiectasias over the chest. There is no scleral icterus. Abdominal examination shows mild epigastric tenderness to palpation. No masses are noted. Bowel sounds are normal. Laboratory studies show:</br>Hemoglobin           13.6 g/dL  </br>Leukocyte count      6400/mm3  </br>Platelet count       150,000/mm3  </br>Serum  </br>Glucose              180 mg/dL  </br>Alkaline phosphatase 120 U/L  </br>AST                  190 U/L  </br>ALT                  80 U/L  </br>Amylase              110 U/L  </br>Lipase               65 U/L (N=13–60)  </br>An x-ray of the abdomen is shown. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: G</br></br>a. Cholangiocarcinoma  </br>b. Cholecystitis  </br>c. Clostridium difficile colitis  </br>d. Colon cancer  </br>e. Gastric lymphoma  </br>f. Hepatocellular carcinoma  </br>g. Pancreatitis  </br>h. Peptic ulcer disease</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>g. Pancreatitis  (chronic pancreatitis with pancreatic calcifications)</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Key phrases that point to chronic pancreatitis<br /></br>   • “6-month history of moderate midepigastric pain that worsens with eating” – classically pancreatic pain is epigastric, radiates to the back, and flares after meals when the pancreas is stimulated.<br /></br>   • “8-kg (18-lb) weight loss … four to six bowel movements daily, typically after eating” – suggests fat-malabsorption (steatorrhea) from pancreatic exocrine insufficiency.<br /></br>   • Long alcohol and tobacco history (two martinis daily; 20 pack-year smoking) – principal risk factors for chronic pancreatitis.<br /></br>   • Glucose 180 mg/dL – new‐onset diabetes mellitus from islet cell destruction is common in chronic pancreatitis.<br /></br>   • AST 190 U/L &gt; ALT 80 U/L – AST &gt; ALT pattern fits alcohol use. Mild amylase/lipase elevations or even normal values are typical once acinar cells are burnt out.<br /></br>   • Abdominal x-ray (not shown in text) – on the exam this image shows coarse, speckled calcifications over the pancreatic bed, pathognomonic for chronic pancreatitis.</p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Pertinent negatives<br /></br>   • No scleral icterus or marked cholestatic labs → argues against biliary obstruction, cholangiocarcinoma, or pancreatic head cancer.<br /></br>   • No fever, leukocytosis, Murphy sign → argues against acute cholecystitis.<br /></br>   • No recent antibiotic use or pseudomembranous diarrhea → argues against C. difficile colitis.</p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>Metacognitive tip:<br /></br>When you see chronic epigastric pain + weight loss + steatorrhea + diabetes in a drinker, immediately think “chronic pancreatitis ⇒ look for pancreatic calcifications on imaging.”</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (Stepwise)  </p></br><ol></br><li>Identify pain pattern: meal-related epigastric pain ⇒ pancreas, biliary tree, peptic ulcer disease.  </li></br><li>Evaluate etiology clues: alcohol, smoking ⇒ pancreatitis or pancreatic cancer.  </li></br><li>Look for pancreatic insufficiency signs: fat malabsorption stools, weight loss, diabetes ⇒ chronic process.  </li></br><li>Corroborate with imaging: plain-film or CT calcifications clinch chronic pancreatitis.<br /></br>Core takeaway: Recurrent or chronic pancreatic inflammation leads to fibrosis and dystrophic calcification; eventually patients develop exocrine insufficiency (steatorrhea, weight loss) and endocrine insufficiency (diabetes).</li></br></ol></br><p>Documentation pearls (real patient): Character, radiation, and timing of pain; alcohol/tobacco quantities; stool description; weight trend; imaging findings; HbA1c; fat-soluble vitamin levels.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• Repeated bouts of alcohol-induced acute pancreatitis → chronic inflammation → fibrosis and intraductal calcifications.<br /></br>• Loss of acinar cells → ↓ digestive enzymes → steatorrhea, ADEK vitamin deficiency, weight loss.<br /></br>• Loss of islet cells → secondary diabetes (“Type 3c”).<br /></br>• Calcifications are dystrophic (normal Ca²⁺ level, deposition in damaged tissue).</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Diagnostics &amp; Management  </p></br><p>Diagnostics:<br /></br>– Plain film or CT: pancreatic calcifications.<br /></br>– Fecal elastase &lt; 200 µg/g confirms exocrine insufficiency.  </p></br><p>Management (goals = pain control, enzyme replacement, risk-factor modification):<br /></br>1. Alcohol and smoking cessation.<br /></br>2. Pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy (e.g., pancrelipase with meals).<br /></br>3. Small, low-fat meals; fat-soluble vitamin supplementation.<br /></br>4. Analgesia → NSAIDs → opioids; consider celiac plexus block for intractable pain.<br /></br>5. Endoscopic therapy or surgery for ductal obstruction/strictures.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>High-Yield Memory Aids  </p></br><p>“Pain + Pancreas + Poo” mnemonic for chronic pancreatitis:<br /></br>P – Post-prandial epigastric Pain<br /></br>A – Alcohol history<br /></br>I – Insulin need (diabetes)<br /></br>N – maldigestion (steatorrhea, multiple BMs)<br /></br>P – Pancreatic calcifications on imaging<br /></br>A – ADEK vitamin loss<br /></br>N – Normal/near-normal amylase/lipase late<br /></br>C – Chronic course<br /></br>E – Enzyme replacement therapy<br /></br>S – Smoking risk factor</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Incorrect Answer Rundown  </p></br><p>a. Cholangiocarcinoma – Painless jaundice, markedly elevated direct bilirubin/ALP; none present.<br /></br>b. Cholecystitis – Acute RUQ pain, fever, Murphy sign, gallbladder wall thickening on US; not chronic epigastric pain.<br /></br>c. Clostridioides difficile colitis – History of antibiotics, profuse watery diarrhea with leukocytosis; absent here.<br /></br>d. Colon cancer – May cause weight loss and altered bowel habits but not epigastric pain or pancreatic calcifications; stool guaiac usually positive.<br /></br>e. Gastric lymphoma – Can cause weight loss/epigastric pain but imaging would not show pancreatic calcifications and steatorrhea is atypical.<br /></br>f. Hepatocellular carcinoma – Cirrhotic stigmata, mass on imaging, rising AFP; pain is RUQ, not post-prandial epigastric.<br /></br>h. Peptic ulcer disease – Epigastric pain may improve (duodenal) or worsen (gastric) with meals, but does not produce steatorrhea, diabetes, or calcifications.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Take-Home Point  </p></br><p>Chronic alcohol use + post-prandial epigastric pain + steatorrhea + weight loss + diabetes + pancreatic calcifications on imaging = chronic pancreatitis.</p>
179. A previously healthy 67-year-old woman comes to the physician because of a 9-month history of moderate abdominal pain after eating. Eating frequent, small meals has minimized the pain. During this period, she has had an 11-kg (25-lb) weight loss. She has smoked one pack of cigarettes daily for 28 years. She is 160 cm (5 ft 3 in) tall and weighs 45 kg (99 lb); BMI is 18 kg/m2. Abdominal examination shows no abnormalities. Examination of the stool for ova and parasites is negative. An x-ray of the abdomen and esophagogastroduodenoscopy show no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. Capsule endoscopy</br>b. Colonoscopy</br>c. CT angiography</br>d. Small-bowel endoscopy</br>e. Upper gastrointestinal series with small bowel follow-through</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>C. CT angiography  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues<br /></br>• 67-year-old woman with vascular risk (28-pack-year smoker).<br /></br>• 9-month history of moderate, crampy abdominal pain that begins after meals (“intestinal angina”).<br /></br>• Relief with frequent, small meals and an 11-kg (25-lb) weight loss ⇒ “food fear” (sitophobia).<br /></br>• Normal physical exam, stool ova/parasites, abdominal plain film, and EGD.  </p></br><p>Pertinent negatives<br /></br>• No acute peritonitis, GI bleeding, or obstructive findings.<br /></br>• No abnormalities on EGD makes peptic ulcer, gastric malignancy, and celiac disease less likely.  </p></br><p>Pattern recognition<br /></br>Post-prandial pain + weight loss + vascular risk factors in an older patient = chronic mesenteric ischemia (CMI).  </p></br><p>Cognitive framework<br /></br>1. Elderly patient with risk factors → Think vascular disease.<br /></br>2. Pain starts ∼15–60 min after eating → Bowel demand ↑, flow limited → ischemic pain.<br /></br>3. Weight loss from avoidance of meals.<br /></br>4. Negative EGD &amp; plain imaging = luminal causes unlikely → Evaluate mesenteric vessels with non-invasive vascular imaging (CTA or MRA).  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Chronic mesenteric ischemia is usually due to atherosclerotic narrowing of at least two of the three major splanchnic arteries (celiac, SMA, IMA). The first-line, high-resolution, non-invasive test is CT angiography (CTA). It delineates arterial stenoses, collaterals, and guides revascularization planning.  </p></br><p>Documentation points (real patient)<br /></br>• Timing and character of post-prandial pain, weight change, risk factors.<br /></br>• Results of normal EGD/imaging.<br /></br>• Plan: CTA to evaluate mesenteric blood flow, possible vascular surgery referral.  </p></br><p>Take-home message<br /></br>Think “chronic mesenteric ischemia” when abdominal pain occurs predictably after meals in an older smoker with weight loss; order CTA.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• Etiology: Atherosclerosis → ≥70% stenosis of celiac, SMA, and/or IMA.<br /></br>• Mechanism: Post-prandial intestinal oxygen demand ↑ → flow can’t ↑ → ischemic pain.<br /></br>• Clinical triad: 1) Post-prandial epigastric pain, 2) Weight loss/sitophobia, 3) Vascular disease history.<br /></br>• Complications: Acute on chronic mesenteric ischemia, bowel infarction.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Diagnostic &amp; Treatment Overview  </p></br><p>Diagnosis<br /></br>• First-line imaging: CTA (sensitivity ≈ 95%), or MRA if renal dysfunction/contrast allergy.<br /></br>• Duplex ultrasound may be screening tool but operator-dependent.  </p></br><p>Treatment<br /></br>• Definitive: Revascularization (percutaneous transluminal angioplasty ± stent or surgical bypass).<br /></br>• Risk-reduction: Smoking cessation, statin, antiplatelet, control HTN/DM.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tip  </p></br><p>“Painful food = fear of food = feed the CT.”<br /></br>If eating hurts and patient avoids food, feed the CT scanner (CTA) to look at mesenteric vessels.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>A. Capsule endoscopy<br /></br>• Used for obscure small-bowel GI bleeding (e.g., angiodysplasia) or Crohn disease assessment.<br /></br>• Does not visualize mesenteric vasculature; would miss CMI.  </p></br><p>B. Colonoscopy<br /></br>• Evaluates colonic pathology (cancer, IBD, ischemic colitis).<br /></br>• Ischemic colitis pain is usually acute, left-sided, often with bloody diarrhea, not chronic post-prandial pain.  </p></br><p>D. Small-bowel endoscopy (double-balloon enteroscopy)<br /></br>• For lesions found on capsule or ongoing obscure bleeding; again luminal evaluation only.  </p></br><p>E. Upper GI series with small-bowel follow-through<br /></br>• Barium study for structural abnormalities (strictures, masses, malrotation).<br /></br>• Non-vascular; would be normal in CMI, adding radiation without diagnostic yield.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line<br /></br>Chronic, meal-induced abdominal pain and weight loss in an older smoker strongly suggest chronic mesenteric ischemia. The best next diagnostic step is CT angiography to visualize mesenteric arterial stenosis and plan revascularization.</p>
180. A 67-year-old man comes to the physician because of a 3-week history of dull, intermittent, left-sided chest pain that radiates to the left side of his jaw. The pain occurs when he mows his lawn or chops wood. When the pain occurs, he also has light-headedness and nausea. The pain resolves after he rests for several minutes. He has not had fever, night sweats, or changes in weight. He had viral upper respiratory tract infection symptoms 1 month ago. He has a long-standing history of hypertension and arthritis. His medications are lisinopril, amlodipine, glucosamine, and acetaminophen as needed; acetaminophen and antacid therapy have not improved his pain. He smoked one pack of cigarettes daily for 40 years but quit 10 years ago. His pulse is 52/min, respirations are 20/min, and blood pressure is 158/86 mm Hg. On cardiac examination, an S3 is heard; a grade 3/6 systolic murmur is heard best at the second right intercostal space. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. Chest x-ray</br>b. CT scan of the chest</br>c. ECG</br>d. Exercise stress test</br>e. Serum lipid studies</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>c. Resting electrocardiogram (ECG)</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues that frame the differential:<br /></br>• “Dull, intermittent chest pain that radiates to the jaw” + “occurs with exertion, relieved by rest” → Classic exertional angina‐type chest pain (either coronary artery disease or fixed obstructive lesion such as aortic stenosis).<br /></br>• Associated light-headedness &amp; nausea, but NO fever, weight loss, pleurisy, or positional change → favors a cardiovascular cause over infectious, pulmonary, or MSK etiologies.<br /></br>• S3 gallop → suggests underlying left-ventricular (LV) systolic dysfunction.<br /></br>• Grade 3/6 systolic murmur at the right 2nd intercostal space → typical location of an aortic stenosis (AS) murmur; AS itself can give exertional chest pain, presyncope, and dyspnea.<br /></br>• Multiple coronary risk factors: age, male sex, long smoking history, hypertension.</p></br><p>Cognitive framework for NBME/Step exams:<br /></br>1. All chest-pain patients need a baseline ECG to look for acute ischemia, prior silent MI, arrhythmia, or LVH.<br /></br>2. A baseline ECG is also required before ANY form of exercise stress test; it serves as the comparison tracing and rules out existing ST-segment abnormalities that would invalidate treadmill interpretation.<br /></br>3. If the resting ECG is diagnostic (e.g., ST elevation, Q waves, LBBB), immediate management changes. If nondiagnostic, you progress to stress testing or imaging, depending on clinical stability and ability to exercise.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (Why ECG is the essential next step)  </p></br><p>Step-wise thought process:<br /></br>• The patient is currently pain-free, hemodynamically stable, and describes exertional chest pain relieved by rest = stable angina phenotype → outpatient work-up is appropriate.<br /></br>• First diagnostic study in every evaluation of suspected myocardial ischemia is a RESTING 12-lead ECG.<br /></br>   – May show LVH, old infarction (Q waves) or ischemic changes guiding therapy.<br /></br>   – Provides baseline ST segments &amp; conduction status before a treadmill test or pharmacologic stress imaging.<br /></br>• Downstream testing (exercise stress test, stress echo, nuclear perfusion, coronary CT angiography) is guided by ECG findings and exercise capacity.<br /></br>Take-home: “Stable chest pain? Start with an ECG; if nondiagnostic and patient can walk &amp; has interpretable baseline ECG, THEN proceed to exercise stress test.”</p></br><p>Documentation in a real encounter: chest-pain characteristics, risk factors, ROS negatives, focused exam, plan for ECG with time/date, and discussion of warning signs.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (High-yield)  </p></br><ol></br><li>Stable angina: fixed ≥70% epicardial coronary stenosis → exertional demand &gt; supply → transient subendocardial ischemia.  </li></br><li>Aortic stenosis: calcific degeneration → fixed obstruction → ↑afterload → LVH → subendocardial ischemia; classic triad = angina, syncope, heart failure.  </li></br><li>S3 = rapid ventricular filling into a dilated, poorly compliant ventricle → marker of systolic dysfunction and poorer prognosis in CAD and AS.</li></br></ol></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Algorithm (Succinct)  </p></br><ol></br><li>Resting ECG (today).  </li></br><li>If ECG nondiagnostic and patient can exercise &amp; has no baseline ST-segment abnormalities → treadmill exercise ECG.<br /></br>   • Positive → coronary angiography for revascularization evaluation.  </li></br><li>If unable to exercise, abnormal baseline ECG, or suspicion of AS → stress echocardiography or pharmacologic nuclear perfusion imaging.  </li></br><li>Modifiable risk management: aspirin, high-intensity statin, β-blocker, ACE inhibitor; consider aortic valve assessment (echo) if AS suspected.</li></br></ol></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“CPR for Chest Pain Work-up”<br /></br>C – Clinical story (typical vs atypical)<br /></br>P – Plain ECG first<br /></br>R – Risk-stratify with treadmill/ imaging next  </p></br><p>Or: “No ECG? No Treadmill.” – must have a baseline 12-lead.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>a. Chest x-ray<br /></br>   • Useful for pulmonary causes or heart failure assessment; does not diagnose ischemia and would not precede ECG in typical angina work-up.<br /></br>b. CT scan of the chest<br /></br>   • High radiation, used for PE, aortic dissection, or coronary CT angiography; not first-line without prior ECG and risk stratification.<br /></br>d. Exercise stress test<br /></br>   • Appropriate second step, but ONLY after obtaining a baseline ECG to ensure interpretable ST segments and to rule out resting ischemia.<br /></br>e. Serum lipid studies<br /></br>   • Important for long-term risk modification but do not address the immediate diagnostic need. Should be ordered after the acute ischemic evaluation begins with ECG.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Big Picture “One-liner”  </p></br><p>In a stable patient with exertional chest pain suggestive of myocardial ischemia (or AS-related angina), the very first diagnostic test is a resting 12-lead ECG; it guides immediate management and is mandatory before any exercise stress testing.</p>
181. A 17-year-old girl comes to the physician because of irregular menstrual periods since menarche at the age of 11 years. Menses occur at 14- to 90-day intervals and last 1 to 9 days. Her last menstrual period was 2 weeks ago. She has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. She is 163 cm (5 ft 4 in) tall and weighs 91 kg (200 lb); BMI is 34 kg/m2. Examination shows dark skin near the base of the neck, under the axillae and breasts, and over the perineum. Central obesity is noted. Measurement of which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Androstenedione</br>b. Hemoglobin A1c</br>c. Serum estradiol concentration</br>d. Serum follicle-stimulating hormone concentration</br>e. Serum progesterone concentration</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>B. Hemoglobin A1c  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Key clues<br /></br>  – Long-standing oligomenorrhea/irregular cycles beginning at menarche → chronic anovulation<br /></br>  – Obesity (BMI 34 kg/m²) &amp; central fat distribution<br /></br>  – Acanthosis nigricans (hyperpigmented, velvety plaques on neck, axillae, groin) → marker of insulin resistance<br /></br>• Cognitive framework<br /></br>  1. Teen girl with chronic anovulation + clinical hyperandrogenism/insulin resistance → think polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS).<br /></br>  2. Once PCOS suspected, obligatory screening for cardiometabolic comorbidities (type 2 DM, dyslipidemia, obstructive sleep apnea, NAFLD).<br /></br>  3. HbA1c (or 2-h oral glucose tolerance test) is the recommended diabetes screen.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Step-wise approach for PCOS work-up:<br /></br>1. Establish likelihood of PCOS clinically (oligo-/anovulation + hyperandrogenism ± polycystic ovaries).<br /></br>2. Exclude mimickers (thyroid disease, hyperprolactinemia, non-classical CAH, Cushing).<br /></br>3. Evaluate metabolic risk (glucose intolerance, dyslipidemia).<br /></br>Therefore, measuring HbA1c is an evidence-based “next best step” before ordering specialized reproductive labs or imaging.  </p></br><p>Core takeaway: Any patient with suspected or confirmed PCOS must be screened for type 2 diabetes with HbA1c or OGTT because ≥50 % will have impaired glucose metabolism.  </p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real-life encounter)<br /></br>• Assessment: “Irregular menses with obesity &amp; acanthosis nigricans – likely PCOS.”<br /></br>• Plan: “Order HbA1c to screen for diabetes; fasting lipid panel; counsel on weight loss.”  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>PCOS triad:<br /></br>1. Hyperandrogenism (↑ ovarian theca cell androgen production driven by LH &amp; insulin).<br /></br>2. Chronic anovulation (→ oligomenorrhea).<br /></br>3. Polycystic ovaries on US (not required for diagnosis).<br /></br>Insulin resistance is central: it amplifies ovarian androgen synthesis and drives metabolic sequelae (impaired glucose tolerance → T2DM).  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Tests<br /></br>• Lifestyle modification (diet, exercise, 5-10 % weight loss).<br /></br>• First-line pharmacologic for hirsutism/anovulation control: combined oral contraceptive pill (ethinyl estradiol + progestin).<br /></br>• Metformin addresses insulin resistance, helps weight, restores cycles.<br /></br>• Screen:<br /></br>  – HbA1c or 2-h OGTT now, repeat q3-5 yrs.<br /></br>  – Fasting lipid panel.<br /></br>  – Blood pressure, BMI, consider sleep apnea evaluation.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────<br /></br>High-Yield Memory Aids<br /></br>“PCOS = PCOSt-IT”:<br /></br>P – Periods irregular<br /></br>C – Central obesity/ acanthosis<br /></br>O – Ovarian cysts<br /></br>S – Screen for Sugar (HbA1c) &amp; lipids<br /></br>IT – Insulin-resistance Treatment (Metformin)  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Options Are Incorrect  </p></br><p>a. Androstenedione<br /></br>• Would confirm hyperandrogenism, but clinical signs already present; result would not change initial management as urgently as diabetes screening.  </p></br><p>c. Serum estradiol<br /></br>• Typically normal to mildly elevated in PCOS; not diagnostic, no management impact.  </p></br><p>d. Serum follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH)<br /></br>• PCOS may show low-normal FSH with ↑LH, but isolated FSH adds little; LH/FSH ratio is neither sensitive nor specific and does not guide therapy.  </p></br><p>e. Serum progesterone<br /></br>• Mid-luteal progesterone (day 21) assesses ovulation. This patient is clearly anovulatory; confirming what is already clinically evident does not address the important metabolic risk.  </p></br><p>Common pitfall: Jumping to reproductive hormone assays before addressing metabolic screening requirements in PCOS.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line<br /></br>Suspected PCOS? Always look beyond the ovaries—screen for type 2 diabetes first with HbA1c (or OGTT).</p>
182. A 14-year-old girl is brought to the physician by her mother because of a 2-month history of moderate breast pain and constant brown to bloody right nipple discharge and tenderness. She first noticed the discharge on her bra at the end of cross-country running season and initially attributed it to bra irritation. She is otherwise asymptomatic. Menarche was at the age of 13 years. Menses occur at regular 28-day intervals. She has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. She has never been sexually active. She is at the 50th percentile for height and 70th percentile for weight. On examination, breast development is sexual maturity rating (SMR) stage 3, and pubic hair development is SMR stage 4. The breasts are small and symmetric. The lateral edges of the right nipple are tender to palpation; a small amount of brown discharge is expressed. Test of the discharge for occult blood is positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Breast cancer</br>b. Ductal ectasia</br>c. Fibroadenoma</br>d. Mastitis</br>e. Prolactinoma</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>B. Ductal ectasia  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues in the stem<br /></br>• Age 14 years &amp; early–mid puberty (SMR breast stage 3) – benign, hormonally driven breast problems are far more common than malignancy.<br /></br>• 2-month history of CONSTANT, unilateral, brown-to-bloody nipple discharge ± mild subareolar tenderness.<br /></br>• No breast mass, no systemic signs of infection, normal menstrual pattern, no medications (eg, no phenothiazines or OCPs).<br /></br>• Discharge is occult-blood positive → truly bloody rather than galactorrhea.  </p></br><p>High-yield pattern recognition<br /></br>Unilateral, brown/bloody discharge + subareolar tenderness in an adolescent with otherwise normal breast exam = dilated, inflamed subareolar ducts → ductal (periductal) ectasia.  </p></br><p>Approach / test-taking framework<br /></br>1. Bloody vs milky vs serous discharge?<br /></br>2. Unilateral vs bilateral?<br /></br>3. Associated mass/systemic findings?<br /></br>4. Age group / risk factors.<br /></br>Using this grid quickly eliminates the other options.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Why ductal ectasia fits<br /></br>• Hormone-related ductal dilation occurs in 2 groups: peri-menopausal multiparous women and pubertal adolescents (rapid estrogen/progesterone swings).<br /></br>• Dilated ducts become inflamed → brown (old blood) discharge, ± tenderness, but usually no discrete mass.<br /></br>• Most cases resolve spontaneously; imaging reserved only if exam is atypical or discharge persists &gt;6 mo.  </p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real life)<br /></br>Breast exam description, color/character of discharge, whether spontaneous or expressed, laterality, presence/absence of mass or lymphadenopathy, and counseling given.  </p></br><p>Core takeaway<br /></br>In adolescents, unilateral brown or bloody nipple discharge without a mass is almost always benign ductal ectasia. Reassure, observe, ± short-interval follow-up.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><ol></br><li>Hormonal surge → ductal epithelial proliferation and duct widening.  </li></br><li>Stasis of secretions in widened ducts → chronic granulomatous inflammation.  </li></br><li>Breakdown of duct wall vessels → hemoglobin degradation → brown (old blood) or frank bloody discharge.  </li></br></ol></br><p>Risk factors in adults: smoking (vitamin C depletion), multiparity, perimenopause. Adolescents: rapid pubertal growth.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Management Snapshot  </p></br><p>• Reassure; advise supportive bra &amp; NSAIDs for tenderness.<br /></br>• Re-examine in 3–4 mo; persistent or worsening discharge → ultrasound ± duct excision.<br /></br>• No antibiotics unless overt cellulitis develops.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“Bloody brown from the duct that’s blown = ec-TA-sia.”<br /></br>Think “E for Ectasia = Enlarged ducts Easily ooze old blood.”  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>A. Breast cancer – Extraordinary rare in teens; would present with a painless hard mass ± skin retraction, not isolated discharge.  </p></br><p>C. Fibroadenoma – Most common adolescent breast mass; firm, rubbery, MOBILE nodule, no nipple discharge.  </p></br><p>D. Mastitis – Presents with erythematous, warm, painful breast, fever, and purulent (not bloody) discharge; almost always in lactating women.  </p></br><p>E. Prolactinoma – Bilateral, milky (galactorrhea) discharge; accompanied by menstrual irregularities, headaches/visual field defects; prolactin levels elevated.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line:<br /></br>In a pubertal girl, unilateral brown-to-bloody nipple discharge with minimal tenderness and no mass is classic for benign ductal ectasia.</p>
183. One week after an uncomplicated spontaneous vaginal delivery, a hospitalized 27-year-old woman, gravida 2, para 2, has a temperature of 38.7°C (101.7°F). During the past week, her temperature has ranged from 37.2°C (99°F) to 38.7°C (101.7°F). Pregnancy was uncomplicated. She has been receiving broad-spectrum antibiotic therapy for the past 5 days. Blood and urine cultures were negative on postpartum days 2 and 5. Today, she appears comfortable. Her pulse is 72/min, and blood pressure is 102/64 mm Hg. Pelvic examination shows a small amount of vaginal discharge and no uterine tenderness. Her leukocyte count is 14,000/mm 3. Yesterday, her leukocyte count was 18,000/mm 3. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Antibiotic resistance</br>b. Endometritis</br>c. Pneumonia</br>d. Septic pelvic thrombophlebitis</br>e. Wound infection</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>D. Septic pelvic thrombophlebitis (SPT)</p></br><p>──────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis &amp; Reasoning Framework  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Time course<br /></br>   • Post-partum day 7 with a 1-week history of persistent, daily fevers.<br /></br>   • Endometritis classically peaks on days 2-3 and improves within 48 h of correct antibiotics; continued fever beyond 3–5 days despite coverage is a red flag for SPT.</p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Current clinical picture<br /></br>   • Afebrile appearance, normal pulse (72/min) and BP—she is not septic or toxic.<br /></br>   • Uterus nontender, scant normal lochia → argues against ongoing uterine infection.<br /></br>   • Cultures repeatedly negative.<br /></br>   • WBC count is falling (18 → 14 K) = inflammatory focus is controlled, but fever persists.</p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Response to treatment<br /></br>   • Broad-spectrum antibiotics x 5 days with no resolution of fever = hallmark of SPT.<br /></br>   • SPT = infected, thrombosed ovarian/iliac veins → antibiotics alone do not lyse clot;
