fever abates only after anticoagulation is added.</p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>Metacognitive tip:<br /></br>“Post-partum fever that WON’T QUIT after adequate antibiotics and with an unremarkable pelvic exam = think clot + bug → Septic Pelvic Thrombophlebitis.”</p></br><p>──────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology High-Yield Review  </p></br><p>• Etiology: Endothelial trauma (delivery), venous stasis, and hypercoagulability (Virchow triad) predispose to thrombosis of the ovarian or deep pelvic veins.<br /></br>• Bacteria seed the clot → low-grade, ‘sterile-culture’ bacteremia.<br /></br>• Clinical: Intermittent/high-spiking fevers, minimal abdominal findings, negative cultures, normal-to-low pulse (relative bradycardia sometimes).<br /></br>• Imaging: CT/MRI may show an enlarged, thrombosed ovarian vein but diagnosis is usually clinical after exclusion of other sources.  </p></br><p>Management Algorithm<br /></br>1. Continue broad-spectrum IV antibiotics (e.g., clindamycin + gentamicin).<br /></br>2. Add IV heparin (or LMWH) for ≥48 h afebrile → dissolves clot &amp; defervescence typically &lt; 48 h.<br /></br>3. Transition to oral anticoagulation x 2–6 w if large clot or risk factors persist.</p></br><p>──────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong  </p></br><p>A. Antibiotic resistance<br /></br>   – Would expect persistent leukocytosis, clinical toxicity, and often positive cultures or localizing findings. Falling WBC and a comfortable patient argue against it.</p></br><p>B. Endometritis<br /></br>   – Presents day 2–3 with uterine fundal tenderness, foul lochia; &gt;90 % respond rapidly to clinda + gent. None of these are present.</p></br><p>C. Pneumonia<br /></br>   – No cough, dyspnea, abnormal lung exam, or infiltrate on CXR provided. Unlikely silent for a full week in hospital.</p></br><p>E. Wound infection (episiotomy or cesarean)<br /></br>   – Requires local erythema, induration, drainage, or incisional pain; not described.</p></br><p>──────────────────────<br /></br>Memory Aids  </p></br><p>“Persistent PosT-partum Pyrexia” → the 3 P’s spell SPT.  </p></br><p>Think “Fever + Fall in WBC but Not in temp = Fever from a Clot.”</p></br><p>──────────────────────<br /></br>Key Take-away  </p></br><p>On Step 2 and NBME:<br /></br>• Any post-partum patient whose fever persists &gt;48–72 h on adequate antibiotics and lacks pelvic tenderness likely has septic pelvic thrombophlebitis; treat by ADDING ANTICOAGULATION.</p>
184. A 42-year-old man comes to the physician for a health maintenance examination. He runs 30 miles weekly and recently completed his third marathon. He works as an airline pilot. He has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. His father had a myocardial infarction at the age of 54 years. The patient's pulse is 56/min, respirations are 12/min, and blood pressure is 110/60 mm Hg. Examination shows no abnormalities. An ECG is shown. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: F</br></br>a. Administration of atropine</br>b. Administration of epinephrine</br>c. Atrioventricular node ablation</br>d. Placement of an implantable cardioverter defibrillator</br>e. Placement of a pacemaker</br>f. Observation only</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>f. Observation only  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Key clues<br /></br>   • 42-year-old endurance athlete who “runs 30 miles weekly and recently completed his third marathon.”<br /></br>   • Resting pulse 56/min (physiologic bradycardia).<br /></br>   • Completely asymptomatic, normal BP, normal physical exam.<br /></br>   • Father’s premature MI is irrelevant to the rhythm question.<br /></br>   • ECG image (not shown here) on NBME typically demonstrates either:<br /></br>     – Sinus bradycardia with normal P-wave morphology/axis, OR<br /></br>     – First-degree AV block with PR ≈ 220 ms but otherwise normal intervals.<br /></br>   Both are common, benign adaptations in conditioned athletes (“athlete’s heart”).  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Pertinent positives/negatives<br /></br>   • Positive: Low heart rate.<br /></br>   • Negative: No dizziness, syncope, exertional intolerance, heart failure symptoms, ischemic symptoms, or medications that depress AV node.<br /></br>   • No ECG evidence of Mobitz II, high-grade AV block, bundle-branch block with wide QRS, or ventricular ectopy.  </p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>Cognitive road-map to similar questions<br /></br>Step 1: Establish if the bradyarrhythmia is symptomatic or causing hemodynamic instability.<br /></br>Step 2: Review ECG for pathologic blocks (Mobitz II, 3° AV block) or escape rhythms.<br /></br>Step 3: In healthy athletes with isolated sinus bradycardia/1° AV block and no symptoms, choose reassurance/observation.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Why observation is correct<br /></br>• Endurance training ↑ vagal tone and ↑ stroke volume → resting sinus rates in the 40s–50s and mild PR prolongation.<br /></br>• These changes are physiologic, not pathologic, do not progress, and do not require therapy in the absence of symptoms.<br /></br>Core takeaway: Asymptomatic sinus bradycardia or first-degree AV block in a well-conditioned athlete = no intervention.  </p></br><p>Documentation (real-world)<br /></br>– “Asymptomatic sinus bradycardia attributed to high aerobic conditioning. No treatment indicated. Patient educated regarding red-flag symptoms (syncope, presyncope, palpitations).”  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (High-Yield)  </p></br><p>Athlete’s heart adaptations<br /></br>1. Autonomic: ↑ parasympathetic (vagal) tone, ↓ sympathetic tone at rest → lower sinus rate, slower AV nodal conduction (mild PR ↑).<br /></br>2. Structural: Mild concentric or eccentric LV hypertrophy with preserved function.<br /></br>3. Functional: Large stroke volume; cardiac output maintained despite lower heart rate.  </p></br><p>Because stroke volume is high, these patients remain normotensive and perfused even with HR in the 40s.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Management Algorithms – Bradycardia  </p></br><p>Symptomatic or unstable (hypotension, AMS, chest pain, HF)<br /></br>→ IV atropine 0.5 mg q3-5 min (max 3 mg) → transcutaneous pacing → epi infusion if needed.  </p></br><p>Asymptomatic<br /></br>• Identify reversible causes (medications, ischemia, hypothyroidism).<br /></br>• If physiologic (athlete) → observe.<br /></br>• If pathologic (Mobitz II, 3° AVB) → pacemaker even if asymptomatic.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“Runner’s PR = ‘Pretty Regular’ ”<br /></br>• Sinus Brady + long PR in Runner → Reassure.<br /></br>“BRADY mnemonic for atropine”<br /></br>B: Bad BP (hypotension)<br /></br>R: Reduced consciousness<br /></br>A: Anginal pain<br /></br>D: Decomp HF<br /></br>Y: You can’t wait (unstable)  </p></br><p>If none apply, don’t treat.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>a. Atropine<br /></br>   • First-line for symptomatic or unstable bradycardia. This patient is asymptomatic and stable.  </p></br><p>b. Epinephrine infusion<br /></br>   • Used when atropine ineffective or in profound bradycardic shock. Not indicated here.  </p></br><p>c. AV-node ablation<br /></br>   • Used to control refractory atrial tachyarrhythmias (e.g., AF with RVR) after failed medical therapy. Irrelevant for benign bradycardia.  </p></br><p>d. Implantable cardioverter-defibrillator (ICD)<br /></br>   • Prevents sudden death from VT/VF in structural heart disease or low EF, not for simple bradycardia.  </p></br><p>e. Pacemaker<br /></br>   • Indicated for symptomatic sinus node dysfunction, Mobitz II, high-grade or complete AV block, or chronotropic incompetence. Neither symptoms nor dangerous block are present.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line  </p></br><p>An endurance athlete with isolated, asymptomatic sinus bradycardia (± mild first-degree AV block) requires no intervention beyond routine follow-up and patient education—observation only.</p>
185. A 52-year-old man comes to the office for a follow-up examination 4 months after undergoing allogeneic stem cell transplant for acute myelogenous leukemia. He has had five to six loose, watery stools during the past 3 days. He has a 3-week history of an itchy rash over his face, chest, upper back, and arms. He reports that he has continued to have fatigue and mild dryness of the eyes and mouth since his last visit 1 month ago. He has not had fever, night sweats, or cough. The patient and the donor tested positive for cytomegalovirus before the transplant. The patient has no other history of serious illness. His medications are tacrolimus, prednisone, mycophenolate, trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole, and acyclovir; he has had no adverse effects. Vital signs are within normal limits. Examination shows a faint macular rash without scaling across the face, chest, upper back, and upper extremities. There is no lymphadenopathy. The eyes are mildly dry, and the sclerae are erythematous. Cardiopulmonary examination shows no abnormalities. Serum studies show:</br>Total bilirubin 2.1 mg/dL</br>Alkaline phosphatase 145 U/L</br>AST 112 U/L</br>ALT 175 U/L</br>Results of a complete blood count, measurement of serum electrolyte concentrations, and renal function tests are within the reference ranges. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Acute hepatitis A</br>b. Disseminated cryptococcal infection</br>c. Disseminated varicella infection</br>d. Graft-versus-host disease</br>e. Pityriasis rosea</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>D. Graft-versus-host disease (GVHD)</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li>Transplant timing: 4 months (≈120 days) after an allogeneic hematopoietic stem-cell transplant. GVHD—especially the chronic form—classically begins &gt;100 days post-transplant.  </li></br><li>Triad of target organs:<br /></br>   • Skin – faint, pruritic macular/maculopapular eruption over face, trunk, extremities.<br /></br>   • Gastrointestinal tract – new watery diarrhea.<br /></br>   • Liver – cholestatic &amp; hepatocellular enzyme elevation (↑bilirubin, ↑ALP, ↑AST/ALT).  </li></br><li>Additional exocrine involvement: dry eyes and mouth (sicca) is typical of chronic GVHD.  </li></br><li>Absence of infectious “red flags”: afebrile, no pulmonary findings, on adequate antimicrobial prophylaxis.</li></br></ol></br><p>Pattern recognition: “Allogeneic SCT + 3-organ involvement (skin/GI/liver) after day 100” → think chronic GVHD.</p></br><p>Metacognitive tip:<br /></br>Step 1 – If the stem says “allogeneic,” immediately screen for GVHD manifestations.<br /></br>Step 2 – Check timing (&lt;100 days = acute; &gt;100 days = chronic).<br /></br>Step 3 – Look for the triad above. If ≥2 organs are involved, GVHD is very high on the list.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Donor T-lymphocytes (particularly CD4⁺ and CD8⁺) become immunologically active against host (recipient) antigens. This immune attack most commonly damages rapidly renewing tissues (skin, GI epithelium, hepatic bile ducts) and exocrine glands.</p></br><p>Documentation-worthy findings: extent/description of rash, stool characteristics/volume, LFT panel, ocular/oral dryness, immunosuppressive regimen, prophylactic antimicrobials.</p></br><p>Core takeaway: Multisystem inflammatory syndrome after an allogeneic transplant = GVHD until proven otherwise.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (high-yield level)  </p></br><p>• Acute GVHD (within 100 days): cytokine storm → apoptosis of host epithelial cells in skin, liver, gut.<br /></br>• Chronic GVHD (&gt;100 days): resembles autoimmune disease; fibrosis/atrophy of involved organs with lymphocytic infiltrates producing sicca symptoms, cholestasis, and persistent dermatitis.</p></br><p>Risk factors: HLA mismatch, unrelated donor, older recipient, prior acute GVHD.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Tests  </p></br><p>Diagnosis is clinical, often supported by skin or GI biopsy showing apoptotic keratinocytes/crypt cells and donor lymphocytes.<br /></br>Management: escalate systemic immunosuppression—typically high-dose corticosteroids ± calcineurin inhibitor optimization (tacrolimus/cyclosporine) or addition of sirolimus, mycophenolate, or ruxolitinib for steroid-refractory disease. Topical steroids or ocular lubricants for local control.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>Mnemonic “GVHD = Guts, Vanity, Hepatic Damage”<br /></br>• Guts → diarrhea<br /></br>• Vanity (skin) → rash<br /></br>• Hepatic Damage → cholestatic LFTs</p></br><p>Visualization: Imagine donor T-cells as tourists invading the host “country,” attacking the skin border, gut highways, and liver customs office.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>A. Acute hepatitis A<br /></br>• Would present with prodrome (fever, malaise, RUQ pain, jaundice) and marked hyperbilirubinemia (often &gt;10 mg/dL), NOT diarrhea, rash, sicca, or recent SCT history. HAV is rare in immunocompromised adults with no travel/outbreak exposure.</p></br><p>B. Disseminated cryptococcal infection<br /></br>• Typically causes subacute meningoencephalitis ± pulmonary disease, fever, headaches. Cutaneous lesions are papular/umbilicated; diarrhea &amp; elevated transaminases are not hallmark. Patient is afebrile and on TMP-SMX (does not cover Crypto, but presentation is wrong).</p></br><p>C. Disseminated varicella infection<br /></br>• Produces diffuse vesicular (not macular) rash, often hemorrhagic in immunosuppressed patients, with fever and pneumonitis/hepatitis. Acyclovir prophylaxis further lowers likelihood.</p></br><p>E. Pityriasis rosea<br /></br>• Self-limited papulosquamous eruption in healthy young adults; begins with a herald patch, Christmas-tree distribution on trunk; no GI or hepatic involvement and extremely unlikely in an immunosuppressed 52-year-old man.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Key Take-Home Message  </p></br><p>In any post-allogeneic transplant patient—especially after day 100—concurrent rash, watery diarrhea, and cholestatic/hepatocellular LFT pattern strongly signals graft-versus-host disease. Early recognition and prompt intensification of immunosuppression are critical to prevent progressive organ damage.</p>
186. An 11-year-old boy is brought to the office by his parents because of a 2-day history of dark urine. Five days ago, he had a mild upper respiratory tract infection, which resolved spontaneously. He has not had pain with urination. He has not had sick contacts. He had frequent episodes of otitis media during infancy. He failed a hearing test at school 6 months ago, and his parents plan to schedule audiometry. He has no other history of serious illness and receives no medications. Immunizations are up-to-date. Growth and development are appropriate for age. The patient’s maternal uncle has end-stage renal disease. The patient’s temperature is 37°C (98.6°F), pulse is 80/min, respirations are 12/min, and blood pressure is 100/70 mm Hg. Examination shows no abnormalities. Urinalysis shows:</br>RBC 50/hpf</br>WBC 1–5/hpf</br>Protein 3+</br></br>Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Alport syndrome</br>b. IgA vasculitis (formerly Henoch-Schönlein purpura)</br>c. Poststreptococcal glomerulonephritis</br>d. Sickle-cell disease</br>e. Systemic lupus erythematosus</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>a. Alport syndrome  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key words &amp; clues<br /></br>1. Dark urine with hematuria + heavy proteinuria (3+) → glomerular disease.<br /></br>2. Sensorineural hearing loss suggested by “failed a hearing test” and planned audiometry.<br /></br>3. Positive family history of a maternal uncle with end-stage renal disease (ESRD).<br /></br>4. Male patient → think X-linked conditions.<br /></br>5. Recent URI was “mild” and the hematuria began only 2 days after; the timing is too short for post-streptococcal GN and the episode was not clearly streptococcal.</p></br><p>Pertinent negatives<br /></br>• No rash, abdominal pain or arthritis (makes IgA vasculitis unlikely).<br /></br>• Normal blood pressure and no edema (argues against acute nephritic syndrome such as PSGN).<br /></br>• No pain crises, anemia, or sickle-cell history.<br /></br>• No systemic findings (malar rash, joint pains) typical of SLE.</p></br><p>Pattern recognition<br /></br>Glomerular hematuria + sensorineural deafness + X-linked family history → classic board stem for Alport syndrome.</p></br><p>Test-taking framework<br /></br>When you see hematuria in a boy + hearing/ocular issues, immediately place Alport at top of differential. Always check pedigree clues (maternal male relatives) for X-linked transmission.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (Why choice A is correct)  </p></br><p>Alport syndrome is caused by mutations in type IV collagen (most commonly COL4A5, X-linked). Defective collagen → thinning &amp; splitting of the glomerular basement membrane (GBM) → microscopic or gross hematuria and progressive proteinuria → ESRD in adolescence/early adulthood. Type IV collagen is also in the cochlea and lens, explaining sensorineural hearing loss and ocular abnormalities (e.g., anterior lenticonus). A maternal uncle with ESRD in an 11-year-old male strongly supports an X-linked disorder.</p></br><p>Core takeaway: Hematuria + hearing loss + family history in a boy = Alport.</p></br><p>Documentation (real patient): document hematuria/proteinuria, family history of renal disease/ hearing loss, plan for audiology, nephrology referral, genetic counseling, ACE inhibitor initiation.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• Etiology: Mutations in COL4A3/4/5; 85% X-linked dominant.<br /></br>• Pathology: GBM “basket-weave” appearance on electron microscopy from alternating thinning and thickening.<br /></br>• Clinical course: episodic gross hematuria after minor infections → persistent microscopic hematuria → progressive proteinuria and renal failure (teen years to 30s).<br /></br>• Extra-renal: bilateral high-frequency sensorineural hearing loss; ocular (anterior lenticonus, macular flecks).</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Diagnostics &amp; Management  </p></br><p>Diagnostics<br /></br>• UA: hematuria ± proteinuria.<br /></br>• Urine microscopy: dysmorphic RBCs, RBC casts.<br /></br>• Renal biopsy EM: thin/split GBM.<br /></br>• Genetic testing confirms.<br /></br>• Audiometry &amp; slit-lamp eye exam.</p></br><p>Management<br /></br>1. ACE inhibitor or ARB (e.g., lisinopril) to reduce intraglomerular pressure and slow proteinuria.<br /></br>2. Tight BP control, low-salt diet.<br /></br>3. Ultimately many need renal transplant. (No recurrence in graft, but anti-GBM antibodies can rarely appear.)</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“Can’t hear, can’t pee, can’t see—Alport it must be!”<br /></br>Visualize a kid holding a broken iPod (hearing loss) and leaking red soda (hematuria) while wearing split-patterned jeans (split GBM).</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>b. IgA vasculitis (Henoch-Schönlein)<br /></br>• Would show palpable purpura, arthralgia, abdominal pain, and IgA-mediated nephritis. None are present.  </p></br><p>c. Poststreptococcal glomerulonephritis<br /></br>• Typically 1–3 weeks after a documented group A β-hemolytic strep pharyngitis or impetigo, not 2 days after a “mild URI.” Presents with HTN, edema, low C3, and no hearing loss.  </p></br><p>d. Sickle-cell disease<br /></br>• Hematuria is due to papillary necrosis, usually painless, but patient would have chronic hemolytic anemia, pain crises, and positive neonatal screen; not in this vignette.  </p></br><p>e. Systemic lupus erythematosus<br /></br>• Much more common in adolescent females; would expect constitutional symptoms, rash, arthritis, positive ANA, and often hypertension. Hearing loss is not a classic feature.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line<br /></br>An 11-year-old boy with hematuria, heavy proteinuria, sensorineural hearing loss, and X-linked family history → Alport syndrome.</p>
187. A 17-year-old girl comes to the office for a health maintenance examination for school. She has no history of serious illness or surgical procedures. Her mother has type 2 diabetes mellitus and hypercholesterolemia, and her father died of a heart attack at the age of 54 years. Menses occur at regular 28-day intervals. Her last menstrual period was 2 weeks ago. She does not smoke cigarettes and has never been sexually active. Her diet consists of mainly carbohydrates and fats with minimal protein or vegetables. She drinks two glasses of whole cow milk daily. She is 157 cm (5 ft 2 in) tall and weighs 68 kg (150 lb); BMI is 27 kg/m2. Her pulse is 84/min, and blood pressure is 110/60 mm Hg. Examination shows no other abnormalities. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: F</br></br>a. ECG</br>b. Determination of hemoglobin A1c</br>c. Measurement of serum thyroid-stimulating hormone concentration</br>d. Oral glucose tolerance test</br>e. Pap smear</br>f. Serum lipid studies</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>f. Serum lipid studies  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Patient profile: 17-year-old girl presenting for routine health maintenance (well-visit).<br /></br>• Key risk factors for premature atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease<br /></br>  – BMI 27 kg/m² (overweight)<br /></br>  – First-degree relative with premature MI (father died at 54 y)<br /></br>  – Mother with hypercholesterolaemia<br /></br>• Age: 17 y falls into the 17-21 y bracket in which professional societies (AAP, NHLBI, ACC/AHA) recommend universal lipid screening, with earlier and more frequent screening when additional risk factors are present.<br /></br>• No symptoms or exam findings prompting other targeted work-ups.  </p></br><p>Metacognitive framework:<br /></br>1. Ask, “Is the visit preventive or problem-focused?” → preventive.<br /></br>2. Recall age-based screening bundles (lipids at 9–11 y and 17–21 y; Pap ≥21 y; diabetes/TSH only if specific indications).<br /></br>3. Layer on family and personal risk factors to decide whether to advance or broaden screening frequency.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Step-wise thought process<br /></br>1. Identify guideline-driven age window → 17 y triggers repeat lipid panel.<br /></br>2. Confirm she carries ≥1 high-risk condition (overweight, family history) → absolutely screen now.<br /></br>3. No competing indication outranks the lipid panel at this well-visit → order fasting serum lipid profile (total, LDL-C, HDL-C, triglycerides).  </p></br><p>Take-home concept: Adolescent lipid screening is performed universally at 17–21 y and earlier/more often in the presence of obesity, diabetes, or a strong family history.  </p></br><p>Documentation bullet: “Counseled patient on diet/exercise; ordered fasting lipid profile per AAP/NHLBI guidelines given BMI 27 and paternal premature CAD.”  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Brief Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Atherosclerosis begins in childhood; elevated LDL accelerates fatty-streak formation.<br /></br>• Early identification allows diet, lifestyle, and if necessary pharmacologic therapy (e.g., statins ≥10 y) to modify lifetime cardiovascular risk.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment / Follow-up<br /></br>• Non-pharmacologic: Mediterranean-style diet, increased fruits/vegetables, aerobic activity ≥60 min/d, weight management.<br /></br>• Pharmacologic threshold (per NHLBI): LDL ≥190 mg/dL (≥160 mg/dL with high-risk factors) → consider statin such as simvastatin, atorvastatin.<br /></br>• Re-check lipids 6–12 weeks after lifestyle changes or medication initiation.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“LIPID TIMERS – 9-11 and 17-21”<br /></br>• Think of elementary school (9–11 y) and high-school graduation (17-21 y) as the two universal “report-card” years for cholesterol.<br /></br>Mnemonic FAME for extra screening indications:<br /></br>F – Family history of premature CAD<br /></br>A – Atherogenic conditions (obesity, diabetes, HTN)<br /></br>M – Metabolic disease (Kawasaki with aneurysm, CKD)<br /></br>E – Endocrine (hypothyroid)  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>a. ECG – Screening ECG is not recommended in asymptomatic adolescents without cardiac symptoms or sports clearance concerns.<br /></br>b. Hemoglobin A1c – USPSTF does not recommend routine diabetes screening in asymptomatic adolescents; would be considered if BMI ≥95th percentile plus ≥2 risk factors (she has one). Lipids are still guideline-mandated; diabetes screen is optional.<br /></br>c. Serum TSH – No symptoms of thyroid disease; no risk factors such as autoimmune history.<br /></br>d. Oral glucose tolerance test – Same rationale as option b; far more burdensome and not first-line for screening in this age group.<br /></br>e. Pap smear – Start at age 21 irrespective of sexual activity; performing it now would be premature.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Key Takeaway<br /></br>At age 17, universal lipid screening is due, and her additional cardiovascular risk factors make it the most appropriate next step.</p>
188. A hospitalized 82-year-old man has a 3-day history of progressive irritability after undergoing uncomplicated aortic valve replacement. He also has had insomnia during this time. He says he feels restless and edgy after sundown, and he repeatedly asks to go home “so I can at least sleep.” He has chronic kidney disease, asthma, and central sleep apnea. His hospital medications are albuterol, cefepime, oxycodone, and sevelamer. He drinks one 12-oz beer once weekly and has smoked two packs of cigarettes daily for 58 years. He appears fatigued but alert and attentive. Vital signs are within normal limits. Physical examination shows a clean, dry sternal incision and a barrel-shaped chest. On mental status examination, he has a surly mood and restricted affect. Cognition is intact. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management of this patient’s insomnia?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. Begin clonazepam therapy at bedtime</br>b. Begin diphenhydramine therapy at bedtime</br>c. Begin nicotine transdermal therapy</br>d. Discontinue albuterol therapy</br>e. Increase the dose of oxycodone throughout the day</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>C. Begin nicotine transdermal therapy  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Key clues<br /></br>  – “Smoked two packs of cigarettes daily for 58 years.”<br /></br>  – Hospitalized for 3 days after surgery → sudden forced abstinence from nicotine.<br /></br>  – New symptoms beginning 24-72 h after last cigarette: progressive irritability, restlessness, insomnia, repeated requests to “go home.”<br /></br>  – Cognition intact (rules out delirium); vitals normal (rules out acute medical instability).  </p></br><p>• Pattern recognition<br /></br>  Heavy, long-term smoker + abrupt cessation + 1–3-day latency → classic presentation of nicotine-withdrawal syndrome (Mnemonic: the 4 I’s—Irritable, Insomniac, Increased appetite, Inability to concentrate).  </p></br><p>Cognitive approach:<br /></br>1. Ask, “What was suddenly stopped?” (post-op, NPO, new unit rules).<br /></br>2. Match timeline of withdrawal (Alcohol 6–24 h; Opioid 6-12 h; Nicotine 24-48 h; Benzos 2-7 d).<br /></br>3. Treat withdrawal with a safer substitution (patch, gum, lozenge).  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Nicotine-withdrawal is the only option that simultaneously explains irritability AND insomnia without altering mental status. In an elderly post-operative patient, the safest and most effective way to treat withdrawal and improve sleep is nicotine-replacement therapy (NRT) with a transdermal patch.  </p></br><p>Take-home message: Always consider substance withdrawal—especially nicotine—in hospitalized smokers with new-onset insomnia, agitation, or mood changes.  </p></br><p>Documentation pearls for the chart:<br /></br>• “Patient exhibiting symptoms consistent with nicotine-withdrawal syndrome (irritability, insomnia).”<br /></br>• “Initiated 21-mg nicotine transdermal patch daily; will reassess sleep and mood.”  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Nicotine stimulates nicotinic acetylcholine receptors → ↑ dopamine and norepinephrine.<br /></br>• Chronic exposure down-regulates receptors; abrupt cessation → neurotransmitter drop → withdrawal symptoms: irritability, anxiety, dysphoria, restlessness, insomnia, ↑ appetite.<br /></br>• Peak symptoms at 2–3 days, subside over 2–4 weeks without treatment.  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Considerations<br /></br>First-line for inpatient withdrawal:<br /></br>• Nicotine patch (steady state) ± short-acting gum/lozenge for breakthrough cravings.<br /></br>Outpatient long-term cessation aids:<br /></br>• Varenicline (partial agonist).<br /></br>• Bupropion SR (NE/DA reuptake inhibitor).  </p></br><p>No additional labs/imaging are required once withdrawal is recognized; improvement after NRT supports the diagnosis.  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“NICO-TWO”:<br /></br>N – Nighttime insomnia<br /></br>I – Irritability<br /></br>C – Cravings<br /></br>O – Onset 2 days after last cigarette<br /></br>T – Treat with Transdermal patch<br /></br>W – Withdrawal  </p></br><p>Visualization: Picture the patient’s cigarette pack turning into a nicotine patch stuck on the hospital gown as the sun sets (insomnia after sundown).  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>A. Clonazepam at bedtime<br /></br>• Benzodiazepines risk oversedation, falls, and paradoxical agitation in the elderly (Beers criteria). Treats symptom, not cause; does nothing for nicotine withdrawal.  </p></br><p>B. Diphenhydramine at bedtime<br /></br>• Anticholinergic; causes confusion, urinary retention, constipation in older adults; also does not address withdrawal.  </p></br><p>D. Discontinue albuterol<br /></br>• Albuterol can cause tremor or mild insomnia but this patient’s chronic asthma requires it, and symptoms started only after hospitalization, not albuterol initiation.  </p></br><p>E. Increase daytime oxycodone<br /></br>• Opiate escalation for sedation is dangerous: respiratory depression, delirium, constipation. Symptoms are not opioid withdrawal (he’s receiving opioids).  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line<br /></br>In a heavy smoker who becomes restless and insomniac a few days after hospital admission, think nicotine withdrawal and start nicotine-replacement therapy.</p>
189. A 62-year-old woman comes to the office because of a 3-month history of progressive fatigue that has not been associated with any illness or other precipitating factors. She has gained 4 kg (8 lb) during the past 2 months despite attempting to follow a weight loss diet; she also has had constipation during this time. She has hypertension treated with lisinopril. She drinks one alcoholic beverage weekly and does not smoke cigarettes. She is not in distress. She is 165 cm (5 ft 5 in) tall and weighs 73 kg (160 lb); BMI is 27 kg/m2. Vital signs are temperature 37.0°C (98.6°F), pulse 70/min, respirations 12/min, and blood pressure 130/72 mm Hg. Neck is supp</br>le with no thyromegaly or adenopathy. Cardiopulmonary examination discloses no abnormalities. Examination of the lower extremities shows no edema; reflexes are 1+ at the knees with delayed relaxation. Which of the following is the most likely complication of this patient's condition?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Hypercholesterolemia</br>b. Hyperkalemia</br>c. Hypernatremia</br>d. Hypocalcemia</br>e. Hypochloremia</br>f. Hypokalemia</br></br></br><p>Correct answer provided by user: A. Hypercholesterolemia  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• “Progressive fatigue, weight gain, constipation” = classic constellation of slowed basal metabolic rate.<br /></br>• “Reflexes 1+ with delayed relaxation” = pathognomonic neuromuscular clue for hypothyroidism.<br /></br>• Absence of thyromegaly does not exclude a primary thyroid failure (most commonly Hashimoto).<br /></br>Key takeaway: All pertinent positives (fatigue, weight gain, constipation, slow-relaxing reflexes) plus lack of hyperadrenergic or catabolic signs → strongly favor hypothyroidism. </p></br><p>Cognitive framework for similar questions:<br /></br>1. Identify metabolic-slow symptoms (tired, cold, constipated, weight gain).<br /></br>2. Look for neuromuscular hint (delayed relaxation phase of DTRs).<br /></br>3. Recognize that the board-relevant downstream complication they often test is lipid derangement (esp. ↑LDL).  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (Why A is correct)<br /></br>Hypothyroidism → ↓thyroid hormone →<br /></br>• ↓expression of hepatic LDL receptors and<br /></br>• ↓activity of lipoprotein lipase.<br /></br>Result: impaired LDL &amp; triglyceride clearance → hypercholesterolemia and hypertriglyceridemia.<br /></br>This lipid disorder is so common that a newly discovered hyperlipidemia mandates checking a TSH in real life.</p></br><p>Core Step-2 takeaway: Always think “↑LDL/↑TG” when you see untreated hypothyroidism.</p></br><p>Documentation (real patient): record symptoms, exam (slow DTRs), order TSH/T4, counsel on cardiovascular risk of untreated disease.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Etiology in older woman = Hashimoto thyroiditis until proven otherwise (autoimmune destruction).<br /></br>• Low T3/T4 slows hepatic 7α-hydroxylase → ↓conversion of cholesterol to bile acids, compounding LDL elevation.<br /></br>• Untreated patients therefore have accelerated atherosclerosis, ↑CAD &amp; stroke risk.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostics<br /></br>1. Confirm with ↑TSH + ↓free T4.<br /></br>2. Start levothyroxine; re-check TSH in 6-8 weeks.<br /></br>3. Lipid panel often normalizes once euthyroid—may obviate lifelong statin therapy.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“HypO-thyroid = HypER-cholesterol”—the “O” and “ER” are mirror images.<br /></br>Visual: traffic jam of LDL particles on a sluggish (hypo) highway.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong<br /></br>B. Hyperkalemia – Seen in Addison disease, renal failure, ACE-i overdose; thyroid hormone does not meaningfully alter K⁺ handling.<br /></br>C. Hypernatremia – Occurs with water loss states (DI, severe burns). Hypothyroidism more often causes mild hyponatremia via ↓free-water clearance.<br /></br>D. Hypocalcemia – Results from hypoparathyroidism, vit-D deficiency, pancreatitis; unrelated to isolated hypothyroidism.<br /></br>E. Hypochloremia – Typically with vomiting or loop diuretics causing metabolic alkalosis; no link to thyroid function.<br /></br>F. Hypokalemia – Vomiting, diuretics, hyperaldosteronism. Again, no physiologic basis in hypothyroidism.</p></br><p>Common pitfall: confusing adrenal insufficiency (fatigue + hyperkalemia/hyponatremia) with hypothyroidism. The reflex slowing and constipation point you toward thyroid disease, not adrenal.</p>
190. A 48-year-old man comes to the emergency department 6 hours after the sudden onset of severe right-sided chest pain that increases with inspiration. He also has had shortness of breath during this time. Ten days ago, he sustained a nondisplaced right ankle fracture while playing basketball. He was taken to the emergency department, and a cast was placed on his right lower extremity. He has hypertension treated with amlodipine and has been taking ibuprofen for ankle pain. His temperature is 37.2°C (98.9°F), pulse is 110/min and regular, respirations are 32/min, and blood pressure is 90/50 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on room air shows an oxygen saturation of 90 percent. Cardiac examination shows no other abnormalities. There is splitting of the right hemithorax. The remainder of the examination shows no abnormalities. Which of the following sets of cardiac output and systemic vascular resistance findings is most likely to be found in this patient?</br>Cardiac Output Systemic Vascular Resistance	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Decreased decreased</br>b. Decreased increased</br>c. Increased decreased</br>d. Increased increased</br>e. Normal normal</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>B. Cardiac output – decreased; Systemic vascular resistance – increased  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues:<br /></br>• Sudden pleuritic chest pain + dyspnea = classic for pulmonary embolism (PE).<br /></br>• Recent lower-extremity immobilization in a cast = major venous thromboembolism risk.<br /></br>• Tachycardia (110/min), tachypnea (32/min), hypoxemia (SaO₂ 90 %), hypotension (90/50 mm Hg) = PE severe enough to cause shock.<br /></br>• Pleuritic chest pain, unilateral findings, and absence of other lung or cardiac exam abnormalities further favor PE over pneumonia, ACS, or aortic dissection.  </p></br><p>Approach: When a patient is hypotensive, immediately classify the type of shock (hypovolemic, cardiogenic/obstructive, distributive, or neurogenic). A massive PE causes obstructive shock.</p></br><p>Cognitive framework:<br /></br>1. Identify risk factor → casted ankle.<br /></br>2. Recognize acute pulmonary vascular obstruction.<br /></br>3. Map to hemodynamic profile of obstructive (or cardiogenic) shock: ↓CO, compensatory ↑SVR.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Pathophysiology chain in massive PE:<br /></br>1. Thrombus lodges in pulmonary artery → abrupt ↑pulmonary vascular resistance.<br /></br>2. Acute right-ventricular (RV) afterload ↑ → RV dilatation &amp; failure.<br /></br>3. RV cannot deliver blood to left heart → ↓left-ventricular preload → ↓stroke volume → ↓cardiac output.<br /></br>4. Baroreceptor-mediated sympathetic response → systemic vasoconstriction → ↑SVR to maintain blood pressure.  </p></br><p>Thus the hemodynamic pattern is decreased cardiac output with increased systemic vascular resistance (answer B).</p></br><p>Take-home algorithm (easy recall):<br /></br>• Cold, clammy extremities = vasoconstriction → high SVR → think hypovolemic or cardiogenic/obstructive.<br /></br>• Warm, flushed = vasodilation → low SVR → think distributive (sepsis, anaphylaxis, neurogenic).  </p></br><p>Document in chart: “High-risk PE causing obstructive shock; patient hypotensive with presumed ↓CO and compensatory ↑SVR—will begin emergent anticoagulation/thrombolysis once confirmed.”</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>Obstructive shock causes<br /></br>• Massive PE<br /></br>• Cardiac tamponade<br /></br>• Tension pneumothorax  </p></br><p>Shared hemodynamics<br /></br>• ↓Preload to LV (mechanical obstruction)<br /></br>• ↓Cardiac output<br /></br>• ↑SVR (sympathetic response)<br /></br>• PCWP may be normal or low (unique vs cardiogenic shock, where PCWP is high).  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostics (for context)  </p></br><ol></br><li>Immediate supportive care: oxygen, IV fluids if RV preload very low, vasopressors (norepinephrine) for persistent hypotension.  </li></br><li>Rapid imaging: bedside echocardiography (RV strain) and CT pulmonary angiography.  </li></br><li>Anticoagulation: IV unfractionated heparin initiation unless contraindicated.  </li></br><li>Thrombolysis (alteplase) or embolectomy for massive PE with hemodynamic collapse.  </li></br></ol></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>Mnemonic “COld CArdiac” for cardiogenic/Obstructive = Cold skin, Cardiac Output low, Afterload high.<br /></br>Distributive (Sepsis/Anaphylaxis) = “SAndy Warm” → SVR low, CO high (early), warm skin.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>A. Decreased CO / Decreased SVR<br /></br>• Pattern of early distributive shock (sepsis before heart catches up). Would present with warm flushed skin, not the cold, clammy extremities typically seen in PE-induced shock.  </p></br><p>C. Increased CO / Decreased SVR<br /></br>• Classic for fully developed septic or anaphylactic shock. Patient would be warm, bounding pulses, wide pulse pressure—none are described.  </p></br><p>D. Increased CO / Increased SVR<br /></br>• Rare; might be seen in hyperthyroidism or early exercise, not in any form of shock.  </p></br><p>E. Normal CO / Normal SVR<br /></br>• Hemodynamics would not explain hypotension to 90/50 mm Hg.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Core Takeaway<br /></br>Massive pulmonary embolism causes obstructive shock → right-sided outflow obstruction → low cardiac output; sympathetic vasoconstriction raises SVR. Therefore, “↓CO, ↑SVR” is the expected hemodynamic profile.</p>
191. A 4-year-old girl is brought to the office by her 26-year-old mother for a well-child examination. The mother says that her daughter is full of energy and is always asking her parents to play with or read to her. She becomes irritable and sulks when her mother refuses. The mother says she is exhausted from taking care of the patient and a 2-year-old son. She says the family has had a difficult year; their home was repossessed by the bank, and they are now living with the father’s parents. The mother becomes tearful as she describes their ongoing financial problems and how much they miss their old home. The mother’s sister and father have major depressive disorder. Physical examination of the patient shows no abnormalities. The mother asks what she can do to best help herself and her family. Which of the following is the most appropriate recommendation?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Couples therapy</br>b. Financial advisor services for the parents</br>c. Play therapy for the children</br>d. Psychiatric evaluation for the mother</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>D. Psychiatric evaluation for the mother  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues that point toward maternal major depressive disorder (MDD):<br /></br>• “Exhausted” and overwhelmed caring for 2 young children → fatigue, loss of energy.<br /></br>• Tearful affect in the office.<br /></br>• Multiple severe psychosocial stressors (home foreclosure, living with in-laws, financial strain).<br /></br>• Strong family history: sister and father with MDD.<br /></br>• Irritability and diminished interest in previously enjoyed activities (playing/reading to daughter now feels burdensome).  </p></br><p>Pertinent negatives: No concerning behavior in either child; normal physical exam; no marital conflict described.  </p></br><p>Metacognitive approach for similar questions:<br /></br>1. Screen every caregiver in pediatric visits for mood disorders.<br /></br>2. Ask, “Who in this vignette is actually symptomatic?”<br /></br>3. Choose the intervention that addresses the primary, reversible pathology first.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Why evaluate (and likely treat) the mother?<br /></br>• Maternal untreated depression is the single strongest modifiable risk factor for emotional, behavioral, and developmental problems in children.<br /></br>• Effective maternal treatment improves parenting, child attachment, and long-term family functioning.<br /></br>• A formal psychiatric evaluation establishes the diagnosis, rules out suicidality, and initiates evidence-based therapy (SSRIs such as sertraline/escitalopram and/or CBT).  </p></br><p>Core takeaway: In pediatrics, when caregiver psychopathology is suspected, the most high-yield, child-protective intervention is to get that caregiver evaluated and treated.  </p></br><p>Documentation if real encounter: maternal mood/affect, stressors, PHQ-9 score, suicidality screen, discussion/referral plan.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review – Major Depressive Disorder (MDD)<br /></br>Risk factors: genetic predisposition, female sex, acute psychosocial stress, prior episodes, postpartum period.<br /></br>Neurobiology: ↓ serotonergic, noradrenergic transmission; HPA-axis hyperactivity; hippocampal volume loss.<br /></br>Symptoms (≥5 for ≥2 weeks): depressed mood, anhedonia, sleep disturbance, guilt, energy loss, concentration problems, appetite/weight change, psychomotor change, suicidality (SIGECAPS).  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Management Algorithm<br /></br>1. Psychiatric assessment → confirm MDD ± suicidality.<br /></br>2. Mild–moderate: CBT or interpersonal therapy (IPT) ± SSRI (eg, sertraline, fluoxetine, escitalopram).<br /></br>3. Moderate–severe or suicidal: medication + therapy; consider expedited referral.<br /></br>4. Reassess in 2–4 weeks; monitor adherence, side effects, and child outcomes.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“Sad Mom = Sad Home.” If the vignette centers on parental distress, fix the parent first.<br /></br>Risk mnemonic: “HALT” – Housing loss, Anhedonia, Loss (financial), Tearfulness → think depression screen.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>A. Couples therapy<br /></br>• No interpersonal conflict between parents is identified. Therapy aimed at the relationship misses the mother’s individual depressive symptoms.  </p></br><p>B. Financial advisor services<br /></br>• Financial stress is a trigger, but counseling will not treat an underlying mood disorder. Even with better finances, untreated MDD persists.  </p></br><p>C. Play therapy for the children<br /></br>• Neither child shows emotional or behavioral problems. Treating asymptomatic children diverts resources and leaves the primary pathology (maternal depression) unaddressed.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line<br /></br>A caregiver’s untreated depression jeopardizes the entire family system. The first, most impactful step is a psychiatric evaluation and treatment plan for the affected parent.</p>
192. A 19-year-old man who is a US Army recruit is brought to the emergency department by his commanding officer 30 minutes after he was found in a supply shed. The patient covered the windows in aluminum foil to "keep enemy radio signals from penetrating the command center." The commanding officer searched for the recruit after he did not arrive for morning formation. The patient is insisting that he is "battalion commander." Physical examination and laboratory studies show no other abnormalities. Toxicology screening results are negative. The patient's parents are contacted, and they state that he has no history of psychiatric illness. Recruits bunking next to him in the barracks are interviewed and state that his behavior has become progressively odd during the past month. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Delusional disorder</br>b. Paranoid personality disorder</br>c. Schizoaffective disorder</br>d. Schizophrenia</br>e. Schizophreniform disorder</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>E. Schizophreniform disorder  </p></br><p>–––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––</br>Vignette Analysis – How the stem guides you<br /></br>• Age 19 y: prime age for first-episode psychosis.<br /></br>• Progressive “odd behavior” over the past month → duration ≈ 4 weeks.<br /></br>• Psychotic content: persecutory and grandiose delusions (“enemy radio signals,” “I am battalion commander”), disorganized behavior (covering windows with foil).<br /></br>• No substance intoxication or medical condition (negative tox screen, normal exam/labs).<br /></br>• No prior psychiatric history, no prominent mood episodes reported.  </p></br><p>Putting it together: new-onset psychosis lasting &gt; 1 week but &lt; 6 months, with functional decline → classic setup for schizophreniform disorder on the schizophrenia spectrum.  </p></br><p>Metacognitive tip:<br /></br>1. Establish the main syndrome (psychosis).<br /></br>2. Ask “How long has it been?”<br /></br>   • &lt; 1 month → Brief psychotic d/o<br /></br>   • 1–6 months → Schizophreniform d/o<br /></br>   • ≥ 6 months → Schizophrenia<br /></br>3. Superimposed mood episodes present ≥ 50 % of total illness + ≥ 2 weeks psychosis alone? → Schizoaffective d/o.<br /></br>4. Only non-bizarre, systematized delusions without other psychotic features → Delusional d/o.<br /></br>5. Lifelong, non-psychotic mistrust starting in adolescence → Paranoid personality d/o.</p></br><p>–––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>• Core takeaway: Duration is the discriminating criterion within the schizophrenia spectrum diagnoses.<br /></br>• Functional decline (missed formation, odd behavior) supports a primary psychotic process rather than an isolated delusion.<br /></br>• Documentation (real life): onset, duration, psychotic symptoms, substance screen, functional impact, safety assessment, treatment plan (antipsychotic initiation).</p></br><p>–––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––</br>Pathophysiology Review (High-yield)<br /></br>Schizophreniform is thought to share the same dopaminergic, glutamatergic, and neurodevelopmental abnormalities as schizophrenia; the six-month mark is arbitrary but epidemiologically useful—about 1/3 remit, 2/3 progress to schizophrenia or schizoaffective disorder.</p></br><p>Risk factors: male sex, late adolescence, stress (military training), family history (not given here but often present).  </p></br><p>–––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––</br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Work-up<br /></br>1. First-line: start an antipsychotic (e.g., risperidone, olanzapine, haloperidol).<br /></br>2. Assess for danger to self/others—may require inpatient admission.<br /></br>3. Supportive psychotherapy; educate patient &amp; family.<br /></br>4. Continue antipsychotic for at least 6 months after symptom resolution; monitor for progression to schizophrenia.  </p></br><p>No imaging/labs needed beyond routine “first-break psychosis” screen (CBC, CMP, TSH, HIV, RPR, urine tox) already addressed in the stem.</p></br><p>–––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––</br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“Brief – Form – Phrene – Phrenia” ladder:<br /></br>• Brief psychotic (&lt;1 mo)<br /></br>• Schizo-“form” (1–6 mo)<br /></br>• Schizo-“phrenia” (≥6 mo)  </p></br><p>Visual: imagine a calendar staircase with 1-month, 6-month, and 12-month landings labeled BP, SF, SZ.</p></br><p>–––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––</br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong  </p></br><p>A. Delusional disorder<br /></br>   – Requires ≥ 1 month of delusions only, no bizarre behavior, and relatively intact functioning. This patient has disorganization, clear functional impairment, and bizarre (“foil to block radio waves”) content.</p></br><p>B. Paranoid personality disorder<br /></br>   – Lifelong pattern beginning by early adulthood; involves mistrust but not frank psychosis. Sudden onset of delusions at 19 y argues against a personality disorder.</p></br><p>C. Schizoaffective disorder<br /></br>   – Needs major mood episode (MDD or bipolar) concurrent with psychosis plus ≥ 2 weeks of psychosis alone. No depressive or manic symptoms described.</p></br><p>D. Schizophrenia<br /></br>   – Same symptom criteria but duration ≥ 6 months (with ≥ 1 month active symptoms). The vignette specifies approximately one month → too short.</p></br><p>–––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––</br>Key Take-Home Message<br /></br>For a first-episode psychosis, always time the illness. One to six months of schizophrenia-like symptoms without prominent mood disorder equals schizophreniform disorder—treat promptly with antipsychotics and monitor for progression.</p>
193. A nurse is preparing a 65-year-old man for discharge when the patient has the acute onset of left-sided weakness. Two days ago, he was admitted to the hospital for treatment of dehydration. He has type 2 diabetes mellitus and hypertension. His medications are pioglitazone and lisinopril. His temperature is 37.1°C (98.8°F), pulse is 82/min, respirations are 16/min, and blood pressure is 175/88 mm Hg. Examination shows left lower facial weakness, left hemiplegia, and dysarthria. No other abnormalities are noted. Results of laboratory studies are within the reference ranges. CT scan of the head shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Carotid ultrasonography</br>b. Intravenous administration of heparin</br>c. Intravenous administration of labetalol</br>d. Intravenous administration of tissue plasminogen activator</br>e. MRI of the brain</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>d. Intravenous administration of tissue plasminogen activator (tPA)</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Abrupt neurologic deficit while still in the hospital (“acute onset of left-sided weakness” with facial involvement and dysarthria) → classic for an acute ischemic stroke.<br /></br>• Non-contrast head CT shows “no abnormalities” → rules out intracranial hemorrhage but is often still normal in the first few hours of an ischemic infarct.<br /></br>• Time of onset is known (just occurred while the nurse was discharging him) and is therefore &lt; 4.5 h.<br /></br>• No listed contraindications to thrombolysis (no recent brain bleed, surgery, active bleeding, severe uncontrolled HTN, platelet disorder, anticoagulation, etc.).<br /></br>• BP is 175/88 mm Hg, which is below the 185/110 mm Hg threshold that would require lowering before giving tPA.</p></br><p>Cognitive framework for similar questions<br /></br>1. Stroke symptoms → STAT non-contrast CT.<br /></br>2. If CT shows hemorrhage → neurosurgical ± BP control.<br /></br>3. If CT is negative/ischemic and onset &lt; 4.5 h and no contraindications → give IV alteplase (tPA).<br /></br>4. If &gt; 4.5 h or contraindications → consider mechanical thrombectomy (if large vessel) or aspirin.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>The core concept tested: The immediate management of an acute ischemic stroke is intravenous alteplase within 3 h (extendable to 4.5 h in selected patients) after ruling out hemorrhage. All other interventions are secondary and must not delay thrombolysis.</p></br><p>Documentation (real-life)<br /></br>• Exact time last known well/onset<br /></br>• NIH Stroke Scale score<br /></br>• Blood pressure and glucose<br /></br>• Informed consent discussion and checklist of contraindications<br /></br>• CT interpretation (“no evidence of acute hemorrhage”)<br /></br>• Alteplase dose, time started, and any BP-control meds if needed</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review (high-yield)<br /></br>• Most ischemic strokes are thrombo-embolic (large artery atherosclerosis, cardio-embolism, lacunar disease).<br /></br>• Early reperfusion with tPA lyses fibrin → restores cerebral perfusion, salvaging the ischemic penumbra.<br /></br>• Greatest benefit and lowest risk when given early; intracerebral hemorrhage is the main complication (≈6%).</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────</br>Treatments &amp; Diagnostics Summary<br /></br>First-line (0–4.5 h, no contraindications):<br /></br>• IV alteplase 0.9 mg/kg (max 90 mg): 10% bolus, remainder over 60 min.<br /></br>• Keep BP &lt; 185/110 before and &lt; 180/105 after infusion (labetalol, nicardipine).</p></br><p>Second-line / adjuncts (if outside window or large-vessel occlusion):<br /></br>• Mechanical thrombectomy ≤ 24 h (internal carotid/M1 occlusion).<br /></br>• Aspirin 24 h after thrombolysis (or immediately if no tPA).<br /></br>• High-intensity statin, evaluate for etiologic work-up (echo, carotid imaging, A1c, lipids, etc.).</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“FAST CT = tPA FAST”<br /></br>F – Face droop<br /></br>A – Arm weakness<br /></br>S – Speech difficulty<br /></br>T – Time (&lt; 4.5 h)<br /></br>Immediately get a non-contrast CT; if no blood, go “FAST” to give tPA.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────</br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>a. Carotid ultrasonography<br /></br>• Useful for secondary prevention (detect carotid stenosis) but does not treat the acute neurologic deficit. Delays reperfusion therapy → worse outcomes.</p></br><p>b. Intravenous administration of heparin<br /></br>• Routine anticoagulation has no proven benefit acutely and increases risk of hemorrhagic conversion.</p></br><p>c. Intravenous administration of labetalol<br /></br>• BP 175/88 is below the threshold that mandates treatment. Giving antihypertensives first can lower cerebral perfusion and delay tPA. (If BP were &gt; 185/110 and tPA planned, give labetalol then tPA.)</p></br><p>e. MRI of the brain<br /></br>• More sensitive than CT for early ischemia but takes longer; guidelines state imaging must not delay thrombolysis. Non-contrast CT is sufficient to rule out bleed.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────</br>Take-Home Message<br /></br>In an acute ischemic stroke with a clear onset time &lt; 4.5 h, a normal non-contrast CT, and no contraindications, intravenous alteplase is the immediate, guideline-directed therapy.</p>
194. A 4-year-old boy is brought to the office by his parents because of a 2-year history of sunburns and redness of both eyes. His parents say his skin becomes red and irritated after 15 minutes of sun exposure. Whenever he is outside, his eyes become red. Growth and development otherwise have been appropriate for age. Vaccinations are up-to-date. He is at the 50th percentile for height, weight, and BMI. His temperature is 37.0°C (98.6°F), pulse is 106/min, and respirations are 24/min. A photograph of the skin is shown. Ocular examination discloses injection of the conjunctival vessels without discharge bilaterally. Which of the following is most likely to develop in this patient during the next 10 years?	Correct answer: F</br></br>a. Albinism</br>b. Atopic dermatitis</br>c. Colon cancer</br>d. Dyshidrosis</br>e. Seborrheic dermatitis</br>f. Squamous cell carcinoma</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer</br>f. Squamous cell carcinoma</p></br><p>──────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis</br>• “Skin becomes red and irritated after 15 min of sun exposure” + “2-year history of sunburns” + “redness of both eyes” = marked photosensitivity beginning in early childhood.<br /></br>• Photograph (not shown here) on NBME typically demonstrates mottled hyper-/hypopigmented macules, freckling, and/or atrophic skin—classic for xeroderma pigmentosum (XP).<br /></br>• Ocular involvement (conjunctival injection, photophobia) is another hallmark of XP.<br /></br>• Normal growth and development and normal vitals argue against systemic metabolic or immunologic disease; the problem is restricted to UV-exposed tissue.</p></br><p>Test-taking framework<br /></br>1. Identify extreme, early-onset photosensitivity → think of inherited DNA-repair disorders.<br /></br>2. XP = inability to repair UV-induced thymine (pyrimidine) dimers due to defective nucleotide excision repair.<br /></br>3. Core complication tested on NBME/USMLE: dramatically increased risk of UV-induced skin cancers (basal cell, squamous cell, melanoma) before age 20.<br /></br>Thus, the question asks, “What is most likely to develop in the next 10 years?” → pick a skin cancer; squamous cell carcinoma (SCC) is in the answer choices.</p></br><p>──────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (Why SCC?)</br>• UV-B radiation → pyrimidine dimers → accumulate in XP → mutated TP53, PTCH, other tumor-suppressor genes → malignancy.<br /></br>• First cancers usually appear in sun-exposed areas during childhood; SCC is very common.<br /></br>• Basal cell carcinoma and melanoma are also common but were not listed.</p></br><p>Documentation pearls for real life<br /></br>“Child with confirmed/suspected xeroderma pigmentosum, extreme UV sensitivity. Counsel: strict sun avoidance, broad-spectrum sunscreen, UV-blocking clothing, regular dermatologic surveillance for early SCC/BCC/melanoma, ophthalmology follow-up.”</p></br><p>──────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review</br>• Etiology: Autosomal-recessive mutations in any of ≥8 genes (XPA–XPG, POLH) coding for nucleotide excision-repair proteins.<br /></br>• Mechanism: Failure to remove UV-induced pyrimidine dimers → unrepaired DNA → mutagenesis.<br /></br>• Clinical triad: (1) Severe sunburn/photosensitivity, (2) Poikiloderma—mottled hyper-/hypopigmented macules, skin atrophy, telangiectasias, (3) Early skin/ocular malignancies.<br /></br>• Other findings: Keratitis, cataracts, neurologic degeneration in some complementation groups.</p></br><p>──────────────────────</br>Management Snapshot</br>1. Absolute UV avoidance: protective clothing, window film, high-SPF (≥50) broad-spectrum sunscreens.<br /></br>2. Dermatologic surveillance every 3–6 mo; excise premalignant lesions early.<br /></br>3. Oral/systemic retinoids (e.g., isotretinoin) or topical DNA-repair enzymes (T4 endonuclease V) can reduce new skin cancers.<br /></br>4. Genetic counseling.</p></br><p>──────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips</br>“XP = Xtreme Photosensitivity” → think: X-ray vision to the sun, can’t fix the damage.<br /></br>SCC in XP: “Sun Causes Cancer” (SCC) in Xtreme Photosensitivity.</p></br><p>──────────────────────</br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong</br>a. Albinism – Congenital absence of melanin synthesis leads to diffuse hypopigmentation from birth, not painful sunburn after 15 min; ocular findings include nystagmus, reduced visual acuity, not conjunctival injection.<br /></br>b. Atopic dermatitis – Chronic, pruritic, flexural eczematous patches; exacerbated by allergens, not by brief UV exposure.<br /></br>c. Colon cancer – Associated with familial adenomatous polyposis (APC mutation) or Lynch syndrome; no GI history, polyp burden, or mismatch-repair defect described.<br /></br>d. Dyshidrosis (dyshidrotic eczema) – Pruritic vesicles on palms/soles triggered by sweating or stress, unrelated to sun.<br /></br>e. Seborrheic dermatitis – Greasy, yellowish scales on scalp/face (infants: cradle cap; adults: dandruff), not UV-triggered and not strongly linked to cancer.</p></br><p>──────────────────────</br>Key Takeaway</br>A child with extreme UV photosensitivity and ocular irritation likely has xeroderma pigmentosum, which confers a very high risk of developing cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma (and other skin cancers) in childhood.</p>
195. A 76-year-old man is brought to the clinic by his wife for consultation regarding left total knee replacement. During the past 2 years, he has required a cane to ambulate because of increasingly severe left knee pain. He has hypertension, major depressive disorder, and early stage dementia, Alzheimer type. His medications are nifedipine, sertraline, and aspirin. He lives with his wife, who assists him in all activities of daily living. He is oriented to person and place but not year. Pulse is 78/min, and blood pressure is 160/68 mm Hg. Physical examination shows crepitus and moderate pain on passive range of motion of the left knee. There is no warmth, erythema, or effusion. On mental status examination, he has a happy mood and congruent affect. He registers three of three objects immediately but recalls zero after 1 minute. The patient is provided with information regarding total knee replacement. When he is asked if he understands the benefits of the procedure, he says, "It will fix my knee." He is then given information about the risks and alternative treatment options. When asked about his understanding, he replies, "It will be OK." Which of the following is the greatest indication that this patient may not have sufficient capacity for informed consent?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. Complete dependence on his wife in activities of daily living</br>b. Dementia, Alzheimer type</br>c. Inability to articulate the risks and benefits of the operation</br>d. Major depressive disorder</br>e. Poorly controlled hypertension</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>c. Inability to articulate the risks and benefits of the operation  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Core clues<br /></br>   • Mild-to-moderate cognitive impairment: Alzheimer dementia, disoriented to year, 0/3 recall at 1-minute<br /></br>   • Asked to “teach-back” the information: can only say “It will fix my knee” and “It will be OK.”<br /></br>   • Cannot describe RISK, BENEFIT, or ALTERNATIVES → fails the understanding/practical reasoning component of capacity.  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Pertinent positives/negatives<br /></br>   (+) Dementia, dependence on wife → raise suspicion but do NOT automatically remove decision-making authority.<br /></br>   (–) No evidence of psychosis, intoxication, severe pain, or delirium that would transiently impair capacity.  </p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>Test-taking framework (teach-back method):<br /></br>   • Every capacity assessment asks four questions (CURVES mnemonic):<br /></br>     C – Can the patient Communicate a choice?<br /></br>     U – Do they Understand relevant information?<br /></br>     R – Can they Reason about consequences?<br /></br>     V – Do they give a Value-consistent choice?<br /></br>     E – Is the decision the same Even when repeated?<br /></br>     S – Are they able to express a Stable preference?<br /></br>   • Failure of any domain → insufficient capacity. In this vignette the “U” and “R” domains are missing.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>• Capacity = task-specific, decision-specific, and time-specific ability to make an informed choice.<br /></br>• Key legal/ethical elements of informed consent: disclosure, competence/capacity, understanding, voluntariness.<br /></br>• The most direct evidence that capacity is lacking is inability to demonstrate comprehension of information presented—in practice, an inability to “teach back” the risks, benefits, and alternatives in their own words.  </p></br><p>Take-home: A patient with cognitive impairment still retains decision-making authority unless they cannot (1) understand, (2) appreciate, (3) reason, or (4) communicate a choice. The safest indicator is failure of the teach-back test.  </p></br><p>Documentation (real life): “Patient unable to state specific risks (infection, prosthesis failure, anesthesia) or alternatives (analgesics, PT, bracing). Lacks capacity; wife will be approached as surrogate per state law.”  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (Brief)  </p></br><p>Dementia (e.g., Alzheimer) leads to progressive deficits in new learning and executive function, but capacity fluctuates and must be judged per decision. Mild dementia patients often can consent to low-risk treatments but not complex surgeries.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Management/Diagnostic Pearl  </p></br><p>• If capacity lacking → identify appropriate surrogate decision maker (usually healthcare proxy or next of kin) and follow substituted-judgment standard.<br /></br>• Always attempt to optimize reversible factors (delirium, pain, medications) before declaring lack of capacity.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“Teach-back = Capacity intact;<br /></br>Can’t teach = Capacity lacks.”  </p></br><p>Mnemonic for elements of capacity: “URCA”<br /></br>U – Understand<br /></br>R – Reason/Rank risks &amp; benefits<br /></br>C – Communicate choice<br /></br>A – Appreciate consequences  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>a. Complete dependence on wife in ADLs<br /></br>   • Functional dependence does NOT equal incapacity; many physically dependent patients make their own medical decisions.  </p></br><p>b. Dementia, Alzheimer type<br /></br>   • Diagnosis alone is insufficient. Capacity is task-specific; many patients with early dementia consent to simple procedures.  </p></br><p>d. Major depressive disorder<br /></br>   • Depression can cloud judgment but is not presumptive evidence of incapacity. Patient must still fail a capacity domain.  </p></br><p>e. Poorly controlled hypertension<br /></br>   • A physiologic condition unrelated to cognitive processing; does not bear on ability to understand or reason.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line  </p></br><p>The gold-standard test for decision-making capacity is the patient’s ability to verbalize—in their own words—the nature, benefits, risks, and alternatives of the proposed intervention. Failure to do so, as in this vignette, is the clearest indication that capacity is insufficient.</p>
196. A 23-year-old woman comes to the emergency department because of a 3-month history of increasingly painful circumferential headaches that are most severe when she lies down. During this time, she also has had intermittent episodes of “grayed out” vision with constriction of her visual fields. The episodes last 5 seconds and resolve spontaneously. The patient was treated for Chlamydia trachomatis infection 2 years ago. She has no other history of serious illness and takes no medications. Her diet consists mainly of “junk food.” She appears anxious. She is 157 cm (5 ft 2 in) tall and weighs 124 kg (273 lb); BMI is 50 kg/m2. Her blood pressure is 165/82 mm Hg; other vital signs are within normal limits. Findings on funduscopic examination of the right eye are shown; similar findings are noted in the left eye. The remainder of the examination shows no abnormalities. Lumbar puncture is performed, and opening pressure is 39 cm H2O. Results of cerebrospinal fluid analysis are within the reference ranges. MRI of the brain and MR venography show no abnormalities. Which of the following is most likely to have prevented this patient's current symptoms?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. Use of an oral contraceptive</br>b. Vitamin A supplementation</br>c. Weight loss</br>d. Yearly glaucoma testing</br>e. Yearly measurement of blood pressure</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer: c. Weight loss  </p></br><hr /></br><p>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Demographics &amp; risk factors: 23-year-old woman, BMI = 50 kg/m² (morbid obesity) – classic profile for idiopathic intracranial hypertension (IIH, “pseudotumor cerebri”).<br /></br>• Headache description: chronic, progressively worsening, circumferential (“pressure-like”) and worse when lying down → suggests elevated intracranial pressure (ICP).<br /></br>• Visual phenomena: brief episodes of “graying out” with peripheral constriction (transient visual obscurations) – pathognomonic clue for papilledema from raised ICP.<br /></br>• Exam: bilateral papilledema on fundus photo.<br /></br>• Diagnostic studies:<br /></br>  – Opening pressure 39 cm H₂O (normal ≤ 25) with otherwise normal CSF → fulfills IIH criterion of isolated ICP elevation.<br /></br>  – Normal brain MRI/MRV (rules out mass, venous sinus thrombosis).  </p></br><p>Pertinent negatives: no fever, focal neurologic deficits, or abnormal CSF chemistries → excludes meningitis, encephalitis, SAH.  </p></br><p>Metacognitive approach for similar questions:<br /></br>1. “Obese woman + headache + transient visual loss + elevated opening pressure + normal imaging” → think IIH every time.<br /></br>2. Ask: What are the modifiable risk factors? Excess body weight is #1.  </p></br><hr /></br><p>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Core concept: Rapid weight gain or sustained obesity is the strongest established risk factor for developing IIH. Sustained weight control (≥ 5–10 % reduction) markedly lowers incidence and can reverse early disease; therefore, weight loss would have prevented or mitigated her current symptoms.  </p></br><p>What must be documented clinically: counsel on weight-loss strategies (diet, exercise, possibly bariatric surgery), schedule ophthalmology follow-up, discuss acetazolamide if symptomatic.  </p></br><hr /></br><p>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• IIH = chronically elevated ICP without ventriculomegaly, mass, infection, or hydrocephalus.<br /></br>• Proposed mechanism: impaired CSF absorption at arachnoid villi; obesity may increase intra-abdominal &amp; intrathoracic pressure → elevated central venous &amp; intracranial venous pressure → reduced CSF resorption.<br /></br>• Other associations: recent weight gain, female sex (20-40 y), certain drugs (isotretinoin/vitamin A derivatives, tetracyclines, growth hormone).  </p></br><hr /></br><p>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Pearls<br /></br>Acute/ongoing therapy:<br /></br>1. First-line pharmacologic – Acetazolamide (carbonic anhydrase inhibitor ↓ CSF production).<br /></br>2. Adjunct – Topiramate (also promotes weight loss).<br /></br>3. Weight reduction = disease-modifying.<br /></br>4. Refractory vision threat – optic nerve sheath fenestration or CSF shunting.<br /></br>Monitoring: visual fields every 3–6 mo.  </p></br><hr /></br><p>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“Fat, Female, Fertile, Fixed vision” → IIH mnemonic.<br /></br>Another: “Pressure without a tumor? Lose the weight sooner.”  </p></br><hr /></br><p>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>a. Use of an oral contraceptive – Not established as a risk factor for IIH; only weak anecdotal links. Would not prevent disease.<br /></br>b. Vitamin A supplementation – Hypervitaminosis A actually precipitates IIH; supplementation could worsen, not prevent.<br /></br>d. Yearly glaucoma testing – Glaucoma involves optic nerve cupping from increased intra-ocular (not intra-cranial) pressure; would not stop IIH.<br /></br>e. Yearly measurement of blood pressure – Chronic hypertension is NOT a recognized cause of IIH. Monitoring BP has no preventive effect on intracranial pressure.  </p></br><p>Key differentiator: obesity is the only proven modifiable factor among the choices.  </p></br><p>Take-Home Message: In idiopathic intracranial hypertension, weight control/weight loss is the single most effective preventive and therapeutic measure.</p>
197. A clinician would like to increase the accuracy of diagnosing streptococcal pharyngitis among his pediatric patients in order to begin treatment sooner. He considers implementing a new diagnostic test for streptococcal pharyngitis and reviews the receiver operator characteristic (ROC) curve shown. Which of the following is the most likely clinical impact of using cut point C instead of cut point B as a positive test?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. More patients will be correctly diagnosed as being infected</br>b. More patients will be correctly diagnosed as not being infected</br>c. More patients will be incorrectly diagnosed as being infected</br>d. More patients will be incorrectly diagnosed as not being infected</br>e. Cannot be determined based on the data provided</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer (per answer key):<br /></br>c. More patients will be incorrectly diagnosed as being infected (↑ false-positives)</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis</br>• Goal stated by clinician: “increase the accuracy of diagnosing streptococcal pharyngitis … in order to begin treatment sooner.”<br /></br>• The only information given for decision-making is the ROC (receiver-operator characteristic) curve and two possible decision thresholds (cut points B and C).<br /></br>• ROC basics students must recall:<br /></br>  – X-axis = 1 – specificity (false-positive rate).<br /></br>  – Y-axis = sensitivity (true-positive rate).<br /></br>  – Moving toward the upper-left corner raises sensitivity but also raises the false-positive rate (because 1 – specificity increases).<br /></br>• Cognitive approach:<br /></br>  1. Locate points B vs C on the ROC.<br /></br>  2. Ask: which has the higher X-coordinate? → higher X means more false-positives.<br /></br>  3. Ask: which has the higher Y-coordinate? → higher Y means more true-positives.<br /></br>  4. Decide what the question is really asking (“clinical impact”) and tie it back to the definitions above.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning</br>Step-wise linkage:</p></br><ol></br><li>Cut point C lies to the right of B on the ROC (larger 1 – specificity) while also lying slightly higher (larger sensitivity).  </li></br><li>Larger 1 – specificity = lower specificity → more false-positive results.  </li></br><li>“False-positive” in this context = patient is not infected but the test says “positive,” so the child is incorrectly labeled as having strep throat and may receive unnecessary antibiotics.  </li></br><li>Therefore, switching from B ➜ C will increase the number of patients who are incorrectly diagnosed as infected.</li></br></ol></br><p>Core takeaway: Lowering the diagnostic threshold to capture more disease (↑ sensitivity) inevitably trades off with ↓ specificity, thereby increasing false-positives.</p></br><p>Clinical note: If this were a real encounter, documentation should state the rationale for the chosen diagnostic threshold and how false-positive results will be managed (e.g., confirmatory culture, antibiotic stewardship considerations).</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology / Epidemiology Review (brief)</br>• Streptococcal pharyngitis (Group A β-hemolytic Streptococcus, Streptococcus pyogenes) is common in school-aged children.<br /></br>• Rapid antigen detection tests (RADTs) or molecular tests offer faster turnaround than throat culture.<br /></br>• High sensitivity desired to prevent rheumatic fever, but false-positives lead to antibiotic overuse.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Diagnostics &amp; Management Algorithms (high-yield)</br>1.
