Centor/McIsaac criteria → decide whether to test.<br /></br>2. Tests: RADT (~85–95 % sensitive, &gt;95 % specific for modern assays) vs throat culture (gold standard).<br /></br>3. Positive result: treat with a 10-day course of penicillin V or amoxicillin (first-line).<br /></br>4. Negative RADT in children/adolescents is usually backed up by culture because of imperfect sensitivity.<br /></br>Cut-point choice in a new test must balance these factors.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips</br>“SLANT” for ROC movement:<br /></br>• Slide Left (toward y-axis) → Specificity ↓, fALse-positives ↑, seNsitivity ↑.<br /></br>Think of “Left shift gives Lots of positives (true + false).”</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Incorrect Answer Analysis</br>a. “More patients will be correctly diagnosed as being infected”<br /></br>• Sensitivity does increase moving to C, so true-positives rise—but the question asks for the MOST LIKELY clinical impact. The bigger distinguishing impact is the jump in false-positives; true-positives also rise but is not the primary trade-off emphasized in ROC questions.</p></br><p>b. “More patients will be correctly diagnosed as not being infected”<br /></br>• Incorrect: Specificity falls, so fewer (not more) true-negatives.</p></br><p>d. “More patients will be incorrectly diagnosed as not being infected”<br /></br>• Incorrect: False-negatives decrease because sensitivity increases.</p></br><p>e. “Cannot be determined”<br /></br>• Incorrect: The ROC explicitly shows relative sensitivity and specificity at each threshold; thus, the impact is determinable.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Take-Home Message</br>Lowering the decision threshold (moving right/up along an ROC curve) raises sensitivity but lowers specificity. Therefore, more FALSE-POSITIVES occur.</p>
198. A 17-year-old girl comes to the office because of irregular menstrual periods since menarche at the age of 13 years. Menses occur at 28- to 40-day intervals and last 10 days; they are not painful. Menstrual flow is heavy. Her last menstrual period was 45 days ago. She has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. She became sexually active 6 months ago with one male partner, and they use condoms consistently. The patient is 165 cm (5 ft 5 in) tall and weighs 91 kg (200 lb); BMI is 33 kg/m2. Vital signs are within normal limits. Sexual maturity rating is stage 5 for breast and pubic hair development. Physical examination, including pelvic examination, shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Oral contraceptive therapy</br>b. Pap smear</br>c. Pelvic ultrasonography</br>d. Pregnancy test</br>e. Progestin challenge test</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer</br>D. Pregnancy test (serum or urine β-hCG)</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis</p></br><p>Key clues:</br>• 17-year-old, post-menarche &gt; 3 yrs (now has established menstrual cycles)<br /></br>• Cycles “28- to 40-day intervals” → oligomenorrhea already, but now 45 days with no menses = new episode of amenorrhea<br /></br>• Sexually active for 6 mo, relies on condoms only → non-zero pregnancy risk<br /></br>• No pelvic pain, infection signs, or contraceptive use that would explain amenorrhea<br /></br>• Elevated BMI (33 kg/m²) hints at possible anovulation/PCOS, but pregnancy MUST be excluded first</p></br><p>High-yield algorithm:<br /></br>Any woman of reproductive age with amenorrhea/abnormal bleeding → Step 1 = pregnancy test, regardless of history, contraception, or suspected alternative diagnosis.</p></br><p>Metacognitive tip: On NBME/Step exams, the first branch point in ANY abnormal uterine bleeding or amenorrhea work-up is “rule out pregnancy.” Scan vignette for “last menstrual period ___ days ago” + “sexually active.” If present, choose β-hCG before jumping to imaging, hormones, or treatment.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning</p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Does the patient meet the threshold for evaluation?<br /></br>   • Expected maximal cycle length for her baseline is 40 days; she is now 45 days.<br /></br>   • Therefore, she has secondary amenorrhea (&gt; 30 days beyond longest usual cycle).</p></br></li></br><li></br><p>First-line test?<br /></br>   • Pregnancy is the most common cause and changes all subsequent management (prenatal care vs. teratogenic drugs, etc.).<br /></br>   • Test is inexpensive, rapid, non-invasive.</p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>Take-home core concept: β-hCG is ALWAYS the initial step when evaluating amenorrhea or abnormal bleeding in a woman who could be pregnant.</p></br><p>Documentation note: “Urine pregnancy test ordered today to rule out pregnancy prior to further evaluation of oligomenorrhea.”</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review (brief)</p></br><p>• Obesity → increased peripheral aromatization of androgens to estrogen + insulin resistance → disrupted LH/FSH pulsatility → anovulatory cycles → irregular, often heavy menses.<br /></br>• However, an ovulatory cycle can still occur unpredictably → conception is possible; hence pregnancy must be excluded.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Diagnostic &amp; Treatment Framework</p></br><p>Stepwise evaluation of secondary amenorrhea:</br>1. β-hCG (rule out pregnancy)<br /></br>2. If negative: TSH, prolactin → then progestin challenge / estrogen-progestin challenge, LH/FSH, imaging as indicated.<br /></br>3. Management tailored to etiology (e.g., PCOS → weight loss, OCPs, metformin).</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips</p></br><p>“PAT” for any amenorrhea workup:<br /></br>P – Pregnancy test first<br /></br>A – Assess axis labs (TSH, prolactin) second<br /></br>T – Test hormones (progesterone challenge, LH/FSH) third</p></br><p>Visual: Flash a big red STOP sign before every amenorrhea algorithm—STOP = “Check β-hCG!”</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Why the Other Choices Are Incorrect</p></br><p>A. Oral contraceptive therapy<br /></br>   • Empiric treatment of heavy/irregular menses, but contraindicated until pregnancy is excluded; could mask ectopic pregnancy and expose embryo to hormones.</p></br><p>B. Pap smear<br /></br>   • Cervical cancer screening begins at age 21, independent of sexual activity; does not address acute amenorrhea.</p></br><p>C. Pelvic ultrasonography<br /></br>   • Useful for suspected structural lesions or PCOS (enlarged, cystic ovaries, endometrial stripe), but done after pregnancy is ruled out.</p></br><p>E. Progestin challenge test<br /></br>   • Part of the hormonal evaluation algorithm to distinguish anovulation from outflow obstruction, performed only after negative pregnancy test and basic labs.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Bottom Line</p></br><p>In any reproductive-age female with delayed, missed, or abnormal menses—regardless of contraception use—the very first step is a pregnancy test.</p>
199. A 20-year-old man, who is a US soldier, is evaluated in the hospital 1 day after transfer from a military field hospital following injury from an improvised explosive device. He was thrown approximately 20 feet during the explosion and sustained an open fracture of the right tibia and fibula. He was stabilized and intubated in the field hospital prior to being transferred. CT scans of the head, neck, chest, abdomen, and pelvis have shown no abnormalities. The patient underwent open reduction and external fixation of the fractures and was extubated 1 hour ago. Medical history is unremarkable. Medications are morphine and cephalexin. Temperature is 37.7°C (99.8°F), pulse is 100/min, respirations are 20/min, and blood pressure is 120/80 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on 4 L/min of oxygen by nasal cannula shows an oxygen saturation of 96 percent. Physical examination shows an external fixation device on the right lower extremity with a clean dressing. This patient is at greatest risk for which of the following?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. Blurred vision</br>b. Decreased sense of smell</br>c. Hearing loss</br>d. Numbness of the fingers</br></br></br><p>CORRECT ANSWER<br /></br>c. Hearing loss  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>VIGNETTE ANALYSIS  </p></br><p>Key clues<br /></br>• “Injury from an improvised explosive device … thrown ≈20 ft” → classic blast mechanism<br /></br>• All CT scans (head, neck, chest, abdomen, pelvis) normal → no penetrating or intracranial bleed seen<br /></br>• Orthopedic injuries addressed; now extubated and stable → question is not about immediate life-threats but about delayed/occult blast sequelae (“greatest risk”)  </p></br><p>Blast-injury framework (USMLE favorite)<br /></br>1. Primary blast injury = due to pressure wave itself; affects air-filled or fluid–air interface organs (ears, lungs, GI)<br /></br>2. Secondary = penetrating/shrapnel<br /></br>3. Tertiary = body displacement/impact (being “thrown 20 ft”)<br /></br>4. Quaternary = burns, crush, toxic inhalants  </p></br><p>Among primary injuries, the EAR is the most sensitive structure → tympanic-membrane rupture, ossicular/inner-ear damage → conductive ± sensorineural hearing loss. Up to 50–70 % of blast casualties have auditory injury, often under-recognized in the acute setting.  </p></br><p>Metacognitive tip: Anytime you see “blast” + patient appears otherwise OK, think “hidden ear and lung problems.”  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>CLINICAL CRITICAL REASONING  </p></br><p>Step-by-step<br /></br>1. Identify mechanism (blast).<br /></br>2. Recall primary targets (ear &gt; lung &gt; bowel).<br /></br>3. Ask, “Which complication from that list is represented in answer choices?” → Hearing loss.<br /></br>4. Rule out distractors by matching them to organs typically damaged in blasts or by other mechanisms.  </p></br><p>Document in chart: Perform otoscopic exam (look for hemotympanum/perforation), bedside audiometry; schedule formal audiology follow-up.  </p></br><p>Core takeaway: Primary blast pressure readily disrupts the tympanic membrane and cochlea, so soldiers in explosions need routine auditory screening even when scans are normal.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>PATHOPHYSIOLOGY PEARLS  </p></br><p>• Rapid overpressure → TM rupture at ~5 psi; ossicular chain disruption or cochlear hair-cell injury at higher pressures → immediate or delayed hearing loss, tinnitus, vertigo.<br /></br>• TM rupture may actually “vent” middle ear, lessening inner-ear damage, but hearing deficit can persist.<br /></br>• Inner-ear (sensorineural) injury may show normal TM.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>DIAGNOSTICS &amp; MANAGEMENT  </p></br><p>Initial: Otoscopy, whisper/512-Hz tuning fork, lung auscultation (blast lung), abdominal exam (hollow-organ injury).<br /></br>Follow-up: Audiogram in 72 h, ENT referral.<br /></br>Treatment: Most TM perforations heal spontaneously; persistent conductive loss may require tympanoplasty; sensorineural loss → corticosteroid trial, hearing aids.  </p></br><p>Mnemonic: “BLAST”<br /></br>B = Barotrauma to TM<br /></br>L = Lungs (pulmonary contusion)<br /></br>A = Abdomen (bowel perforation)<br /></br>S = Sensorineural hearing loss<br /></br>T = Tinnitus  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>INCORRECT ANSWER ANALYSIS  </p></br><p>a. Blurred vision – Eye injuries can occur from debris (secondary blast) or globe rupture, usually evident early; CT head/orbit normal and no facial trauma mentioned. Not the most common or “greatest” hidden risk.  </p></br><p>b. Decreased sense of smell – Anosmia implies cribriform plate/olfactory-nerve injury from frontal lobe contusion; CT head was normal, no facial fractures. Much less common than ear damage in blasts.  </p></br><p>d. Numbness of the fingers – Suggests peripheral-nerve or spinal-cord injury/compartment syndrome; neuro exam and spine imaging were normal, fracture is in leg, not upper extremity. No mechanism favoring this over auditory injury.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>MEMORIZATION TIP  </p></br><p>“First thing to go when it blows? The EAR always hears the blast.”<br /></br>Visualize a comic soldier covering his ears as the shock wave expands—cementing the association between explosions and hearing loss.</p>
200. A 67-year-old man comes to the office because of a 3-month history of worsening difficulty swallowing when he eats solid food, especially meats. The patient has no difficulty eating soup and drinking liquids, but swallowing solid foods causes choking. He reports a 9-kg (20-lb) weight loss since his symptoms began, which he attributes to his difficulty with eating. Medical history is remarkable for nephrolithiasis and restless legs syndrome. His only medication is pramipexole. The patient has smoked one-half pack of cigarettes daily for 50 years. He does not drink alcoholic beverages or use illicit drugs. He is 185 cm (6 ft 1 in) tall and weighs 68 kg (150 lb); BMI is 20 kg/m2. Temperature is 37.0°C (98.6°F), pulse is 64/min, respirations are 16/min, and blood pressure is 118/78 mm Hg. Physical examination discloses no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in evaluation?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis</br>b. Esophagogastroduodenoscopy</br>c. Ultrasonography of the neck</br>d. Urine albumin concentration</br>e. Videofluoroscopic swallow study</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>B. Esophagogastroduodenoscopy (EGD)</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis (how the clues narrow the differential)</p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Key words/phrases<br /></br>   • “67-year-old man,” “3-month history,” “progressive difficulty swallowing solid food (especially meat).”<br /></br>   • “No difficulty … with liquids.”<br /></br>   • “Choking,” 9-kg (20-lb) unintentional weight loss.<br /></br>   • 50-pack-year smoking history.<br /></br>   • Normal vitals and exam (nothing to suggest oropharyngeal neuromuscular disorder).</p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Pertinent positives<br /></br>   • Age &gt;60, significant smoking → malignancy risk.<br /></br>   • Progressive solids-only dysphagia → classic for an obstructing/mechanical lesion in the esophagus (esophageal carcinoma, Schatzki ring, peptic stricture).  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Pertinent negatives<br /></br>   • No dysphagia to liquids early → argues against primary motility disorders (achalasia, diffuse esophageal spasm).<br /></br>   • No coughing/nasal regurgitation → less likely oropharyngeal dysphagia.</p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>Pattern/Algorithm (cognitive framework)<br /></br>Step 1: Decide oropharyngeal vs esophageal → solids hang up “a few seconds after swallowing,” no nasopharyngeal symptoms = esophageal.<br /></br>Step 2: Solids first, then liquids? Yes → mechanical obstruction.<br /></br>Step 3: Progressive course, weight loss, risk factors → malignancy highest on list.<br /></br>Step 4: Initial test for suspected obstructing esophageal lesion = direct visualization &amp; biopsy = EGD.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning</p></br><p>Why EGD now?<br /></br>• Provides direct visualization to diagnose (tumor, stricture, ring) AND allows biopsy if cancer is present, plus therapeutic dilation if benign stricture.<br /></br>• Barium swallow can be used first for long-standing strictures, but any red-flag features (age &gt;60, weight loss, rapid progression) mandate immediate endoscopy.</p></br><p>Document in note: progressive solids dysphagia, weight loss, 50-pack-year smoker ⇒ high suspicion of esophageal carcinoma; plan for urgent EGD with biopsy.</p></br><p>Take-home message: Progressive solids-only dysphagia + “alarm” findings → go straight to EGD.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review</p></br><p>Esophageal squamous-cell or adenocarcinoma produces an eccentric mass that narrows the lumen; early symptoms limited to solids because they require a wider lumen than liquids. Continued growth eventually obstructs liquids as well. Risk factors: smoking, alcohol (squamous), chronic GERD/Barrett (adenocarcinoma).  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Diagnostic &amp; Treatment Pearls</p></br><p>First-line evaluation (alarm features)<br /></br>• Esophagogastroduodenoscopy with biopsy.<br /></br>Staging (next step once diagnosis confirmed)<br /></br>• CT chest/abdomen ± PET, endoscopic ultrasound for depth/N-stage.<br /></br>Treatment<br /></br>• Local disease: esophagectomy or endoscopic mucosal resection (T1), chemoradiation.<br /></br>• Advanced/metastatic: palliative stenting, systemic therapy.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips</p></br><p>“Solid first = Something solid in the esophagus.”<br /></br>If dysphagia → solids then liquids, think obstruction; go “SEE” it with “Scope” (EGD).</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Incorrect Answer Analysis</p></br><p>A. CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis<br /></br>• Used for staging once cancer is diagnosed or for abdominal pathology. It cannot diagnose an intraluminal esophageal lesion as well as EGD.</p></br><p>C. Ultrasonography of the neck<br /></br>• Evaluates thyroid/parathyroid or carotid lesions; unrelated to esophageal dysphagia.</p></br><p>D. Urine albumin concentration<br /></br>• Screens for nephropathy; no relevance to dysphagia.</p></br><p>E. Videofluoroscopic swallow study<br /></br>• Best for oropharyngeal dysphagia (stroke, neuromuscular disease) characterized by difficulty initiating a swallow, choking immediately, or nasal regurgitation—none of which are present here.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Bottom Line</p></br><p>Progressive, solids-predominant dysphagia with weight loss in an older smoker = suspect esophageal cancer → immediate esophagogastroduodenoscopy with biopsy.</p>
